Also N. fowleri lacks a heme oxygenase homolog, limiting efficient heme utilization. The authors still consider chelation therapy as a potential approach. Therefore the existence of partial hemin rescue does not imply chelation would be ineffective or fully bypassed; the option overstates the implication and is not supported by the text.

14.Option: The downregulation of cytosolic iron-dependent enzymes indicates that N. fowleri is less dependent on iron overall, reducing its vulnerability to chelation therapy.
Evidence: The list of downregulated proteins based on whole-cell proteomics under iron-deficient conditions contained 20% of predicted iron-containing proteins, most of which were nonheme enzymes such as desaturases and oxygenases, or hydrogenase (NF0008540) and its maturases HydE (NF0081220) and HydG (NF0081230). Importantly, most of the downregulated iron-containing proteins were typically located outside mitochondria.

Proteomic analysis proved to be a valuable resource in determining the cellular changes in N. fowleri induced by iron starvation and provided a foundation for further discoveries that are shown in this study (Table 1). The most fundamental finding of comparative proteomic analysis is that mainly cytosolic iron-containing proteins were downregulated when iron was limited.
Iron plays a vital role in many biochemical processes; therefore, it is rational to expect that decreasing the bioavailability of iron in the surrounding environment would hinder cell growth.
iron chelators have the ability to hinder the proliferation of N. fowleri.
14.Rationale: Downregulation of nonessential cytosolic iron-dependent enzymes is an adaptive redistribution of limited iron, not evidence that the parasite is overall less dependent on iron. The passage repeatedly emphasizes that iron is vital and that chelators hinder growth. Thus the observed proteomic re-prioritization does not imply reduced vulnerability to chelation; instead it reflects a survival strategy within a still iron‑limited and vulnerable organism.

14.Option: The upregulation of alternative respiratory pathways like AOX under iron deficiency suggests that N. fowleri can fully compensate for iron loss, making it less vulnerable to chelation therapy.
Evidence: In contrast, in the iron-deficient cells, the activity of AOX increased by 104% (p-value <0.01). This finding shows that AOX, despite being an iron-containing enzyme, is able to rescue respiration when iron deficiency causes a decrease in complex IV activity.

the total respiration of the cells grown under iron-deficient conditions was decreased by 35%, although this change was not statistically significant (p-value >0.01), and that this decrease was based on the diminished activity of complex IV (55% decrease; p-value <0.01).
even though the overall generation of the proton gradient and therefore ATP synthesis is hindered.
iron chelators have the ability to hinder the proliferation of N. fowleri.
14.Rationale: AOX activity increases and can partly rescue respiration, but the passage makes clear that total respiration and ATP generation are nonetheless impaired ("overall generation of the proton gradient and therefore ATP synthesis is hindered"). The compensation is partial, not full, and chelators still limit growth. Therefore the claim that AOX enables full compensation and reduced vulnerability is not supported.

14.Option: The parasite's prioritization of mitochondrial iron-dependent respiration under iron deficiency creates a metabolic vulnerability to iron chelators that cannot be compensated for by alternative pathways
Evidence: Proteomic and metabolomic investigations showed that N. fowleri responded to low iron levels by maintaining iron-containing proteins in mitochondria, while the activity of nonvital, mostly cytosolic, iron-requiring pathways declined.

This mitochondrial sequestration of iron to ensure respiration was confirmed by increased complex I activity with a concomitant decrease in the activity level of hydrogenase (Fig 2D) as well as a reduction in the iron-dependent catabolism of amino acids (Fig 2B).
iron chelators have the ability to hinder the proliferation of N. fowleri

iron-deficiency is a highly unfavorable condition for N. fowleri, and targeted interference with its uptake could be an effective method of controlling the propagation or viability of this organism in the host.
we believe that iron chelation therapy is a promising approach to hinder and attenuate the progress of the infection
14.Rationale: The study shows N. fowleri reallocates limited iron to maintain essential mitochondrial respiration while downregulating nonessential cytosolic iron-dependent pathways. This prioritization leaves the parasite dependent on a constrained mitochondrial iron pool; alternative pathways (e.g., AOX) only partially compensate and overall growth is hindered by chelators. The authors explicitly conclude that chelation could be used to hinder the parasite, consistent with a metabolic vulnerability arising from this redistribution.

14.Option: The parasite's inability to induce iron uptake mechanisms makes it inherently resistant to iron chelation, as it does not actively seek iron from the environment.
Evidence: Surprisingly, there was no significant difference in iron uptake between cells preincubated in the iron-rich and iron-deficient conditions, showing that the organism failed to stimulate its iron uptake machinery.

Measurements of ferric reductase activity revealed that the level of ferric iron converted to ferrous iron was not significantly changed in the iron-deprived cells (p-value >0.05). This finding confirms that N. fowleri cannot efficiently adjust the rate of iron acquisition from its surroundings to overcome its dependence on iron availability.
Iron plays a vital role in many biochemical processes; therefore, it is rational to expect that decreasing the bioavailability of iron in the surrounding environment would hinder cell growth.
iron chelators have the ability to hinder the proliferation of N. fowleri
we believe that iron chelation therapy is a promising approach to hinder and attenuate the progress of the infection
14.Rationale: The passage documents that N. fowleri cannot upregulate iron uptake under starvation, but this lack of inducible acquisition makes the parasite more, not less, vulnerable to chelation because it cannot compensate by increasing uptake. The authors present chelators as effective inhibitors of growth; thus the statement that lack of uptake induction confers resistance is contrary to the evidence.

15.Option: A. aegypti populations in California are likely established and overwintering due to the presence of multiple introductions and high genetic diversity across regions
Evidence: Our analyses suggest multiple introductions of A. aegypti into CA that came from at least two different regions in North America.
Genetic diversity was significantly lower within CA populations than among other North American populations (Table 2 and S1 Table).
Southern California populations are less genetically diverse.
15.Rationale: The option correctly cites 'multiple introductions' (supported by the passage), but it incorrectly asserts 'high genetic diversity across regions.' The passage explicitly reports that genetic diversity was significantly lower within California populations overall and that Southern California populations are less diverse. Thus the combination of multiple introductions and high diversity is not supported; lower, not higher, diversity is observed in CA, so this option is incorrect.

15.Option: A. aegypti populations in California are likely established and overwintering because of the absence of long-distance dispersal and high population density in urban areas
Evidence: The large pairwise FST values between Southern California sites may indicate limited gene flow among the populations, consistent with the relatively short active dispersal distance that has been found for A. aegypti [23].

The dense highway system and highly discontinuous human habitats in Southern California could well cause A. aegypti to be broken into almost entirely isolated small local populations given the evidence suggesting this mosquito avoids crossing major roads/highways [48].
We found strong evidence for overwintering in Madera and mixed results for San Mateo.
15.Rationale: While the passage documents short active dispersal and limited gene flow (supporting restricted movement), it does not attribute overwintering to 'high population density in urban areas,' nor does it state an 'absence of long-distance dispersal' as the primary cause of persistence. The direct evidence for overwintering is genetic temporal stability (e.g., Madera). Therefore the causal explanation in this option (high urban population density + absence of long-distance dispersal) is not supported by the passage, so the option is incorrect.

15.Option: A. aegypti populations in Madera, California, are likely established and overwintering, as evidenced by stable genetic structure and minimal genetic diversity loss over time
Evidence: We found strong evidence for overwintering in Madera and mixed results for San Mateo.
The FST values between Madera 2015 and the two Madera 2013 populations is lower (0.01 and 0.047) than between Madera 2015 and any of the sites in the Central South populations (range = 0.091–0.15).
The genetic diversity also changed very little: for example, the allelic richness was 3.5 in 2013 and 3.6 in 2015.
temporal stability indicates that some populations in Northern California are stable and likely continuously breeding in situ, rather than being recolonized by their original source.
15.Rationale: The passage explicitly states strong evidence for overwintering in Madera, supports that with low FST between Madera samples across years (indicating genetic continuity), and documents almost no loss in allelic richness between 2013 and 2015. These lines of genetic and temporal evidence directly support the option's conclusion that Madera populations are established and overwintering.

15.Option: A. aegypti populations in Southern California are likely established and overwintering, as evidenced by high genetic differentiation between sites and consistent presence across multiple years
Evidence: We observed significant population structure with genetic differentiation even among populations in close geographic proximity, particularly among Southern California populations.
Southern California populations are less genetically diverse.
Invasion events are often accompanied by a bottleneck in population size and subsequent decrease in genetic diversity, especially allelic richness.
15.Rationale: The passage documents high genetic differentiation among Southern California sites and lower genetic diversity there, but it does not present temporal repeat-sampling evidence (stable allelic richness or low FST across years) demonstrating overwintering for Southern California populations. In fact, lower diversity may reflect founder effects or bottlenecks rather than stable, overwintering populations. Because the necessary temporal continuity evidence for Southern California is not provided, this option is not supported.

15.Option: A. aegypti populations in Northern California are likely established and overwintering, as evidenced by their genetic similarity to South Central US populations and consistent temporal sampling
Evidence: Most of the Northern California populations clustered with the South Central US and Southeast US populations.
We found strong evidence for overwintering in Madera and mixed results for San Mateo.
The FST values between Madera 2015 and the two Madera 2013 populations is lower (0.01 and 0.047) than between Madera 2015 and any of the sites in the Central South populations (range = 0.091–0.15).
temporal stability indicates that some populations in Northern California are stable and likely continuously breeding in situ, rather than being recolonized by their original source.
15.Rationale: The passage supports that some Northern California populations are persistent and likely overwintering: Madera shows direct temporal genetic stability, and the authors state that temporal stability indicates some Northern CA populations are stable and breeding in situ. While genetic similarity to South Central US speaks to origin rather than overwintering, the combination of origin information and the explicit temporal stability findings support the broader conclusion that Northern California populations (or at least some of them) are established and persisting despite limited surveillance. Therefore this option is judged correct.

16.Option: Hypermethylation of the DDX58 promoter, associated with the rs3739674 GG genotype and linked to reduced mRNA expression, accounts for a substantial proportion of disease severity variance
Evidence: The results demonstrate that severe EV71-HFMD patients exhibit higher levels of DDX58 promoter methylation than healthy controls and mild EV71-HFMD patients (Fig 1A).

At DDX58 SNP rs3739674, genotype GG (median 0.112, IQR 0.094–0.133) was associated with significantly lower DDX58 mRNA expression levels than both the CC (median 0.146, IQR 0.010–0.185) and GC genotypes (median 0.132, IQR 0.106–0.161, Fig 6A).

At DDX58 SNP rs3739674, genotype GG (median 0.009, IQR 0.008–0.009) was associated with significantly higher DDX58 methylation levels than both the CC (median 0.007, IQR 0.006–0.007) and GC genotypes (median 0.008, IQR 0.007–0.008, Fig 6B).
Simple linear regression analysis were performed to show the variance in DDX58 expression accounted by HFMD severity (R square = 0.293, p<0.001), by DDX58 methylation (R square = 0.058, p = 0.008),
16.Rationale: The first part of the option is supported by the passage: severe cases have higher DDX58 promoter methylation, the rs3739674 GG genotype is associated with higher methylation and lower DDX58 mRNA expression. However the assertion that this combination "accounts for a substantial proportion of disease severity variance" is not supported. The regression results show that DDX58 methylation explains only a small fraction of variance in DDX58 expression (R2 = 0.058), and the authors report that HFMD severity explains a larger share of expression variance (R2 = 0.293). Thus while the molecular/genetic pattern is consistent with impaired sensing, the claim that it accounts for a substantial proportion of disease severity variance overstates the evidence.

16.Option: Elevated IFIH1 mRNA expression in severe cases, coupled with increased methylation at the 50 and 86 CpG sites, suggests a compensatory immune response that mitigates disease severity
Evidence: IFIH1 mRNA expression levels were significantly lower in severe patients (median 0.035, IQR 0.027–0.040) than in mild patients (median 0.043, IQR 0.036–0.060, P = 0.001; Fig 4F), and IFIH1 mRNA levels were able to differentiate between mild and severe EV71-HFMD (AUC = 0.669 on average; Fig 4E).

We observed that the 50, 86 CpG sites, and the average methylation level of 50 CpG sites was significantly higher, while 86 CpG sites was significantly lower in severe HFMD patients than that in the corresponding healthy controls and mild HFMD patients (Table 2, Fig 2B).
The IFIH1 average methylation level was not significantly different among the three groups (Table 2),
16.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the factual premises are wrong. The passage reports that IFIH1 mRNA expression is lower, not elevated, in severe cases. Regarding methylation, the 50 CpG site is higher in severe cases but the 86 CpG site is actually lower; the average IFIH1 methylation was not significantly different among groups. Therefore the suggested compensatory pattern (elevated expression coupled with increased methylation at both sites) is not supported by the data.

16.Option: The rs3739674 GG genotype is associated with increased DDX58 expression and reduced methylation, suggesting a protective genetic variant that enhances innate immune sensing in severe EV71-HFMD
Evidence: At DDX58 SNP rs3739674, genotype GG (median 0.112, IQR 0.094–0.133) was associated with significantly lower DDX58 mRNA expression levels than both the CC (median 0.146, IQR 0.010–0.185) and GC genotypes (median 0.132, IQR 0.106–0.161, Fig 6A).

At DDX58 SNP rs3739674, genotype GG (median 0.009, IQR 0.008–0.009) was associated with significantly higher DDX58 methylation levels than both the CC (median 0.007, IQR 0.006–0.007) and GC genotypes (median 0.008, IQR 0.007–0.008, Fig 6B).

After adjusting for sex and age, there were significant differences in genotype frequencies of DDX58 rs3739674 identified through binary logistic regression analysis between the mild and severe groups according to an allele model for the C allele (G vs. C: OR 1.421, 95% CI 1.077–1.873, P = 0.013) (Table 4).
16.Rationale: This option states the opposite of the observed findings. The rs3739674 GG genotype is associated with lower DDX58 expression and higher methylation (not increased expression and reduced methylation). Thus it does not represent a protective variant that enhances innate sensing; if anything, the GG genotype is linked to the molecular pattern (higher methylation, lower expression) consistent with impaired sensing and increased severity.

16.Option: The negative correlation between DDX58 promoter methylation and mRNA expression, combined with the higher methylation in severe cases, indicates that epigenetic silencing is the primary driver of disease severity
Evidence: Spearman’s correlation analysis showed that the average level of CpG methylation in the DDX58 promoter region was negatively correlated with the level of DDX58 mRNA expression among the three groups.
The results demonstrate that severe EV71-HFMD patients exhibit higher levels of DDX58 promoter methylation than healthy controls and mild EV71-HFMD patients (Fig 1A).
Simple linear regression analysis were performed to show the variance in DDX58 expression accounted by HFMD severity (R square = 0.293, p<0.001), by DDX58 methylation (R square = 0.058, p = 0.008),

Correlation analysis and simple linear regression analysis between DDX58/IFIH1 expressions and their promotor CpG sites methylation suggest that promotor methylation might have a minimal effect on DDX58 expressions.
16.Rationale: While the passage documents a negative correlation between DDX58 promoter methylation and mRNA expression and reports higher methylation in severe cases, it does not support the stronger claim that epigenetic silencing is the primary driver of disease severity. Regression analyses indicate methylation explains only a small portion of expression variance (R2 = 0.058), and the authors explicitly note promoter methylation might have a minimal effect on DDX58 expression. Therefore epigenetic silencing may contribute but is not demonstrated as the primary driver.

16.Option: The rs1990760 TT genotype in IFIH1 is associated with higher methylation and lower expression, indicating a direct genetic mechanism that impairs innate immune sensing in severe EV71-HFMD
Evidence: At IFIH1 SNP rs1990760, genotype TT (median 0.012, IQR 0.011–0.013) was associated with significantly higher IFIH1 methylation levels than both the CC (median 0.010, IQR 0.009–0.011) and CT genotypes (median 0.011, IQR 0.010–0.012, Fig 6D).
In addition, there was no correlation between IFIH1 expression and the genotypes of IFIH1 SNP rs1990760 (Fig 6C).
After adjusting for sex and age, there were no significant differences in genotype frequencies of IFIH1 rs1990760 among the mild and severe groups (Table 4).
16.Rationale: This option is only partially true: the rs1990760 TT genotype is associated with higher IFIH1 methylation. However the passage explicitly reports no correlation between rs1990760 genotype and IFIH1 expression, and there were no significant differences in rs1990760 genotype frequencies between mild and severe groups. Thus there is no support for the claim that rs1990760 TT causes lower expression or directly impairs innate sensing in severe EV71-HFMD.

17.Option: Genotype assignment accuracy is maximized when using the most conserved genomic region, regardless of phylogenetic signal variation across lineages
Evidence: Suitability of sub-genomic regions for genotyping purposes.
For DENV, the only sub genomic region that supports confident genotype assignment across the four different serotypes was the envelope gene.
Our likelihood-mapping analyses show that for DENV, the envelope, NS1, NS3 and NS5 had good phylogenetic signal across all four serotypes.

In summary, these analyses show that the envelope genes of the reference datasets of the three pathogens (DENV, 1,485 nucleotides; CHIKV, 1,317 nucleotides; ZIKV, 1,525 nucleotides) are the most suitable targets for reliable genotype classification.
17.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that regions with good phylogenetic signal (for example the envelope gene) are the most suitable for genotype assignment. It does not endorse using the most conserved region regardless of signal; in fact, conserved regions often lack the variation necessary to resolve genotypes. The authors evaluated phylogenetic signal explicitly (likelihood-mapping, boot-scanning) and selected regions based on that signal rather than on conservation alone.

17.Option: The consistency of bootstrap support across viral lineages determines the reliability of genotype assignment in regions with variable phylogenetic signal
Evidence: The aim was to find the size and segments of the genome that would correctly classify a query sequence with a bootstrap support of >70%.

The boot-scanning analysis showed that for ZIKV, segments of around 1,200–1,500 base pairs support the genotype assignment with bootstrap > 70% (Fig 3). This was the case over the entire genome, with the exception of the end of the genome (i.e. the non-coding region) and near the NS3 region, where bootstraps fell below 60%.
The query sequence is assigned to a particular genotype if it clusters monophyletically with that genotype clade with bootstrap support >70%.
17.Rationale: The investigators used bootstrap support thresholds (e.g., >70%) in boot-scanning and phylogenetic assignment to judge whether a genomic segment reliably classifies genotypes. Where bootstrap support dropped (e.g., <60%), assignment was unreliable. Thus, consistency and magnitude of bootstrap support across lineages is the critical factor determining reliability when phylogenetic signal varies.

17.Option: The length of the genomic region used for classification must exceed 1,500 base pairs to ensure sufficient phylogenetic signal for accurate genotype assignment
Evidence: The boot-scanning method uses a sliding window of a 1500 base pair segment that moves with steps of 100 base pairs along the genome.
The boot-scanning analysis showed that for ZIKV, segments of around 1,200–1,500 base pairs support the genotype assignment with bootstrap > 70% (Fig 3).
For CHIKV, the envelope region E1 was the only region that allowed consistent assignment.

In summary, these analyses show that the envelope genes of the reference datasets of the three pathogens (DENV, 1,485 nucleotides; CHIKV, 1,317 nucleotides; ZIKV, 1,525 nucleotides) are the most suitable targets for reliable genotype classification.
17.Rationale: The passage reports that segments around 1,200–1,500 bp supported assignment for some viruses, and that effective envelope regions for different viruses vary in length (e.g., CHIKV E1 = 1,317 bp; DENV envelope = 1,485 bp). It does not state a universal requirement that the region must exceed 1,500 bp. Thus a fixed >1,500 bp rule is not supported.

17.Option: The accuracy of genotype assignment is primarily determined by the number of reference sequences available for the genomic region, not by the phylogenetic signal within the region
Evidence: The accuracy and consistency with which a method identifies viral species and genotype clades depends on the selection of a set of representative reference sequences [29–31].
Two different methods were used to verify the suitability of sub-genomic regions for genotyping purposes: a boot-scanning method and a likelihood-mapping method (see Methods).
Our likelihood-mapping analyses show that for DENV, the envelope, NS1, NS3 and NS5 had good phylogenetic signal across all four serotypes.
For DENV, most of the genotypes were classified with great accuracy (i.e. specificity and sensitivity > 99%) using the envelope gene.
17.Rationale: While the passage notes that choosing representative reference sequences is important, it also repeatedly emphasizes the need for sufficient phylogenetic signal and bootstrap support within the genomic region. The authors evaluated regions using boot‑scanning and likelihood‑mapping precisely because phylogenetic signal in the region critically affects assignment accuracy. Therefore, phylogenetic signal (and corresponding bootstrap support) is at least as critical as reference sequence number, and for a region with variable signal, the latter dominates.

17.Option: Genotype assignment can be reliably performed using any genomic region as long as it is amplified with high sequencing depth and coverage
Evidence: For DENV, the only sub genomic region that supports confident genotype assignment across the four different serotypes was the envelope gene.

The boot-scanning analysis showed that for ZIKV, segments of around 1,200–1,500 base pairs support the genotype assignment with bootstrap > 70% (Fig 3). This was the case over the entire genome, with the exception of the end of the genome (i.e. the non-coding region) and near the NS3 region, where bootstraps fell below 60%.

Since a good phylogenetic signal was reported for the DENV and ZIKV NS5 region and the CHIKV E2 region, a classification analysis was performed for these regions as well. For the DENV NS5 region a sensitivity of 57,48% and specificity of 31,35%) was observed.

For CHIKV, the E2 region showed perfect accuracy, similar to the E1 region (i.e. specificity and sensitivity of 100%). However, our previous boot-scanning support showed that the genetically variable E2 region may cause problems for some strains to be correctly identified as ECSA genotype.
17.Rationale: High sequencing depth/coverage improves data quality but does not overcome lack of phylogenetic signal in a region. The passage provides examples where some regions (e.g., NS5 for DENV) yielded poor sensitivity/specificity despite being sequenceable, and where variable regions (E2) can cause misclassification for some strains. Thus not any region will suffice regardless of depth/coverage; phylogenetic signal and bootstrap support matter.

18.Option: Conduct a multivariate regression analysis to assess the independent contribution of individual, organizational, and community-level factors to MDVC coverage
Evidence: We used the CFIR to organize the range of responses received from the different stakeholders using specific domains to address intricate, interrelated, and multilevel ideas [22] to pinpoint barriers that impede adequate vaccination coverage.
We conducted seven focus groups to obtain community perspectives on barriers to their MDVC participation, during and after the COVID-19 peak.
All recordings, conducted in Spanish, were transcribed and checked for quality.

Between March and May 2023, using the codebook, the research team (LOC, JC) independently double-coded the transcripts using Dedoose version 9.0.90 for all focus group, workshop, and field note data (which was already in a Google document as noted by the observer (EWD)).
18.Rationale: The study is qualitative, using focus groups, workshop-based group model building, and thematic coding (CFIR-based) rather than quantitative cohort or survey data that would provide the numerators/denominators and variable measurement needed for multivariate regression. The quoted passages show the explicit use of CFIR, focus groups, transcription, and qualitative coding, indicating multivariate regression was not the methodological approach used to identify multilevel factors.

18.Option: Apply a systems thinking approach by mapping causal pathways between individual beliefs, organizational policies, and community-level behaviors to visualize how they interact over time
Evidence: we facilitated fifteen small focus groups with MDVC implementers and authorities to obtain their perspectives on barriers and facilitators for mass dog vaccination, conducted during a day-long group model building [31,32] workshop, aimed at planning for an improved MDVC.
We mapped results from focus groups with community members, MDVC implementers, authorities, and observation field notes (Research Field Team) using the updated CFIR [22].
We organized the data within the five CFIR domains, according to the number of barriers identified in each domain.
18.Rationale: While elements of systems thinking (e.g., a group model building workshop) were used in the study, the passage makes clear that the primary, explicitly stated methodological framework for organizing and distinguishing multilevel, interrelated factors was the CFIR. The option describes a useful complementary approach (mapping causal pathways), and the study did use GMB, but the question asks which methodological choice best supports the ability to identify and distinguish multilevel interacting factors in this study; the CFIR (an explicit multilevel implementation framework) is the principal method used. Therefore, although systems mapping was present as a component, it is not presented as the main organizing analytical choice and so is not the best answer.

18.Option: Use a cross-sectional survey with stratified sampling to compare vaccination coverage across urban and periurban areas and assess differences in barriers by socioeconomic status
Evidence: We conducted seven focus groups to obtain community perspectives on barriers to their MDVC participation, during and after the COVID-19 peak.

We also facilitated fifteen small focus groups with MDVC implementers and authorities to obtain their perspectives on barriers and facilitators for mass dog vaccination, conducted during a day-long group model building workshop, aimed at planning for an improved MDVC.
We used purposive sampling to recruit 56 community members for focus groups...
All recordings, conducted in Spanish, were transcribed and checked for quality.
18.Rationale: The investigators used qualitative purposive sampling, focus groups, and workshop methods rather than a cross-sectional survey approach. Although stratified cross-sectional surveys can quantify coverage differences by urban/periurban status and SES, the passage documents a qualitative, CFIR-based study design aimed at mapping barriers across levels. Thus this option does not reflect the methodological choice the study used to identify and distinguish multilevel interrelated factors.

18.Option: Implement a realist evaluation framework to test the mechanisms by which different implementation strategies produce varying levels of MDVC coverage across settings
Evidence: Implementation science is crucial for identifying and addressing barriers that hinder the adoption of evidence-based interventions in public health programs.

The Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) is highly regarded as an optimal framework for guiding the implementation of practices and interventions, due to its comprehensive and structured approach (covering numerous domains), ability to consider various contextual factors, and flexibility for both prospective and retrospective use [17].

We used the CFIR to organize the range of responses received from the different stakeholders using specific domains to address intricate, interrelated, and multilevel ideas [22] to pinpoint barriers that impede adequate vaccination coverage.
18.Rationale: Realist evaluation is a valuable framework for testing mechanisms in different contexts, but the passage explicitly states that the study used the CFIR to structure and analyze multilevel implementation barriers. There is no indication that a realist evaluation was employed. The CFIR is described as the implemented framework, making realist evaluation an incorrect choice for what best supported their ability to distinguish multilevel, interrelated factors in this study.

18.Option: Use of the Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) to systematically categorize and analyze barriers across multiple levels of the health system
Evidence: The Consolidated Framework for Implementation Research (CFIR) is highly regarded as an optimal framework for guiding the implementation of practices and interventions, due to its comprehensive and structured approach (covering numerous domains), ability to consider various contextual factors, and flexibility for both prospective and retrospective use [17].

We used the CFIR to organize the range of responses received from the different stakeholders using specific domains to address intricate, interrelated, and multilevel ideas [22] to pinpoint barriers that impede adequate vaccination coverage.
We mapped results from focus groups with community members, MDVC implementers, authorities, and observation field notes (Research Field Team) using the updated CFIR [22].

We organized the data within the five CFIR domains, according to the number of barriers identified in each domain. Most barriers appeared in the Implementation Process domain, followed by the Inner, Individuals, and Outer Setting domains.
18.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that CFIR was the framework used to organize and analyze stakeholder responses and to map barriers across multiple domains and levels (Implementation Process, Inner Setting, Individuals, Outer Setting, etc.). CFIR is designed to help identify and distinguish multilevel, interrelated factors affecting implementation, which directly answers the question about the methodological choice that best supports distinguishing factors operating at different levels of the health system.

19.Option: The induction of mitochondrial damage in Leishmania amastigotes leads to irreversible metabolic collapse, preventing recovery even after treatment cessation
Evidence: It has been shown that apigenin induces mitochondrial damage and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [16–20].

Apigenin induced ROS production in Leishmania-infected macrophages in a dose-dependent manner (p < 0.01) (Fig 2A); however, it did not induce an increase in ROS production in non-infected macrophages, suggesting that such increase is specific to infected cells.
19.Rationale: The passage does state that "apigenin induces mitochondrial damage and the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS)", which supports that mitochondria are affected. However, it does not provide any evidence that this mitochondrial damage is irreversible or that it prevents recovery after treatment cessation. The data are limited to in vitro effects (72 h exposure) and in vivo efficacy during treatment; no longitudinal recovery or permanence of mitochondrial collapse after stopping treatment is reported. Therefore the specific claim of irreversible metabolic collapse and prevention of recovery after cessation is not supported by the passage.

19.Option: ROS-dependent leishmanicidal activity that is amplified by the parasite's own metabolic response to oxidative stress, creating a self-reinforcing cycle of parasite death
Evidence: Apigenin induced ROS production in Leishmania-infected macrophages in a dose-dependent manner (p < 0.01) (Fig 2A); however, it did not induce an increase in ROS production in non-infected macrophages, suggesting that such increase is specific to infected cells.
These data suggest that apigenin-induced leishmanicidal activity occurs at least in part through the production of ROS.
The linear correlation (R2 = 0.9306) observed between the percent inhibition of the infection index and ROS production by apigenin reinforces this hypothesis (Fig 2B).
19.Rationale: The passage clearly supports that apigenin's leishmanicidal activity is ROS-dependent and that there is a strong correlation between ROS production and parasite inhibition. However, it does not state or provide evidence that the parasite's own metabolic response amplifies ROS production or that a self-reinforcing cycle driven by the parasite occurs. The idea that the parasite amplifies ROS generation (creating a positive feedback loop) is not described in the text, so the additional mechanistic claim about parasite-driven amplification is unsupported by the passage.

19.Option: The dose-dependent reduction in infection index indicates that apigenin maintains consistent efficacy across repeated dosing cycles, suggesting a stable pharmacodynamic profile
Evidence: Apigenin reduced the infection index in a dose-dependent manner (p < 0.001) with an IC50 value of 4.3 μM, and inhibited the growth of L. amazonensis by 71% after 72 h at the highest dose tested (12 μM).
The experiments were performed thrice.
19.Rationale: The passage documents a dose-dependent reduction in infection index in vitro and reports an IC50, which demonstrates potency and a dose-response relationship over a 72-hour exposure. However, there is no data on 'repeated dosing cycles' or long-term pharmacodynamic stability across multiple treatment rounds. The in vivo experiments show efficacy with daily oral dosing to day 45, but they do not specifically demonstrate maintenance of identical efficacy across repeated cycles or prove a stable pharmacodynamic profile over prolonged treatment beyond the reported experiments. Thus the extrapolation to consistent efficacy across repeated dosing cycles is not directly supported by the provided evidence.

19.Option: Induction of host autophagy, a self-sustaining cellular defense mechanism, reduces the likelihood of parasite resistance development during prolonged treatment
Evidence: Leishmania-infected macrophages treated with apigenin exhibited an increase in double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like membrane inclusions, characteristic of autophagosomes.

treatment induced a remarkable increase in the number of double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like membrane inclusions within macrophages, characteristics of autophagic pathway (Fig 5A and 5B); additionally, these structures were found to be co-localized with L. amazonensis amastigotes (Fig 5; panel B and panel E).
Autophagy is another mechanism of defense against intracellular pathogens.

Therefore, it can be postulated that the effects observed following treatment of L. amazonensis-infected macrophages with apigenin occur through the activation of NOX, generating ROS and leading to an increase in autophagy.
19.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that apigenin induces host autophagy (increased autophagosomes co-localized with amastigotes) and links ROS/NOX activation to autophagic induction. Host-directed mechanisms such as augmentation of autophagy operate via the host cell rather than directly targeting parasite-encoded proteins, so they are less likely to create direct selective pressure on parasite genes that would drive classical drug resistance. Thus induction of host autophagy is the feature in the passage that most directly supports the concept of sustained efficacy under prolonged treatment because it harnesses a host defense pathway (autophagy) rather than relying solely on a parasite-targeted action that the parasite could more readily mutate or evade.

19.Option: Oral bioavailability combined with a high selectivity index ensures that therapeutic concentrations are maintained in target tissues over time, minimizing the risk of resistance
Evidence: Apigenin presented great probabilities (98.9% and 85.4%) for human intestinal absorption (HIA) and Caco-2 cell permeability, respectively.
The IC50 value of apigenin against murine macrophages was 78.7 μM, which corresponds to a selectivity index of 18.2.
Taken together, these data suggest that apigenin is safe and orally absorbed.
19.Rationale: The passage supports that apigenin has predicted oral absorption and a favorable selectivity index, which are important for feasibility and safety of prolonged oral therapy. However, it does not provide pharmacokinetic or tissue-concentration data demonstrating that therapeutic concentrations are actually 'maintained in target tissues over time.' Nor does the passage directly link oral bioavailability/selectivity to a minimized risk of resistance development. While good oral bioavailability and selectivity facilitate sustained dosing, they do not by themselves ensure maintained tissue concentrations or reduced resistance risk; those require pharmacokinetic data and mechanistic arguments about selective pressure, which are not provided. Therefore this option overstates what the passage supports.

20.Option: Differences in symptom severity between socioeconomic groups may be masked by the study’s reliance on physical examination alone
Evidence: A key limitation, of this study is the sole reliance on physical examination of symptoms by non-medical persons which may have lowered the precision of diagnosis.
There were no statistically significant differences in the results (Table 5).
20.Rationale: While the passage does acknowledge a limitation that "sole reliance on physical examination... may have lowered the precision of diagnosis," this note is framed as a general study limitation rather than presented as the most likely explanation for the observed disparity in productivity losses. The authors emphasize economic and occupational explanations (e.g., subsistence farming dependence and absence of risk-pooling) as drivers of greater burden among the poorest. Therefore, although misclassification could conceivably mask real symptom severity differences, the passage points to socioeconomic mechanisms as the primary explanations, making this option unlikely to be the most likely factor according to the study.

20.Option: Wealthier households may have greater access to treatment, reducing the duration of illness and thus minimizing productivity loss
Evidence: Overall, only about 14% of respondents sought treatment in the one month preceding the survey, 16% in the one year preceding survey and more than half (60%) had never sought any treatment for their condition.
The table also shows that about half (54.2%) of those that sought treatment did so from a private facility.
20.Rationale: The passage reports low overall treatment-seeking and that about half who sought care used private facilities, but it does not provide direct evidence that wealthier households had greater access to or use of treatment, nor that differences in treatment access by SES explain the productivity gap. The authors focus instead on dependence on subsistence work and lack of financial protection as explanations. Because the passage lacks explicit data linking higher SES to greater treatment access and reduced productivity loss, this option is not supported as the most likely explanation.

20.Option: Wealthier households may have better access to alternative income sources that reduce the perceived need to work during illness
Evidence: Subsistence farming provides employment for over 85% of the population in the community.

The average monthly cost of productivity loss of US$19.5 from patients and caregivers could be considered a huge economic burden on the affected individuals and their families, largely subsistence farmers whose consumption depends largely on their productive ability.
20.Rationale: The passage documents that most people are subsistence farmers and that poorer households depend on productive ability for consumption, which implies poorer households are highly vulnerable to lost work. However, the text does not provide direct evidence that wealthier households have alternative income sources that buffer them from missing work. Because the study emphasizes the reliance of the community on subsistence farming and the disproportionate burden on the poorest, the specific claim that wealthier households have alternative incomes reducing the need to work during illness is not substantiated by the provided quotes and thus is not supported as the most likely explanation.

20.Option: Poorer households may have more physically demanding work that increases the likelihood of missing work due to illness
Evidence: Subsistence farming provides employment for over 85% of the population in the community.
Patients in the poorest (Q1) and the third (Q3) quintile groups missed work for greater number of days (4 days) than the rest of the SES groups.
Our findings also raises some equity concerns in both productivity losses and associated costs, given that lost productivity was greater among the poorest socio-economic.
20.Rationale: The passage indicates a predominance of subsistence farming in the community and explicitly reports that patients in the poorest quintiles missed more work and had greater productivity losses. Physically demanding subsistence farming makes it harder to continue work when symptomatic, explaining why poorer households would miss more days despite similar measured clinical manifestations. This occupational explanation is directly supported by the text and is a plausible primary driver of the observed disparity.

20.Option: Lack of financial protection mechanisms amplifies the economic impact of productivity loss on poorer households
Evidence: The absence of risk-pooling mechanisms in Nigeria to protect households from financial shocks that could arise from common healthcare expenditures mean that households and more importantly, poorer households are likely to experience a disproportionate burden of the disease.

The average monthly cost of productivity loss of US$19.5 from patients and caregivers could be considered a huge economic burden on the affected individuals and their families, largely subsistence farmers whose consumption depends largely on their productive ability.
20.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the absence of risk-pooling/financial protection mechanisms increases the disproportionate burden on poorer households. Combined with the finding that lost productivity represents a sizable monthly cost and that many households are subsistence farmers, lack of financial protection directly amplifies the economic impact of lost work for the poorest. This structural financial vulnerability is clearly presented in the study as a likely cause of the observed disparity.

21.Option: The conservation of the hco-miR-5352 cluster across multiple parasitic nematodes suggests a shared mechanism for host immune evasion that could enable cross-species transmission
Evidence: BLASTN searches of the WormBase ParaSite database (parasite.wormbase.org) identified homologues of the miR-5352 cluster in an additional ten clade V nematode species: Teladorsagia circumcincta, Oesophagostomum dentatum, Nippostrongylus brasiliensis, Necator americanus, Strongylus vulgaris, Heligmosomoides polygyrus, Cylicostephanus goldi, Ancylostoma duodenale, Ancylostoma ceylanicum and Dictyocaulus viviparus.

For all four miRNAs, there was significant identity between the precursor sequences across all nine species, ranging from 61.9 to 78.8% pairwise identity. The identity increased to over 95% when only the mature sequences were considered.
This suggests there is some conservation of the repertoire of nematode miRNAs packaged into EVs.
21.Rationale: The passage documents clear conservation of the hco-miR-5352 cluster across multiple parasitic nematodes, which supports the idea of an evolutionarily conserved function. However, conservation alone does not provide direct evidence that H. contortus can infect humans or that conservation 'enables cross-species transmission.' The text does not report human infections or demonstrate that the conserved miRNAs permit host range expansion. Therefore, while conservation is relevant background (and may suggest similar host interactions in different host species), it does not by itself most strongly support classification as a zoonotic agent with spillover potential.

21.Option: The presence of multiple miRNA sequences in excretory-secretory products indicates a mechanism for inter-species molecular communication that could facilitate spillover to humans
Evidence: An interesting aspect of miRNA research stems from the finding that small RNA species can be secreted from cells and are detectable in the circulation
In all these worms, miRNAs were shown to be released into the excretory-secretory (ES) products collected in vitro.
In some cases, specific miRNAs were also detected in the serum of infected patients or animals suggesting that some helminth parasites release miRNAs in vivo as well as in vitro [13].

In order to characterise the complement of small RNA species present in H. contortus ES, a small RNA library was constructed from adult worm ES. Over 15 million reads were sequenced and mapped to previously identified H. contortus RNA sequences [5].
Of these 195 accepted H. contortus microRNAs, 85 were found in the adult ES small RNA library.

These data suggest that there may be selectivity in the release of miRNAs by adult worms and that some miRNAs could have the potential to interact directly with host cells rather than functioning within the parasite.
21.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that H. contortus releases many miRNAs in its excretory-secretory (ES) products and that secreted miRNAs from parasites can be detectable in host circulation for other helminths. Secreted miRNAs (including those packaged in EVs) are a plausible molecular mechanism for communicating with host cells and modulating host responses across species boundaries. Although detection in ES does not prove human infection, the presence of parasite-derived molecules that can act on host cells is a biologically plausible feature that supports potential inter-species effects and thus is a strong indicator relevant to spillover risk at the human-animal-environment interface.

21.Option: The detection of parasite miRNAs in abomasal tissue and lymph nodes indicates that the parasite can disseminate systemically, enabling immune modulation in distant organs and increasing spillover risk
Evidence: To determine whether Haemonchus miRNAs could be detected in tissue from infected sheep, RNA was extracted from small pieces of abomasal tissue or from the abomasal lymph nodes dissected from H. contortus infected and pathogen-free sheep at post-mortem.

For abomasal tissue samples and particularly the lymph node, hco-miR-5352 could be detected at a greater level in infected relative to uninfected animals (P<0.0001), while hco-miR-5895 gave no signal from tissue from uninfected animals but was detected in infected tissue samples (P<0.001).

Elevated levels of both cluster miRNAs were detected in ex vivo tissue from H. contortus infected sheep, indicating that these miRNAs are secreted in vivo and that they have the potential to interact with and influence the function of cells within both the site of infection and regional lymph nodes.
21.Rationale: The passage supports detection of parasite miRNAs in abomasal tissue and draining lymph nodes, indicating local secretion and interaction with regional immune tissue. However, it does not provide evidence of systemic dissemination to distant organs; the data are limited to the infection site and regional lymph nodes. The claim that this 'indicates the parasite can disseminate systemically' and thereby increases spillover risk to humans overstates the findings. Therefore this option is incorrect because it extrapolates beyond the supported observations.

21.Option: The detection of parasite-derived miRNAs in host draining lymph nodes suggests systemic dissemination and potential for host immune modulation across species boundaries
Evidence: To determine whether Haemonchus miRNAs could be detected in tissue from infected sheep, RNA was extracted from small pieces of abomasal tissue or from the abomasal lymph nodes dissected from H. contortus infected and pathogen-free sheep at post-mortem.
For abomasal tissue samples and particularly the lymph node, hco-miR-5352 could be detected at a greater level in infected relative to uninfected animals (P<0.0001)...
the signal from the draining lymph node in infected sheep was significantly higher than that from the abomasal tissue (P = 1.59E-05).

Elevated levels of both cluster miRNAs were detected in ex vivo tissue from H. contortus infected sheep, indicating that these miRNAs are secreted in vivo and that they have the potential to interact with and influence the function of cells within both the site of infection and regional lymph nodes.
These results indicate that the presence of the cluster may relate to life in the gut or at a mucosal site.
21.Rationale: The passage documents detection of parasite miRNAs in the host's draining lymph nodes and highlights that levels were higher in lymph nodes than at the local abomasal site. This provides direct in vivo evidence that parasite-derived small RNAs reach host immune tissue and therefore have the capacity to modulate host immune responses. While 'systemic dissemination' to many organs is not definitively shown, the presence in lymph nodes (a central immune organ for antigen processing and immune modulation) supports the biological plausibility that H. contortus secretions could influence host immunity beyond the immediate gut microenvironment and, importantly, that these molecular interactions could operate across species boundaries—features relevant to spillover potential at the human-animal-environment interface. Thus this feature strongly supports concern about cross-species immune-modulatory interactions.

21.Option: The stage-specific expression of miRNAs in adult worms indicates that the parasite is adapted to exploit host immune responses, increasing the likelihood of zoonotic transmission
Evidence: The hco-miR-5352 cluster is most abundant in female worms

Minimal expression of the hco-miR-5352 cluster sequences was observed in L3 stages (both sheathed L3 and activated L3 ...), the L4 stage and in adult female gut tissue. In contrast, all four miRNAs in the cluster were abundant in adult worms, with females showing higher levels of each miRNA compared to male worms (Fig 1),

These data imply that the cluster is likely to be co-ordinately expressed and may indicate a stage-specific function for these small RNAs within adult worms or in interacting with their immediate environment.
21.Rationale: The passage clearly shows stage-specific (adult/female-enriched) expression of the miR-5352 cluster, suggesting a role tied to adult-stage biology or reproduction. However, inferring that such stage-specific expression 'increases the likelihood of zoonotic transmission' is speculative and not supported by the data. Stage-specific adaptation to exploit host immunity does not equate to proven ability to infect humans or to spill over; the passage lacks evidence of human infection or host-range expansion. Therefore this option is not the best-supported feature for classifying the parasite as zoonotic.

22.Option: Fit a hurdle model with a Poisson distribution for the count component to model infection intensity, assuming that the variance equals the mean among infected individuals
Evidence: The standard Poisson distribution, which assumes equal mean and variance, commonly employed to model such count data, is inappropriate to fit observed egg counts since the variance of the counts is much larger than their mean, a case known as over-dispersion [8].

With regards to zero capturing, the Poisson model was again not appropriate as it could only capture 515 of the zeros whereas the NB-Zero adjusted based models were much better in capturing the zero counts.
22.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the Poisson distribution's equal-mean-variance assumption is inappropriate for these egg-count data because they are over-dispersed. Although a hurdle structure separates zeros and positives, using a Poisson for the count component would fail to accommodate the observed over-dispersion and was shown empirically to perform poorly at capturing zeros. Therefore a Poisson-count hurdle is not the best choice.

22.Option: Use a negative binomial regression model with an offset term for exposure time to adjust for varying contact with contaminated water, while treating all zero counts as missing data
Evidence: Although NB models may be ideal for over-dispersion, they may not be suitable when data is zero-inflated. Other distributions like the hurdle models or zero inflated (ZI) or zero augmented models that may be more appropriate for modeling data with such excess zeros are reported [8].
This paper demonstrates the applications of hurdle models to helminth epidemiology (S. haematobium) ... Its advantage is that it allows joint modeling of infection status and intensity.

Since NB logit hurdle model offered the best fit to zero inflated helminth data in terms of the AIC (minimum value for all the models fitted) as well as true zero count capturing, it therefore became a natural choice for fitting a final model to model helminth infection intensity and determination of factors that foster infections.
22.Rationale: A plain negative binomial regression addresses over-dispersion but does not by itself handle excess zeros or preserve infection status and intensity as distinct processes. Treating zeros as missing would discard information and bias inference; the passage recommends two-part (hurdle) approaches that explicitly model both prevalence (infection status) and intensity rather than ignoring zeros.

22.Option: Use a negative binomial logit hurdle model to separately model infection status and infection intensity while accounting for over-dispersion and zero inflation
Evidence: we assumed a negative binomial logit hurdle (NBLH) model for joint analysis of infection prevalence and intensity of Schistosomiasis hematobium in Malawi.
The NB model is suited for count data with over-dispersion.

The idea for a hurdle model, a modified count model in which the two processes generating the zeros and the positives are not constrained to be the same, was developed in [14]. The two processes are modeled using a mixture of two models (i.e, two part or a hurdle model). The first part is a binary outcome model, and the second part is a truncated count model.

This paper demonstrates the applications of hurdle models to helminth epidemiology (S. haematobium) and encourage its wider application in helminth disease control programmes. Its advantage is that it allows joint modeling of infection status and intensity.
Using the AIC and zero capturing, the predicted counts using the NBLH indicate a closer fit with the observed values.

The NBLH model captured 971 zeros which were equal to the observed (Table 3). Since NB logit hurdle model offered the best fit to zero inflated helminth data in terms of the AIC (minimum value for all the models fitted) as well as true zero count capturing, it therefore became a natural choice for fitting a final model to model helminth infection intensity and determination of factors that foster infections.
22.Rationale: The passage explicitly proposes and implements the negative binomial logit hurdle (NBLH) model for these data. The NBLH handles over-dispersion via the NB count component and treats zeros and positives in a two-part framework (logit for infection probability, truncated NB for intensity), preserving interpretability of infection status and intensity and showing superior fit and zero-capturing in the study.

22.Option: Use a zero-inflated Poisson (ZIP) model with a binary logistic regression for infection status and a Poisson regression for intensity, assuming that the number of eggs follows a Poisson distribution among infected individuals
Evidence: In [8], it was concluded that the ZIP models were inadequate for the data as there was still evidence of over-dispersion.

ZI models attempt to account for excess zeros, i.e., zero inflation arises when one mechanism generates only zeros and the other process generates both zero and nonzero counts hence they can be expressed as a two-component mixture model where one component has a degenerate distribution at zero and the other is a count model [11].

The standard Poisson distribution, which assumes equal mean and variance, commonly employed to model such count data, is inappropriate to fit observed egg counts since the variance of the counts is much larger than their mean, a case known as over-dispersion [8].
22.Rationale: Although a ZIP model separates an excess-zero process from a count process, using a Poisson count component is inappropriate here because the data are over-dispersed. The passage specifically notes that ZIP models were inadequate for these helminth data due to remaining over-dispersion, so ZIP would not be the best approach.

22.Option: Apply a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) model to account for excess zeros and over-dispersion, assuming that all zeros arise from a single process of non-exposure
Evidence: ZINB models assume that the number of worms among those who are at risk of infection has a negative binomial distribution [6].

ZI models attempt to account for excess zeros, i.e., zero inflation arises when one mechanism generates only zeros and the other process generates both zero and nonzero counts hence they can be expressed as a two-component mixture model where one component has a degenerate distribution at zero and the other is a count model [11]. ZI models estimate two equations, one for the count model and one for the excess zeros. ZI models assume that a proportion of individuals have no chance to be infected, as they are not exposed.

This paper demonstrates the applications of hurdle models to helminth epidemiology (S. haematobium) and encourage its wider application in helminth disease control programmes. Its advantage is that it allows joint modeling of infection status and intensity.
Using the AIC and zero capturing, the predicted counts using the NBLH indicate a closer fit with the observed values.

Since NB logit hurdle model offered the best fit to zero inflated helminth data in terms of the AIC (minimum value for all the models fitted) as well as true zero count capturing, it therefore became a natural choice for fitting a final model to model helminth infection intensity and determination of factors that foster infections.
22.Rationale: While ZINB does handle both excess zeros and over-dispersion and is discussed in the passage, the option's wording is flawed ('all zeros arise from a single process of non-exposure' is inconsistent with the ZI two-component mixture that allows some zeros from the count process as well). More importantly, the authors found the negative binomial logit hurdle model to offer superior fit, clearer separation of infection status and intensity, and better zero-capturing in these data, making ZINB a less preferable choice in this context.

23.Option: The detection of parasite DNA by qPCR is due to the presence of a mixed infection with multiple Discrete Typing Units (DTUs) that are resistant to Nifurtimox
Evidence: For the amplification of the mini-exon gene, we were able to characterize 46% (25/54) of qPCR positive samples from the 62 patients in pre-treatment. Of these 25 samples: 22 were identified as TcI, 2 were identified as being one or more of DTUs Tc II to VI (TcII-TcVI), and 1 being from both the group Tc I and from the group of Tc II to Tc VI (mixed).
The biological and genetic characteristics of the parasite can affect treatment efficacy.
Studies in Colombia suggest the existence of circulating strains of T. cruzi that are naturally resistant to BNZ [44].
The differences in proportions of Tc I and Tc II-VI in pre-treatment and post-treatment samples suggest that the genetics of the parasite play an important role in therapeutic response.
23.Rationale: The passage does describe mixed DTUs and notes that parasite genetics can influence treatment response and resistance (mostly citing BNZ resistance). However, this explanation is presented as one of several possible (a third potential explanation) and the passage does not single mixed, resistant DTUs out as the most likely cause of persistent qPCR positivity after treatment. The authors emphasize other explanations (reinfection and persistence of DNA from lysed parasites) more directly and provide data arguing incidence alone cannot explain all positives. Thus while mixed DTUs/resistance is plausible for some cases, the passage does not support this as the most likely overall explanation.

23.Option: Detection of parasite DNA in qPCR likely reflects remnants from lysed parasites rather than ongoing active infection
Evidence: A second explanation is the persistence of T. cruzi DNA from lysed parasites.

Given that the incidence of T.cruzi infection is unlikely to be that high to explain all positive qPCR cases as a consequence of reinfection, and that antibody titers and parasite load decreased significantly after treatment, the results also suggest that NFX had indeed a therapeutic effect, but only a small part achieved a complete elimination of T.cruzi DNA during the whole follow-up period (6 to 30 months).

Similar results were found by Solari et al. (2011) in a study also with NFX in children. They found that all the patients slowly converted from positive to negative PCR tests after two years follow-up, suggesting that the clearance of T.cruzi DNA is a slow process than can take months or years after treatment [34].
They have proposed a potential explanation that this DNA corresponds not to live but to lysed parasites from infected cells [34].
23.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies persistence of parasite DNA from lysed parasites as a principal explanation and supports it with reasoning and external study findings (Solari et al.) showing slow PCR clearance after effective treatment. The authors argue that because parasitemia and antibody titers fell significantly (suggesting drug effect) and incidence alone is unlikely to explain all positives, residual DNA from killed parasites is the most consistent explanation for persistent qPCR positivity in many patients.

23.Option: Persistent qPCR positivity is most likely due to reinfection with Trypanosoma cruzi from ongoing exposure to infected triatomine vectors in the home environment
Evidence: A first explanation is reinfection.

For the survival analysis, several variables were evaluated, whereas the presence of bad housing characteristics (thatched roof, dirt floor and mud walls) represented shorter survival time (time to positive test) with a cumulative survival of 50% (95%CI: 32.3–67.7) versus only 17.2% (95%CI: 16.5–17.9) for children living in good-housing conditions. This difference was statistically significant (log-rank test, p = 0.03) (Fig. 6).
Two of the patients continued to show positive blood cultures for T. rangeli at 6, and 12 months post-treatment.
High densities of infected insects in the palms constantly intrude homes, especially at night, and therefore, people living in infested homes may be reinfected [41].
23.Rationale: The passage supports reinfection as a plausible contributor and provides epidemiologic evidence (association of poor housing with shorter time to positive qPCR and detection of T. rangeli indicating ongoing transmission). However, the authors explicitly state that incidence is unlikely to be high enough to explain all persistent qPCR positives and therefore treat reinfection as a partial, not the predominant, explanation. Because the question asks for the factor 'most likely' explaining persistence despite reductions, reinfection is an important contributor but not presented as the single most likely cause in the passage.

23.Option: The persistence of qPCR positivity reflects a lack of treatment efficacy, as Nifurtimox failed to eliminate the parasite in a substantial proportion of patients
Evidence: A third potential explanation is resistance to treatment or a lack of anti-parasitic activity.
The biological and genetic characteristics of the parasite can affect treatment efficacy.

The results show for the first time, the therapeutic response and safety of NFX treatment for T. cruzi infection in a population of school-age children in the asymptomatic chronic phase of Chagas disease in Colombia.

In the current study, during the follow-up a significant reduction in the antibody titers was observed, which was faster and more substantial for ELISA than for IFAT. Likewise, the proportion of patients with positive qPCR pretreatment (88.7%) was dramatically reduced at 30 months post-treatment (11.2%), and the parasitic load was significantly reduced over the follow-up.
23.Rationale: While the authors acknowledge that resistance or incomplete drug activity could account for some persistent qPCR positivity (and indicate parasite genetics may influence response), the passage reports clear, substantial reductions in parasitemia and serological titers after NFX treatment — evidence of therapeutic effect. The authors therefore consider lack of efficacy/resistance a possible but not the primary or most likely explanation for the persistent qPCR signal overall; they emphasize residual DNA from lysed parasites (and reinfection for some cases) as more consistent with the observed pattern.

24.Option: High sensitivity of 80% ensures that nearly all infected individuals are detected, minimizing false negatives in population screening
Evidence: C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.

C9 showed the best results for AUC (0.871), with sensitivity of 87.5%, specificity of 80% and diagnostic accuracy of 82.5%, followed by clones C10 and D3, which also had lower cross-reactivity with serum samples from patients with other parasites.
24.Rationale: The passage reports sensitivity around 80–87.5% for C9 (values are reported in different places), which is moderate but not "nearly all" infected individuals. Thus the statement that 80% sensitivity "ensures that nearly all infected individuals are detected" overstates what the data show. For population screening, higher sensitivity would be preferable to minimize false negatives; the quoted sensitivity does not justify the absolute claim in the option.

24.Option: The high diagnostic efficiency of 82.5% indicates that C9 can reliably distinguish between infected and non-infected individuals in diverse epidemiological settings
Evidence: C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.
The restricted sample size used for Trichuris sp. and Giardia lamblia in our cross-reactivity assays can be considered as a weakness of this study;
24.Rationale: While C9's diagnostic efficiency (DE ≈ 82.5%) does indicate good performance in the study sample, the passage also notes limitations in the cross-reactivity evaluation (restricted sample sizes for some parasites). Therefore the claim that this DE alone indicates reliability "in diverse epidemiological settings" is not fully supported by the passage. Generalizability to diverse settings would require broader validation; the existing DE supports potential utility but does not by itself guarantee reliable performance across diverse epidemiological contexts.

24.Option: The use of a heterologous antigen from S. venezuelensis in the competitive assay confirms that C9 mimics authentic S. stercoralis epitopes, enhancing its reliability for population studies
Evidence: each phage clone was partially inhibited in a dose dependent manner when serum of S. stercoralis patient was pre-incubated with saline extract from S. venezuelensis (heterologous antigen).

we observed a partial reduction of the reactivity of clones in a dose-dependent manner, allowing us to affirm that the phage clones selected by phage display technology may mimic epitopes present in the total antigenic extract from S. venezuelensis
24.Rationale: The competitive Phage-ELISA showed dose-dependent inhibition by S. venezuelensis extract, and the authors explicitly conclude the phage clones "may mimic epitopes" present in the heterologous extract. Demonstrating that C9 is an authentic epitope mimotope supports its biological relevance and therefore increases confidence in its reliability for sero-epidemiologic (population-level immunity) studies.

24.Option: High specificity with minimal cross-reactivity, particularly to hookworm, enables accurate seroprevalence estimation in endemic populations
Evidence: C9 was the only clone that did not cross-react with hookworm patient sera and recognized few other species of parasites sampled.
C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.
the low cross-reactivity observed for the C9 clone, with satisfactory specificity (80%), suggest its use as a promising antigen in the serodiagnosis of strongyloidiasis
24.Rationale: For estimating population-level seroprevalence, specificity and low cross-reactivity are critical to avoid false positives (especially in co-endemic areas). The passage highlights that C9 had minimal cross-reactivity (notably did not cross-react with hookworm) and satisfactory specificity, which directly supports its utility for accurate seroprevalence estimation in endemic populations.

24.Option: The ability to detect IgG in both symptomatic and asymptomatic individuals supports its use in identifying latent infections during population surveys
Evidence: Strongyloidiasis is frequently under diagnosed since many infections remain asymptomatic and conventional diagnostic tests based on parasitological examination are not sufficiently sensitive,

All phage clones showed ability to distinguish S. stercoralis infected patients from non-infected individuals (p<0.0001), with an AUC higher than 0.8, which represents a good accuracy of diagnostic test.
24.Rationale: Although the passage notes that infections can be asymptomatic and that the phage clones distinguish infected from non-infected individuals, it does not provide explicit evidence that C9 specifically detects IgG in asymptomatic (latent) infections versus only in clinically confirmed cases. Group III comprised "apparently healthy individuals," but the study does not demonstrate that C9 reliably identifies latent infections in asymptomatic persons. Therefore the claim is not directly supported by the passage.

25.Option: The fact that no patient required granulocyte colony-stimulating factor or broad-spectrum antibiotics despite severe neutropenia suggests that the immune system can compensate for low ANC without intervention
Evidence: They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics.
No patients were given hematopoietic growth factors or broad-spectrum antibiotics, even though three patients were older than 65 years, and one patient had an ANC of 0.04×109/L.
Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
No patients experienced life-threatening complications.
25.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that even patients with very low ANC (e.g., ANC of 0.04×109/L) were managed with supportive care rather than G-CSF or prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics and recovered rapidly without life-threatening complications. That clinical observation supports the interpretation that severe neutropenia in this cohort did not necessitate aggressive immune-supporting interventions, implying that severity of ANC nadir alone did not drive severe complications.

25.Option: The rapid normalization of ANC after discontinuation of benzylpenicillin, even in patients with severe neutropenia, indicates that the duration of neutropenia, not its severity, determines complication risk
Evidence: Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
For the majority (13/14) of patients, the duration of treatment before onset of neutropenia ranged from 10 to 14 days, and the cumulative dose of benzylpenicillin varied from 240 to 324 megaunits.
No patients experienced life-threatening complications.
25.Rationale: While the passage documents rapid recovery of ANC after stopping benzylpenicillin, it does not provide evidence that duration rather than severity determines complication risk. Rapid normalization shows reversibility of drug-induced neutropenia, but it does not prove that duration is the primary driver of risk; severity could still matter in other contexts. The statement in the option is a causal inference not directly supported by the provided data.

25.Option: The lack of metabolic disorders or comorbidities in patients with severe neutropenia suggests that the absence of underlying disease, not ANC nadir, is the primary determinant of favorable outcomes
Evidence: Among the 14 NS patients with acute neutropenia in this study, none had metabolic disorders, severe infections, septicemia or septic shock.
No patients experienced life-threatening complications.
Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
25.Rationale: The passage notes that the cohort with neutropenia lacked metabolic disorders or severe comorbidities and that no life-threatening complications occurred even among those with severe neutropenia. This supports the view that absence of underlying disease or other high-risk conditions likely contributed to favorable outcomes, implying that factors other than the ANC nadir (such as comorbidities) are important determinants of complication risk.

25.Option: The presence of fever in patients with mild neutropenia, but not in those with severe neutropenia, indicates that symptom severity correlates with ANC level rather than infection status
Evidence: According to previous reports, acute neutropenia was often well tolerated and normalized rapidly [2]. In our study, fever accompanied by general malaise was the first and often the only manifestation in patients.

Another two patients (case 1 with general paresis and case 13 with syphilitic meningitis) did well until they had fever, and repeat CBC revealed thrombocytopenia and severe neutropenia (ANC of case 1: 0.04×109/L; ANC of case 13: 0.21×109/L) near the end of therapy.
25.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that fever occurred only in mild neutropenia; in fact, two patients with severe neutropenia manifested fever. Thus the statement is contradicted by the text. Fever was a common manifestation across severities, so this option is not supported.

25.Option: The absence of life-threatening infections despite severe neutropenia suggests that underlying infection risk, not ANC nadir, determines complication severity
Evidence: No patients experienced life-threatening complications.
Among the 14 NS patients with acute neutropenia in this study, none had metabolic disorders, severe infections, septicemia or septic shock.
Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
It also normalized rapidly without aggressive management for those with severe neutropenia after withdrawing anti-neurosyphilis regimen.
