The experts highlighted that the large gap between supply and demand for CL in most of these countries may be associated with lack of funding for CL and political commitment. It is also due to frequent outbreaks of CL, which makes forecasting unreliable.
115.Rationale: While the passage links lack of funding and political commitment to gaps between supply and demand, it frames this as a driver of procurement and funding shortfalls rather than a direct cause of unreliable reported case numbers. The text does not state that lack of political commitment causes inconsistent case reporting; therefore this option is less directly supported than underreporting itself.

115.Option: Inconsistent use of treatment guidelines across countries leads to variability in case reporting, reducing the reliability of data for program evaluation
Evidence: We learned from expert consultations that mismatch is possibly due to underreporting of cases, unexpected outbreaks, changes in treatment guidelines and overstock or stockout of products from previous years.
115.Rationale: The passage notes that changes in treatment guidelines can contribute to mismatches between treatments delivered and reported cases, but it presents guideline changes as affecting demand/usage patterns rather than directly degrading the reliability of reported case counts. The statement about inconsistent guidelines affecting case reporting is not directly made, so this option is not the most directly supported factor.

115.Option: Overstock or stockout of medicines from previous years distorts case reporting, leading to inaccurate estimates of treatment needs
Evidence: We learned from expert consultations that mismatch is possibly due to underreporting of cases, unexpected outbreaks, changes in treatment guidelines and overstock or stockout of products from previous years.
Some of the differences observed in supply and demand imbalance may be the result of quality of procurement data as well as underreporting of leishmaniasis cases.
115.Rationale: The passage identifies overstock or stockout as one factor contributing to mismatches between reported cases and procurements (supply-demand imbalance). However, overstock/stockout affects the supply side and procurement records rather than directly undermining the accuracy of reported case counts. Thus it is not the most direct influence on the reliability of reported case numbers.

115.Option: Frequent outbreaks of leishmaniasis lead to unreliable case data, making it difficult to estimate treatment needs accurately
Evidence: The experts highlighted that the large gap between supply and demand for CL in most of these countries may be associated with lack of funding for CL and political commitment. It is also due to frequent outbreaks of CL, which makes forecasting unreliable.

We learned from expert consultations that mismatch is possibly due to underreporting of cases, unexpected outbreaks, changes in treatment guidelines and overstock or stockout of products from previous years.
115.Rationale: The passage indicates that frequent or unexpected outbreaks make forecasting unreliable and contribute to mismatches between supply and demand. However, outbreaks cause temporal fluctuations that complicate forecasting rather than directly rendering reported case counts unreliable. The passage explicitly cites underreporting as the direct factor undermining reported case numbers, making outbreaks a secondary contributor to estimation difficulty rather than the primary one.

116.Option: The degree of IL-4Rα expression in macrophages dictates whether the immune response is protective or pathological, with lower expression always leading to reduced fibrosis
Evidence: IL-4Rα-responsive macrophages [25], pan-T cells [27] and smooth muscles cells [28] are individually essential for driving host survival and limiting tissue pathology during acute schistosomiasis.

Liver granuloma size (Fig 4J and 4K) and fibrosis (Fig 4L and 4M) were reduced in S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam2 similar to global IL-4Rα-/- mice, translating into a significantly reduced level of hepato- (Fig 4N) and splenomegaly (Fig 4O) compared with IL-4Rα-responsive control mice. As expected, from our previous mortality studies [25,27], body weights of S. mansoni-infected IL-4Rα-/- and iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam2 mice rapidly declined starting 6 weeks post-infection (Fig 4P) that preceded the death of these animals (Fig 4Q) when compared to IL-4Rα-responsive control mice.
Conversely, arginase expression by macrophages has been shown to counter tissue inflammation and fibrosis [38].
116.Rationale: While the passage notes an important role for IL-4Rα-responsive macrophages in survival and in modulating fibrosis ("IL-4Rα-responsive macrophages ... are individually essential" and "arginase expression by macrophages ... counter tissue inflammation and fibrosis"), it also shows that reduced IL-4Rα (including in macrophage-responsive contexts) does not always produce a beneficial outcome: early knockdown reduced granuloma size and fibrosis yet was associated with rapid weight loss and death (quotes above). Thus the statement that macrophage IL-4Rα level alone "dictates" outcome and that lower expression "always" leads to reduced fibrosis (and beneficial outcome) is not supported.

116.Option: The stage of infection determines whether IL-4Rα-mediated type 2 responses are protective or harmful, with early disruption causing death and late disruption improving outcomes
Evidence: Host survival requires IL-4Rα mediated signaling during acute but not chronic schistosomiasis
This indicated that IL-4Rα is necessary for host survival during acute schistosomiasis, but not required for host survival at the chronic phase of the disease.
Early IL-4Rα knockdown before egg production (week 2) exacerbates acute schistosomiasis

Liver granuloma size (Fig 4J and 4K) and fibrosis (Fig 4L and 4M) were reduced in S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam2 ... body weights ... rapidly declined ... that preceded the death of these animals

Tamoxifen-induced knockdown of the IL-4Rα after egg deposition during the chronic phase (16 weeks post-infection) ... IL-4Rα knockdown during chronic schistosomiasis did not lead to gut bleeding and did not affect animal viability but ameliorated liver pathology with reduced granuloma size and fibrosis
116.Rationale: The passage repeatedly contrasts outcomes after IL-4Rα disruption at different infection stages: knocking down IL-4Rα early (Tam2, Tam6) during the acute phase impaired type-2 responses and led to gut bleeding, rapid weight loss and premature death, whereas knockdown during chronic infection (Tam16) did not cause mortality and instead reduced granuloma size and fibrosis and improved liver pathology. These statements directly support that infection stage (timing of IL-4Rα disruption) is the dominant factor determining whether IL-4Rα-mediated type 2 responses are protective (in acute) or pathological (in chronic).

116.Option: The presence of regulatory T cells is the primary factor determining disease outcome, with higher levels always associated with improved survival and reduced pathology
Evidence: the frequencies of Foxp3+ regulatory T cell responses were increased in the MLNs of S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 7G and 7H), when compared with S. mansoni-infected IL-4Rα-/lox control mice.

Taken together, these results suggest that knocking down IL-4Rα at the chronic phase of experimental schistosomiasis considerably skews the host immune response away from the type 2 arm of the immune response, fosters a qualitatively more regulatory, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic profile with no deleterious effect on host survival.
Whether IL-4Rα mediated signaling causally dictates the anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic activities of these regulatory cells during chronic schistosomiasis is not known.

However, most likely as a result of total CD4+ T cell drop (S7A Fig), Treg cell numbers were reduced following Tam16 treatment in S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 animals when compared to their littermate controls (Fig 7I).
116.Rationale: Although regulatory T cells (Foxp3+) increase in frequency after chronic IL-4Rα knockdown and the authors associate a more regulatory profile with improved chronic pathology, the text explicitly cautions that causality is not established ("Whether IL-4Rα mediated signaling causally dictates ... is not known"). Moreover, total Treg numbers were reduced in some contexts because of overall CD4+ T cell loss. Therefore regulatory T cells are not demonstrated as the primary determinant, nor is the claim that "higher levels always associated with improved survival" supported.

116.Option: The level of parasite egg burden determines the severity of disease outcomes, with higher egg burdens leading to worse pathology regardless of IL-4Rα signaling status
Evidence: Liver egg burden was similar between the different groups (Fig 4I), ruling out a differential level of infection as the cause of the observed diminished type-2 responses in iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam2 and IL-4Rα-/- mice.

Liver egg burden was similar between the different groups (Fig 5I), ruling out a differential level of infection as the cause of the observed diminished type 2 responses in iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam6 and IL-4Rα-/- mice.
Liver egg burden was similar between the control IL-4Rα-/lox (Fig 6D), ruling out a differential level of infection between both groups of mice.
116.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that liver egg burden was similar across groups in multiple experiments and states this rules out differences in infection level as the explanation for altered immune responses or pathology. Thus egg burden did not explain the divergent outcomes after IL-4Rα knockdown; timing of IL-4Rα disruption, not differing egg loads, determined whether pathology worsened or improved. The assertion that egg burden is the primary determinant regardless of IL-4Rα status is therefore unsupported by the data.

116.Option: The presence of gut bleeding is the primary determinant of mortality in acute schistosomiasis, and its absence in chronic knockdown explains the improved outcomes
Evidence: Bleeding was visible in the gut of the animals that rapidly succumbed to infection following removal of IL-4Rα.

A more likely explanation for their premature death would be the intensive gut bleeding reported in our previous study on IL-4Rα deficient mice [25], and similarly observed in this study. As a result of the compromised gut integrity, bacteria would translocate to the blood stream and death by septic shock would ensue, as previously demonstrated [25].

S. mansoni-infected mice following IL-4Rα knockdown at 16 weeks post-infection, i.e. iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 6A) did not result in any weight loss (Fig 6B) or mortality (Fig 6C), for up to 24 weeks post infection.

IL-4Rα knockdown during chronic schistosomiasis did not lead to gut bleeding and did not affect animal viability but ameliorated liver pathology with reduced granuloma size and fibrosis in the liver and no visible scarification and reduced level of liver and spleen enlargement.
116.Rationale: The authors link gut bleeding after early IL-4Rα knockdown to subsequent bacterial translocation and septic shock as the probable cause of premature death (quoted). They also report that chronic-stage knockdown did not produce gut bleeding and that those mice had no mortality but improved pathology. Together the passage supports that gut bleeding is a proximate determinant of mortality in acute schistosomiasis in this model and that its absence in chronic knockdown helps explain improved survival and outcomes.

117.Option: Presence of identical alleles at multiple loci across all lineages, indicating a shared recent common ancestor
Evidence: The T. cruzi species displays a considerable genetic and phenotypic diversity [2], [3], which is likely to be the result of a predominantly clonal mode of evolution through large time spans [4], [5].

Lineages TcV and TcVI (which include the strain used for the first genomic sequence of T. cruzi, CL Brener) have a very high degree of heterozygosity but otherwise very homogeneous population structures with low intra-lineage diversity [16], [17].
The currently favored hypothesis suggest that these two lineages originated after either one or two independent hybridization events between strains of DTUs TcII and TcIII [17]–[19].
117.Rationale: The option asserts identical alleles across all lineages implying a shared recent ancestor, but the passage emphasizes considerable genetic diversity across T. cruzi and specifically highlights high heterozygosity in some lineages (TcV and TcVI) that is interpreted as evidence of hybrid origin. The cited lines attribute the origin of TcV/TcVI to hybridization rather than uniform identical alleles across lineages, so the option is not supported by the passage.

117.Option: High frequency of synonymous mutations across all loci, indicating neutral evolution without selection
Evidence: When analyzing the diversity in the TcSC5D gene, we observed a total of 81 fixed differences (70 synonymous, 11 non-synonymous), the majority of which were bi-allelic (changing one specific nucleotide base for another).

The two proposed markers (TcSC5D and TcMK) are both single copy genes, that are apparently under purifying selection, with a ratio of non-synonymous to synonymous changes of 0.16 and 0.39, respectively (data not shown for the TcMK gene).
The currently favored hypothesis suggest that these two lineages originated after either one or two independent hybridization events between strains of DTUs TcII and TcIII [17]–[19].
117.Rationale: Although the passage notes many synonymous changes in the TcSC5D locus, it also states these loci are under purifying selection and explicitly links the origin of TcV/TcVI to hybridization events. Synonymous mutation frequency alone does not provide the specific signature of hybrid origin; the passage points to heterozygosity and hybrid-specific patterns as the supporting features. Therefore this option does not best explain hybrid origin.

117.Option: Low heterozygosity with high inter-lineage diversity, suggesting long-term isolation and divergence
Evidence: Lineages TcV and TcVI (which include the strain used for the first genomic sequence of T. cruzi, CL Brener) have a very high degree of heterozygosity but otherwise very homogeneous population structures with low intra-lineage diversity [16], [17].
The currently favored hypothesis suggest that these two lineages originated after either one or two independent hybridization events between strains of DTUs TcII and TcIII [17]–[19].
117.Rationale: The option describes low heterozygosity, but the passage explicitly describes TcV/TcVI as having a very high degree of heterozygosity. The hybridization hypothesis is supported by high heterozygosity (consistent with combining distinct parental alleles) rather than low heterozygosity from long-term isolation. Thus this option contradicts the passage.

117.Option: High heterozygosity combined with loss of heterozygosity at multiple loci in otherwise genetically uniform lineages
Evidence: Lineages TcV and TcVI (which include the strain used for the first genomic sequence of T. cruzi, CL Brener) have a very high degree of heterozygosity but otherwise very homogeneous population structures with low intra-lineage diversity [16], [17].
The currently favored hypothesis suggest that these two lineages originated after either one or two independent hybridization events between strains of DTUs TcII and TcIII [17]–[19].

In the case of these DTUs, there are another 6 discriminant sites that separate strains from these DTUs, all of which represent cases of loss of heterozygosity (not shown), that can be exposed by Sanger-based sequencing of the amplification product.

The observation of so few discriminant sites between DTUs TcV and TcVI is not surprising, given the relatively recent origin of these DTUs [17], and because the majority of polymorphic sites in sequences from these DTUs are expected to be in heterozygosity, and shared by alleles from the TcII and TcIII DTUs (their proposed parental types) [14], [18], [19].
As mentioned, the only difference identified between strains from DTUs TcV and TcVI in each gene is a loss of heterozygosity in one SNP.
117.Rationale: The passage links high heterozygosity in TcV/TcVI to a hybrid origin (combining alleles from distinct parental DTUs TcII and TcIII) and also documents occurrences of loss of heterozygosity at specific loci (including differences between TcV and TcVI). This combination — extensive heterozygosity consistent with hybrid ancestry, together with locus-specific loss of heterozygosity in otherwise homogeneous lineages — is presented in the text as the hallmark supporting hybridization events. Therefore this option best matches the passage.

117.Option: High genetic diversity across multiple loci with no evidence of heterozygosity in the population
Evidence: The T. cruzi species displays a considerable genetic and phenotypic diversity [2], [3], which is likely to be the result of a predominantly clonal mode of evolution through large time spans [4], [5].
Lineages TcV and TcVI ... have a very high degree of heterozygosity but otherwise very homogeneous population structures with low intra-lineage diversity [16], [17].

The observation of so few discriminant sites between DTUs TcV and TcVI is not surprising, given the relatively recent origin of these DTUs [17], and because the majority of polymorphic sites in sequences from these DTUs are expected to be in heterozygosity, and shared by alleles from the TcII and TcIII DTUs (their proposed parental types) [14], [18], [19].
117.Rationale: While the species does show genetic diversity, the passage emphasizes heterozygosity (especially in TcV/TcVI) as a key signature of hybrid origin. The option's claim of no evidence of heterozygosity contradicts the passage's statements about high heterozygosity in hybrid lineages, so it does not support the hybridization hypothesis described.

118.Option: The high correlation between peptide and SGH responses indicates that the test is suitable for use in sheep, regardless of low positivity rates
Evidence: Results with sheep sera were difficult to interpret due to the very low positivity with SGH (10%).
Although the correlation with two peptides (OR24 P1 and OR65 P1) was above 0.7, we therefore do not recommend these peptides for screening sheep sera.
118.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that despite correlation >0.7, the authors do not recommend the peptides for screening sheep because of the very low SGH positivity (10%), which makes interpretation unstable. Thus high correlation alone does not make the test suitable when baseline seropositivity is very low.

118.Option: Low baseline exposure prevalence limits the ability to accurately assess sensitivity, even when specificity and predictive values are high
Evidence: Results with sheep sera were difficult to interpret due to the very low positivity with SGH (10%).

Similar positivity was found with all four peptides: even a relatively small change in the number of false positives or false negatives would significantly change calculation of PPV, NPV, specificity, or sensitivity.
With sheep sera, all statistical values were very high (> 0.9) except for the low sensitivity (0.25).
118.Rationale: The passage indicates that very low baseline positivity (10%) makes results difficult to interpret and that small changes in false positives/negatives markedly alter PPV/NPV/sensitivity/specificity. This directly supports that low prevalence (baseline exposure) limits reliable assessment of sensitivity even if some metrics appear high.

118.Option: Low sensitivity is acceptable in sheep because the high NPV indicates that negative results are trustworthy
Evidence: Results with sheep sera were difficult to interpret due to the very low positivity with SGH (10%).

Similar positivity was found with all four peptides: even a relatively small change in the number of false positives or false negatives would significantly change calculation of PPV, NPV, specificity, or sensitivity.
Although the correlation with two peptides (OR24 P1 and OR65 P1) was above 0.7, we therefore do not recommend these peptides for screening sheep sera.
118.Rationale: Although some metrics (e.g., NPV) may be high in the low-prevalence setting, the authors caution that low positivity makes these statistics unstable and that they do not recommend the peptides for sheep. Therefore asserting that low sensitivity is acceptable because NPV is high is contradicted by the authors' conclusion and the instability of predictive values in low prevalence.

118.Option: High specificity in the presence of low positivity rates ensures that the test is reliable for identifying truly exposed individuals
Evidence: Results with sheep sera were difficult to interpret due to the very low positivity with SGH (10%).

Similar positivity was found with all four peptides: even a relatively small change in the number of false positives or false negatives would significantly change calculation of PPV, NPV, specificity, or sensitivity.
Although the correlation with two peptides (OR24 P1 and OR65 P1) was above 0.7, we therefore do not recommend these peptides for screening sheep sera.
118.Rationale: High specificity can help reduce false positives, but the passage warns that in the context of very low positivity (prevalence), even small changes in counts have outsized effects on performance metrics and interpretation; the authors explicitly do not recommend the peptides for sheep despite some favorable metrics. Thus high specificity alone does not ensure reliable identification in this context.

118.Option: The use of recombinant proteins as a prior gold standard justifies the continued use of synthetic peptides in sheep, despite low sensitivity
Evidence: The optical density values of anti-SGH antibodies were used as the gold standard to validate peptides in ELISA tests using positive (PPV) and negative predictive values (NPV), sensitivity, and specificity.
Results with sheep sera were difficult to interpret due to the very low positivity with SGH (10%).
Although the correlation with two peptides (OR24 P1 and OR65 P1) was above 0.7, we therefore do not recommend these peptides for screening sheep sera.
However, promising results for sheep sera have previously been achieved with both recombinant proteins [21].
118.Rationale: While SGH (and previously tested recombinant proteins) serve as reference comparators, the authors do not justify continuing to use peptides for sheep given the very low positivity and problematic sensitivity. The passage explicitly states they do not recommend the peptides for sheep despite prior recombinant results, so prior use of recombinants does not justify peptide use when sensitivity is low.

120.Option: The detection of pathogenic Leptospira in Kandy but not in Girandurukotte is primarily due to differences in irrigation water chemistry, which supports the survival of virulent strains.
Evidence: differences in agro-ecological regions can impact on the diversity of Leptospira species, correlated environmental bacteria and reservoir animal species.
Irrigation water can be a reservoir and source of spreading urine-containing Leptospira.

Since the population density of cattle and buffalo is similar in both Kandy and Badulla districts, our results suggested that the greater number of sequence-reads detected in each sample is a consequence of different agro-ecological regions.
120.Rationale: The passage attributes the Kandy vs GK differences to broader agro-ecological factors (climate, soil, terrain, microbiome) and does not single out irrigation water chemistry as the primary cause. The text explicitly links differences to agro-ecological regions and to irrigation water acting as a reservoir, but it does not provide evidence that water chemistry is the main driver supporting virulent strains. Therefore the statement that the pattern is 'primarily due to differences in irrigation water chemistry' is not supported by the passage.

120.Option: Water buffalo are the primary reservoir for pathogenic Leptospira in Sri Lanka, as evidenced by their strong correlation with L. borgpetersenii in Kandy and their widespread presence in paddy fields.
Evidence: water buffalo showed high correlation with a particular OTU of Leptospira in Kandy (r = 0.858, d.f. = 8, p = 0.006 with L. borgpetersenii group, respectively).
cattle exhibited high correlation with several species of Leptospira (e.g., r = 0.896, d.f. = 8, p = 0.003 with L. interrogans group ...).
Thus, in agrarian communities, large animals such as buffalo and cattle might be the main source excreting pathogenic leptospires into paddy lands.

However, the association of large mammals with Leptospira barcodes should be interpreted with caution because large mammals wading in surface waters (for drinking or for draught activities) also resuspend soil and sediment particles, this association could be indirect reflecting the resuspension of local bacteria by chance and not an association with reservoir animals.
120.Rationale: While water buffalo show a notable correlation with L. borgpetersenii in Kandy, the passage also shows that cattle have strong correlations with multiple pathogenic Leptospira species and explicitly treats both cattle and buffalo as potential large-animal sources. Moreover, the authors caution that eDNA correlations may be indirect (e.g., from resuspension) and do not establish a single 'primary reservoir' species. Thus the strong claim that water buffalo are the primary reservoir is not supported by the evidence presented.

120.Option: The high correlation between cattle and pathogenic Leptospira in Kandy is likely due to indirect contamination from soil resuspension during agricultural activities, rather than direct shedding from animals.
Evidence: cattle exhibited high correlation with several species of Leptospira (e.g., r = 0.896, d.f. = 8, p = 0.003 with L. interrogans group ...).
large mammals which excrete much more urine than rats shed significantly more Leptospira per day [23].

the association of large mammals with Leptospira barcodes should be interpreted with caution because large mammals wading in surface waters (for drinking or for draught activities) also resuspend soil and sediment particles, this association could be indirect reflecting the resuspension of local bacteria by chance and not an association with reservoir animals.
120.Rationale: The passage presents both plausible explanations: direct shedding (large mammals shed much more Leptospira per day) and the possibility of indirect association via soil/sediment resuspension. It does not endorse one explanation as 'likely' over the other. Therefore the specific assertion that the observed cattle–Leptospira correlation is likely due to indirect resuspension rather than direct shedding is not supported by the text; the authors emphasize that both interpretations are possible and that caution is needed.

120.Option: The absence of pathogenic Leptospira detection in Girandurukotte is due to the lack of large domesticated animals, despite similar population densities to Kandy.
Evidence: Since the population density of cattle and buffalo is similar in both Kandy and Badulla districts, our results suggested that the greater number of sequence-reads detected in each sample is a consequence of different agro-ecological regions.

Leptospira species detected in Girandurukotte, belong to the P1 subclade-low virulent group and P2 subclade and we could not detect any correlation with tetrapod animals which support the idea that these Leptospira species might not need a mammal reservoir in their biology.
120.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that cattle and buffalo population densities are similar between the regions, so the absence of pathogenic Leptospira detection in Girandurukotte cannot be attributed to a lack of large domesticated animals. Instead, the authors ascribe differences to agro-ecological conditions and note that some Leptospira in Girandurukotte may not be associated with mammal reservoirs. Thus the option is contradicted by the passage.

120.Option: Cattle are a key driver of pathogenic Leptospira persistence in irrigation water in wet agro-ecological zones, likely due to their high shedding rates and frequent contact with paddy fields.
Evidence: Most small-holder dairy farmers allow their cattle to graze in paddy fields before the cultivation starts, thus, household animals seemed to be important for transmission of leptospirosis to humans [18].
cattle exhibited high correlation with several species of Leptospira (e.g., r = 0.896, d.f. = 8, p = 0.003 with L. interrogans group and r = 0.922, d.f. = 8, p = 0.001 with L. noguchii CZ214)
a meta-analysis to estimate the load of Leptospira excreted in urine had also shown that large mammals which excrete much more urine than rats shed significantly more Leptospira per day [23].
Thus, in agrarian communities, large animals such as buffalo and cattle might be the main source excreting pathogenic leptospires into paddy lands.

Our data provide a platform to understand the eco-epidemiology of leptospirosis in Sri Lanka, where the diverse climate and large number of reservoir animals in each region made it difficult to implement disease control activities.

This study showed, for the first time, the great Leptospira species diversity in Kandy ... The eDNA analysis also showed unexpected leptospiral correlation with other pathogenic bacterial species ... The wet region facilitates leptospirosis transmission.
120.Rationale: The passage reports strong eDNA correlations between cattle and multiple pathogenic Leptospira species in the wet-region (Kandy), documents common cattle contact with paddy fields, and cites evidence that large mammals shed far more Leptospira by urine than rodents. The authors conclude that large animals likely contaminate irrigation water in agrarian settings and that wet agro-ecological zones facilitate transmission. Together these statements support the inference that cattle are an important driver of persistence of pathogenic Leptospira in irrigation water in wet zones.

121.Option: The rainy season timing of the 3-month follow-up survey may confound short-term declines in water quality, making it difficult to attribute changes to vaccine-related behavioral shifts
Evidence: We conducted cross-sectional household-level surveys 1 month before the OCV campaign during November–December 2012 (baseline), 3 months after the campaign during May–June 2013 (first follow-up), and 12 months after the campaign in March 2014 (second follow-up).

We observed several temporary changes at the first follow-up survey not sustained at the second follow-up, including decreased observation of safe water containers, increased reported use of the camp tap as the primary drinking water source, and increased E. coli contamination of stored household drinking water.

While increased fecal contamination of stored drinking water at first follow-up was correlated with decreased use of safe water containers in households, the temporary increase could alternatively reflect the shift to the rainy season during May–June that marks increased opportunities for water contamination and increased use of unsafe water sources in the camp (F. Cavalazzi, personal communication).
environmental factors and WaSH behaviors may be impacted by seasonal trends, making assessment of OCV campaign-associated changes difficult [27].
121.Rationale: The passage explicitly places the first follow-up in May–June (the rainy season) and documents a temporary increase in E. coli contamination at that time. The authors state that the contamination increase could alternatively reflect the shift to the rainy season with greater opportunity for contamination and use of unsafe sources, and note that seasonal trends complicate attributing changes to the OCV campaign. Therefore, the rainy-season timing of the 3-month follow-up can confound short-term declines in water quality and make attribution to vaccine-related behavior change difficult.

121.Option: The campaign’s timing during the dry season may reduce the observed impact on hygiene practices due to lower baseline transmission risk
Evidence: The 2-dose OCV campaign in Maela camp was implemented by PU-AMI with technical assistance by the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) in three rounds during January–March 2013, with support from the Thailand Ministry of Public Health (MOPH) and the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation [19].

baseline and second follow-up surveys (both occurring in the dry season), and the amount of residual chlorine detected in stored drinking water was low in all three surveys; these results suggest no long-term change in practices which has also been seen in other studies [35–38].
environmental factors and WaSH behaviors may be impacted by seasonal trends, making assessment of OCV campaign-associated changes difficult [27].
121.Rationale: While the passage documents the timing of surveys and warns that seasonal trends affect interpretation, it does not state that the campaign itself occurred squarely in the dry season or that dry-season timing would reduce observed impact because of lower baseline transmission. The campaign occurred Jan–Mar, and the authors specifically identify the first follow-up (May–June) as rainy season and baseline/second follow-up as dry season; they emphasize that seasonal variation complicates interpretation but do not make the specific claim that a dry-season campaign necessarily reduces observed impact due to lower transmission risk. Because the passage does not provide direct evidence for this specific mechanism, the option is not supported by the text.

121.Option: The high baseline prevalence of cholera knowledge may limit the ability to detect meaningful changes in hygiene practices over time
Evidence: To our knowledge, this was the first evaluation of the short and long-term impact of an OCV campaign and associated messaging on KAPs regarding cholera, WaSH, and OCV through inclusion of surveys at three time points (1 month before, and 3 and 12 months after the OCV campaign).
Despite repeated outbreaks and outbreak response efforts in Maela refugee camp during 2005–2010 [19], baseline cholera knowledge and safe water practices were generally low (45–70%).
121.Rationale: The option asserts that baseline cholera knowledge was high, limiting detection of changes. The passage actually reports that baseline cholera knowledge and safe water practices were generally low (45–70%). Therefore the premise of this option contradicts the passage and is incorrect in this context.

121.Option: The use of self-reported data on hygiene practices may introduce recall bias that undermines the validity of long-term trend interpretation
Evidence: Social desirability bias may have led to reporting favorable practices to interviewers who work as CHWs in the camp.

Although assessing vaccination history from recall may introduce bias [34], we observed similarly high coverage (first dose: 93%; second dose: 84%) among those retaining vaccination cards distributed before the campaign.
Non-response bias, due to frequent absence and relocation of camp residents, may have affected outcomes in either direction.
Accurate translation of the questionnaire into Burmese and Karen was made difficult by the existence of multiple dialects and the low education level among residents in the camp.
121.Rationale: The passage highlights multiple biases related to self-reported data that can undermine interpretation of trends over time: social desirability bias for reported practices, recall bias for vaccination history, non-response bias, and translation/understanding issues. While the text emphasizes social desirability more than the term 'recall bias' for hygiene practices specifically, it clearly supports the broader point that self-reported KAP measures are susceptible to bias and thus can weaken the validity of long-term trend interpretation.

122.Option: Prostaglandin synthesis acts as a modulator of immune gene expression, and its suppression impairs the activation of Toll, IMD, and Jak-STAT pathways, reducing effector molecule production
Evidence: Here, we show for the first time that, blocking of PG production compromises humoral immune responses in the mosquito Ae. aegypti, downregulating Toll and IMD pathways.
PGs trigger hemocyte spreading behavior, nodule formation, AMP expression and melanization cascade, participating in cellular and humoral responses.
The microarray analysis revealed that the upregulation of immune genes induced by Ec challenge is no longer observed if the cells were pretreated with ASA.

In ASA-pretreated Aag2 cells, 99 immune-related genes were suppressed (S2 Dataset). Transcripts for clip-domain serine proteases were strongly down-regulated, followed by transcripts related to the toll pathway, and transcripts for protease inhibitors serpins. Some transcripts of the IMD pathway were strongly down-regulated, such as the transcriptional factor REL 2, the receptors PGRP-LE and PGRP-LB (Peptidoglycan Recognition Protein), and some AMPs, e.g. defensins and cecropins.

Altogether, our results indicate that prostaglandins are an important component of the immune response in mosquitoes, not being responsible for the activation of the immune response but playing a role in the amplitude of this response. We shed some light on how PGs are responsible for this “fine tuning” of the immune response by providing a genome wide analysis of the effects of PG on Aedes gene expression, revealing that PGs may modulate the expression of several genes from the main mosquito immune pathways, Toll, IMD and Jak/STAT, and probably, as a consequence of this modulation, alter the expression of effector genes.
122.Rationale: The passage directly reports that blocking prostaglandin production downregulates Toll and IMD pathways and that ASA pretreatment suppressed numerous immune-related transcripts (including REL2, PGRPs, and AMPs). It also explicitly states PGs modulate genes in Toll, IMD and Jak-STAT and affect the amplitude of immune responses. This mechanism (immune-gene modulation leading to reduced effector molecules) explains increased susceptibility to bacteria and viruses when PG synthesis is suppressed.

122.Option: Prostaglandins directly kill pathogens by disrupting microbial membranes, and their suppression reduces this antimicrobial activity
Evidence: PGs trigger hemocyte spreading behavior, nodule formation, AMP expression and melanization cascade, participating in cellular and humoral responses.
Thus, it seems that in insects, depletion of PGs promotes immune suppression, reducing the effectiveness of the immune response against bacteria and virus.

Altogether, our results indicate that prostaglandins are an important component of the immune response in mosquitoes, not being responsible for the activation of the immune response but playing a role in the amplitude of this response.
122.Rationale: The passage consistently describes prostaglandins as modulators of immune processes (hemocyte behavior, AMP expression, melanization) rather than as direct microbicidal compounds. There is no text stating PGs themselves directly disrupt pathogen membranes or act as antimicrobial chemicals; instead, the increased pathogen replication after PG inhibition is explained by suppressed immune gene expression and cellular responses. Therefore the option claiming direct microbicidal activity is not supported by the passage.

122.Option: Prostaglandins are required for the activation of the microbiota, and their suppression leads to dysbiosis that increases infection susceptibility
Evidence: Prostaglandins can be produced by the midgut tissue in response to the microbiota [11, 14].
Midgut prostaglandins attract hemocytes and are the first signal necessary to establish immune memory in An. gambiae [11].
The reduction of microbiota levels strongly suppressed prostaglandin production by the midguts (Fig 1D and S1 Fig).
Microbiota proliferation induces the expression of HPX7 and HPX8 that results in PG production in the midgut.
122.Rationale: The passage indicates the causal direction is microbiota → prostaglandin production (microbiota induces PGs) rather than PGs being required to 'activate' the microbiota. The evidence shows reducing microbiota suppresses PG production, not that reducing PGs alters microbiota composition to increase susceptibility. Thus the proposed mechanism (PGs required for microbiota activation leading to protection) is not supported by the text.

122.Option: Prostaglandins act as a direct signal to activate the Toll and IMD pathways, and their suppression prevents pathway initiation
Evidence: Altogether, our results indicate that prostaglandins are an important component of the immune response in mosquitoes, not being responsible for the activation of the immune response but playing a role in the amplitude of this response.

In invertebrate immune responses work on an on/off switch model, where the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off. In mosquitoes, besides PAMPs, no other molecules are known to directly regulate the activation and the amplitude of immune responses.
122.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that PGs are not responsible for activation of the immune response but instead modulate its amplitude. Activation (initiation) is driven by PAMP recognition in this system. Therefore the claim that PGs directly initiate Toll and IMD pathways (and that suppression prevents initiation) contradicts the passage; PG suppression reduces the amplitude of pathway output rather than blocking pathway initiation.

122.Option: Prostaglandins enhance hemocyte recruitment and phagocytosis, and their suppression impairs cellular immune responses
Evidence: PGs trigger hemocyte spreading behavior, nodule formation, AMP expression and melanization cascade, participating in cellular and humoral responses.
Midgut prostaglandins attract hemocytes and are the first signal necessary to establish immune memory in An. gambiae [11].
In cellular immune responses, hemocytes are the main players and mediate phagocytosis, nodulation and encapsulation of microorganisms [2].
We demonstrated that, in Aag-2 cells, prostaglandins are mainly synthetized in the nuclear and perinuclear region upon bacterial stimuli (Fig 1D).
122.Rationale: The passage describes prostaglandins as promoting hemocyte behaviors (spreading, attraction, nodule formation) and notes hemocytes mediate phagocytosis. Thus suppression of PG synthesis would impair hemocyte recruitment/activation and downstream cellular defenses (phagocytosis, nodulation), providing a mechanistic explanation for increased susceptibility to bacterial and viral infections. This cellular-immune impairment complements the transcriptional (AMP/pathway) effects described elsewhere in the text.

123.Option: The ability of the vaccine to induce a strong initial immune response immediately after the final immunization
Evidence: Immunization of mice with KSAC+GLA-SE induced a robust antibody response following the first immunization and afterwards maintaining a positive IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio (Fig. 1 A, B).
These mice were protected in a delayed challenge 12 weeks after the last vaccination using infected sand flies, indicative of the generation of long-lasting immunity.

The reduced protection we observed against the two L. major strains tested using L110f+GLA-SE correlate well with the relatively weak shift to a Th1 response that was observed after vaccination, but before parasite challenge.
123.Rationale: While the passage documents that KSAC induced a robust early antibody response, the question concerns long-term protection after a prolonged interval. The authors link long-term protection to generation of long-lived immunity (e.g., effector memory T cells and sustained Th1 responses) rather than only the immediate post-immunization magnitude. The quote noting the correlation of reduced protection with a weak shift to Th1 'after vaccination, but before parasite challenge' implies that features present at later times (memory/Th1 bias) are more explanatory than an immediate short-term response alone.

123.Option: The use of a stable emulsion formulation (GLA-SE) that enhances antigen delivery and immune activation
Evidence: KSAC and L110f recombinant proteins were prepared as previously described [11], [12], [21] and mixed at the time of injection with a stable emulsion (SE) formulation of the pure, synthetic hexa-acylated TLR4 agonist glucopyranosyl lipid A (GLA) [22].
Both antigens are well-defined and used in formulation with MPL®-SE or GLA-SE, adjuvants suitable for human use.
123.Rationale: Both vaccine candidates were formulated with the same adjuvant/emulsion (GLA-SE). Because the adjuvant formulation was common to KSAC and L110f, differences in long-term protection between the two candidates cannot be attributed to use of GLA-SE per se. The passage even discusses both antigens 'formulated with GLA-SE,' so the formulation does not explain the differential long-term efficacy.

123.Option: The consistent production of high levels of IgG2a antibodies throughout the post-vaccination period
Evidence: Protection was associated with a consistently positive IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio for KSAC.
Mice vaccinated with KSAC+GLA-SE maintained a positive KSAC-specific IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio for at least 12 weeks after the final vaccination (Fig. 5A).

In this delayed challenge, mice vaccinated with L110f+GLA-SE exhibited an almost equal IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio lower than that of KSAC+GLA-SE vaccinated mice (Figure 5A), possibly reflecting a slower and less dramatic shift to a Th1 response.
123.Rationale: The passage explicitly links KSAC's long-term protection with a 'consistently positive IgG2a:IgG1 ratio' maintained for at least 12 weeks, and contrasts this with L110f where the ratio was lower or fluctuated. A sustained IgG2a-biased (Th1-associated) antibody profile is therefore supported as a factor associated with better long-term protection in the delayed vector challenge.

123.Option: The sustained presence of antigen-specific effector memory T cells capable of rapid reactivation upon challenge
Evidence: KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).

Before sand fly challenge the percentage of CD4+CD62LlowCCR7low effector memory T cells producing IFN-γ was greater in KSAC-immunized mice stimulated ex vivo with KSAC compared to GLA-SE alone or L110f-immunized mice stimulated with L110f (Figure 3D, KSAC and L110f panels).
KSAC-immunized mice generate a pool of effector memory CD4+IFN-γ+ T cells specific to KSAC that was efficiently stimulated with SLA both before and after a sand fly challenge.
These mice were protected in a delayed challenge 12 weeks after the last vaccination using infected sand flies, indicative of the generation of long-lasting immunity.
123.Rationale: The authors emphasize that KSAC vaccination generated and maintained antigen-specific effector memory CD4+ IFN-γ+ T cells that were present before the delayed challenge and were reactivated on stimulation. This sustained effector memory pool that can rapidly produce IFN-γ upon challenge is the mechanistic explanation most strongly supported by the text for superior long-term protection against vector-transmitted infection.

123.Option: The ability of the vaccine to prevent early parasite establishment by inducing a rapid innate immune response
Evidence: Peters et al. [20] demonstrated that transmission of Leishmania parasites by sand fly bites generates a specific innate immune response involving a sustained recruitment of neutrophils that promotes parasite establishment.
It has been long established that protection from Leishmania parasites requires the induction of a Th1 immune response [16], [17], [18].
Overall, antigen-stimulated splenocytes produced a low level of IL-10 compared to the relatively high concentration of IFN-γ produced (Figure 4A, B).
123.Rationale: The passage notes that vector transmission triggers an innate neutrophil response that actually promotes parasite establishment, and it repeatedly frames protection in terms of adaptive Th1 (IFN-γ producing) responses and effector memory T cells rather than vaccine-driven innate responses. There is no evidence in the text that either vaccine's superior long-term protection was due to induction of a rapid innate response preventing early establishment; instead adaptive memory T cell and Th1 antibody profiles are presented as explanatory factors.

124.Option: The dominance of the dog-associated China I lineage suggests that wildlife reservoirs have been eliminated from the epidemic cycle in China
Evidence: China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.
The phylogenetic analysis, together with the observation that lineages China II, China III and IV are established in wildlife, suggests that they were dominant in earlier epidemics.
124.Rationale: The passage explicitly states other lineages (China II, III, IV) are established in wildlife and that China I has only recently been isolated in wildlife. That indicates wildlife reservoirs remain part of the epidemic ecology; they have not been eliminated. The dominance of a dog-associated lineage does not equal eradication of wildlife reservoirs.

124.Option: Domestic dogs are the primary reservoir for all rabies lineages in China, including those that were dominant in earlier epidemics
Evidence: China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.
The phylogenetic analysis, together with the observation that lineages China II, China III and IV are established in wildlife, suggests that they were dominant in earlier epidemics.
124.Rationale: While domestic dogs are the main host for the currently dominant China I lineage, the passage states that China II, III and IV have a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates and were likely dominant in earlier epidemics. Therefore dogs are not described as the primary reservoir for all lineages across time.

124.Option: Domestic dogs are the only source of the current epidemic, as no wildlife isolates have been linked to the dominant China I lineage
Evidence: China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.

Despite the geographic expansion of China I and the association of the majority of new isolates with this lineage, the samples were isolated almost exclusively from dogs, with almost all the remaining samples collected from humans and domesticated animals. Only two samples were isolated from wildlife (deer and ferret badger).
124.Rationale: The passage notes that China I is almost exclusively dog-associated but also states that China I strains have only recently been isolated in wildlife and that two wildlife samples exist. Thus wildlife has been linked to China I (albeit rarely), so dogs are not the only source.

124.Option: Domestic dogs have become the primary driver of the current epidemic, replacing wildlife-associated lineages that may have been more prominent in earlier waves
Evidence: China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.
Figure 7 shows how rapidly China I displaced China II and gained dominance.
The phylogenetic analysis, together with the observation that lineages China II, China III and IV are established in wildlife, suggests that they were dominant in earlier epidemics.

This data appears to highlight the fundamental role of dogs in the spread of the disease as well as the dissemination of a new lineage and it appears the spillover into wildlife only occurs once the lineage is well established.
124.Rationale: The passage documents rapid displacement of previously circulating lineages by China I, which is almost exclusively dog-associated, and contrasts that with earlier lineages that were established in wildlife. The authors explicitly interpret this as highlighting the fundamental role of dogs in spreading the current lineage, consistent with dogs becoming the primary driver of the present epidemic.

124.Option: The shift from wildlife-associated lineages to dog-associated lineages indicates that dog populations have increased in size and density across China over time
Evidence: China I isolates are almost exclusively from dogs, domestic animals and human cases, whereas China II, III and IV contains a far higher proportion of wildlife isolates; it is only recently that China I strains have been isolated in wildlife.

The geography of high incidence regions such as Guangxi, Hunan and Guizhou makes it possible for disease reservoirs to exist in remote or inaccessible regions and the custom of eating dog meat ensures the continual presence of localized concentrations of dog populations, which further benefits the survival of RABV [9].

It is more informative that there was no measurable correlation between population density and sampling/number of cases (r = -0.08634058, p = 0.6442/r = 0.04543189, p = 0.8083)- i.e, highly populated regions, including major cities such as Beijing, Shanghai or Chongqing with better resources, are not being preferentially sampled.
124.Rationale: The passage attributes the lineage shift to viral phylogenetics and dog-mediated spread, but it does not present data showing an increase in dog population size or density across China. Instead it notes localized dog population practices and lacks evidence of nationwide increases; therefore the claim that dog populations have increased in size and density over time is not supported by the provided data.

125.Option: The absence of CxFV Izabal in saliva of singly-infected mosquitoes indicates that the virus is unable to establish infection in the salivary glands without co-infection with WNV
Evidence: Interestingly, both CxFV Izabal and WNV were found in saliva of co-infected specimens when mosquitoes were exposed to both viruses simultaneously by intrathoracic inoculation, but no CxFV Izabal was found in the saliva of singly-infected specimens.
This observation suggests that CxFV Izabal may be infecting the salivary glands by “piggybacking” on WNV.
125.Rationale: The quoted text documents the observation that CxFV was not found in saliva when present alone and suggests a 'piggybacking' phenomenon during co-infection. However, this observation does not explain the lack of interference between CxFV Izabal and WNV (i.e., why WNV replication/transmission was not suppressed). In fact, the passage implies this observation might relate to enhanced salivary gland presence during co-infection rather than being the reason for absence of interference. Therefore the option describes an observation but not the most likely explanation for the lack of viral interference.

125.Option: The lack of interference is due to the fact that CxFV Izabal replicates in mosquito cells without causing cytopathic effects, preserving cellular resources for WNV replication
Evidence: CxFV Izabal caused evident cytopathic effects (CPE) in C6/36 cells, completely destroying the cell monolayer by 8 days post infection (DPI) following inoculation at an MOI of 0.1.
CxFV apparently replicates avirulently in its mosquito host.
125.Rationale: The passage actually reports CPE in the Aedes-derived C6/36 cell line, so CxFV does cause cytopathic effects in that cell culture system; it only 'apparently replicates avirulently in its mosquito host.' The authors do not propose preservation of cellular resources as the reason for lack of interference. Instead, they discuss sequence-specific RNAi mismatch and other explanations. Thus this option is not supported as the primary explanation in the text.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal and WNV likely use different cellular receptors, preventing competition for entry and enabling co-infection without interference
Evidence: CxFV may target and replicate in different mosquito tissues than WNV or other flaviviruses.

In Culex quinquefasciatus inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV, midgut and head tissues became infected with both viruses, demonstrating a potential for physical interaction between CxFV Izabal and WNV (Figs. 7,8).
125.Rationale: While the passage raises the possibility of differing tissue tropisms, it provides no evidence about specific cellular receptors or that receptor differences explain the lack of interference. In fact, the observation that both viruses can infect the same tissues (midgut and head) argues against complete segregation at the tissue-entry level. The authors emphasize sequence homology/RNAi specificity and other mechanisms rather than receptor competition, so this option is not supported by the text.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal induces a broad-spectrum antiviral state in Cx. quinquefasciatus that suppresses all flaviviruses, including WNV, but this effect is not detectable in the study design
Evidence: our data demonstrate that prior infection with CxFV Izabal does not interfere with WNV replication when the viruses are inoculated simultaneously, or when mosquitoes are exposed to WNV one week following inoculation with CxFV Izabal.

If an RNAi pathway was induced in Cx. quinquefasciatus by CxFV Izabal it would most likely still be effective seven days post-inoculation when mosquitoes were exposed to WNV as it has been previously reported that viRNAs targeting Sindbis virus in C6/36 cells were first detected 48h following infection and were still abundant 7 DPI [...].
125.Rationale: This option posits a broad antiviral state that should suppress WNV, but the passage reports that prior CxFV infection did not inhibit WNV replication or transmission. The authors specifically note that if RNAi (a key antiviral pathway) had been induced it would likely still be effective at the time of WNV exposure, yet no suppression was observed. Thus a hypothesized broad-spectrum antiviral state that suppresses WNV is inconsistent with the study findings and the authors' discussion.

125.Option: Low sequence homology between CxFV Izabal and WNV limits the effectiveness of RNA interference-mediated antiviral defense
Evidence: RNAi is a highly sequence-specific mechanism with little tolerance to mismatches between the viRNA trigger and mRNA target sequences [39],
Kim et al. [13] reported between 25 and 52% nucleotide sequence homology between CxFV (TX24518) and WNV among structural and non-structural genes.
Therefore, any interference of CxFV Izabal viRNAs with WNV was probably minimal.
125.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify limited nucleotide sequence homology and the sequence-specific nature of RNAi as a likely reason why viRNAs generated against CxFV Izabal would not effectively target WNV sequences. They conclude that interference via RNAi would probably be minimal, making low sequence homology the most plausible explanation offered for the lack of observed interference.

126.Option: Attachment of L. interrogans to E-cadherin on epithelial cells facilitates translocation across the intestinal barrier, promoting systemic spread
Evidence: To directly test the possibility that cadherins are receptors for L. interrogans, we tested two purified, commercially available cadherins, VE- and E-cadherin, for L. interrogans binding by immobilizing the proteins in 96 well plastic dishes and probing with 35S-labeled L. interrogans.
L. interrogans bound significantly more efficiently than L. biflexa to both VE- and E- cadherins
we were not able to reduce L. interrogans binding to Caco2BBE cells with anti-E-cadherin antibodies.
Whether the initial interaction of the bacteria with host cells through cadherins plays a role in this process needs to be investigated further.
126.Rationale: Although the study demonstrates that L. interrogans can bind E-cadherin in vitro, the authors did not provide direct evidence that E-cadherin–mediated attachment to epithelial cells (for example the intestinal barrier) facilitates early translocation and systemic spread. They explicitly report inability to block binding to Caco2BBE cells with anti‑E‑cadherin antibody and state that the role of such interactions requires further investigation. The passage emphasizes endothelial (VE‑cadherin) interactions as the mechanism most directly linked to dissemination.

126.Option: Interaction of L. interrogans with N-cadherin on renal tubule cells enables persistent colonization and subsequent release into the bloodstream
Evidence: The role of N-cadherin as a substrate for L. interrogans attachment also needs to be explored, as this receptor is produced by both epithelial and endothelial cells, as well as other cell types.
N-cadherin is also the predominant cadherin in the renal proximal tubules but is not expressed in other nephron segments [69].
persistently infected reservoir animals harbor the spirochete in the proximal convoluted tubules of the kidney and chronically excrete Leptospira through the urine.
126.Rationale: The passage notes N-cadherin as a potential receptor and that renal proximal tubules are the site of persistent colonization, but it does not provide experimental evidence that N-cadherin mediates persistent colonization or causes release into the bloodstream. Moreover, persistent renal colonization is a later-stage phenomenon in reservoir hosts, not an example of the endothelial interface facilitating early dissemination. The authors state the role of N‑cadherin 'needs to be explored,' indicating lack of direct supporting data.

126.Option: Dose-dependent binding of L. interrogans to fibronectin in the extracellular matrix promotes bacterial adherence and subsequent invasion into endothelial cells
Evidence: L. interrogans bound efficiently to ICAM-2, and to fibronectin, which was used as a positive control.
binding to the cells is considerably more efficient than to the ECM deposited by the cells.
while there was no significant difference between L. interrogans and L. biflexa attachment to fibronectin.
126.Rationale: Although the bacteria bind fibronectin in vitro (used as a positive control), the study emphasizes stronger, specific interactions with cell surface cadherins (especially VE‑cadherin) and reports that binding to intact cells is considerably more efficient than to ECM. The authors do not present data showing fibronectin‑mediated invasion of endothelial cells or that fibronectin binding is the mechanism by which endothelial dissemination occurs; thus this option is not directly supported by the study.

126.Option: Binding of L. interrogans to VE-cadherin disrupts endothelial integrity, enabling bacterial dissemination through the vascular barrier
Evidence: To directly test the possibility that cadherins are receptors for L. interrogans, we tested two purified, commercially available cadherins, VE- and E-cadherin, for L. interrogans binding by immobilizing the proteins in 96 well plastic dishes and probing with 35S-labeled L. interrogans.
L. interrogans bound significantly more efficiently than L. biflexa to both VE- and E- cadherins
The dose-dependent and saturable binding of L. interrogans to VE-cadherin indicates that the receptor-bacteria interaction is specific.
Inhibition of bacterial attachment was observed when purified receptor was pre-treated with 20 µg/ml anti-VE-cadherin antibody, resulting in a 40% decrease in L. interrogans binding (Figure 6A).

we hypothesize that VE-cadherin plays a critical role for the L. interrogans to attach to the vasculature, resulting in endothelial damage and facilitating the escape of the pathogen from the bloodstream into different tissues during disseminated infection.
we hypothesize that VE-cadherin interaction with the pathogen activates signaling pathway leading to loss of vascular integrity, facilitating bacterial dissemination.
126.Rationale: The authors provide multiple lines of evidence that pathogenic L. interrogans binds specifically and saturably to VE‑cadherin on endothelial cells and that blocking VE‑cadherin reduces attachment. They explicitly hypothesize that VE‑cadherin engagement could lead to loss of endothelial integrity and facilitate escape of bacteria from the bloodstream into tissues. This directly supports the concept that endothelial cell–VE‑cadherin interactions constitute a critical interface for early bacterial dissemination.

126.Option: Binding of L. interrogans to ICAM-2 on endothelial cells triggers inflammatory signaling that enhances bacterial clearance and limits dissemination
Evidence: L. interrogans bound efficiently to ICAM-2, and to fibronectin, which was used as a positive control.

The contribution of ICAM-2 as Leptospira receptor on endothelial cells needs to be further evaluated in future work, but it is of interest because it is a well-known ligand for the leukocyte integrin lymphocyte function-associated antigen-1 (LFA-1) on endothelial surfaces [60].

ICAM-2, along with other adhesion molecules E-selectin and VCAM, has been shown to be highly expressed in lungs of patients that succumbed to leptospirosis [61], and is thought to participate in recruitment of inflammatory cells and release of effectors at the infected site [62].
126.Rationale: While the study shows L. interrogans can bind ICAM-2 and notes ICAM-2's role in recruiting inflammatory cells, there is no evidence presented that ICAM-2 binding triggers signaling that enhances bacterial clearance or limits dissemination. In fact, ICAM‑2 expression has been associated with severe disease (high expression in lungs of fatal cases). The authors call for further evaluation of ICAM‑2's contribution, so the specific claim that ICAM‑2 binding limits dissemination is unsupported by the data.

127.Option: Regions with Acrisol soil types, high agricultural exposure, and a prevalence of chronic disease are most likely to show clustering of Bp despite similar climatic conditions
Evidence: Acrisol type contributed either high or very high suitability for Bp (see Fig 5).

Most often, they are involved in agriculture or domesticated animal industry. This is exemplified by workers tilling soil in rice paddy fields barefoot and are constantly exposed to the soil environment [15,4].

Using different infection modeling schemes, additional health risk factors were identified with the incidence rate of melioidosis in a highly endemic area. These include mobility change of residents, working males between 45 to 59 years old and more importantly, diabetic individuals [8,9]. Individuals with chronic renal and liver diseases were also reported to be susceptible to melioidosis [10].

three of the seven bioclimatic and soil characteristics were among the most contributory predictors. Bio8 (mean temperature) contributed 67·9% probability of suitability, followed by Biocateg1 (soil type) and then Bio12 (annual precipitation).
127.Rationale: The passage identifies Acrisol soil as a strong predictor of Bp suitability and specifically links occupational agricultural exposure (rice paddy workers barefoot) to infection risk. It also lists diabetes and chronic renal/liver disease as important host risk factors associated with incidence. Together, localized soil suitability (Acrisol), high agricultural exposure (increasing contact with soil reservoirs), and a population with prevalent chronic diseases plausibly explain clustering in some regions even when climatic conditions are similar.

127.Option: Regions with high levels of air pollution and industrial activity are most likely to show clustering of Bp despite similar climatic conditions
Evidence: To consider the environmental variables that contributed to the presence of the bacterium, six bioclimatic and one edaphic (soil) variables were identified while maintaining a high-level quality of model fitting.

three of the seven bioclimatic and soil characteristics were among the most contributory predictors. Bio8 (mean temperature) contributed 67·9% probability of suitability, followed by Biocateg1 (soil type) and then Bio12 (annual precipitation).

The implication of primarily associating Acrisol type or with other soil types is with the infection risk for melioidosis since many individuals are working for their livelihood in the tropical rural areas.
This is exacerbated by various anthropogenic activities on a shift in rural land use.
127.Rationale: The study focuses on bioclimatic and edaphic (soil) predictors (temperature, soil type, precipitation) and anthropogenic land-use changes in rural settings; it does not identify air pollution or industrial activity as contributors. The provided evidence therefore does not support industrial pollution as an explanation for the observed clustering.

127.Option: Regions with high population density and urban infrastructure are most likely to show clustering of Bp despite similar climatic conditions
Evidence: The implication of primarily associating Acrisol type or with other soil types is with the infection risk for melioidosis since many individuals are working for their livelihood in the tropical rural areas.

Most often, they are involved in agriculture or domesticated animal industry. This is exemplified by workers tilling soil in rice paddy fields barefoot and are constantly exposed to the soil environment [15,4].
These rural areas in SEA are the ones lacking adequate medical services that missed out on the definitive diagnosis of melioidosis.
127.Rationale: The passage highlights rural, agricultural exposures and soil types (Acrisol) as key drivers of Bp occurrence and notes deficiencies in rural medical services. It does not point to urban population density or urban infrastructure as causes of clustering; indeed the examples focus on rural agricultural settings.

127.Option: Regions with high humidity and frequent storms are most likely to show clustering of Bp despite similar climatic conditions
Evidence: Descriptions on the aberration in climate and weather events were found to increase melioidosis cases after storms in Taiwan and cyclones in Northern Australia [13].

Significant rise of disease burden among adults and children in Cambodia was observed during rainy days with high humidity and maximum wind speed while low visibility and windy conditions with humidity were noted in Laos [14].
Acrisol type contributed either high or very high suitability for Bp (see Fig 5).

Most often, they are involved in agriculture or domesticated animal industry. This is exemplified by workers tilling soil in rice paddy fields barefoot and are constantly exposed to the soil environment [15,4].
127.Rationale: Although the passage does state that storms, high humidity and windy conditions can increase melioidosis cases (climatic drivers), the question asks why clustering occurs despite similar climatic conditions. Climatic factors alone would not explain differential clustering across areas with similar climate; the passage emphasizes soil type (Acrisol) and agricultural exposure as additional, non-climatic determinants, making the humidity/storms-only explanation insufficient.

127.Option: Regions with high annual precipitation and frequent rainfall events are most likely to show clustering of Bp despite similar climatic conditions
Evidence: Seasonal foci of disease outbreaks have long been associated with heavy rainfall events [11].
As shown in Fig 4A, an annual precipitation of <2300 mm contributes to high to very high suitability for the bacterial pathogen.
Acrisol type contributed either high or very high suitability for Bp (see Fig 5).

Most often, they are involved in agriculture or domesticated animal industry. This is exemplified by workers tilling soil in rice paddy fields barefoot and are constantly exposed to the soil environment [15,4].
127.Rationale: Rainfall and heavy precipitation are implicated in outbreaks, but the model indicates optimal suitability is associated with particular precipitation ranges (e.g., <2300 mm), not uniformly 'high' precipitation. More importantly, precipitation alone cannot account for clustering where climatic conditions are similar; the passage attributes important additional explanatory factors to soil type (Acrisol) and agricultural exposure, which better explain clustering despite comparable climate.

128.Option: The co-occurrence of TcV and TcVI in both domestic and sylvatic cycles in Argentina and Bolivia, but not in Brazil, indicates a vector-specific barrier that restricts their transmission to certain triatomine species
Evidence: TcV and TcVI have never been identified in US in 148 determinations, nor in Central America in 120 cases. In contrast, in South America, these DTUs together have frequently been identified in several countries, Argentina (76.9%), Bolivia (44.6%), Chile (28.6%) and Paraguay (55.2%)—but very rarely in others such as in Colombia (1.1%) [24, 39–41], Ecuador (3.3%) [42], and Brazil (1.5%) [24].
both DTUs are clearly associated with domestic cycles since only 10.5% of them are identified in hosts from wild cycles.
They are identified in some Didelphimorphia and in different species of rodents but only in the Southern Cone countries and Bolivia.
128.Rationale: The passage documents geographic differences in TcV/TcVI frequency (high in Argentina/Bolivia, rare in Brazil) and notes they are primarily associated with domestic cycles with only a small fraction in wild hosts. However, there is no direct evidence in the text that a vector-specific barrier (i.e., restriction to certain triatomine species) is the causal mechanism. The observed pattern could reflect other factors (historical origin, domestic ecology, host reservoir distribution, sampling bias). Therefore the claim that this pattern indicates a vector-specific barrier is not supported strongly by the passage.

128.Option: The near-total absence of TcIII and TcIV in North America, despite their presence in sylvatic cycles in South America, suggests a climatic barrier that prevents their survival in temperate environments
Evidence: TcIII and TcIV, which are thought to result from ancestral hybridization between TcI and TcII, reached 3.4% and 3.6% of the identifications, respectively.
In North America, both of these DTUs were reported in Mexico in several publications [29, 30, 33, 34], but for the moment only TcIV has been identified in the US [24, 31, 35, 36].
In Central America, only TcIV has been identified in Guatemala in humans and vectors [37, 38]. In other Central American countries, neither TcIII nor TcIV has been reported.
There is little doubt that TcIII and TcIV DTUs have a sylvan origin...
128.Rationale: The passage documents limited detection of TcIII/TcIV in North/Central America and a sylvatic origin in South America, but it does not present evidence linking this distribution to climatic constraints. Multiple alternative explanations are possible (historical biogeography, vector or host community composition, sampling effort). Thus inferring a climatic barrier from the distribution alone is speculative and not directly supported by the provided text.

128.Option: The high frequency of TcI in sylvatic cycles across all regions, combined with its absence in domestic cycles in Central America, indicates a host-specific barrier preventing its transmission to human populations
Evidence: TcI has a wide distribution, from the southern United States to northern Argentina and Chile; this DTU is the most frequently sampled in sylvatic cycles, but it is also frequent in domestic cycles and it is the dominant DTU responsible for the transmission of Chagas disease in endemic countries located north of the Amazon basin;
In Central America only two DTUs (TcI and TcIV) were identified
As mentioned above, TcI was the most frequently identified DTU in the overall sample, with a lower percentage in South America (58.2%) than in North America (79.5%) and Central America (93.3%).
128.Rationale: The passage actually indicates TcI is frequent in both sylvatic and domestic cycles and is highly prevalent in Central America (93.3%). It therefore contradicts the premise that TcI is absent from domestic cycles in Central America. Because the key premise is false, the inference of a host-specific barrier preventing transmission to humans is unsupported by the text.

128.Option: The exclusive association of TcV and TcVI with domestic cycles in Southern Cone countries, despite their absence in similar environments in North and Central America, suggests a region-specific ecological barrier limiting their spread
Evidence: TcV and TcVI have never been identified in US in 148 determinations, nor in Central America in 120 cases.

In contrast, in South America, these DTUs together have frequently been identified in several countries, Argentina (76.9%), Bolivia (44.6%), Chile (28.6%) and Paraguay (55.2%)—but very rarely in others such as in Colombia (1.1%) [24, 39–41], Ecuador (3.3%) [42], and Brazil (1.5%) [24].
both DTUs are clearly associated with domestic cycles since only 10.5% of them are identified in hosts from wild cycles.
128.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that TcV/TcVI are concentrated in Southern Cone countries and strongly associated with domestic cycles, while being essentially absent from North and Central America despite similar continental presence. This geographic and ecological confinement (domestic Southern Cone presence vs absence elsewhere) is consistent with a region-specific ecological or ecological–historical barrier limiting their spread, making this option the best-supported inference from the data presented.

128.Option: The consistent detection of TcII in domestic cycles across South America, despite its absence in North and Central America, suggests a host immune barrier that prevents its establishment in human populations outside South America
Evidence: TcII was much more rarely identified (9.6% of overall DTUs identified). It was not identified in Central America out of 120 identifications, and only 13 identifications were reported from North America out of 459 (2.8%).

In humans, it is relatively abundant, accounting for 20% of human strains, but it is highly abundant in Brazil (66% of human strains identifications) and rare in most other countries except Colombia (15%) and Chile (30%).
It seems that TcII strains would not have had the same expansion capacity as TcI among the wild cycles, and they probably found refuge mostly in certain wild mammals.
128.Rationale: While the passage documents geographic concentration of TcII in South America and rarity in North/Central America, it does not provide evidence that a host immune barrier in non–South American human populations prevents establishment.
