147.Rationale: A higher prevalence in chickens suggests they are an important reservoir and increases plausibility of transmission, but prevalence data alone do not prove direct transmission to humans. The authors report higher isolation rates from chickens but explicitly state that they could not provide direct evidence of poultry-to-human transmission; therefore prevalence associations are insufficient as the strongest evidence without genetic or epidemiologic linkage (e.g., identical strains and temporally linked exposures).

148.Option: Compare anti-MOMP antibody levels between children re-infected with the same ompA genotype and those re-infected with a different genotype using a matched case-control analysis
Evidence: Children with a positive C. trachomatis result at baseline, negative result at 2 months, and positive result at 6 months were considered to have cleared infection after antibiotic treatment and been subsequently re-infected.

A total of 24 children had a swab positive for C. trachomatis at baseline, negative at 2 months, and then positive again at 6 months, and 21 of these children had successful ompA sequencing results at both the baseline and 6-month visits.
The ompA types of children living in the same household were more similar than the ompA types of children living in different households of the same community (PERMANOVA P<0.001).
148.Rationale: While comparing antibody levels between children re-infected with the same versus different ompA genotypes is directly relevant, a matched case-control design is not the most appropriate here. The number of re-infected children with sequencing data is very small (21 total, 14 with same genotype), limiting feasibility and statistical power for matched analyses. Matching is also unnecessary if potential confounders (age, sex, household clustering) can be adjusted for in a regression model; moreover, matching on household could overmatch because household is also a likely source of exposure (the passage notes strong household clustering). Therefore a straightforward analytic approach using regression among the re-infected (or among the at-risk cohort) is preferable to a matched case-control in this dataset.

148.Option: Use a Cox proportional hazards model to assess the association between anti-MOMP antibody titer at baseline and time to re-infection with any ompA genotype
Evidence: Conjunctival swabs were collected on all pre-school children at standardized timepoints before and after azithromycin treatment, regardless of symptoms.

Children with a positive C. trachomatis result at baseline, negative result at 2 months, and positive result at 6 months were considered to have cleared infection after antibiotic treatment and been subsequently re-infected.

A total of 24 children had a swab positive for C. trachomatis at baseline, negative at 2 months, and then positive again at 6 months, and 21 of these children had successful ompA sequencing results at both the baseline and 6-month visits.
148.Rationale: A Cox model requires reasonably precise event times and follow-up person-time. This study collected specimens at fixed discrete visits (baseline, 2 months, 6 months), so exact times to re-infection are interval-censored and not well suited to standard Cox regression. In addition, the question asks specifically about re-infection with the same ompA genotype; the proposed model evaluates time to re-infection with any genotype, which does not directly answer the question about genotype-specific protection.

148.Option: Perform a logistic regression analysis with anti-MOMP antibody level as the predictor and re-infection with the same ompA genotype as the outcome, adjusting for age and sex
Evidence: Conjunctival swabs were collected on all pre-school children at standardized timepoints before and after azithromycin treatment, regardless of symptoms.

Children with a positive C. trachomatis result at baseline, negative result at 2 months, and positive result at 6 months were considered to have cleared infection after antibiotic treatment and been subsequently re-infected.

A total of 24 children had a swab positive for C. trachomatis at baseline, negative at 2 months, and then positive again at 6 months, and 21 of these children had successful ompA sequencing results at both the baseline and 6-month visits.
Data on ompA type were contributed by a total of 274 children (mean age 3.2 years; 48% female) from 237 households
148.Rationale: Logistic regression is an appropriate analytic approach for a binary outcome (re-infection with the same ompA genotype vs not) and allows adjustment for individual-level confounders such as age and sex (both available in the dataset). The study has standardized timepoints and a clear definition of re-infection, so one can use baseline anti-MOMP antibody level (measured at standard timepoint) as the predictor and model odds of same-genotype re-infection among those who cleared infection. Logistic regression also permits inclusion of household clustering (e.g., with robust SEs or random effects) to account for the household-level similarity noted in the passage.

148.Option: Match children by household and compare anti-MOMP antibody levels between those who were re-infected with the same ompA genotype and those who were not re-infected
Evidence: The ompA types of children living in the same household were more similar than the ompA types of children living in different households of the same community (PERMANOVA P<0.001).

A total of 24 children had a swab positive for C. trachomatis at baseline, negative at 2 months, and then positive again at 6 months, and 21 of these children had successful ompA sequencing results at both the baseline and 6-month visits.
Conjunctival swabs were collected on all pre-school children at standardized timepoints before and after azithromycin treatment, regardless of symptoms.
148.Rationale: Matching by household could control for shared exposure risk, but it is not ideal here for two reasons. First, household matching may overcontrol for the very exposure patterns that determine same-genotype re-infection (the passage emphasizes strong household clustering), obscuring the association of interest. Second, the comparison proposed mixes outcome groups (same-genotype re-infected vs not re-infected), whereas the most direct test of antibody correlates would compare those re-infected with the same genotype to those re-infected with a different genotype or model the binary outcome across the at-risk cohort using regression with household as a random effect or clustered SEs. Given the modest number of re-infections, matching by household would also further reduce effective sample size and power.

148.Option: Calculate the seroprevalence of anti-MOMP antibodies in the entire cohort at baseline and compare it to the incidence of re-infection with the same ompA genotype over time
Evidence: Conjunctival swabs were collected on all pre-school children at standardized timepoints before and after azithromycin treatment, regardless of symptoms.

A total of 24 children had a swab positive for C. trachomatis at baseline, negative at 2 months, and then positive again at 6 months, and 21 of these children had successful ompA sequencing results at both the baseline and 6-month visits.

The mean community-level prevalence of C. trachomatis among 1–5 year-olds was 56% (95%CI 49–64%) before treatment, 9% (95%CI 7–11%) 2 months after the antibiotic distribution, and 12% (95%CI 9–15%) 6 months after antibiotics.
148.Rationale: Comparing cohort-level seroprevalence to incidence over time is an ecological approach and is prone to ecological fallacy; it does not establish an individual-level correlation between anti-MOMP antibodies and protection against same-genotype re-infection. The question asks about whether presence of antibodies correlates with reduced individual risk, which requires individual-level analysis (e.g., regression), not aggregate comparisons. Also, the relatively small number of confirmed re-infections with sequencing would make population-level comparisons insensitive and potentially misleading.

149.Option: The genetic similarity between the vector species and known competent vectors of related arboviruses
Evidence: Detection of virus in field-collected insects is not sufficient to prove their ability to transmit the virus.
laboratory studies are therefore needed to experimentally test the ability of vectors to become infected with, maintain, and successfully transmit arboviruses (i.e., vector competence) [19].
149.Rationale: The passage does not emphasize genetic similarity as the critical determinant; instead it stresses empirical demonstration of vector competence via laboratory studies. The quoted sentences explicitly state that detection in the field is insufficient and that experimental testing of infection, maintenance, and transmission (vector competence) is required. Genetic similarity might be suggestive, but it is not presented as the decisive factor for whether a species can actually transmit and sustain spread.

149.Option: The frequency with which the vector species is found in proximity to susceptible hosts and climatic conditions conducive to viral transmission
Evidence: Arbovirus transmission cycles can only become established when competent vectors and susceptible hosts encounter under suitable climatic conditions.

To gain insights into the potential of Culicoides biting midges and mosquitoes to function as vectors of SHUV, we studied the susceptibility of four main arbovirus vector species (Culicoides nubeculosus and C. sonorensis biting midges, and Culex pipiens pipiens and Aedes aegypti mosquitoes) for SHUV.
149.Rationale: Proximity to hosts and suitable climatic conditions are important ecological prerequisites (the passage explicitly notes the need for vectors and susceptible hosts to encounter under suitable climatic conditions). However, the question asks what most critically determines a vector's ability to contribute to sustained spread beyond mere detection. The passage identifies vector competence (ability to become infected, replicate, and transmit) as the key requirement that must be demonstrated experimentally. Thus while host proximity and climate are necessary for transmission cycles, they are not the single most critical determinant emphasized for establishing a vector's role beyond detection.

149.Option: The ability of the vector to support viral replication and overcome midgut and salivary gland barriers to enable transmission
Evidence: Detection of virus in field-collected insects is not sufficient to prove their ability to transmit the virus.
Arboviruses need to overcome several barriers (i.e. midgut and salivary gland barriers) inside their vector, before they can be transmitted [17, 18].
laboratory studies are therefore needed to experimentally test the ability of vectors to become infected with, maintain, and successfully transmit arboviruses (i.e., vector competence) [19].
No SHUV was detected in any of the saliva samples taken from either Cx. p. pipiens or Ae. aegypti.
149.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that detecting virus in field-collected insects is insufficient and that arboviruses must overcome midgut and salivary gland barriers inside the vector to be transmitted. It further emphasizes the need for laboratory tests of infection, maintenance, and transmission (vector competence). These points directly identify the vector's ability to support viral replication and bypass internal barriers (midgut and salivary glands) as the critical determinant of whether it can contribute to sustained spread.

149.Option: The proportion of vector populations that test positive for the virus in field-collected samples from endemic regions
Evidence: SHUV was previously isolated from field-collected pools of Culicoides biting midges and from mosquitoes, but their relative importance in SHUV transmission remained to be confirmed.
Detection of virus in field-collected insects is not sufficient to prove their ability to transmit the virus.
149.Rationale: The passage notes that isolation/detection from field-collected insects does not confirm transmission ability, and that the relative importance of such detections must be verified. Therefore the mere proportion testing positive in field samples does not critically determine a species' capacity to sustain spread; what matters is demonstrated vector competence and successful transmission.

149.Option: The duration of time the vector remains infectious after acquiring the virus, as measured by the persistence of viral RNA in the body
Evidence: Wells were scored for virus induced CPE at 3 and 7 days post inoculation.
In the majority of mosquito saliva samples, SHUV titers were below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3D).
Detection of virus in field-collected insects is not sufficient to prove their ability to transmit the virus.
149.Rationale: While the duration of infectiousness is relevant to transmission dynamics, the passage emphasizes the need for demonstrable replication and the ability to overcome midgut and salivary gland barriers, assessed by infectious virus assays (CPE and titers), not merely persistence of viral RNA. Measuring RNA persistence does not necessarily indicate infectiousness. The text focuses on infectious virus detection (titers, CPE, saliva positivity) as the critical evidence for transmission potential, so persistence of RNA alone is not presented as the most critical determinant.

150.Option: The differential expression of IL-1Ra between species, which is higher in L. (L.) amazonensis infection, most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by modulating IL-1β activity and limiting tissue damage
Evidence: Both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis induced significant amounts of TNFα, IL-8, IL-1Ra and IL-10.
L. (L.) amazonensis induced considerably higher levels of TNFα, IL-1Ra and IL-10 than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1C).

Since IL-32γ is linked to IL-1Ra and LL-37 production after L. (L.) amazonensis infection, we suggest that IL-1Ra may indeed play a role in the balance of the inflammatory state caused by L. amazonensis suggesting a mechanism of feedback dependent on IL-32γ/LL-37/IL-1Ra.
150.Rationale: While the passage notes that IL-1Ra is induced and is higher after L. (L.) amazonensis infection and that IL-1Ra may help balance inflammation, the study repeatedly identifies IL-32γ as the central regulator that drives cytokine and microbicidal responses. The text frames IL-1Ra as one downstream mediator linked to IL-32γ, not as the primary host factor that 'most strongly' determines the balance between control and pathology. Therefore the statement that IL-1Ra is the most influential factor is not supported by the passage.

150.Option: IL-32γ expression level and its downstream induction of microbicidal molecules, modulated by the infecting Leishmania species, determine the balance between parasite control and immunopathology
Evidence: The present study demonstrates the important role that endogenous IL-32γ plays in regulating cytokines and microbicidal molecules induced by L. (L.) amazonensis or L. (V.) braziliensis in THP-1-derived macrophages.
Here, results indicate that L. (L.) amazonensis induces higher levels of IL-32γ mRNA and IL-32 protein than L. (V.) braziliensis.

We then evaluated microbicidal molecules known to be involved in Leishmania control [30,54,55] as possible targets of IL-32γ. Our data demonstrate that IL-32γ is linked with the induction of iNOS, cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 expression and consequently NO, LL-37 peptide, and β-defensin 2 release during Leishmania species infection.
IL-32γ upregulates the induction of microbicidal molecules, which may contribute to control Leishmania species infections (S6 Fig).

In summary, we demonstrate that Leishmania species induce IL-32γ, and suggest that these parasites can inhibit the IL-32γ splicing into the less pro-inflammatory isoforms IL-32β and IL-32α. Furthermore, we demonstrated that during L. (V.) braziliensis and L. (L.) amazonensis infection IL-32γ was differentially associated with the production of pro and anti-inflammatory mediators.
150.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies IL-32γ as a crucial intracellular cytokine that is differentially induced by the two Leishmania species and that controls both cytokine production (e.g., TNFα, IL-8) and microbicidal effector molecules (iNOS/NO, cathelicidin/LL-37, β-defensin 2). These downstream effects alter macrophage ability to control infection and modulate inflammation. The authors conclude that IL-32γ can result in different clinical consequences between species, supporting that IL-32γ expression level and its downstream induction of microbicidal molecules most strongly influence the balance between parasite control and immunopathology in this model.

150.Option: The level of IL-10 production, which is higher in L. (L.) amazonensis infection, most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by suppressing excessive inflammation
Evidence: Both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis induced significant amounts of TNFα, IL-8, IL-1Ra and IL-10.
L. (L.) amazonensis induced considerably higher levels of TNFα, IL-1Ra and IL-10 than L. (V.) braziliensis (Fig 1C).
No differences were observed for IL-10 mRNA expression and protein production for both Leishmania species in IL-32 knockdown cells (Fig 3D).
No differences were found between the two Leishmania species on IL-10 mRNA expression and proteins levels (Fig 4D).
150.Rationale: Although IL-10 is mentioned as induced (and at times higher with L. (L.) amazonensis) and could contribute to limiting inflammation, the passage does not present IL-10 as the principal determinant. The study stresses IL-32γ as the central regulator of macrophage responses and microbicidal activity. Furthermore, IL-10 was not consistently altered by manipulations of IL-32γ, and the authors do not claim IL-10 'most strongly' influences the balance; thus this option overstates IL-10's role relative to the evidence provided.

150.Option: The induction of IL-1β by L. (V.) braziliensis most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by enhancing macrophage activation and pathogen clearance
Evidence: Remarkably, only L. (V.) braziliensis induced a major increase in IL-1β production.
L. (V.) braziliensis-induced IL-1β and IL-1Ra as well as IL-10 induced by both Leishmania species were not affected by the up or down regulation of IL-32γ.
That IL-32γ had no effect on IL-1β production was unexpected since silencing of IL-32 in THP-1 cells reduced TNFα, IL-8 and IL-1β after infection with MTB [6].
150.Rationale: While L. (V.) braziliensis uniquely induces IL-1β in this model, the passage does not present IL-1β as the dominant factor determining the tradeoff between parasite control and immunopathology. The authors note IL-1β induction but emphasize IL-32γ's central regulatory role over microbicidal molecules and pro/anti-inflammatory mediators. Moreover, IL-1β levels were not modulated by IL-32γ manipulation here, and the text does not claim IL-1β alone most strongly influences the balance; therefore this option is not supported as the primary factor.

150.Option: The species-specific induction of TNFα, which is higher in L. (L.) amazonensis infection, most strongly influences the balance between parasite control and immunopathology by driving both microbicidal activity and tissue damage
Evidence: In this study, TNFα production was higher in macrophages infected with L. (L.) amazonenis than L. (V.) braziliensis.

Heinhuis et al. [53] demonstrated that TNFα is a potent inducer of IL-32 in human synovial fibroblasts. Indeed, we now show that IL-32 protein production induced by Leishmania species and by LPS is also dependent on TNFα.
IL-32γ upregulates the induction of microbicidal molecules, which may contribute to control Leishmania species infections (S6 Fig).
In summary, we demonstrate that Leishmania species induce IL-32γ ... IL-32γ was differentially associated with the production of pro and anti-inflammatory mediators.
150.Rationale: The passage documents that TNFα is higher after L. (L.) amazonensis infection and is linked in a regulatory loop with IL-32γ. However, the authors frame TNFα largely as a downstream (and upstream) player in the IL-32γ-centered regulatory network rather than as the single dominant factor. The study's conclusions repeatedly highlight IL-32γ as the crucial intracellular cytokine driving microbicidal responses and modulation of inflammation. Thus although TNFα is important, the evidence points to IL-32γ expression (and its downstream effects) as the primary determinant in this experimental model.

151.Option: Including an internal amplification control (IAC) in every sample to detect PCR inhibition and ensure assay validity
Evidence: Extraction was performed using 300 μl of GEB and 5 μl (40 ρg/μl) of an internal amplification control (IAC), were added to each sample at the beginning of the purification process to ensure the quality of the DNA purification process and the absence of PCR inhibitors.

To assure adequate DNA purification, a recombinant plasmid (pZErO-2) containing the aquaporin gene of Arabidopsis thaliana as a heterologous extrinsic internal amplification control (IAC) was used [12,20,23].
151.Rationale: Including an IAC directly addresses a major source of false-negative results: PCR inhibition or failed extraction. The passage explicitly states that IAC was added to each sample "at the beginning of the purification process to ensure the quality of the DNA purification process and the absence of PCR inhibitors." Thus the IAC allows detection of assay failures (pre-analytical or analytical) that would otherwise produce false-negatives, making this methodological choice directly protective against false-negative results.

151.Option: Using a standardized DNA extraction protocol for both clot and whole blood samples to ensure consistent processing conditions
Evidence: Blood samples mixed with Guanidine/EDTA (GEB) were processed using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit (Roche Diagnostics GmbH, Mannheim, Germany) as previously described [12,20].
Clot samples were initially homogenized using the FastPrep machine followed by extraction using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit.

The technique is based on the initial disruption of clot using the fast prep machine followed by the internationally standardized DNA extraction protocol for the molecular diagnosis of Chagas disease for GEB samples.
151.Rationale: Pre-analytical variability in extraction methods can cause inconsistent DNA yield and increase false-negatives. The study applied the same (internationally standardized) Roche High Pure extraction protocol to both sample types, which reduces procedural variability across samples and helps ensure comparable, reliable DNA recovery — directly reducing the risk of false-negative results caused by inconsistent processing.

151.Option: Employing a high-efficiency homogenization method (FastPrep) to ensure complete disruption of the clot matrix
Evidence: Clot was initially disrupted using the FastPrep technique and then DNA was extracted using the High Pure PCR Template Preparation kit.
DNA extracted from clot proved to have a higher sensitivity and presented a lower Cq value compared to DNA extracted from whole blood-GE (GEB) samples.

Initially Lysing Matrix C, F, H and J were tested using spiked blood clot, only Lysing Matrix E and Lysing Matrix H gave comparable results as measured by the Cq values. However, Lysing Matrix E was chosen because the results were more reproducible.
151.Rationale: Incomplete disruption of clot can trap parasite DNA and reduce extraction yield, producing false-negatives. The FastPrep homogenization directly improves release of parasite DNA from the clot, evidenced by higher sensitivity and lower Cq values for clot-extracted DNA. The study also optimized lysing matrix choice to improve reproducibility. Therefore this methodological choice directly reduces pre-analytical loss of target DNA and consequent false-negatives.

151.Option: Avoiding clot washing prior to DNA extraction to prevent loss of circulating parasite DNA
Evidence: washing the clot with water or with erythrocyte lysing buffers as previously reported [14,33] most likely lyses the T. cruzi parasites releasing the genetic material, which will also be washed out along with hemoglobin. Thus, washing the clot might lead to decrease the sensitivity of any technique targeting the T. cruzi DNA, such as the qPCR.
Our protocol avoided any washing steps of the clot previous to the DNA purification to ensure that most of the DNA from the parasites could be recovered from the sample.
151.Rationale: Pre-analytical washing of clots can physically remove released parasite DNA and lower target concentration, increasing false-negative risk. The investigators explicitly avoided washing because it "might lead to decrease the sensitivity" and stated their protocol "avoided any washing steps... to ensure that most of the DNA from the parasites could be recovered." This choice directly preserves target material and thus reduces false-negatives.

151.Option: Using a single, consistent sample collection time to minimize biological variability in parasite load
Evidence: The blood obtained without additives was allowed to sit for at least 30 min to let the clot to form (no more than 60 min) and then centrifuged at 1100 x g for 20 min [18].

A total of 265 pairs of samples (one GEB and a clot sample) were obtained from archived specimens in our biorepository. The samples were obtained from two sample sets: Archived samples from women presenting for delivery at the Hospital Municipal Camiri in Camiri, Bolivia (n = 100) [15]; and samples collected at the Hospital Municipal San Juan de Dios, Santa Cruz, Bolivia (n = 165) [16,17].
151.Rationale: While controlling collection timing can reduce biological variability, the study did not implement a single, precise collection time (they allowed clotting between "at least 30 min" and "no more than 60 min") and used archived samples from two different sites and populations. Therefore this option was not a primary methodological choice in the study and is less directly related to the pre-analytical measures the authors emphasized (IAC inclusion, standardized extraction, FastPrep homogenization, and avoiding washing) that more directly prevented false-negatives.

152.Option: Chilling duration and compaction height must be limited to preserve male mosquito survival and longevity while enabling efficient packing and transportation
Evidence: It is therefore essential to determine the tolerable and optimal range of chilling temperature, duration, and compaction for male mosquitoes.

no significant difference was observed on the survival rate of IHC males when they were chilled for less than 6 h at 5°C and 1 h at 10°C (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), however, the survival was slightly reduced when the chilling time reached 24 h at 5°C and over 1 h at 10°C (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05)
reduced survival was found for IHC males when compacted at a height of 8 cm (Dunnett's multiple comparisons test, 5°C-3 h: P < 0.05; 5°C-24 h: P < 0.05; 10°C-3 h: P < 0.05)
For improved efficiency, immobilized males need to be transported and packed under cold conditions.
we recommend the chilling of Ae. albopictus male mosquitoes at 10°C for less than 3 h due to its lack of negative effects on their survival, longevity and mating competitiveness.
152.Rationale: The passage directly states that both chilling duration and compaction (vertical stacking height) affect survival and other quality metrics and that packing/transportation under cold conditions is required for operational efficiency. It also gives explicit thresholds (e.g., negative effects at 24 h chilling and at 8 cm compaction, and recommendation of 10°C for <3 h). Therefore balancing limits on chilling duration and compaction height is the appropriate operational factor to preserve male quality while enabling efficient packing/transport.

152.Option: The temperature during transportation must be maintained between 1 and 5°C to prevent premature recovery and ensure high survival rates
Evidence: The data from the temperature tracker showed that the temperature during packing and transportation fluctuated between 8 and 12°C.

Reduced survival of males was observed when males were chilled at 1°C, regardless of the durations (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.0001) (Table 1). After chilling for 24 h, the mean survival of IHC males was only 2.5% at 1°C as compared to about 89% at both 5 and 10°C (Table 1).
IHC males chilled at 5 and 10°C had a higher survival than at 1°C (Tukey post-hoc test: F(2, 87) = 35.07, P < 0.0001).
we recommend the chilling of Ae. albopictus male mosquitoes at 10°C for less than 3 h due to its lack of negative effects on their survival, longevity and mating competitiveness.
152.Rationale: The passage shows that 1°C causes heavy mortality and that optimal handling temperatures are in the 5–10°C range; transportation in the study fluctuated around 8–12°C and the authors recommend 10°C for short durations. Thus specifying 1–5°C is contrary to the evidence (1°C is harmful) and not supported as the correct transportation temperature range.

152.Option: Vertical stacking at a height of 8 cm should be used to maximize packing density and reduce transportation costs
Evidence: reduced survival was found for IHC males when compacted at a height of 8 cm (Dunnett's multiple comparisons test, 5°C-3 h: P < 0.05; 5°C-24 h: P < 0.05; 10°C-3 h: P < 0.05).

When males were compacted at different heights during chilling, we found that the compaction height had a significant impact on the survival of IHC males (One-way ANOVA, 5°C-3 h: F(3, 16) = 4.043, P = 0.0257; 5°C-24 h: F(3, 16) = 3.382, P = 0.0442; 10°C-3 h: F(3, 16) = 5.074, P = 0.0117).
Our results indicate that 3 h of chilling at 5°C or 10°C with either horizontal stacking or vertical compaction height of less than 6 cm did not affect the survival of IHC males.
a reduced survival rate was observed when males were vertically compacted a height of 8 cm.
152.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that vertical compaction at 8 cm reduces survival and that heights less than 6 cm do not affect survival. Therefore recommending 8 cm vertical stacking to maximize density is contraindicated by the data and would harm male quality.

152.Option: The use of sugar solution feeding before release should be avoided to prevent energy depletion and maintain mating competitiveness
Evidence: Two different feeding regimes were applied daily to the post-chilled IHC males: either water only or sugar solution (10%). Mosquitoes were allowed to feed on water or sugar solution ad libitum.

When post-chilled males were fed on water only, longevity of IHC males was reduced by a median of one-day when chilled for 24 h compared to the control ... The median adult longevity was 4 d for IHC0h, IHC5°C-3h and IHC10°C-3h males, and was reduced to 3 d for IHC5°C-24h and IHC10°C-24h males, respectively.

However, when post-chilled males were fed on 10% sugar solution, IHC5°C-24h males were found to live one-day less than other treatments of males ... The median longevity was 11 d for IHC5°C-24h males, and 12 d for the other treatment groups.

As glucose plays an important role in maintaining life, flight and mating of insects [53], we speculate that the decline in mating competitiveness may also be linked to the decrease in glucose reserves in post-chilled male mosquitoes as observed in this study.
152.Rationale: The passage shows that sugar feeding (10% solution) substantially increases longevity compared with water-only feeding and that glucose reserves support flight and mating. Thus avoiding sugar feeding before release would be expected to reduce, not preserve, male quality. The option is inconsistent with the study findings.

152.Option: The duration of chilling should be maximized to ensure complete immobilization and reduce the risk of male escape during release
Evidence: no significant difference was observed on the survival rate of IHC males when they were chilled for less than 6 h at 5°C and 1 h at 10°C ... however, the survival was slightly reduced when the chilling time reached 24 h at 5°C and over 1 h at 10°C (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05)
the competitive performance of the males in this study was reduced by approximately 50% when the chilling duration reached 24 h (Fig 3B & 3D).
male longevity significantly decreased if the chilling duration extended to 24 h.
we recommend the chilling of Ae. albopictus male mosquitoes at 10°C for less than 3 h due to its lack of negative effects on their survival, longevity and mating competitiveness.
152.Rationale: The passage documents negative effects of prolonged chilling (e.g., 24 h) on survival, longevity and mating competitiveness, and recommends short chilling durations (e.g., <3 h at 10°C). Maximizing chilling duration would therefore harm male quality and is contrary to the authors' findings and recommendations.

153.Option: The infection status of the brown dog tick vector must be confirmed, as the absence of infected ticks would explain the low prevalence in dogs and prevent spillover to humans
Evidence: E. canis is transmitted by the brown dog tick Rhipicephalus sanguineus [3], [4].
This low prevalence rate is interesting, as one would expect Malaysia to be a highly endemic region for E. canis due to the suitable climate and the abundance of the tick vector.
Thus further studies are needed to detect E. canis DNA from ticks, as there would be no transmission to dogs if the ticks are not infected.
153.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies Rhipicephalus sanguineus as the vector and states that detecting E. canis in ticks is necessary because "there would be no transmission to dogs if the ticks are not infected." Therefore confirming infection in the tick population is the critical factor in interpreting the low dog prevalence and directly determines potential transmission to dogs and onward spillover risk to humans.

153.Option: The geographic distribution of the samples must be analyzed, as regional differences in tick abundance and climate could influence the prevalence and affect spillover risk to humans
Evidence: A total of 500 blood samples were collected from dogs in Peninsular Malaysia, comprising 177 samples from stray dogs at shelters around Selangor state (144) and Langkawi Island (33), and 323 samples from dogs that were presented to private veterinary clinics from Selangor (86) Johor (30), Melaka (27), Sabah (3), and the veterinary teaching hospital at Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM), Selangor (177) for routine health care or specific treatment.
This low prevalence rate is interesting, as one would expect Malaysia to be a highly endemic region for E. canis due to the suitable climate and the abundance of the tick vector.
153.Rationale: The study sampled dogs from multiple locations and the authors note that climate and tick abundance would predict higher endemicity. Regional variation in tick density, infection prevalence in ticks, and climate can therefore influence observed dog prevalence and will affect local human spillover risk. The passage supports that geographic/contextual factors matter for interpreting prevalence.

153.Option: The use of PCR as a diagnostic method must be critically evaluated, as its sensitivity may be insufficient to detect low-level or chronic E. canis infections, which could explain the low prevalence rate
Evidence: Conversely, polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is a sensitive method of detection of acute monocytic ehrlichiosis in dogs; in fact it is designed to aim for the organism itself which makes PCR an invaluable technique capable of detecting traces of pathogen even before the onset of clinical signs [14].

subclinical and chronic ehrlichial infections are not as readily diagnosed as acute infections when canine blood is used for the detection of E. canis. Therefore, ideally, PCR using both blood and splenic aspirates should be considered to overcome this limitation [25].
Furthermore PCR sensitivity varies between laboratories and this fact may have contributed to the low number of positive dogs identified.
153.Rationale: The authors note both strengths and limitations of PCR: while sensitive for acute infection, chronic/subclinical infections may be missed when testing blood alone, and laboratory-to-laboratory sensitivity varies. Thus diagnostic sensitivity (and specimen choice) critically influences interpretation of the observed low prevalence because under-detection would underestimate true infection burden and hence mischaracterize spillover risk.

153.Option: The presence of clinical signs such as thrombocytopenia and anemia in dogs must be evaluated, as these symptoms are highly specific indicators of active E. canis infection and influence the interpretation of prevalence rates
Evidence: Among the clinic cases 27 (8.3%) dogs had both thrombocytopenia and anemia, but only one of them was positive for E. canis.
over diagnosis or misdiagnosis may result if a diagnosis is made solely on clinical or hematological findings as they are not specific for CME.
153.Rationale: The passage directly contradicts the option's claim of high specificity: hematological signs like thrombocytopenia and anemia are not specific for E. canis and relying on them can lead to misdiagnosis. The data show many dogs with those signs were PCR-negative, so these clinical signs are not reliable specific indicators and thus do not critically determine interpretation in the way the option asserts.

153.Option: The age and sex distribution of the dog population must be considered, as younger and male dogs are more susceptible to E. canis infection and could explain the low observed prevalence
Evidence: Information (age, breed, sex, and all laboratory results) of all the clinic cases was recorded, and the sex of the stray dogs was noted (Table 1).

Five hundred blood samples (323 clinic cases and 177 stray dogs) were evaluated using PCR in this study out of which ten were identified as E. canis positive, giving an overall prevalence rate of two (2.0%) percent.
153.Rationale: While age and sex were recorded, the passage provides no evidence that younger or male dogs are more susceptible or that age/sex explain the low prevalence. The authors do not report any age- or sex-specific differences in infection, so the specific assertion about younger/male susceptibility is unsupported by the text and therefore not a correct critical factor as stated.

154.Option: The presence of more health extension workers in Amhara and Somali regions increases early detection and reporting, leading to the observed regional variation
Evidence: An opportunity lies in leveraging the existing health extension program, which is a key component of the primary health system workforce.

The assessment indicated that noma is endemic in Ethiopia, with cases reported from all regions of the country and regional variation in case distribution. The highest case coming from Amhara region (45%) followed by Somali region (13%).
The strong focality of noma cases in Amhara and Somali region also goes also with the highest prevalence of childhood malnutrition on those regions [21].
154.Rationale: The passage mentions the health extension program as an opportunity to expand services but does not state that Amhara and Somali have more health extension workers or that such a workforce distribution explains the clustering. The text explicitly links the regional focality to high childhood malnutrition rather than to differential numbers of health extension workers, so there is no direct supporting evidence for this option in the passage.

154.Option: The higher population density in Amhara and Somali regions increases the likelihood of noma transmission and explains the regional clustering
Evidence: The assessment indicated that noma is endemic in Ethiopia, with cases reported from all regions of the country and regional variation in case distribution. The highest case coming from Amhara region (45%) followed by Somali region (13%).
The strong focality of noma cases in Amhara and Somali region also goes also with the highest prevalence of childhood malnutrition on those regions [21].
154.Rationale: The passage does not mention population density as an explanatory factor for noma clustering. Instead, it directly associates the focality in Amhara and Somali with higher childhood malnutrition. There is no evidence provided that higher population density underlies the observed regional variation, so this option is not supported by the text.

154.Option: The greater availability of specialized surgical care in Amhara and Somali regions leads to higher case reporting and thus explains the regional variation
Evidence: Most of the report (67 patients) were extracted from the NGO data base who are working on noma care.
Two of the only NGOs (Project Harar and Facing Africa) involved in Noma management and care in the country have been included in the study.

Tibebe Ghion Specialized Hospital: a public teaching Hospital of Bahir Dar University, in Amhara region, Bahir Dar. The Hospital has a head and neck surgeon who is performing noma surgery integrated in the routine care (Fig 2).

The assessment indicated that noma is endemic in Ethiopia, with cases reported from all regions of the country and regional variation in case distribution. The highest case coming from Amhara region (45%) followed by Somali region (13%).
The strong focality of noma cases in Amhara and Somali region also goes also with the highest prevalence of childhood malnutrition on those regions [21].

The claim that chickens are a primary source is therefore not supported by the evidence presented.

Typhi) and avian-adapted (S. Gallinarum) serovars, so it does not specifically implicate avian co-evolution. Thus the definitive causal claim in this option is not supported.

158.Rationale: The passage provides clear experimental evidence that spermidine (Spd) and spermine (Spm) increase autophagy and that polyamines can promote differentiation (metacyclogenesis). However, the option asserts that polyamine metabolism alone is sufficient to drive adaptation to the insect-vector environment. The passage also emphasizes that nutrient deprivation (starvation) is the common and frequent stimulus for differentiation and autophagy, and other experimental manipulations (e.g., DFMO, mutants, starvation) affect autophagy. Therefore, although polyamines contribute and can induce autophagy, the data do not demonstrate that polyamine metabolism alone is sufficient to drive adaptation to the insect gut environment; other stressors and pathways are involved.

158.Option: The colocalization of DQ-BSA and Atg8.1 vesicles in starved parasites indicates that autophagy is involved in nutrient recycling, supporting the hypothesis that autophagy enables adaptation through metabolic reprogramming
Evidence: we observed that starved parasites displayed a higher frequency of colocalization of DQ-BSA with TcAtg8.1, as compared to controls (Mander´s overlap coefficient 0.87 +/- 0.03 vs. 0.42 +/- 0.05), indicating fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes to form autolysosomes (Fig 2E).

nutrient deprivation, the classical inducer of autophagy, is the common most frequent stimulus required to trigger epimastigote differentiation.

This provides direct functional evidence that autophagy is operating to digest cellular components under starvation, consistent with nutrient recycling and metabolic reprogramming. Because the insect gut imposes nutritional stress and the passage links nutrient deprivation to induction of autophagy and differentiation, this observation strongly supports the hypothesis that autophagy enables adaptation to the insect-vector environment.

158.Option: Treatment with rapamycin increases autophagy and metacyclogenesis, suggesting that mTOR inhibition is the primary mechanism enabling adaptation to the insect vector environment

158.Rationale: The passage documents that rapamycin (an mTOR inhibitor) increases autophagy and can increase metacyclogenesis when applied in specific conditions, so it supports the idea that mTOR modulation can influence adaptation-related processes. However, stating that mTOR inhibition is the primary mechanism enabling adaptation to the insect gut overstates the data. Other pathways (e.g., Vps34/beclin-1 as shown by wortmannin effects, and polyamine-driven induction) also modulate autophagy and metacyclogenesis. The experiments show rapamycin is sufficient to induce autophagy in vitro but do not establish that mTOR inhibition is the primary physiological mechanism in the insect gut. Thus the observation is supportive but not definitive for the stronger causal claim in the option.

158.Option: Inhibition of autophagy during metacyclogenesis reduces the production of infective metacyclic trypomastigotes, indicating that autophagy is essential for parasite adaptation to nutrient scarcity in the insect vector
Evidence: Conversely, the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.

Conversely, treatment with 100 nM Wort of parasites exposed to differentiation conditions impaired the autophagic response (Fig 3A).

158.Rationale: The passage reports that inhibition of autophagy by wortmannin both impairs the autophagic response and significantly reduces the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes generated and the subsequent infection rate of host cells. This directly links autophagy activity to successful differentiation into infective forms under the starvation/differentiation protocol that models insect gut conditions. Such loss-of-function evidence (inhibition reduces infective-form production) is strong support that autophagy is essential for parasite adaptation to nutrient scarcity in the insect vector.

The percentage of parasites with more than two Atg8.1 positive vesicles was significantly higher in TAU (80 +/- 1.65%) than in control conditions (32.45 +/- 4.35%) (Fig 1B).
nutrient deprivation, the classical inducer of autophagy, is the common most frequent stimulus required to trigger epimastigote differentiation.
158.Rationale: The experimental data in the passage demonstrate that autophagy is markedly induced by nutritional and thermal stress (TAU starvation conditions) and that starved parasites show far higher numbers of Atg8.1-positive autophagosomes and lysosomal markers than parasites in nutrient-rich control media. Together these statements directly support the hypothesis that sustained exposure to live antigen (as in Live/Live) yields durable effector responses, so this option correctly answers the question.

159.Option: The Live/Live regimen induced a higher IFN-γ/IL-10 ratio than the DNA/Live regimen, indicating that repeated antigen exposure enhances balanced Th1/Regulatory responses
Evidence: The Leishmania-specific IFN-γ/IL-10 ratios upon stimulation with rA2-rCPA-rCPB and L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP recall antigens were higher in G1 and G2 compared to the control G3, G4 and G5 mice both at 4 and 8 weeks after challenge.

Because the option asserts a G2>G1 relationship that the passage does not document, the claim that Live/Live induced a higher ratio than DNA/Live is not supported and thus the option is incorrect.

159.Option: The Live/Live regimen induced higher IgG2a titers than the DNA/Live regimen, suggesting that repeated antigen exposure enhances Th1 memory formation
Evidence: Interestingly, in G2 vaccinated mice with Live L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP/Live L.tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP, a higher amount of F/T L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE-GFP specific IgG1 and IgG2a was detected in comparison to G1 and G4 vaccinated with DNA/Live modality (Fig. 4B).

Elevated IgG2a is an indirect humoral correlate and does not most directly demonstrate sustained antigen exposure producing durable immunity; a more direct feature is the sustained effector function (e.g., NO at 8 weeks) and longer-term parasite control. Because the option points to a less direct correlate, it does not best answer the question and is judged incorrect for the purpose asked.

The numbers of non L. infantum vectors species attracted to the dogs in the field experiments was too low to determine if there was a preference for infected rather than uninfected dogs.
160.Rationale: The passage emphasizes specific vector species (P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi) rather than a benefit from broader vector diversity. It notes P. perniciosus is the dominant vector and explicitly states that numbers of non-L. infantum vector species were too low to assess preference, which undermines the claim that simply having multiple sand fly species is the primary driver of emergence. The paper instead focuses on the role of particular competent vectors and host–vector interactions.

160.Option: The density of sand fly populations in proximity to domestic dog habitats, as higher vector abundance increases the probability of transmission initiation
Evidence: From an eco-epidemiological view, the parasite requires an overlap between the vector (P. perniciosus) and the reservoir host (dogs), which is a prerequisite for the emergence of a ZVL focus.
Phlebotomus perniciosus was the most abundant sand fly species (n = 1228, 63.3%) followed by P. perfiliewi (n = 662, 34.1%).
Our results showed that dogs infected with L. infantum were highly attractive to both female and male P. perniciosus.
This odour manipulation ensures successful transmission of the parasite to the vector.
160.Rationale: While vector abundance and proximity (overlap) are indeed relevant—as indicated by the statement that overlap between vector and reservoir is a prerequisite—the passage's main observed biological interaction that the authors highlight as amplifying transmission is that infected dogs become more attractive to sand flies. Thus although sand fly density near dogs matters, the passage emphasizes altered attractiveness of infected dogs as the strongest mechanistic driver of emergence, so density alone is not presented as the single most influential factor.

160.Option: The proportion of dogs in the population that are symptomatic and clinically infected with L. infantum, as these individuals are the most infectious to sand flies
Evidence: Domestic dogs are the main reservoir host for L. infantum in the Old World [8,9] and in the New World [10] ...
In previous studies in Tunisia we showed that the prevalence of ZVL in dogs is an important parameter for determining transmission to humans [4,15].
Several studies have reported that symptomatic dogs, infected with L. infantum are highly infectious to their sand fly vectors compared to oligosymptomatic and asymptomatic dogs [51–58].
160.Rationale: The passage supports that higher prevalence of infected dogs and symptomatic infection increases infectiousness to vectors, which would raise emergence risk. However, the core experimental finding the paper stresses is that infected dogs are more attractive to sand flies (odour manipulation), creating a feedback that enhances transmission. Thus although the proportion of symptomatic/clinically infected dogs is important, the passage emphasizes attractiveness-mediated amplification as the key mechanistic influence, making the symptomatic proportion an important but not the single most emphasized factor in the text.

160.Option: The presence of infected dogs that are more attractive to sand flies due to altered odour, creating a feedback loop that amplifies transmission
Evidence: From an eco-epidemiological view, the parasite requires an overlap between the vector (P. perniciosus) and the reservoir host (dogs), which is a prerequisite for the emergence of a ZVL focus.

Our hypothesis ... physiological changes in dogs parasitized by L. infantum change the dog’s odour making them more attractive to P. perniciosus and therefore enhancing the parasite’ s transmission success.
Our results showed that dogs infected with L. infantum were highly attractive to both female and male P. perniciosus.
This odour manipulation ensures successful transmission of the parasite to the vector.
Greater attractiveness of infected dogs compared to uninfected dogs would have major epidemiological significance.
160.Rationale: The passage explicitly demonstrates and emphasizes that infected dogs emit altered odours that make them substantially more attractive to the sand fly vectors, and it frames this odour-mediated attraction as a mechanism that enhances transmission and could create a feedback loop amplifying parasite spread. The authors identify this host manipulation as a major epidemiological driver and as having direct impact on transmission dynamics, making this factor the strongest influencer of new focus emergence according to the study.

160.Option: The availability of alternative hosts such as rodents or livestock, as these can dilute vector feeding and reduce transmission to dogs
Evidence: The general zoophilic feeding behaviour of P. perniciosus and L. longipalpis based on host availability rather than on attractiveness to specific hosts should lead to a dilution of the parasite as it is spread to non-competent hosts, and this would subsequently exert a zooprophylactic effect on the transmission of L. infantum [49].
Our work illustrates a potential zoopotentiation effect exerted by infected reservoir hosts that are highly attractive to sand fly vectors of L. infantum.
160.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that alternative hosts can dilute vector feeding (zooprophylaxis) and thus reduce transmission to dogs, which would lower the likelihood of a new focus. However, this is described as a countervailing effect; the paper's main finding is that infected dogs can override such dilution through increased attractiveness (zoopotentiation). Therefore availability of alternative hosts is a factor that could reduce emergence risk, but it is not presented as the dominant influence compared with the strong amplification caused by attractive infected dogs.

161.Option: The size of the administrative unit selected for surveying directly determines the number of individuals sampled and thus influences both cost and accuracy of intervention classification
Evidence: The district, or an area of equivalent size, is the recommended implementation unit (IU) for LF elimination [23].
The MoH-GoSS therefore decided to consider both the payam and the county as IUs of the Integrated NTD Control Programme.

The outcome from this investment was that 21 payams in the five counties of Northern Bahr-el-Ghazal State, an area the size of Belgium, were surveyed for LF, schistosomiasis, STH infection and loiasis.

If the standard operating procedures of the survey were thus modified, quantities of ICT tests, safety lancets and other supplies required to safely take blood samples would be reduced by approximately 80%, incurring substantial savings on survey consumables, particularly ICTs that cost >USD 4.0 per test. Furthermore, existing vehicles and staff could be deployed in three teams, which would decrease the survey duration by about one day per payam and reduced the majority of running costs accordingly. The cost per IU classified could thus be reduced by about 25% without any apparent risk of missing LF foci.
This procedure would have yielded the same classification of IUs for LF elimination in Northern Bahr-el-Ghazal State [19] and is likely to be appropriate throughout Southern Sudan.
161.Rationale: The passage explicitly treats the choice of IU (payam versus county/district) as central to both sampling strategy and costs. The recommended IU for LF is at district/county level and the programme considered payam and county as possible IUs. Changing the IU size alters the number of sites and individuals sampled (and thus the quantities of ICTs and other consumables) and can substantially reduce cost per IU (∼25% stated) while maintaining classification accuracy for LF in this setting. Therefore unit size is a critical factor that governs both accuracy of classification and cost-efficiency.

161.Option: The diagnostic method used for urinary schistosomiasis affects both the accuracy of intervention classification and the cost-efficiency of survey design due to its specificity in the local context
Evidence: Testing for urinary schistosomiasis infection could, for example, be conducted only with Hemastix reagent sticks (Bayer Diagnostics, Basingstoke, UK), instead of the current procedure whereby all samples with blood in urine and a sub-sample of negatives were cross checked using urine filtration. By cutting out the cost of these and other supplies for urine filtration, the cost per implementation unit surveyed would have decreased by 2% (Table 3).

This small reduction in economic cost is explained by the fact that urine filtration can be easily conducted alongside other operations; personnel requirements (the major cost driver) and survey duration would thus remain unchanged.

In Southern Sudan, however, this modification would result in considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment with praziquantel owing to the relatively low specificity of Hemastix reagent strips in this setting [34].
161.Rationale: The passage directly addresses the trade-off between diagnostic choice, cost, and classification accuracy for urinary schistosomiasis. Using reagent strips alone would yield modest cost savings (~2%) but, because Hemastix has relatively low specificity in Southern Sudan, it would cause considerable misclassification of IUs for treatment. Thus the diagnostic method chosen critically affects both the accuracy of intervention classification and the cost-efficiency of the survey design in this local context.

161.Option: The use of a single standardized diagnostic test for all NTDs across all administrative units ensures both cost-efficiency and high accuracy in intervention classification
Evidence: Because of these differing transmission ecologies, prevalence data on each disease are required to identify areas of overlap and to target these with integrated PCT delivery [13].

The composition of survey teams varied depending on the number of NTDs to be surveyed in each location. Where all four NTDs were surveyed, teams included ... up to four technicians ... One or two technicians undertook blood sampling and LF testing while the other two to three prepared and read stool and urine samples.
161.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that different NTDs have differing transmission ecologies and require disease-specific prevalence data and diagnostic procedures (e.g. blood tests for LF, stool/urine for schistosomiasis and STH). Team composition and procedures varied by disease surveyed. There is no support for a single standardized diagnostic for all NTDs; in fact the text implies that disease-specific diagnostics are necessary for accurate classification. Therefore the option claiming a universal single test both ensures cost-efficiency and high accuracy is not supported.

161.Option: The geographic distribution of disease transmission zones determines the optimal implementation unit and thus balances cost and accuracy in survey design
Evidence: Co-endemicity, the prerequisite for the anticipated efficiency gains of integrated control, applies not at the country level, but at sub-national levels where climatic and other determinants are suitable for the transmission of more than one of above diseases [6], [7].

For example, onchocerciasis is associated with fast flowing rivers [8], schistosomiasis occurs near calmer or stagnant waterbodies [9], whilst STH infection, trachoma and LF occur over relatively large areas [10]–[12]. Because of these differing transmission ecologies, prevalence data on each disease are required to identify areas of overlap and to target these with integrated PCT delivery [13].
The MoH-GoSS therefore decided to consider both the payam and the county as IUs of the Integrated NTD Control Programme.
161.Rationale: The passage makes clear that differing transmission ecologies produce distinct geographic distributions for each disease and that co-endemicity is a sub-national phenomenon. These spatial patterns inform the choice of implementation unit (payam versus county) used to classify areas for intervention. Thus geographic distribution of transmission zones is a key determinant of the optimal IU, which in turn affects both the accuracy of classification and the cost-efficiency of survey design.

161.Option: The number of survey teams deployed across administrative units affects the precision of intervention classification and the total financial burden of the survey
Evidence: The composition of survey teams varied depending on the number of NTDs to be surveyed in each location. Where all four NTDs were surveyed, teams included two drivers, one supervisor, one interviewer/translator, and up to four technicians, travelling in two vehicles.

If the standard operating procedures of the survey were thus modified, quantities of ICT tests, safety lancets and other supplies required to safely take blood samples would be reduced by approximately 80%, incurring substantial savings on survey consumables, particularly ICTs that cost >USD 4.0 per test. Furthermore, existing vehicles and staff could be deployed in three teams, which would decrease the survey duration by about one day per payam and reduced the majority of running costs accordingly.
We found the major cost drivers to be two categories of recurrent costs – laboratory consumables and personnel.
161.Rationale: While the passage indicates that team composition and deployment affect operational duration and running costs (and thus the financial burden), it does not present the number of teams as the principal determinant of the precision (accuracy) of intervention classification. Precision/accuracy are tied more directly to sampling design (IU size, number of sites and individuals sampled) and diagnostic methods, and the authors identified consumables and personnel as the major cost drivers. Thus although number of teams influences costs and logistics, the claim that it most critically affects both precision of classification and total financial burden is not supported by the text.

162.Option: The human-animal-environment interface shaped by occupation and land use patterns
Evidence: Climate and environmental changes could affect the reservoir ecology and dynamics of rodent carriers and hence trigger the spread of hantavirus transmission [5,8,9].

With the acceleration of China’s urbanization process, especially in the process of rapid transition of China’s agriculture-related landscapes to urban landscapes, the dual role of climate change and environmental change has led to a leap in the epidemic area range of HFRS.

This study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.

factors relating to the dynamics of the rodent hosts and human activities, such as urbanization indicators, should be considered when understanding the results from these ecological studies, given the fact that China is a topographically heterogeneous country.
air temperature as an isolated indicator that cannot explain the HFRS incidence fully, confounding factors should always be considered.
162.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that temperature is indirectly associated with HFRS incidence and that variation in human activities, land use (urbanization, agriculture-to-urban transition), and rodent reservoir dynamics shape where and when people are exposed. These human-animal-environment factors (occupation, land use) therefore critically alter the observed temperature–disease association across regions and times by changing exposure and host ecology, and the authors explicitly state such factors should be considered when interpreting results.

162.Option: The seasonality of rodent population dynamics in different regions
Evidence: Climate and environmental changes could affect the reservoir ecology and dynamics of rodent carriers and hence trigger the spread of hantavirus transmission [5,8,9].
Effect of temperature on the HFRS epidemic was seasonal

The study from Changchun City, Jilin Province [18] and Changsha City [33] found that HFRS was prone to outbreaks and epidemics during relatively dry, high temperature and low wind conditions from late July to late September.
162.Rationale: While the passage links reservoir ecology and seasonal patterns to HFRS activity and gives examples of seasonal outbreaks, it frames rodent dynamics as one of several important determinants. The question asks which factor most critically influences interpretation across geographic and temporal scales; the passage gives stronger emphasis to broader human-animal-environment interface and to data scale/aggregation issues. Seasonal rodent dynamics are relevant but are a component of the broader interface rather than the single most critical factor emphasized across the review.

162.Option: The type of statistical model used to analyze the temperature-HFRS relationship
Evidence: With regards to the statistical methods adopted by the researchers to explore the correlation between air temperature and HFRS incidence, Spearman correlation analysis, Pearson correlation analysis, generalized additive model, seasonal differential autoregressive moving average model, negative binomial multivariate regression analysis, distributed lag nonlinear model conditions, conditional logistic regression analysis and wavelet analysis have been indicated in the included studies, as shown in Table 1.

The choice of appropriate statistical methods according to the type of HFRS epidemic area, the availability, fineness and data distribution characteristics of historical data such as epidemic situation or meteorological data in various regions is being another challenge.
162.Rationale: The passage notes a wide variety of statistical methods were used and that method choice is a challenge; however, it highlights spatial/temporal scale, study period, confounding (rodent/human factors), and data aggregation as primary reasons for heterogeneous findings. Thus the statistical model is important but not portrayed as the single most critical influence on interpretation across geographic and temporal scales.

162.Option: The length of the lag period between temperature exposure and HFRS onset
Evidence: Twenty-one studies considered the lagged effect of air temperature factors on the HFRS activity and the longest lag period considered in the included studies was 34 weeks.

Further investigation is warranted to detect the thresholds of meteorological factors for the HFRS early warning purposes, to measure the duration of lagged effects and determine the timing of maximum effects for reducing the effects of meteorological factors on HFRS via continuous interventions and to identify the vulnerable populations for target protection.

The appropriate adoption of geographical scale, data aggregation unit, the length of lag period and the length of incidence collection period should be considered when exploring the relationship between HFRS incidence and meteorological factors such as air temperature.
162.Rationale: Lag period is clearly important—the review documents many studies considered lags and recommends measuring lag duration—but the authors present lag length as one of several methodological considerations (alongside spatial/temporal scale, data aggregation, study period, and host/human factors). The question asks for the most critical influence across geographic and temporal scales; lag length matters but is not emphasized as the primary driver of inter-regional and temporal heterogeneity.

162.Option: The spatial resolution of the temperature data used in the analysis
Evidence: ecological effects of air temperature on HFRS incidence could be affected by the spatial or temporal scale of the data and also the study period involved, which might help to partly understand the contradicting observations in the included studies.

The choice of appropriate statistical methods according to the type of HFRS epidemic area, the availability, fineness and data distribution characteristics of historical data such as epidemic situation or meteorological data in various regions is being another challenge.

Among the included 27 studies, one study analyzed the HFRS data at the national level which included the data from 31 provinces, autonomous regions and municipalities in mainland China, and the remaining 26 studies involved 12 provinces and autonomous regions. ... Five studies collected HFRS data at provincial level, 17 studies collected HFRS data at municipal level, and four studies collected HFRS data at county (local) level.

Regarding the temporal unit of data aggregation, in the included studies, fifteen studies were based on monthly HFRS incidence or the number of monthly incident HFRS cases, five studies were based on the number of daily reported HFRS cases, two studies based on annual HFRS incidence, four studies based on both monthly and annual HFRS incidence, and one study was based on the number of weekly reported HFRS cases.

The appropriate adoption of geographical scale, data aggregation unit, the length of lag period and the length of incidence collection period should be considered when exploring the relationship between HFRS incidence and meteorological factors such as air temperature.
162.Rationale: The review repeatedly highlights that spatial (geographical) scale and the fineness ('availability, fineness') of meteorological data affect observed temperature–HFRS associations and may explain contradictory findings across studies. Differences in spatial resolution (national vs provincial vs municipal vs county, and corresponding temperature data) directly influence exposure measurement, ecological inference, and comparability across regions and times, making spatial resolution a critical influence on interpretation.

164.Option: Infants with low basal ETK and high maternal basal ETK are at substantially increased risk of severe infantile beriberi
Evidence: A low maternal basal ETK (<lower limit reference range for age, Table 2) was significantly associated with infantile beriberi (OR 5.4, 95%CI 1.11 to 26.0, n = 17 matched sets giving 45 observations for analysis).
Maternal basal ETK was significantly linearly correlated with infant basal ETK (Pearson's r = 0.66, p<0.001) (Fig. 2).

Basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb gave a sensitivity (95%CI) of 75.0 (47.6 to 92.7)% and specificity (95%CI) of 85.2 (66.3 to 95.8)% for predicting beriberi. The odds of having beriberi were 15.9 times higher (95%CI 2.03 to 124.2; p = 0.008, area under ROC curve = 0.80) for infants with basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb when compared with patients with basal value ≥ 0.59.
164.Rationale: The passage reports that low (not high) maternal basal ETK was significantly associated with infantile beriberi and that maternal and infant basal ETK are positively correlated. Therefore a combination of infant low basal ETK plus high maternal basal ETK is not supported as indicating increased risk. The relevant evidence shows maternal low basal ETK (not high) is associated with infant beriberi and that infant low basal ETK itself is strongly predictive (OR 15.9).

164.Option: Infants with high α coefficient and detectable troponin T are at substantially increased risk of severe infantile beriberi
Evidence: In contrast, the infant α coefficient did not discriminate between cases and controls (OR 1.0 95%CI 0.997–1.01; n = 40 matched sets providing 112 observations for analysis). Using α≥25% or >31% to define biochemical thiamin deficiency were likewise not useful for predicting infantile beriberi (OR 1.48, 95%CI 0.66–3.31 and OR 1.90, 95%CI 0.77–4.70, respectively).

However, the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations). Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.
164.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the infant α coefficient did not discriminate between cases and controls and that common α cutoffs were not useful predictors. Although detectable troponin T was associated with beriberi (OR 3.4), a high α is not supported as a strong indicator. Therefore the combination of high α plus detectable troponin T is not a strongly supported marker of high risk.

164.Option: Infants with elevated ntBNP and detectable troponin T are at substantially increased risk of severe infantile beriberi
Evidence: The continuous measure for ntBNP was borderline associated with beriberi (OR 1.00, 95%CI 1.000009–1.0002), but this analysis included only 11 matched sets with at least one control (n = 28 observations).

The median (range) plasma ntBNP in beriberi infants (35,000 (128 to ≥35,000) pg/ml) was higher than in those without beriberi (1,049 (96 to 35,000) pg/ml). Using the Soldin et al. [41] cutoffs for ntBNP, eleven (73.3%) infants with beriberi, but only one of the controls (4.8%), had raised ntBNP. Prediction using these ntBNP cut-offs gave a sensitivity (95%CI) of 63.6 (30.8 to 89.1) % and specificity (95%CI) of 94.1 (71.3 to 99.9) %.

However, the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations).
164.Rationale: While ntBNP was often markedly higher in beriberi infants and using cutoffs it showed good specificity, the analysis was limited (small subset: only 11 matched sets) and the association was described as 'borderline'. The study emphasizes basal ETK and troponin T as consistent predictors; ntBNP data are promising but limited. The passage does not present combined ntBNP+troponin analyses or show that this combination is the strongest indicator, so this option is not well supported as 'most strongly' suggesting high risk.

164.Option: Infants with both low basal ETK (<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb) and detectable troponin T are at substantially increased risk of severe infantile beriberi
Evidence: Basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb gave a sensitivity (95%CI) of 75.0 (47.6 to 92.7)% and specificity (95%CI) of 85.2 (66.3 to 95.8)% for predicting beriberi. The odds of having beriberi were 15.9 times higher (95%CI 2.03 to 124.2; p = 0.008, area under ROC curve = 0.80) for infants with basal ETK<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb when compared with patients with basal value ≥ 0.59.

However, the odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations). Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.

Detectable plasma troponin T and infant basal ETK<59 micromoles/min/gHb remained significant independent predictors for infantile beriberi (OR 4.3, 95%CI 1.25 to 15.0, adjusted for mother observed food taboos, and OR 15.9, 95%CI 2.03 to 124.2, for detectable troponin T and ETK<59 micromoles/min/gHb, respectively).
Infants with low basal ETK tended to have higher troponin T (Spearman's rho = −0.39, p = 0.02, Fig. 1).
164.Rationale: The passage identifies low infant basal ETK (<0.59 micromoles/min/gHb) as a strong predictor (OR 15.9) and detectable troponin T as an independent predictor (OR ~3.4), and states that both remained significant in adjusted models. The coexistence of low basal ETK and detectable troponin T therefore most strongly indicates a high risk of clinical infantile beriberi and severe (cardiac) disease in this study population.

165.Option: The spatial resolution of the administrative units used to define trial sites
Evidence: Given the extensive spatial heterogeneity in LF incidence, we collated epidemiological data at the 1st and 2nd administrative levels (admin1 and admin2) in each country within the study region.

Our FOI estimates and modeled annual LF incidence rates indicate that Sierra Leone, southern Guinea near the border with Sierra Leone and Liberia, and a few high incidence regions within Nigeria would likely yield the highest LF case incidence rates during a vaccine trial. Comparisons of our estimates at the 1st and 2nd administrative levels show that there is considerable spatial heterogeneity among different admin2s within the same 1st administrative unit (particularly in Nigeria, Guinea, and Mali), and therefore predictions from the 2nd administrative level are likely to be more useful for site selection.

At the 2nd administrative level, LF incidence rates calculated from projected FOI estimates were >10 per 1,000 for several administrative units with seroreversion rates of 3% or 6%, but not 0% (Tables 3, S7 and S9).
165.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that finer spatial resolution (admin2) is more useful for identifying high-incidence hotspots and for site selection, so spatial resolution is an important design consideration. However, the question asks which factor would most critically influence the ability to detect a causal effect on symptomatic disease; the passage places stronger emphasis on factors that determine the number of observed symptomatic endpoints (e.g., surveillance sensitivity, baseline susceptibility), rather than spatial resolution per se. Thus although spatial resolution matters for choosing sites, it is not the single most critical factor for detecting a causal vaccine effect.

165.Option: The estimated force of infection (FOI) at the 1st administrative level in the target region
Evidence: In our epidemiological model, we included 14 West African countries ... and collated epidemiological data at the 1st and 2nd administrative levels (admin1 and admin2) in each country within the study region.
Due to the spatial heterogeneity in our FOI estimates, the highest LF incidence rates were found at the admin2 as opposed to the admin1 level.

No admin1 units had an LF incidence rate of greater than 10 per 1,000 (1%) with a 0% or 3% seroreversion rate, and only Ondo State in Nigeria had an LG incidence rate >10/1,000 at a 6% seroreversion rate based on our projected FOI estimates.
165.Rationale: FOI is certainly relevant because it underlies incidence and thus the number of symptomatic cases available to measure vaccine effect. However, the passage stresses that admin1-level FOI can mask heterogeneity and that admin2-level estimates are more informative for finding high-incidence sites.
