More importantly, the text highlights surveillance/reporting sensitivity and baseline serostatus as the primary determinants of observed symptomatic endpoints. Therefore relying on admin1 FOI alone is not the most critical factor for detecting a causal effect.

165.Option: The presence of a well-established healthcare infrastructure in the trial region
Evidence: Access to healthcare facilities varies considerably, which—along with other factors—influences the probability that symptomatic individuals will seek treatment.

To identify population-level covariates associated with LF occurrence, we used spatial datasets of environmental, climate, and socioeconomic variables ... the Healthcare Access and Quality Index (HAQ) based on mortality from causes amenable to personal health care [37],

Our model does not account for non-epidemiological considerations that may influence site selection, such as the strength of the existing local or national health infrastructure system, political stability, or cultural barriers to trial implementation.
165.Rationale: Healthcare infrastructure affects healthcare-seeking and surveillance capacity, which in turn influence case detection. The passage recognizes health infrastructure as an important non-epidemiological consideration for site selection. However, the question asks which factor would most critically influence the ability to detect a causal effect on symptomatic disease; the passage identifies surveillance sensitivity (the fraction of infections captured) and baseline seroprevalence as the proximate drivers of observed endpoints. Infrastructure is an indirect contributor to surveillance sensitivity, not the single most critical factor discussed.

165.Option: The proportion of the population that is seropositive at baseline in the trial region
Evidence: Trial site selection also needs to account for the baseline seroprevalence in a target population, as that will influence the fraction of the population that is susceptible to infection.
Locations with high baseline seroprevalence may experience few LF cases even if LASV is actively circulating in the rodent population.

The level of protection against reinfection and disease among both seropositive and seroreverted individuals also influenced expected LF incidence rates in our model, with higher levels of protection against disease resulting in lower expected incidence rates.
165.Rationale: Baseline seroprevalence directly determines how many individuals are susceptible and therefore the expected number of symptomatic cases during a trial. The passage explicitly says seroprevalence will influence the susceptible fraction and that locations with high baseline seroprevalence may experience few LF cases despite circulation. Fewer cases reduce power to detect a vaccine effect on symptomatic disease. Thus baseline seroprevalence is a critical factor for trial ability to detect causal effects.

165.Option: The sensitivity of surveillance for symptomatic cases relative to the true number of infections
Evidence: A majority of LF cases are not detected by current clinical surveillance systems, at least in part because common LF symptoms—such as fever, malaise, headache, and muscle pain—closely resemble those of other febrile illnesses endemic to the region.
We also estimate that as few as 0.2% of LASV infections are captured by current surveillance systems.

Ensuring sufficient statistical power may require a large increase in the number of individuals enrolled in a field trial or a longer trial period spanning multiple transmission seasons. One alternative to increasing the size of the study population would be to use protection against infection as a primary endpoint instead of protection against symptomatic disease. Due to the high number of asymptomatic infections, active monitoring for seroconversion—while more difficult and costly than symptom-based surveillance methods—would increase the number of expected endpoints without increasing the size of the study population.
165.Rationale: Detecting a causal effect on symptomatic disease depends on observing enough symptomatic endpoints. The passage emphasizes that syndromic surveillance currently captures a tiny fraction of infections (as low as 0.2%) and that under-detection severely limits power, often necessitating much larger or longer trials or alternative endpoints (e.g., seroconversion). Therefore, the sensitivity of surveillance for symptomatic cases is one of the most critical factors determining whether a vaccine effect on symptomatic disease can be detected.

166.Option: Perform a single hotspot analysis using the full 6-year dataset and identify sub-counties that remain high-priority across all years
Evidence: Cholera cases varied by month and year, and while there was not a readily discernable seasonal or cyclical pattern, there were extended periods with relatively high cholera transmission (May 2015-August 2016, June 2017-July 2018, and February 2019-November 2019) interspersed with months of relatively sparse or low activity (Fig 1).

The sub-counties with the highest case count and incidence varied by year, suggesting that outbreaks in different regions of the country contributed to the periods with relatively high transmission (Figs B2 in S1 Text and 2).
166.Rationale: Aggregating all six years into a single hotspot analysis obscures temporal variation and would not directly assess whether the spatial distribution of hotspots is consistent across separate time periods. The passage explicitly notes that cases and the sub-counties with the highest incidence varied by year, so a single pooled analysis cannot determine stability of hotspots over time.

166.Option: Calculate the overall mean annual incidence and persistence across all years to identify the most consistently high-risk sub-counties
Evidence: For each geographic scale, we identified cholera burden hotspots, also known as priority intervention areas, according to the GTFCC-recommended method [12], which uses two quantitative indicators: 1) mean annual incidence (MAI), the mean of the annual cholera incidence rate (cases per 100,000 population) over the analysis period; 2) persistence, the percentage of weeks in the analysis period where at least one cholera case was reported [12],

The sub-counties with the highest case count and incidence varied by year, suggesting that outbreaks in different regions of the country contributed to the periods with relatively high transmission (Figs B2 in S1 Text and 2).
166.Rationale: While the study used MAI and persistence across the analysis period to identify hotspots, calculating overall MAI/persistence across all years does not test whether the spatial pattern is consistent across distinct time periods. The passage notes year-to-year variation, so an approach that preserves temporal resolution is needed to assess consistency.

166.Option: Use a time-series clustering algorithm to group sub-counties based on their case reporting patterns and identify stable clusters over time
Evidence: Cholera cases varied by month and year, and while there was not a readily discernable seasonal or cyclical pattern, there were extended periods with relatively high cholera transmission (May 2015-August 2016, June 2017-July 2018, and February 2019-November 2019) interspersed with months of relatively sparse or low activity (Fig 1).

The sub-counties with the highest case count and incidence varied by year, suggesting that outbreaks in different regions of the country contributed to the periods with relatively high transmission (Figs B2 in S1 Text and 2).
166.Rationale: Time-series clustering is an appropriate analytic method to group sub-counties by their temporal case patterns and to identify clusters that exhibit stable high-risk behavior versus those with sporadic outbreaks. Given the documented month-to-month and year-to-year variability and lack of simple seasonal pattern, clustering temporal trajectories would directly evaluate whether certain spatial units show consistent patterns over time.

166.Option: Conduct annual hotspot analyses at the sub-county level and assess the overlap of high-priority areas across time periods
Evidence: The sub-counties with the highest case count and incidence varied by year, suggesting that outbreaks in different regions of the country contributed to the periods with relatively high transmission (Figs B2 in S1 Text and 2).

We also explored the impact of alternate thresholds for MAI (Fig E and Table E in S1 Text). For example, when reducing the primary analysis’s 90th percentile threshold for MAI to the 80th percentile threshold, 11% or over 5 million people lived in priority areas (compared to the 6% or roughly 3 million people in the primary sub-county analysis).
high priority sub-counties were relatively stable under different cleaning methods and threshold settings.
166.Rationale: Performing the hotspot analysis separately for each year (or defined time period) and then measuring spatial overlap (which sub-counties are repeatedly classified as high-priority) is a direct and practical way to assess temporal consistency. This preserves temporal resolution and mirrors the study’s own approach of sensitivity analyses and examining stability of identified high-priority areas under different assumptions.

166.Option: Compare the proportion of high-priority sub-counties that reported cases in each year to assess whether the same areas remain at risk
Evidence: 151 sub-counties (45% of the population) that had no reported cases in the analysis period.

Cholera cases varied by month and year, and while there was not a readily discernable seasonal or cyclical pattern, there were extended periods with relatively high cholera transmission ... interspersed with months of relatively sparse or low activity (Fig 1).
While the IDSR program reports cholera line lists at the county level, sub-county level reporting is not as consistent.
166.Rationale: Simply comparing proportions of high-priority sub-counties that reported any cases each year is a crude metric that does not capture spatial overlap or stability of hotspot locations, and it is sensitive to reporting completeness. Given substantial numbers of sub-counties with no reported cases and inconsistent sub-county reporting, this approach could be misleading and would not robustly determine whether the spatial distribution of high-risk areas is consistent over time.

167.Option: The Andean region's robust healthcare infrastructure leads to more accurate case detection and reporting, while the Amazon region's underreporting due to remote geography results in artificially low case counts despite high incidence
Evidence: Some departments in the Orinoco and Amazon regions, have shown high transmission rates and prevalence in independent studies [9, 32]. These same departments report fewer cases to the central surveillance system than departments in other regions with more robust healthcare infrastructure.
Despite the existence of a reporting system, underreporting is common in the most remote rural areas.
The Andean region had the highest number of cases for all periods, reaching a total of 12,847 cases between 2009 and 2010.
Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
167.Rationale: While the passage does state that departments in the Orinoco and Amazon report fewer cases to the central system and that underreporting is common in remote areas, the option frames Amazon as having "artificially low case counts despite high incidence." The passage actually reports the Amazon region as having the highest incidence rates (not artificially low incidence) and highlights that the Andean region's high case counts occur alongside a much larger population base. The primary explanation offered in the text for the discrepancy between high case counts but not high incidence in the Andean region is the large population base, not solely differences in reporting completeness. Therefore this option overstates reporting as the most likely single explanation and is not the best match to the passage's emphasized cause.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher socioeconomic status leads to better access to healthcare and earlier diagnosis, resulting in more reported cases, while the Amazon region's lower socioeconomic status results in delayed diagnosis and fewer reported cases despite high transmission
Evidence: Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].
Despite the existence of a reporting system, underreporting is common in the most remote rural areas.

Some departments in the Orinoco and Amazon regions, have shown high transmission rates and prevalence in independent studies [9, 32]. These same departments report fewer cases to the central surveillance system than departments in other regions with more robust healthcare infrastructure.
The Andean region had the highest number of cases for all periods, reaching a total of 12,847 cases between 2009 and 2010.

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
167.Rationale: The passage does mention socioeconomic diversity and differences in reporting/healthcare sophistication between regions, which could influence case detection. However, it does not present ‘‘higher socioeconomic status’’ of the Andean region as the principal explanation for the pattern described. Instead, the text emphasizes that the Andean region contains over 50% of the country's population and that its large population base is why many cases do not translate into high incidence. Thus socioeconomic differences and delayed diagnosis may play a role, but they are not presented as the most likely explanation in the passage.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher urbanization and population density increase the number of susceptible individuals, leading to more cases, while the Amazon region's lower population density limits transmission despite high vector abundance
Evidence: The Andean region showed an increasing tendency, which might be due to a variety of factors, including outbreaks, and changes which facilitate the infection’s permanence, urbanization, and deforestation [22–26].

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
The Amazon region exhibits the highest incidence rates for all observed periods. Incidence in this region varies from 76.75 to 240.93 cases per 100,000 inhabitants.
This can be attributed to the wealth of vector species and of mammals that serve as disease reservoirs, which helps maintain the epidemiological circuit and, in turn, transmission rates [8].
167.Rationale: The passage does link urbanization and deforestation to disease persistence in the Andean region, but it explicitly states that the Amazon has the highest incidence rates despite a relatively low population. The Amazon's lower population density does not ‘‘limit transmission’’ according to the text; rather, high vector abundance and reservoirs in the Amazon contribute to high incidence. The key explanation for why the Andean has many cases but not high incidence is its large population base (dilution), not that the Amazon's lower density limits transmission. Therefore this option misstates the Amazon pattern and is not supported as the primary factor.

167.Option: The Andean region's large population base dilutes its incidence rate despite high case counts, while the Amazon region's smaller population amplifies its incidence despite lower absolute case numbers
Evidence: The Andean region had the highest number of cases for all periods, reaching a total of 12,847 cases between 2009 and 2010.
Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography [27].

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
The Amazon region exhibits the highest incidence rates for all observed periods. Incidence in this region varies from 76.75 to 240.93 cases per 100,000 inhabitants.

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
167.Rationale: The passage directly states that the Andean region accounts for the largest number of cases but also contains over half the country's population, and that the Amazon region has a relatively small population yet very high incidence. Thus the most likely explanation for high absolute case counts in the Andean region coexisting with relatively stable (not elevated) incidence is a large denominator (population) diluting incidence, while the Amazon's smaller population makes its incidence rates appear much higher despite fewer absolute cases. This answer best matches the textual evidence.

167.Option: The Andean region's higher prevalence of multiple Leishmania species leads to more severe cases and thus higher case counts, while the Amazon region's lower species diversity results in milder disease and fewer reported cases
Evidence: It is worth noting that Colombia is the country with the greatest number of circulating species in the world, 10 in total (L. panamensis, L. braziliensis, L. guyanensis, L. infantum chagasi, L. mexicana, L. lainsoni, L. amazonensis, L. colombiensis, L. equatoriensis, L. naiffi), and that the most prevalent species exhibit high genetic diversity [9–11].
Antioquia showed the highest number of vector species, 11 overall, followed by Meta, Caldas, Boyacá, and Amazonas, with 9 species each.
The Andean region had the highest number of cases for all periods, reaching a total of 12,847 cases between 2009 and 2010.

The population in this region is relatively low, when compared with other regions with similar transmission rates (S1 Table), due in part to the large percentage of the territory occupied by tropical rainforest.
167.Rationale: Although the passage notes that Colombia has high Leishmania species diversity and that some departments (including Antioquia in the Andean region) have many vector species, it does not ascribe the specific discrepancy between high case counts and stable incidence in the Andean region to species diversity or greater disease severity. The text instead emphasizes population base (Andean's large population versus Amazon's small population) as the key reason incidence is lower in the Andean region despite high case counts. Therefore linking the discrepancy primarily to species diversity and severity is not supported as the most likely explanation in the passage.

168.Option: The co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax on CD4+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates that these cells are in a state of exhaustion, which contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed that Tax was expressed on PBMC from some subjects following 24 hours of culture and was detected on both Tim-3+ as well as Tim-3− CD4+ T cells (Figure 4A).
We further identified a unique subset of Tax expressing CD4+ T cells that were Tim-3hi and lacked PD-1 in most of the subjects expressing Tax (Fig. 4A).
Our data suggest that downregulation of Tim-3, rather than PD-1, marks global and Tax-specific CD8+ T cells, which are hyperfunctional.
168.Rationale: The passage documents that Tax can be detected on both Tim-3+ and Tim-3− CD4+ T cells and even describes a Tim-3hi PD-1− subset; it does not present co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax as evidence of exhaustion. In fact the authors argue the opposite overall — that downregulation of Tim-3 (low Tim-3) marks hyperfunctional T cells. Therefore the statement that co-expression of Tim-3 and Tax indicates exhaustion driving progression is not supported by the passage.

168.Option: The absence of co-expression of Tim-3 and PD-1 on CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals indicates a lack of T cell exhaustion, which supports the hypothesis of persistent T cell activation driving disease progression
Evidence: Only a few T cells co-expressed both Tim-3 and PD-1, and no differences were observed between uninfected subjects and those with HTLV-1 asymptomatic infection or HAM/TSP patients.

We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).
Our data suggest that downregulation of Tim-3, rather than PD-1, marks global and Tax-specific CD8+ T cells, which are hyperfunctional.
168.Rationale: While the passage notes that few cells co-express Tim-3 and PD-1, it does not claim that absence of co-expression by itself is the strongest evidence for lack of exhaustion or for persistent activation driving disease. The authors emphasize the low Tim-3 expression and its association with hyperfunctional, inflammatory T cells as the mechanistic link to immunopathology. Thus this option overstates what the absence of co-expression demonstrates and is not the best-supported finding in the text.

168.Option: The higher frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells in HTLV-1-infected individuals compared to controls would indicate a state of T cell exhaustion that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).
168.Rationale: This option is factually incorrect relative to the passage: the authors report a significant decrease (not an increase) in Tim-3+ PD-1− CD8+ T cells among HTLV-1-infected individuals compared with controls. Moreover, the paper argues that low Tim-3 (not high Tim-3) is linked to hyperfunctional T cells, so the premise that Tim-3+ PD-1− elevation would indicate exhaustion and drive progression is not supported by the text.

168.Option: Lower Tim-3 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with higher IFN-γ production, suggesting a hyperfunctional T cell state that may drive immunopathology
Evidence: we observed a statistically significant inverse correlation between the frequency of Tim-3 on both Tax-specific as well as CMV-specific CD8+ T cells and the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = −0.8982; P = 0.0046; r = 0.9710; P = 0.0028; Figure 3A).
Our data suggest that downregulation of Tim-3, rather than PD-1, marks global and Tax-specific CD8+ T cells, which are hyperfunctional.
we propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
168.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports a strong inverse correlation between Tim-3 frequency on antigen-specific CD8+ T cells and IFN-γ secretion, meaning lower Tim-3 is associated with greater cytokine production. The authors interpret this low Tim-3 state as marking hyperfunctional T cells and propose that such persistent effector activity may drive inflammatory immunopathology and disease progression. This finding directly supports the hypothesis in the question.

168.Option: Elevated PD-1 expression on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells is associated with increased cytokine production, indicating a state of chronic T cell activation that contributes to disease progression
Evidence: The frequency of PD-1 expression on Tax-specific or CMV-specific CD8+ T cells also did not associate with the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = 0.1317; P = 0.7520 and r = 0.2245; P = 0.594, respectively) (Figure 3B).

This opposing relationship of PD-1 and Tim-3 expression on T cells in patients with HTLV-1 infection suggests that the downregulation of Tim-3 expression potentially leads to more vigorous T cell activity in the HTLV-1-infected individual, whereas PD-1 may not fully reflect T cell dysfunction, but rather an activated status of the T cell response to infection.
168.Rationale: The passage indicates that PD-1 expression on antigen-specific CD8+ T cells did not correlate with IFN-γ production, and the authors caution that PD-1 may reflect activation rather than dysfunction. Therefore elevated PD-1 is not shown to be associated with increased cytokine production in this study, and it is not the primary immunological finding supporting persistent activation-driven disease progression.

169.Option: Since ZIKV does not cause pathological damage in mosquito ovaries, it cannot be maintained in mosquito populations through vertical transmission
Evidence: No pathogenetic injury was found in the germarium, oocytes, trophocytes and follicular epithelium in the ZIKV-positive ovaries compared with the control ovaries.
In this study, we experimentally demonstrated that Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically from a female parent to her F1 progeny (eggs and adults).

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The option claims that lack of pathological damage in ovaries implies ZIKV cannot be maintained vertically. While the passage does state "No pathogenetic injury was found...", it also provides direct evidence that vertical transmission occurs ("Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically"), that infected F1 females can transmit the virus to mammalian hosts ("Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites"), and the authors explicitly conclude that vertical transmission "might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population". Therefore absence of ovarian pathology does not preclude maintenance via vertical transmission; the passage contradicts the option.

169.Option: ZIKV vertical transmission in Ae. albopictus is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle and does not support long-term persistence in mosquito populations
Evidence: During the FGC, the infection rate of the ovaries was 19.04% at 4 dpi, 30.43% at 7 dpi, 39.28% at 10 dpi and 75% at 14 dpi.
During the SGC, the infection rates of the ovaries were similar and reached the maximum value (100%) at 33 dpi.
In this study, we experimentally demonstrated that Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically from a female parent to her F1 progeny (eggs and adults).

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.
169.Rationale: The option asserts vertical transmission is limited to the first gonotrophic cycle. The passage shows infection rates not only in the first gonotrophic cycle (FGC) but also in the second gonotrophic cycle (SGC), where infection reached 100% at 33 dpi. The study also demonstrates transmission to F1 progeny and suggests vertical transmission could help maintain ZIKV in vector populations. Thus the claim that vertical transmission is limited to FGC and cannot support persistence is contradicted by the data.

169.Option: The absence of significant differences in hatching, pupation, and emergence rates between ZIKV-infected and control mosquitoes implies that vertical transmission has no ecological impact on mosquito populations
Evidence: In the control group, the rates of hatching, pupation and emergence were 61.01%, 93.08% and 95.15%, respectively, while the rates in the ZIKV group were 57.49%, 91.19% and 98.12%, respectively. No significant difference was found between them.
However, at 33 dpi of the SGC, the number of eggs in the control groups was significantly higher than that in the ZIKV groups (Fig 3B) (t = 2.767, P = 0.008).

As Fig 3C shows, oviposition rates were calculated, and no significant difference was detected, which suggested that ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity but not oviposition of the infected mosquitoes.
169.Rationale: The option overstates the implication of no differences in some life-stage rates. The passage indeed reports "No significant difference" in hatching, pupation and emergence, but also reports a significant reduction in the number of eggs at 33 dpi and states that "ZIKV may negatively influence fecundity but not oviposition." Thus there is evidence of ecological impact (reduced fecundity) even though some developmental rates were unchanged. Therefore the blanket statement that vertical transmission has no ecological impact is not supported by the passage.

169.Option: Vertical transmission enables Zika virus to persist in mosquito populations across generations, allowing for re-initiation of human transmission cycles even without ongoing external exposure
Evidence: In this study, we experimentally demonstrated that Ae. albopictus could transmit ZIKV vertically from a female parent to her F1 progeny (eggs and adults).

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.

Importantly, congenitally infected females are able to carry the virus for their entire life and transmit the virus horizontally by feeding without the requirement for an extrinsic incubation period (approximately 8 to 14 days) [31].

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.

In conclusion, ZIKV can be vertically transmitted in Ae. albopictus via transovarial transmission. ... Even though the vertical transmission rates were low, the female mosquitoes infected via the congenital route horizontally transmitted ZIKV to immunocompetent mice through feeding.
169.Rationale: The option matches the study's findings and interpretation: the authors demonstrate vertical transmission to eggs and adults, show that vertically infected F1 females can infect mammalian hosts via biting (thereby initiating a horizontal cycle), note congenitally infected females can carry and transmit virus throughout life without extrinsic incubation, and explicitly state vertical transmission "might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population" and could help establish endemicity. These findings support the conclusion that vertical transmission can enable persistence across generations and re-initiation of human transmission even without continuous external exposure.

169.Option: The low filial infection rate of 2.06% in F1 eggs indicates that vertical transmission is insufficient to sustain ZIKV in mosquito populations over time
Evidence: the minimal filial infection rates in eggs from two gonotrophic cycles were 2.06% and 0.69%, without statistical significance (p>0.05).

the effective population transmission rate (minimum of 1.87%) was calculated in F1 adult tissues instead of F1 adult whole bodies; this result indicated that ZIKV can not only vertically transmit to F1 eggs but also persistently exist in F1 adults, even though the ratio was not high.

Importantly, congenitally infected females are able to carry the virus for their entire life and transmit the virus horizontally by feeding without the requirement for an extrinsic incubation period (approximately 8 to 14 days) [31].

Considering this situation, the low filial infection rates indicated that vertical transmission is an alternate transmission mechanism, and ZIKV might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population, which could play a significant role in establishing endemicity.

Vertically infected F1 female offspring transmitted ZIKV to C57BL/6 suckling mice via mosquito bites, which is the first evidence that female offspring infected through vertical transmission can initiate a new horizontal cycle.
169.Rationale: While the filial infection rates are low (e.g., "2.06%"), the authors do not conclude that vertical transmission is insufficient to sustain ZIKV. Instead, they report persistent infection in F1 adults, note congenitally infected females can transmit throughout life without waiting the extrinsic incubation period, demonstrate F1 females can infect mice, and explicitly state that vertical transmission "might use this mechanism to maintain in a vector population" and "play a significant role in establishing endemicity." Thus the presence of low but demonstrable vertical transmission combined with life-long infectious potential of infected females supports the possibility of persistence, making the option's categorical claim incorrect.

170.Option: The higher proportion of rural health facilities in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district led to lower RHD service readiness due to geographic isolation and transportation barriers
Evidence: All four districts had a mix of urban and rural administrative units.
Fifty percent of facilities were located in rural areas (Table 1).
In addition, three health facilities in Wakiso district were exceptionally difficult to access due to poor roads.
ongoing research by our group has begun to shed light on the significant transportation barriers and catastrophic out-of-pocket costs faced by Ugandans with RHD.[25]
170.Rationale: The passage states that all four districts included a mix of urban and rural units and that half of facilities overall were rural, so it does not provide evidence that Tororo had a higher proportion of rural facilities than Wakiso. It also notes access/road problems in Wakiso. Because the passage does not document a greater rural share or unique geographic isolation for Tororo relative to other districts, rurality/transportation is not the most directly supported explanation for Tororo's lower readiness.

170.Option: The absence of prior RHD-specific research and clinical programs in Tororo district likely contributed to lower health system capacity and readiness compared to districts with such initiatives
Evidence: In contrast to the other three districts, the Tororo district had no previously diagnosed cases of RHD on its hospital registers.
Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.

We note that, though the overall level of health worker awareness was low in our sample, the proportion of health workers that had received training was higher in Lira and Mbarara districts compared to Wakiso and Tororo districts. Awareness may have been increased due to concurrent RHD research activities in those two districts.
We found that the Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study.
170.Rationale: The passage explicitly links Tororo's low readiness to its lack of prior RHD research/clinical activity and contrasts that with districts where concurrent research likely increased awareness and readiness. These statements most directly explain the observed inter-district disparity.

170.Option: The lower number of nurses per health facility in Tororo district compared to Wakiso district resulted in reduced RHD service readiness due to workforce shortages
Evidence: A higher number of nurses in a health facility was also associated with a significantly higher RHD readiness score.
Compared to Lira district facilities, Wakiso and Mbarara district facilities had significantly higher readiness scores, whereas Tororo district facilities had non-significantly lower scores.
170.Rationale: While the passage shows that more nurses at a facility are associated with higher readiness, it does not provide district-level comparisons of nurse counts (no quote states Tororo had fewer nurses than Wakiso). Thus there is insufficient evidence in the text to support the claim that differential nurse numbers between Tororo and Wakiso directly explain the readiness disparity.

170.Option: Differences in the availability of essential antibiotics like benzathine penicillin G and amoxicillin across districts explain the variation in RHD service readiness despite similar population-level risk factors
Evidence: Amoxicillin and benzathine penicillin G were widely available across all health facility levels.
These drugs were less likely to be available at primary healthcare facilities, including at health centers III and especially at health centers II.
170.Rationale: The passage reports that benzathine penicillin G and amoxicillin were widely available across facility levels, so differential availability of these essential antibiotics across districts is not presented as the primary driver of inter-district readiness differences. The text instead points to other bottlenecks (training, diagnostics, prior research presence).

170.Option: The lack of national policy prioritization for RHD in Tororo district, unlike in Wakiso, led to lower service readiness due to insufficient government funding and political will
Evidence: Participants also expressed concern over lack of national policies regarding RHD. They felt that, because of insufficient policy prioritization, funding for RHD services in their districts has remained poor:

"..right now… [the government] are not even aware that [RHD is] there… I have always sat on meetings, [and] we have always done the budget together, but there has never been any funding for RHD." (HW 008 Lira)
The 71st World Health Assembly (WHA) adopted a Resolution on RHD in 2018, mandating Member States to prioritize RHD control programs.[8]
170.Rationale: The passage documents a general lack of national policy prioritization and insufficient funding for RHD as a nationwide issue, but it does not state that national policy existed for some districts (e.g., Wakiso) and not for Tororo. Because the policy gap is portrayed as national and the text specifically attributes Tororo's low scores to absence of prior research/clinical programs, lack of national policy differing by district is not the most directly supported explanation.

171.Option: The detection of viral antigen and RNA in the brain and lungs of animals that reached endpoint criteria, but not in surviving animals, confirms that NiV replication was necessary for disease development
Evidence: The brains of animals that reached euthanasia criteria showed a consistently high viral load, regardless of the inoculation dose (Fig 2A).
Similarly, high virus titers were observed in the brains of these animals (Fig 2B).
Despite direct inoculation into the CNS, high viral loads were detected in the lungs of animals that reached euthanasia criteria (Fig 2C and 2D).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
Although viral RNA was detected in the brains of some surviving animals (Fig 2A), infectious virus could not be detected (Fig 2B).
171.Rationale: The presence of high viral RNA levels and infectious virus (high virus titers) in brains and detectable virus in lungs specifically in animals that reached euthanasia criteria, together with the absence of viral antigen/lesions in surviving and mock animals, is direct evidence that active NiV replication coincided with and was associated with the neurological disease. This links the pathology to viral infection rather than to non-specific procedural effects.

171.Option: The absence of neutrophilic infiltrates in mock-inoculated animals and the identification of a non-bacterial origin for the 16S rRNA signal rule out procedural trauma and bacterial contamination as causes of the neurological disease
Evidence: One of the unusual features of neuropathological lesions observed upon intracranial inoculation is the presence of large number of neutrophils.
However, no bacteria were visible in tissue slides and neutrophils were not observed in intracranially inoculated mock animals.

To definitively prove that no bacterial contamination occurred during the inoculation procedure, RNA was extracted from brain tissues and 16S rRNA RT-PCR was performed. We detected a nonspecific amplified product of 650 bp (S4A Fig). Sanger sequencing showed the amplified product corresponds to a mitochondrial gene of the Syrian hamster (S4B Fig).
Thus, the neutrophilic infiltrate observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters was due to NiV infection.
171.Rationale: This set of observations and the targeted 16S rRNA follow-up exclude bacterial contamination as the cause of the neutrophil-rich inflammation, and the lack of neutrophils in mock-inoculated animals argues against the procedural injection itself causing the inflammatory lesions. These controls strengthen the inference that the pathology was virus-driven rather than due to bacterial contamination or a generalized procedural artifact.

171.Option: The rapid progression of disease within 2–6 days post-inoculation, consistent with direct CNS access, supports that NiV was the cause of neurological disease rather than procedural effects
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
Direct inoculation of NiV into the cerebellomedullary cistern results in a rapid progression to severe neurological disease.
Direct inoculation of NiV into the cerebellomedullary cistern provides the virus an opportunity to easily breach these barriers, partially explaining the rapid development of disease.
171.Rationale: While rapid onset is consistent with direct CNS infection by NiV and is supportive, rapid progression alone is not a design feature that definitively rules out procedural or non-specific causes (e.g., acute procedural injury could also produce rapid signs). The study included more direct evidence (viral detection, mock controls, bacterial exclusion) that more strongly attribute disease to NiV. Thus relying on rapidity of disease by itself is insufficient to support the conclusion.

171.Option: The uniformity of neurological signs across all animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M, combined with the absence of signs in mock-inoculated animals, indicates that the disease was specifically caused by NiV
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
All mock-inoculated animals survived until study endpoint at 14 dpi (Fig 1).
Neither lesions nor viral antigen were detected in animals who survived the intracranial challenge or mock-inoculated animals (Fig 5J and 5K).
171.Rationale: The consistency of severe neurologic disease in the virus-inoculated groups across a wide dose range, contrasted with the complete absence of disease in mock-inoculated controls, provides strong experimental evidence that the observed disease phenotype is specifically induced by NiV exposure rather than by the inoculation procedure or other non-specific factors.

171.Option: The dose-dependent development of neurological disease, with no signs at doses below 0.04 TCID50, supports a causal link to NiV rather than procedural or non-specific factors
Evidence: Animals inoculated with 1–3125 TCID50 NiV-M uniformly displayed severe neurological signs and reached endpoint criteria within 2–6 days post inoculation (dpi) (Fig 1).
Lowering the inoculation dose to 0.2 TCID50 resulted in only 50% of animals requiring euthanasia between 4–6 dpi; no signs of disease were observed in the surviving animals.
Doses of 0.04 TCID50 or below did not result in disease signs nor lethality.
Even a dose as low as 1 TCID50 resulted in rapid development of neurological disease requiring euthanasia.
171.Rationale: A dose-response relationship (increasing frequency/severity of disease with higher inoculum and absence of disease at very low doses) is a classic indicator of causality in experimental infectious disease studies. This pattern argues that the neurological disease depended on the amount of virus delivered, supporting a viral cause rather than non-specific procedural effects which would not be expected to show such a dose-dependent trend.

172.Option: Direct transmission from infected cat fleas to other arthropods via cofeeding on a shared vertebrate host can explain R. felis presence in non-flea vectors
Evidence: Transmission of R. felis from infected cat fleas to naïve rat fleas (Xenopsylla cheopis) cofeeding on vertebrate hosts was demonstrated [9];

To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.

Cofeeding transmission occurs when a pathogen is transmitted between two arthropods feeding in proximity on a vertebrate host, negating the intrinsic incubation period required for some pathogens during transmission events on a vertebrate host [39].

Fleas deposit rickettsiae in the dermis at the vector-host skin interface resulting in dissemination across the skin [9], presumably where other arthropods, including ticks that have mouthparts in the same tissue, can acquire the agent during feeding events;
172.Rationale: The passage directly reports cofeeding bioassays and concludes that R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit rickettsiae to ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding on a vertebrate host. It also describes the biological basis (deposition in the dermis and local dissemination) and defines cofeeding transmission as occurring between arthropods feeding in proximity. Therefore direct flea→other-arthropod transmission via cofeeding is a supported mechanism to explain R. felis in non-flea vectors.

172.Option: Vertical transmission from infected female ticks to their offspring could account for R. felis presence in non-flea arthropods, even without vertebrate host involvement
Evidence: More recently, in sub-Saharan African countries where R. felis has been reported as an emerging cause of unknown fever, R. felis has been detected in mosquitoes [3,15,16]. Detection of R. felis in male mosquitoes in China and unfed adult mosquitoes in the United States suggests vertical transmission in nature [17,18].
laboratory studies have demonstrated R. felis was vertically transmitted by the American dog tick, Dermacentor variabilis, when adult ticks were exposed via capillary feeding [14].
Under laboratory conditions, adult D. variabilis are susceptible to infection and subsequent transovarial transmission of R. felis for at least one generation [14].
172.Rationale: The passage documents laboratory evidence that D. variabilis can vertically transmit R. felis (transovarial transmission) and notes detections in male/unfed mosquitoes that suggest vertical transmission in nature. Thus vertical transmission within non-flea arthropod populations is a plausible mechanism that could explain R. felis presence without requiring sustained vertebrate-to-vector transmission.

172.Option: R. felis may be transmitted from infected vertebrate hosts to non-flea arthropods through blood-feeding, even if the pathogen is not detectable in the host's bloodstream
Evidence: Cofeeding transmission occurs when a pathogen is transmitted between two arthropods feeding in proximity on a vertebrate host, negating the intrinsic incubation period required for some pathogens during transmission events on a vertebrate host [39].
The cofeeding mechanism has been described for both flea- and tick- borne rickettsial pathogens, and occurs in the absence of a rickettsemic host [9,40].

There are several pieces of evidence supporting the potential for horizontal transmission of R. felis to vertebrate hosts, such as identification of R. felis in the salivary glands of cat fleas [41] and detection of R. felis in the blood of vertebrate hosts exposed to R. felis-infected fleas [5,42–44].
172.Rationale: Although the passage notes that R. felis has been detected in the blood of some vertebrate hosts, it emphasizes that cofeeding transmission can occur in the absence of a rickettsemic (infected-blood) host. The option asserts transmission despite non-detectable bloodstream infection; the passage does not provide evidence that undetectable vertebrate bloodstream infections commonly permit transmission to vectors. Instead, the text stresses cofeeding in the absence of vertebrate rickettsemia as the explanatory mechanism, so the specific claim in this option is not supported.

172.Option: R. felis may persist in the environment and be acquired by non-flea arthropods through contact with contaminated surfaces on the vertebrate host
Evidence: Fleas deposit rickettsiae in the dermis at the vector-host skin interface resulting in dissemination across the skin [9], presumably where other arthropods, including ticks that have mouthparts in the same tissue, can acquire the agent during feeding events;

Cofeeding transmission occurs when a pathogen is transmitted between two arthropods feeding in proximity on a vertebrate host, negating the intrinsic incubation period required for some pathogens during transmission events on a vertebrate host [39].

Transmission of R. felis from infected cat fleas to naïve mosquitoes occurred after only 20 mins of cofeeding and acquisition efficiency increased corresponding to the duration infected fleas were fed on the vertebrate host.
172.Rationale: The passage describes how fleas deposit rickettsiae into the dermis and cause dissemination across the skin at the vector-host interface, and it proposes that other arthropods can acquire the agent from this local dermal contamination during feeding. This supports the idea that contamination of host skin/tissues (environmental contamination at the bite site) can lead to acquisition by other arthropods, which is consistent with the option's mechanism.

172.Option: Horizontal transmission of R. felis from infected mosquitoes to other arthropods via cofeeding on a vertebrate host could explain the presence of the pathogen in non-flea vectors
Evidence: To test the hypothesis that R. felis-infected cat fleas may contribute to dissemination of the agent to other hematophagous vectors, cofeeding bioassays were employed to examine rickettsial transmission from infected cat fleas (C. felis) to ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (Anopheles quadrimaculatus) on a vertebrate host.
The data suggest R. felis-infected cat fleas transmit rickettsiae to other hematophagous vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host.

Transmission of R. felis from infected cat fleas to naïve mosquitoes occurred after only 20 mins of cofeeding and acquisition efficiency increased corresponding to the duration infected fleas were fed on the vertebrate host.
172.Rationale: The study and passage present evidence that infected cat fleas act as donors transmitting R. felis to ticks and mosquitoes via cofeeding. There is no evidence presented that infected mosquitoes serve as donors to other arthropods via cofeeding. The data support flea→other-arthropod transmission but do not demonstrate mosquito→other-arthropod horizontal cofeeding transmission, so this option is not supported by the passage.

173.Option: Delayed sample collection may lead to underestimation of acute infections, particularly for pathogens with short-lived biomarkers like NS1 antigen or rickettsial DNA, and reliance on serology without paired samples may misclassify past exposure as current infection
Evidence: Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
In contrast, 14 (93%) of murine typhus patients were diagnosed by paired sera IFA and only one patient was PCR positive (Table 3).

We are not aware of evidence on the duration of Rickettsia typhi bacteraemia. Our PCR positive R. typhi patient had 8 days of fever before admission to hospital. Wattanaworawit et al. [57] found among positive paired sera R. typhi IFA patients, 36% were also diagnosed by PCR. Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].
173.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that detection of acute infection depends on timing. Dengue NS1/PCR identified most acute dengue cases, showing that antigen/PCR are time-sensitive markers. For rickettsial infections, most diagnoses relied on paired sera IFA rather than PCR, and the text notes limited duration of detectable rickettsial DNA (detectable only in early illness), indicating that delayed sampling reduces PCR yield. The authors explicitly state missing convalescent samples could mean missed seroconversions. Prior antibiotics before sampling also reduce culture yield. Together these facts support that delayed collection can underestimate acute infections and that relying on single serology (without paired samples) risks misclassifying past exposure as current infection.

173.Option: The use of PCR and antigen detection methods at admission provides the most reliable evidence of acute infection, minimizing the risk of misclassification due to timing of sample collection
Evidence: Of 76 (30%) patients with dengue as a single disease, 71/76 (93%) had positive NS1 and/or PCR assays and, 40/58 (68%) patients had seroconversion;
In contrast, 14 (93%) of murine typhus patients were diagnosed by paired sera IFA and only one patient was PCR positive (Table 3).

We are not aware of evidence on the duration of Rickettsia typhi bacteraemia. Our PCR positive R. typhi patient had 8 days of fever before admission to hospital. Wattanaworawit et al. [57] found among positive paired sera R. typhi IFA patients, 36% were also diagnosed by PCR. Murai et al. [58] found that Orientia tsutsugamushi DNA was detectable in patient’s blood until day 3–8 after the start of chemotherapy with minocycline or doxycycline.

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.
173.Rationale: While PCR and antigen detection (e.g., dengue NS1/PCR) provided strong evidence of acute dengue in this study, the passage also shows that PCR often misses rickettsial infections and that detectable DNA/antigen may be present only early in illness. The authors note PCR positivity was uncommon for murine typhus and that rickettsial DNA is only detectable for a limited interval. Thus PCR/antigen at admission does not universally 'minimize' misclassification—timing still matters and PCR can give false negatives if samples are taken late or after treatment. The statement overgeneralizes the reliability of PCR/antigen methods across pathogens and time windows, so it is incorrect as an absolute claim.

173.Option: The absence of convalescent samples limits the ability to confirm acute infection for all pathogens, but this is not a major concern because most infections are self-limiting and severity is determined by clinical presentation alone
Evidence: convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).

Important limitations of this study include that it was hospital, rather than community, based and therefore could not estimate the incidence of causes of fevers amongst pregnant women in the community and we were not able to recruit matched afebrile pregnant women.
173.Rationale: The first clause is supported: missing convalescent samples reduced the ability to confirm acute infections by seroconversion. However, the second clause is false: the passage documents substantial severe outcomes (including maternal death, miscarriages, stillbirths, preterm births) associated with infections such as dengue, indicating that severity is not determined solely by clinical presentation and that accurate laboratory diagnosis matters for understanding and management. The authors list the lack of convalescent samples as an important limitation, contradicting the assertion that it is not a major concern.

173.Option: Testing for all pathogens simultaneously at admission ensures accurate assessment of disease severity, as it captures the full spectrum of infections regardless of the timing of symptom onset
Evidence: Forty percent of pregnant women did not have confirmed diagnoses. Forty-four patients (18%) came to hospital with influenza-like illnesses [50] and 22/44 (50%) of them did not have laboratory confirmation diagnoses.
18% received antibiotics before collecting samples for cultures, which may have reduced the frequency of positive culture results [63].

convalescence samples were not available for all serological investigations (17%, 37% and 56% without convalescence samples for dengue/JEV ELISA, leptospiral MAT and scrub/murine typhus IFA, respectively) and we may therefore have missed some patients who seroconverted.

We focused on key pathogens describing in previous studies in this area [24,36,40]. We were not able to investigate diverse other important viral infections in pregnancy [64] or other potential pathogens such as Coxiella burnetii.
173.Rationale: Simultaneous testing at admission cannot 'ensure' accurate assessment regardless of timing. The passage shows many reasons why a single admission sample can fail to detect infections: prior antibiotics reducing culture yield, lack of convalescent sera preventing detection of seroconversion, and certain pathogens (e.g., rickettsial DNA) only detectable early. The study also lacked testing for some pathogens (respiratory viruses, others). Therefore testing only at admission does not capture the full spectrum nor overcome timing-related detection windows, and thus the option's claim is incorrect.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates represents a new, locally evolved variant of S. Typhi that emerged independently in the modern era due to selective pressure from widespread antibiotic use
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.
Amongst the 151 non-H58 isolates, few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms were detected.
174.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the tight 2011–2012 cluster is unlikely to have arisen by rapid local evolution during acute transmission given the known mutation rate (<1 SNP/genome/year). Instead the authors propose a chronic carrier as the likely source. Additionally, the dataset shows few antimicrobial resistance mechanisms among non-H58 isolates, arguing against widespread antibiotic-driven selection producing a novel resistant local variant. Therefore the option asserting independent local emergence driven by antibiotic selective pressure is not supported by the passage.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are primarily due to re-emergence of environmental transmission from untreated sewage irrigation, as evidenced by the persistence of ancient S. Typhi genotypes in the modern population
Evidence: In April 1991, the government of Chile abruptly prohibited the practice of irrigating crops with untreated sewage wastewater in Santiago ... Following imposition and strict enforcement of the prohibition of use of untreated sewage water for irrigation, the Santiago cholera epidemic quickly came to an end, and the incidence of typhoid dropped precipitously in subsequent years.

The current rare sporadic cases of typhoid in Santiago include illnesses among: travellers who visit countries with high incidence rates of typhoid; immigrants from typhoid-endemic countries; and transmission from Chilean chronic carriers who were originally infected decades earlier (pre-1991) during the era of hyperendemic transmission.
The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.
174.Rationale: While the passage notes that use of untreated sewage for irrigation was a major driver of past hyperendemic transmission, it also describes that this practice was banned in 1991 and typhoid incidence dropped sharply thereafter. The authors attribute modern sporadic cases to travellers, immigrants, and especially chronic carriers shedding strains from the pre-1991 pool. The genomic continuity (persistence of ancient genotypes) is interpreted as consistent with long-term human carriage rather than re-emergent environmental irrigation-related transmission. Therefore the option claiming primary cause is re-emergent environmental irrigation is not supported.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are caused by imported strains from high-endemic regions, as indicated by the presence of H58 genotypes in the modern isolates
Evidence: we also detected 11 additional distinct genotypes across the two time periods. These include three previously described importations of isolates belonging to H58 genotypes 4.3.1.1 and 4.3.1.2.

Although there have been several detected introductions of the globally dominant H58 S. Typhi clade (4.3.1) into Santiago, as occurs with most industrialized countries in North America, Europe, and Australia, this was not a frequent genotype found in the sporadic modern Chilean cases.
174.Rationale: Although the passage notes a few detected introductions/importations of H58 genotypes, it explicitly states H58 was not a frequent genotype among modern sporadic cases in Santiago. The primary genomic and epidemiologic interpretation in the passage is that most modern sporadic cases reflect persistence of local historical lineages (likely via chronic carriers) rather than being predominantly due to imported H58 strains. Thus the option claiming imported H58 strains as the primary cause is not supported.

174.Option: The 2011–2012 cluster of genotype 1.1 isolates likely resulted from a single environmental exposure event, such as a contaminated food source, given the high genetic similarity among isolates
Evidence: The twenty isolates from 2011–2012 showed an interesting cluster with a median SNP distance of 8 SNPs which is unlikely to have evolved among acute cases, where the mutation rate is calculated to be < 1 SNP/genome/year, in one year.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.

If this hypothetical carrier was working as a food handler, intermittently contaminating food or water consumed by subjects during 2011–12, this could result in the cluster of closely related isolates we observe.
174.Rationale: The passage argues against rapid clonal evolution among acute cases and instead proposes a chronic carrier shedding related but heterogeneous bacteria over time as the plausible source. While intermittent contamination of food by a carrier (a food handler) is explicitly proposed, the authors emphasize a carrier-related explanation rather than a single, one-time environmental exposure event. Therefore concluding a single environmental exposure as the cause is not supported by the presented evidence.

174.Option: Sporadic typhoid cases in Santiago during the 2010s are likely sustained by long-term human carriers who harbor and intermittently shed genetically stable S. Typhi lineages originating from the hyperendemic era
Evidence: The intermingling of historical and recent sequences suggests that the 2010s isolates originated from progenitors within the same pool of S. Typhi circulating in the 1980s.
These may, in turn, be linked to shedding of S. Typhi from chronic typhoid carriers.
This is expected decades after hyper-endemic typhoid is controlled, as chronic carriers will continue to shed sporadically until they are treated or die out of the population.
A potential explanation to account for this cluster is that it was an outbreak caused by bacteria shed from a long-term carrier.
In conclusion, this genomics analysis suggests that asymptomatic typhoid carriers (or other unknown reservoirs) are likely responsible for continuing sporadic typhoid cases in Chile.
174.Rationale: The passage consistently interprets the genomic similarity between 1980s and 2010s isolates, and the tightly related 2011–2012 cluster, as evidence that modern sporadic cases derive from the historical pool—most plausibly via chronic human carriers who intermittently shed S. Typhi. The authors explicitly state and conclude that asymptomatic carriers are likely responsible for continuing sporadic cases, making this option the inference best supported by the presented genomic and epidemiologic data.

175.Option: The dose-dependent and reversible nature of PZQ-induced phenotypes suggests a shared mechanism involving modulation of excitable cell function, likely through disruption of bioaminergic signaling pathways.
Evidence: Exposure of regenerating planarian (D. japonica) trunk fragments to praziquantel (PZQ) yielded two-headed worms (Fig 1A, [17]), an effect never observed in the absence of drug exposure. This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].

This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B), with a concentration-dependence similar to that observed for the (longer term) polarity effect (EC50 = 38±3.6μM for bipolarity in trunk fragments versus EC50 = 23±2.4μM for mobility in intact worms, Figs 1B and 4B) and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).

What is the molecular basis of this phenology between planarian regenerative polarity and schistosome motility? An appealing explanation relates to the recent identification of muscle cells as the coordinating nexus of positional signaling during planarian regeneration [21].
suggests that drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.
PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
Therefore, we conclude that PZQ action is functionally antagonized by serotonergic activation in schistosomule motility experiments, just as observed in planarian assays (Figs 1 and 4C).
175.Rationale: The passage explicitly states PZQ effects are concentration-dependent and reversible, and links both regenerative polarity and motility phenotypes to modulation of muscle/excitable cell physiology. It also documents functional interactions with bioaminergic signaling (PZQ mimics serotonergic blockade and is opposed by 5-HT agonists). Together these statements support the interpretation that the dose–response informs a mechanism acting on excitable cells via bioaminergic pathways rather than a non-specific or purely developmental target.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of bipolarity indicates that PZQ acts as a direct agonist of serotonergic receptors, leading to aberrant head specification during regeneration.
Evidence: Strikingly, the bipolarizing action of PZQ was blocked by co-incubation with 5-HT, or the analogue O-methylserotonin (O-MT), but not by co-incubation with other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 1C).
PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
We propose these pathways differentially regulate second messenger (cAMP) action, muscle function and—in planarians—‘head’ versus ‘tail’ specification.
however the possibility that PZQ directly targets flatworm bioaminergic receptors as an ergomimetic cannot be excluded until heterologous expression of platyhelminth GpCRs is optimized.
175.Rationale: The passage indicates that serotonergic activation (adding 5-HT or O-MT) blocks PZQ-evoked bipolarity and that PZQ phenocopies serotonergic blockers. Those facts are inconsistent with PZQ being a direct serotonergic agonist (which would be expected to produce similar effects to 5-HT). The authors explicitly state PZQ mimics serotonergic blockade and is opposed by 5-HT agonists; they only note that direct targeting of GpCRs cannot be excluded, but do not present evidence that PZQ is a 5‑HT agonist. Therefore this option is not supported by the passage.

175.Option: The similar EC50 values for regenerative polarity and motility defects suggest that PZQ exerts its effects through a non-specific cytotoxic mechanism that damages cellular integrity at high concentrations.
Evidence: This effect was dose-dependent (EC50 38±3.6μM, Fig 1B), with maximal doses being completely penetrant [17].

This effect was dose-dependent (Fig 4B), with a concentration-dependence similar to that observed for the (longer term) polarity effect (EC50 = 38±3.6μM for bipolarity in trunk fragments versus EC50 = 23±2.4μM for mobility in intact worms, Figs 1B and 4B) and reversible following drug removal (Fig 4C).
suggests that drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.
The immobilizing action of PZQ was also reversed by co-incubation with O-MT, but not other bioaminergic neurotransmitters (Fig 4C).
175.Rationale: A non-specific cytotoxic mechanism would be expected to cause irreversible damage; however the passage notes PZQ effects are reversible after washout. Moreover, the authors link PZQ phenotypes to modulation of excitable cell physiology and specific interactions with bioaminergic signaling (reversible antagonism by serotonergic agonists), arguing against simple non-specific cytotoxicity.

175.Option: The concentration-dependent induction of both polarity and motility defects implies that PZQ acts as a broad-spectrum neuromodulator that disrupts multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.
Evidence: PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.

PZQ action in planarians has previously been shown to depend on Cav1A and tyrosine hydroxylase (TH) functionality (red, [18,20]). Activity of this pathway is opposed by Cav1B and tryptophan hydroxylase functionality (blue, [18,20]).
dopamine and serotonin regulate cAMP turnover with opposing effects on flatworm musculature [34–36].
suggests that drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.
175.Rationale: The passage emphasizes specific interactions with bioaminergic systems (dopaminergic and serotonergic pathways) and Cav channel–linked pathways rather than indiscriminate disruption of many neurotransmitter systems. While some compounds (e.g., ergots) can be 'dirty' ligands, the study's data and mechanistic model focus on modulation of particular bioaminergic/cation-channel pathways; there is no direct evidence presented that PZQ broadly disrupts multiple transmitter systems simultaneously.

175.Option: The dose-response relationship supports the hypothesis that PZQ induces bipolarity by directly activating Wnt signaling pathways, which are known to regulate body axis formation in planarians.
Evidence: dopamine and serotonin also are known regulators of Wnt signaling. D2Rs selectively associate with both β-catenin (to inhibit Wnt signals [40]) and Cav channels (to regulate their expression [41]). 5-HT is a well-established wounding signal [42], long range messenger involved in regenerative proliferation [43,44] and a reciprocally permissive cue for Wnt signaling [45,46].
We propose these pathways differentially regulate second messenger (cAMP) action, muscle function and—in planarians—‘head’ versus ‘tail’ specification.
suggests that drugs which miscue regenerative patterning all possess a shared ability to modulate excitable cell physiology and perturb muscle function.
PZQ action mimics the bipolarizing ability of serotonergic blockers, and is opposed by 5-HT agonists.
175.Rationale: Although Wnt signaling is implicated in axis formation and is modulated by bioaminergic inputs (as the passage notes), the study does not present evidence that PZQ directly activates Wnt pathways. Instead, the authors argue that PZQ acts via excitable cell/muscle physiology and bioaminergic (dopamine/serotonin) pathways, which may secondarily influence Wnt. The dose–response data are therefore better interpreted as supporting modulation of excitable/bioaminergic signaling rather than direct activation of Wnt.

176.Option: Use of a decision tree model that incorporates both clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters to improve diagnostic accuracy
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.

The decision algorithm for the diagnosis of dengue within the first three days of illness made use of a combination of platelet count, total white cell count, body temperature, absolute lymphocyte and neutrophil counts, in sequential order (Figure 1A).
176.Rationale: While incorporating both clinical symptoms and laboratory parameters improves diagnostic accuracy (as the passage explicitly states), this feature by itself does not most directly support causal inference. Causal inference requires temporality (predictors measured before outcomes), control of confounding, and objective, prior measurement — whereas adding predictors improves prediction but does not establish that those predictors are causally related to severe outcome. The quoted text supports that the model uses both types of data for diagnosis, but does not establish the temporal or design features needed for causal inference.

176.Option: Application of k-fold cross-validation with k=10 to assess model performance and reduce overfitting in the decision tree
Evidence: The calculated algorithms were validated using the k-fold cross-validation approach.
The fold value was set to k = 10.
This is considered to be a powerful methodology to overcome data over-fitting [15].
176.Rationale: K-fold cross-validation (k=10) is explicitly used in the study to assess model performance and reduce overfitting, as the passage states. However, cross-validation addresses internal predictive validity and model generalizability, not the key elements of causal inference such as temporal ordering of predictors before outcomes, use of objective prior biomarkers, or control for selection/confounding. Thus it supports model reliability but not most directly the validity of causal inference.

176.Option: Inclusion of both dengue and non-dengue cases from multiple geographic sites to ensure representation of diverse clinical presentations
Evidence: We constructed a decision tree for dengue diagnosis with 1,200 patients with acute febrile illness. Of these, 1,012 were recruited from the EDEN study and 188 from Vietnam.
we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.
176.Rationale: Including cases from multiple sites and both dengue and non-dengue illnesses improves representativeness and external validity (generalizability), which helps make findings applicable across diverse clinical presentations. However, while important, this does not most directly support causal inference about early predictors of severe dengue — causal inference depends more directly on temporality and objective measurement prior to outcome. The quoted text supports multi-site inclusion for robustness, but that is not the single design feature most directly tied to causal inference.

176.Option: Use of temporally prior, objective biomarkers collected within 72 hours of illness onset in a multi-site cohort with validation across subgroups
Evidence: we show here that a decision tree approach can be useful to develop an intuitive diagnostic algorithm, using clinical and haematological parameters, that is able to distinguish dengue from non-dengue disease in the first 72 hours of illness.
All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.

The best performing decision algorithm made used of platelet count, the crossover value (Ct) of the real-time RT-PCR for dengue viral RNA ... as the first, second and third splitting parameters, respectively (Figure 3). All 3 parameters were obtained from the first visit.
we have included 188 DHF/DSS paediatric and adult cases from Vietnam in order to ensure that the diagnostic algorithm developed here is robust across a spectrum of dengue presentations.
176.Rationale: This option most directly addresses key components of causal inference: temporality (predictors measured within 72 hours/at the first visit, i.e., before later severe outcomes), objectivity (biomarkers such as platelet count and RT-PCR Ct are objective measures), and robustness/validation (multi-site data and subgroup robustness are described).
