The passage explicitly notes that the prognostic algorithm used platelet count, Ct, and IgG obtained at the first visit (within 72 hours), and that multi-site data were included to ensure robustness — together these design features support a stronger inference that early predictors precede and are associated with later severe outcomes, which is central to causal interpretation.

176.Option: Development of a decision tree using data from hospitalized patients only to ensure accurate outcome ascertainment
Evidence: The use of data obtained from the 89 hospitalised cases alone resulted in a very similar decision algorithm, although the AUCs were substantially lower than the above analysis due largely to the smaller dataset.
hospitalisation of the dengue cases is subject to variation arising from physician-to-physician differences in decision-making,
176.Rationale: Restricting the development dataset to hospitalized patients might improve ascertainment of severe outcomes but introduces selection bias and limits generalizability; the passage notes that hospitalized-only data produced lower AUCs (smaller dataset) and that hospitalization is influenced by physician decision-making. Those factors undermine the validity of causal inference rather than strengthen it. Thus this feature would not most directly support valid causal interpretation of early predictors.

177.Option: Use mosquito surveillance as an adjunct to human testing to detect current infection and validate findings from human-based surveillance
Evidence: By contrast, mosquito surveillance gives a snapshot indication of current infection, and could serve as a useful adjunct to human surveillance methods[27, 47].
There were also examples where human testing did not detect significant transmission but xenomonitoring did.
For example, the study by Ramaiah et al. reported a mosquito infection rate of 4.7% of mosquitoes when a community survey performed concurrently found no evidence of human infection on ICT testing.
177.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that mosquito surveillance provides a snapshot of current infection and can serve as a useful adjunct to human surveillance, and gives concrete examples where xenomonitoring detected infection that human testing missed. Using mosquito surveillance alongside human testing therefore reduces the chance of missing residual hotspots due to measurement error or the persistence/limitations of human markers (antigen/antibody), making this strategy the most effective at reducing missed hotspots.

177.Option: Replace antigen-based testing with antibody-based testing in all surveillance activities to increase sensitivity and detect historical infections
Evidence: The studies included in this review indicate that there may be benefit in moving from the conventional rapid antigen tests to antibody tests as they increase the proportion of positive results and, hence, the likelihood that residual hotspots will be detected.
Antibody tests are a measure of the host response to infection which can persist for some time after all antigenic material from the original infection has been eliminated.

This means that antibody tests are associated with an increased false-positive rate and the detection of more historical cases, meaning there would be financial and logistical implications to switching to widespread antibody testing[13].
177.Rationale: While antibody tests increase the proportion of positive results and could improve sensitivity, the passage warns they detect historical infections and have increased false-positive rates and cost/logistical implications. Replacing antigen testing entirely with antibody testing would therefore risk overcalling historical (non-transmitting) infections and would not necessarily reduce the risk of missing a current transmission hotspot; it could instead create measurement error in the opposite direction (false signals of transmission).

177.Option: Conduct repeated Transmission Assessment Surveys (TAS) at shorter intervals to improve detection of transmission hotspots despite low sensitivity
Evidence: TAS methodology is resource-intensive and may also lack sensitivity in low-prevalence settings[5, 7].

TAS is an important programmatic tool to guide decisions on when to stop MDA but several studies report that it lacks sensitivity when used in low prevalence settings, such as a post-validation context[13, 17, 23, 38], and may not accurately describe the spatial distribution of LF at community-level[14].
177.Rationale: The passage states that TAS is resource-intensive and lacks sensitivity in low-prevalence settings. Simply repeating TAS more frequently would increase resource burden without overcoming the underlying sensitivity limitations, so this approach is unlikely to be the most effective way to reduce the risk of missing residual hotspots due to measurement error.

177.Option: Use a single diagnostic test with the highest reported sensitivity across all studies to standardize surveillance and minimize measurement error
Evidence: A significant degree of heterogeneity was identified in the included studies. This included variation in study design, baseline endemicity, population sampled, use of diagnostic tests and reporting metrics.

All methods of human surveillance are affected by the persistence of the marker (antibody or antigen) in circulation. This is of variable duration for different test types, meaning that their results are not directly comparable. It also means that results are not truly indicative of current infectivity and will therefore include cases of historical disease.

The studies included in this review indicate that there may be benefit in moving from the conventional rapid antigen tests to antibody tests as they increase the proportion of positive results and, hence, the likelihood that residual hotspots will be detected.
177.Rationale: Although standardization can reduce some measurement error, the passage emphasizes heterogeneity in contexts and that different tests measure different biological signals (antigen vs antibody) with different persistence and implications. Choosing a single test with highest sensitivity (likely an antibody test) may increase detection but also capture historical, non-transmitting infections and would not necessarily minimize the specific measurement errors relevant to detecting current transmission hotspots.

177.Option: Target surveillance exclusively to adult males, as they are more likely to harbor infection and show higher prevalence rates
Evidence: Studies reported that LF tests typically report higher prevalence of infection in adults than in children[14, 31] and it is thought that adults (particularly adult men) may represent the majority of the reservoir of infection for LF[37].
Other recommended sampling methods include community-based methods targeting adults and children, school-based surveys with a wider age range and snowball sampling of positive cases[14].
Post-validation surveillance in Togo found positive cases in low-risk areas, highlighting the importance of developing surveillance systems with nationwide coverage[40, 42].
177.Rationale: While adult males may have higher prevalence and represent an important reservoir (supporting targeted approaches), the passage cautions that surveillance should have broad coverage and gives examples of positive cases in low-risk areas. Exclusively targeting adult males risks missing hotspots occurring in other groups or locations; the recommended approach is targeted augmentation (e.g., focusing on high-risk groups) rather than exclusive surveillance of one demographic.

178.Option: The inclusion of baseline microfilaria prevalence data in the model calibration process
Evidence: Thus, the four data streams considered for investigating the value of information gained from each survey were respectively: scenario 1—baseline mf prevalence data only, scenario 2—baseline and post-MDA 3 mf prevalence data, scenario 3—baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data, and scenario 4—baseline and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data.

It is also noticeable that the best constraining data stream for each combination of site and model also produced as expected the lowest range in predictions of the numbers of years of annual MDA required to achieve the 1% mf prevalence in each site, with the widest ranges estimated for model-only predictions (scenario 0) and the shorter data streams (scenario 1).

The results also show, however, that data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
178.Rationale: While baseline mf data are part of all examined calibration streams, the passage shows that baseline-only (scenario 1) and other short data streams produced wider prediction ranges and less reduction in uncertainty than streams that include later post-MDA measurements. Thus inclusion of baseline data alone is not the most critical factor for reliable forecasts; later post-MDA data (not baseline alone) drive the largest reductions in forecast uncertainty.

178.Option: The use of a sequential data assimilation method rather than a batch estimation approach in model calibration
Evidence: Note that this was also irrespective of the two types of longitudinal data assimilation procedures (sequential vs. simultaneous) used by the different models in this study.

Although a limitation of this batch estimation approach is that the posterior probability of each model is fixed for the whole simulation period, unlike the case in sequential data assimilation where a restricted set of parameters is exposed to each observation ... here we use both methods to include and assess the impact that this implementation difference may have on the results presented below.
178.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that improved predictive precision from data-constrained models occurred irrespective of whether sequential (EPIFIL) or simultaneous/batch (LYMFASIM, TRANSFIL) assimilation was used. Hence preferring sequential over batch estimation is not presented as the critical determinant of forecast reliability in this study.

178.Option: The use of a deterministic model structure over a stochastic one in simulating intervention outcomes
Evidence: This gain in precision, and thus the information gained using these data streams, is, as expected, greater for the stochastic LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL models compared to the deterministic EPIFIL model.
Note that variations in scenario 0 predictions among the three models directly reflect the different model structures, parameterizations, and the presence (or absence) of stochastic elements.

Thus, for all models and sites, model-only predictions made in the absence of data (scenario 0) showed the highest prediction uncertainty, highlighting the need for data to improve the predictive performance of the present models.
178.Rationale: The passage documents differences between deterministic and stochastic model behaviors, but it emphasizes that calibration to appropriate data (especially including post-MDA5) is what most reduces uncertainty. It does not claim that choosing a deterministic structure over a stochastic one is the primary factor for reliable forecasts; in fact stochastic models showed larger gains when informed by data, indicating model type alone is not the critical determinant.

178.Option: The number of parameters estimated from the data stream used to calibrate the model
Evidence: The number of parameters which were left free to be fitted to these data by each model range from 3 (LYMFASIM and TRANSFIL) to 21 (EPIFIL).

The assessment of the parameter space constraint achieved through the inclusion of data was made by determining if the fitted parameter distributions for the model became reduced in comparison with priors as data streams were added to the system [33]. The exercise showed that the size of the estimated parameter distributions reduced with addition of data, with even scenario 1 data producing reductions for Kirare and Peneng (Fig 5). In the case of Alagramam, however, there was very little, if any, constraint in the fitted parameter space compared to the prior parameter space.

This result, together with the fact that even using all the data in Kirare and Peneng produced up to only between 2.5 to 5% reductions in fitted parameter distributions when compared to the priors, indicate that the observed model prediction uncertainty in this study may be due to other complex factors connected with model parameterization.
178.Rationale: Although the number of parameters estimated varies across models and parameter constraint is assessed, the passage indicates that simply estimating more parameters does not by itself guarantee reliable forecasts. Some sites showed little constraint despite data, and prediction uncertainty was linked more strongly to the presence and timing of informative data streams (e.g. post-MDA5) than to the sheer number of estimated parameters.

178.Option: The inclusion of post-MDA 5 microfilaria prevalence data in model calibration
Evidence: Thus, the four data streams considered for investigating the value of information gained from each survey were respectively: scenario 1—baseline mf prevalence data only, scenario 2—baseline and post-MDA 3 mf prevalence data, scenario 3—baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data, and scenario 4—baseline and post-MDA 5 mf prevalence data.

The results also show, however, that data streams including post-MDA 5 mf survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) reduced model uncertainty (based on both the variance and entropy measures) most compared to data streams containing only baseline and/or post-MDA 3 mf survey data (scenarios 1 and 2) (Table 2).
overall, scenario 3, which includes baseline, post-MDA 3, and post-MDA 5 data, was shown to most often reduce model uncertainty the greatest.

The shaded regions show the weighted 95% percentile interval from the composite predictions of all three models of the timelines required to cross the WHO 1% elimination threshold for all five scenarios. ... The range of predictions is tightest when the models were constrained with data from scenarios 3 and 4.
178.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies inclusion of post-MDA5 survey data (scenarios 3 and 4) as producing the largest reductions in prediction uncertainty and the tightest prediction intervals across models and sites. Given the observed variability across data assimilation strategies, the most critical factor for reliable forecasts is therefore the availability and assimilation of informative later post-MDA prevalence data (particularly the post-MDA5 measurement).

179.Option: The non-specific clinical presentations of zoonotic pathogens make them difficult to distinguish from other febrile illnesses, leading to consistent misattribution and under-diagnosis
Evidence: Clinical recognition of many zoonoses is made difficult by their nonspecific clinical presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses.

In contrast, the three bacterial zoonoses of key interest in this study have non-specific manifestations in both humans and animals, greatly complicating recognition and contributing to their apparent on-going invisibility to healthcare providers.
179.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that many zoonoses have nonspecific presentations that overlap with other febrile illnesses and that the specific bacterial zoonoses studied have non-specific manifestations in humans and animals. These statements directly support the conclusion that non-specific clinical presentations make zoonotic infections hard to distinguish clinically, producing misattribution and persistent under-diagnosis.

179.Option: Healthcare providers in the region lack awareness of the existence of zoonotic pathogens, leading to a failure to include them in differential diagnoses
Evidence: The few studies conducted to date reveal limited awareness of zoonoses among health workers [15–17].
When asked about possible explanations for the apparent under-reporting of zoonoses, 44 (75%) respondents indicated that healthcare providers lacked knowledge or awareness of zoonoses.
179.Rationale: The passage documents limited awareness of zoonoses among health workers and reports that 75% of respondents identified lack of knowledge/awareness as an explanation for under-reporting. This supports the option that lack of provider awareness leads to failure to consider zoonotic pathogens in differential diagnoses and thus contributes to under-diagnosis.

179.Option: Healthcare providers consistently over-diagnose malaria due to a lack of training in differential diagnosis, which diverts attention from zoonotic causes of fever
Evidence: Previous studies in northern Tanzania have indicated that clinicians’ perceptions of patient pressure play a role in malaria over-diagnosis and over-treatment, suggesting a need to raise awareness about alternative causes of febrile illness, including zoonoses, among both the healthcare provider and patient populations [13,14].
This may partly explain the frequent misattribution of febrile zoonoses to pathogens for which there is greater awareness, such as malaria and typhoid [9].
malaria was clinically diagnosed in 60.7% of this cohort despite being the actual cause of fever in only 1.6% [9].
Seventeen (29%) respondents mentioned a common assumption among healthcare providers that a fever indicates malaria.
179.Rationale: The passage links clinician behavior (including perceived patient pressure and habit) to malaria over-diagnosis and misattribution of febrile illnesses. The large discrepancy between clinical malaria diagnoses and confirmed malaria, plus respondents reporting the assumption that fever equals malaria, supports that over-diagnosis of malaria (driven in part by insufficient differential diagnostic consideration/training and systemic pressures) diverts attention from zoonotic causes and contributes to under-diagnosis.

179.Option: The lack of available diagnostic tests for zoonotic diseases prevents healthcare providers from confirming suspected cases, resulting in systematic under-diagnosis
Evidence: It is widely recognized that limited testing capacity (considering both test availability and performance) in sub-Saharan Africa severely limits the options for accurate laboratory diagnostic testing for zoonoses [12].

A lack of testing capacity was also identified as playing a crucial role in the apparent under-diagnosis of zoonoses. Forty-six respondents (78%) pointed to the lack of available tests for zoonoses in the quantitative survey and diagnostic constraints were described explicitly in interviews:

"People come to the hospital with fever, but when you test they don’t have malaria, then what do they have? You start to guess, but if we had facilities to test diseases then you could test and confirm" (BZQ-037, Human healthcare sector respondent).
179.Rationale: The passage emphasizes limited testing capacity as a major constraint and reports that 78% of respondents pointed to lack of available tests. The included interview quote shows clinicians resort to guessing when diagnostics are unavailable. Together these support that absence of diagnostics prevents confirmation of zoonotic cases and leads to systematic under-diagnosis.

179.Option: The high prevalence of malaria in the region leads to a clinical bias where all febrile patients are assumed to have malaria, preventing consideration of alternative diagnoses
Evidence: The low frequency of zoonoses diagnoses also appears to be tied to persistent perceptions that malaria is the primary etiology of fever, even though epidemiological evidence increasingly contradicts this notion [9,16].
Seventeen (29%) respondents mentioned a common assumption among healthcare providers that a fever indicates malaria.

"The doctors have developed a habit where they assume people with fever symptoms are suffering from malaria. That is why many clinicians do not remember to check zoonotic diseases" (BZQ-062, Human healthcare sector respondent).
malaria was clinically diagnosed in 60.7% of this cohort despite being the actual cause of fever in only 1.6% [9].
179.Rationale: The passage documents a persistent clinical perception that fever indicates malaria, supported by respondent comments and large discrepancies between clinical and confirmed malaria prevalence. These findings indicate a diagnostic bias toward malaria that prevents consideration of alternative causes, including zoonoses, and thus contributes to ongoing under-diagnosis.

180.Option: The interaction between NDMA and ESP results in compensatory downregulation of Cx43 to limit excessive proliferation, indicating a protective cellular feedback mechanism
Evidence: The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).
Western blotting indicated increases in Cx26 and Cx43 expression in cells treated with ESP and NDMA + ESP (Fig 3).
In conclusion, our results suggest that Cx43 plays a key role in cell proliferation, potentially leading to CHCA development upon stimulation by ESP of C. sinensis and NDMA.
180.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports increased Cx43 expression after combined NDMA + ESP treatment, not downregulation. Cx43 is described as playing a key role in proliferation and potentially promoting carcinogenesis, so the claim of a compensatory downregulation to limit proliferation contradicts the reported findings.

180.Option: The combined exposure leads to a non-specific increase in cell proliferation and gap junction protein expression, suggesting a general stress response rather than a targeted biological mechanism
Evidence: Compared to control cells, the average increase for cells treated with NDMA was 112%, for cells treated with ESP was 120% (P < 0.05), and for cells treated with NDMA + ESP was 146% (P < 0.001). NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).

The expression levels of several cell proliferation- including E2F1, Ki-67 and Cy-19, and Cox-2 (an essential regulator of the G2/M transition) as cancer-related makers, were upregulated, especially in NDMA + ESP-treated cells (Fig 2).

Cx43 knockdown using siRNA reduced cell proliferation and significantly suppressed the expression of Cx26 and Cox-2 in H69 cells stimulated with NDMA and ESP compare to silencing negative control stimulated with NDMA and ESP.
180.Rationale: While proliferation and connexin expression increase, the passage documents specific molecular changes (upregulation of E2F1, Ki-67, Cy-19, Cox-2, and Cx43) and shows that silencing Cx43 alters the response. This indicates a targeted, mechanistic pathway (involving Cx43 and Cox-2/NFκB) rather than merely a non-specific general stress response.

180.Option: The combined exposure induces cell cycle arrest in the G2/M phase, which is counteracted by Cx43-mediated proliferation, suggesting a balance between growth inhibition and promotion
Evidence: In H69 cells treated with NDMA, ESP, or NDMA + ESP for 72 h, the number of cells in SubG1 phase did not change significantly, whereas cell numbers in the G0/G1 phase significantly decreased, but cells in the G2/M phase increased significantly compared to cell counts in the control group (Fig 1B).
The proportions of G2/M-phase cells in each condition were as follows: control, 17%; NDMA, 20%; ESP, 26%; NDMA + ESP, 37% (P = 0.007).
NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).

Cx43 knockdown using siRNA reduced cell proliferation and significantly suppressed the expression of Cx26 and Cox-2 in H69 cells stimulated with NDMA and ESP compare to silencing negative control stimulated with NDMA and ESP.
180.Rationale: The data show an increased proportion of cells in G2/M and overall increased proliferation, which the authors interpret as stimulation of cell cycle progression rather than arrest. There is no evidence presented that G2/M represents a stable arrest that is then 'counteracted' by Cx43; instead, Cx43 appears to promote proliferation and be part of the pro-proliferative response. Thus the statement about G2/M arrest counteracted by Cx43 is not supported.

180.Option: The synergistic effect on proliferation is primarily driven by Cox-2 upregulation, with Cx43 playing a secondary role in modulating the inflammatory response
Evidence: The expression levels of several cell proliferation- including E2F1, Ki-67 and Cy-19, and Cox-2 (an essential regulator of the G2/M transition) as cancer-related makers, were upregulated, especially in NDMA + ESP-treated cells (Fig 2).

Cx43 knockdown using siRNA reduced cell proliferation and significantly suppressed the expression of Cx26 and Cox-2 in H69 cells stimulated with NDMA and ESP compare to silencing negative control stimulated with NDMA and ESP.
Taken together, the present findings suggest that Cx43 expression induces Cox2 over-expression via NFκB activation.
180.Rationale: The passage indicates Cox-2 is upregulated, but evidence shows Cx43 knockdown reduces Cox-2 and proliferation, and the authors propose Cx43 may induce Cox-2 via NFκB. This places Cx43 upstream or central to the cascade rather than strictly secondary. Therefore the claim that Cox-2 is the primary driver with Cx43 secondary contradicts the data suggesting a Cx43-dependent signaling contribution to Cox-2 upregulation and proliferation.

180.Option: The combined exposure triggers a Cx43-dependent signaling cascade that amplifies proliferation and inflammatory mediator expression, indicating a coordinated pro-carcinogenic response
Evidence: Compared to control cells, the average increase for cells treated with NDMA was 112%, for cells treated with ESP was 120% (P < 0.05), and for cells treated with NDMA + ESP was 146% (P < 0.001). NDMA and ESP had synergistic effects on cell proliferation (Fig 1A).
The expression levels of Cx26 and Cx43 were high in NDMA + ESP-treated cells, as confirmed by immunoblotting (Fig 3).

Cx43 knockdown using siRNA reduced cell proliferation and significantly suppressed the expression of Cx26 and Cox-2 in H69 cells stimulated with NDMA and ESP compare to silencing negative control stimulated with NDMA and ESP.
Taken together, the present findings suggest that Cx43 expression induces Cox2 over-expression via NFκB activation.
In conclusion, our results suggest that Cx43 plays a key role in cell proliferation, potentially leading to CHCA development upon stimulation by ESP of C. sinensis and NDMA.
180.Rationale: The passage documents a synergistic increase in proliferation with NDMA + ESP, increased Cx43 expression with combined exposure, and that silencing Cx43 reduces both proliferation and Cox-2 (an inflammatory/cell-cycle mediator). The authors propose a Cx43→NFκB→Cox-2 link. These points support a model in which the combined exposures activate a Cx43-dependent signaling cascade that amplifies proliferation and inflammatory mediator expression, consistent with a coordinated pro-carcinogenic biological response.

181.Option: Reliance on the Kato-Katz technique alone may overestimate cure rates and lead to incorrect conclusions about the effectiveness of repeated PZQ dosing
Evidence: Most studies reporting on the efficacy of PZQ have used microscopy-based methods, such as urine filtration for S. haematobium and the stool-based Kato-Katz (KK) technique for S. japonicum and S. mansoni. However, these methods lack sensitivity, especially for detection of low-intensity infections [17, 18]. Hence, reported CRs based on these parasitological methods are likely an overestimation [19, 20].
POC-CCA ... has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

CRs based on POC-CCA were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% (95% CI 11–27%) in the standard treatment group and 36% (95% CI 26–46%) in the intense treatment group (P<0.01).
not being able to detect eggs does not necessarily mean that the infection is cured.
181.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that microscopy-based methods like Kato-Katz lack sensitivity and that CRs based on these methods are likely overestimates. The trial found substantially higher KK-based cure rates than POC-CCA-based cure rates. Therefore relying solely on KK would produce inflated estimates of cure and could lead investigators to conclude that repeated dosing is more efficacious than it truly is in eliminating worms, biasing the interpretation of the optimal dosing strategy.

181.Option: The use of both diagnostic methods creates measurement inconsistency, making it impossible to determine the true cure rate and invalidating the trial’s conclusions about dosing strategy
Evidence: To more accurately assess the CR, the POC-CCA test was employed in addition to KK.
CRs based on POC-CCA were much lower compared to CRs based on the KK technique; only 18% ... and 36% ...

While the CR in the standard treatment group was significantly higher four weeks after treatment compared to the CR obtained 10 weeks after treatment, there was no significant difference in the CRs between the standard treatment group and the intense treatment group four weeks after the last treatment, measured with both KK and POC-CCA. Hence, following this evaluation approach, there is no indication that four repeated PZQ treatments outperform a single treatment in curing schistosomiasis.
181.Rationale: Although the two diagnostics give discrepant CR estimates, the study explicitly used both tests to provide complementary perspectives and performed analyses (including comparing the same post-treatment intervals) to interpret efficacy. The passage does not claim the conclusions are invalidated or that it is impossible to determine anything; instead, authors interpret the discordance cautiously, discuss implications (e.g., remaining worms vs. egg reduction, reinfection), and draw nuanced conclusions. Thus the statement that the trial's conclusions are invalidated is not supported.

181.Option: The use of POC-CCA may underestimate cure rates due to false positives from urinary tract infections, leading to an overestimation of treatment failure and incorrect conclusions about dosing strategy
Evidence: Previous studies have demonstrated false positive POC-CCA results in people with urinary tract infections [43] as well as potentially in pregnant women [44] and new born babies.
In our study, no urinary tract infections were noted, two female participants were excluded because of possible pregnancy, and only children aged 5 years and above were included.
To further minimize the inclusion of false positives, traces were considered as negative in our analysis.
181.Rationale: While POC-CCA can produce false positives in some contexts (e.g., urinary tract infections), the investigators report that no urinary tract infections were noted in this trial and took steps (excluding possible pregnancies, treating traces as negative) to minimize false positives. Therefore although the general concern is valid in other settings, in this trial POC-CCA false positives from UTIs are unlikely to explain the lower POC-CCA cure rates, so the option's asserted mechanism for underestimating cure and misguiding conclusions does not apply here.

181.Option: The discrepancy in cure rates between diagnostic methods suggests that PZQ is ineffective against immature worms, undermining the rationale for repeated dosing and invalidating the trial's conclusions
Evidence: In addition to the possible overestimation of CRs due to insensitive diagnostic tools, the limited activity of PZQ on immature worms as well as continuing reinfection might have led to an underestimation of the efficacy [24–27].

Our aim of administering PZQ four times at 2-week intervals was to achieve a high CR, as this approach not only targets adult schistosomes, but also the immature forms, which were not yet drug-susceptible during the first treatment [12, 29, 42].

Based on stool microscopy, we observed a significantly higher CR after four closely spaced PZQ treatments compared to a single dose, measured 10 weeks after the first treatment ... Employing the POC-CCA test, the observed CRs were considerably lower compared to KK, even after four repeated treatments, indicating that worms are still present and that PZQ might be less efficacious than previously published.
181.Rationale: The passage acknowledges limited activity of PZQ on immature worms and gives that as the rationale for repeated dosing. The observed diagnostic discrepancy does not demonstrate PZQ is ineffective against immature worms; rather, it points to complexities (immature worms, antigen persistence, reinfection, or egg-production reduction). The trial's rationale for repeated dosing remains biologically plausible and the discrepancy does not, by itself, invalidate conclusions about the strategy.

181.Option: The higher cure rate observed with the Kato-Katz technique reflects a true biological advantage of repeated PZQ dosing, and the lower POC-CCA rate is due to assay degradation over time, invalidating the use of POC-CCA in this study
Evidence: POC-CCA ... has shown a higher sensitivity for detecting S. mansoni infections than the KK technique [20–22].

Employing the POC-CCA test, the observed CRs were considerably lower compared to KK, even after four repeated treatments, indicating that worms are still present and that PZQ might be less efficacious than previously published.
POC-CCA might also originate from new infections or immature worms [55] or perhaps from dead worms that were killed by PZQ.
To more accurately assess the CR, the POC-CCA test was employed in addition to KK.
181.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence of assay degradation of POC-CCA over time; instead it describes POC-CCA as more sensitive and interprets persistent POC-CCA positivity as possible continued worm presence, new infections, immature worms, or antigen persistence. The authors used POC-CCA deliberately to improve assessment. Therefore the claim that POC-CCA degradation invalidates its use in this study is not supported by the text.

182.Option: Induction of Granzyme B-producing CD4+ T cells, which are associated with cytotoxic activity and protection against severe disease forms in leishmaniasis
Evidence: With regard to CD4+ T cells producing GrB, we showed a significant increase in their percentages after stimulation with P8 (4/12; 1.4/3.6), P9 (3.7/6.1; 1.4/3.6), P16 (4/11; 1.4/3.6) and P21 (6/12; 1.4/3.6) (p≤0.03) (Fig 8B and S1 Fig).

GrB, is a proapoptotic protease that has been associated with both tissue damage and good prognosis in CL and VL patients [28–32, 95, 96]. It was recently reported that GrB constitutes a good correlate for protection against severe forms of CL [33].
182.Rationale: The passage documents that peptide pools induced increases in GrB-producing CD4+ T cells and cites evidence that GrB can correlate with protection against severe disease. However, the question asks which feature most strongly supports durable protective immunity. While GrB production is associated with cytotoxic function and may correlate with protection from severe forms, the passage frames GrB more in relation to prognosis/severity rather than long-term, durable immunity. Therefore, although supportive of protection from severe disease, this feature is not the strongest evidence for durable (long-term) protective immunity in the study.

182.Option: Induction of high levels of IFN-γ and TNF-α, which are critical for macrophage activation and parasite clearance during acute infection
Evidence: We showed significant IFN-γ levels in response to pools containing HLA-I, HLA-II or HLA-I and -II multi-epitope peptides in cured CL individuals, whereas we could not detect any significant IFN-γ production in healthy subjects, indicating the Leishmania specificity of the responses observed.
Significant TNF-α levels were induced by P21 in cured (median/IQR in stimulated; unstimulated cultures: 10/13; 0/1.9) (p = 0.001), but not in healthy individuals (5.4/2; 1.9/10) (p>0.05) (Fig 6A).
It is generally considered that a dominant T helper 1 (Th1)-type cellular immune response and IFN-γ production are critical for infection control.
182.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that peptide pools induce IFN-γ and TNF-α, cytokines important for macrophage activation and parasite clearance during acute infection. However, the question focuses on evidence for durable protective immunity. IFN-γ and TNF-α indicate effective effector responses and are essential for acute control, but the study emphasizes other measures (central memory T cells) as specifically linked to long-term resistance. Thus induction of IFN-γ/TNF-α supports protective mechanisms but is not the strongest evidence for durable immunity in this work.

182.Option: Induction of a strong and specific IFN-γ response in healthy individuals, indicating broad population-level immunogenicity and potential for universal vaccine coverage
Evidence: These responses were specific as no significant IFN-γ levels were detected in healthy individuals, except for P12 (Figs 2 and 3).
H2BI and H2BII were not able to induce a significant IFN-γ response in healthy individuals (Fig 1B).
182.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that significant IFN-γ responses were observed in cured CL individuals but not in healthy controls (with the exception of one peptide pool, P12). Therefore the claim that a strong, specific IFN-γ response was induced in healthy individuals is not supported by the data. This option is incorrect both because the evidence contradicts it and because population-level immunogenicity in unexposed (healthy) individuals is not shown.

182.Option: Induction of multifunctional CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2, which are associated with robust and sustained immune responses in chronic infections
Evidence: No significant increase was observed in the percentage of CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, IL-2 and TNF-α, in response to both peptide pools and SLA in cured CL individuals.

Finally we evaluated the ability of peptide pools to stimulate a multifunctional CD4+ T cell response, using Boolean gating (S4 Fig). As shown in Fig 10, no significant increase was observed in the percentage of CD4+ T cells producing IFN-γ, IL-2 and TNF-α, in response to both peptide pools and SLA in cured CL individuals.

However, ... a significant increase in the frequencies of bifunctional IFN-γ+/TNF-α+, IL-2+/TNF-α+, and monofunctional IFN-γ+ and TNF-α+ CD4+ T cells, was shown in response to SLA and to peptide pools P13, P14 and P21 (p<0.04).
182.Rationale: Although multifunctional T cells (simultaneous IFN-γ+, TNF-α+, IL-2+) are often considered desirable for durable responses, the study explicitly reports that no significant increase in triple-functional CD4+ T cells was observed after peptide stimulation. The study did find increases in bifunctional and monofunctional T cells, but not the three-cytokine multifunctional subset claimed here. Therefore this option is not supported by the data.

182.Option: Induction of central memory T cells (TCM) in response to peptide stimulation, which are associated with long-term resistance in the absence of persistent infection
Evidence: A significant increase in the percentage of CD4+ TCM as well as CD8+ TCM cells was observed in response to P8 (median/IQR ...), P13 (...), P14 (...) and P21 (...) (p≤0.04) (Fig 11A and 11C).
Finally, we showed that healed CL individuals exhibited a significant increase in the percentages of CD4+ and CD8+ TCM (CD45RO+CCR7+) cells when stimulated with P8, P13, P14 and P21 peptide pools.

It was demonstrated in a murine model of leishmaniasis that unlike TEM, TCM are maintained in the absence of parasites and mediate long-term resistance, suggesting that these cells should be the targets for nonlive vaccines against infectious diseases requiring cell-mediated immunity [103].

Moreover, the same peptide pools (except P13) also induced significant levels of IFN-γ, when analyzed by the cultured ELISPOT assay, which has been described to detect central memory T cells [101, 102].
182.Rationale: The passage documents that peptide pools induced significant increases in CD4+ and CD8+ central memory T cells (TCM). It also cites experimental evidence that TCM are maintained without persistent infection and mediate long-term resistance, making them specifically linked to durable immunity. The combination of (1) observed induction of TCM by the peptides and (2) the established role of TCM in long-term resistance provides the strongest support in this study for the potential to induce durable protective immunity.

183.Option: Implementation of a control band that appears at a fixed location on the test strip to confirm assay validity and reduce false-negative interpretations
Evidence: A control band of anti-rabbit antibodies located above the test band also becomes visible as the sample travels along the length of the dipstick. The presence of this band is used to confirm the proper function of the test strip (Fig 2).
A dark purple control band is visible near the top of each test strip, indicating the proper functioning of the dipsticks.
183.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a control band that confirms proper function of the dipstick and thereby helps detect failed assays (supporting reduction of false-negatives due to assay failure). However, the question asks which design feature most directly reduces measurement error associated with subjective interpretation of results. While the control band improves assay validity, it does not directly standardize or simplify the operator's interpretation of whether a sample is positive or negative; it only indicates the strip worked. The feature that most directly addresses subjective interpretation is the binary visual test-band readout that standardizes results (see evidence cited under Option 3). Therefore, while the control band reduces some error related to assay failure, it is not the most direct contributor to reducing subjective-interpretation measurement error.

183.Option: Incorporation of a biotin-labeled primer and a FAM-labeled probe to enable dual-label detection and increase signal specificity
Evidence: Detection of the amplified product with HybriDetect test strips requires that the DNA product be labeled with both a biotin tag (in order to bind to the streptavidin band of the test strip) and a FAM tag (in order to bind to the gold-labeled anti-fluorescein antibodies that result in a visible band for detection).
Biotin was incorporated into the product of the PCR reaction by labeling the reverse primer as described above. FAM was incorporated into the product using a 5’ FAM-labeled sequence-specific probe...

Polyclonal (rabbit) anti-FITC antibodies, each conjugated to a gold particle, then bind to the FAM-labeled product, and the congregation of gold particles at the streptavidin test line causes a visible accumulation of color that is indicative of a positive result.

When tested using the B. malayi field-friendly PCR platform described above, all pools containing non-Brugian filarial DNA tested negative, regardless of whether results were visualized by gel electrophoresis or test strip-based detection. However, B. pahangi genomic DNA produced positive results, indicating cross-reactivity, when examined by both visualization techniques (S1 Fig).
183.Rationale: Dual-labeling (biotin + FAM) is described in the passage and supports specific binding to the test strip components and generation of a visible signal, which can increase molecular specificity of detection. However, the question targets reduction of measurement error due to subjective interpretation (operator reading). Dual-label chemistry improves the biochemical specificity of the assay but does not itself standardize the visual interpretation (presence/absence) or make the readout less subjective. Moreover, the passage notes cross-reactivity with B. pahangi, indicating that molecular specificity is not perfect. Thus this feature does not most directly address subjective-interpretation measurement error.

183.Option: Use of agarose gel electrophoresis as a secondary validation method to cross-check results and reduce false-positive calls
Evidence: HDA assays typically rely on agarose gel electrophoresis, a proven means of effective target detection, such techniques are prone to sample contamination [33], and are difficult to perform in a field setting.
All conventional PCR products were visualized using both test strip-based detection and agarose gel electrophoresis.
In the second instance of discordance (Sample #46), real-time PCR and test strip-based detection assays returned positive results, while the gel electrophoresis results were negative.
183.Rationale: Agarose gel electrophoresis can serve as a secondary validation method and is a traditional way to visualize PCR products. However, the passage explicitly describes gels as prone to contamination and difficult to perform in field settings, and presents discordant examples where gel results did not agree with other methods. Gels also require subjective interpretation of band presence/intensity and are less field-friendly. Therefore, while gel electrophoresis can help cross-check results, it does not most directly reduce the measurement error associated with subjective interpretation compared with the standardized, binary test-strip readout described in the passage.

183.Option: Use of a binary visual readout (presence or absence of a test band) on a test strip to standardize result interpretation
Evidence: When running LAMP assays detection of positive samples relies on the visualization of either sample turbidity or sample fluorescence.
As such, results are not “presence” or “absence”-based, but rather occur on a spectrum. This potentially raises concerns regarding susceptibility to technician bias, fatigue, or interpretation.

In contrast, the field-friendly, “Backpack PCR” platform we describe here utilizes test strip-based DNA detection methods. Such technology partners the advantages of standardizing assay readouts with the capacity to minimize laboratory equipment needs.

Polyclonal (rabbit) anti-FITC antibodies, each conjugated to a gold particle, then bind to the FAM-labeled product, and the congregation of gold particles at the streptavidin test line causes a visible accumulation of color that is indicative of a positive result.
183.Rationale: The passage contrasts subjective, spectrum-based LAMP readouts (turbidity/fluorescence) that "raise[] concerns regarding susceptibility to technician bias" with the Backpack PCR test strip platform, which "partners the advantages of standardizing assay readouts." The description of a visible accumulation of color "indicative of a positive result" indicates a clear presence/absence visual cue. Thus the binary visual readout on the test strip most directly targets and reduces measurement error stemming from subjective interpretation.

183.Option: Use of a smartphone-controlled thermal cycler to ensure consistent amplification conditions and minimize technical variability across runs
Evidence: portable, battery-powered and smartphone-controlled thermal cycler (miniPCR) which weighs less than 1 lb.

"Backpack PCR" minimizes equipment needs since it requires only a dry bath, a low-speed microcentrifuge, and a portable, battery-powered and smartphone-controlled thermal cycler (miniPCR) which weighs less than 1 lb.
183.Rationale: A smartphone-controlled miniPCR provides portability and consistent thermal cycling conditions, which reduces technical variability in amplification. However, the question asks specifically about reducing measurement error associated with subjective interpretation of results. Consistent amplification reduces analytical variability but does not directly change how an operator interprets the final result visually. Therefore this feature does not most directly address the subjective-interpretation measurement error compared with the binary test-strip readout.

184.Option: Increased population of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells in the spleen after treatment with AAL and AAS
Evidence: A low population of CD4+ (8.2%) and CD8+ (5.1%) T cells were detected in the spleens of mice with established L. donovani infection (Fig. 7). The population of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells increased 10 days after treatment with 50 mg/kg b.w. of AAL (14.4% and 10.9%) and AAS (16.8% and 12.7%), respectively. The increase was slightly more at a treatment dose of 100 mg/kg b.w. of AAL (18.4% and 13%) and AAS (18.9% and 15.4%). The CD4+ and CD8+ T cell population was, however, highest at 200 mg/kg b.w. treatment with AAL (20.7% and 15.2%) and AAS (21.8% and 16.12%) (Fig. 7). These findings demonstrate a prominent inclination toward Th1 effector function and the involvement of both CD4+ and CD8+ T cells at cure with AAL and AAS treatment.
184.Rationale: While increased splenic CD4+ and CD8+ T cell counts indicate an augmented cellular immune response and are consistent with recovery/cure, they do not specifically demonstrate long-term, antigen-specific memory or resistance to reinfection. The question asks for the parameter that most strongly supports long-term protection; total increased T cell populations are evidence of enhanced effector responses but are less direct as a marker of durable memory than explicit memory-cell phenotypes (e.g., CD44high CD62Llow).

184.Option: Elevated levels of serum IFN-γ and reduced levels of IL-4 and IL-10 in treated mice
Evidence: Mice treated with AAL and AAS (200 mg/kg b.w.) induced significantly elevated levels of serum IFN-γ (2771±50.91 and 3033±396.69 pg ml−1) and reduced levels of IL-4 (4020±91.92 and 3961.5±208.24 pg ml−1) and IL-10 (4231.5±459.27 and 4077.5±35.0 pg ml−1) compared to untreated infected controls INF (low IFN-γ; 1236±12.37, high IL-4; 5696±79.9 and high IL-10; 5049±101.47) (Fig. 6A).

Bhattacharjee et al., [55] reported that the expression of Th1 signature cytokines (IFN-γ and IL–2) is protective for VL whereas expression of Th2 cytokines viz. IL-4 and IL-10 increases during infection.
184.Rationale: A shift to a Th1 cytokine profile (higher IFN-γ and lower IL-4/IL-10) is a strong indicator of protective immunity and effective parasite clearance. However, cytokine milieu reflects the current immune polarization and effector function rather than directly proving the existence of long-lived memory cells and durable protection against reinfection. Therefore, although supportive, these cytokine changes are not the single strongest immunological parameter for long-term protection compared with explicit memory T-cell markers.

184.Option: Increased generation of CD44high CD62Llow effector memory CD8+ T cells
Evidence: Resistant to Leishmania re-infection is attributed to generation of memory T cells in the host [16].

CD44 CD62L expression was low in infected control group (10.1%), which was up-regulated after treatment with AAL (13.8%) and AAS (15.1%) at 200 mg/kg b.w. demonstrating resolution of infection and generation of memory.

Further the percentage of effector memory cytotoxic T cells (CD44high CD62Llow) was also increased following treatment with AAL (37.1%), AAS (38.8%) and AMB (37.3%) and no such up-regulation was evident in case of ART (27.9%) treatment (Fig. 10).
184.Rationale: The presence and increase of CD44high CD62Llow CD8+ T cells are explicit markers of effector/memory T-cell differentiation. The passage directly links generation of memory T cells to resistance to re-infection and documents increased frequencies of CD44high CD62Llow cells after AAL/AAS treatment. That direct connection between memory phenotype and resistance to reinfection makes this parameter the strongest support for long-term protection.

184.Option: Enhanced expression of CD80 and CD86 co-stimulatory molecules on antigen-presenting cells
Evidence: Treatment with AAL and AAS significantly enhanced CD80 and CD86 expression and co-expression on both CD11b+ and F4/80+ cells however maximum expression was observed in case of F4/80+ population.
Ligation of CD28 with CD80 and CD86 is known to induce the secretion of IL-6 and IFN-γ by DCs for T and B cell activation, proliferation, and differentiation [62].
184.Rationale: Up-regulation of CD80/CD86 on APCs indicates improved antigen presentation and costimulation, which promotes effective priming and activation of T cells and supports development of immunity. Nevertheless, this is an upstream activational signal and does not itself constitute direct evidence of durable, antigen-specific memory or long-term protection against reinfection. Therefore it is supportive but not the strongest single indicator of long-term protection.

184.Option: Significant reduction in spleen weight and hepatic parasite burden following treatment with AAL and AAS
Evidence: AAL and AAS administered to 10 weeks infected BALB/c mice at 200 mg/kg b.w. for 10 consecutive days caused 95.45±2.05% and 95.84±1.95% (P<0.001) reduction of parasite burden in spleen and 86.67±2.53% and 89.12±1.92% (P<0.001) in liver, respectively (Fig. 1A & B) at 10 days post treatment.

AAL and AAS treatment (200 mg/kg b.w.) also resulted in significant reduction (48.84% and 45.35% respectively) in spleen weight compared to infected controls (Fig. 1 C & D) that was comparable with AMB (52.33%).
Taken together, our findings indicate that treatment of infected mice with AAL and AAS significantly decreased the hepatic and splenic parasite load with reduction in spleen weight.
184.Rationale: Reduction in organ parasite burden and splenomegaly demonstrates effective treatment and clinical cure. However, these end points document current parasite clearance rather than providing direct evidence of long-term, immunological memory that would protect against future reinfection. Thus they support efficacy but are not the most specific immunological parameter for durable protection.

185.Option: Individuals who know that the MDA is for preventing lymphedema and that everyone is at risk for LF
Evidence: knowing the MDA was for LF (adjusted OR = 3.3; 95% CI: 1.7–6.6)
knowing everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 2.2; 95% CI 1.0–4.8; p = 0.04)
knowing both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 16.1; 95% CI: 8.8–29.3)
185.Rationale: The multivariable model reports an adjusted OR of 3.3 for "knowing the MDA was for LF" and 2.2 for "knowing everyone was at risk for LF." However, the largest effect (and the interaction retained in the model) was for knowing both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk (adjusted OR = 16.1). The option pairs "MDA is for preventing lymphedema" with "everyone at risk," which is not the interaction shown to give the highest likelihood; therefore this group would not be expected to have the highest adherence.

185.Option: Individuals who know both that the MDA is scheduled in advance and that everyone is at risk for LF
Evidence: A statistically significant interaction between knowing about the MDA in advance of its occurrence and knowing everyone was at risk for LF was found and therefore was kept in the model.
knowing both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 16.1; 95% CI: 8.8–29.3)
knowing about the MDA in advance (adjusted OR = 4.8; 95% CI: 3.8–8.1)
knowing everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 2.2; 95% CI 1.0–4.8; p = 0.04)
185.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there was a statistically significant interaction between knowing about the MDA in advance and knowing everyone was at risk, and reports that the combined knowledge had an adjusted OR of 16.1 (95% CI: 8.8–29.3), the largest effect reported. Thus individuals with both pieces of knowledge would be expected to have the highest likelihood of adhering to the MDA regimen.

185.Option: Individuals who know that lymphedema management includes leg washing, leg elevation, and leg exercises and that everyone is at risk for LF
Evidence: knowing at least one component of lymphedema management self-care (adjusted OR = 3.3; 95% CI: 1.6–6.9).
knowing everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 2.2; 95% CI 1.0–4.8; p = 0.04)
knowing both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 16.1; 95% CI: 8.8–29.3)
185.Rationale: Knowing components of lymphedema management predicted adherence (adjusted OR = 3.3) and knowing everyone is at risk also predicted adherence (adjusted OR = 2.2), but the passage shows the largest effect arises from the interaction between knowing the MDA in advance and knowing everyone is at risk (adjusted OR = 16.1). There is no evidence that the leg-care + everyone-at-risk combination produces the highest adherence, so this option is not the best answer.

185.Option: Individuals who know that the MDA is scheduled in advance and that the medication is safe and effective
Evidence: knowing about the MDA in advance (adjusted OR = 4.8; 95% CI: 3.8–8.1)
making people aware that side effects of DEC are infrequent and mild.
185.Rationale: The model reports a strong effect for knowing the MDA is scheduled in advance (adjusted OR = 4.8). The passage discusses messaging about side effects being infrequent and mild, but it does not present an adjusted OR for "knowing the medication is safe and effective." Critically, the largest effect was the interaction between advance knowledge of the MDA and knowing everyone was at risk (adjusted OR = 16.1). Because "safe and effective" is not quantified as a predictor in the multivariable model, and because the advance-scheduling + everyone-at-risk interaction was stronger, this option is not supported as the group with the highest expected adherence.

185.Option: Individuals who know that the MDA is scheduled in advance and that lymphedema management includes leg washing, leg elevation, and leg exercises
Evidence: knowing about the MDA in advance (adjusted OR = 4.8; 95% CI: 3.8–8.1)
knowing at least one component of lymphedema management self-care (adjusted OR = 3.3; 95% CI: 1.6–6.9)
knowing both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk for LF (adjusted OR = 16.1; 95% CI: 8.8–29.3)
185.Rationale: Both knowing the MDA in advance (adjusted OR = 4.8) and knowing lymphedema management components (adjusted OR = 3.3) were independent predictors of adherence. However, the passage identifies the greatest likelihood of adherence for those who knew both about the MDA in advance and that everyone was at risk (adjusted OR = 16.1). There is no evidence that the specific combination in this option exceeds that effect, so this group would not be expected to have the highest adherence.

186.Option: The degree of antigenic similarity between the venom toxins and the immunogens used in antivenom production
Evidence: the study highlighted a markedly reduced cross-neutralizing competence of four commercial Indian anti-venoms against the said neglected snake venoms [4].
Each anti-venom showed a marginal or insignificant cross-reactivity/paraspecific reactivity with the other two venoms.
Thus, paradoxically, marginal or lack of formation of cross-reacting antibodies underscore the immunological distinctness of homologous snake venoms.
186.Rationale: The passage repeatedly links lack of antivenom effectiveness against non-target venoms to poor antigenic match and lack of cross-reactivity. If the immunogens used to raise the antivenom are antigenically similar to the toxins in the envenoming species, antibodies will bind and neutralize; conversely, markedly reduced cross-neutralizing competence and marginal cross-reactivity (quotes above) explain failure of protection. Therefore the degree of antigenic similarity is the critical determinant of whether a therapeutic antivenom protects against an immunologically distinct venom.

186.Option: The duration of time between venom exposure and antivenom administration
Evidence: Groups of mice (n = 6) were independently injected intraperitoneally (i.p.) with 2 LD50 (7 mg/kg) dose of Hhv in 50 μl of PBS. Post 10 min of venom injection, the mice were administered with ED50 (140 mg/kg), and 2 ED50 (280 mg/kg) doses of HhAV, and 700 mg/kg body weight of EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV independently via tail vein (i.v.).

EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV did not neutralize the biochemical properties such as proteolytic, indirect hemolytic, L-amino acid oxidase, and 5’-nucleotidase activities of H. hypnale venom even at an anti-venom dose of 10000 μg/ml (Fig 8).
186.Rationale: While time-to-antivenom is clinically important, the passage shows that even when antivenoms were administered shortly after venom (e.g., 10 minutes post-injection), antivenoms that lacked antigenic match still failed to neutralize H. hypnale venom (quote above). This indicates that timing alone cannot overcome a lack of antigenic compatibility; thus duration between exposure and administration is not the most critical factor for cross-species protection in this context.

186.Option: The molecular weight distribution of the venom proteins in the target species
Evidence: All three venoms displayed a unique protein-banding pattern in SDS-PAGE (10%) under non-reduced condition.
Thus, understanding the finer details of antigenicity/immunogenicity of snake venoms is highly challenging.
Each anti-venom showed a marginal or insignificant cross-reactivity/paraspecific reactivity with the other two venoms.
186.Rationale: The passage documents differences in protein-banding and molecular-weight patterns, but emphasizes that the key issue for neutralization is antigenicity/cross-reactivity rather than simple molecular-weight distribution. Differences in banding reflect biochemical variability but do not by themselves determine whether antibodies raised to one venom will recognize another; the presence or absence of shared antigenic epitopes is the decisive factor.

186.Option: The concentration of antivenom administered relative to the venom dose in the envenomed individual
Evidence: EcAV, DrAV, BhAV, and ViAV did not neutralize the biochemical properties such as proteolytic, indirect hemolytic, L-amino acid oxidase, and 5’-nucleotidase activities of H. hypnale venom even at an anti-venom dose of 10000 μg/ml (Fig 8).

Wherever neutralization was achieved, the anti-venoms, HhAV/EcAV/DrAV/BhAV/ViAV neutralized the biochemical and pathological properties of H. hypnale, E. carinatus, and D. russelii venoms at a venom to anti-venom ratio of 1: 30–60 (w/w).
186.Rationale: Dose (concentration) is important for neutralization when antibodies can bind the venom, and the passage reports typical venom:antivenom ratios where neutralization was achieved. However, it also shows that even very high antivenom doses failed to neutralize H. hypnale venom when the antivenom lacked cross-reactive antibodies (quote above). Therefore concentration cannot compensate for a lack of antigenic match and is not the most critical factor for protection against an immunologically distinct venom.

186.Option: The presence of cross-reactive epitopes on the venom proteins that are conserved across species
Evidence: Each anti-venom showed a marginal or insignificant cross-reactivity/paraspecific reactivity with the other two venoms.
In order to quantify the extent of reactivity/cross-reactivity, indirect ELISA was performed.
So, paradoxically, marginal or lack of formation of cross-reacting antibodies underscore the immunological distinctness of homologous snake venoms.
186.Rationale: The ability of an antivenom to neutralize a heterologous venom depends on whether antibodies recognize shared (cross-reactive) epitopes on toxin proteins. The passage directly links marginal cross-reactivity and lack of cross-reacting antibodies to failure of neutralization, and describes experiments (Western blot/ELISA) that measure cross-reactivity (quotes above). Thus the presence of conserved cross-reactive epitopes is a critical determinant of whether a therapeutic antivenom will protect against an immunologically distinct venom.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will lead to a lower estimated seroprevalence, indicating a reduced risk of transmission and allowing for less intensive control measures in endemic regions
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA.

One of the consequences of such variable specificities and sensitivities of different assays for CanL immunodiagnosis is that the seroprevalence in endemic areas can present marked fluctuations depending on the chosen assay [10].
tests that are not sensitive enough to detect infected subclinical dogs can lead to ineffective control of the disease, since these dogs would not be cared for and might act as reservoirs [32].
187.Rationale: This option claims high sensitivity would lower estimated seroprevalence; that is the opposite of epidemiologic expectation and what the passage describes. A more sensitive test detects more subclinical infections (passage: LPG-ELISA identified 90% of subclinical dogs), which increases measured seroprevalence, not lowers it. The passage also explicitly notes seroprevalence can increase or fluctuate depending on assay sensitivity. Therefore the statement is incorrect.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will result in a higher number of false-negative results, leading to underestimation of the true infection burden and undermining the effectiveness of control programs
Evidence: The present LPG-ELISA exhibited 98.5% specificity and 91.5% sensitivity, while the TLA-ELISA, despite having the same specificity, achieved only 85.0% sensitivity.
tests that are not sensitive enough to detect infected subclinical dogs can lead to ineffective control of the disease, since these dogs would not be cared for and might act as reservoirs [32].
Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive...
187.Rationale: High sensitivity reduces false negatives; the passage contrasts more- and less-sensitive assays showing the more sensitive LPG-ELISA detected many more subclinical infections. The option incorrectly asserts high sensitivity increases false negatives and underestimation; in fact low sensitivity causes underestimation. Thus this option is incorrect.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will reduce the number of false-positive results, leading to more accurate estimates of population immunity and enabling broader culling of seropositive dogs in control programs
Evidence: The present LPG-ELISA exhibited 98.5% specificity and 91.5% sensitivity, while the TLA-ELISA, despite having the same specificity, achieved only 85.0% sensitivity.
False-positive results can lead to the unnecessary treatment or even culling of non-infected dogs.

The LPG-ELISA did not result in any cross-reactions in the serum samples from dogs infected by L. braziliensis, T. cruzi, Babesia sp., Ehrlichia sp. or Hepatozoon sp. that were tested in this study, while the TLA-ELISA presented cross reactivity for Ehrlichia sp. (18.2% - 2/11) and Babesia sp. (12.5% - 1/8).
187.Rationale: Reduction of false-positive results is a function of specificity, not sensitivity. A test can be highly sensitive without lowering false positives. The passage distinguishes sensitivity and specificity (gives both values for LPG-ELISA) and warns that false positives can cause unnecessary culling. The option wrongly attributes reduction of false positives to high sensitivity and promotes broader culling, which the passage cautions against; therefore the option is incorrect.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will improve the accuracy of predicting disease progression, allowing for early intervention and reducing the need for population-wide control strategies
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive, while just one sample (1/9) was detected as positive by the TLA-ELISA.

The proper diagnosis of subclinically infected dogs is very important for the control of leishmaniasis in endemic areas, since these dogs can remain untreated or unfollowed. Susceptible subclinically infected dogs can develop active infection, representing a source of infection for other dogs and humans.

Leishmania-specific humoral responses in susceptible dogs are characterized by high levels of specific immunoglobulins [48]. However, such abundant immunoglobulins are non-protective and are instead associated with active infection, intense disease development and presentation of clinical signs in dogs [47, 48].
187.Rationale: The passage explains that detecting subclinical infection is important because such dogs may later progress to active, transmissible disease, and that humoral responses (antibody levels) are associated with susceptibility and active disease. A highly sensitive serologic test that captures subclinical infections therefore improves identification of dogs at risk, enabling earlier targeted interventions (clinical follow-up, treatment, or vector control around infected animals) and could reduce reliance on blunt, population-wide measures. While sensitivity alone does not guarantee perfect prediction of progression, the passage supports that seropositivity and antibody levels are informative about risk of progression, so the option's main idea is supported.

187.Option: A serological test with high sensitivity for subclinical infection will increase estimated seroprevalence, potentially misrepresenting population immunity and necessitating more targeted control measures
Evidence: Regarding the ELISA tests carried out with serum samples from dogs with subclinical infection by L. infantum, the LPG-ELISA identified 9/10 (90%) as positive...

One of the consequences of such variable specificities and sensitivities of different assays for CanL immunodiagnosis is that the seroprevalence in endemic areas can present marked fluctuations depending on the chosen assay [10].

Leishmania-specific humoral responses in susceptible dogs are characterized by high levels of specific immunoglobulins [48]. However, such abundant immunoglobulins are non-protective and are instead associated with active infection, intense disease development and presentation of clinical signs in dogs [47, 48].
187.Rationale: A more sensitive test detects more infections (including subclinical), so measured seroprevalence will increase. The passage warns seroprevalence varies by assay and that antibody detection does not necessarily equate to protective immunity (antibodies can be non-protective and associated with disease). Thus higher seroprevalence might be misinterpreted as greater population-level immunity when it actually reflects more complete detection of infections; this supports shifting to more targeted control measures (e.g., focusing on infected animals or hotspots) rather than assuming herd immunity or applying uniform broad measures. Therefore this option is correct.

188.Option: Fit separate models for each country to estimate individual-level effects while controlling for country-specific baseline infection rates
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.
Data from countries that had only one individual were excluded since it would not be possible to represent the country in both the training set and the testing set.
188.Rationale: The investigators used pooled mixed models rather than fitting separate models per country. Fitting separate country-specific models would be infeasible and unstable given small sample sizes for many countries (noting the authors excluded countries with only one individual). Instead the study used mixed-effects models to borrow strength across countries and explicitly model country-level variation, so the per-country separate-model approach is not the methodological choice used to distinguish country- and individual-level influences.

188.Option: Apply a fixed-effects model with country dummies to control for unobserved country-level confounders while estimating individual-level effects
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.

The second model (M2) accounts for the country-level indicators and an additional term accounting for the variance between countries which could not be accounted for by the covariates which will be referred to as the country random effects.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.
First, only the fixed effects were included as covariates in a standard generalized linear model using iterative least squares estimation.
188.Rationale: The study explicitly used mixed-effects models with country random effects (not a fixed-effects specification with country dummy variables). While fixed-effects with country dummies can control for time-invariant unobserved country confounding, the authors chose random-effects mixed models (M2 and M3) to model between-country variance and to allow prediction for new countries, so the fixed-effects country-dummy approach is not the methodological choice described.

188.Option: Use a mixed-effects model with separate random effects for individuals and countries to disentangle the contributions of individual-level and country-level factors
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.

The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect.

The second model (M2) accounts for the country-level indicators and an additional term accounting for the variance between countries which could not be accounted for by the covariates which will be referred to as the country random effects.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.
188.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes fitting binomial mixed-effects models (M1, M2, M3) that include separate random effects for both individuals and countries. That modelling choice directly enables disentangling contributions of measured fixed effects at both levels while accounting for unmeasured heterogeneity at the individual and country levels, matching this option.

188.Option: Include country-level indicators as fixed effects and individual-level variables as random effects to isolate the impact of country-specific factors on infection risk
Evidence: First, only the fixed effects were included as covariates in a standard generalized linear model using iterative least squares estimation.
The third model (M3) accounts for both individual-level variables and country-level indicators, along with an individual random effects term and a country random effects term.

The first model (M1) accounts for the individual-level variables and an additional term accounting for the variance between individuals which could not be accounted for by the covariates and will be referred to as the individual random effect.
188.Rationale: The study treated individual-level and country-level variables as fixed-effect covariates, and then included separate random-effect terms for individuals and countries to capture residual heterogeneity. The option's suggestion to treat individual-level variables themselves as random effects is a mischaracterization: individual-level variables were fixed covariates, not random effects, so this choice does not reflect the authors' approach.

188.Option: Use a hierarchical regression model with country-level indicators as predictors and individual-level variables as outcome measures to assess the influence of country context on individual infection status
Evidence: We compare three different Binomial mixed models, making use of either individual-level variables, country-level indicators or both sources of information.
The predictive power is assessed using a cross-validation technique outlined below.
The predictive power is assessed using a cross-validation technique outlined below.
Predictions for the test set were made for each prediction scenario, where for the second scenario, the name of the country the individual was from was first removed. These predictions were recorded.
188.Rationale: This option reverses the modelling direction: the study modelled individual infection status (a binomial outcome) as a function of individual-level covariates and country-level indicators in a hierarchical/mixed-effects framework. It did not treat individual-level variables as outcomes. Therefore this choice does not describe the methodological approach used to separate country- and individual-level influences on infection risk.

189.Option: Use a permutation test to randomly reassign variant labels to years and assess whether the observed shift in dominance is more extreme than expected by chance
Evidence: From 2001 to 2010 the two major phylogenetic groups (A1 and A2) occurred in similar abundances. However, significant shifts in abundances of the two groups occurred in the following years. In 2011, 80% confirmed patients were infected with A1 virus variant (and 20% with A3). On the contrary, in 2012 we detected the A2 virus variant alone (we sequenced 92% confirmed CCHF cases). In 2013, again both A1 and A2 variants were present (9% and 50% confirmed cases, respectively).

The passage shows the investigators used coalescent/Bayesian skyline approaches (BEAST) to infer evolutionary rates and demographic history, which are the appropriate tools to infer transmission dynamics.
