223.Rationale: While the quoted GPS study supports that pigs spend most of their time near their homes and contact human defecation areas (which supports localized transmission), the option misattributes the mechanism as ‘direct contact with tapeworm carriers’ rather than environmental contamination. The passage explicitly emphasizes exposure to human feces/open defecation areas and environmental contamination around households (e.g., interaction with defecation areas), not direct physical contact with infected people, as the plausible exposure pathway. Thus the inference in the option is incorrect even though the underlying behavioral fact is supported.

223.Option: The strong association between infection and proximity to tapeworm carriers, even after adjusting for pig age, indicates that environmental contamination is the only possible transmission route
Evidence: After adjusting for pig age, pigs living less than 50 meters from a human tapeworm carrier were 4.56 times (95% CI: 1.33, 15.6) more likely to be infected with at least one cyst than pigs living more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier.
Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: The data do show a strong adjusted association between proximity (<50 m) and infection, which supports localized exposure. However the option asserts that environmental contamination is the only possible route; that is not supported by the passage. The authors explicitly note many infected pigs lived far from identified carriers and discuss possible long-distance dispersion (vectors) and undetected carriers. Therefore the absolute claim 'only possible transmission route' is incorrect.

223.Option: A sharp decline in infection prevalence beyond 50 meters, coupled with pigs' high activity in this zone, indicates that transmission occurs primarily through localized environmental contamination near human dwellings
Evidence: Prevalence of T. solium cysticercosis decreased monotonically as distance from a human tapeworm carrier increased (15.6%, 8.3%, and 4.4% for pigs living < 50 meter, 50–500 meters, and > 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier, respectively).

The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend) (Fig 2).

A GPS tracking study of pigs in rural Peru found that pigs spent an average of 70% of their time within 50 meters of their residence and interacted with human defecation areas nearly 30 minutes per day inside these 50 meters rings (compared to just 7 minutes per day outside of 50 meters) [26].
223.Rationale: The combination of a marked drop in prevalence beyond the ~50 m zone and independent behavior data showing pigs spend most of their time and interact with defecation areas within that same 50 m supports localized environmental contamination (fecal contamination near dwellings) as the dominant transmission mechanism. These two lines of evidence together constitute the strongest pattern in the data favoring localized contamination over long-distance dispersal.

223.Option: The absence of infection in pigs located more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier suggests that T. solium eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond this distance in the environment
Evidence: The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend) (Fig 2).

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: The premise is false: there was not an absence of infection beyond 500 m (prevalence was 4.4%). Moreover, the authors explicitly discuss infected pigs far from identified carriers and possible long-distance dispersal by vectors or undetected carriers. Thus the data do not support the categorical conclusion that eggs cannot survive or remain infectious beyond 500 m.

223.Option: The lack of a significant association between infection and distance beyond 50 meters suggests that long-distance dispersal mechanisms are not involved in transmission
Evidence: Pigs living more than 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, including pigs living between 50 and 100 meters from a tapeworm carrier, did not have an increased odds of infection at any cyst burden analyzed.

Although distance to human tapeworm carriers was an important predictor for T. solium infection among pigs, many infected pigs in our study did not reside in close proximity to a tapeworm carrier. In fact, only 27% (12 out of 44) of the infected pigs in this study lived within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and some infected pigs lived more than 1 km from it’s the nearest identified tapeworm carrier.

It is also possible that pigs appearing distant from tapeworm carriers were infected through egg dispersion mechanisms, such as dung beetles and flies, which have been identified as possible mechanical vectors capable of dispersing T. solium eggs over long distances [27–31].
223.Rationale: The observed lack of association beyond 50 m indicates weaker localized effect at larger distances, but it does not rule out involvement of long-distance dispersal mechanisms. The authors note that a substantial fraction of infected pigs lived far from identified carriers and explicitly discuss possible long-distance vectors and undetected carriers. Therefore the absence of a strong association at >50 m does not demonstrate that long-distance dispersal is not involved.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is uniquely associated with increased effector memory CD4 T cells, a subset highly susceptible to HIV, in multivariable analysis, unlike other helminth species
Evidence: W. bancrofti infection was associated with significantly increased frequencies of effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells, in univariable (mean 24.1% versus 20.0%, p = 0.0096) as well as multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S5). This was not observed for other analyzed helminth species.

The association between increased frequencies of activated HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos and effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells in peripheral blood and W. bancrofti infection was independent of other host and environmental factors, suggesting a causal link between these two factors.
The frequency of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells strongly correlates with the frequency of effector memory CD4 T cells and both subsets are significantly increased W. bancrofti infected subjects.
Activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for continuous HIV replication in vivo.
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that W. bancrofti infection is associated with significantly increased effector memory CD4 T cells in both univariable and multivariable analyses and that this finding was not observed for other helminth species. Effector memory and activated CD4 T cells are described as important targets for HIV replication. Because the association persisted after adjustment for other helminths and confounders, this provides the strongest support that W. bancrofti has a unique contribution to HIV susceptibility beyond general helminth co-infection.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased eosinophil counts, which are known to correlate with systemic immune activation and may contribute to HIV susceptibility
Evidence: we found a median number of 130x103/μl eosinophils in helminth free individuals compared to 400x103/μl in W. bancrofti-infected individuals and 260x103/μl in participants harboring other helminths.

Eosinophil counts were available for 37 study participants without helminth infection, 87 with one helminth species, 46 with two species, and 7 with three or more helminth species. Increasing numbers of eosinophils were seen with polyparasitism.
Polyparasitism was not significantly associated with increased frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells (Fig 3B).
Polyparasitism was not significantly associated with increased frequencies of HLA-DRposCD38pos CD4 T cells (Fig 4B).
224.Rationale: While W. bancrofti-infected individuals had higher eosinophil counts, the study does not present evidence that eosinophil counts drive the systemic CD4 T cell activation linked to HIV susceptibility. Eosinophilia was also associated with polyparasitism and other helminth infections, and the authors show polyparasitism did not associate with increased HLA-DR+ or HLA-DR+CD38+ CD4 T cells. Thus increased eosinophils do not uniquely support a W. bancrofti-specific effect on HIV susceptibility.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased expression of CCR5 on CD4 T cells, which directly facilitates HIV entry and explains the increased susceptibility
Evidence: Addressing the expression of HIV entry receptor CCR5, no difference was found regarding the percentage of CCR5 positive CD4 T cells, CCR5 positive regulatory CD4 T cells, or the mean fluorescence intensity of CCR5 on memory CD4 T cells between the different helminth infections (Tables 3, S6, S7 and S8).

We have previously shown in the same cohort that both CD27 negative effector memory CD4 T cells and HLA-DR+ memory CD4 T cells express significantly higher levels of the HIV co-receptor CCR5 compared to CD27+ central memory or naive CD4 T cells [21], which may facilitate better HIV entry into these specific cell subsets.
224.Rationale: The passage explicitly states there was no difference in CCR5 expression on CD4 T cells between the different helminth infections, so increased CCR5 expression cannot be cited as a unique mechanism by which W. bancrofti increases HIV susceptibility in this study. The authors instead point to increased activated and effector memory CD4 T cells (which as cell types overall express more CCR5) as the plausible link.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection leads to a higher overall burden of helminth co-infection, which may drive increased immune activation and thus HIV susceptibility
Evidence: Almost all W. bancrofti-infected subjects (32 of 33) were also infected with other helminths (Table 1) impeding an evaluation of only monoinfected individuals. A mean of 2.3 helminths species were detected in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.

The composition of the “additional” species in W. bancrofti-infected individuals was almost identical to that in the group harboring other helminth species (Table 1). Therefore we compared the effect of W. bancrofti on T cell activation not only with the group of helminth free individuals, but in addition with the “background” infection of the combined helminth group to distinguish the effect of W. bancrofti from an overall helminth influence on immune activation.
Polyparasitism was not significantly associated with increased frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells (Fig 3B).
224.Rationale: Although W. bancrofti-infected individuals commonly had multiple helminth species, the authors took care to compare W. bancrofti cases to a background group with similar co-infection composition and found that the increased T cell activation was specific to W. bancrofti. They also report polyparasitism did not associate with increased HLA-DR+ CD4 T cells. Thus a higher overall helminth burden does not explain the unique association attributed to W. bancrofti.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is associated with increased HLA-DRpos CD8 T cells, which are also targets for HIV replication, suggesting a similar mechanism to CD4 T cell activation
Evidence: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

In addition we measured increased percentage of HLA-DRpos/CD38pos CD8 T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, however, there is no evidence that CD8 T cell may contribute to increased HIV susceptibility.
224.Rationale: Although unadjusted data showed higher HLA-DR+ CD8 T cell frequencies in W. bancrofti-infected individuals, this difference did not remain significant in multivariable models. Moreover, the authors explicitly state there is no evidence that CD8 T cells contribute to increased HIV susceptibility. Therefore increased CD8 activation does not support a unique W. bancrofti-driven mechanism for HIV susceptibility.

225.Option: A rapid decline in platelet count observed between day 1 and day 3 of illness, as it reflects early immune-mediated thrombocytopenia and predicts progression to DHF
Evidence: It was revealed that DHF cases had higher median hematocrit during the later days of illness and the median hematocrit in DHF was significantly higher than DF and UF during day 4–6 of illness.

All spectra of dengue also had lower median peripheral platelet count during the later days of illness, although the median value in DF and UF did not meet the WHO's criterion for thrombocytopenia, i.e. lower than 100,000/mm3.

Moreover, the level of platelet count showed reverse correlation with the disease severity, i.e. highest in UF and lowest in DHF. The median platelet count in DHF was significantly lower than the other 2 groups during day 3–6 of illness, while DF had significantly lower median platelet count compared to UF only during day 3–5 of illness.
Complete blood count were done in 43, 11, 8, 2, 2, 2 UF and in 113, 69, 32, 20, 7, 4 DF, and 32, 19, 12, 8, 2, 2 DHF cases on day 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 of illness, respectively.
Complete blood counts were done selectively by clinicians based on clinical need and may not be accurately extrapolated to all patients with UF and DF.
225.Rationale: The passage documents that lower platelet counts are associated with DHF, particularly during days 3–6, but does not present evidence that a rapid decline specifically between day 1 and day 3 is a validated early predictor. Platelet declines are described as occurring in the later days of illness, and counts were measured selectively, limiting generalizability. Therefore the specific assertion of a day 1–3 rapid decline as the strongest early warning is not supported by the study text.

225.Option: Persistent or increasing gastrointestinal symptoms (anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain) beyond day 2 of illness
Evidence: It was found that nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, rash, diarrhea and petechiae were statistically more common in DHF compared to DF and UF.

The prevalence of most of the symptoms was highest during the first 2 days of illness and then slowly declined. The exceptions are for anorexia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain and diarrhea that tended to increase in prevalence in DHF during day 3–5 of illness.

It was also revealed that most symptoms were more common in DHF and, specifically, DHF cases had significantly higher prevalence of anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain during day 3–6 and diarrhea during day 4–6 of illness (Chi-square test; p<0.05).

This study describes the prevalence of each clinical symptom day by day starting from the onset of illness... It was found that some clinical symptoms, for example, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain are significantly more common in DHF compared with non-DHF.
The absence of these clinical manifestations suggests that the patient should have non-DHF while the presence of them should alert the physician to be watchful for DHF.
225.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that anorexia, nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain are more common in DHF and that these gastrointestinal symptoms tend to increase in prevalence during days 3–5 in DHF. It further states that the presence of these manifestations should "alert the physician to be watchful for DHF." Thus persistent or increasing GI symptoms beyond day 2 are supported as an early clinical cue warranting heightened vigilance for progression to severe dengue.

225.Option: A positive tourniquet test observed on day 1 of illness, as it indicates early plasma leakage and is a reliable predictor of severe dengue
Evidence: Tourniquet test was done by inflating a blood pressure cuff on the patient's upper arm to the point midway between the systolic and diastolic blood pressure for 5 minutes.
Positive tourniquet test was found in 72% and flushed face was found in approximately half of the dengue infected patients.

some clinical manifestations (e.g. rash, positive tourniquet test) were considered as more specific to DF/DHF and are included in clinical diagnostic criteria for DF. This can explain why these clinical manifestations were more common in DF/DHF.

Table 6 shows the occurrence of selected clinical manifestations and their predictive value for DHF among symptomatic dengue infected cases. It was found that all of these clinical manifestations had low positive predictive value but high negative predictive value for DHF.
225.Rationale: Although a positive tourniquet test was common and is part of diagnostic criteria, the study notes that such clinical manifestations have low positive predictive value for DHF. The passage does not claim that a tourniquet test on day 1 reliably predicts progression to severe disease; rather, its presence is more useful for ruling out DHF when absent. Therefore the option's claim that it is a reliable early predictor is not supported by the text.

225.Option: The occurrence of drowsiness or lethargy during day 2–5 of illness, as it is a common symptom in both mild and severe dengue and may indicate early neurological involvement
Evidence: Conversely, drowsiness/lethargy was significantly more common in UF during day 2–5 of illness.
We do not have an explanation for this finding. Further studies are needed to clarify this issue.

we found that the presence of lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days of illness may not necessarily indicate severe dengue infection. The significance of drowsiness/lethargy needs to be further defined.
225.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that drowsiness/lethargy was significantly more common in undifferentiated fever (UF, the mild spectrum) during days 2–5 and cautions that lethargy in the first few days does not necessarily indicate severe dengue. Thus this symptom is not supported as a strong early indicator of progression to severe disease.

225.Option: The presence of rhinorrhea during the first 2 days of illness, as it may indicate a co-infection with a respiratory virus that could worsen dengue outcomes
Evidence: It is interesting that rhinorrhea was commonly found in this study. Whether this symptom is one manifestation of dengue infection or is caused by co-incidental respiratory infection is still unclear.
However, it has been suggested that dengue infection should be included in the differential diagnosis of acute infection of the upper respiratory tract [14].
225.Rationale: The passage notes rhinorrhea was common but specifically states it is unclear whether it is due to dengue itself or a co-incident respiratory infection. The study provides no evidence that rhinorrhea predicts worse outcomes or progression to DHF. Therefore the option's suggestion that rhinorrhea indicates co-infection that could worsen dengue outcomes is not supported by the text.

226.Option: Pigs serve as the primary reservoir host for T. penetrans in this setting, given their high infection prevalence and intensity, strong correlation with human cases, and significant bidirectional association in multivariate analysis
Evidence: Pigs were identified as the most important domestic animal hosts for T. penetrans.
Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
In multivariate analysis, presence of animal tungiasis in households was strongly influenced by presence of human tungiasis (OR = 6.5, p < 0.0001) and presence of pigs (OR = 5.9, p = 0.0002).
The odds of households to have pigs infected with T. penetrans were significantly increased if there was also human tungiasis as illustrated in Fig 7B (OR = 2.1, p = 0.001).
226.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies pigs as the major domestic host and documents that pigs have the highest parasite loads. It also provides statistical evidence of a strong correlation between pig infection measures and human tungiasis at household and village levels and shows multivariate models in which presence of pigs and human tungiasis are strongly associated (in both directions for household-level models and for pig-specific models). These statements directly support the inference that pigs serve as the primary reservoir host in this setting.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection and the presence of other ectoparasites, suggesting shared environmental risk factors
Evidence: The reasons for the association between T. penetrans and other pig ectoparasites such as lice in this study are not known as the ecological interactions between these parasites have not been studied yet.
226.Rationale: The passage notes an observed association between T. penetrans and other pig ectoparasites but explicitly states the reasons are unknown. It does not claim pigs are the only species with such associations, nor does it present this association as the primary evidence that pigs are reservoirs. The principal evidence for pigs as reservoirs in the paper is their high prevalence/intensity and strong correlation with human cases, not a unique link to other ectoparasites. Therefore the specific causal claim in this option is not supported.

226.Option: Pigs are the most important reservoir host for T. penetrans because they have the highest number of lesions per infected animal and are the only species with viable lesions detected in the study
Evidence: Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig);
Of the 3357 lesions in pigs, 2243 (66.8%) were viable (Fortaleza stage IIa-IIIb) and 1114 (33.3%) were non-viable (Fortaleza stage IV).
The 20 infected dogs had a total of 53 lesions of which 32 (60.4%) were viable while 21 (39.6%) were non-viable.
Of the total 40 lesions found on the two goat kids, 27 (67.5%) were viable while the other 13 (32.5%) were non-viable.
226.Rationale: While it is true pigs had the highest lesion counts, the statement that pigs were the only species with viable lesions is false: both dogs and the infected goat kids had viable lesions. The key reason pigs are inferred as the most important reservoir in the study is their higher prevalence and intensity and strong correlations with human infections, not exclusivity of viable lesions.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species with a significant association between infection prevalence and household size, suggesting greater exposure in larger households
Evidence: The strong association of animal infections with large household sizes is probably also indirect and may be due to the fact that such households have many children below 15 years who constitute the most vulnerable group of humans to T. penetrans.

In multivariate analysis, ... the number of animal species reared in the household (OR = 1.6, p = 0.02) and the size of the homestead (OR = 1.4, p = 0.02) significantly increased the odds detecting animal tungiasis among households.
226.Rationale: The passage reports an association between larger household size and animal tungiasis overall, but frames this as likely indirect and related to more children and other factors. It does not state that pigs are the only species with a household-size association, nor present household size as the primary evidence that pigs are reservoirs. The study’s direct evidence for pigs as reservoirs centers on pig prevalence/intensity and pig–human correlations, not a unique link to household size.

226.Option: Pigs are the primary reservoir host because they are the only species whose infection intensity correlates with the number of infected humans in the household, indicating a direct transmission pathway
Evidence: At household level, an increase in human tungiasis intensities corresponded with an increase in the odds of occurrence of animal infections (OR = 1.8 CI 1.4–2.2, p<0.0001) and vice versa (OR = 1.3 CI 1.1–1.4, p < 0.0001).
The median number of lesion in children 3–8 years old on one randomly selected foot was plotted against the median number of lesions in animals from the same household (rho = 0.47, p<0.001).
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).
At household level, the prevalence of tungiasis in the mostly affected animal species strongly correlated with human prevalence (dogs rho = 0.34, p = 0.0002; pigs rho = 0.5, p<0.001).
226.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that pig infection intensity correlates with human tungiasis, supporting pigs’ epidemiological importance. However, the claim that pigs are the only species with such a correlation is not supported: the text shows correlations for animal species generally and gives a non-zero correlation for dogs as well. The authors also caution that cross-sectional data demonstrate association but do not prove direct transmission pathways. Thus the exclusivity and direct transmission implication in this option are not supported by the study.

227.Option: Use a time-series analysis to assess changes in tsetse presence over the course of the survey period to account for temporal variation in environmental conditions
Evidence: Trapping at each site lasted 72 hours and was conducted by teams led by district entomologists. Single collection was made at the end of this 72 hour period.

A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression [37, 38].
227.Rationale: The critical issue highlighted in the passage is spatial autocorrelation of residuals, not temporal variation over the short single-collection survey window. The entomological survey used a single 72-hour trap collection per site, so a time-series analysis is not feasible or indicated by the data. The authors explicitly constructed a residual variogram and applied autologistic regression to address spatial dependence, indicating that spatial autocorrelation — not temporal series modeling — is the factor that must be addressed to avoid confounding.

227.Option: Match cases and controls on geographic proximity to reduce spatial clustering and ensure balanced comparison between tsetse presence and absence groups
Evidence: Tsetse survey count data were transformed to a binary variable representing tsetse fly presence or absence (0, 1). Presence of tsetse flies was represented by a “1” while absence was represented by “0”.

A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression [37, 38].
227.Rationale: The study used presence/absence data across many geo-referenced trap sites and addressed spatial clustering by incorporating spatial dependence in the model (autologistic regression with an autocovariate). The passage does not describe a case-control matching design or matching on proximity; instead it treats spatial autocorrelation directly in the regression model. Matching on geographic proximity is not presented as the solution and would not be the standard approach here to estimate environmental covariate effects while accounting for spatial dependence across many sampling locations.

227.Option: Apply a non-parametric regression model such as random forest to reduce sensitivity to violations of model assumptions and better capture complex environmental interactions
Evidence: Autologistic regression was applied based on the seven significant variables obtained from the multivariate logistic model together with the computed autocovariate.

Other methodological approaches can be used to deal with spatially autocorrelated data, such as model-based geostatistics [15], although the fitting of these models and subsequent spatial predictions are very demanding computationally.
227.Rationale: While machine learning methods like random forests can capture complex interactions, the passage identifies spatial autocorrelation in residuals as the specific problem that can bias parameter estimates and inflate significance. The authors address this by using autologistic regression that explicitly incorporates an autocovariate (or by using model-based geostatistics). The passage does not propose random forest as the remedy for spatial dependence; without explicit spatial structure, non-spatial random forest models would not by themselves remove confounding due to spatial autocorrelation.

227.Option: Account for spatial dependence in the data using a model that incorporates an autocovariate to adjust for neighboring observations
Evidence: A residual variogram was constructed to assess the presence of spatial autocorrelation in the model residuals. Autologistic regression was applied to account for the residual spatial autocorrelation [30, 31, 32, 33, 34, 35].
Autologistic regression involves the introduction of a new explanatory variable (autocovariate).
Since non-spatial models fail to account for the autocorrelation effect, there was a need to apply a spatial model: in this case, autologistic regression [37, 38].

The autocovariate term captured part of the spatial pattern in the data observations, thus, providing a more robust estimation of the covariate effects after accounting for the spatial dependence in the observations.
227.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies residual spatial autocorrelation as a problem that can bias parameter estimates and states that autologistic regression — which adds an autocovariate summarizing neighbouring observations — was applied to account for spatial dependence. The authors report that the autologistic model reduced spatial autocorrelation in residuals and yielded more reliable estimates of environmental covariate effects. Therefore, directly modelling spatial dependence with an autocovariate is the correct critical factor to address.

227.Option: Include additional environmental covariates such as land cover and vegetation indices to improve model fit and reduce residual spatial autocorrelation
Evidence: Several covariates (Table 1) were used in the analysis, based on an understanding of the factors important for tsetse reproduction and survival [11, 27, 28]. These included; (i) land cover, (ii) temperature, (iii) normalised difference vegetation index (NDVI), (iv) elevation, and (v) rainfall.
After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the covariates temperature, rainfall, and cropland lost their statistical significance in influencing tsetse presence or absence.
The autologistic regression model reduced the spatial autocorrelation in the residuals compared to the multivariate logistic model.
227.Rationale: Although including relevant environmental covariates (land cover, NDVI, etc.) is necessary to explain tsetse distribution and improve model fit, the passage shows that adding covariates alone did not eliminate residual spatial autocorrelation and that some covariates lost significance after accounting for spatial dependence. The authors therefore applied an autologistic model to explicitly handle spatial autocorrelation. Thus, simply adding more environmental predictors is not the critical action required to prevent confounding by spatial dependence; explicitly accounting for spatial autocorrelation in the model is.

228.Option: Defining communities based on OSM polygons without a buffer may underestimate transmission risk in areas with high water contact density near community edges, leading to an overestimation of the association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: The spatial boundaries of communities were defined by Open Street Map (OSM) polygons (Fig 2) abstracted using QGIS software (version 2.12.3), an approach validated in a case study [29]. Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon.
The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.
Most models do not account for this spatial mismatch between exposure and outcome measures [11].
228.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the investigators applied a 1 km buffer because nearly all known water contact occurred within 1 km of community boundaries; therefore omitting the buffer would be expected to miss relevant exposure and likely underestimate transmission risk. However, the option further claims that this underestimation would lead to an overestimation of the association between environmental predictors and prevalence. That causal direction is not supported by the passage. Missing relevant exposure data (no buffer) more plausibly weakens or biases associations (usually toward the null or unpredictable bias), not systematically overestimates them. The cited text supports the part about underestimating transmission if no buffer were used, but does not support the asserted consequence of overestimating the predictor–prevalence association.

228.Option: The 1 km buffer may overestimate exposure in communities with low water contact, leading to an inflated association between environmental predictors and prevalence due to non-differential misclassification
Evidence: The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.
Most models do not account for this spatial mismatch between exposure and outcome measures [11].
228.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that the choice of buffer could affect exposure classification and recommends exploring alternative community definitions, it also states the buffer was chosen based on empirical knowledge that nearly all contact occurred within 1 km. The option asserts the buffer would 'overestimate exposure' in low-contact communities and that such non-differential misclassification would inflate the association. Non-differential exposure misclassification typically biases effect estimates toward the null rather than inflating associations. The passage does not provide evidence that the 1 km buffer systematically overestimates exposure in low-contact communities or that misclassification would inflate associations, so the claimed direction of bias is unsupported.

228.Option: Using a 1 km buffer around community boundaries may misclassify exposure if actual water contact sites extend beyond this distance, potentially weakening the observed association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: Subsequently, a buffer radius of 1 km was applied to each polygon. The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.
Most models do not account for this spatial mismatch between exposure and outcome measures [11].
228.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that the buffer was chosen based on local knowledge but also recommends testing other buffer radii, implying the potential for misclassification if real contact patterns differ from the assumed 1 km. If important water contact sites lie beyond the 1 km buffer, those exposures would be missed (misclassified), which would dilute the measured relationship between environmental predictors (extracted within the buffer) and observed prevalence — i.e., weaken the observed association. The quoted recommendations to explore alternative community definitions and the explicit choice rationale support this concern.

228.Option: Aggregating environmental variables within a 1 km buffer may reduce spatial resolution and obscure fine-scale transmission patterns, leading to a spurious association between environmental predictors and prevalence due to ecological fallacy
Evidence: In epidemiologic literature, schistosomiasis is known as a focal disease, meaning that neighboring villages with seemingly similar conditions can have drastically different transmission profiles and disease prevalence levels [10,18,19].
This study attempted to characterize some of the sources of spatial heterogeneity at small spatial extents using fine resolution RS data and WASH-related risk factors.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.
For aggregation of environmental variables, primarily the median pixel values were used, except for stream order, where maximum value was used.
228.Rationale: The passage emphasizes that schistosomiasis transmission is highly focal and that fine-scale heterogeneity matters. Aggregating raster predictors (median within a 1 km buffer) inherently reduces spatial detail and can obscure micro-scale transmission hotspots (e.g., particular contact points along a stream). Such aggregation creates the risk of ecological inference problems: community-level summaries may not reflect individual-level exposures, producing spurious or misleading associations. The authors explicitly recommend exploring alternative definitions and aggregation approaches, supporting the concern that aggregation within a fixed buffer can obscure fine-scale patterns and induce ecological fallacy.

228.Option: The use of a fixed 1 km buffer may introduce misclassification if the buffer size is too small in areas with high population density and extensive water use, potentially leading to an underestimation of the true association between environmental predictors and prevalence
Evidence: The buffer distance was chosen because nearly all known contact with water bodies occurred within 1 km of community boundaries.

Exploring different methods of defining “communities” over which risk factor variables are aggregated (e.g., varying the buffer radius within which transmission occurs) in other geographic, demographic, and cultural contexts is also recommended.

Because snail populations, cercarial densities, human water contact patterns, and subsequent schistosomiasis infections exhibit strong spatial heterogeneity [10,18,19], further investigation of localized transmission drivers at smaller spatial extents is needed [10,11].
228.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that buffer choice affects exposure classification and recommends testing alternative radii. If, in some settings (for example where people travel farther for water or where high population density concentrates use at more distant sites), the 1 km buffer is too small, relevant water contact exposures could be missed. Missing such exposures would be a form of exposure misclassification that would tend to attenuate (underestimate) the measured association between environmental predictors (as aggregated within the buffer) and observed prevalence. The quoted passages support both the sensitivity of results to buffer definition and the recommendation to vary buffer sizes.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with living in an urban neighborhood, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent infestation in densely populated areas with poor infrastructure
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03)

Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01)
A recent investigation of rodent Leptospira spp. carriage in USVI identified leptospires in the kidneys of 45.7% of 140 rodents tested from the three main islands
229.Rationale: While the passage provides evidence that both urban residence (bivariate pRR = 3.0) and rodent exposure (bivariate pRR = 2.6; rodent carriage 45.7%) are associated with seropositivity, this option emphasizes rodents + urban environment but does not involve contaminated water sources. The question asks specifically for evidence supporting a zoonotic transmission pathway involving domestic animals and contaminated water sources. Rodents + urban neighborhood supports a rodent-driven pathway in dense areas, but it does not address contaminated water or domestic animal (e.g., cattle) contribution, so it does not best match the hypothesized pathway described.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with contact with cistern water that lacks protective covers, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent urine contaminating rainwater collected in uncovered cisterns
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), ... contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002)
Covering cisterns and other rainwater collection containers was significantly protective against seropositivity on bivariate analysis
A cover over these rainwater collection containers would help to prevent animals, animal excrement, and debris from entering and potentially contaminating the water

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR
229.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence linking rodent exposure to human seropositivity and separately shows that uncovered cisterns are a plausible route of water contamination (covering cisterns was protective; Leptospira spp. detected in cistern water). Combining rodent exposure with uncovered cisterns forms a biologically plausible zoonotic pathway in which rodent urine contaminates rainwater collection containers, exposing people through contact with that contaminated water. This combination directly ties an animal reservoir (rodents) to contaminated water as a transmission route, and is supported by multiple explicit statements in the text.

229.Option: Exposure to rodents or their body fluids combined with contact with untreated cistern water, supporting a transmission pathway via rodent-contaminated rainwater collected in uncovered containers
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included ... seeing rodents or evidence of rodents in or around the house or land (pRR: 2.6; 95% CI: 1.2–6.0; p = 0.01), ... contact with rodents or their body fluids (pRR: 8.0; 95% CI: 1.9–34.1, p = 0.002)
Even though cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants, many indicated contact with untreated cistern water, which could have been used for bathing, household chores, or farming.

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR
229.Rationale: This option pairs rodent exposure (a documented risk factor with supporting data) with contact with untreated cistern water (also explicitly noted in the passage). The text confirms that people had contact with untreated cistern water and that Leptospira spp. have been detected in cistern water, making rodent-contaminated cistern water a plausible transmission pathway. Because 'contact with untreated cistern water' is explicitly stated (even if not primarily for drinking), this combination is well supported by the passage.

229.Option: Contact with cows or their body fluids combined with drinking untreated cistern water, supporting a transmission pathway via ingestion of water contaminated by cattle urine
Evidence: Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001)
recent leptospirosis testing of cows at an abattoir in St. Croix demonstrated 25% seropositivity among cows tested, and one cow that was shedding leptospires in the urine [43]
Even though cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants, many indicated contact with untreated cistern water, which could have been used for bathing, household chores, or farming.

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR
229.Rationale: Contact with cows is very strongly associated with seropositivity and cows have been shown to be seropositive and a shedder in USVI; cistern water has been found to contain Leptospira spp. However, the passage explicitly notes that cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants—many had contact with untreated cistern water for bathing/chores rather than drinking. Because the specific behavior 'drinking untreated cistern water' is not supported as common in the surveyed population, the ingestion route via drinking cistern water is less well supported by the data presented. Thus, although cow contact + cistern contamination is plausible, the specific assertion about 'drinking' untreated cistern water is not strongly supported by the survey findings.

229.Option: Exposure to cows or their body fluids combined with contact with cistern water that lacks protective covers, supporting a transmission pathway via contaminated rainwater collected from animal-exposed environments
Evidence: Characteristics and exposure factors independently associated with seropositivity by multivariate logistic regression included contact with cows or their body fluids (OR = 39.5, 95% CI = 9.0–172.7, p = <0.001)
recent leptospirosis testing of cows at an abattoir in St. Croix demonstrated 25% seropositivity among cows tested, and one cow that was shedding leptospires in the urine [43]
Covering cisterns and other rainwater collection containers was significantly protective against seropositivity on bivariate analysis
A cover over these rainwater collection containers would help to prevent animals, animal excrement, and debris from entering and potentially contaminating the water

Leptospira spp. were previously identified in cistern water associated with a post-hurricane case of leptospirosis in USVI [1], and a recent study of cistern water in USVI identified Leptospira spp. in 3/47 (6.4%) cisterns tested by PCR
Even though cisterns were often not used for drinking water by participants, many indicated contact with untreated cistern water, which could have been used for bathing, household chores, or farming.
229.Rationale: This option links the strongest animal risk factor identified in multivariate analysis—contact with cows (OR = 39.5) and evidence of cattle shedding—with direct evidence that uncovered cisterns are vulnerable to contamination and that Leptospira spp. have been detected in cistern water. The passage explicitly states that covering cisterns was protective and explains that covers prevent animals and animal excrement from entering and contaminating collected rainwater. Together these facts most directly support a zoonotic transmission pathway in which domestic animals (cows) contaminate rainwater/cisterns, exposing people via contact with that contaminated water. This combination is therefore well supported by the data and text and best matches the hypothesis as phrased.

230.Option: Weekly reporting by community-based lay reporters allows for the rapid identification of individual cases, enabling immediate clinical intervention
Evidence: The survey, which was applied once weekly, included information on coded household location, number of NS children/household, seizure frequency, availability of anti-seizure medication, and injuries and deaths.
The study was not designed or approved to conduct or implement a medical intervention.

While each and every report requires medical confirmation, this is an extraordinary improvement in a region where major disease outbreaks have been recognized very slowly, monitoring is unavailable, and the distribution and utilization of vaccines and drugs are unclear.
230.Rationale: Although weekly reports from lay reporters do permit relatively rapid identification of suspected cases (supported by the quote that the survey was applied once weekly), the passage explicitly states the study was not designed or approved to implement medical interventions. It also emphasizes that reports require medical confirmation. Therefore the claim that weekly reporting by lay reporters enables "immediate clinical intervention" overstates what the method directly enables in this context and is inconsistent with the study's stated limits.

230.Option: Weekly, time-stamped, geolocated data collection by community-based reporters enables the construction of an epidemic curve to detect sudden increases in health events
Evidence: customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].

The survey, which was applied once weekly, included information on coded household location, number of NS children/household, seizure frequency, availability of anti-seizure medication, and injuries and deaths.
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.
230.Rationale: Time-stamped, geolocated weekly data provide the temporal resolution and location information needed to tabulate counts over successive time intervals and thus construct epidemic curves or time series. The passage explicitly notes Magpi stores GPS coordinates and time stamps and that the interface can visualize data over defined periods to assess fluctuations and perform temporal interpretations, which directly supports using those data to detect sudden increases in health events.

230.Option: High data completeness (97.3% weekly) ensures that the surveillance system can reliably detect rare events, such as the emergence of a new infectious disease
Evidence: Overall, a total of 240 households (30 households per mHealth reporter) and an average of 326 children with NS (40.7 ± 4.57 children per mHealth reporter) were monitored every week by the mHealth team.

With the exception of one reporter (targeting Ludok and Olam villages) who abandoned the program on week 6 due to health-related issues, the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).

Most importantly, because syndromic surveillance is designed to detect population patterns of disease and cannot be used to establish individual diagnoses, it is important to assess whether the healthcare workforce may have the capacity to absorb additional mobile-based responsibilities (e.g., data management and processing, clinical examination of suspect cases, deployment of clinical/educational/research interventions, etc.) before any attempt is made to scale-up.
230.Rationale: High completeness (97.3% weekly) improves the sensitivity and reliability of surveillance, and the passage documents that high completion. However, the option's wording that high completeness "ensures" reliable detection of rare emerging events is too strong. The passage also notes that syndromic surveillance detects population patterns and cannot establish individual diagnoses; detection of rare events depends on sampling design, population coverage, and diagnostic follow-up, not completeness alone. Thus the statement overclaims what completeness by itself guarantees.

230.Option: The use of GPS-stamped data enables the system to track disease progression in individual patients over time, supporting early outbreak detection
Evidence: customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].

All data acquired and integrated into the Magpi database could be exported, with no particular conversion problems, from the platform into statistical (Excel) or spatial (Magpi maps) analysis software.

However, after comparing our maps (Fig 3) and the data collected (Table 2), it became readily apparent that many geotracers malfunctioned or, most likely, many remote areas had weak or no coverage of cellular communication, causing limitations in acquiring GPS stamps and compromising the ability to produce maps using GPS coordinates.

Most importantly, because syndromic surveillance is designed to detect population patterns of disease and cannot be used to establish individual diagnoses, it is important to assess whether the healthcare workforce may have the capacity to absorb additional mobile-based responsibilities...
230.Rationale: GPS-stamped data are valuable for mapping and locating cases and can help detect spatial clusters, but the passage indicates limitations in reliably acquiring GPS stamps (malfunctioning geotracers and weak coverage). Moreover, tracking individual disease progression over time requires repeated, linked clinical data per person; the study focused on household-level, syndromic reporting and cautions that syndromic surveillance cannot establish individual diagnoses. Therefore the claim that GPS-stamped data enable tracking disease progression in individual patients over time (and thereby support early outbreak detection) is not directly supported and overstates the capability described.

230.Option: Real-time aggregation and mapping of health data allow for immediate detection of spatial clusters of illness, even without clinical confirmation
Evidence: their weekly reports, transmitted by simple smartphones, were instantaneously aggregated, analyzed and mapped by data collection centers locally (Gulu, Uganda) and remotely (Oregon, USA).
customized a data collection platform named Magpi that uses mobile phones and real-time cloud-based storage with global positioning system (GPS) coordinates and time stamping [15].

this approach has numerous advantages over the current paper-based documentation system, the most important of which is the accurate monitoring of events at the household level (GPS coordinates) that allows for more targeted disease control by location. Displayed on a map, the data collected provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots, drug availability, injuries, deaths, and missed and new/newly reported cases of NS with no more than 1–7 days of delay.

The surveillance system described herein relied on weekly inputs of neurological (NS) health data via basic smartphones operated by minimally trained lay mHealth reporters dispersed widely and resident within the communities under scrutiny.
230.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes instant aggregation, analysis and mapping of time- and location-stamped reports and states that displayed maps provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots and new or missing cases with only 1–7 days delay. These features directly enable detection of spatial clusters (potential early outbreak signals) even before clinical confirmation, which is the core of syndromic real-time surveillance described.

231.Option: Increase the frequency of leprosy elimination campaigns in all counties to detect asymptomatic cases and interrupt transmission
Evidence: The most common detection mode was outpatient clinic findings (2,983, 27.0%), followed by self-reported illness (2,279, 20.6%), clue investigation (1,963, 17.8%), reports by others (1,807, 16.4%), contact examination (1,272, 11.5%), focused surveys (in villages with leprosy cases) (343, 3.1%), other methods (157, 1.4%), leprosy elimination campaigns (LECs) (135, 1.2%), and group examinations (113, 1.0%) (Table 2 and Fig 2).

contact tracing may not substantially influence the trend in new case detection [20,21] because leprosy patients are identifiable only when they have signs and symptoms of the disease, meaning that transmission might already have taken place during the asymptomatic stage [20].
Understanding which geographical areas require intervention is fundamental for cost-effective disease control, and spatial analysis can indicate priority areas for local control programs [14,15].
231.Rationale: Leprosy elimination campaigns (LECs) accounted for only a very small fraction of detections (1.2%), so simply increasing frequency of broad campaigns across all counties is unlikely to reliably detect many additional (especially asymptomatic) infections. The passage notes that patients are identifiable only when symptomatic and that contact tracing/campaigns may not substantially influence trends because transmission can occur during an asymptomatic stage, and it emphasizes targeted spatial analysis for identifying priority areas for interventions. Thus broad increases in campaign frequency are unlikely to be the most effective approach for identifying persistent geographic transmission.

231.Option: Implement a province-wide screening program targeting individuals in households with confirmed leprosy cases to detect early-stage disease
Evidence: In total, 7,533 (68.2%) had a history of close contact with a leprosy patient, 3,350 (30.3%) had contact with a leprosy patient within the family, and 4,183 (37.9%) had contact with a leprosy patient outside the family.
In terms of the mode of detection, only 12% of new cases were detected by contact examination.

it has been suggested that contact surveys should not focus only on household contacts but should be extended to entire neighborhoods or villages to target a greater spectrum of social contact networks [15].
231.Rationale: Household-targeted screening would miss a substantial portion of contacts and potential transmission because a large share of contacts are outside the family and the authors explicitly recommend extending contact surveys beyond households to neighborhoods or villages. The passage also notes that only 12% of new cases were detected by contact examination, indicating household-focused screening alone is unlikely to be sufficient to identify geographic areas with ongoing transmission.

231.Option: Compare the new case detection rate across counties over time to identify those with increasing incidence despite overall decline
Evidence: The new case detection rate (NCDR) was defined as the number of newly detected cases per year per 100,000 of the general population.
A total of 11,052 newly detected cases of leprosy were reported in Yunnan from 1990 to 2019, with the NCDR per 100,000 ranging from 1.93 to 0.38.
Historically, newly detected leprosy patients have been reported in all 129 counties of the 16 prefecture-level divisions of Yunnan Province.
Currently, 17/117 (14.5%) of the counties remain leprosy-endemic regions.
231.Rationale: Comparing NCDR across counties over time uses the surveillance metric the study already defines and reports (NCDR per 100,000) and would directly reveal counties where incidence is stable or increasing despite provincial declines. The passage presents county-level endemic status and NCDR trends, supporting this analytic approach as a straightforward way to identify geographic areas with ongoing transmission.

231.Option: Perform contact tracing for all newly detected cases and map the geographic distribution of contacts to identify clusters of transmission
Evidence: In total, 7,533 (68.2%) had a history of close contact with a leprosy patient, 3,350 (30.3%) had contact with a leprosy patient within the family, and 4,183 (37.9%) had contact with a leprosy patient outside the family.
In terms of the mode of detection, only 12% of new cases were detected by contact examination.

contact tracing may not substantially influence the trend in new case detection [20,21] because leprosy patients are identifiable only when they have signs and symptoms of the disease, meaning that transmission might already have taken place during the asymptomatic stage [20].

it has been suggested that contact surveys should not focus only on household contacts but should be extended to entire neighborhoods or villages to target a greater spectrum of social contact networks [15].
231.Rationale: While contact tracing and mapping can help find clusters, the passage indicates important limitations: only 12% of new cases were detected via contact examination and the authors caution that contact tracing may not substantially change case detection trends because of asymptomatic transmission. They also recommend extending surveys beyond household contacts. Therefore performing contact tracing for cases and mapping contacts alone is unlikely to be the most effective standalone method to identify geographic areas with ongoing transmission compared with spatial analyses that integrate broader surveillance data.

231.Option: Conduct spatial analysis integrating case distribution with socioeconomic risk factors to identify persistent transmission hotspots
Evidence: Understanding which geographical areas require intervention is fundamental for cost-effective disease control, and spatial analysis can indicate priority areas for local control programs [14,15].

Maps showing the geographical distribution of newly detected leprosy patients were generated using the inverse distance weighted interpolation method (IDW) in ArcGIS software version 10.1 (Environmental Systems Research Institute, Inc., Redlands, CA, USA).

Overcrowding, lower socioeconomic indicators, a lack of access to health services and precarious sanitation are underlying characteristics that impact the incidence of leprosy, indicating that poverty increases the likelihood of transmission of M. leprae [15].
231.Rationale: The passage explicitly recommends spatial analysis to indicate priority areas for local control programs and describes generating maps of case distribution. It also links socioeconomic factors (overcrowding, poverty, poor sanitation, limited access to services) to leprosy incidence. Integrating spatial distribution with socioeconomic risk factors therefore directly targets identification of persistent transmission hotspots despite overall declines and is the most effective approach described in the text.

232.Option: The laboratory confirmation of rabies in two dogs, combined with the clustering of cases in suburban areas, supports the hypothesis that a single rabid dog was responsible for multiple infections
Evidence: Data collected from the Central Veterinary Laboratory in Maputo showed laboratory confirmed rabies in two dogs during the period under investigation.
we were able to link seven cases of human rabies to bites of five dogs, as shown in Fig 4.
Most of the dog bite victims and cases of human rabies resided in the suburban areas of Maputo and Matola cities.
232.Rationale: Laboratory confirmation in two dogs and the epidemiologic linkage to five different dogs argue against a single-dog source. While suburban clustering is consistent with local transmission, the passage explicitly links human cases to multiple dogs and reports laboratory confirmation in two animals, which weakens the claim that one rabid dog alone was responsible for multiple infections.

232.Option: The fact that one dog was linked to three human cases, and that the majority of bites were from dogs, indicates that a single rabid dog was likely responsible for the outbreak
Evidence: we were able to link seven cases of human rabies to bites of five dogs, as shown in Fig 4.
four of the five dogs were each linked to one rabies victim, and one was linked to three victims, two of whom were identified in the community investigation.
All of the 14 cases of human rabies involved dog bite.
Most animal bites were caused by dogs (97.8%, 801/819).
232.Rationale: The strongest direct epidemiologic evidence for a single-source event is the reported linkage of one dog to three human rabies cases. That explicit transmission chain demonstrates that a single rabid dog bit multiple people who subsequently developed rabies. This aligns with rabies biology and epidemiology: rabies is transmitted in saliva via bites, an infected dog can bite and infect multiple humans during its infectious period, and most exposures in this outbreak were from dogs (nearly all bites were canine), making such a single-dog transmission cluster plausible.

232.Option: The high proportion of bites from stray dogs (23.7%) suggests that multiple rabid animals were likely circulating, increasing the probability of a single dog being responsible for multiple cases
Evidence: In 23.7% (194/801) and 17.1% (140/801) the biting dog was stray or unknown, respectively.
we were able to link seven cases of human rabies to bites of five dogs, as shown in Fig 4.
232.Rationale: A high proportion of bites by stray or unknown dogs supports the possibility of multiple circulating rabid animals rather than increasing the probability that one single dog caused multiple cases. The passage itself links cases to five different dogs, so the stray-dog proportion is more consistent with multi-dog circulation and does not strengthen the single-dog hypothesis as the option claims.

232.Option: The fact that only one of the 14 rabies victims received post-exposure prophylaxis suggests that the virus was highly transmissible and likely originated from a single infected animal
Evidence: No rabies victim received full post-exposure vaccination.
Of the 14 cases of human rabies, 12 were children below 15 years of age and 8 were male.
All of the 14 cases of human rabies involved dog bite.
232.Rationale: Failure to receive post-exposure prophylaxis increases the likelihood that bitten persons will develop clinical rabies, but it does not indicate that infections originated from a single animal nor that the virus was unusually transmissible. The passage documents multiple dog-human linkages and laboratory confirmation in dogs; lack of PEP explains susceptibility but does not support the specific claim of a single-source origin.

232.Option: One rabid dog was linked to three human cases, and laboratory confirmation of rabies in two dogs supports the possibility of a single source driving multiple infections
Evidence: four of the five dogs were each linked to one rabies victim, and one was linked to three victims, two of whom were identified in the community investigation.
Data collected from the Central Veterinary Laboratory in Maputo showed laboratory confirmed rabies in two dogs during the period under investigation.
232.Rationale: While one dog being linked to three victims supports a single-dog transmission event, laboratory confirmation in two different dogs indicates that more than one rabid dog was present during the outbreak period. Thus, the laboratory evidence does not uniquely support a single-source explanation and instead suggests multiple infected animals were involved; combining these facts does not strengthen the single-source hypothesis.

233.Option: A sustained Th17 response throughout infection, accompanied by high levels of IL-17A, is most strongly associated with resolution of infection and prevention of chronicity
Evidence: In this study, we show T lymphocytes with regulatory profile polarization in the popliteal lymph node of a self-healing animal model of CBM, while Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM. The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.
Neutralization of IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.

High doses of IL-17 in lesions essentially represent a host strategy against fungal infections. However, an average Th17 response may overlap the regulatory role of Tregs, and this imbalance may eventually result in a less effective response to the fungus [16].

IL-23 directs the development of Th17, promoting the development of chronic inflammatory processes dominated by the presence of IL-17, IL-6, IL-8, and TNFα, as well as the intense activity of neutrophils and monocytes.
233.Rationale: The passage describes Th17 as important early in infection but explicitly indicates that Th17 dominance is followed by Th1 during remission; sustained/high Th17 is associated with inflammatory pathology and potential chronicity. Neutralization experiments show IL-17 is needed for early clearance, but the overall message is that prolonged or excessive Th17 (IL-17/IL-23 axis) can contribute to chronic inflammation. Therefore a sustained Th17 response throughout infection is not the pattern most strongly associated with resolution.

233.Option: High levels of Treg activity in the infected tissue, without concurrent effector T cell responses, are sufficient to resolve infection and prevent chronicity in chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: These cells exert their function by releasing inhibitory cytokines such as IL-10 and TGF-β [20].

However, considering that Treg-mediated response may limit the effectiveness of the protective immune response, when not followed by an active cellular response such as Th1, the consequence may be the persistence of fungal infection [59,60].

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.
233.Rationale: The passage shows Tregs modulate inflammation but warns that Treg activity alone, without effective effector (Th1/Th17) responses, can permit persistence of the fungus. The anti-CD25 depletion experiment indicates removal of Tregs reduced early fungal burden but impaired later clearance and resolution, illustrating that Tregs must act in concert with effector responses rather than alone being sufficient to resolve infection.

233.Option: A balanced coexistence of Th17 and Th1 responses throughout the infection, without a clear temporal shift, is the optimal immunological pattern for resolving chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.

The numbers of Th17 cells (CD4hi IL-17+) rose sharply during the early stages of the disease (until 14 days after infection) and fell gradually during later time points in both footpad (Fig 1D and S3B Fig) and dLN (Fig 1D and S4B Fig).

In summary, our work expands long-standing Th1/Th2 dualized comprehension of CBM immunopathogenicity, showing the relevance of Dectin-2 mediated Th17 and Treg cells in a self-healing model of CBM. In this context, it has been demonstrated that both Th1 and Th17 responses, with the aid of Treg cells, are required for appropriate immune response in CBM, varying in intensity throughout the immune response in the course of the disease.
233.Rationale: The data and authors' interpretation emphasize a temporal sequence (early Th17 followed by later Th1) rather than a constant, unchanging coexistence. The passage repeatedly describes dynamic shifts in dominance over time and concludes that responses vary in intensity during the course of disease. Thus, a static balanced coexistence without temporal shift is not supported.

233.Option: A dominant Th1 response from the onset of infection, characterized by high IFN-γ production, is the most effective pattern for preventing chronicity in chromoblastomycosis
Evidence: In the footpad, the IL-17+ population was higher than IFN-γ+ cells in the first 7 days of infection (E). The opposite occurs after 21 days, when the IFN-γ+ population is higher than IL-17+ T cells (F), reversing the ratio between these populations.
Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D). In turn, neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G).

These data suggest that Th17 cells might contribute to fungal elimination at the early stages of infection, whereas Th1 cells might be needed during the early and later stages of infection to eliminate the fungal pathogen.
233.Rationale: While Th1/IFN-γ is important (neutralization impairs clearance), the passage documents that Th17 predominates early and contributes to early fungal elimination; Th1 dominance arises later and coincides with remission. Therefore a Th1-dominant response from the onset is not the pattern observed or identified as most effective; rather a temporal contribution of both subsets is required.

233.Option: A sequential shift from Th17 to Th1 dominance, supported by regulatory T cell activity, enables effective fungal clearance and prevents chronic disease progression
Evidence: In this study, we show T lymphocytes with regulatory profile polarization in the popliteal lymph node of a self-healing animal model of CBM, while Th17 was predominant in the infected area during the early stages of experimental murine CBM. The Th17 profile was then followed by Th1 popularization in later stages, coinciding with the remission phase of the disease in this experimental model.
In vivo analysis with IL-17A and IFN-γ neutralization hindered fungal cell elimination in the course of the disease.

In vivo depletion of CD25+ cells leads to Treg cells’ impairment in draining lymph nodes (dLN), reflected by a reduction in fungal burden after 14 days of infection. Furthermore, fungal cells were not cleared in later stages of the disease, prolonging CBM clinical features in those animals.

In summary, our work expands long-standing Th1/Th2 dualized comprehension of CBM immunopathogenicity, showing the relevance of Dectin-2 mediated Th17 and Treg cells in a self-healing model of CBM. In this context, it has been demonstrated that both Th1 and Th17 responses, with the aid of Treg cells, are required for appropriate immune response in CBM, varying in intensity throughout the immune response in the course of the disease.
233.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence that early Th17 activity contributes to early fungal elimination, that Th1 responses predominate later coincident with remission, and that Treg activity in draining lymph nodes helps modulate responses to enable resolution. Neutralization/depletion experiments support that both effector arms and regulatory control are necessary for timely clearance and prevention of persistence. Thus a sequential Th17→Th1 shift with supportive Treg activity best correlates with resolution and prevention of chronicity.

234.Option: The fact that the novel genotype HhMzEb1 was identified only in Maputo and is genetically similar to a known human genotype suggests that it may have been introduced from a human population, indicating person-to-person transmission rather than environmental reservoirs.
Evidence: Nucleotide sequence analysis on the ITS region of the nine positive cases revealed the presence of four genotypes, three previously reported (Peru11, Type IV, and S2) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.

Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the novel genotype HhMzEB1 clustered within Group 1 when its relationship with other genotypes of human and animal origin previously reported in Africa was evaluated (Fig 1).

All genotypes identified in the present study, including novel HhMzEb1, belonged to Group 1 (Fig 1) that includes E. bieneusi genotypes with low host specificity that are found not only in humans but also in domestic and wild animals worldwide [10,11,14].
234.Rationale: The option asserts person-to-person transmission based on the novel genotype being only in Maputo and similar to a human genotype. The passage does state the novel genotype was found in Maputo, but phylogenetic results place HhMzEb1 in Group 1, which is characterized by low host specificity and presence in both humans and animals. That fact argues against concluding solely human-to-human introduction and instead supports potential zoonotic or shared environmental origins. The passage does not provide evidence that HhMzEb1 is more similar to human-only genotypes or that person-to-person spread is the primary route.

234.Option: The detection of multiple E. bieneusi genotypes, including a novel genotype, in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children across different geographic regions of Mozambique suggests a common source of infection, possibly environmental.
Evidence: Nucleotide sequences of the ITS revealed the presence of four distinct E. bieneusi genotypes, three previously reported in humans (Type IV, S2, and Peru11) and a novel genotype (named HhMzEb1).
