This study aims to investigate the occurrence and genetic diversity of E. bieneusi in symptomatic and asymptomatic paediatric populations in two of the most populous provinces of Central (Zambézia) and Southern (Maputo) Mozambique.
In Zambézia, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.
234.Rationale: While the detection of multiple genotypes (including a novel one) across symptomatic and asymptomatic children in different regions is consistent with exposure from one or more common sources, this observation alone is indirect. The passage provides stronger, more specific evidence for environmental/zoonotic reservoirs (for example, genotypes belonging to Group 1 and prior reports of these genotypes in animals and water). Therefore, although the option is plausible, it is not the most direct or strongest factor supporting a shared human–animal environmental reservoir.

234.Option: The higher prevalence of E. bieneusi in asymptomatic children in rural areas, combined with the presence of livestock and poor sanitation, supports the hypothesis of a shared environmental reservoir between humans and animals.
Evidence: In Zambézia, 807 asymptomatic schoolchildren and 290 children presenting with gastrointestinal complaints seeking for medical care were investigated ...
Overall, E. bieneusi was detected by nested-PCR and confirmed by sequencing in 0.7% (9/1,247, 95% CI: 0.4–1.4) of the investigated children population.
The prevalence was more than 10-fold higher in the Manhiça district in Maputo (4.0%, 6/150, 95% CI: 1.8–8.6) compared to all the districts in Zambézia combined (0.3%, 3/1,097, 95%CI: 0.1–0.8).
This is the first molecular study of E. bieneusi in Mozambique. The parasite was primarily found in asymptomatic children in Maputo (Manhiça district) and Zambézia province.
The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission.
234.Rationale: The passage notes higher prevalence in certain areas and that many cases were in asymptomatic children in rural settings, and it discusses animal-associated genotypes and poor sanitation as plausible contributors elsewhere. However, the study did not present direct data on livestock contact or specific sanitation metrics linked statistically to E. bieneusi infection in this dataset (it notes missing information on some variables). Thus, while rural prevalence and environmental/sanitation concerns are suggestive, the passage provides stronger direct evidence (genotypes in Group 1 and known animal occurrences) for a shared human–animal environmental reservoir than the demographic prevalence pattern alone.

234.Option: The presence of genotypes from Group 1, which have broad host specificity and are found in both humans and animals, in both symptomatic and asymptomatic children across multiple regions of Mozambique supports the existence of a shared environmental reservoir
Evidence: All genotypes identified in the present study, including novel HhMzEb1, belonged to Group 1 (Fig 1) that includes E. bieneusi genotypes with low host specificity that are found not only in humans but also in domestic and wild animals worldwide [10,11,14].

The identification of potentially zoonotic genotypes Peru11 and Type IV, commonly found not only in humans but also in domestic and wild animals worldwide, indicates the potential for zoonotic or cross-species transmission.
In Zambézia, E. bieneusi was found in 3 asymptomatic children.
In Maputo province, E. bieneusi was found in six male children, one presenting with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea and 5 asymptomatic.

environmental (water) samples should be also considered as a source of infection by microsporidia, as E. bieneusi has been demonstrated to be involved in waterborne outbreaks of gastrointestinal disease [50].
234.Rationale: The strongest evidence in the passage supporting a shared environmental reservoir between humans and animals is the detection of Group 1 genotypes, which are known to infect a broad range of mammalian species and are commonly found in both humans and animals worldwide. The authors explicitly state that the identification of genotypes previously described in animals "indicates the potential for zoonotic or cross-species transmission," and they also note waterborne outbreaks as an environmental route. This combination (broad host-range genotypes + known animal occurrence + environmental transmission routes) provides the most direct support for a shared environmental reservoir hypothesis.

234.Option: The co-infection of E. bieneusi with other enteric pathogens such as G. duodenalis and Cryptosporidium spp. in the same children suggests that these pathogens share a common environmental source, including potential zoonotic transmission routes.
Evidence: All six E. bieneusi-positive samples were co-infected with G. duodenalis.

In Maputo province... Among the 4 boys infected with the novel genotype, HhMzEb1, one was HIV positive, presented with moderate-to-severe diarrhoea, and was co-infected with Cryptosporidium meleagridis. Another boy with this genotype was co-infected with C. parvum, and a third one was co-infected with Rotavirus.
Univariable analysis (Table 4) of the combined data showed that significant risk associations included infections with G. duodenalis or Cryptosporidium spp. and age, but handwashing was protective.

environmental (water) samples should be also considered as a source of infection by microsporidia, as E. bieneusi has been demonstrated to be involved in waterborne outbreaks of gastrointestinal disease [50].
234.Rationale: Co-infection with other fecal-oral pathogens that are known to be water- or environment-associated (Giardia, Cryptosporidium) supports the interpretation of common environmental exposure. However, co-infection alone does not specifically demonstrate a shared reservoir between humans and animals; it indicates shared exposure routes (e.g., contaminated water or poor sanitation). The passage provides more direct and specific evidence for human–animal sharing via the identification of Group 1 genotypes commonly found in animals, which is stronger support for a shared environmental reservoir than co-infection data alone.

235.Option: Use community-wide microfilaria surveys in high-risk areas to directly assess the presence of active transmission
Evidence: Microfilaremia rates were <1% in all PHI areas studied.

Microfilaria (Mf) testing was performed for people with positive filarial antigen tests (in community household surveys, school surveys, and TAS) with three-line blood smears (60 µl total volume of night blood tested).

Community antibody testing was performed in a subset of PHIs that were surveyed in the comprehensive surveillance study (Table S1). Although CFA and Mf rates in these communities were below provisional target levels, community antibody rates were high in all of these PHIs, and this probably reflects high infection rates that were present in these areas prior to implementation of the national MDA program.
235.Rationale: The passage shows that microfilaremia (Mf) rates were very low (<1%) across PHIs and Mf testing was only done among antigen-positive persons using night blood smears, indicating community-wide Mf surveys are insensitive and operationally burdensome. The authors highlight that antibody rates were higher and more informative in detecting prior exposure than Mf, implying that community Mf surveys alone would not be the most effective strategy to identify residual transmission after TAS.

235.Option: Increase the sample size of transmission assessment surveys in each evaluation unit to improve detection of low-level transmission
Evidence: Our results clearly show that TAS performed according to WHO guidelines were not sensitive for detecting ongoing LF transmission in Sri Lanka.

First, we believe that EUs of 1 to 2 million are too much too large, because risk factors that affect LF transmission often vary widely across such large populations/areas. This problem could be mitigated by reducing the size of EUs (for example, to areas with populations of 100,000 or less), but that would significantly increase the cost of TAS.
235.Rationale: The authors identify the main TAS limitation as overly large evaluation units (EUs) and recommend reducing EU size (not merely increasing sample size within large EUs) to improve sensitivity. They explicitly note that reducing EU size (to capture local heterogeneity) is a preferred mitigation; increasing sample size in a large heterogeneous EU is not recommended and would likely still miss localized hotspots. Thus increasing TAS sample size per EU is not presented as the most effective approach.

235.Option: Target school-based surveys to older children (grades 5–6) instead of younger children (grades 1–2) to increase the likelihood of detecting persistent transmission
Evidence: Since CFA rates in school aged children were much lower than those in adults. Therefore, the sensitivity of TAS might be improved by using a similar cluster sampling method to test adults (for example, those attending primary health clinics) instead of children in schools.
The TAS sampling strategy required filarial antigen testing of approximately 1500 primary grade children in each EU.
235.Rationale: The passage suggests sampling adults rather than school children to improve sensitivity because antigenemia rates are higher in adults than in school-aged children. It does not suggest targeting older schoolchildren (grades 5–6) as an alternative; the specific recommendation is to sample adults (or consider antibody testing), so switching to older children is not supported as the most effective strategy in the text.

235.Option: Conduct molecular xenodiagnosis in high-risk sub-districts to detect persistent transmission missed by standard transmission assessment surveys
Evidence: Results from this study strongly support the use of molecular xenodiagnosis for post-MDA surveillance in areas where LF is transmitted by Culex mosquitoes.
MX does not require collection of blood samples or active participation by large numbers of people in endemic areas.

We recommend antibody testing of children using TAS sampling methods and/or MX (especially in areas believed to be at high risk) to complement antigen-test based TAS, because these methods appear to be more sensitive than TAS for detecting ongoing LF transmission.

Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs. Mosquitoes from both PHIs surveyed in Galle district and one in Matara district had parasite DNA rates of more than 1%... Most of the other filariasis parameters were also low in these PHIs.
235.Rationale: The authors explicitly endorse molecular xenodiagnosis (MX) as a sensitive post-MDA surveillance tool that can detect persistent transmission missed by TAS, and they recommend MX (especially in high-risk areas) to complement TAS. The evidence shows MX detected filarial DNA in mosquitoes in PHIs where human antigen markers were low, supporting its effectiveness for identifying residual transmission.

235.Option: Perform repeated transmission assessment surveys in the same evaluation units over multiple years to monitor for changes in antigenemia rates
Evidence: LF elimination is a dynamic process [29], and point estimates of persistent infection may be less important than trends over time. For this reason, we plan to restudy Peliyagodawatta and several other PHIs with elevated LF parameters three years after the evaluations described in this publication.
Our results clearly show that TAS performed according to WHO guidelines were not sensitive for detecting ongoing LF transmission in Sri Lanka.

We recommend antibody testing of children using TAS sampling methods and/or MX (especially in areas believed to be at high risk) to complement antigen-test based TAS, because these methods appear to be more sensitive than TAS for detecting ongoing LF transmission.
235.Rationale: While the authors acknowledge the value of monitoring trends and indicate they will resurvey some PHIs in the future, they do not present repeated TAS alone as the most effective way to find ongoing transmission after a ‘pass.’ Instead, they emphasize that TAS lacks sensitivity and recommend more sensitive complementary methods (antibody testing and MX) and targeted re-evaluation of high-risk areas. Thus repeated TAS alone is not supported as the most effective strategy.

236.Option: Differential assessment methods for therapeutic response between groups introduce measurement bias that undermines comparability
Evidence: Another potential limitation is that the intervention arm was evaluated via photographs, while the control arm was evaluated in person.
In the intervention (app) arm, the study physician evaluated the therapeutic response via photographs taken by the CHV using the phone app at four time points.
In the control (standard of care) groups, evaluation of therapeutic response was conducted in-person by a physician at the CIDEIM clinic.
However, we have recently shown that evaluation by photographs is highly accurate [12].
236.Rationale: While the passage explicitly notes differential assessment (photos in the app arm vs in-person in control) which could introduce measurement bias, the authors also state that photographic evaluation has been shown to be highly accurate. The passage highlights other threats to validity (notably the non-randomized control group) as more directly compromising internal validity. Therefore this factor is a recognized limitation but not described in the text as the most critical threat to the validity of the comparison.

236.Option: The lack of blinding in the study introduces performance bias, as patients and investigators may have influenced outcome reporting based on treatment assignment
Evidence: Following enrollment neither the investigators nor the patients were blinded to the allocation.
The statistical analysis followed the methods pre-defined in the protocol and in the statistical analysis plan, and was by intention to treat.
236.Rationale: The passage does state that neither investigators nor patients were blinded, which can introduce performance or ascertainment bias. However, the authors specifically call out the addition of a non-randomized control group as restricting internal validity. Thus although lack of blinding is a downside, the text points to the non-randomized control group as the more critical influence on the validity of comparisons.

236.Option: The use of a paper-based form for adverse event reporting in the control group creates a data collection bias that affects the reliability of safety outcomes
Evidence: In addition to the app, a standard paper form was also used by CHV to monitor treatment adherence and adverse events for all study participants, as is done for all leishmaniasis patients in CIDEIM.
In the control groups, the patient recorded any adverse events on a printed checklist that was delivered to the health personnel of the clinical facility.
236.Rationale: The passage documents differences in how adverse events were recorded (app-based weekly recording by CHV versus printed checklist in controls), which could affect completeness and reliability of safety data. However, the authors emphasize that the principal threat to internal validity arises from the added non-randomized control group. Thus while differential data collection methods are a limitation, they are not presented as the most critical factor undermining the randomized comparison.

236.Option: The asynchronous 'store-and-forward' data transmission strategy used in the app introduces information lag that affects the timeliness and accuracy of outcome assessment
Evidence: CHV were able to record information on adherence to treatment, adverse reactions and therapeutic outcome without connectivity, and transmit the saved data afterwards when in an area having internet coverage using an asynchronous “store-and-forward” strategy.
The limitation of connectivity could therefore be managed, though not totally overcome, in the setting of this study.
236.Rationale: The passage acknowledges the store-and-forward approach and notes connectivity limitations could be managed, implying this introduces some logistical limitations but was not portrayed as a primary threat to the internal validity of the randomized comparison. The text singles out the non-randomized control group as the major internal validity concern.

236.Option: The addition of a non-randomized control group introduces selection bias that compromises the internal validity of the comparison between intervention and control groups
Evidence: A non-randomized control group was added later (see below).
The study was expanded in order to enroll such patients and hence increase study recruitment.

On the other hand, this group was included because of another limitation, namely that the intervention could only be carried out where a CHV was available and security concerns did not impede interaction with the dispersed households, a factor that restricts the external validity (generalizability).

Another potential limitation is that the intervention arm was evaluated via photographs, while the control arm was evaluated in person. However, we have recently shown that evaluation by photographs is highly accurate [12].
Another potential limitation of the study is that a non-randomized control group was added, and comparisons with this group are restricted in terms of internal validity.
236.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that a non-randomized control group was added and that "comparisons with this group are restricted in terms of internal validity." Because patients in that group were not randomized (they could not be randomized when a CHV was not available nearby), selection bias and confounding are likely, and this most directly undermines the validity of comparisons despite the randomized component of the trial. The authors single this out as a key limitation affecting internal validity.

237.Option: The negative correlation between low SES and infection in some villages (e.g., Magodzoni) suggests that individuals with lower SES avoid high-risk environments, indicating that personal risk assessment and behavioral avoidance are key drivers of infection patterns.
Evidence: The correlation was either positive, mostly in Kinango (malaria, S. haematobium) and in Milalani/Nganja (hookworm and Trichuris), or negative (with malaria, filariasis, S. haematobium, and hookworms in Magadzoni, and with hookworm in Jego).

Lower SES, lack of access to sanitation, and absence of a safe source of drinking water were associated with a higher risk of infection and co-infection, although SES was not associated with filariasis or Trichuris infection.
237.Rationale: The passage documents mixed associations between SES and infection (positive in some villages, negative in others). The mere fact of a negative correlation in a particular village does not logically imply that low-SES individuals are avoiding high-risk environments through personal risk assessment or behavioral avoidance. Rather, the authors interpret spatial clustering and environmental constraints (e.g., proximity to risk sites) as key drivers of infection patterns. Thus the option's causal interpretation (behavioral avoidance) is not supported by the passage and is a speculative individual-level explanation inconsistent with the study's emphasis on structural/environmental determinants.

237.Option: The significant positive correlation between high household density and infection prevalence in all villages suggests that crowded living conditions promote individual-level transmission behaviors, such as close contact and shared hygiene practices.
Evidence: Results based on the Gi*(d) test showed that high density households and lower income were clustered in all villages (Figure 5).
In several villages clusters of single infections and of co-infections were significantly correlated with the presence of clusters of high household density and of low SES (Table 6).
237.Rationale: The passage does report a significant positive correlation between high household density and infection prevalence. However, the option attributes that correlation to individual-level transmission behaviors (close contact, shared hygiene). The study emphasizes structural and environmental explanations (e.g., clustering of low-income housing near vector habitats) rather than individual behaviors as the primary drivers. Therefore, while the correlation exists, interpreting it as evidence for individual-level behavioral transmission is not supported by the authors' conclusions and so this option is not the best answer to the question asking for evidence of structural (not behavioral) drivers.

237.Option: The fact that infection hotspots co-occur with clusters of high household density and low SES in multiple villages indicates that shared community-level behaviors, such as bathing or waste disposal, are responsible for the clustering of infections.
Evidence: The spatial distribution of prevalence of single parasitic infections and of poly-parasitism in each village was significantly clustered (Figures 6 and 7). Hot spots of diseases were not confined to a particular area in the villages, but, rather, overlapped each other (Gi*(d) test, p<0.05).
In several villages clusters of single infections and of co-infections were significantly correlated with the presence of clusters of high household density and of low SES (Table 6).
These co-occurring clusters were found near aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
237.Rationale: The passage documents co-occurrence of infection hotspots with high household density and low SES. However, attributing that co-location specifically to shared community-level behaviors (bathing, waste disposal) is speculative and not the strongest interpretation offered by the authors. The authors emphasize structural/environmental constraints (e.g., proximity of low-income housing to snail/Anopheles habitats) as the primary explanation. Thus this option shifts the cause to community behaviors rather than structural constraints and is not supported as the strongest suggestion in the passage.

237.Option: The spatial overlap between low-income housing and environmental risk sites (e.g., snail habitats) indicates that infection clustering is driven by structural constraints on settlement rather than individual exposure choices
Evidence: Low-income houses were clustered closer to sites that were suitable for snail hosts of S. haematobium (Figure 5 and Figure S1 in Text S1).
Results based on the Gi*(d) test showed that high density households and lower income were clustered in all villages (Figure 5).
These co-occurring clusters were found near aquatic habitats favorable for Anopheles mosquitoes and snails.
237.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that low-income houses are clustered closer to environmental risk sites (snail habitats) and that clusters of low SES and high household density overlap with infection hotspots and aquatic habitats favorable to vectors. That spatial coincidence indicates people are settling in higher-risk locations for structural reasons (poverty, housing location) rather than individual behavioral choices alone. This direct spatial evidence most strongly supports a structural explanation for the observed clustering.

237.Option: The variation in infection patterns across age groups indicates that individual risk-taking behaviors, such as water contact or outdoor activity, differ by age and drive the observed clustering of infections.
Evidence: Children and young adults were more likely to be infected with malaria parasites, S. haematobium, and Trichuris, while adults were more likely to be infected with filariasis and hookworms.
Individuals in the 8–16 age group were at high risk of exposure to malaria, schistosomiasis, and Trichuris, but not for filariasis and hookworms, which mostly affected adults.
237.Rationale: Age-patterns in infection prevalence do suggest that exposure risk varies by age, which could reflect behavior. However, the question asks which factor most strongly suggests structural conditions rather than individual behavior. Age-related differences point to behavioral heterogeneity but do not demonstrate that clustering is due to structural constraints (e.g., settlement near vector habitats or low-SES spatial segregation). The passage gives stronger spatial/environmental evidence (see Option 3) supporting structural drivers, so this age-based explanation is not the best answer.

238.Option: The duration of follow-up after treatment completion critically affects the observed cure rate and must be standardized across studies to enable valid comparisons of long-term outcomes
Evidence: Lesions were also re-measured during follow-up visits 3 months, 6 months and 12 month post treatment.
In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.

Since the number of cured patients did not differ at 6 and 12 month follow-ups, it seems that complete therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine could be observed 6 month post treatment. From this time on, it is possible to make a definite opinion on the patient’s response to oral miltefosine. However, follow up sessions are recommended due to check for recurrence.
238.Rationale: The passage explicitly shows that observed cure rates change across the scheduled follow-up times (3, 6, 12 months) and that conclusions about definite therapeutic effect depend on length of follow-up (authors state the therapeutic effect appears complete by 6 months and recommend follow-up to detect recurrence). Therefore when comparing studies with varying follow-up durations, differences in how long patients were observed will critically influence apparent long-term cure rates and must be standardized or accounted for to make valid comparisons.

238.Option: The dosage and duration of drug administration are the most critical factors in determining long-term treatment success and must be consistent across studies to enable meaningful comparisons
Evidence: Volunteers received miltefosine orally at a dosage of ∼2.5 mg/kg daily for 28 days as follows: <25Kg one capsule daily; 25–45 Kg two capsules per day; ˃45Kg three capsules per day.
Thus, longer treatment courses with systemic miltefosine might cause higher improvement rate among patients.
Oral miltefosine regimen dose of ∼2.5 mg/kg/day for 28 days will be tolerated by majority of patients.
238.Rationale: While dosage and treatment duration clearly affect efficacy (the passage notes regimen and speculates longer courses might improve cure), the question specifically concerns interpretation when comparing studies with varying follow-up durations. The passage emphasizes follow-up timing as the key determinant of when definitive cure can be assessed (e.g., 6 vs 12 months), so although dosing is important, it is not the most critical factor for interpreting long-term outcomes across studies that differ in follow-up length.

238.Option: The presence of a control group in a study design determines the reliability of long-term cure rates and should be consistent across comparative trials
Evidence: The result of this study need to be seen in the sight of some limitations. A major limitation is lack of control group as well as low number of samples which restrict definite conclusion. The study was an open non-controlled case series.
238.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that the absence of a control group is a major limitation affecting reliability. However, in the specific context of comparing long-term outcomes across studies with different follow-up durations, the duration of follow-up more directly influences observed cure and relapse rates. Presence/absence of a control group affects internal validity but does not address the temporal issue the question highlights, so it is not the single most critical factor in that specific context.

238.Option: The rate of patient dropout during treatment is the primary determinant of long-term treatment success and must be minimized to ensure accurate outcome comparisons
Evidence: There were two dropouts (7.40%) due to creatinine level elevation, suspicious to drug induced nephrotoxicity in one patient and dissatisfaction to continue in one patient.
Of 27 enrolled patients for systemic miltefosine treatment, 25 patients completed treatment.
Considering the early dropouts in the present trial, efficacy of miltefosine for definite patient cure in per protocol rate of 68% might be achieved.
cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis
238.Rationale: Dropout rate influences apparent efficacy (per-protocol vs intention-to-treat results differ and authors mention early dropouts affect estimated efficacy). Nevertheless, when the comparative issue is varying follow-up durations across studies, the length of follow-up is more directly determinative of whether relapses are captured and when cure can be declared. Dropouts are important but not the primary factor affecting interpretation across studies with different follow-up lengths.

238.Option: The species of Leishmania responsible for infection must be standardized across studies to allow valid comparisons of long-term treatment outcomes
Evidence: anthroponotic cutaneous leishmaniasis (ACL) and zoonotic cutaneous leishmaniasis (ZCL) caused by L. tropica and L. major respectively, are the most common types of CL in Iran [3].

It should be also noted that 69.04% of lesions had more than 1 year duration. Also, different efficacy rates may be related to the difference in cure rate between L. major versus L. tropica in Iran, as the former could be even self-limited and the latter causes more refractory lesions.
In Iran, as endemic place for OWCL, therapeutic effects of oral miltefosine on ZCL have been studied previously.
238.Rationale: The passage indicates species matters because L. major and L. tropica have different natural histories and treatment responses, so species differences affect comparability of outcomes. However, the question asks which factor most critically influences interpretation when comparing studies that differ in follow-up duration. Although species heterogeneity is important for valid comparisons, differing follow-up lengths will directly change observed long-term cure/recurrence rates regardless of species, making follow-up duration the more critical factor in the specific context described.

239.Option: The consistent presence of genetically similar V. cholerae isolates in both household water and human samples, coupled with contamination of stored water whenever source water was contaminated, indicates a shared environmental source as a primary transmission route
Evidence: When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.
All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.

The genetic characterization of V. cholerae isolates from cholera patient households showed that the majority of water and clinical samples within the same household had isolates with closely related MLVA genotypes.
239.Rationale: The option correctly identifies the key evidence that supports a shared environmental source: identical or closely related MLVA genotypes found in both water and clinical isolates from the same household and the observation that source contamination co-occurred with contamination of stored drinking water. Those findings indicate that household water (an environmental reservoir) carried the same strains infecting people in the household, consistent with water-to-person transmission within households rather than transmission solely by person-to-person contact.

239.Option: The fact that 90% of infected household members had closely related MLVA genotypes supports person-to-person transmission as the dominant route, not a shared environmental source
Evidence: In support of person-to-person transmission or a shared contaminated source in the household, we found that the vast majority (90%) of infected household members had closely related MLVA genotypes.
Vibrio cholerae isolates from the same household were more closely related than isolates from different households.
239.Rationale: The quoted passage explicitly states that the finding of closely related genotypes among household members supports EITHER person-to-person transmission OR a shared contaminated source in the household. Thus the 90% relatedness does not uniquely support person-to-person transmission to the exclusion of a shared environmental source. The option incorrectly asserts it rules out a shared environmental source.

239.Option: The high proportion of household contacts with V. cholerae infections who reported consuming street vended food suggests that contaminated food from external sources is the primary transmission route, not shared household water
Evidence: Household contacts consuming street vended food had a significantly higher odds of a V. cholerae infection (Odds Ratio (OR): 9.45, 95% confidence interval (CI): 2.14, 41.72) (V. cholerae infections: 33% (consumed street vended food) vs. 5% (did not consume street vended food))
This is likely because of a recently ill food handler contaminating food or water as was found in rural Micronesia.
239.Rationale: While street-vended food was a strong risk factor and may represent an external transmission route, the question asks which finding most strongly supports transmission via a shared environmental source within households. Evidence for contaminated household water (genetically linked water and clinical isolates, and contamination of stored water) more directly supports within-household environmental transmission. The street-food finding points to an external source and does not contradict but does not most strongly support a shared household environmental source.

239.Option: The finding that all households with V. cholerae in their source water had unimproved sanitation options suggests that poor sanitation is the main driver of transmission, not shared water sources
Evidence: Only households with unimproved sanitation options had detectable V. cholerae in their tube wells.
All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
In rural Bangladesh tube wells are often located adjacent to household latrines, making these water sources more susceptible to fecal contamination.
239.Rationale: The cited sentences show a link between unimproved sanitation and contaminated water sources, but they do not support the option's claim that poor sanitation (as opposed to shared water) explains transmission 'not shared water sources.' In fact, poor sanitation likely explains how source water became contaminated, which is consistent with shared environmental (water) transmission. Thus the option misstates the implication of the findings and is not the best support for transmission occurring within households via a shared environmental source.

239.Option: The observation that 27% of stored household water samples were positive for V. cholerae, compared to 13% of source water samples, indicates that contamination occurs primarily during household storage, supporting a shared environmental source
Evidence: twice as many stored household water samples were positive for V. cholerae compared to source water samples (27% vs. 13%). This finding suggests high rates of household contamination of stored water.
All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.
239.Rationale: The higher proportion of contaminated stored water than contaminated source water indicates that contamination often occurs within the household (during collection, transport, or storage). Together with the genetic evidence linking water and clinical isolates, this supports a shared household environmental reservoir (stored water) that can expose multiple household members, rather than transmission solely via direct person-to-person contact.

240.Option: Downregulation of dendritic cell surface receptors through chronic antigen exposure, leading to reduced responsiveness
Evidence: Fig 2D shows that BMDC upregulation of surface activation markers (MHCII and CD86) in response to LPS treatment was not affected by McES.

These results demonstrated that McES limited LPS-driven BMDC activation independently of the time of exposure and also diminished the effector response (IL-12p70 production) of LPS-activated BMDCs in vitro.
240.Rationale: The passage explicitly reports that McES did not affect upregulation of DC surface activation markers MHCII and CD86 in response to LPS (quoting Fig 2D). That finding contradicts the idea that chronic exposure causes downregulation of surface receptors as the primary mechanism of reduced responsiveness. Instead, suppression occurred without loss of these surface activation markers, indicating a mechanism other than receptor downregulation.

240.Option: Induction of regulatory T cells that suppress dendritic cell activation through cell-cell contact
Evidence: Excretory-secretory (ES) products of metacestodes are instrumental in the mitigation of IL-12 production by host DCs [2,11].
In vitro exposure of BMDCs to McES impaired their subsequent responsiveness to other pathogen products including ligands for TLRs and C-type lectins.

These results indicate that only live M. corti tetrathyridia massively attracted host CD11c+ cells up to day 7 post-infection whereas dead (ametabolic) larvae recruited CD11c+ cells just for the 3 days that followed injection. Given the ability of live helminths to excrete-secrete molecules which directly interact with host immune cells [11,23] and considering the reported predominance of dendritic cells within murine peritoneal CD11c+ cells, our data suggest a central role for ES products of M. corti (McES) in the modulation of host DC responses in vivo.
240.Rationale: The passage emphasizes a direct effect of parasite excretory-secretory products on DCs in vitro and in vivo (impairing BMDC responsiveness and recruiting CD11c+ cells), without describing regulatory T cell induction or Treg-mediated, contact-dependent suppression. The evidence supports a direct McES→DC suppression mechanism rather than suppression mediated indirectly via induction of regulatory T cells.

240.Option: Persistent exposure to parasite antigens that induce anergy in dendritic cells through repeated stimulation
Evidence: In vitro exposure of BMDCs to McES impaired their subsequent responsiveness to other pathogen products including ligands for TLRs and C-type lectins.
Fig 2D shows that BMDC upregulation of surface activation markers (MHCII and CD86) in response to LPS treatment was not affected by McES.
We noted a significant and dose-dependent ability of McES to neutralize IL-12p70 release by LPS-activated BMDCs (Fig 2C).
240.Rationale: While repeated stimulation–inducing 'anergy'–could reduce responsiveness, the passage shows McES suppression occurs without loss of surface activation marker expression (MHCII/CD86) and can suppress DCs even after they are activated with LPS. Those findings are inconsistent with a classical anergic state of DCs caused simply by persistent antigen stimulation; instead the data point to an active suppressive factor (McES glycoproteins) acting on cytokine output and responsiveness across pathways.

240.Option: Continuous secretion of glycoproteins that broadly suppress dendritic cell function across multiple activation pathways
Evidence: Excretory-secretory (ES) products of metacestodes are instrumental in the mitigation of IL-12 production by host DCs [2,11].
In vitro exposure of BMDCs to McES impaired their subsequent responsiveness to other pathogen products including ligands for TLRs and C-type lectins.
Biochemical analyses of M. corti tetrathyridia ES products (McES) narrowed down the immunosuppressive activity to glycoproteins.
McES broadly impair BMDC responsiveness to different stimuli
Our work also uncovered a glycoprotein nature of the mediator(s) of the DC-suppressing effect in McES.

We found that viable tetrathyridia significantly recruited cells within the peritoneum ... These results indicate that only live M. corti tetrathyridia massively attracted host CD11c+ cells up to day 7 post-infection ... our data suggest a central role for ES products of M. corti (McES) in the modulation of host DC responses in vivo.
240.Rationale: The passage directly supports this mechanism: live larvae continuously release ES products that act on DCs; McES exposure impairs DC responsiveness to many different activation ligands (TLR and C-type lectin agonists); and biochemical tests specifically narrow the active principle to glycoproteins. Together these statements make continuous secretion of glycoprotein immunomodulators that broadly suppress DC function the most plausible explanation for sustained suppression despite ongoing PAMP exposure.

240.Option: Gradual degradation of pathogen-associated molecular patterns by parasite-derived enzymes, reducing immune recognition over time
Evidence: McES broadly impair BMDC responsiveness to different stimuli
Biochemical analyses of M. corti tetrathyridia ES products (McES) narrowed down the immunosuppressive activity to glycoproteins.
We noted a significant and dose-dependent ability of McES to neutralize IL-12p70 release by LPS-activated BMDCs (Fig 2C).
240.Rationale: The passage does not present evidence that parasite-derived enzymes degrade PAMPs to reduce recognition. Instead, McES act broadly to suppress DC cytokine outputs across many stimuli (including when added after DC activation), and activity maps to secreted glycoproteins. Degradation of PAMPs would not readily explain suppression when McES are applied after DCs have already been activated by LPS; the data favor direct immunomodulatory glycoproteins rather than enzymatic removal of PAMPs.

241.Option: A negative result for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense may indicate the absence of the target subspecies, but only if the fly was captured during the peak transmission season
Evidence: For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present. To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.
241.Rationale: The passage identifies insufficient DNA quantity as the critical determinant of whether a negative result truly reflects absence of the target subspecies. It does not state that capture during a peak transmission season is the key condition for interpreting negatives. Therefore the seasonal capture condition in this option is not supported by the passage; the primary issue described is whether sufficient genetic material is present.

241.Option: The detection of T. b. brucei in a fly may lead to false-negative results for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense due to competition for PCR reagents
Evidence: Flies that were positive for PLC but negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense were considered to be infected with livestock trypanosome T. b. brucei.
The assay demonstrated high specificity with no cross-reaction with other non-target trypanosome species also transmitted by tsetse.
241.Rationale: While the passage notes that some PLC-positive-only flies were considered infected with T. b. brucei, it also emphasizes that the assay showed high specificity and no cross-reaction with non-target species. The passage does not present or imply evidence that the presence of T. b. brucei causes PCR reagent competition that yields false negatives for the human-infective subspecies. The described critical problem for negative interpretation is insufficient DNA, not reagent competition from T. b. brucei.

241.Option: The presence of T. congolense Savannah in the fly may interfere with the detection of T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense due to cross-reactivity in the PCR assay
Evidence: No non-specific amplification was seen when the assay was challenged with a range of non-target trypanosome species, namely T. congolense (Savannah, Kilifi and Forest subgroups), T. vivax, T. simiae, T. simiae Tsavo, T. godfreyi and T. grayi.
None of the flies positive for T. congolense Savannah produced any non-specific amplification.
241.Rationale: The passage directly reports that testing against non-target species including T. congolense Savannah showed no non-specific amplification, and that flies positive for T. congolense did not produce non-specific results. Thus cross-reactivity or interference from T. congolense is not supported and is not identified as the critical factor affecting interpretation of negative results.

241.Option: Insufficient DNA in the sample may prevent detection of the target subspecies, leading to unreliable negative results
Evidence: For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.

To differentiate between these two scenarios, primers have been designed to screen for other single-copy genes [21], namely the single-copy phospholipase-C (PLC) gene, present in all members of T. brucei s. l. [22–25]. Samples found to have sufficient DNA can then be screened using primers specific for T. brucei s. l. subspecies.

Of the 96 flies screened, 44.8% were positive for the universal-Trypanozoon PLC gene by HRM, indicating that they contained sufficient DNA for single-copy gene detection and therefore the detection of the SRA or TgsGP targets; if present. The remaining 55.2% of tsetse that were negative for the single-copy PLC gene can be assumed to have insufficient DNA present for the detection of the sub-species targets, we can therefore not rule these out as being negative for either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
241.Rationale: The passage repeatedly states that detection of single-copy subspecies targets depends on having sufficient genetic material and that PLC is used as an internal control to identify samples with adequate DNA. It explicitly notes that over half the flies were PLC-negative and therefore may have insufficient DNA, meaning negative subspecies results in such samples are unreliable. This directly supports the option that insufficient DNA is the critical factor affecting interpretation of negatives.

241.Option: The absence of a positive result for the PLC gene in a fly indicates that the fly is not infected with any member of the Trypanozoon group, including T. b. gambiense and T. b. rhodesiense
Evidence: Of the 96 flies screened, 44.8% were positive for the universal-Trypanozoon PLC gene by HRM, indicating that they contained sufficient DNA for single-copy gene detection and therefore the detection of the SRA or TgsGP targets; if present. The remaining 55.2% of tsetse that were negative for the single-copy PLC gene can be assumed to have insufficient DNA present for the detection of the sub-species targets, we can therefore not rule these out as being negative for either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.

For detection of these low copy genes, the presence of sufficient genetic material is crucial. A negative result may indicate the absence of the target species or simply that insufficient DNA is present.
241.Rationale: The passage makes clear that PLC negativity indicates insufficient DNA rather than definitive absence of Trypanozoon infection. It explicitly states that PLC-negative flies cannot be ruled out as negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense because low DNA quantity could prevent detection. Therefore the absence of a PLC signal does not mean the fly is uninfected with Trypanozoon species.

242.Option: The in vitro-adapted T. evansi populations exhibit reduced metabolic activity, which indirectly preserves drug sensitivity by lowering drug uptake
Evidence: Unlike T. brucei, T. evansi and T. equiperdum cannot develop in tsetse flies due to their inability to transform into the procyclic life stage. They can only survive in a mammalian host where they produce ATP exclusively through glycolysis.

Growth curves were generated for T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E and all seven in vitro adapted stocks (Fig 2). T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E and T. evansi RoTat 1.2 had the shortest Td, 7.5 ± 0.3 h-1 and 7.7 ± 0.2 h-1 respectively, and reached the highest maximum population density (MPD) of ± 3–4 x 10^6 cells ml-1, while T. evansi KETRI 2479 had a longer Td, 10.8 ± 0.2 h-1, and a lower MPD of ± 1 x 10^6 cells ml-1.

In our collection of T. evansi stocks we did not observe differences in drug sensitivity between populations that were partially or completely akinetoplast confirming earlier evidence that the presence or absence of kDNA is irrelevant within this context
242.Rationale: The passage documents active, variable in vitro growth (doubling times, high population densities), and states parasites produce ATP by glycolysis in hosts. There is no evidence that in vitro-adapted populations exhibit globally reduced metabolic activity that would lower drug uptake and thereby preserve drug sensitivity. Moreover, the authors report no association between kDNA loss and drug sensitivity, which argues against a simple metabolic-rate explanation for preserved drug sensitivity.

242.Option: The in vitro culture process induces epigenetic changes that stabilize drug sensitivity despite kDNA loss
Evidence: In T. evansi, drug resistance has been reported in several type A strains originating from Africa, Asia and Latin America [31–34].

In our collection of T. evansi stocks we did not observe differences in drug sensitivity between populations that were partially or completely akinetoplast confirming earlier evidence that the presence or absence of kDNA is irrelevant within this context
Therefore, an in vivo drug sensitivity profile of all our Trypanozoon strains against the commonly used trypanocides remains to be elucidated.
242.Rationale: The passage does not describe or provide evidence for epigenetic changes caused by in vitro culture as an explanation. Instead, the authors emphasize observed lack of correlation between kDNA status and drug sensitivity and caution that defining resistance based on the in vitro assay alone is uncertain. There are no quoted statements about epigenetic stabilization of drug sensitivity, so this option is not supported by the text.

242.Option: The absence of host immune pressure in vitro allows T. evansi to express different drug transporters, leading to altered sensitivity profiles
Evidence: In T.b. brucei, resistance against suramin and isometamidum hydrochloride has been linked to several proteins [58,59], while resistance to diamidine and melaminophenyl classes of drugs is attributed to the transporter protein TbAT1 and the aquaporin AQP2 [60–62].
The lower sensitivity to diminazene diaceturate was not caused by mutations in the T. evansi TeAT1 [63].

In our collection of T. evansi stocks we did not observe differences in drug sensitivity between populations that were partially or completely akinetoplast confirming earlier evidence that the presence or absence of kDNA is irrelevant within this context
242.Rationale: While the passage notes that certain transporters are implicated in drug resistance in related species, it does not provide evidence that removal of host immune pressure in vitro causes altered expression of drug transporters producing the observed differences. In fact, the authors found no clear relation between kDNA loss and drug sensitivity and explicitly report that TeAT1 mutations did not explain lower sensitivity. Therefore the specific mechanism proposed (immune-pressure-driven transporter expression changes in vitro) is not supported by the text.

242.Option: The presence of F1-ATP synthase γ subunit mutations allows in vitro-adapted T. evansi to maintain metabolic function and drug sensitivity despite kDNA loss
Evidence: Bloodstream forms of T. evansi, T. equiperdum and laboratory-generated T. brucei strains that have lost all or critical parts of their kDNA, can survive without kDNA due to specific single amino acid mutations in the gamma (γ) subunit of the mitochondrial F1-ATP synthase [28].

Non-vital loss of the kinetoplast is made possible by mutations in the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit of T. evansi allowing to uncouple from the Fo subunit and effectively circumventing the requirement for mitochondrial gene expression [65].
Moreover, in such genetically modified T. brucei, independence of kDNA maintenance and expression is associated with multidrug resistance [30].

In our collection of T. evansi stocks we did not observe differences in drug sensitivity between populations that were partially or completely akinetoplast confirming earlier evidence that the presence or absence of kDNA is irrelevant within this context
we confirm that the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit gene, that is not related to the VSG repertoire nor to the presence of kDNA, may become an interesting target for genotyping T. evansi stocks
242.Rationale: The passage documents that specific mutations in the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit permit survival after kDNA loss by maintaining mitochondrial/energetic function and uncoupling requirements for kDNA-encoded products. This provides a mechanistic explanation for why in vitro-adapted parasites can lose kDNA yet retain viability and similar drug-sensitivity phenotypes. The authors also highlight the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit as a genotyping target and explicitly note that they observed no relation between kDNA presence and drug sensitivity in their collection, consistent with the idea that γ-subunit mutations maintain function despite kDNA loss.

242.Option: The in vitro culture environment selects for parasites with higher intrinsic drug resistance due to prolonged exposure to suboptimal growth conditions
Evidence: In contrast, in vitro cultivation seems to reduce the diversity by selecting for only one particular type, in our study T. evansi type B.
some stocks appeared to have raised IC50 values for suramin (> 200 ng ml-1) and diminazene diaceturate (> 50 ng ml-1).

Nevertheless, defining our T. evansi stocks as either sensitive or resistant based solely on the in vitro drug sensitivity results may be too audacious, given the fact that IC50 values were determined in only one assay, the resazurin viability assay [55–57]. Therefore, an in vivo drug sensitivity profile of all our Trypanozoon strains against the commonly used trypanocides remains to be elucidated.
242.Rationale: While the passage states that in vitro cultivation can select for particular parasite types (e.g., type B) and that some in vitro stocks had higher IC50s, the authors caution against interpreting these in vitro IC50 changes as true resistance without in vivo confirmation. The option claims selection for higher intrinsic drug resistance driven by prolonged suboptimal growth conditions; the text does not provide direct evidence for culture-driven selection of resistance via suboptimal conditions, only selection of parasite type and isolated IC50 differences that are insufficient to conclude genuine resistance. Thus this option is not supported.

243.Option: Higher pathogen acquisition per feeding event during the wet season likely drives increased infection prevalence in keds despite lower overall ked abundance
Evidence: The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).

The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).

The average number of keds varied significantly with seasons (glm; F(1,699) = 7835, p < 0.001). The highest number of keds were present on camels in the middle of dry season, reaching up to 100 keds/camel. In contrast, during the wet season, the number of keds/camel reduced to an average of seven keds/camel (n = 14 camel herds)., Camel keds numbers increased gradually to an average of 10 keds/camel towards the late wet season in June-July 2018.

The presence of Anaplasma DNA in or on the camel keds was not surprising because when they bite infected camel to acquire bloodmeal, they also inadvertently ingest the accompanying blood-borne pathogens [21].
we were not able to amplify the longer ~1000-bp 16S rRNA gene sequences from keds, unlike in mouse and camels, suggesting that ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ does not multiply in the fly.
flies may only acquire fresh bacteria through subsequent infected blood meals.

The probability of Anaplasma transmission to the experimental animals by keds increased with the frequency of fly bites (OR = 0.6504 (95% CI = 0.4714–0.8373), p = 0.0004, n = 115) (Table A-C in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The passage shows camel infection prevalence remains high across seasons while the proportion of keds with detectable Anaplasma DNA rises in the wet/late-wet season despite fewer keds then. The authors emphasize that keds acquire pathogen DNA during blood-feeding and that the pathogen does not multiply in the fly, so detection in keds reflects recent ingestion rather than internal amplification. Therefore seasonal variation in the probability that a single bloodmeal contains detectable pathogen (i.e., higher acquisition per feeding event or more recent feeding on infected hosts) best explains the pattern. In other words, because presence in keds is transient and tied to bloodmeals, a higher per-feeding acquisition/detection rate during the wet season would yield a higher proportion of contaminated keds even when ked abundance is lower.

243.Option: Increased ked longevity during the wet season allows more time for pathogen acquisition and persistence, leading to higher infection rates
Evidence: The average number of keds varied significantly with seasons (glm; F(1,699) = 7835, p < 0.001). The highest number of keds were present on camels in the middle of dry season, reaching up to 100 keds/camel. In contrast, during the wet season, the number of keds/camel reduced to an average of seven keds/camel (n = 14 camel herds)., Camel keds numbers increased gradually to an average of 10 keds/camel towards the late wet season in June-July 2018.
we were not able to amplify the longer ~1000-bp 16S rRNA gene sequences from keds, unlike in mouse and camels, suggesting that ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ does not multiply in the fly.
flies may only acquire fresh bacteria through subsequent infected blood meals.
243.Rationale: The passage does not present evidence that ked longevity increases in the wet season; in fact ked numbers are lowest in the wet season. Moreover, the authors state the pathogen does not multiply in keds and appears to be transiently present after blood-feeding, so increased longevity would not explain higher detection/persistence. The text does not support longer-lived keds causing the seasonal pattern.

243.Option: The seasonal increase in pathogen prevalence in keds is due to higher pathogen load in camels during the wet season, which enhances transmission efficiency
Evidence: The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: If higher camel pathogen load during the wet season drove the observed increase in ked contamination, camel prevalence or load would be higher in the wet season. The cited camel infection rates do not show an increased prevalence in the wet season (wet = 63.9% is lower than dry = 70.3% and late wet = 77.9%). Thus the passage does not support higher camel pathogen burden in the wet season as the explanation.

243.Option: The wet season provides more favorable environmental conditions for pathogen replication within keds, increasing infection prevalence despite lower ked numbers
Evidence: we were not able to amplify the longer ~1000-bp 16S rRNA gene sequences from keds, unlike in mouse and camels, suggesting that ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ does not multiply in the fly.
flies may only acquire fresh bacteria through subsequent infected blood meals.
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The authors explicitly state they could not amplify longer Anaplasma sequences from keds and interpret this as evidence that the bacterium does not multiply within the fly, implying mechanical (not biological) transmission. Therefore increased replication within keds under wet-season conditions is inconsistent with the study findings.

243.Option: The seasonal increase in pathogen prevalence in keds is due to increased vector competence of keds during the wet season, allowing more efficient transmission per feeding event
Evidence: we were not able to amplify the longer ~1000-bp 16S rRNA gene sequences from keds, unlike in mouse and camels, suggesting that ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ does not multiply in the fly.
flies may only acquire fresh bacteria through subsequent infected blood meals.
This finding implies mechanical transmission of this Anaplasma species by the camel keds.

The probability of Anaplasma transmission to the experimental animals by keds increased with the frequency of fly bites (OR = 0.6504 (95% CI = 0.4714–0.8373), p = 0.0004, n = 115) (Table A-C in S1 Text).
243.Rationale: The study interprets keds as mechanically transmitting Anaplasma and finds no evidence of internal replication or biological amplification in the fly; 'vector competence' in the biological sense (supporting pathogen development) is not supported. The authors instead link transmission success to bite frequency and contamination from recent bloodmeals, not to seasonally increased intrinsic vector competence.

244.Option: Use a fixed cluster sampling design based on the most recent national census data, with adjustments for non-response and clustering to maintain representativeness
Evidence: Most predictors of snakebite have been identified and described using surveys, epidemiological and modelling studies, which often use sampling methods based on a fixed residence. They assume that individuals reside within an administrative unit, a geographic space, a house, or a physical structure, and that each household represents a fixed domestic unit [4,6].

sampling frames and household lists from previous censuses can rapidly become obsolete, requiring a closer-to-real-time sampling frame and household lists which better reflect the situation in which the study is conducted [62].

Epidemiological studies involving nomadic populations need to factor in the fluidity in geographic localisation and household definition that are characteristic of mobile pastoralist populations. Highly mobile populations require specific considerations in selecting or creating sampling frames and sampling units for epidemiological research. Snakebite risk has a strong spatial component and using census-based sampling frames would be inappropriate in nomadic populations.
244.Rationale: The passage explicitly warns that census-based, fixed-residence sampling frames become rapidly obsolete and are inappropriate for nomadic populations. A fixed cluster design anchored to recent national census data would therefore risk selection bias from population mobility and would not directly address the mobility problem described.

244.Option: Use high-resolution satellite imagery to create a dynamic spatial sampling frame and adjust for household movement by reassigning relocated households to their original mapped locations
Evidence: We created a spatial sampling frame by directly identifying and creating data points for all features that looked like a household within the 2018 level 1 global administrative unit boundary for Samburu County, using high-resolution satellite images.
We identified 50,707 homesteads from the whole of Samburu, and these were all manually digitised and converted into datapoints using ArcGIS.

We used a modified grid-based GIS sampling method to select our primary sampling units [55]. Our sampling unit in this study was the grid, with each grid serving as a proxy for the smallest administrative unit.
We accounted for this by assuming that their originally mapped location was their true place of residence and relocating these inhabitants to their original grids.

Our sampling strategy can be conducted entirely digitally using open-source satellite imaging and GIS software. It provides recent information on clusters and households in the study area, and there are fewer bureaucratic delays or bottlenecks that would have been associated with gaining access or permission to census household lists.
244.Rationale: The passage describes this exact approach as the novel method used to address mobility: creating a spatial sampling frame from high-resolution satellite images, using grids as sampling units, navigating to GPS-located households, and reassigning relocated households (within a radius) to their original mapped locations. These features directly tackle selection bias from population movement while preserving representativeness.

244.Option: Apply a waterpoint-based sampling approach to identify clusters of households near water sources, assuming these locations are stable and representative of the mobile population
Evidence: Approaches such as the waterpoint approach may correlate with snake population density, especially in arid and semi-arid regions.
The sampling methods that have historically been used to study nomadic populations are at risk of selection bias and could result in biased disease estimates [22, 25–28, 52].
Approaches such as the camp approach, enumeration area and social structure approach are likely to capture only a fraction of the true mobile populations.
244.Rationale: While proximity to waterpoints may correlate with snake density, the passage cautions that such targeted approaches (camp, enumeration area, social-structure approaches) can capture only a fraction of mobile populations and are at risk of selection bias. The authors do not endorse a waterpoint-based method as the best way to maintain representativeness in highly mobile pastoralists.

244.Option: Conduct a longitudinal cohort study with repeated household visits to track population movements and update the sampling frame over time
Evidence: The third approach involves conducting a survey of the entire population and collecting data on the disease of interest, often via a pre-established disease surveillance system. This approach provides the closest approximation to the true population estimate but is limited by prohibitive setting up and running costs.

An example is the Kilifi Health and Demographic Surveillance System that was established in Kilifi, Kenya in 2000 and which routinely collects data on births, pregnancies, population movements, and deaths.

Data collection was carried out between December 2019 and March 2020, three months after completing the geospatial cluster and household mapping. In highly mobile populations, such delays could reduce the chances of identifying households in their originally mapped locations.
244.Rationale: A longitudinal cohort with repeated visits could indeed address mobility and maintain representativeness in principle, but the passage frames such surveillance-based approaches as the most resource-intensive and often cost-prohibitive. The authors present the satellite/GIS grid approach as a feasible method; they do not present a longitudinal cohort as the preferred or most direct solution in the context of resource-limited settings.

244.Option: Use a social structure-based sampling method that selects clusters based on kinship networks and tribal affiliations to ensure cultural alignment and reduce non-response
Evidence: Approaches such as the camp approach, enumeration area and social structure approach are likely to capture only a fraction of the true mobile populations.
The sampling methods that have historically been used to study nomadic populations are at risk of selection bias and could result in biased disease estimates [22, 25–28, 52].

In mobile nomadic populations, households are fluid and some households split into two and one group takes some assets that include livestock and migrate in search of fertile grazing lands. These movements are harder to predict, track and account for and they can reduce the power of a fixed sample of households [8].
244.Rationale: The passage warns that social structure-based approaches are likely to miss portions of the mobile population and are prone to selection bias. While culturally aligned sampling might reduce some non-response, it does not directly solve the core problem of geographic fluidity and representativeness in highly mobile pastoralists, and thus is not presented as the best choice.

245.Option: The presence of larval food in breeding sites as a proxy for nutrient availability
Evidence: When larval food was present in rearing water during the nanoparticle exposure (Fig 6A), AgCNP and AgNO3 had significantly greater larvicidal impact on 3rd instar Ae. aegypti larvae in comparison to the control group (25.2±2.39% and 81.5±2.14% larvae dead, respectively, p-values <0.001).
When larval food was withheld for either (b) 24h or (c) 48h following treatment with AgCNPs, the observed larvicidal activity was even greater compared to control (p-value <0.001).
we observed that the absence of food during the treatments accentuated the nanoceria larvicidal activity.

Given the findings of this study that the presence of food (i.e., organic matter) reduced the nanoparticle larvicidal activity, nanoceria such as AgCNPs would likely best be employed for larval control in environments with low organic material and/or in situations where repeated applications are feasible.
245.Rationale: The passage reports experiments directly manipulating the presence/absence of larval food and shows larvicidal activity of AgCNPs was much greater when food was withheld. The authors explicitly equate larval food with organic matter and conclude that presence of food (organic matter) reduced nanoparticle activity and therefore should guide deployment decisions (e.g., use in low-organic environments). Thus, presence of larval food (nutrient/organic matter level) is a critical factor influencing deployment strategy.

245.Option: The frequency of reapplication required to maintain larvicidal efficacy
Evidence: Given the findings of this study that the presence of food (i.e., organic matter) reduced the nanoparticle larvicidal activity, nanoceria such as AgCNPs would likely best be employed for larval control in environments with low organic material and/or in situations where repeated applications are feasible.
245.Rationale: The passage mentions repeated applications as a possible consideration ('...and/or in situations where repeated applications are feasible.'), but it presents this as an alternative or secondary mitigation strategy rather than the primary determinant. The authors emphasize organic matter (presence of larval food) as the main environmental factor that reduces efficacy and thus most critically influences where and how to deploy the larvicide. Therefore, while reapplication frequency is relevant operationally, it is not described as the primary factor dictating deployment strategy in the passage.

245.Option: The concentration of organic matter in breeding sites
Evidence: we observed that the absence of food during the treatments accentuated the nanoceria larvicidal activity.

Given the findings of this study that the presence of food (i.e., organic matter) reduced the nanoparticle larvicidal activity, nanoceria such as AgCNPs would likely best be employed for larval control in environments with low organic material and/or in situations where repeated applications are feasible.

These data indicate an approximate four orders of magnitude increase in bioactivity for AgCNP over the Ag+ positive control. Additionally, the preliminary ICP-MS results indicate that free Ag+ in AgCNP suspensions is negligible. Together, these data suggest that the observed biological activity of AgCNP against Ae. aegypti does not result from free Ag+.
245.Rationale: The passage explicitly links organic matter in breeding water (operationalized as larval food in experiments) to reduced larvicidal efficacy and concludes that AgCNPs are best used where organic material is low. That makes the concentration of organic matter a critical determinant of deployment suitability in urban breeding sites. The extra quote on mechanism underlines that the observed patterns are specific to AgCNP behavior rather than simple free-silver toxicity, reinforcing that environmental organic load modifies nanoparticle activity.

245.Option: The ability of nanoparticles to penetrate the gut epithelium of mosquito larvae
Evidence: Confocal fluorescence images (Fig 5C–5E) show bright fluorescence emanating from the gut of a larva exposed to CNPs.
As AgCNP has no fluorescence, larvae exposed to AgCNPs do not show fluorescence in the gut.

From our studies, we have shown similar kill rates in AgCNP-exposed conditions to those with higher titers of free Ag+. Additionally, through fluorescence microscopy, we were able to confirm the presence of nanoparticles within mosquitoes (larvae).
245.Rationale: The passage documents nanoparticle presence/uptake in larval guts, so penetration/uptake is demonstrated. However, the question asks what most critically influences choice of deployment strategy in urban environments; the authors emphasize environmental organic matter (food) as the key factor affecting efficacy and thus deployment decisions. Gut penetration is mechanistically relevant but not presented as the primary operational determinant for where/how to deploy the larvicide.

245.Option: The batch-to-batch consistency of silver doping in nanoparticle formulations
Evidence: The reduction in egg clutch following AgCNPs exposure was variable suggesting some AgCNP batch-to-batch dependence.

Because of the surface properties of nanoceria and the nature of the oxidative dissolution-based chemical treatment/processing, it is possible that nanoparticle suspensions may have variance in dispersion, resulting in varying dosage of bioactive AgCNPs.

Subsequent studies will seek to define and deconvolute dissolution kinetics of silver nanoparticles, nanoceria-incorporated silver micro-phases, and ceria surface etching processes to optimize the chemical treatment/processing towards producing homogenous, monodisperse solutions.
245.Rationale: The authors acknowledge variability and batch-to-batch dependence in biological effects, which is important for product quality and scale-up. However, when discussing deployment strategy in urban environments the passage highlights the environmental context—specifically organic matter in breeding sites—as the critical factor determining where AgCNPs would be most effective. Batch consistency affects manufacturing and reliability but is not identified in the text as the principal factor guiding deployment decisions in the field.

246.Option: Recovery of vegetation after natural disasters and land-use changes favoring secondary growth created ideal habitats for chigger mites and their rodent hosts in northern mountainous areas
Evidence: One of possible factors leading to the expansion of the epidemic zones and the shift of major foci is that secondary vegetation areas where chiggers and small mammal hosts are more abundant arose [13,45].

The mountainous area of Fujian province suffers from flood and other natural disasters and the ecological environment was gradually destroyed at first until Fujian provincial government has placed a more prominent strategic position in strengthening the capacity to mitigate the growing threat of natural disasters in the ecological circumstances [46]. Nevertheless, the generation of secondary vegetation, which helps provide moist terrain essential for chigger survival, was very beneficial to the formation of tsutsugamushi foci especially when the original vegetation of the mountainous foci recently recovered from natural hazards [45].

Crop sown areas have decreased significantly and converting farmland to forest has become common in Fujian province [47,48]. Subsequently, partial abandonment of agricultural cultivation creates favorable habitats for trombiculid mites because crop remnants and natural vegetative regrowth provide the food and shelter necessary for proliferation of rodent hosts.
246.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the shift and expansion of foci to the emergence of secondary vegetation and land-use changes, especially in mountainous areas recovering from natural disasters. These ecological changes produce moist terrain, vegetative regrowth and abandoned farmland that increase habitat suitability for trombiculid mites and their rodent hosts, providing a direct mechanistic explanation for the observed northward shift in high-risk counties.

246.Option: Improved diagnostic accuracy and reporting in northern counties led to higher case detection rates, creating the appearance of a shift in high-risk areas
Evidence: Despite our research brings important new knowledge on the epidemiological characteristics of scrub typhus in Fujian province, this study was subject to two specific limitations when interpreting our findings. Firstly, bias inevitably exists in this study due to the passive nature of the surveillance system [13]. Data quality may be influenced by the availability of diagnostic technology and underreporting.

Only cases who sought medical care in hospital were reported to local CDC and used for analysis, therefore the under report of clinical cases or subclinical infection who recovered without healthcare were missed from the current analysis [37].
