There is no evidence in the passage that genital colonization is the principal reservoir in this community, and systemic azithromycin would be expected to treat mucosal carriage. The authors specifically point to survival on healthy non-genital skin as a plausible source of ongoing infection.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the gut of infected individuals, which is not affected by systemic azithromycin and allows for re-infection via fecal-oral transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.

We detected T. p. pertenue positive results in standard and real-time PCR for 4 flies and only in real-time PCR for the 5th fly. All of the T. p. pertenue-positive flies also carried detectable H. ducreyi DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage describes transmission as occurring via non-intact skin contact and discusses skin carriage, flies, and fomites (linens) as potential reservoirs. There is no mention or evidence of a gut reservoir or fecal-oral transmission for H. ducreyi in the text, so this option is unsupported.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through a reservoir in the respiratory tract of asymptomatic individuals, which is not targeted by systemic azithromycin and allows for airborne transmission
Evidence: Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
We previously reported that approximately 2% of the total population in Lihir Island and 7% of the children aged 5–15 years had ulcers containing H. ducreyi DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage consistently frames H. ducreyi transmission as skin-associated (contact of wounds with infectious lesions) and explores skin carriage, flies, and linens as reservoirs. There is no data or discussion of respiratory tract carriage or airborne spread, so this explanation is not supported by the provided evidence.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through direct transmission from symptomatic cases via skin-to-skin contact, which remains frequent despite mass drug administration
Evidence: Although H. ducreyi cutaneous ulcer strains might be exclusively transmitted through contact of wounds with infectious lesions, it seems unlikely that this mode of transmission could account for the high prevalence of infection or persistence after MDA.
Notably, people from households without an ulcer case had positivity rates that were similar to people with an H. ducreyi ulcer case in the household.
253.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that transmission solely via contact with symptomatic ulcers is unlikely to explain the high prevalence or persistence after MDA. Additionally, similar positivity rates in households without ulcer cases argue against ongoing persistence being driven only by symptomatic-case-to-contact transmission.

253.Option: H. ducreyi persists in the community through asymptomatic skin carriage and environmental reservoirs such as flies and contaminated linens, which are not effectively targeted by systemic azithromycin
Evidence: Asymptomatic colonization of exposed skin may serve as a reservoir for transmission to family members, classmates, or playmates.
We used PCR techniques to demonstrate that 20% of asymptomatic children living in H. ducreyi-endemic communities had detectable H. ducreyi DNA on the skin.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA
If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
253.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of asymptomatic skin carriage (20% PCR-positive), frequent detection of H. ducreyi DNA on flies and bed linens, and raises the specific concern that systemic azithromycin may not reach the outer skin surface to clear such carriage. The authors propose that skin colonization plus environmental contamination (flies, fomites) could explain continued presence of infection after MDA, making this option the best-supported explanation.

254.Option: Exposure to bat bodily fluids through contact with surfaces where bats have roosted
Evidence: Bats can harbor zoonotic pathogens that may be shed in excreta and bodily fluids (eg., feces, urine, saliva, blood, etc.) [19–20].
bats often roost in livestock enclosures in this region and may deposit feces or bodily fluids in spaces frequently used by domestic animals [43].

Bats will roost frequently in buildings simultaneously used by humans across Africa [28–31], including the focus area [32], and this region of Kenya is forecasted as a hotspot for zoonotic pathogen emergence where surveillance and mitigation efforts are needed [14].
254.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that bats shed pathogens in bodily fluids and that bats roost in buildings where they can deposit feces or bodily fluids on surfaces used by people or animals. Contact with these contaminated surfaces constitutes indirect environmental contamination and thus directly increases the likelihood of indirect pathogen transmission to humans.

254.Option: Consumption of fruit pulp contaminated by bat saliva in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Many frugivorous bat species chew and eject saliva-covered fruit pulp below their roosts, which domestic animals may then consume and become exposed to shed pathogens [44].
Indeed, it is thought that Nipah and Hendra virus, both paramyxoviruses with high human mortality, emerged in pigs and horses in this fashion, respectively [25].
254.Rationale: The passage describes saliva-contaminated fruit pulp as a pathway by which domestic animals (e.g., pigs, horses) can become exposed, and gives examples of animal amplifying hosts (Nipah, Hendra). It does not provide direct evidence that humans in shared indoor built environments commonly consume such contaminated fruit pulp, so this option is not supported as the most direct pathway for indirect environmental transmission to humans in the study context.

254.Option: Contact with bat feces and urine in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
Indirect interactions were the most reported of the two interaction types;

Bat excreta reported in these indirect interactions, mostly feces, can contain pathogens shed by bats, including coronaviruses, rotaviruses, and paramyxoviruses that are viral families of human health concern [20].
254.Rationale: The passage directly identifies contact with bat feces and urine as the predominant form of indirect contact reported by respondents and states that bat excreta can contain pathogens of human health concern. Therefore, contact with feces and urine in shared indoor environments most directly increases the likelihood of indirect pathogen transmission.

254.Option: Inhalation of bat fecal dust from contaminated surfaces in shared indoor environments
Evidence: Fungal pathogens, like Histoplasma capsulatum, the causative agent of histoplasmosis, may also be inhaled from bat fecal dust and have infected people living in buildings with bat roosts in Africa [40].
Indirect interactions were the most reported of the two interaction types;
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98).
254.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that pathogens (e.g., Histoplasma) can be inhaled from bat fecal dust and that people living in buildings with bat roosts have been infected this way. Inhalation of contaminated fecal dust is therefore a direct form of indirect environmental transmission in shared built environments.

254.Option: Direct contact with live bats during attempts to remove them from buildings
Evidence: Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).
Attempts to remove bats were common and frequently led to direct contact with bats that could facilitate pathogen exposure.
254.Rationale: While the passage documents that attempts to remove bats often lead to direct contact and that such direct interactions can facilitate pathogen exposure, the question asks about indirect pathogen transmission through environmental contamination. Direct contact with live bats is a direct transmission route, not an indirect environmental contamination pathway, so this option does not answer the question as asked.

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate exposure and SIR to predict the number of new infections per day, allowing for direct inference of the proportion of diarrhea cases attributable to waterborne transmission without considering the probability of symptomatic illness
Evidence: To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2), assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).
first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child.

If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries.
255.Rationale: The option is incorrect because, while the combined QMRA+SIR framework does use QMRA to generate daily infection probabilities and the SIR to follow infections over time, the study explicitly models the probability that an infection produces symptomatic (diarrheic) illness (P(s)). The quoted text shows QMRA assigns P(i) and that conditional on infection the model applies a separate modeled probability of symptomatic illness (P(s)). Thus it is false to say the model infers diarrhea burden 'without considering the probability of symptomatic illness.'

255.Option: The combined model uses QMRA to estimate pathogen concentration and SIR to simulate the spread of infection, allowing for direct comparison of model outputs to observed diarrhea rates without adjusting for household-level water treatment behaviors
Evidence: To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2), assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).

To account for the effects of boiling water (the only point of use treatment method used by the study population) on parasite viability, we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling ... and used household survey data collected from study households (n = 355) to estimate the fraction of households that boiled their drinking water (9% and 16% of deep and shallow tubewell users, respectively, reported boiling; see Table 1, B, boiling rates) (Appendix B.5 in Supporting Information).

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season ... and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: This option is incorrect because, although the combined model does use QMRA to characterize contamination and SIR to simulate infections and compare model outputs to observed diarrhea, the study explicitly accounts for household-level water treatment (boiling) in both the QMRA exposure calculation and the SIR assignments. The quoted passages show households were assigned boiling behavior (with empirically-derived boiling rates) and boiling was incorporated into the dose and viability calculations; therefore the claim that the comparison is done 'without adjusting for household-level water treatment' is false.

255.Option: The combined model simulates how daily exposure leads to cumulative illness, recovery, and immunity, enabling estimation of time-varying diarrhea prevalence that can be compared to observed surveillance data
Evidence: To estimate the waterborne child diarrhea disease burden in the study population attributable to Cryptosporidium and Giardia protozoal contamination of tubewell drinking water, we used an SIR model coupled with the QMRA model, above, to longitudinally track the daily infection status of each individual child in our study population over time (Fig 2), assigning each to a tubewell type (TW) and to boiling (B) before drinking as a function of the household fractions for each practice in the population (see TW and B in Table 1).

SIR models track the daily infectious status of individuals in three states; susceptible to acquiring an infection when exposed (S), infected from an exposure event (I), and recovered from an infection event with some degree of immunity (R).

we ran on a daily time step for 98 days each year (allowing for eight days of model warm-up to establish baseline distributions of SIR states), such that for a given day, each child was in one of the three states, with each child’s state being independent of other children.
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season (mid-June to mid-September 2012 and 2013) for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.

Thus, to properly compare our QMRA-SIR modeled daily average prevalence over the monsoon period simulation (mid-June to mid-September) to the Trial’s observed 7-day recall period prevalence on a single day during each monsoon season, we sampled the simulated child population and their daily diarrhea prevalence time series over the 90-day monsoon period as they were sampled in the Trial’s surveillance.
255.Rationale: This option correctly describes the added value of integrating QMRA with an SIR model. QMRA provides daily infection probabilities from exposure; the SIR model then simulates transitions S→I→R (including temporary immunity) on a daily time step so daily exposures accumulate into illness episodes, recovery, and short-term immunity. The study explicitly used these time-varying simulated prevalences and sampled the time series to compare modeled 7-day recall prevalence to observed surveillance data, exactly as the option states.

255.Option: The combined model applies QMRA to estimate daily infection risk and SIR to calculate the average duration of illness, enabling estimation of the total number of diarrhea episodes per child over the study period
Evidence: To estimate the probability of a symptomatic case of cryptosporidiosis or giardiasis from drinking water in children < 5 years old, and to estimate prevalence of diarrhea over time in the study population of 3,835 children in study communities exposed to contaminated tubewell drinking water, we used a quantitative microbial risk assessment (QMRA) approach [23] (Fig 1) coupled with a susceptible-infected-recovered (SIR) model (Fig 2).
first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child.

Not all cases of symptomatic Cryptosporidium and Giardia infection persist for the same duration of time. To model this variability, we used a duration of illness parameter (D) (see Table 1) ... From these studies, the mean duration of cryptosporidiosis and giardiasis is 10 (IQR 3–14) and 11 (IQR 6–14) days respectively. Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Using results from the SIR model, we estimated the diarrheal disease burden associated with drinking tubewell water during each monsoon season ... for the population of < 5 children in the Sanitation Trial (population burden) and compared modeling estimates to observed levels of child diarrhea measured in the Sanitation Trial over the same periods.
255.Rationale: This option is correct in substance. QMRA provides per-child daily infection risk (P(i)) from exposure; the SIR model incorporates an illness duration parameter (D) to determine how long individuals remain in the infected state before recovery and temporary immunity. By tracking individuals daily through SIR, the model produces counts of infection episodes over the study period (which can be translated to total number of diarrhea episodes per child or population burden). The quoted text shows QMRA assigns daily infection probabilities, and the SIR component explicitly models illness duration and recovery, enabling estimation of episode counts over time.

255.Option: The combined model estimates the probability of infection from a single exposure event and directly calculates the total number of symptomatic cases over the study period without accounting for individual immunity or reinfection
Evidence: first the QMRA model was used to assign a probability of infection, P(i), modeled as a Bernoulli variable (1 = infection, 0 = no infection), to each child.

If P(i) = 1, the probability that the infection resulted in a diarrheic symptom (P(s)) was modeled based on published studies of the diarrhea morbidity ratio for children diagnosed with Cryptosporidium [45, 47] or Giardia [9, 46] in developing countries.
Once the illness event ended a child was assumed to transition into the recovered state and be temporarily immune to reinfection for a duration of seven days post-infection [17].

Only children in the susceptible state could transition into the infected state and only if their infection resulted in diarrheic symptoms (i.e. both P(i) and P(s) equal to 1 in the model); susceptible children who were infected without symptoms (P(i) = 1 but P(s) = 0) remained in the susceptible state, while children in the infected and recovered states were protected from infection in the model.
255.Rationale: This option is incorrect because the combined model does more than estimate infection from a single exposure event and it explicitly accounts for individual immunity and reinfection dynamics. The SIR model simulates daily exposures over the monsoon period, includes a modeled probability that infections are symptomatic (P(s)), and assigns recovered individuals temporary immunity (seven days) before they can be reinfected. The quoted passages directly contradict the statement that immunity or reinfection are not accounted for.

256.Option: Combined facial defects involving the cheek, nose, and upper lip, as these complex lesions are associated with the highest risk of postoperative complications and poor aesthetic outcomes
Evidence: Most defects resulting from Noma involve the lateral and anterolateral aspects of the face and are often combined with severe functional deficits [19].
Nearly 56% (n = 91) of the total facial defects reported in the overall medical records are combined in form.
256.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that defects are often combined and that combined lesions are 'often combined with severe functional deficits.' Combined defects therefore imply more extensive anatomical involvement and associated functional impairment, which supports an interpretation that combined lesions predict poorer long-term prognosis (functional and aesthetic) among survivors. While the passage does not use the exact phrase 'highest risk of postoperative complications,' the documented association of combined lesions with severe functional deficits and the description of extensive multi-region involvement support the option's core claim that combined complex lesions indicate worse outcomes.

256.Option: Complete loss of the nose or lips, as these anatomical defects are associated with the most severe facial disfigurement and social stigma
Evidence: complete absence of facial tissues such as the nose, upper lip, and lower lip has been reported.

Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].
Noma survivals are often left with devastating orofacial defects [23].

As a result, there is little awareness of the dire need to eradicate this disease, notably in East Africa. Noma oftentimes occurs in impoverished individuals, particularly in malnourished individuals. The condition is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. The findings of this study shed light on the severity of Noma in Ethiopia. Most of the surveyed patients suffered from extensive Noma-induced facial disfigurements, which exposed them to eminent functional limitations, social discrimination, and negative psycho-social effects.
256.Rationale: The passage documents complete absence of key facial structures (nose, upper lip, lower lip) and repeatedly links survivors' facial deformities to 'devastating orofacial defects' and social consequences, including 'social discrimination' and rejection. Complete loss of the nose or lips represents maximal disfigurement and is therefore strongly suggestive of poor psychosocial prognosis and major functional/aesthetic impairment in survivors, supporting this option.

256.Option: Severe trismus, as it leads to impaired mastication and swallowing, which can perpetuate malnutrition and stunting in survivors
Evidence: Most survivors present with prominent facial deformities, trismus or ankylosis of the temporomandibular joint, and extensive muscle and skin contracture, which leads to the difficulty of opening and closing of the mouth, thereby, trouble in mastication and swallowing, oral incontinence, and speech difficulties [12].
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth [13].

A retrospective study that investigated the long-term results of trismus release among Noma patients reported a poor prognosis. This study showed that trismus is one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals [41].
Out of the 163 medical records reviewed, 74.9% (n = 122) have reported grade 1 to grade 4 levels of trismus
256.Rationale: The passage directly links trismus (jaw ankylosis) to critical functional deficits—difficulty opening the mouth, impaired mastication and swallowing—that can perpetuate or worsen malnutrition and stunting. It further cites a study reporting poor prognosis after trismus and calls trismus 'one of the most disabling sequelae among Noma survivals.' These explicit statements make severe trismus a particularly strong clinical indicator of poor long-term prognosis despite surviving the acute disease.

256.Option: Grade-4 facial tissue damage (75–100% anatomical damage), as it represents the most severe structural destruction and is associated with the highest mortality risk
Evidence: The NOIPTUS classifies facial anatomical tissue damage into four classes. The classes are graded from 1 to 4, depending on the severity of facial tissue damage. Grade-1, Grade-2, Grade-3 and Grade-4 imply 0–25%, 25–50%, 50–75%, and 75–100% facial anatomical damages respectively.

All the anatomical landmarks reported in the reviewed medical records have shown minor to severe tissue damage... Accordingly, 42.3% (n = 69) of the total medical records reviewed have reported Grade-2 facial anatomical damages. Furthermore, 30.7% (n = 50), 19% (n = 31), and 8% (n = 13) have reported Grade-3, Grade-1, and Grade-4 tissue damages respectively.
The acute phase of the disease has a high mortality, and the evolution is exceptionally rapid, causing the loss of soft and bony facial tissues within days [17,18].
256.Rationale: While Grade-4 denotes the most extensive anatomical destruction (75–100% damage), the passage does not explicitly state that Grade-4 is associated with the highest mortality risk among survivors; mortality is described generally as high during the acute phase, not tied specifically to the NOIPTUS grade post-acute. The option's additional claim that Grade-4 is 'associated with the highest mortality risk' is not supported by a direct quote. Therefore, although Grade-4 indicates severe structural damage and likely poor outcomes, the specific assertion about highest mortality risk is not substantiated in the text, so the option is incorrect as worded.

256.Option: The presence of multiple missing teeth, as this indicates extensive dental destruction and is linked to poor oral hygiene and malnutrition
Evidence: Out of the total 163 patients reviewed, 7.4% (n = 12) had no dental 266 information. On the other hand, 25.8% (n = 42) are reported to have healthy teeth, while the remaining 66.9% (n = 109) have reported a varying number of missing teeth.
The number of missing teeth ranges from 1 to 19 in patients affected by the disease.
Furthermore, out of the 109 medical records, 14 have reported different levels of disoriented teeth.
Oral incompetence, speech difficulties, post-wound healing ankylosis, and dental or malalignment are reported as a sequela of Noma [34].
256.Rationale: The passage documents that missing teeth are common among survivors and describes dental involvement as part of sequelae. However, it does not identify multiple missing teeth specifically as the clinical feature that most strongly predicts a poor prognosis. The text emphasizes functional impairments (trismus, mastication/swallowing problems), severe tissue loss, and psychosocial consequences more strongly than dental loss alone. Therefore, although missing teeth contribute to morbidity, the passage does not support the claim that this feature most strongly suggests poor prognosis compared with features like severe trismus or major tissue loss.

257.Option: High levels of synonymous polymorphism across multiple lineages of the pathogen, indicating relaxed selection on the gene
Evidence: Positive selection is inferred when ω [13] (the ratio of non-synonymous (dN) to synonymous (dS) mutations between species) exceeds 1.

A comparison of these virulence factors with housekeeping genes in P. brasiliensis showed that a higher proportion of virulence genes evolve under positive selection (37.5% vs. 0%), suggesting that at least some of these genes have an adaptive role.
257.Rationale: Immune escape is expected to produce excess non-synonymous change (dN) in antigenic regions rather than primarily synonymous polymorphism. The passage frames detection of immune-driven evolution in terms of dN/dS > 1 and excess non-synonymous substitutions in virulence genes; high synonymous polymorphism indicates neutrality or relaxed constraint and therefore does not strongly point to immune escape.

257.Option: A significant excess of non-synonymous substitutions in the gene's coding region compared to synonymous substitutions, detected using the M-K test
Evidence: Deviations from this model form the basis for various tests of natural selection, such as the HKA test [17], and the M-K test [18].

M-K tests.M-K tests [18] between the P. brasiliensis sensu lato and H. capsulatum, using the aligned regions previously sequenced as well as sequences retrieved from GenBank, were calculated using the DNASP analysis program [42].
M-K tests were significant for p27, cdc42 and fks (p27: P = 0.043594; cdc42: P = 0.000993; fks: P = 0.000017; Table 3) when H. capsulatum was used as an outgroup.
257.Rationale: An excess of non-synonymous substitutions relative to synonymous ones (as revealed by a significant McDonald–Kreitman test) is a standard signature of adaptive (positive) selection. The passage explicitly uses M-K tests to identify genes (p27, cdc42, fks) under positive selection, consistent with immune-driven adaptation of virulence genes.

257.Option: Persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms in antigenic regions predicted to be recognized by host immune cells
Evidence: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].
Using the Bayesian Empirical Bayes (BEB) analysis, 19 sites with a posterior probability greater than 95% of having a greater than 1 value were identified.

In order to predict potential antigenic determinants for HLA recognition, we used the program SYPFETHI [44]. As illustrated in Figure 3, seven of the sites under positive selection were located as potential epitopes as predicted with SYFPEITHI.
257.Rationale: Persistent (long-lived) non-synonymous polymorphisms in regions predicted to be antigenic (epitopes) are strong evidence that host immune pressures have maintained diversity (balancing or diversifying selection) to escape recognition. The passage gives gp43 as exactly this pattern: many persistent non-synonymous polymorphisms coincide with predicted epitopes and are interpreted as maintained by selection.

257.Option: A high dN/dS ratio in a gene with no known antigenic function, suggesting general adaptive evolution unrelated to host immunity
Evidence: Human-fungal interactions provide a privileged system to study the impact of natural selection on the genome of fungal pathogens... especially in the genes encoding pathogenesis-related proteins which are likely to evolve in response to selective pressure from the host's immune system.

A comparison of these virulence factors with housekeeping genes in P. brasiliensis showed that a higher proportion of virulence genes evolve under positive selection (37.5% vs. 0%), suggesting that at least some of these genes have an adaptive role.
257.Rationale: A high dN/dS ratio indicates adaptive evolution, but when the gene has no known antigenic or virulence function, that signal does not specifically implicate immune escape. The passage emphasizes that immune-driven evolution is most plausibly inferred in pathogenesis- or antigen-related genes; positive selection in non-antigenic genes is less direct evidence for immune escape.

257.Option: A recent fixation of a single non-synonymous mutation in a virulence gene across all pathogen lineages, indicating strong directional selection
Evidence: Positive directional selection occurs when successive amino acid changes make a protein better adapted in a particular biological context, and as a result the changes will tend to be fixed in future lineages.
Conversely, the TRMCAs for cdc42, p27 and fks were significantly lower than the other genes as is expected if a gene is under positive directional selection.

The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection [51].
257.Rationale: Fixation of a single non-synonymous change indicates directional selection but does not specifically point to immune escape (it could reflect adaptation to other pressures). Immune escape is often indicated more strongly by persistent polymorphism in antigenic sites (balancing/diversifying selection). While directional sweeps can be immune-driven, fixation across all lineages is a weaker, less specific signal of immune escape than the sustained, antigenic-site polymorphism described above.

258.Option: The use of blood samples collected close to the time of exposure reduced misclassification of pre-existing immunity, enhancing the accuracy of the exposure-outcome relationship
Evidence: Two major strengths of this study design likely explain why a significant association between NTs and protection from infection was observed in contrast to previous prospective cohort studies.

Whereas earlier cohort studies typically analyzed NTs in blood samples collected up to six months or more prior to the incident DENV infection, our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.
the day 0 blood sample was presumed to reflect the neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure or infection.
the two week interval was likely sufficient to ensure that day 0 blood collections occurred before exposure.
258.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify the proximity of day 0 blood collection to the time of exposure (within two weeks) as a major strength that minimized confounding from changing antibody kinetics and ensured that measured PRNT titers reflected pre-exposure immunity. This reduced misclassification of the exposure (pre-existing NTs) and therefore increased ability to detect an association despite small sample size.

258.Option: The use of PCR-positive cases and PCR-negative controls from the same household ensured that both groups were exposed to the same viral strain, reducing confounding by serotype variation
Evidence: In order to create a 'non-susceptible' comparison group, we identified contact subjects from the two studies who lived in the same house as another subject who was DENV positive on day 0, but were themselves DENV negative by nested PCR and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 15 (KPSII study), or by NS1 ELISA and IgM ELISA on both days 0 and 14 (DEVOL study).
These DENV negative 'non-susceptible' subjects were presumed to have a high likelihood of exposure to DENV during the cluster investigation, yet still did not become infected.
In both the KPSII and DEVOL studies, the exposure serotype for all 'non-susceptible' subjects was presumed to be the same as the infecting serotype for the index case from the same cluster.
our selection criteria for 'non-susceptible' subjects maximized the likelihood of such exposure.
258.Rationale: Selecting PCR-positive and PCR-negative subjects from the same household/cluster and presuming the same exposure serotype for non-susceptible subjects reduced heterogeneity in exposure (including serotype) between groups. That tighter control of exposure/serotype likely reduced confounding and increased the study's ability to detect an association, and the authors note this choice maximized the likelihood of exposure.

258.Option: The use of a two-week window for blood collection allowed for the detection of both pre-existing and newly formed neutralizing antibodies, increasing the likelihood of identifying protective immunity
Evidence: our study provided blood samples from within two weeks prior to virus exposure, thus minimizing the confounding factor of variations in antibody kinetics.
the day 0 blood sample was presumed to reflect the neutralizing antibody status just prior to exposure or infection.
All five subjects were PCR negative at day 0, and DENV IgM ELISA negative at days 0 and 14. No data or blood was collected after day 14.
No data or blood was collected after day 15.
258.Rationale: The study used the two-week window to obtain a pre-exposure (day 0) sample that reflected existing neutralizing antibodies prior to infection; it was not intended to detect newly formed antibodies. Indeed, the authors emphasize day 0 samples reflect pre-exposure status and that no later (post-exposure) samples were used for PRNT in the analysis. Thus the claim that the two-week window was used to detect newly formed antibodies (and that this increased detection of protective immunity) is not supported by the passage.

258.Option: The use of multiple DENV serotypes in the PRNT assay allowed for the detection of cross-protective immune responses, increasing the sensitivity of the association analysis
Evidence: day 0 blood samples were tested by an in-house plaque reduction neutralization test (PRNT) using all four DENV serotypes and JEV as previously described [24], [25].

Homotypic NTs were more strongly associated with protection than heterotypic NTs except in the case of DENV-2 infections, in which pre-existing heterotypic DENV-4 NTs were also positively associated with protection against DENV-2.
Although the samples in our study were unique and informative, the number that met testing criteria was small, limiting the power of our analysis.
258.Rationale: While the PRNT used multiple serotypes and the authors examined both homotypic and heterotypic responses (noting some heterotypic association for DENV-2), the passage identifies the timing of blood collection and household exposure selection as the major strengths enabling detection of an association despite small numbers. The use of multiple serotypes helped characterize the nature of protection (homotypic vs heterotypic) but is not presented as the primary reason the study detected a significant association despite the small sample size.

258.Option: The inclusion of both children and adults in the study increased the diversity of immune responses, improving the generalizability of the association between neutralizing antibodies and protection
Evidence: Because of the small numbers, we used data from two different geographic cluster studies over multiple years. The inclusion of both adults and children with likely differing immunological backgrounds may have affected our findings for the DENV-2 analysis, possibly accounting for the higher DENV-2 NTs associated with protection.
In addition, as mentioned earlier, we could not be certain that 'non-susceptible' subjects had, in fact, been exposed to DENV infection.

Of 1599 contact subjects from 50 positive and 53 negative geographic cluster investigations in the KPSII study [18], [30], 12 subjects were found to be DENV PCR positive at day 15 and, therefore, considered to be 'susceptible'... Twenty-two subjects from KPSII were selected as 'non-susceptible'... Of 740 contact subjects from 195 DENV-2 geographic cluster investigations in the DEVOL study, five subjects were found to be DENV-2 PCR positive at day 14 and, therefore, considered as 'susceptible.'
258.Rationale: The authors actually note that including adults (in DEVOL) versus only children (in KPSII) could have introduced confounding and affected interpretation, particularly for DENV-2, rather than being described as a strength that increased ability to detect associations. Therefore, the inclusion of both age groups is not presented as the key factor that enabled detection; if anything, it is a potential limitation.

259.Option: In high-transmission settings, the high proportion of sub-microscopic infections indicates that routine surveillance using light microscopy is sufficient to detect most P. vivax cases and guide interventions effectively
Evidence: Similar to the epidemiology of SM P. falciparum reported by Okell et al.[6], a negative relationship was identified between the relative proportion of SM P. vivax and LM P. vivax prevalence in 31 cross-sectional surveys.

SM P. vivax constitutes between 1.5% and 100% of all P. vivax infections detected by PCR (average 69.5%) in these settings, and this proportion is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in these settings.
without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought.
259.Rationale: The option claims that in high-transmission settings routine LM is sufficient because of a "high proportion of sub-microscopic infections"—this is self-contradictory and unsupported. The passage states there is a negative relationship between LM prevalence and the proportion of SM infections (i.e. SM proportion is higher in low-transmission settings), and overall a large fraction of P. vivax infections (average ~69.5%) are sub-microscopic and would be missed by LM. Therefore it is incorrect to assert LM is sufficient to detect most P. vivax cases; even where LM prevalence is higher, substantial infections may still be undetected and PCR or more sensitive methods are needed to fully ascertain burden for elimination planning.

259.Option: Since sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are asymptomatic, they do not contribute to transmission and can be safely ignored in elimination planning
Evidence: The SM P. vivax gametocytes shown to be able to successfully infect mosquitoes [8], [9].

These reports demonstrate LM's limitation in detecting infections with low parasite densities; levels well below the threshold for symptomatic malaria, but of sufficient density to enable transmission of parasites to mosquitoes [3], [4].
This results in a potentially large group of people carrying parasites that have the potential to transmit P. vivax [8], [9] and maintain the transmission cycle.

Based on findings of six surveys, between 89% and 100% (average 97.5%) of SM P. vivax infected subjects were asymptomatic at the time of survey ... If these individuals remain asymptomatic then they would not be identified by either active or passive case detection based on LM or RDTs with a similar sensitivity to LM.
259.Rationale: Although most SM P. vivax infections are asymptomatic (the passage cites averages ~97%), the text explicitly states that SM infections can carry gametocytes capable of infecting mosquitoes and can maintain transmission and relapses. Therefore they do contribute to transmission and cannot be ignored in elimination planning; the passage argues these undetected reservoirs present a major challenge and require more sensitive diagnostics or alternative strategies.

259.Option: Because SM P. vivax infections are more common in adults, surveillance should focus on adult populations to detect the majority of transmissible infections
Evidence: SM P. falciparum is more common in adults, in areas with low transmission intensities and in chronic infections [6].

Of the 16 surveys that analysed the relationship of SM P. vivax with age, eight reported the relative proportion of SM P. vivax infection and host age ... found no significant association ... while five publications (eight surveys) reported that the relative proportion of SM P. vivax increased with age.

This difference may be related to the transmission intensities in study regions, however a comparison of the average LM P. vivax prevalence between these two groups did not show a significant difference.
259.Rationale: The passage does not provide a consistent finding that SM P. vivax is more common in adults; results were mixed (about half the surveys report an association with age, half do not). Given this inconsistency and the passage's discussion of transmission intensity and other factors, it is not justified to focus surveillance solely on adults to detect the majority of transmissible infections. Surveillance strategies should account for heterogeneous evidence and transmission context rather than assume adults are the primary reservoir.

259.Option: The negative relationship between LM prevalence and SM proportion suggests that increasing the sensitivity of light microscopy would reduce the proportion of sub-microscopic infections across all transmission settings
Evidence: a negative relationship was identified between the relative proportion of SM P. vivax and LM P. vivax prevalence in 31 cross-sectional surveys.
Many factors can contribute to the occurrence and level of SM P. vivax. These include technical, parasite and host factors.
From a technical point of view, the quality of microscopy is an important factor that can contribute to the level of LM P. vivax, and thus SM P. vivax.
The prevalence and relative proportion of SM P. vivax would increase if the microscopist is relatively poor at detecting parasites ...

However, SM P. vivax infections are not detectable using LM or RDTs, as such these infected individuals would not receive any anti-malarial therapy for P. vivax blood or liver stages and thus continue to transmit P. vivax.
259.Rationale: While better microscopy quality can reduce false negatives due to technician error, the passage emphasizes that SM infections are defined by parasite densities below the detection threshold of LM. Technical improvements have limits: many SM infections are inherently below LM detection and are detectable only by molecular methods. The text explicitly notes multiple non-technical contributors (parasite biology, host immunity) and states SM infections are not detectable using LM or RDTs. Therefore increasing LM sensitivity alone would not reliably eliminate the SM reservoir across all settings; more sensitive diagnostics (e.g., PCR or comparable methods) are needed, particularly in low-transmission/ elimination settings.

259.Option: In low-transmission settings, surveillance based on light microscopy will substantially underestimate the true burden of P. vivax and fail to identify a major reservoir of transmissible infections, necessitating more sensitive diagnostics for elimination success
Evidence: a negative relationship was identified between the relative proportion of SM P. vivax and LM P. vivax prevalence in 31 cross-sectional surveys.

This suggests that a relatively larger proportion of P. vivax infections are SM in areas with low transmission intensity compared to areas with high transmission intensity, although SM P. vivax is prevalent in both type of settings.

SM P. vivax constitutes between 1.5% and 100% of all P. vivax infections detected by PCR (average 69.5%) in these settings ... without PCR or other comparably sensitive methods, an average of 69% of P. vivax infections would be undetected hence antimalarial treatment will usually not be sought.
some form of mass screening using PCR-based or comparably sensitive methods and radical cure approach would be required to identify and treat all SM P. vivax infected subjects.
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the proportion of SM infections rises as LM prevalence falls, meaning in low-transmission settings LM-based surveillance misses a larger share of infections. It quantifies substantial under-detection (average ~69% undetected without PCR) and argues that more sensitive diagnostics (PCR or comparable) and mass screening/radical cure strategies would be required to identify and treat SM carriers to achieve elimination. Therefore this option accurately reflects the passage's conclusions and implications for surveillance and intervention planning.

260.Option: The lack of integration of NTDs into the core curriculum's transmission-based classification may result in students failing to recognize the shared epidemiological patterns among these diseases
Evidence: The epidemiology of communicable diseases course for medical and nursing students is divided into general epidemiology (infectious cycle, pattern of disease occurrence, and general measures of prevention and control of communicable diseases) and specific epidemiology.
The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.
260.Rationale: The passage states the course is organized by transmission categories and that most NTDs appear distributed across those categories, yet the label 'NTDs' is not used. If NTDs are taught only as individual diseases within transmission-based modules without being integrated or framed as a coherent group, students may not recognize their shared epidemiological patterns or the concept of 'NTDs' as a distinct public health priority. The quotes show the courses include the diseases but do not explicitly present them under the unifying NTD concept, supporting this explanation for low awareness.

260.Option: The absence of a unifying conceptual framework for NTDs in the curriculum may prevent students from recognizing these diseases as a distinct public health priority despite exposure to individual diseases

Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.
260.Rationale: The passage documents that although individual NTDs are covered, the curriculum does not explicitly present them under the unifying label 'NTDs' and only a small fraction of students learned about NTDs from the epidemiology course. Absence of a conceptual framework that groups these diseases and emphasizes their public health importance would plausibly prevent students from recognizing them as a distinct priority despite exposure to individual diseases, which matches the study's findings.

260.Option: The low awareness among students may be due to the fact that NTDs are not covered in the clinical training phase of medical education, which limits their real-world exposure
Evidence: Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

260.Rationale: The passage attributes the students' low awareness to how NTDs are presented (or not labeled) in the epidemiology course and to insufficient coverage of prevention and control measures in that course. It does not provide evidence that the clinical training phase omitted NTDs or that lack of clinical exposure is the main cause. Because the study focuses on the epidemiology course content and explicitly notes course omissions (term not mentioned, poor coverage), the claim that clinical-phase omission explains the low awareness is not supported by the provided text.

260.Option: The lack of emphasis on NTDs in the epidemiology course may stem from the absence of standardized national guidelines for teaching these diseases in medical education
Evidence: Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This gap of epidemiological knowledge about NTDs is consistent with the content analysis of the epidemiology course which indicates that the term “NTDs” was not mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specification.

Although the Ministry of Health in Egypt applies control programs for NTDs by offering anti-helminthic drugs free of charge and deworming campaign [25], this study showed that only 5% of the included medical and nursing students knew specific activities for prevention and control of these disease in Egypt.
260.Rationale: The passage documents curricular omissions and low student awareness but does not state that standardized national teaching guidelines are absent. It even notes existing national control programs by the Ministry of Health, which suggests national-level activity on NTDs (though not necessarily curricula guidance). Because the passage does not present evidence that lack of standardized national teaching guidelines is the cause, this option is not supported by the text.

260.Option: The limited exposure to NTDs in the epidemiology course may be due to the course's focus on high-profile diseases such as HIV and tuberculosis, which overshadow less common but endemic conditions
Evidence: The specific epidemiology of infectious diseases is divided according to the 4 main modes of transmission, namely: droplet, foodborne, contact, and arthropod borne infections.
Most of the NTDs are included in the course such as schistosomiasis, lymphatic filariasis, leishmaniasis, rabies, soil transmitted helminthiasis (STH) as ascariasis and hookworm infections.
Neither the term “neglected tropical disease” nor the abbreviation “NTDs” was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.

This may be attributed to the fact that specific prevention and control measures for NTDs and the efforts exerted nationally or globally to combat them are not covered properly in the epidemiology course they studied.
260.Rationale: The passage does not mention HIV, tuberculosis, or a curricular emphasis on high-profile diseases overshadowing NTDs. Instead, it indicates that many NTDs are included in the course content but that the unifying term and specific prevention/control measures are not adequately covered. Therefore, attributing the gap to competition from high-profile diseases is not supported by the provided text.

261.Option: Perform the assay within 1 hour of drug addition to minimize the impact of time-dependent changes in parasite motility and drug metabolism
Evidence: there is an instantaneous effect at 1min that remains with similar activity values at least for one hour for all the benznidazole concentrations.

These assays allowed the selection of the conditions of the method using VDRL plates. We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x104 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.
However the activity of the Biospeckle pattern at 1.25μg/mL is similar at all the tested times in spite of the fact that desiccation affects each time curve differently.
261.Rationale: The passage documents an instantaneous effect at 1 minute that persists for at least one hour, but it does not prescribe restricting assays to within 1 hour as the required control for excluding confounding physical processes. The main physical confounder discussed is desiccation/evaporation (which can alter drug concentration), and the authors in fact used and validated longer observation periods (up to a maximum of 204 minutes) while monitoring desiccation. Therefore recommending a strict <1 hour limit is not the central control advocated by the passage and is not necessary per the authors' validated conditions.

261.Option: Limit the assay duration to less than 200 minutes to prevent desiccation from altering drug concentration and confounding the interpretation of parasite activity
Evidence: The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways.

The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes, indicating that as was seen in Figs 2, 3 and 4 (Effect of Parasite Quantity), the Biospeckle pattern under the conditions of this work, reveals the total number of parasites within the well of the VDRL plate.
However, it can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies desiccation (evaporation) of the well liquid as a physical process that can 'interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.' It also shows the Biospeckle measure remains stable 'up to around 200 minutes.' Limiting assay duration to under ~200 minutes is therefore a directly supported way to avoid desiccation-induced changes in effective drug concentration and thus prevent this physical confounder from biasing interpretation of drug effects.

261.Option: Use a sealed chamber or humidified environment to prevent evaporation and maintain constant liquid volume throughout the assay
Evidence: The desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells and its implications on the activity of the Biospeckle pattern was evaluated in several ways.
The results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes...
However, it can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: The passage identifies evaporation/desiccation of the air-exposed wells as a confounding physical process that can increase drug concentration over time. Controlling evaporation (for example by using a sealed chamber or humidified environment to maintain constant liquid volume) is a direct procedural approach to prevent that physical confounder and ensure observed changes in parasite activity reflect the drug's effect rather than changes in concentration caused by evaporation.

261.Option: Standardize the initial volume of liquid in each well to ensure consistent baseline conditions across all experimental groups
Evidence: Unless otherwise stated, all the assays were performed with an initial volume of 100μL.

These assays allowed the selection of the conditions of the method using VDRL plates. We selected the following conditions: 100μL in the test well, parasites in exponential phase, 4x104 parasites per well, a maximum time of evaluation of the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 204 minutes.
In the experiment of variable density 5 different test wells had 100μL of LIT medium containing from 0 to 4 x103 parasites per well.
261.Rationale: The authors emphasize using a consistent initial well volume (100 μL) as part of their standardized assay conditions. Varying initial volume would change parasite numbers in the object plane, liquid height, and the effective concentration of drug (and how desiccation affects concentration), thus introducing a physical confounder. Standardizing initial volume is therefore necessary to ensure differences in Biospeckle activity are attributable to drug effects rather than baseline volume-related physical variation.

261.Option: Match the concentration of benznidazole across all wells to ensure that differences in activity are due to parasite viability rather than drug concentration variation
Evidence: With these conditions, the effect of varying amounts of benznidazole on the activity of the parasites was tested and compared to the pattern obtained from the LIT medium with benznidazole and without parasites.
In this work the concentration of benznidazole is used in a range of 0.0195 to 20μg/mL (from 0.075 to 76.8μM) and the activity is detected in a minimum time of 1 minute.
However, it can be assumed that desiccation may interfere by increasing drug concentration at the longer times.
261.Rationale: While ensuring intended drug concentrations are applied and consistent within each experimental group is basic experimental practice, the question asks about controlling a confounding physical process. The passage highlights evaporation/desiccation (which can alter the effective concentration over time) as the key physical confounder. Simply 'matching' nominal benznidazole concentrations across wells does not control for evaporation-driven concentration changes during the assay. Thus this option does not address the specific physical confounder emphasized in the text.

262.Option: Living in homes with earthen/mud floors, which reflects poor housing conditions and limits access to protective footwear
Evidence: A large percentage of the population (79.4%) stay in homes with earthen/mud floors [20].
Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
Variables retained in the model apart from age are indicative of low economic status. This corroborates the finding of [5, 10].
262.Rationale: While earthen/mud floors are clearly a structural condition associated with tungiasis (passage quotes showing high prevalence of earthen floors and listing them among aggravating factors), the option asserts an additional claim—'limits access to protective footwear'—that is not stated in the passage. The passage treats earthen floors primarily as an environmental/structural risk factor rather than a direct measure of the interplay between individual behavior and structural constraints. Therefore this option is not the most direct reflection of the interplay asked for.

262.Option: Inconsistent use of closed footwear, driven by economic constraints and shaped by high child population density
Evidence: Factors that have been reported to aggravate the problem include earthen and dusty floors, non regular use of closed footwear, common resting place outside the house and pigs on the compound [10].
In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).
They are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies non-regular/occasional use of closed footwear as a risk factor and links age/children's behavior (playing barefoot) with higher exposure. It also emphasizes low economic status as a driver of risk. Thus inconsistent footwear use captures an individual behavioral choice (wearing shoes) that is plausibly constrained by structural/economic conditions and directly increases exposure to fleas—making it the clearest marker of interplay between behavior and structure in this study.

262.Option: Age ≤14 years, which reflects both developmental vulnerability and high exposure due to outdoor play in endemic areas
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2).

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99) (Table 3).
They are more exposed due to them playing barefoot [10] and also getting reinfected at school in endemic areas.
262.Rationale: Age ≤14 is an important risk factor and reflects behavioral exposure (children play barefoot), but age itself is a demographic attribute rather than a direct measure of the interaction between personal behaviors and structural constraints. The option is partly correct in capturing exposure patterns, but it does not most directly represent the behavior-structure interplay the question asks for (which is better captured by a behavior that is shaped by poverty, such as footwear use).

262.Option: Lack of electricity and lack of a toilet, which are indicators of low economic status and contribute to poor hygiene practices
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
Variables retained in the model apart from age are indicative of low economic status. This corroborates the finding of [5, 10].
This study found a prevalence of tungiasis at 21.5% among the study participants.
262.Rationale: Lack of electricity and toilets are clear structural indicators of poverty associated with tungiasis, and the passage links such variables to low economic status. However, they are structural conditions rather than individual behaviors; they do not by themselves most directly capture the interplay between behavior and structure in the way that a behavior constrained by poverty (e.g., not wearing shoes) does. Thus this option is not the best single choice for the question.

262.Option: Bathing without soap, which reflects poor hygiene and is associated with low literacy and lack of health education
Evidence: In the bivariate analysis, the age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis (Table 2). Others were being illiterate or low literacy level, bathing without soap, common resting place inside the house, having rats in the compound, lack of electricity and lack of a toilet.
Bathing without soap is a factor that has been elucidated in previous studies as well [7, 13].
Almost a half, 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on the transmission of tungiasis.
262.Rationale: Bathing without soap is an individual hygiene behavior linked in the passage to low literacy/knowledge and is plausibly shaped by structural factors (education, poverty). However, compared with footwear use, bathing without soap is less clearly tied to direct exposure to the flea lifecycle in soil. The question asks for the factor that most directly reflects the interplay between behavior and structural conditions that increase vulnerability; inconsistent use of closed footwear is a more direct and transmission-relevant example, so bathing without soap is not the best single answer.

263.Option: The delayed peak in dengue cases is caused by a lag in public health reporting, with more cases being diagnosed and recorded in October due to increased surveillance during the peak transmission season
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
The study used public health surveillance data routinely collected through the Ministry of Health notifiable diseases surveillance program.
263.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes the July–October timing to climatic seasonality and a biological lag (extrinsic incubation period) rather than to surveillance artefacts. While the data come from routine surveillance, the authors link the timing to wet/hot periods and mosquito-driven dynamics. Therefore a reporting lag/increased surveillance in October is not the primary explanation provided by the passage.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases in October is due to increased susceptibility among children who return to school after summer break, leading to higher transmission in densely populated urban areas
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).
The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
263.Rationale: The passage points to climate-driven increases in mosquito abundance and an extrinsic incubation lag as the reason for the seasonal peak. Moreover, most notified cases are in adults (>15 years), not children, so increased transmission from school return among children is not supported by the data presented.

263.Option: The peak in dengue cases occurs in October because of increased human mobility during the autumn festival season, which enhances transmission in urban centers
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
All local climatic variables show a statistically significant association with dengue in the 1-year mode (figure 5, left panel).
263.Rationale: The passage attributes the seasonal peak to climatic drivers (temperature, rainfall) and mosquito biology with observed time lags, and documents significant associations between climate variables and dengue. It does not provide evidence that increased human mobility for festivals is the main driver of the October peak, so this option is not supported by the passage.

263.Option: The seasonal peak in dengue cases is due to a higher proportion of secondary dengue infections in October, which are more likely to result in severe clinical illness and thus be reported
Evidence: In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].

As dengue disease severity is modulated by age, prior dengue exposure history, and the infecting dengue serotype, the incidence of clinically apparent dengue cases is unlikely to have a linear relationship with transmission intensity [38], [39], [53].
263.Rationale: Although prior exposure and secondary infections affect severity, the passage directly links the timing of the seasonal peak to climate-driven increases in vector abundance and the extrinsic incubation period. The authors do not present evidence that October specifically has a higher proportion of secondary infections leading to reporting bias. Thus the climate/vector explanation is the primary, supported mechanism in the passage.

263.Option: Seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall enhance mosquito abundance and virus transmission, leading to a delayed peak in cases due to the extrinsic incubation period
Evidence: The annual outbreaks typically begin around July, peak in October, and then decrease towards the end of the year, corresponding with the wet and hot periods (figure 1b).

In Hanoi dengue transmission demonstrates clear annual cycles that are associated with a lag of around two months with seasonal increases in mean temperatures and rainfall. This pattern reflects climate driven changes in vector abundance, survival, and biting frequency; and the time it takes for a newly infected mosquito to become infectious to humans (the extrinsic incubation period) [17]–[20].
On average, a delay of 1–2 months was observed for rainfall and mean temperature whereas a 4–5 months delay was seen for humidity and wind velocity (figure 5, right panel).
263.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that seasonal increases in temperature and rainfall are associated with dengue's annual cycle and that a lag (about 1–2 months) arises from climate-driven changes in mosquito abundance, behavior, and the extrinsic incubation period. This directly explains the observed July–October peak in cases in a seasonal, urban setting.

264.Option: The morphological similarity between Amphimerus sp. and Haplorchis pumilio metacercariae allows for cross-protection in human hosts, reducing the need for frequent exposure to maintain infection
Evidence: Eggs from the families Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae measuring <30 μm in length have been labeled as small trematode eggs because their morphological similarities prevent detailed discrimination via light microscopic diagnosis [17–19].

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys...
264.Rationale: The passage documents morphological similarity between Amphimerus and heterophyid metacercariae and eggs, but it does not provide any evidence of immunological cross-protection in humans. The quoted text describes diagnostic indistinguishability and the need for molecular methods, not cross-immunity. There is no mention of immunity or ‘cross-protection’ that would reduce exposure requirements; thus the statement that morphological similarity confers cross-protection is unsupported and biologically implausible based on the passage.

264.Option: The widespread presence of morphologically indistinguishable metacercariae in multiple edible fish species undermines accurate detection and enables sustained transmission even in low-fish-consumption communities
Evidence: We found metacercariae of Amphimerus sp. in all fish species recognized by local people as edible, belonging to three different families (i.e., Characidae, Cichlidae, and Lebiasinidae, Fig 3 and Table 2).

From the fish collected, we removed scales, fins, viscera, and heads, and used only muscle tissue to search for metacercariae, since the same procedure is applied when local people prepare fish for consumption.

Because we can barely rely on morphology to distinguish eggs or metacercariae from members of the Opisthorchiidae and Heterophyidae families, we recommend to implement the PCR-RFLP molecular approach presented in this study in epidemiological surveys aiming to identify fish species involved in Amphimerus sp. life cycle

264.Rationale: The passage shows that multiple commonly eaten fish species are infected with Amphimerus metacercariae and that morphological similarity with other small trematode metacercariae/eggs makes detection and species discrimination difficult. It also notes a high human prevalence in a community where fish are not the primary daily protein source. Together, these facts support that widespread, hard-to-detect infection in many edible fish species can sustain human transmission even where overall fish consumption is lower, because occasional consumption or local practices still expose people and routine microscopy may miss infections.

264.Option: The presence of multiple fish species as second intermediate hosts increases the likelihood of zoonotic spillover, enabling transmission even in the absence of definitive animal hosts
Evidence: Briefly, eggs arriving into freshwater water sources (e.g., streams, lakes, ponds) are ingested by prosobranch molluscs, the first intermediate hosts ... Rediae produce cercariae, which escape the snail and search for fish, the second intermediate host, where they mature to become metacercariae, the infective stage. The vertebrate definitive host (e.g., mammals) ingests the metacercariae in fish bodies, and the larva inside the metacercarial cyst wall escapes and migrates from the intestines to the biliary ducts where it matures to become adult parasite and starts laying eggs [5,10,24,26].
We isolated 18 and 45 adult flukes from biliary ducts of the dog and cat livers respectively.
264.Rationale: While the passage supports that multiple fish species acting as second intermediate hosts increase opportunities for zoonotic transmission, it explicitly describes the requirement of vertebrate definitive hosts (humans, dogs, cats) to ingest metacercariae and produce eggs that re-enter freshwater and continue the cycle. Thus transmission cannot be maintained 'in the absence of definitive animal hosts.' The statement's first half is true (multiple fish species raise spillover risk) but the claim that this enables transmission without definitive hosts contradicts the described life cycle and is therefore incorrect.

264.Option: The consumption of raw fish, even in small quantities, ensures consistent exposure to infective metacercariae and maintains transmission regardless of fish species diversity
Evidence: In this area, use of fish as a daily food source is secondary. Primary protein sources include seafood and livestock products; however, when freshwater fish are eaten, they are prepared as raw fish with salt and lemon juice (Fig 5).

Consumption of infected fish to sustain or complement diets for the populations in Esmeraldas and Manabí, respectively, is an ideal means of maintaining the life cycle of Amphimerus sp. and potentially other trematodes [13,45].

Recognition of these local fish as a source of metacercariae of liver (i.e., Amphimerus sp.) and intestinal flukes (e.g., H. pumilio) could prompt development of public health education campaigns regarding safe food practices and sanitation...
264.Rationale: The passage documents that even in communities where fish are not the main daily protein source, freshwater fish are sometimes consumed raw (e.g., with salt and lemon), and that consumption of infected fish is an 'ideal means of maintaining the life cycle.' This supports the idea that even occasional/raw consumption (i.e., small quantities) can provide consistent exposure to infective metacercariae and sustain human infection, making dietary practice (raw preparation) a critical factor irrespective of how many fish species are infected.

264.Option: The high prevalence of small trematode eggs in human populations is primarily due to the use of contaminated water sources for fish collection, which sustains the life cycle across communities
Evidence: Briefly, eggs arriving into freshwater water sources (e.g., streams, lakes, ponds) are ingested by prosobranch molluscs, the first intermediate hosts ... Rediae produce cercariae, which escape the snail and search for fish, the second intermediate host, where they mature to become metacercariae, the infective stage.
