Thus the option misstates the model assumption.

290.Option: The model assumes that the biological and epidemiological processes driving infection intensity transitions remain stable over time, enabling long-term projections even with incomplete or imprecise data
Evidence: The Markov model assumes the “stationarity” of the TP, in the sense that if the intervention remains the same (e.g. albendazole is administered once a year) the TPMS will also remain constant throughout the duration of the control programme.

For example, Fig 1 shows that if 37% of heavy-intensity hookworm infections become light-intensity infections after the first year of preventive chemotherapy, the same proportion (37%) of the remaining heavy-intensity infections will become light-intensity infections after the second year of the intervention.

The model tested here assumes the “stationarity” of the TP and it is possible that without this assumption the predictions, especially the one at long term, could be further improved. We consider the prediction provided by the tool to be sufficiently precise to facilitate the planning and evaluation of programme performance, and concluded that the “stationarity” of the TP is an acceptable assumption for predicting STH changes in prevalence.
290.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies 'stationarity' of transition probabilities as the key assumption: if interventions remain the same, the TPMS remain constant over time. That is equivalent to assuming the biological and epidemiological processes that govern transitions between intensity states are stable, which permits projection of future prevalence even when detailed intensity or follow-up data are limited.

290.Option: The model assumes that the diagnostic accuracy of Kato-Katz improves over time as control programs gain experience, allowing for more reliable long-term trend estimation
Evidence: In all the datasets used in the present study (i.e. those from the studies used to calculate the model parameters and those from control programmes used to evaluate the prediction capacity of the model), Kato-Katz was the only method used to collect parasitological data. We understand the poor precision of the diagnostic methods traditionally used to monitor STH control programmes (i.e. Kato-Katz) especially when intensities of infection are low [1].

We recognize that the prevalence would have been higher if measured with a more precise method than Kato-Katz, but we wanted to provide programme managers with a tool capable of interpreting the imperfect data collected during routine control activities.
290.Rationale: The passage notes Kato-Katz is the only method used in datasets and explicitly states its poor precision; it does not claim that diagnostic accuracy improves over time. On the contrary, the authors acknowledge imperfect diagnostics and design the model to work with those imperfect data. Therefore the option is not supported by the text.

290.Option: The model assumes that reinfection rates decline over time due to reduced environmental contamination, which allows for stable long-term projections despite limited follow-up data
Evidence: Additionally, we assume that the pharmacological interventions conducted in only a part of the population (i.e. school-aged children) do not reduce significantly the faecal contamination of the soil in the short term.

During the development of the model we observed that when the entire population is treated (as is the case when albendazole is distributed for control of lymphatic filariasis), the predicted prevalence was consistently higher than the observed values; this discrepancy between the two predictions was increasing at the increase of the baseline prevalence. We interpret this discrepancy as a progressive decrease of reinfection due to the reduction of soil contamination consequent to administration of anthelminthics to the entire population. We adjusted for this factor by including a standard modification, dependent on the initial level of prevalence: if baseline prevalence is > 80% the standard modification becomes active after 5 years of follow up; for lower initial prevalence the standard modification becomes active after 3–4 years of follow up.
290.Rationale: While the authors do describe an observed decline in reinfection (and include a specific adjustment) when the entire population is treated, this is an empirical correction for that special scenario, not the general underlying assumption used to project trends with limited data. In fact they explicitly assume that partial-population interventions (typical school-based programs) do not significantly reduce environmental contamination in the short term. Thus the blanket statement that the model assumes reinfection rates decline over time is not accurate as a general model assumption.

290.Option: The model assumes that the effectiveness of anthelminthic drugs increases over time due to cumulative exposure, enabling stable long-term projections despite data limitations
Evidence: The two simplified models (SM1 and SM2) do not calculate specific TPMS for the control programme but apply standard TPMS that are calculated from programmes using a similar intervention; for example, to estimate the change in STH prevalence in Myanmar, the TPMS developed from the data from Viet Nam (TPMS1) were applied because the intervention in the two countries is the same (albendazole two rounds per year); to predict the change in prevalence in Cambodia the TPMS developed from the data from Zanzibar were applied (TPMS4).

For the other five types of intervention (TPMS 5,6,7,8 and 10) we could not measure the TP directly but we estimated the impact of the intervention applying two assumptions: When ivermectin with albendazole is provided during the first round of treatment and albendazole or mebendazole is provided during the second round of treatment the impact is similar to TPMS1/TPMS2 with a marginal additional efficacy against hookworm infection [13]. The impact of diethylcarbamazine on STH is considered absent [1].
290.Rationale: The passage does not state any assumption that drug effectiveness increases over time with cumulative exposure. Instead the model applies standard TPMS derived from program data and treats differences in drug combinations explicitly (including assuming no impact for some drugs). There is no support for the claim that the model assumes improving drug effectiveness over time; therefore this option is incorrect.

291.Option: Including open-ended questions to capture patient-reported symptoms and improve the comprehensiveness of the assessment
Evidence: Thus our OMI incorporated two open-ended questions “How does your skin problem affect you?” and “What are the three worst aspects of having your skin problem?”
The aim was to assess the patient’s perspectives with a view to adapting the tool or developing a relevant patient reported outcome measure encompassing HRQoL assessment in the future.
We adopted this pragmatic approach in the first instance to try and ensure the patient’s perspective was recorded on paper.
291.Rationale: The cited text shows the purpose of including open-ended questions was to capture patient perspectives and HRQoL, not to standardize clinician measurement. While useful for comprehensiveness and patient-centered assessment, open-ended responses do not directly reduce measurement bias from inconsistent interpretation of clinical signs across evaluators and may themselves introduce variability in interpretation.

291.Option: Excluding erythema and pain from assessment because they are not universally present in all LCL patients
Evidence: Since erythema was not appreciated as a reliable or reproducible sign in skin of colour and pain was not considered as a universal symptom of LCL by the stakeholders, there was agreement not to measure erythema and pain.
erythema was not considered a reliable measure of treatment efficacy and scars and pigmentation were noted to be important sequelae which were considered in a separate domain.
291.Rationale: Removing erythema and pain addresses concerns about unreliable or non-universal signs and can reduce sources of heterogeneity. However, exclusion of variables is an indirect strategy and does not most directly address inter-rater measurement bias. It eliminates certain problematic items but does not provide standardized measurement procedures or objective scoring to harmonize interpretation across evaluators.

291.Option: Using a nominal group technique to achieve consensus on the inclusion of clinical signs in the outcome measure
Evidence: A subsequent judgment process was then undertaken adopting the nominal group technique (NGT) to reach a final consensual agreement on the set of Core Outcome Domains.
The NGT approach was selected to ensure that there was an opportunity to share clinical experience and secure clear consensus among stakeholders.
291.Rationale: The nominal group technique was used to reach consensus about what to measure (which domains and signs to include). That helps ensure agreement on content, but it does not itself standardize how clinicians will measure those signs in the field. Therefore NGT reduces disagreement about inclusion but is not the most direct method to prevent measurement bias from inconsistent interpretation across different evaluators.

291.Option: Developing a new palpability score to quantify induration and ulcer edge characteristics
Evidence: Furthermore, a new palpability score was developed to assess the edge of the ulcerated and the overall induration of the non-ulcerated lesions.
a newly developed subjective assessment (a palpability score of 0,3,6,9) was described for both non-ulcerated and ulcerated lesions (0 = flat, 9 = severely raised) (Figs 2 and 3).
Schematic images and/or photos alongside descriptions were used to standardize the assessment.
291.Rationale: Creating a defined palpability score (with explicit numeric anchors 0,3,6,9 and descriptive anchors such as '0 = flat, 9 = severely raised') provides an objective, structured method for assessing induration and ulcer edges. Quantified scoring reduces subjective judgement and helps harmonize assessments across different evaluators, directly addressing measurement bias from inconsistent interpretation.

291.Option: Incorporating visual and textual standardization in the case report form to reduce inter-rater variability in clinical sign assessment
Evidence: Written as well as diagrammatic and photographic instructions with clinical examples were provided for measuring and assessing LCL to minimize any potential ambiguities.
Schematic images and/or photos alongside descriptions were used to standardize the assessment.

The next two pages of the CRF contained instructions for the user on individual items. Written as well as diagrammatic and photographic instructions with clinical examples were provided for measuring and assessing LCL to minimize any potential ambiguities.
291.Rationale: Providing clear written instructions, diagrams and photographic examples in the CRF directly targets inter-rater variability by standardizing how clinicians measure and interpret clinical signs. This methodological choice most directly reduces measurement bias due to inconsistent interpretation across evaluators by minimizing ambiguity and ensuring a common reference for assessments.

292.Option: Misunderstanding of the Ebola laboratory test process led individuals to delay seeking care until they were certain of their diagnosis
Evidence: When asked what they knew about the laboratory Ebola test, nearly half of participants said they did not know anything.
Most did not have a clear idea of how the testing process worked.
Some also misunderstood the need to re-test for Ebola.

Participants also reported they would ensure that a fever was due to Ebola, rather than another illness, before seeking care. In the interim they would try to self-medicate or visit local trusted health care facilities.
292.Rationale: The passage documents misunderstandings about the laboratory test and shows participants would try to be sure a fever was Ebola before seeking care. However, the authors explicitly link the observed delay (two to three days) to a generalized reluctance to seek care and fear of the response system (e.g., ambulance removal, chlorine), rather than primarily to confusion about laboratory procedures. Thus, while test misunderstandings may have contributed somewhat, they are not presented as the most likely primary cause of the delay.

292.Option: Fear that chlorine spray in ambulances could be lethal contributed to delayed care-seeking despite high intentions to use the response system
Evidence: This time lag may be explained by a reluctance to seek care.
In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
Some expressed fears that by calling they would never see the person again: "With 117, the ambulance takes people away and sometimes they never come back … I think this is what frightens people."
Some participants feared that the chlorine used by ambulance workers was toxic and potentially lethal.

Chlorine suffocates patients. If a patient has been vomiting and toileting then in the end he or she is suffocated with chlorine, because when they spray the patient in the vehicle they usually close the door where the patient will be placed and no air enters the cabin.

One day the team came to pick up a female patient … She was strong enough to walk from the house to the ambulance but when they arrived at the treatment center the lady became so weak and couldn’t walk because of the chlorine sprayed in the ambulance. It was the chlorine. She didn’t have Ebola. The chlorine weakened her.
292.Rationale: The passage directly links reluctance to seek care with fear of the response system and provides multiple participant quotes specifically describing fear that chlorine sprayed by ambulance workers could suffocate or kill patients. Those fears are given as explanations for why people delayed calling the hotline or seeking hospitalization despite stated intentions to use the system, making this factor the most likely contributor.

292.Option: Lack of access to transportation to health facilities resulted in delayed hospitalization despite high intentions to seek care
Evidence: By January 2015, there were eleven laboratories nationwide, and every district had a laboratory and a contact tracing team to monitor quarantined individuals [19].
A national hotline (call 117), established on August 5, 2014, responded to reports of suspected Ebola cases and reports of any deaths, and also provided information about Ebola to citizens [20].
Ambulance workers dressed in personal protective equipment (PPE) arrived to transport suspected cases to holding centers.
In response to the question “what would you do if you had a fever?” very few participants said they would call 117 right away.
292.Rationale: The passage emphasizes fear and reluctance to use the response system (including fear of ambulances and chlorine) as explanations for the delay and describes an expanded transport and response capacity (hotline, ambulances, beds, laboratories). There is no discussion attributing the two- to three-day delay to lack of transportation to facilities; instead the cited reasons are behavioral and trust-related. Therefore lack of access to transportation is not supported by the passage as the most likely cause.

293.Option: Neutralizing antibody titers against the homologous virus are not predictive of protection against heterologous challenge, as the animals were protected despite low cross-reactive titers
Evidence: Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
293.Rationale: The option asserts that homologous nAb titers are not predictive because animals were protected despite low cross-reactive titers. The passage instead reports that all animals had measurable neutralizing antibodies (PRNT90 titers ≥20) and that nAb titers were similar against African and Asian ZIKV, i.e., cross-reactive. These findings contradict the claim of 'low cross-reactive titers' and therefore do not support the option.

293.Option: Protection from heterologous viremia is mediated by T cell responses rather than neutralizing antibodies, as evidenced by the absence of detectable viremia despite low nAb titers
Evidence: Proliferation of CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, as well as natural killer cells, was observed following ZIKV MR766 challenge (Fig 4A, 4B and S3 Fig).

Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
293.Rationale: While the study documents T cell and NK cell responses, it also documents measurable neutralizing antibodies in all animals (PRNT90 ≥20). The option claims low nAb titers and attributes protection to T cells, but the passage does not present low nAb titers nor provide evidence that T cells, rather than nAbs, mediated protection. The authors explicitly state they do not know the exact correlate of protection and suggest nAbs are likely important, so the option overstates what the data show.

293.Option: The presence of neutralizing antibodies at any detectable level is sufficient to prevent viremia in heterologous Zika virus challenge, regardless of titer or cross-reactivity
Evidence: Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).

We do not currently know the exact correlate of protective immunity in these animals, since robust memory T cell, NK cell, B cell, and nAb responses were elicited, but nAbs alone appeared to be effective in ZIKV vaccine studies in macaques [92].

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).
293.Rationale: The passage documents detectable and cross-reactive neutralizing antibodies and an association with protection, but it explicitly states that the exact correlate of protection is unknown. The data do not support the sweeping claim that any detectable nAb level (regardless of titer) is sufficient; the study measured specific titers (≥20) and does not establish a universal threshold or prove sufficiency of any detectable nAb level alone.

293.Option: The lack of detectable viremia after heterologous challenge indicates sterilizing immunity, which is likely due to the rapid induction of innate immune responses rather than neutralizing antibodies
Evidence: Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.
there was a significant increase in protection ... in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

we did observe an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite the lack of detectable viremia despite the lack of detectable viremia in the animals (Fig 4D) suggesting that low level virus replication did occur, indicating protection from disease rather than sterilizing immunity.
293.Rationale: The option claims sterilizing immunity and attributes it to innate responses rather than nAbs. The passage explicitly notes an increase in nAb titers after re-challenge despite no detectable viremia, which suggests that low-level replication (not sterile immunity) likely occurred and that adaptive responses (nAb) were involved. The study does not provide evidence that innate responses rather than nAbs caused protection.

293.Option: High and cross-reactive neutralizing antibody titers are strongly associated with protection from detectable viremia during heterologous Zika virus challenge
Evidence: Serum neutralizing antibody responses also were measured by plaque reduction neutralization tests (PRNT90), and all animals exhibited neutralizing antibody (nAb) titers ≥20 as early as 21 dpi (Fig 4D).
nAb titer was similar when serum from the original challenge was screened against African versus Asian ZIKV (S5 Fig).
Viral RNA was undetectable in plasma (Fig 2B), saliva, and urine at all timepoints through 21 days post re-challenge.

There was a significant increase in protection against infection with Asian ZIKV (Exact unconditional test) in animals previously infected with African ZIKV (n = 3) as compared to animals only challenged with Asian ZIKV (n = 8, p = 0.0016).

we hypothesize that, similar to other flaviviruses (e.g., Yellow fever virus (YFV), Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV), and Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV)), nAbs are a critical component of the protective immune response [40–43].
293.Rationale: The study findings show measurable neutralizing antibody titers after primary infection, similar cross-reactive titers against African and Asian strains, and complete absence of detectable viremia after heterologous challenge—together with a statistically significant protected outcome compared with naive controls. The authors explicitly hypothesize that neutralizing antibodies are a critical component of protection. These points support the conclusion that high, cross-reactive nAb titers are strongly associated with protection from detectable viremia in this heterologous challenge context.

294.Option: The association remained significant even after adjusting for infant preterm birth and low birth weight, which are known mediators of the relationship between maternal infection and neurodevelopment
Evidence: Although infant characteristics (preterm births, low birth weight, and weight-for-age at MSEL assessment) were hypothesized to be mediators in the association between prenatal helminth infection and infant cognitive function, we adjusted for these variables in a sensitivity analyses.

sensitivity analyses performed by further adjusting for infant preterm status and weight-for-age, yielded similar results in the association between infants gross motor function and prenatal helminth infection.
Further adjustment for LBW (not preterm birth) and weight-for-age showed similar conclusions in the sensitivity analyses.
294.Rationale: While the passage does state that sensitivity analyses adjusting for infant preterm birth, low birth weight and weight-for-age yielded similar results, those variables are described as potential mediators rather than confounders. Adjusting for mediators and observing persistence of the association speaks to the relationship not being wholly mediated by adverse birth outcomes, but it does not directly rule out confounding by maternal anemia or iron deficiency. Therefore this factor does not most strongly indicate the association is not due to confounding by maternal anemia.

294.Option: The study used a validated neurodevelopmental assessment tool (MSEL) administered by trained research nurses, reducing measurement error and increasing confidence in the observed association
Evidence: At the health centers, trained research nurses individually assessed the cognitive function of 635 one-year-old infants using the Mullen Scales of Early Learning (MSEL) from April 2011 to November 2012.

Briefly, five assessors were trained by MJB and FBL in the field and retrained three times. Difficulties to be discussed with FBL and MJB were reviewed during weekly meetings. Inter-rater reliability was checked.
we used a comprehensive assessment for neurodevelopment carried out by research nurses specifically trained by an expert in cognitive assessment in African countries (co-author MJB).
294.Rationale: Use of a validated tool and trained assessors strengthens outcome measurement and reduces measurement error, which increases confidence in the observed association. However, this addresses outcome validity and measurement bias rather than confounding by maternal anemia/iron deficiency. It does not specifically provide evidence that the association is not confounded by maternal anemia, so it is not the best answer to the question.

294.Option: The study adjusted for malaria co-infection during pregnancy, which is a known risk factor for anemia and poor neurodevelopment, thereby reducing the likelihood of confounding by iron deficiency
Evidence: These covariates were considered as potential confounding factors as they are known risk factors for poor cognitive development and may share common causes with helminth infection. Next, we conducted a multiple linear regression adjusting for covariates whose p-values were less than 0.20 in the univariate analysis.
Maternal malarial infection was not statistically significantly associated with ELC and gross motor scores (see Table 3).
Malaria has also been assessed several times during pregnancy allowing for the study of the impact of malaria-helminth co-infection on child development.
294.Rationale: The passage indicates malaria was assessed and found not to be significantly associated with the outcomes, and malaria-helminth co-infection was examined. Because malaria can cause anemia, showing it was not associated reduces one potential source of anemia-related confounding. However, the study did not directly measure or adjust for maternal hemoglobin/iron status, and malaria is only one cause of anemia. Thus adjusting for or ruling out malaria does not most strongly rule out confounding by maternal anemia/iron deficiency from other sources.

294.Option: The association persisted after adjusting for multiple socioeconomic and maternal health confounders, suggesting it is not fully explained by unmeasured anemia
Evidence: we used a comprehensive assessment for neurodevelopment carried out by research nurses specifically trained by an expert in cognitive assessment in African countries (co-author MJB). An additional strength of this study is the consideration of several potential confounding factors such as socio-economic status, maternal depression and RPM and HOME scores.

After adjusting for gravidity, maternal education, family possession, child sex and HOME score, helminth infection at first ANC visit was negatively associated with infant gross motor function (p = 0.028).

Pregnant women who were infected with helminths at least, once during pregnancy had children with poorer ELC scores, thus-4.4 (95% CI: -7.2 to-1.5) compared to those of mothers who were never infected during pregnancy after adjustment (see Table 5).
294.Rationale: The study adjusted for multiple socioeconomic and maternal factors (maternal education, family possessions, HOME and RPM scores, gravidity, etc.) and the association persisted after adjustment. Because socioeconomic status and related maternal health factors are correlated with anemia/iron deficiency, controlling for them reduces the likelihood that the observed association is entirely due to confounding by unmeasured maternal anemia. Therefore this factor most strongly suggests the association is not simply confounding by maternal anemia/iron deficiency.

294.Option: The association was strongest for hookworm infection, which is the most common helminth species associated with iron deficiency, suggesting a biologically plausible causal pathway
Evidence: Our study has shown that intestinal helminth infection at first ANC visit is associated with poorer infant cognitive and gross motor functions at the age of one-year after adjusting for other known risk factors of cognitive and gross motor development. In our study population, prenatal hookworm infection was related to lower performance in gross motor tests.
In our study, although infection by any helminths at second ANC visits was not associated with poor cognitive or gross motor function, hookworm infection remained associated with ELC scores.
Helminth infection especially with hookworms is a known risk factor for ID.
294.Rationale: A strong association specifically with hookworm—an agent known to cause iron deficiency—supports a biologically plausible causal pathway via iron deficiency rather than arguing against confounding by anemia. In fact, this pattern could be consistent with anemia mediating the causal effect of hookworm on neurodevelopment. Therefore this observation does not suggest the association is not due to confounding by anemia; rather it is compatible with anemia being on the causal pathway.

296.Option: The observation that minor variants are primarily found at frequencies below 1%, which is below the detection limit of Sanger sequencing, supports the idea that immune pressure generates rare, transient antigenic variants that are quickly eliminated
Evidence: The major proportion of the variants in primary syphilis samples was in the 1–5% (181/237) range, and the lowest was in the 10–60% (22/237) range.
These minor variants were found to be mostly distributed at a frequency of 1–5% (Fig 2B), which was extremely below the detection limit for Sanger sequencing [36].

the intrastrain heterogeneous sequences were valid if the following conditions were simultaneously verified for any variant sequence: 1) being supported by at least fifty reads and 2) displaying a frequency above 1%.
296.Rationale: The option asserts minor variants are primarily below 1%, but the passage explicitly reports that the majority of minor variants lie in the 1–5% frequency range and that the analysis required variants to be >1% to be considered. Thus the factual premise of the option is incorrect and cannot be used as support. The passage does note that many minor variants are at low frequency (below Sanger detection), but they are mostly 1–5%, not below 1%.

296.Option: The high frequency of a single predominant sequence in most V regions, especially V1, indicates that immune pressure stabilizes a conserved antigenic profile early in infection
Evidence: there was a predominant sequence in each V region of ten samples directly from primary syphilis patients, and the proportion of this sequence was almost above 80%.
Among the seven V regions, V1 and V6 were found to have the lowest and highest variability, respectively (Figs 1 and 2A).

This feature may represent a logical fitness-based evolution where high-frequency sequences are better fitted to avoid immune recognition and numerous low-frequency minor variants may simply emerge and most of them would likely disappear if they were not advantageous for syphilis developing [37].
296.Rationale: While the passage does report a high-frequency predominant sequence in most V regions (including V1), the interpretation offered in the option—that immune pressure 'stabilizes a conserved antigenic profile early in infection'—is not the strongest or only inference. The authors interpret the pattern as fitness-based selection (predominant sequences better fitted to avoid immune recognition) coexisting with numerous low-frequency variants that may be emerging. That pattern more directly indicates selection acting on variants (and the presence of minor variants available for selection) rather than simple stabilization of conservation. The option overstates the conclusion by saying immune pressure stabilizes conservation; the passage frames the observation as selection favoring fitter variants while diversity exists, so this option is not the best-supported characterization.

296.Option: The increase in frequency of minor variants in V6, a region with the highest genetic diversity, during early infection suggests immune-driven selection for antigenic escape
Evidence: The number of distinct sequences in the seven regions of the tprK gene ranged from 21–76, with the highest number in V6 and the lowest in V1 across all samples (Fig 1 and S2 Table).
The frequency in V6 of X-6 was even lower at 20.8%.

Additionally, among these four V regions, the frequency of the predominant sequence in V6 was particularly low (Fig 2A), suggesting that V6 may be the first affected region and is involved in immune evasion during the course of infection [21, 24].

The distribution pattern of these variants from these samples may suggest that with disease progression or increasing immunity, the balance of the original sequence distribution was broken and some V regions (e.g., V2, V5, V6 and V7) began to change. As a result, a minor variant (or a new variant) became advantageous and its frequency gradually increased, ultimately replacing the original predominant sequence, which further promoted the antigenic diversity of TprK for T. pallidum to escape immune clearance
296.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies V6 as having the highest diversity and documents cases where the predominant sequence frequency in V6 is notably low, consistent with many competing variants. The authors directly state this pattern suggests V6 may be first involved in immune evasion and describe a process where minor variants increase in frequency under immune pressure and replace original sequences. Thus the observed rise in minor-variant frequency in V6 is the clearest, directly stated piece of evidence in the passage supporting immune-driven selection for antigenic escape during early infection.

296.Option: The presence of multiple length variants in V3, V6, and V7, which are the most variable regions, indicates that structural changes in TprK are driven by immune pressure to evade host recognition
Evidence: The length of the captured sequences within each V region was also found to be variable, particularly in V3, V6 and V7, with 11 or 12 forms.

The size range of the captured sequences was the largest for V3, V6 and V7, which was similar to the findings of Pinto et al. [24], demonstrating that the variations in these three regions could more easily cause changes in length.
the length of all distinct sequences differed by 3 bp or multiples of 3 bp.
The variations in V regions were regulated by a strict gene conversion mechanism to keep the length differences to 3 bp or multiples of 3 bp.
296.Rationale: Length heterogeneity in V3, V6, and V7 is well documented in the passage and shows structural variation is possible without frameshifts. However, while such structural changes enable antigenic variation, the passage does not present length variation alone as the most direct evidence that immune pressure is driving diversity. The authors more directly point to shifts in variant frequencies (especially in V6 and in V2/V5/V7) as evidence of selection under immune pressure. Length variation is supportive of a mechanism that permits antigenic change but is less direct evidence of immune-driven selection than documented frequency shifts of variants.

296.Option: The high inter-population redundancy of amino acid sequences in V1 and V4, despite overall tprK variability, suggests that immune pressure is strongest in these regions, leading to conserved antigenic profiles across patients
Evidence: V1 and V4 presented a strong shared sequence capacity. The sequence IASDGGAIKH in V1 was observed in five strains (5/14) and DVGHKKENAANVNGTVGA in V4 was shared across seven strains (7/14).
Among the seven V regions, V1 and V6 were found to have the lowest and highest variability, respectively (Figs 1 and 2A).
the highly stable shared peptide of V1 and V4 across all the strains would likely be a potential target for vaccine development.
296.Rationale: The passage documents that V1 and V4 show inter-population redundancy (shared conserved sequences) and that V1 is among the least variable regions. However, conserved sequences across patients are more consistent with functional or structural constraint or an optimal 'fitted' antigenic profile rather than indicating that immune pressure is 'strongest' in those regions driving conservation. In fact, immune pressure that promotes escape would be expected to increase diversity. The authors highlight V6 as implicated in immune evasion, not V1/V4. Therefore, inferring that redundancy in V1/V4 indicates the strongest immune pressure is not supported by the passage.

297.Option: Implement population-wide door-to-door screening every six months to detect all cases of PKDL and leprosy in the region
Evidence: Active case finding through door-to-door screening is probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable.
Our teams spent more than one year, with interruptions, to screen a population of approximately 33,000 and were able to examine only three-quarters of those.
297.Rationale: Although door-to-door screening is effective at finding hidden PKDL cases, the passage explicitly states it is not sustainable and documents the very large time and resource burden (over one year to examine ~75% of ~33,000). A proposal to do population-wide door-to-door screening every six months would therefore be impractical and unsustainable in this setting.

297.Option: Prioritize screening for leprosy in households with a history of VL to detect potential PKDL cases due to shared risk factors
Evidence: The three PKDL cases were all from different villages. Given their small numbers, a further spatial analysis was not considered useful.
Targeting active case finding for leprosy to household members of cases and neighbors seems rational, but would miss out on a large number of cases.
For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.
297.Rationale: The passage recommends targeting leprosy active case finding to household members and neighbors of leprosy cases because leprosy shows local spatial clustering; it does not recommend prioritizing leprosy screening based on households with prior VL. PKDL cases in this study were few and dispersed across different villages, and the authors recommend active follow-up of previously treated VL patients for PKDL specifically. Thus prioritizing leprosy screening in VL-history households is not supported and conflates distinct spatial patterns.

297.Option: Use passive case finding through health facility reporting to identify new cases of PKDL and leprosy in areas with high leprosy prevalence
Evidence: To address both leprosy and PKDL, skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
Active case finding through door-to-door screening is probably the most effective way to find the few hidden PKDL cases but unfortunately will never be sustainable.
It is likely that some PKDL cases might have been missed during this period.
297.Rationale: The passage implies passive case finding alone will miss hidden PKDL cases (some cases were likely missed under routine surveillance) and suggests skin camps as a compromise between active (door-to-door) and passive approaches. Therefore relying only on passive facility reporting would be unlikely to identify the hidden transmission risks described.

297.Option: Conduct targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases and follow up past VL patients for PKDL to detect hidden transmission risks
Evidence: For leprosy, door-to-door screening could be considered when focusing on the immediate neighborhood of households in which cases have been identified.

With those living at more than 100 meters as reference category we found rate ratios of 6.3 (95% CI 1.9–21.0) and 3.6 (95% CI 1.3–10.2) respectively for those living in the same household and neighbors at less than 25 meters of a leprosy patient.
Targeting active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases.
For PKDL this should be combined with active follow-up of previously treated VL cases.
297.Rationale: This option directly reflects the study findings and recommendations: leprosy shows strong local clustering (high rate ratios for household and <25 m neighbors), so targeted active case finding around known leprosy cases is rational; for PKDL the authors recommend active follow-up of previously treated VL patients and screening in transmission hotspots. Combining targeted leprosy neighborhood screening with follow-up of past VL patients addresses both spatial patterns and hidden PKDL transmission risk.

297.Option: Establish skin camps in villages with the highest historical VL incidence to detect PKDL and leprosy cases through opportunistic screening
Evidence: To address both leprosy and PKDL, skin camps may be a reasonable compromise between door-to-door screening and passive case finding.
The skin camps in our study were very well attended.
Targeting active case finding for leprosy, with screening for PKDL in hotspots of previous transmission, and regular skin camps seems the most realistic approach to tackle both diseases.
297.Rationale: The passage recommends skin camps as a practical, well-attended compromise that can be combined with hotspot-focused PKDL screening and targeted leprosy case finding. Establishing skin camps in villages with highest historical VL incidence (hotspots of previous transmission) aligns with the authors' suggested realistic approach to detect PKDL and leprosy cases opportunistically while accounting for previous VL spatial patterns.

298.Option: The route of administration of the monoclonal antibody, such as intraperitoneal versus intravenous delivery
Evidence: The 5–6 week old female Balb/C mice from Charles River, (Quebec, Canada), were injected ip with the indicated amount of ZEBOV GP-specific MAbs in 100 µl PBS at the times indicated either before or after i.p. infection with 1,000 LD50 of MA-ZEBOV.

Female guinea pigs (Hartley strain), approximately 250 g, from Charles River, were challenged with 1,000 LD50 of GA-ZEBOV i.p.. At the indicated times post-infection the guinea pigs were treated i.p. with 1 ml of the MAb diluted in PBS.
It is possible that since the MAbs and virus were both injected ip that the MAbs might inhibit ZEBOV infection of cells and extend life.
298.Rationale: The passage documents that mice and guinea pigs were treated intraperitoneally and even notes that the shared ip route might have influenced outcomes, but it does not present route of administration as the primary determinant of complete protection when treatment is delayed. Instead, the text emphasizes timing (1–2 dpi) and use of combinations as decisive for achieving complete protection. Thus route is a possible contributing factor but not the strongest influence according to the study data.

298.Option: The presence of a neutralizing antibody response in the host's endogenous immune system at the time of treatment
Evidence: Both T and B cell immunity was analysed and it was believed that a strong early humoral immune response may have been a factor in survival [11], [16], [17].
Additionally, in fatally infected patients EBOV-specific IgG was absent, and IgM levels were low in comparison to the survivors [16].
The PRNT50 demonstrated that MAbs 1H3, 2G4, and 4G7 were neutralizing with a PRNT50 at a 1/200, 1/800, and 1/6,400 dilution, respectively (Figure 1).
298.Rationale: The passage notes that an early humoral response in the host may be associated with survival and describes neutralizing activity of specific MAbs, but the experimental results demonstrating complete protection when treatment was delayed focus on timing and on delivering an effective MAb combination at 2 dpi. The study does not provide evidence that the host's endogenous neutralizing antibody status at treatment time was the primary determinant of achieving complete protection in these post-exposure experiments.

298.Option: The total dose of monoclonal antibodies administered, regardless of timing relative to infection
Evidence: A dose response was observed, and some MAbs were more potent than others for a given dose.
The lowest doses providing complete protection from lethality for 5D2, 5E6, 7C9, and 7G4 were 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg, respectively.

In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected.
298.Rationale: Dose clearly affects potency (dose-response observed and specific minimal protective doses reported), but the passage explicitly emphasizes that timing of administration (not dose alone) is critical for achieving complete protection, especially when treatment is delayed. Therefore total dose alone, "regardless of timing," is not supported as the strongest influencing factor.

298.Option: The specific epitope targeted by the monoclonal antibody on the Ebola virus glycoprotein
Evidence: Each of these MAbs binds to different regions of GP1,2. 1H3 and 4G7 bind to the N- and C-terminus of GP1, respectively, whereas 2G4 binds to GP2 [36].
Targeting multiple regions of GP appears to be a successful strategy.

There have been several attempts at producing MAbs against ZEBOV GP however no clear pattern has emerged suggesting which primary sequence domain of GP is most immunogenic or whether neutralizing antibodies are more successful [26], [27], [31]–[34].

In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected.
298.Rationale: Epitope specificity and combining antibodies against different GP regions contributed to success (combination targeting multiple regions provided complete protection in guinea pigs). However, the passage repeatedly identifies timing (1–2 dpi) as the critical constraint for effective post-exposure therapy. Thus while epitope targeting is important, it is not presented as the single strongest factor when treatment is delayed beyond the initial hours.

298.Option: The specific timing of treatment relative to infection onset, particularly whether it occurs within 2 days post-infection, is the most critical factor for achieving complete protection
Evidence: In general, the MAbs worked best for both animal models when given after the start of the infection, particularly at 1 and 2 dpi, before efficacy started to decrease at 3 dpi This suggests that there may be a limited time period in which to begin treatment after becoming infected.
The combination of neutralizing MAbs (1.5 mg 1H3+1.5 mg 2G4+2 mg 4G7) was given to guinea pigs either 1 day before, or 1, 2 or 3 days after a 1000 LD50 GA-ZEBOV infection, and survival followed.

In contrast all animals receiving the neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi. survived. When the treatment was given on 3 dpi the percent survival dropped to 66.7% with a mean time to death of 11.17±3.09 days.
This is a significant extension from the 1 hour post-infection required for KZ52. This is an important consideration as it is often difficult to begin treatment as early as 1 hour after an infection.
298.Rationale: Multiple experimental results in the passage demonstrate that delivering therapy at 2 days post-infection was sufficient for complete protection (neutralizing MAb combination at 2 dpi produced 100% survival), whereas delaying to 3 dpi reduced survival. The authors explicitly state that efficacy decreases after 2 dpi and highlight a limited window for initiating treatment. Therefore timing relative to infection onset (particularly within the 2-day window) is the strongest determinant of achieving complete protection when treatment is delayed.

299.Option: Select the cut-off value that corresponds to the lowest antibody level observed in any confirmed VL case to ensure no false negatives
Evidence: To assess the overall performance of the test sensitivity and specificity were calculated and diagnostic accuracy was established by area under the curve (AUC) where AUC = 1 indicates an accurate test.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: The passage indicates the investigators used an ROC-curve-based approach to select the cut-off (0.1875) that balanced sensitivity and specificity; they did not choose the lowest antibody level among cases. Selecting the lowest observed value among cases would minimize false negatives but would likely increase false positives and would not formally balance sensitivity and specificity as the ROC method does. The quoted text shows the study used ROC/AUC to determine the optimal threshold rather than the minimum-case-value approach.

299.Option: Use a receiver-operating characteristic (ROC) curve to identify the optimal cut-off point that maximizes both sensitivity and specificity
Evidence: To assess the overall performance of the test sensitivity and specificity were calculated and diagnostic accuracy was established by area under the curve (AUC) where AUC = 1 indicates an accurate test.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: The ROC-curve method is the appropriate analytic approach to choose a diagnostic threshold because it explicitly evaluates the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity and allows selection of an operating point that optimizes both (commonly via Youden's index or other criteria). The passage explicitly states the investigators used ROC analysis to select the cut-off (0.1875) and reported sensitivity, specificity and AUC calculated from that ROC, supporting that this is the correct method.

299.Option: Set the cut-off value at the mean antibody level of the control group plus two standard deviations to minimize false positives
Evidence: To assess the overall performance of the test sensitivity and specificity were calculated and diagnostic accuracy was established by area under the curve (AUC) where AUC = 1 indicates an accurate test.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: Although using control mean +2 SD is a common rule-of-thumb to reduce false positives, the passage shows the investigators used ROC analysis to select the threshold and to report sensitivity/specificity and AUC. The mean+2SD method is arbitrary and does not directly optimize the balance of sensitivity and specificity as ROC-based approaches do; therefore it is not the most appropriate method here and is not supported by the passage.

299.Option: Use the median antibody level of the VL-positive group as the cut-off to ensure equal distribution of values above and below the threshold
Evidence: To assess the overall performance of the test sensitivity and specificity were calculated and diagnostic accuracy was established by area under the curve (AUC) where AUC = 1 indicates an accurate test.
With the assessment of ROC curve (Fig 4), the test cut-off point, 0.1875 was selected from possible cut-offs at 95% CI.

ROC curve obtained from the ELISA values for the detection of anti-Leishmania IgG antibodies in urine samples. Cut-off value (0.1875), sensitivity (97.94%), specificity (100%) and AUC (0.9984) were determined by this curve using GraphPad Prism software (version 5.0).
299.Rationale: Using the median of positives as a threshold simply splits the positive distribution and does not account for the distribution of controls, nor does it optimize sensitivity and specificity. The passage explicitly describes using ROC/AUC analysis to determine the cut-off and report diagnostic performance; thus the median-of-positives approach is not the method employed and is not the most appropriate for selecting a diagnostic threshold.

300.Option: Respondents may have misreported their feeding behavior due to social desirability bias, especially among those with negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Some unexpected findings also arose from the current survey. For example, although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.

As a household was represented by only one respondent, we also accept that the opinion of one person may not indeed be a true representation of overall knowledge, attitudes and practices of all members in a family.
300.Rationale: While the study acknowledges unexpected findings and a limitation that a single respondent may not represent the whole household (which could allow reporting error), the authors explicitly propose religious convictions as the likely explanation for feeding despite negative attitudes. The passage does not present evidence that social desirability bias is the primary driver; instead it points to religion-driven behavior. Therefore framing the discrepancy mainly as misreporting (social desirability) is not the most supported explanation in the passage.

300.Option: The inconsistency between feeding and attitudes is due to a lack of understanding about the transmission of rabies through licks and scratches, which leads to underestimation of risk
Evidence: less than half (50, 40.7%) were aware that it could also be transmitted through licks/scratches.

Some unexpected findings also arose from the current survey. For example, although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
300.Rationale: The passage does show a knowledge gap about transmission via licks/scratches, so incomplete understanding could contribute to underestimation of some risks. However, the authors specifically interpret the paradoxical feeding as 'likely' due to religious convictions among respondents, despite awareness of rabies generally. Thus lack of knowledge about licks/scratches is an ancillary factor but not the primary explanation favored by the study for the observed discrepancy.

300.Option: Religious beliefs promoting animal care override risk awareness, leading to feeding despite negative attitudes toward free-roaming dogs
Evidence: Some unexpected findings also arose from the current survey. For example, although only 9% of respondents believed that feed provided by villagers was a major source of food for FRD, a larger proportion (39%) actually admitted to feeding them.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52], but, apparently it did not motivate them sufficiently to accept responsibility for immunisations, probably also due to issues of availability of vaccines and their cost.
Most respondents (97%) had heard of rabies.
300.Rationale: The passage directly proposes religious conviction as the likely reason people feed stray dogs despite recognizing risks: most villagers are Hindus and feeding is 'strongly influenced by religious convictions.' The authors link this to the observed discrepancy (high proportion feeding while many view FRD as a nuisance and are aware of rabies), making this explanation the most supported by the text.

300.Option: Higher socio-economic status is associated with greater access to information about rabies, leading to more consistent reporting of feeding practices and attitudes
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level considered FRD were useful (OR 3.09, 95%CI 1.06–8.97, p = 0.03), were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005), and would take an injured stray dog to a veterinarian (OR 2.33 95%CI 1.0–5.48, p = 0.04).
Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.
300.Rationale: The passage indicates that higher socio-economic respondents were more likely both to feed FRD and to hold sympathetic attitudes, not that higher SES simply causes 'more consistent reporting.' The option's claim about greater access to information producing more consistent reporting is not asserted in the text; instead the text links feeding behavior to compassion/religion and notes SES differences in attitudes and behaviors, so the proposed mechanism (consistent reporting due to information access) is not supported.

300.Option: The discrepancy arises because respondents from lower socio-economic groups are more likely to feed dogs out of necessity, while higher SES groups feed them out of compassion, leading to divergent attitudes
Evidence: Participants from the high/middle socio-economic level ... were likely to feed them (OR2.81, 95%CI 1.34–5.88, p = 0.005)
Respondents from a low socio-economic level were less likely to consider FRD useful, feed them or take an injured dog to a veterinarian than participants from high/middle levels.

It is likely that feeding stray dogs is strongly influenced by religious convictions, as most of the respondents were followers of Hinduism, a religion which is tolerant of animals and caring for them [51, 52]...
300.Rationale: The data in the passage show the opposite of the option's assertion: higher (not lower) socio-economic respondents were more likely to feed FRD, consistent with compassion/greater disposable income, while lower SES were less likely to feed. The passage attributes feeding largely to religious conviction/compassion rather than necessity among low SES, so this option does not match the evidence.

301.Option: The pathogen is transmitted directly from infected fleas to their offspring and persists across life stages without requiring vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Vertical maintenance of the agent within the vector (transovarial and transstadial transmission, i.e. passage to offspring and persistence across life stages) provides a direct mechanism by which the pathogen can be maintained in vector populations without requiring vertebrate hosts. The passage explicitly states the organism is "naturally passaged generationally in fleas" and "transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas," which is the most direct and strong evidence consistent with a vector-only maintenance cycle.

301.Option: The pathogen causes severe neurological disease in humans, indicating a high pathogenic potential that likely requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations
Evidence: Typically, it presents as a mild, febrile illness that can be syndromically confused with many other undifferentiated fevers, especially in Africa and Asia, where diagnostic resources are limited.
Neurological involvement has been reported in several cases in Mexico [9] and Sweden [10, 11] but not deaths.
the bacterium is transovarially and transstadially maintained in fleas.
301.Rationale: Severe human disease does not, by itself, indicate that a pathogen requires sustained circulation in vertebrate populations; many vector-borne agents maintained in vectors can cause severe illness in incidental vertebrate hosts. The passage describes R. felis typically as a mild febrile illness and notes that neurological involvement is rare, so severity in humans is not a reliable indicator of need for a vertebrate reservoir. Moreover, the text documents vertical maintenance in fleas, which directly contradicts the assertion that vertebrate circulation is required.

301.Option: The pathogen was first isolated from cat fleas, indicating that fleas are the primary and exclusive vector, which supports a transmission cycle independent of vertebrate hosts
Evidence: Rickettsia felis was first identified as a R. typhi-like organism in the midgut epithelium of a commercial colony of cat fleas, Ctenocephalides felis [1].
It has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
301.Rationale: Being first isolated from fleas indicates fleas are an important vector, but isolation alone does not establish that fleas are the exclusive vector or that vertebrate reservoirs are unnecessary. The passage also notes detection in a variety of other arthropods (including mosquitoes) and explicitly states that the exact mechanisms of transmission and the role of vertebrate reservoirs remain unclear, so first isolation from fleas is insufficient to prove a vector-only cycle.

301.Option: The pathogen is not detected in human blood samples, suggesting it is not adapted to vertebrate hosts and thus likely maintained solely in arthropods
Evidence: Because Rickettsia invade endothelial cells, DNA is typically scarce in sera or CSF [23].
301.Rationale: Low detectability of Rickettsia DNA in blood or CSF reflects biology (intracellular localization in endothelial cells) and limits of assays, not necessarily absence of infection or lack of adaptation to vertebrate hosts. The passage explicitly states DNA is "typically scarce in sera or CSF," so failure to detect pathogen in blood is not strong evidence that it is not adapted to vertebrates or that vertebrate reservoirs are absent.

301.Option: The pathogen has been detected in multiple arthropod species, including mosquitoes and fleas, suggesting a broad vector range that supports transmission without vertebrate hosts
Evidence: It is naturally passaged generationally in fleas and has been found in a variety of other arthropods, including mosquitoes, but the exact mechanisms of transmission, including the possible role of vertebrate reservoirs, are still unclear [4].
It has been found in a variety of arthropod species, including some, like book lice, that are not blood-feeding and others, like mosquitoes, not proven to transmit Rickettsia [4, 24].
301.Rationale: Detection in multiple arthropod species shows broad presence but does not by itself demonstrate maintenance in a vector-only cycle. The passage notes some detected hosts are not blood-feeding and others (e.g., mosquitoes) are "not proven to transmit Rickettsia," and it emphasizes the uncertainty about transmission mechanisms and possible roles of vertebrate reservoirs. Thus, broad arthropod detection is weaker evidence than documented vertical maintenance in fleas for supporting a vector-only maintenance hypothesis.

302.Option: The high in-hospital case fatality rate of 41.3% in elderly dengue patients justifies the use of any available laboratory test for early risk stratification
Evidence: The in-hospital CFR was 41.3% (31 patients), and these patients had a median ICU stay of 4 (IQR: 1–8) days.
From the results of the current study, APTT may be considered as a screening tool for risk stratification.
APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: The passage reports a high case fatality rate (quote 1) and explicitly suggests APTT as a potential single screening tool (quotes 2–3). However, nothing in the text supports the broad claim that a high CFR 'justifies the use of any available laboratory test' for early risk stratification. The authors single out APTT based on its independent prognostic value; they do not endorse using arbitrary laboratory tests merely because CFR is high. Therefore the option overgeneralizes and is not supported by the passage.

302.Option: Prolonged APTT at ICU admission remains an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality even after accounting for multi-parameter severity scores
Evidence: In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, presence of AKI at ICU admission and initial APTT longer than 48 s (Table 3).

APTT prolongation > 48 s remained to be an independent predictive factor for in-hospital fatality after adjusting for APACHE II score (HR: 3.34, P = 0.006), and was marginally significant after adjusting for SOFA score (HR: 2.43, P = 0.053).
APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that initial APTT >48 s was an independent predictor of in-hospital fatality in multivariate analysis (quote 1) and remained an independent predictor after adjusting for APACHE II, with marginal significance after adjusting for SOFA (quote 2). The authors also note that APTT, as a single laboratory parameter, showed valuable prognostic significance (quote 3). These statements directly support the option that prolonged APTT retains independent predictive value even when multi-parameter severity scores are considered, thereby supporting use of a single laboratory test for early risk stratification.

302.Option: High APACHE II and SOFA scores are strongly associated with in-hospital fatality and therefore should be prioritized for early risk stratification in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: Recently a Brazilian study also showed that APACHE II score and sequential organ failure assessment (SOFA) score were associated with in-hospital fatality in patients in an ICU [23].
The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).
APACHE II and SOFA scores include many clinical parameters and were not included in the final model of multivariate Cox analysis as they demonstrated collinearity with other variables.
302.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that APACHE II and SOFA scores are associated with fatality and have higher AUCs than APTT (quotes 1–2), it also emphasizes limitations of these multi-parameter scores: they include many parameters, showed collinearity, and thus were not included in the final multivariate model (quote 3). The question asks which feature supports using a single laboratory test despite availability of multi-parameter systems; the passage supports APTT's independent prognostic value over prioritizing the complex scores. Therefore the option's conclusion that APACHE II and SOFA 'should be prioritized' contradicts the passage's argument and is not supported.

302.Option: APTT prolongation is more sensitive than APACHE II and SOFA scores for detecting early signs of organ failure in elderly dengue patients
Evidence: The area under the ROC curve (AUC) of APTT was 0.76 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.64–0.88), APACHE II score 0.84 (0.75–0.93), and SOFA score 0.85 (0.77–0.94).

Though there was no significant difference between APTT and two scores (APTT vs. APACHE II, P = 0.27; APTT vs. SOFA, P = 0.16), AUCs of APACHE II and SOFA scores, which both were a constellation of multiple clinical and laboratory variables, were higher than that of APTT.
APTT, a single laboratory parameter, exhibited valuable prognostic significance.
302.Rationale: The passage reports AUC values showing APACHE II and SOFA had higher discriminatory ability than APTT (quotes 1–2). It does state APTT has valuable prognostic significance (quote 3), but it does not claim APTT is more sensitive than the multi-parameter scores for detecting early organ failure. Thus the specific statement that APTT is 'more sensitive' is not supported by the evidence provided.

302.Option: The presence of acute respiratory failure at ICU admission is a stronger predictor of fatality than APTT prolongation and should be used for early risk stratification
Evidence: In the univariate Cox model, female gender, cardiac arrest before ICU admission, respiratory failure at ICU admission, high creatinine level, AKI at ICU admission, a high APACHE II or SOFA score, and APTT longer than 48 s were significantly associated with in-hospital fatality.

In the multivariate analysis, cardiac arrest before ICU admission (hazard ratio [HR] 6.26 [95% confidence interval: 1.91–20.54]), AKI at ICU admission (HR 2.48 [1.07–5.74]), and initial APTT longer than 48 s (HR 3.91 [1.69–9.07]) were independent predictive factors for in-hospital fatality...

In the Cox model, although respiratory failure indicative of a deterioration of clinical status and dysregulation in respiratory function, at ICU admission, was marginally significant, respiratory failure still remains to be an important prognostic factor.
302.Rationale: Respiratory failure was associated with fatality in univariate analysis (quote 1) and is described as an important prognostic factor (quote 3). However, in multivariate analysis respiratory failure was not one of the independent predictors retained; instead cardiac arrest, AKI, and initial APTT >48 s were independent predictors (quote 2). Thus the passage does not support the claim that respiratory failure is a stronger predictor than APTT or that it should replace APTT for early single-test risk stratification.

304.Option: Use Bayesian inference with a fixed geographic origin for each node to estimate the timing of transmission events
Evidence: Bayesian inference of ancestral dates was performed by BactDating v1.1.0 [47] from the phylogenetic tree.
The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
304.Rationale: Although the study used Bayesian inference (BactDating) to estimate node dates, geographic origin was not assigned as a fixed value per node. Instead, the authors used stochastic character mapping (make.simmap) with multiple simulations to estimate posterior probabilities for ancestral geographic states. A method that fixes geographic origins for nodes would not account for the uncertainty the authors explicitly quantified via stochastic mapping and posterior probabilities.

304.Option: Apply maximum parsimony to reconstruct the most likely geographic origin of ancestral nodes based on the fewest transmission events
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].
The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
304.Rationale: The passage states the authors used stochastic character mapping (make.simmap) with multiple simulations to obtain posterior probabilities for ancestral geographic states. Maximum parsimony (minimizing number of changes) was not used; parsimony also does not naturally provide the posterior-probability style uncertainty estimates from repeated stochastic simulations that the authors used.

304.Option: Use stochastic character mapping with posterior probability estimation via multiple simulations to infer geographic origin at ancestral nodes
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].
The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
The location of the ancestral nodes was determined with posterior probability >50%.
304.Rationale: This option precisely describes the method used in the study. The authors applied make.simmap (stochastic character mapping) with 100 simulations and an Empirical Bayes approach to derive posterior probabilities for ancestral geographic states, thereby accounting for uncertainty in transmission events when inferring likely geographic origins.

304.Option: Use a parsimony-based ancestral state reconstruction with bootstrapping to assess confidence in geographic origin estimates
Evidence: The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].
The make.simmap function simulates the stochastic character map while treating the continent of origin as a discrete character trait of the tree tips.

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
304.Rationale: The authors did not use parsimony-based ancestral reconstruction with bootstrap support. Instead they used stochastic mapping with multiple simulations to generate posterior probabilities for ancestral locations. While bootstrapped parsimony can give a measure of support, it does not provide the same explicit posterior-probability distribution over geographic states obtained from stochastic character mapping.

304.Option: Perform a likelihood ratio test to compare geographic origin hypotheses at ancestral nodes using a time-calibrated phylogeny
Evidence: Bayesian inference of ancestral dates was performed by BactDating v1.1.0 [47] from the phylogenetic tree.
The geographical transmission history was inferred by the make.simmap function in the R package phytools [48,49].

The geographical transmission history was summarised from 100 simulations using the Empirical Bayes method of the make.simmap function. Thus, the posterior probabilities of the ancestral nodes were estimated and visualised.
304.Rationale: The study combined Bayesian node dating (BactDating) with stochastic mapping (make.simmap) to infer geographic history and quantify uncertainty via posterior probabilities. A likelihood ratio test comparing fixed geographic-origin hypotheses at nodes is not described and would not as straightforwardly yield the posterior-probability estimates of ancestral geographic states obtained from stochastic character mapping across multiple simulations.

306.Option: The use of a quantitative ELISA assay to measure circulating filarial antigen levels, enabling detection of changes in adult worm burden over time
Evidence: This is a benefit of using a quantitative ELISA instead of a qualitative measurement.

We have also presented analytical data which, in addition, shows a significant reduction in the sera CFA levels of RHINO study participants (Figs 3 and 4), indicating a reduced adult worm burden in the infected individuals.
Most filarial infected individuals in 2009 showed high positive results, reflecting a high adult worm burden. In 2019 infected individuals had low positive ODs indicating a low number of adult worms.
The mean OD of CFA positive individuals was 1.55 (SD 0.70) in 2009, 1.11 (SD 0.56), in 2010/11 and 0.49 (SD 0.10) in 2019.
306.Rationale: A quantitative assay strengthens causal inference because it provides more detailed, continuous measures of infection intensity (adult worm burden) rather than only presence/absence. The passage explicitly states that using the quantitative TropBio ELISA allowed detection of significantly lower CFA levels and lower OD values in 2019 compared with earlier surveys, consistent with reduced adult worm burden. This reduces the chance that observed prevalence declines are artefacts of measurement threshold changes and supports that MDA had a biological effect on worm burden.

306.Option: The inclusion of a control group from a non-treatment region to compare changes in filarial prevalence over time
Evidence: The study was a follow-up of the population based EMINI (Evaluation and Monitoring of the Impact of New Interventions, http://www.mmrp.org/projects/cohort-studies/emini.html) cohort study, which was conducted at the National Institute for Medical Research (NIMR)—Mbeya Medical Research Centre (MMRC) ([13,18–22]. The study was carried out in selected villages from nine geographically distinct areas in the Mbeya region (S1 Fig) which included 10% of the households of each selected village. In one of the study sites (Kyela), testing for adult W. bancrofti was done from archived samples collected annually between 2007 and 2011 using the TropBio ELISA as the microfilariae data were not available.

During the ongoing RHINO study (Risk of HIV Infections through Nematode Organism), new data was collected in the Kyela district between March and August 2019 from 1299 previous EMINI participants aged 14–65 years.
The intriguing feature in this cohort is that the very same people have been visited in 2009, 2010/11 and 2019, thus excluding a sampling bias of this study.
306.Rationale: The passage describes a single-cohort follow-up in Kyela and repeatedly emphasizes revisiting the same participants; it does not describe enrolling a control group from a non-treatment region.
