Thus, most cases of FN and FP found in different months were likely due to the incidence of Cryptosporidiosis that varies with changing seasons and across geographical areas.
Moreover, other reports suggest that temperatures above 30°C can affect the overall performance of the RDTs [28] in the case of malaria parasites detection.
Taken together, the accuracy of any RDT result may depend on several factors such as the quality of the RDT, storage, transport and end-user performance.
375.Rationale: The authors identify low parasite density (low antigen concentration) as a likely explanation for low sensitivity and note seasonal/environmental effects (including high temperatures affecting RDT performance) as plausible contributors. These biological and environmental factors can vary across sites even when procedures and QC are standardized, making this option the most likely explanation for between-site variability.

375.Option: Variability in the performance of the CerTest Crypto RDT across sites may be due to differences in the prevalence of non-parvum/hominis Cryptosporidium species, which are less frequently detected by the test
Evidence: some of them are not well validated for the detection of less frequent non-parvum/hominis Cryptosporidium species [2–18].
These primers and probe sequencies are specific for Cryptosporidium parvum and C. hominis, although their efficiency at detecting other Cryptosporidium species has not be evaluated.
some Cryptosporodium genotypes may lack part or all of the protein targeted by the CerTest RDT which could explain the observed numbers of FN.

The performance characteristics of CerTest Crypto RDT were determined based on the ability of the CerTest to detect all species of Cryptosporidium (both human and animal species). Therefore, to assess the performance characteristics of this test to detect 2 of the most frequent species(C. hominis and C. parvum), eight observations that were PCR positive for “C. meleagridis “and “C. xiaoi/bovis” were excluded from the analysis.
375.Rationale: The paper notes that some RDTs (and potentially this one) may not detect less common Cryptosporidium species well, and that genotypic differences could mean missing the targeted antigen. Variation in species mix across sites would therefore plausibly lead to differing sensitivity despite standardized testing.

375.Option: Inconsistent performance across sites may be due to differences in the training or skill level of laboratory technicians despite standardized SOPs
Evidence: Prior to the start of the study, a general training was organized in Tanzania with participants from all the four study sites in attendance.

Training was based on CerTest Crypto RDT testing and qPCR for Cryptosporidium. Laboratory technicians from all the four sites were trained on sample processing, time of testing, and protocols were developed and harmonized for use in all the four sites.

The Certest Crypto Kit is supplied with quality control reagents. The quality control reagent consists of a known positive and negative sample. One negative and one positive control was run once a month in all the four sites and for each new batch of test kits.

Additionally, the internal procedural control is included in the test, wherein the appearance of a green line in the control line result window is an indication of correct procedural technique and confirms enough specimen volume was used.
375.Rationale: Although user technique can affect RDT accuracy in general (the paper even notes that end-user performance can matter), the study implemented centralized training, harmonized SOPs, and monthly and internal quality controls to monitor procedural correctness. These measures make systematic differences in technician skill a less likely primary explanation for the observed site-to-site variability.

375.Option: The variability in test performance may be due to differences in the storage conditions of the RDT kits across study sites, despite manufacturer claims of room-temperature stability
Evidence: The RDTs were stored at room temperature in all the sites according to manufacturer instructions.

Our demographic data did not not provide any substantial evidence on the effect of temperature and humidity on the test kit. Thus the variation in performance of the test kit that we observed in this study is not related to the handling and storage of the kits.
375.Rationale: Kits were stored at room temperature per manufacturer instructions and the authors explicitly state they found no evidence that handling or storage accounted for the variability. While other literature is cited about high temperatures affecting RDTs, the study's data do not support between-site differences in storage conditions as the main cause.

376.Option: The presence of neutralizing antibodies against one phlebovirus indicates prior infection with that specific virus, regardless of cross-reactivity with antigenically similar viruses
Evidence: Both viruses belong to the same virus species, Sandfly fever Naples, and consequently it is difficult to distinguish between them by using broadly reactive serological tests, such as inhibition hemagglutination assay, complement fixation assay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) or indirect immunofluorescence assay [6], [13], [18], [22], [23], [24].
Indeed, serological cross-reactivity in a function of viral antigenic closeness: the more similar the viruses, the more cross-reactive the antibodies.
Results suggested that the presence of TOSV NT-Ab may be responsible for PUNV cross-neutralisation.
For 144 sera (underlined values in table 2), possible cross-neutralisation between PUNV NT-Ab and TOSV NT-Ab precluded definitive interpretation and conclusion.
376.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antigenically related phleboviruses can produce cross-reactive antibodies and that this cross-reactivity can preclude definitive interpretation of serology. Neutralising antibodies are not unambiguous evidence of infection with a specific virus when closely related viruses co-circulate; the authors note cross-neutralisation (e.g., TOSV NT-Ab causing PUNV cross-neutralisation) and that many sera could not be definitively assigned to one virus. Therefore the option is incorrect.

376.Option: The geographic distribution of sand fly vectors determines the likelihood of human infection with a specific phlebovirus, regardless of serological cross-reactivity
Evidence: The risk of human infection with sand fly-transmitted viruses has been shown to cover extended geographic areas (southern Europe, Africa, Middle-East, central and western Asia) because of the presence of the sand fly vectors [1].
In Tunisia, PUNV strains have been isolated from Phlebotomus pernicisosus and Phlebotomus longicuspis collected from the district of Utique where TOSV strains have been also isolated [13], [17].

virus studies conducted on sand flies trapped in the same regions suggested that TOSV circulates at lower level than PUNV, since the latter was detected and isolated 6 times versus 2 times for TOSV of a total of 8,206 sand flies trapped during 3 successive seasons from 2008 to 2010 [13], [17].
Our results indicate that PUNV (or closely related antigenic variants) can infect humans, but it occurs seldom in a region where the virus circulates at high level in sand fly populations.
376.Rationale: While vector presence and geographic distribution affect exposure risk, the passage shows that vector isolation frequency does not solely determine human infection rates (PUNV was isolated more often from sand flies but human infections were rarer). Moreover, serological cross-reactivity complicates interpretation of serology; thus geographic distribution alone, stated 'regardless of serological cross-reactivity', is not the primary factor that determines interpretation of serological evidence. The passage emphasizes antigenic similarity and cross-reactivity as key influences.

376.Option: Antigenic similarity between co-circulating phleboviruses leads to cross-reactivity, reducing the specificity of serological tests for identifying the causative virus
Evidence: Both viruses belong to the same virus species, Sandfly fever Naples, and consequently it is difficult to distinguish between them by using broadly reactive serological tests, such as inhibition hemagglutination assay, complement fixation assay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) or indirect immunofluorescence assay [6], [13], [18], [22], [23], [24].
Indeed, serological cross-reactivity in a function of viral antigenic closeness: the more similar the viruses, the more cross-reactive the antibodies.
Results suggested that the presence of TOSV NT-Ab may be responsible for PUNV cross-neutralisation.
These findings call for further investigation to elucidate the potential effect of these newly discovered phleboviruses on human health in these areas.
376.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes that antigenic similarity causes serological cross-reactivity and reduces the ability of common serological assays to discriminate which virus caused infection. It notes that closely related viruses are more cross-reactive, that TOSV antibodies can produce PUNV cross-neutralisation, and that this complicates interpretation, supporting this option as the factor most strongly influencing serologic interpretation.

376.Option: The frequency of virus isolation from sand flies is the best predictor of human infection risk, even when serological evidence suggests otherwise
Evidence: virus studies conducted on sand flies trapped in the same regions suggested that TOSV circulates at lower level than PUNV, since the latter was detected and isolated 6 times versus 2 times for TOSV of a total of 8,206 sand flies trapped during 3 successive seasons from 2008 to 2010 [13], [17].
Our results indicate that PUNV (or closely related antigenic variants) can infect humans, but it occurs seldom in a region where the virus circulates at high level in sand fly populations.
This demonstrates that although the two viruses are present in sand fly populations, TOSV is involved at a much higher frequency in human infection than PUNV.
376.Rationale: The passage provides a counterexample: although PUNV was isolated more frequently from sand flies than TOSV, human serology showed far more TOSV infections. Thus vector isolation frequency is not necessarily the best predictor of human infection risk and cannot supersede serological findings—especially given serological cross-reactivity issues. Therefore this option is incorrect.

376.Option: Serological tests using ELISA or IFAT are sufficient to distinguish between closely related phleboviruses when applied in regions with known co-circulation
Evidence: Both viruses belong to the same virus species, Sandfly fever Naples, and consequently it is difficult to distinguish between them by using broadly reactive serological tests, such as inhibition hemagglutination assay, complement fixation assay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) or indirect immunofluorescence assay [6], [13], [18], [22], [23], [24].
Indeed, serological cross-reactivity in a function of viral antigenic closeness: the more similar the viruses, the more cross-reactive the antibodies.
the lack of discrimination of ELISA cannot solve the problem of cross-reactivity and thus cannot indisputably involve TOSV as the etiologic agent of the CNS infections.
subsequently lead to conducting studies using neutralisation test which are the only assay with suitable discriminative capacity [6], [18], [26].
376.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that ELISA and IFAT (indirect immunofluorescence assay) are not sufficient to discriminate closely related phleboviruses due to cross-reactivity, and that neutralisation tests are needed because they have superior discriminative capacity. Therefore this option is incorrect.

377.Option: Older age at infection may lead to reduced immune clearance of the virus, resulting in prolonged viral persistence and chronic joint inflammation
Evidence: Subjects aged ≥45 years were significantly more likely to belong to the “persistence” group than those aged <45 years (OR = 4.2, 95% CI 1.9–9.3).
In the multivariate model, three variables remained independently associated with persistence, namely age ≥45 years, initial severity of joint pain, and comorbid osteoarthritis (Table 5).
377.Rationale: While the passage shows older age is epidemiologically associated with persistence of rheumatic symptoms (quotes above), it does not provide evidence for the specific biological mechanism proposed (reduced immune clearance leading to prolonged viral persistence). The text does not mention impaired viral clearance or viral persistence as a mechanism among older individuals, so the causal claim in this option is speculative and not supported by the passage.

377.Option: Chronic rheumatic symptoms may result from an autoimmune response triggered by the acute viral infection, with severity of initial symptoms indicating stronger immune activation
Evidence: Moreover, chronic rheumatic manifestations (in particular, the duration of morning stiffness of ≥45 minutes as a typical sign of inflammatory pattern) at month 15 following infection were assessed.
Finally, the severity of pain at disease onset was strongly associated with persistence (OR = 3.6, 95% CI 1.6–8.1).
377.Rationale: The passage documents an inflammatory clinical pattern (morning stiffness) and reports that greater initial symptom severity predicts persistence, which is compatible with an immune-mediated process (quotes above). However, the authors do not describe or present evidence specifically of an autoimmune mechanism triggered by CHIKV. Because the passage does not mention autoimmunity or provide direct evidence of autoimmune pathology, the specific autoimmune hypothesis in this option is not supported by the provided text.

377.Option: Chikungunya virus may directly infect and damage joint tissues, and the extent of this damage is proportional to the severity of initial pain
Evidence: This fact has been highlighted by evaluations conducted through clinical series of CHIKV-infected patients which clearly identified a wide range of rheumatologic manifestations including tenosynovitis and lesions involving previously injured bones or joints [35].
Finally, the severity of pain at disease onset was strongly associated with persistence (OR = 3.6, 95% CI 1.6–8.1).
377.Rationale: The passage notes a range of rheumatologic manifestations, including tenosynovitis and lesions in previously injured joints, and documents that initial severity predicts persistence (quotes above). However, it does not provide direct evidence that CHIKV directly infects joint tissues or that the degree of direct tissue damage is proportional to initial pain. The authors call for further investigation of pathophysiologic patterns, indicating that direct viral tissue-damage as the mechanism remains unproven in this study. Therefore this specific mechanistic claim is not supported by the text.

377.Option: The presence of hypertension may contribute to systemic inflammation that prolongs joint symptoms after acute infection
Evidence: The presence of an underlying illness was also found to be more frequent in this group (OR = 3.0, 95% CI 1.5–5.9), in particular the presence of hypertension or osteoarthritis.
In the multivariate model, three variables remained independently associated with persistence, namely age ≥45 years, initial severity of joint pain, and comorbid osteoarthritis (Table 5).
377.Rationale: Although hypertension is reported as more frequent in the persistence group (first quote), it did not remain an independent predictor in the multivariate model (second quote indicates the independent predictors were age, initial pain severity, and osteoarthritis). The passage does not discuss hypertension as a causal factor via systemic inflammation prolonging joint symptoms. Thus, the specific biological pathway proposed here is not supported by the evidence in the text.

377.Option: A severe acute inflammatory response may exacerbate pre-existing joint damage, leading to persistent symptoms
Evidence: Moreover, chronic rheumatic manifestations (in particular, the duration of morning stiffness of ≥45 minutes as a typical sign of inflammatory pattern) at month 15 following infection were assessed.
Finally, the severity of pain at disease onset was strongly associated with persistence (OR = 3.6, 95% CI 1.6–8.1).
The presence of an underlying illness was also found to be more frequent in this group (OR = 3.0, 95% CI 1.5–5.9), in particular the presence of hypertension or osteoarthritis.
In the multivariate model, three variables remained independently associated with persistence, namely age ≥45 years, initial severity of joint pain, and comorbid osteoarthritis (Table 5).
Hence, we recognize it can be very challenging to differentiate between osteoarthritis and Chikungunya-related arthralgia.
Nonetheless, the presence of osteoarthritis conditions before CHIKV infection appears to be an independent risk factor for developing late rheumatism manifestations.
377.Rationale: The passage documents an inflammatory clinical pattern (morning stiffness) and reports that greater initial pain severity strongly predicts persistent symptoms (quotes 1 and 2). It also shows pre-existing osteoarthritis is more frequent among those with persistence and is an independent risk factor (quotes 3, 4, and 6), and notes the difficulty in separating pre-existing joint disease from CHIKV-related arthralgia (quote 5). Taken together, these findings most directly support a biologically plausible mechanism in which a severe acute inflammatory response (reflected by intense initial pain and inflammatory signs) aggravates pre-existing joint damage (osteoarthritis), resulting in prolonged or persistent rheumatic symptoms.

378.Option: The snail's immune system effectively clears or limits parasite establishment over time, reducing the proportion of persistently infected individuals in the population
Evidence: In the endemic regions of northeast Thailand, there is a high prevalence of O. viverrini infection in humans and fish, although the prevalence of infected Bithynia snails is low [6–10].
It has been found that the level of O. viverrini infection in Bithynia siamensis goniomphalos decreases over time, going from 62% infectivity at day 1 to less than 20% at 56 days post infection [11].

The immune system of a snail consists of both humoral and cellular arms. The hemolymph, composed of plasma and hemocytes, not only serves as a source of metabolites and nutrients for parasite development, but also plays a key role in the snail’s response to infections [12–15].

hemocytes represent the main immune effectors, playing a major role in the recognition and killing of invading pathogens by different methods such as phagocytosis, encapsulation, and cytotoxic reactions [16–18].
The results observed in the present study suggest that early stages of the infection are critical for the success or failure of parasite establishment.
378.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the paradox (high human/fish prevalence but low snail prevalence) and provides direct evidence that snail infection levels fall markedly over time (62% → <20% by 56 days). It also describes robust cellular and humoral immune mechanisms (hemocytes, plasma proteins) that recognize and kill parasites and emphasizes early-stage responses as critical for parasite establishment. Together these statements support that snail immune clearance or limitation over time is the most likely explanation for the low proportion of persistently infected snails despite high exposure.

378.Option: The parasite's life cycle is inefficient in snails, resulting in poor survival of miracidia and cercariae, which limits transmission to fish and humans
Evidence: In the endemic regions of northeast Thailand, there is a high prevalence of O. viverrini infection in humans and fish, although the prevalence of infected Bithynia snails is low [6–10].
It has been found that the level of O. viverrini infection in Bithynia siamensis goniomphalos decreases over time, going from 62% infectivity at day 1 to less than 20% at 56 days post infection [11].

The cercariae exit the snails and penetrate the skin of cyprinid fish hosts to encyst in the muscle and form metacercariae, the infective stage for humans, and which are acquired upon ingestion of undercooked or raw fish [4, 5].
378.Rationale: If the parasite life cycle in snails were broadly inefficient (poor survival of miracidia/cercariae), one would expect lower infection prevalence in fish and humans as well; however the passage reports high prevalence in humans and fish. Instead the passage documents substantial initial snail infectivity that declines over time, implying that initial infection can be common but is subsequently lost—consistent with host clearance rather than inherent parasite inefficiency in snails. Therefore this option is not supported by the passage.

378.Option: The low prevalence of infected snails is due to a high rate of natural mortality among snails following infection, leading to rapid population turnover in the environment
Evidence: In the endemic regions of northeast Thailand, there is a high prevalence of O. viverrini infection in humans and fish, although the prevalence of infected Bithynia snails is low [6–10].
It has been found that the level of O. viverrini infection in Bithynia siamensis goniomphalos decreases over time, going from 62% infectivity at day 1 to less than 20% at 56 days post infection [11].

The immune system of a snail consists of both humoral and cellular arms. The hemolymph, composed of plasma and hemocytes, not only serves as a source of metabolites and nutrients for parasite development, but also plays a key role in the snail’s response to infections [12–15].
378.Rationale: The passage provides evidence for within-host decline in detectable infection and details immune mechanisms that can clear infection, but it does not present any data or statements about high post-infection snail mortality or rapid population turnover as the cause of low prevalence. The documented drop in infectivity over time (62% to <20%) is more directly explained by clearance than by mortality. Because the passage lacks evidence for elevated mortality causing low prevalence, this option is not supported.

378.Option: The snail population in endemic areas is genetically resistant to O. viverrini, leading to a low rate of initial infection and subsequent transmission
Evidence: It has been found that the level of O. viverrini infection in Bithynia siamensis goniomphalos decreases over time, going from 62% infectivity at day 1 to less than 20% at 56 days post infection [11].

The immune system of a snail consists of both humoral and cellular arms. The hemolymph, composed of plasma and hemocytes, not only serves as a source of metabolites and nutrients for parasite development, but also plays a key role in the snail’s response to infections [12–15].
The results observed in the present study suggest that early stages of the infection are critical for the success or failure of parasite establishment.
378.Rationale: Genetic resistance causing low initial infection would predict few snails infected at day 1, but the passage shows high initial infectivity (62% at day 1) followed by decline over weeks. That pattern indicates that initial infection commonly occurs and is later lost, consistent with immune-mediated clearance rather than an inherently low rate of initial infection due to genetic resistance. The passage therefore does not support this option.

378.Option: Environmental factors such as water temperature and pH in endemic regions inhibit miracidial penetration and sporocyst development in snails
Evidence: In the endemic regions of northeast Thailand, there is a high prevalence of O. viverrini infection in humans and fish, although the prevalence of infected Bithynia snails is low [6–10].
It has been found that the level of O. viverrini infection in Bithynia siamensis goniomphalos decreases over time, going from 62% infectivity at day 1 to less than 20% at 56 days post infection [11].
Prior to experimental infection, the eggs were examined under light microscopy to determine full maturation by detecting active movement of the miracidium within the egg shell [45].
Prior to experimental infection ... were kept at room temperature (28±3°C) for 1 week to enable full development.
378.Rationale: The passage does not attribute low snail prevalence to environmental inhibition of miracidial penetration or sporocyst development. Experimental conditions described (e.g., eggs developing at 28±3°C and hatching miracidia) indicate that temperature in controlled settings supports parasite development rather than inhibiting it. The text instead emphasizes immune mechanisms and within-host decline in infection prevalence over time. Because no evidence in the passage links environmental factors like temperature or pH to inhibited snail infection, this option is unsupported.

379.Option: The use of pooled larvae from multiple samples may obscure individual variation and reduce the sensitivity to detect stage-specific gene expression differences
Evidence: Total RNA was extracted by thawing pooled samples of L1 and L3i larvae at 37°C in a warm water bath and centrifuging the samples at 4°C (805× g) for 10 minutes to obtain a pellet.
For the present study, four technical replicate experiments using pooled L1 and L3i larvae were performed, including one dye swap.
Post-parasitic L1 and L3i larvae (distinct from those hybridized onto the microarray) were collected and total RNA made as described above.
379.Rationale: While the passage confirms that pooled larvae were used (supporting the concern in the option), the investigators performed technical replicates and also validated microarray findings using distinct (non-pooled) L1 and L3i larvae for qPCR. The presence of validation on independent samples reduces the likelihood that pooling alone critically undermines the interpretation. The authors did not cite pooling as a principal limitation; instead they emphasized annotation/genome-related limits. Therefore, although pooling can obscure individual-level variation in principle, the passage does not support that this factor is the most critical influence on interpreting the stage-specific expression differences.

379.Option: The microarray platform may not capture all differentially expressed genes due to probe design limitations, leading to false-negative results for stage-specific genes
Evidence: ESTs (11,335) were identified from L1 and L3i cDNA libraries created as part of the nematode EST project [6], [7]. ESTs were organized into 3,571 contigs by bioinformatics analysis [14].

Oligonucleotide probes designed to hybridize with these contigs were used to develop early versions (V1 and V2) of chips manufactured by Combimatrix (Irvine, CA) based on a variety of algorithms for oligonucleotide design.

Of the six oligonucleotides designed per target, one was designed using the Array Designer program..., two were designed using E-Array... two were designed using E-array 'best Tm' method, and the last was a 40-mer designed using Array Designer. Probes were selected to avoid cross-hybridization to other sequences in the target (contig) dataset manufactured by Agilent SurePrint.

Because a number of C. elegans genes did not have S. stercoralis orthologs that were also differentially expressed according to our predefined ‘cutoffs,’ it was difficult to formulate gene lists organized into functional categories with at least 5 contigs. This limited our ability to analyze biochemical or metabolic pathways of potential importance.
379.Rationale: The microarray was built from an EST-derived contig set (3,571 contigs) and specific probes were selected for those contigs; genes not represented in the EST collection or for which probes performed poorly would be missed. The authors explicitly describe probe selection and contig-based design, and note limitations in gene representation and pathway analysis. Those facts support that probe design/coverage of the platform can produce false negatives and thus critically influence interpretation of observed stage-specific expression differences.

379.Option: The 7-day environmental incubation period prior to L3i collection may confound observed gene expression differences by introducing stress-related or adaptive changes unrelated to larval stage
Evidence: L3i larvae were recovered after 7 days of stool incubation at 25°C.

L3i larvae, by contrast, can survive in harsh environmental conditions, enabled by a comparatively thickened cuticle, constricted gastrointestinal tract, and closed mouth. These larvae are developmentally arrested, non-feeding, stress resistant, and long lived [3]–[5].
Biased expression of genes encoding heat shock proteins in the S. stercoralis L3i relative to L1 larvae, as suggested by GSEA, is consistent with this role.
379.Rationale: The passage documents the 7-day incubation used to obtain L3i and shows L3i are intrinsically stress-resistant and expected to express stress-associated genes (e.g., heat shock proteins). Thus the incubation is a standard, biologically relevant step to generate the infective L3i stage rather than an artificial stressor unrelated to stage. The authors interpret heat shock protein enrichment as consistent with L3i biology. The passage does not present the incubation period as a key confounder; therefore, although incubation could in theory introduce additional stress, the text does not support it as the most critical factor affecting interpretation.

379.Option: The lack of a complete S. stercoralis genome sequence limits the ability to accurately annotate and interpret gene expression differences between L1 and L3i larvae
Evidence: The complete genome sequence of S. stercoralis is not available to date.
Approximately one-third of S. stercoralis genes are of unknown function (manual annotation).

Inferred functional annotations of an analogous nematode C. elegans, while useful, may not be directly applicable to S. stercoralis, as suggested by interspecies differences uncovered in the present comparative microarray analysis.

Because a number of C. elegans genes did not have S. stercoralis orthologs that were also differentially expressed according to our predefined ‘cutoffs,’ it was difficult to formulate gene lists organized into functional categories with at least 5 contigs. This limited our ability to analyze biochemical or metabolic pathways of potential importance.
379.Rationale: The authors explicitly state that absence of a complete S. stercoralis genome and the large fraction of genes of unknown function limit accurate annotation and pathway-level interpretation. They emphasize that reliance on C. elegans annotations may be inappropriate because of interspecies differences. These statements identify genome incompleteness and annotation gaps as a central constraint on interpreting which differentially expressed contigs correspond to known functions or pathways, making this factor critically influential in understanding the biological meaning of observed L3i vs L1 expression differences.

379.Option: The use of laboratory dogs as a host model may introduce host-specific gene expression patterns that are not representative of human infections
Evidence: All larvae used in this analysis were obtained from laboratory dogs infected with S. stercoralis, UPD strain [12].
This finding is also consistent with functional genomics analyses of the C. elegans and human genomes where significant numbers of genes of unknown function were identified [48], [49].

Inferred functional annotations of an analogous nematode C. elegans, while useful, may not be directly applicable to S. stercoralis, as suggested by interspecies differences uncovered in the present comparative microarray analysis.
379.Rationale: The passage states that larvae were derived from laboratory dogs, but it does not flag host-derived effects on parasite gene expression as a principal limitation. The authors focus their limitations on incomplete genome/annotation and EST-based representation rather than on dog versus human host effects. While host environment can influence parasite biology in general, the text provides no specific evidence that dog-derived larvae introduce host-specific transcriptional artifacts that critically bias interpretation relative to human infections. Therefore this option is not supported as the most critical influence by the passage.

380.Option: The use of qPCR as a comparator method introduces bias because it is less sensitive than ddPCR, leading to underestimation of ddPCR's true performance
Evidence: To evaluate the efficiency of the established ddPCR assay for whole blood samples, 94 whole blood samples (61 SAT positive and 33 SAT negative) were tested by qPCR and ddPCR.
Of 61 confirmed sero-positive SAT samples analysed by ddPCR and qPCR, 54 and 46 were positive, respectively.
the positivity rate of ddPCR (88.5%) was higher than that of qPCR (75.4%), respectively, which proved that ddPCR was more sensitive.
380.Rationale: While the passage shows qPCR is less sensitive than ddPCR (ddPCR 88.5% vs qPCR 75.4% positivity among SAT positives), qPCR was not used as the reference standard in this study. The study compared molecular methods against serology (SAT) and did not rely on qPCR as the gold standard. Therefore qPCR's lower sensitivity does not by itself introduce bias that would 'underestimate ddPCR's true performance' when the diagnostic performance is being estimated relative to the stated reference (SAT). The primary potential bias arises from the choice of the reference standard (SAT), not from qPCR serving as the comparator.

380.Option: The inclusion of patients with clinical symptoms and epidemiological risk factors may lead to overestimation of the positive predictive value due to a high-prevalence study population
Evidence: This study included 94 subjects, all with clinical symptoms of brucellosis and epidemiological risk factors.
To evaluate the efficiency of the established ddPCR assay for whole blood samples, 94 whole blood samples (61 SAT positive and 33 SAT negative) were tested by qPCR and ddPCR.
380.Rationale: It is true that enrolling symptomatic, high-risk patients produces a high-prevalence sample (61/94 seropositive here), which will inflate predictive values (PPV) relative to a general-population screening setting. However, the question asks which factor most critically affects the validity of the estimated diagnostic performance when comparing ddPCR to the reference standard. Although spectrum/prevalence effects impact PPV/NPV, the more critical validity threat to the accuracy of sensitivity/specificity estimates in this study is misclassification from the imperfect reference standard (SAT) and absence of culture. Thus, while the option describes a real concern about generalizability and predictive values, it is not the most critical factor undermining the estimated diagnostic performance in this study.

380.Option: The use of a non-validated ddPCR assay with unconfirmed primer specificity may result in false-positive results and compromise the accuracy of the diagnostic performance estimate
Evidence: We designed primers and probes according to bcsp31 of Brucella as the target gene.

For specificity analysis, DNA from Y. enterocolitica O:9, S. typhimurium, V. parahaemolyticus, E. coli O157 and V. cholerae O1 were tested by ddPCR assay. As shown in Fig 4, only Brucella DNA tested positive, while DNA from other organisms tested negative. The results confirmed that this method was specific for detection of Brucella.
The established ddPCR assay displayed good accuracy, repeatability and specificity.
380.Rationale: The passage documents primer/probe design and explicit analytical specificity testing against a panel of potentially cross-reactive organisms, with the statement that 'only Brucella DNA tested positive' and that the method was confirmed specific. It also reports analytical sensitivity and repeatability assessments. Therefore the ddPCR assay was not 'non-validated with unconfirmed primer specificity'—the authors performed validation steps. While further external validation would always strengthen confidence, the specific concern raised by this option is not supported by the passage as a primary validity threat.

380.Option: The use of serological testing (SAT) as the reference standard, rather than bacterial culture, introduces potential misclassification bias and undermines the validity of the diagnostic performance estimate
Evidence: Diagnosis of brucellosis was performed using the Diagnostic Criteria for Brucellosis WS269-2019 [16].
To evaluate the efficiency of the established ddPCR assay for whole blood samples, 94 whole blood samples (61 SAT positive and 33 SAT negative) were tested by qPCR and ddPCR.

The gold standard for brucellosis is isolation of Brucella, which is not suitable for routine clinical practice due to the low clinical isolation rate, time-consuming nature (more than 3 days), and risk of laboratory-acquired infection [18,19].
First, blood culture could not be performed due to a relatively low positive rate and poor pathogen detection capacity in county-level CDCs.
380.Rationale: The passage makes clear that culture/isolation is the true gold standard but was not performed; instead, case classification relied on SAT (serology) per the diagnostic criteria. An imperfect reference standard (here SAT, which can be subject to false positives/negatives and a 'narrow window for antibody detection' as noted elsewhere in the text) can misclassify true infection status and bias estimates of sensitivity and specificity for ddPCR. This source of differential or non-differential misclassification relative to the reference standard therefore most critically undermines the validity of the reported diagnostic performance.

382.Option: The presence of multiple cattle breeds, including West African Short Horn and Sanga, in close proximity to human settlements increases the likelihood of M. bovis transmission through shared environmental exposure
Evidence: Majority of cattle sampled were of the West African Short Horn (WASH) breed (80.0%, 307/384 [data missing for 54 animals])
Other breeds of cattle included Sanga (52), Ndama (16), Fulani white (4), Zebu (3), and Gudaly (2) (S1 Fig).
livestock breeding in the region predominantly relies on semi-intensive farming by smallholder farmers within urban and peri-urban communities.
382.Rationale: While the passage documents multiple cattle breeds and notes that farming occurs in urban/peri-urban settings, it does not attribute breed diversity itself as a driver of zoonotic transmission. The text links close human–cattle relationships, semi‑intensive systems, and shared spaces to transmission risk, not the mere presence of multiple breeds. Therefore the specific claim that breed diversity is the key feature driving ongoing transmission is not supported by the passage.

382.Option: The use of pyruvate-supplemented media in culture methods enhanced the detection of M. bovis, suggesting that the pathogen is more prevalent in cattle and thus more likely to transmit to humans
Evidence: There were significantly more isolates on pyruvate-containing media compared to glycerol-containing media (p<0.001) (Fig 6).

The relatively high M. bovis recovered from this study could be attributed to the use of 0.5% sodium pyruvate supplemented LJ media in our culture method for isolation, thus, we observed that there were significantly more isolates on pyruvate-containing media compared to glycerol-containing media (p<0.001).
livestock breeding in the region predominantly relies on semi-intensive farming by smallholder farmers within urban and peri-urban communities.
382.Rationale: Although pyruvate-supplemented media improved laboratory recovery of M. bovis (a methodological point supported by the passage), this is a laboratory detection issue rather than a feature of the livestock production system that facilitates transmission to humans. The passage treats this as an explanation for higher isolation yield, not as evidence of a production-system mechanism promoting ongoing zoonotic transmission.

382.Option: The high density of cattle in the Northern region, particularly in urban and peri-urban areas, facilitates frequent direct contact between humans and infected animals, enabling ongoing M. bovis transmission
Evidence: The regions are predominantly low-lying grassland, interspersed with guinea savannah woodland. The inhabitants are mostly livestock and cereal farmers producing more than 70% and 80% of Ghana’s cereal and livestock, respectively [19,20].
livestock breeding in the region predominantly relies on semi-intensive farming by smallholder farmers within urban and peri-urban communities.
Thus, daily slaughter was relatively higher in the Northern region abattoir.

The close relationship between the communities and cattle offers potential zoonotic transmission between cattle and humans and humans to cattle in areas where human M. tuberculosis prevalence is very high.
382.Rationale: The passage describes high cattle density, semi‑intensive smallholder systems in urban/peri‑urban settings, and frequent slaughter/market activity in the Northern region. It explicitly links the close relationship between communities and cattle to potential zoonotic transmission. These production‑system features (high density and proximity) directly support ongoing cattle–human transmission.

382.Option: The movement of cattle across national borders combined with the consumption of raw milk and meat from unapproved slaughter slabs in close human settlements
Evidence: These abattoirs receive large and small ruminants from surrounding livestock markets and neighbouring West African countries (Burkina Faso, Mali, Niger, and Togo), which are slaughtered, and carcasses sold in the markets.
It has been suggested that the transhumance cattle movement across the sub region might be attributed to the distribution of SB0944 M. bovis clonal variants in the region [59,60].
Consumption of raw milk and meat products from cattle sometimes slaughtered from unapproved slaughter slabs is common in the region.

The close relationship between the communities and cattle offers potential zoonotic transmission between cattle and humans and humans to cattle in areas where human M. tuberculosis prevalence is very high.
382.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes cross‑border and transhumance movement of cattle into the region and links such movement to the geographic distribution of M. bovis strains. It also documents common consumption of raw milk and meat from animals slaughtered in unapproved settings and close human–cattle interactions. Together these are production‑system and market/consumption practices that strongly support ongoing zoonotic transmission risk.

383.Option: Transmission likely occurs through horizontal gene transfer between strains in the human host, leading to the observed genetic similarity among co-infecting strains
Evidence: we infer from this that the results are consistent with mixed infection in 25% of human samples, which can either be explained by mixed strains in mites or multiple bites of a single human by mono-infected mites.
Recombination between different strains of O. tsutsugamushi could either occur in the mite, or in the rodent reservoir which may become infected on multiple, independent occasions.
383.Rationale: The passage attributes mixed infections to acquisition of multiple strains (mixed strains in mites or multiple bites) and states that recombination is thought to occur in the mite or rodent reservoir, not within the human host. Therefore horizontal gene transfer in humans is not the supported inference for why co-infecting strains are genetically similar.

383.Option: Transmission likely occurs through multiple independent bites from different mites, each carrying genetically distinct strains from a diverse environmental pool
Evidence: we infer from this that the results are consistent with mixed infection in 25% of human samples, which can either be explained by mixed strains in mites or multiple bites of a single human by mono-infected mites.
in many cases where multiple strains were recovered from a single blood sample, that strains tended to be more similar to each other than to other strains in the study.
383.Rationale: The option posits bites from different mites carrying genetically distinct strains drawn from a diverse pool. The passage instead reports that co-infecting strains within individual patients tended to be more similar to each other than to strains from other hosts, which argues against acquisition from a highly diverse environmental pool of unrelated strains via independent bites.

383.Option: Transmission likely occurs through exposure to a localized reservoir of closely related strains, such as a mite population co-infected with multiple genetically similar strains
Evidence: we infer from this that the results are consistent with mixed infection in 25% of human samples, which can either be explained by mixed strains in mites or multiple bites of a single human by mono-infected mites.

in many cases where multiple strains were recovered from a single blood sample, that strains tended to be more similar to each other than to other strains in the study. This may reflect the adaptation of particular bacterial genotypes to specific mite genotypes, although a more extensive dataset is needed from both human and mite hosts to examine this possibility more thoroughly.
This second hypothesis is supported by the detection of multiple antigenic strains of O. tsutsugamushi in both naturally infected and laboratory-reared chigger mites (Leptotrombidium spp.) [25].
383.Rationale: The passage explicitly offers mixed strains in mites as an explanation and documents that co-infecting strains in a patient are often more similar to each other than to strains from other hosts. It further cites evidence for multiple strains coexisting within mites. Together these passages support a localized reservoir (e.g., mites co-infected with closely related strains) as the likely transmission source when co-infecting strains are similar.

383.Option: Transmission likely occurs through a single mite bite that introduces multiple genetically diverse strains simultaneously, indicating high strain diversity within individual mites
Evidence: in many cases where multiple strains were recovered from a single blood sample, that strains tended to be more similar to each other than to other strains in the study.

we infer from this that the results are consistent with mixed infection in 25% of human samples, which can either be explained by mixed strains in mites or multiple bites of a single human by mono-infected mites.
383.Rationale: This option claims the co-infecting strains are genetically diverse within a single mite; the passage instead notes that co-infecting strains within hosts tended to be more similar to each other, consistent with closely related strains (e.g., within a local mite population) rather than highly diverse strains introduced simultaneously.

383.Option: Transmission likely occurs through a single mite bite that results in infection with a single strain, with the observed genetic similarity due to convergent evolution in the human host
Evidence: we infer from this that the results are consistent with mixed infection in 25% of human samples, which can either be explained by mixed strains in mites or multiple bites of a single human by mono-infected mites.

in many cases where multiple strains were recovered from a single blood sample, that strains tended to be more similar to each other than to other strains in the study. This may reflect the adaptation of particular bacterial genotypes to specific mite genotypes, although a more extensive dataset is needed from both human and mite hosts to examine this possibility more thoroughly.
383.Rationale: The passage documents true mixed infections in human samples (multiple genotypes detectable) and attributes similarity to shared source or adaptation to mite genotypes, not to rapid convergent evolution within the human host. Convergent evolution in the short timescale of infection is not supported by the text.

384.Option: Compare the genetic distance between viral sequences from different regions to infer the directionality of viral spread
Evidence: a standard discrete phylogeographic analysis using continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) to estimate the migration events between major geographic regions was set up [27].

The Bayes factor (BF) test, which determines statistically significant phylogeographic links, was performed using SPREAD 1.04. If BF>3, the phylogeographic link between two locations was considered to be statistically significant.

All FTLSV sequences currently available in GenBank are similar regardless of their sampling locations (>90% sequence similarity), suggesting that they descended from a relatively recent common ancestor.
384.Rationale: Simply comparing genetic distances between sequences is not the primary analytical approach used to infer directional, discrete migration events in this study. The authors explicitly used a discrete phylogeographic CTMC framework with BSSVS and Bayes factor testing to identify statistically supported migration links. Moreover, high overall sequence similarity (>90%) can limit inference from raw pairwise distances alone. Therefore option 0 is not the most direct or appropriate approach given the methods and goals described in the passage.

384.Option: Perform a Bayesian skyline plot to estimate changes in viral population size over time and infer periods of increased transmission
Evidence: Preliminary analyses were conducted to determine which clock (strict versus relaxed) and demographic (constant versus Bayesian Skyline population size) models were most appropriate for the datasets, with runs initially consisting of 10,000,000 generations.

An analysis of marginal likelihoods [25] indicated that the relaxed uncorrelated lognormal clock [26] and constant population size model were most appropriate for all three segments (log10 Bayes Factors >2 in all cases).
A standard discrete phylogeographic analysis using continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) to estimate the migration events between major geographic regions was set up [27].
384.Rationale: A Bayesian skyline plot estimates changes in effective population size through time and can indicate demographic expansions or contractions, but it does not directly identify discrete migration links or infer directional movement between geographic regions. The passage describes discrete phylogeographic CTMC/BSSVS and Bayes factor testing as the methods used to detect migration routes. The Bayesian Skyline was considered as a demographic model but the authors selected a constant population size for the phylogeographic analyses, and the skyline approach would not most directly support inference of discrete migration events.

384.Option: Use a maximum likelihood tree with geographic labels to visually trace the most likely path of viral spread from the root to terminal nodes
Evidence: Phylogenetic trees for the three viral RNA segments were constructed using the maximum likelihood (ML) method, a feature of the software MEGA 5 [20], and the Bayesian MCMC method applied using MrBayes 3.2.1 [21].

To determine the geographic origins and movements of FLTSV in China, the results of the Bayesian phylogeographic analyses were summarized by annotating the MCC tree nodes with their most probable location states via color labeling (Figure 4).
a standard discrete phylogeographic analysis using continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) to estimate the migration events between major geographic regions was set up [27].
384.Rationale: While ML trees with geographic labels can provide a visual sense of relationships and possible geographic clustering, the passage indicates the investigators relied on discrete Bayesian phylogeographic methods (CTMC with BSSVS and Bayes factor testing) and annotated MCC trees to statistically infer migration routes. Visual inspection of an ML tree alone is less direct and lacks the statistical machinery (BSSVS/BF) used here to support discrete migration events. Thus this option is not the most direct approach for the stated inference.

384.Option: Use a Bayes factor test with a threshold of BF > 3 to identify statistically significant, discrete transmission links between geographic regions
Evidence: a standard discrete phylogeographic analysis using continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) to estimate the migration events between major geographic regions was set up [27].

The Bayes factor (BF) test, which determines statistically significant phylogeographic links, was performed using SPREAD 1.04. If BF>3, the phylogeographic link between two locations was considered to be statistically significant.

In a further investigation of these diffusion patterns, the BF test identified three well-supported (BF>3) migration routes for FTLSV (Figure 5D): from Huaiyangshan to Jiangsu (BF = 9.74), from Jiangsu to Shandong (BF = 3.35), and from Jiangsu to Liaoning (BF = 3.17).
384.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes using a discrete phylogeographic CTMC framework with BSSVS and then applying a Bayes factor test (BF>3) to determine statistically significant phylogeographic links. The authors used this exact approach to infer discrete migration routes (and reported specific BF-supported routes). Therefore this option directly matches the analytical approach used to support inference of movement via discrete migration events.

384.Option: Apply a continuous diffusion model with a constant rate of spread across all geographic regions to estimate the probability of viral movement between locations
Evidence: a standard discrete phylogeographic analysis using continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) to estimate the migration events between major geographic regions was set up [27].

To investigate the phylogeographic diffusion process along the posterior sets of the trees, relationships between locations were identified using the Bayesian stochastic variable search selection (BSSVS) applications in BEAST version 2.0.2 [22].
Following our discrete phylogeographic visualizations, we used SPREAD 1.04 [28] to estimate the putative spatiotemporal pattern of the spread and visualize the diffusion of the virus.
384.Rationale: A continuous diffusion model (treating space as continuous and modeling movement as a diffusion process) is conceptually different from the discrete-state CTMC/BSSVS approach used in this study. The passage describes a discrete phylogeographic framework to estimate migration events between predefined regions and to identify supported migration links; it does not describe applying a continuous diffusion model with a constant rate. Therefore option 4 does not most directly support the discrete-event inference the question asks about.

385.Option: The decline in night soil use over time reduced the exposure window, leading to fewer infections and making it harder to detect a statistical association in the 2010 data
Evidence: The mean quantity of night soil applied per household declined from 67 buckets per household in 2007 to 32 buckets per household in 2010 (Table 2).
Human waste was used as an agricultural fertilizer in 56% and 46% of households surveyed in 2007 and 2010, respectively.
We found a strong positive association between the amount of night soil used in a village and human schistosomiasis in 2007 but not in 2010.

There were a number of notable changes in the study region from 2007 to 2010: a modest decline in human infection prevalence and intensity, a decline in the frequency and quantity of reported night soil use, a 39% decline in bovine ownership, an increase in SES and ongoing schistosomiasis control activities—changes that could have shifted the dominant drivers of human infection.
385.Rationale: The passage documents a clear decline in both the proportion of households using night soil and the mean quantity applied between 2007 and 2010, and notes a strong association in 2007 that was absent in 2010. A reduction in exposure intensity and frequency can reduce infections and statistical power to detect associations; the authors explicitly cite declines in night soil use among the notable changes that could explain the change in association.

385.Option: The relative contribution of night soil to transmission diminished over time, making the system more sensitive to random fluctuations and reducing the detectable association in statistical models
Evidence: The inconsistent associations between night soil use and infection may reflect shifts in the relative importance of different schistosome sources in a changing environment.

There were a number of notable changes in the study region from 2007 to 2010: a modest decline in human infection prevalence and intensity, a decline in the frequency and quantity of reported night soil use, a 39% decline in bovine ownership, an increase in SES and ongoing schistosomiasis control activities—changes that could have shifted the dominant drivers of human infection.

We suspect schistosomiasis transmission at low infection intensities is highly stochastic—when parasite loads are low, small environmental and behavior changes can lead to substantial shifts in transmission dynamics—creating both challenges and opportunities for elimination.
We found a strong positive association between the amount of night soil used in a village and human schistosomiasis in 2007 but not in 2010.
385.Rationale: The authors propose that changing relative contributions of infection sources (internal vs external, bovines, etc.) could shift which drivers dominate transmission. They also state that at low parasite loads transmission is highly stochastic, so diminished relative contribution from night soil would make associations less consistent and harder to detect—precisely the mechanism described in this option.

385.Option: The decline in bovine ownership reduced the overall parasite load in the environment, decreasing the likelihood of infection regardless of night soil use and masking the true association
Evidence: There were a number of notable changes in the study region from 2007 to 2010: a modest decline in human infection prevalence and intensity, a decline in the frequency and quantity of reported night soil use, a 39% decline in bovine ownership, an increase in SES and ongoing schistosomiasis control activities—changes that could have shifted the dominant drivers of human infection.
Other potential sources of schistosomes include bovines, which produce large quantities of waste and are used to plow fields, placing them in close proximity to irrigation ditches where snails live.

Bovines have been shown to play an important role in S. japonicum transmission in the lakes region of China [34], and we previously found high prevalence of bovine infections in our study region [17].

Hence one interpretation of the lack of association between night soil use and infection in 2010 is that human schistosomiasis infection risk in 2010 is dominated by external sources and/or bovines, due to the decline in night soil use and human infections from 2007 to 2010.
385.Rationale: The passage identifies bovines as an important infection source and documents a 39% decline in bovine ownership between study years. The authors explicitly suggest changing contributions from bovines could alter infection risk patterns and help explain the loss of association with night soil in 2010, consistent with this option.

385.Option: Improved sanitation infrastructure reduced the viability of schistosome eggs in night soil, diminishing the risk of infection and weakening the observed association over time
Evidence: First, we suspected that increased access to improved sanitation in 2010 may have reduced S. japonicum egg content in night soil and, subsequently, the risks of this practice. But this does not appear to be the case.
While access to improved sanitation increased, the fraction of night soil that came from unimproved sources changed minimally from 2007 to 2010 (from 79% to 72%).
We were surprised to find that night soil from households with improved sanitation was positively associated with S. japonicum infection prevalence in 2007.

Controlled studies have demonstrated that anaerobic biogas digesters, the predominant improved sanitation system in the region, remove viable S. japonicum eggs from effluent through chemical inactivation and sedimentation [18, 19]. However, ambient temperatures can impact biogas digestion, particularly in household level systems such as the ones found in our study region with low thermal mass [38] and the length of S. japonicum survival in biogas systems increases at lower temperatures [19]. Anecdotally, some farmers in our study villages report little or no methane production in the winter months, which suggests night soil extracted in the winter or spring may not be sufficiently digested and may contain viable S. japonicum eggs.
385.Rationale: Although improved sanitation can in principle reduce egg viability, the authors examined this explanation and concluded it did not appear to account for the 2010 findings: the share of night soil from unimproved sources changed little, and night soil from improved systems was unexpectedly associated with infection in 2007. They also note biogas digesters may not reliably inactivate eggs under local conditions. Thus the passage argues against this factor as the primary explanation for the weakened association, making the option incorrect.

386.Option: The stability of Wol GreA in the host environment must be assessed to ensure consistent binding to RNAP across different physiological conditions
Evidence: The effect of the denaturants urea and guanidine hydrochloride (GdmCl) on the structural properties of Wol GreA and both of its domains was investigated by equilibrating these recombinants (5-µM) with varying concentrations of the denaturants for 4 h at 4°C in Tris buffer (50-mM, pH 7.0) [38].

native protein conformations were partially unfolded by these denaturants and 8-M urea and 6-M GdmCl brought about 50-53%, 36–38%, and 55–57% unfolding of Wol GreA, Wol NTD, and Wol CTD, respectively (Figure 7B, C).

To determine whether Wol GreA, Wol NTD, and Wol CTD native forms are properly folded, their fluorescence spectra were recorded. Data exhibited a characteristic peak of buried Tyr at sharp 308-nm, indicating that Wol GreA and both the domains exist in their apposite folded conformations.
386.Rationale: While the authors did assess protein folding and denaturant sensitivity in vitro (evidence above), the passage does not discuss host-environment stability or physiological resilience as the primary long-term concern. The passage emphasizes conservation, essentiality, dimerization/CTD binding, and the potential for drug resistance; those molecular features are more directly tied to long-term success of an anti-Wolbachia intervention. Stability in host conditions is relevant but not presented as the critical long-term determinant in this study, so this option is not the best choice based on the passage.

386.Option: The potential for resistance development due to mutations in the dimer interface or CTD binding site of Wol GreA must be evaluated to ensure sustained efficacy
Evidence: This also raises the possibility of parasites becoming resistant to conventional drugs. Indications of resistance are already being noticed in the cases of albendazole and ivermectin [4], [5].

GreA is indispensible, being responsible for facilitating transcription transition from abortive initiation to productive elongation by stimulating intrinsic transcript cleavage activity of RNAP [21].
CTD residues of Wol GreA are responsible for dimerization
Asp120, Val121, Ser122, Lys123, and Ser134 were found to be the residues of CTD leading to GreA dimerization (Table S2 in Text S1) (Figure 6D).

Lys82, Ser98, Asp104, Ser105, Glu106, Tyr109, Glu116, Asp120, Val121, Ser122, Ser127, Ser129, Lys140, Val147, Ser151, Glu153, and Phe163 are the a.a residues of Wol CTD through which Wol GreA interacts with all the subunits of endosymbiont RNAP.
out of 6257 essential bacterial genes, only 57 have been suggested as future drug targets, and GreA is among these [63].
386.Rationale: The passage identifies GreA as an indispensable, essential bacterial transcription factor and a proposed drug target, with specific CTD residues mediating both dimerization and RNAP binding. It also notes that resistance to existing antifilarials has already been observed. Together these facts make the emergence of resistance via mutations at the CTD or dimer interface a principal long-term risk for any GreA-targeting intervention. Evaluating the likelihood and consequences of such mutations (and their impact on inhibitor binding while preserving GreA function) is therefore a critical consideration for sustained efficacy.

386.Option: The ability of Wol GreA to bind multiple RNAP subunits simultaneously must be evaluated to determine if it can compensate for partial inhibition
Evidence: Far Western blotting was performed to determine the interaction of RNAP subunits with Wol GreA and its CTD. Both Wol GreA and Wol CTD acting as a bait protein had affinity for different subunits of RNAP resolved on blot.

Therefore, the characteristic bands, the prey subunits of RNAP (∼ 40-kDa α subunit, ∼160-kDa β subunit, ∼162-kDa β′ subunit, and ∼90-kDa σ subunit), were visualized on reaction with anti-His monoclonal antibody.

The data revealed that Lys82, Ser98, Asp104, Ser105, Glu106, Tyr109, Glu116, Asp120, Val121, Ser122, Ser127, Ser129, Lys140, Val147, Ser151, Glu153, and Phe163 are the a.a residues of Wol CTD through which Wol GreA interacts with all the subunits of endosymbiont RNAP.
However, the mechanism of direct interactions between Wol GreA and Wol RNAP leading to the alteration of enzyme conformation remains obscure, but is being pursued further (unpublished).
386.Rationale: The passage documents that Wol GreA (via its CTD) interacts with multiple RNAP subunits, but it does not provide evidence that this multivalent binding would allow GreA or the bacterium to 'compensate' for partial inhibition by a drug. The mechanism of interaction and consequences for RNAP function remain unclear in the text. While multivalent interactions are biochemically relevant, the most critical long-term considerations highlighted by the authors are conservation and resistance potential rather than compensatory binding capacity; thus this option is not supported as the primary critical factor.

386.Option: The conservation of Wol GreA across different Wolbachia strains must be confirmed to ensure broad-spectrum effectiveness of the intervention
Evidence: Clustal alignment of a.a sequence of Wol GreA with an array of other gram-negative proteobacterial species revealed varying degrees of homology. Wol GreA exhibited 90% alignment score with GreA of Wolbachia of Onchocerca ochengi, 89% with Wolbachia of Drosophila melanogaster, and 86% with Wolbachia of Culex quinquefasciatus.

In the comparison of protein sequences of GreA with various species of prokaryotic origin, Wol GreA demonstrated its essentiality, as the factor remained highly conserved, as shown by the protein sequence alignment that exhibited substantial similarity across the different bacterial species including α, β, and γ proteobacteria.
Phylogenetic analysis anticipated that GreA of α-proteobacteria would group together to form a distinct clade which was also endorsed with their respective score distance from the descended node.
386.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes that Wol GreA is highly conserved across diverse Wolbachia and related proteobacteria, with high alignment scores and a conserved domain architecture. For a GreA-targeted therapy to have broad and lasting public-health impact, it is essential that the target sequence/structure be conserved across Wolbachia strains infecting different filarial hosts; otherwise efficacy may be strain-specific. Thus confirming conservation across strains is a critical factor for long-term, broad-spectrum success.

387.Option: Failure to report the use of randomization in treatment assignment introduces selection bias and undermines the internal validity of efficacy estimates
Evidence: Regarding experimental design, a similar proportion of papers from both periods (5 / 39 and 7 / 44) declared treatment randomization or any effort to minimize subjective bias (e.g. randomized block design). None of the publications in any studied period substantiated the sample size employed (Table 9).
Treatment randomization, an essential step to avoid experimental bias, was declared only in 16% (7 / 44) publications.

Basic details about animal age, weight and microbiological status and source (Tables 3 and 4) were not provided in almost half of the reviewed publications, both pre- or post publication of ARRIVE guidelines.

Outcomes in animal models of T. cruzi infection are dependent on many factors, including animal species, strain, age, sex, T. cruzi strain, inoculum size and route of infection, among others [20]. Since several variables contribute to establish an in vivo model, reported information must be as detailed as possible.
387.Rationale: While lack of randomization is an important threat to internal validity because it can introduce selection bias, the question asks which factor most critically undermines assessing whether observed differences are due to treatment versus unmeasured biological variability. The passage emphasizes pervasive missing reporting of biological and environmental variables (species, strain, age, microbiological status, husbandry) that create unmeasured heterogeneity/confounding. Thus, although failure to randomize is a serious problem (evidence: randomization was scarcely reported), it is not the single most critical factor for the specific problem of unmeasured biological variability described throughout the review.

387.Option: Failure to report key biological and environmental variables introduces unmeasured heterogeneity that confounds the interpretation of treatment effects
Evidence: Variability of animal models for Chagas disease, and the heterogeneity in readout methods used to define drug response (e.g. parasitemia, PCR in blood, PCR in blood and in tissues) have led to highly variable results when evaluating new drug candidates.
crucial information related to species, strains, genetic background, microbiological status, husbandry conditions and procedures are not properly described or even missed in some occasions.

Basic details about animal age, weight and microbiological status and source (Tables 3 and 4) were not provided in almost half of the reviewed publications, both pre- or post publication of ARRIVE guidelines.

Outcomes in animal models of T. cruzi infection are dependent on many factors, including animal species, strain, age, sex, T. cruzi strain, inoculum size and route of infection, among others [20]. Since several variables contribute to establish an in vivo model, reported information must be as detailed as possible.

This wide gap between results in preclinical research and high failure rate in clinical trials may be explained, in part, by the scarce information contained in most experiments that employ laboratory animals...
387.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies missing reporting of biological (species, strain, genetic background, age, sex, microbiological status) and environmental/husbandry variables as pervasive. It also states that outcomes depend on many of these factors and that their omission produces high variability and undermines interpretation. Therefore failure to report these key variables directly produces unmeasured heterogeneity/confounding, and most critically prevents assessing whether outcome differences reflect true treatment effects or underlying biological variability.

387.Option: Inconsistent reporting of treatment administration routes across studies limits the ability to compare drug efficacy and may obscure true treatment effects
Evidence: Before ARRIVE guidelines publication, treatment was administered most commonly by oral (13 / 39) or intraperitoneal routes (14 / 39). After ARRIVE treatment was reportedly administered by the oral route in half of the reviewed papers, while eleven (25%) preferred the intraperitoneal route.
A wide range of inoculum sizes were reported, from less than 1,000 to more than 100,000 trypomastigotes per animal.
Variability of animal models for Chagas disease, and the heterogeneity in readout methods used to define drug response ... have led to highly variable results when evaluating new drug candidates.
387.Rationale: Variation in administration route is an important source of heterogeneity that can hinder cross-study comparison, and the passage documents inconsistent use of oral vs. intraperitoneal administration. However, the question focuses on the ability to distinguish treatment effects from unmeasured biological variability; inconsistent routes contribute to methodological heterogeneity but are less central than the pervasive failure to report fundamental biological and environmental variables (species/strain/age/microbiological status), which the authors identify as the primary undermining factor.

387.Option: Insufficient sample size justification in studies increases the risk of false-negative results and reduces statistical power to detect true treatment effects
Evidence: Sample size was not justified in any of the papers, suggesting that there was no previous sample size calculation, and that animal numbers were more a matter of habit than a statistical decision.
None of the publications in any studied period substantiated the sample size employed (Table 9).
These results agree with a previous quality of reporting survey which observed that only 5 in 72 (7%) of studies using mice informed sample size calculations or treatment randomization [21].
387.Rationale: Lack of sample size justification is a serious problem because it increases the risk of underpowered studies and false negatives. However, the question specifically asks about undermining the ability to assess whether observed differences are due to treatment effects versus unmeasured biological variability. Insufficient sample size affects power and precision but does not create the same kind of unmeasured heterogeneity/confounding that results from missing biological and environmental variable reporting, which the passage highlights as the central impediment to interpretation.

387.Option: Lack of standardized outcome measures across studies prevents reliable meta-analysis and may lead to inconsistent interpretation of treatment effects
Evidence: Variability of animal models for Chagas disease, and the heterogeneity in readout methods used to define drug response (e.g. parasitemia, PCR in blood, PCR in blood and in tissues) have led to highly variable results when evaluating new drug candidates.
Typically, parasitaemia values and mortality were the principal outcomes used to assess trypanocidal activity in the papers reviewed.

To evaluate the quality of information reported in articles referred to new compounds for Chagas disease, we contrasted descriptions of animal use and care with those descriptions suggested by the ARRIVE Guidelines...
387.Rationale: Heterogeneous outcome measures (parasitaemia, PCR, mortality) indeed limit comparability and complicate meta-analysis; the passage explicitly notes heterogeneity in readout methods leading to variable results. Nonetheless, the question centers on determining whether observed differences reflect treatment versus unmeasured biological variability. Measurement heterogeneity is important but the review emphasizes missing biological/environmental covariates (species, strain, age, microbiological status, husbandry) as the critical factor producing unmeasured heterogeneity and confounding interpretation of treatment effects.

388.Option: The high prevalence of asymptomatic infections is due to a delayed seroconversion process, which results in a lag between infection and detection, thereby reducing the apparent incidence of symptomatic cases
Evidence: They seem to be more sensitive for detecting asymptomatic infections than the antibody tests [17], as it is hypothesised that PCR of peripheral blood will be the first marker to become positive after infection (before antibody seroconversion takes place).
This means that individuals without symptomatic disease need on average about 146 days to develop LST-positivity after a PCR-positive finding.
The majority of infected hosts are estimated to become LST-positive five to six months after infection, without showing any sign of disease.
388.Rationale: The passage does describe delayed seroconversion (PCR positive before antibody/LST markers, and LST develops months after infection), so a detection lag exists. However, symptomatic KA is a clinical disease (fever, splenomegaly) that would be detected independently of serologic timing, and the passage explains the discrepancy by biology of asymptomatic infection and their role in transmission rather than by detection delays. The quoted text shows the timing delay but does not support the claim that this lag is the reason for low symptomatic incidence; rather, the passage emphasizes that most infections remain asymptomatic and do not progress to KA.

388.Option: Treatment failure rates are so high that most treated patients relapse into symptomatic disease, which maintains the incidence of KA despite widespread treatment
Evidence: A fraction of treatment failure of 5% is assumed [9], [21].

Pentavalent antimonials have been the first-line treatment for over 70 years. This treatment is long (20 to 30 days), is toxic (3–5% deaths due to treatment) and is accompanied by increasing failure rates; for instance in foci of the Bihar state, India, with up to 60% treatment failures, a phenomenon that is assumed to be caused by drug resistance [19].
These results suggest that transmission of KA is predominantly driven by asymptomatically infected hosts who are not eligible for treatment.
A short and effective treatment regimen or early case detection can reduce the prevalence of KA but has almost no effect on the incidence of the disease (Fig. 2 and Table 4).
388.Rationale: Although localized high failure rates are noted (e.g., up to 60% in Bihar for some drugs), the model and authors do not attribute the persistence of incidence to widespread relapse of treated patients. Instead, they assume modest overall failure rates (e.g., 5%) and explicitly conclude that ongoing transmission is driven by asymptomatic hosts who are not treated. The passage therefore contradicts the option that treatment failures (i.e., relapse of treated patients) are the main explanation for maintained incidence.

388.Option: The low incidence of symptomatic cases is due to a high rate of spontaneous recovery from asymptomatic infection, which prevents progression to clinical disease and reduces transmission
Evidence: Infection with Leishmania parasites proceeds in most cases asymptomatically, with only a minor fraction of cases subsequently developing KA.
