Evidence: Prophylactic vaccination must also induce durable protection. We tested the longevity of the humoral immune response. BALB/c mice treated with two vaccinations on days 0 and 21 ... were observed for about one year and antibodies in the serum of vaccinated mice were analyzed monthly.
Kinetic of virus-neutralizing antibodies in BALB/c mice from (D). Immunization points are indicated by arrows. Mean an SD is presented (n = 8/group).
400.Rationale: A single post-vaccination antibody measurement cannot yield a reliable estimate of antibody half-life; half-life requires at least two (preferably multiple) timepoints per individual to model decline. The passage demonstrates that the investigators used repeated measurements over time (monthly, kinetic plots) to assess durability, reinforcing that extrapolation from a single measurement is not appropriate to determine whether protection persists through a prolonged seasonal risk period.

400.Option: Compare the incidence of rabies cases in vaccinated versus unvaccinated populations during peak transmission months to assess whether protection lasts through the seasonal risk period
Evidence: vaccinating animals has greatly reduced human cases, transmission via stray dogs or other species, such as bats and raccoons, remains problematic in many countries.
A clinical phase I study to test safety and immunogenicity of this RABV-G mRNA vaccine in healthy volunteers has been initiated (EudraCT no. 2013-002171-17, NCT02241135).

These findings, together with the stability of mRNA vaccine [16,55] and projected as well as observed cost for production of mRNA vaccines [19–21] further substantiate the assumption that this new vaccination strategy may be extended to the prophylaxis and treatment of infectious diseases in general.
a widely recognized and standardized functional correlate of protection (virus neutralizing titers).
400.Rationale: Comparing population incidence between vaccinated and unvaccinated groups during peak months is an effectiveness/epidemiologic approach that can show real-world protection during that interval but is subject to confounding (differences in exposure, animal vaccination programs, healthcare access) and does not directly measure individual-level duration of immunity. The passage emphasizes use of virus-neutralizing antibody titers as a standardized correlate of protection and describes longitudinal immunogenicity studies (in animals) to evaluate durability—approaches that more directly determine whether protection persists through a seasonal period. Therefore, incidence comparisons are less direct and susceptible to bias and are not the most appropriate single method to answer the question about duration of protection.

400.Option: Conduct a longitudinal study measuring virus-neutralizing antibody titers over time in vaccinated individuals to determine if protection persists beyond the expected duration of seasonal exposure risk
Evidence: Prophylactic vaccination must also induce durable protection. We tested the longevity of the humoral immune response. BALB/c mice treated with two vaccinations on days 0 and 21 and doses of 1.25 μg, 5 μg, 20 μg, and 80 μg RABV-G mRNA vaccine or 0.1 human dose Rabipur were observed for about one year and antibodies in the serum of vaccinated mice were analyzed monthly.
Kinetic of virus-neutralizing antibodies in BALB/c mice from (D). Immunization points are indicated by arrows. Mean an SD is presented (n = 8/group).

In adult pigs ... were vaccinated intradermally with 80 μg RABV-G mRNA or buffer at days 0, 14 and 49 ... At different time points after immunization, sera were tested for rabies-specific neutralizing antibodies using FAVN testing (n = 6 pigs/group).
a widely recognized and standardized functional correlate of protection (virus neutralizing titers).
400.Rationale: The passage itself uses longitudinal measurement of virus-neutralizing antibody (VN) titers (monthly in mice, multiple timepoints in pigs) to assess durability of the humoral response and to determine whether protective titers persist. VN titers are a standardized correlate of protection for rabies; measuring them repeatedly over the seasonal period directly answers whether immunity remains above the protective threshold throughout the risk window, making a longitudinal serologic study the most appropriate method.

401.Option: The detection of Neoehrlichia mikurensis in bat ticks that fed on domestic dogs and bats indicates that these ticks are primary vectors for zoonotic transmission of this pathogen to humans, warranting immediate public health intervention.
Evidence: We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex (n = 1, EW16, Romania) associated with M. schreibersii and I. vespertilionis (n = 3, EW54, Romania; EW75, EW159, Hungary), previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
These results suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a role in the transmission and maintenance of this pathogen, not only between bat hosts but among other domestic and wildlife species.

However, this probably has moderate epidemiological significance, similar to the role of the hedgehog tick (I. hexagonus), which is taxonomically closely related to bat-associated ticks (subgenus Pholeoixodes) and usually carries N. mikurensis with low prevalence [49].
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.
The presence of pathogens in field-collected ticks does not provide evidence of vectorial capacity, as they may simply originate from the host’s blood meal.
401.Rationale: Although N. mikurensis DNA was detected in bat-associated ticks that had fed on dogs and bats, the authors explicitly caution that detection alone does not prove vectorial capacity or that these ticks are primary vectors to humans. They characterize the likely epidemiological significance as "moderate" and note that frequency of non-bat feeding and vectorial capacity must be determined before assessing public-health impact. Therefore the strong claim that these ticks are primary vectors to humans and require immediate public-health intervention is not supported by the study's data or conclusions.

401.Option: Because bat-associated ticks have been found to feed on humans and domestic animals, and harbor zoonotic pathogens like Neoehrlichia mikurensis, they should be considered a primary vector for emerging infectious diseases in human populations, especially in regions with high bat and dog density.
Evidence: the latter two ixodid species, as well as soft ticks (Ixodida: Argasidae) of bats, such as Chiropterargas boueti, Carios kelleyi, C. vespertilionis, Secretargas transgariepinus, and Reticulinasus salahi occasionally feed on humans [24–26], which has been observed in Europe, North and South America, with an increasing number of accounts over past two decades [24,25,27–30].
We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex ... and I. vespertilionis ..., previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
These results suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a role in the transmission and maintenance of this pathogen, not only between bat hosts but among other domestic and wildlife species.
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.
The presence of pathogens in field-collected ticks does not provide evidence of vectorial capacity, as they may simply originate from the host’s blood meal.
401.Rationale: The study demonstrates occasional feeding on humans and domestic animals and detects zoonotic agents such as N. mikurensis in bat-associated ticks, which supports the possibility of cross-species transmission. However, the authors refrain from labeling these ticks as primary vectors to humans: they emphasize unknown frequency of non-bat feeding, lack of demonstrated vectorial capacity, and the need for further study before assigning primary-vector status. Thus the statement that they 'should be considered a primary vector' for emerging diseases in humans overstates the evidence.

401.Option: Bat-associated ticks may act as bridge vectors facilitating zoonotic pathogen transmission between bats, domestic animals, and humans in shared environments, increasing the risk of disease emergence
Evidence: These data suggest that bat-associated tick species may opportunistically feed on non-chiropteran hosts.
We detected Neoehrlichia mikurensis in both I. simplex ... and I. vespertilionis ..., previously fed on C. lupus familiaris, M. schreibersii, and M. myotis (Fig 5).
These results suggest that bat-associated ticks may play a role in the transmission and maintenance of this pathogen, not only between bat hosts but among other domestic and wildlife species.

Overall, our results reveal a potentially important aspect of wildlife–domestic animal–human interactions, in this case with the role of cave-visiting stray dogs and stabled horses, that may serve as bridge hosts in the transmission of bat-associated pathogens.
Knowing that bat ticks may also feed on humans, this should be evaluated further as a hitherto underestimated route of zoonotic transmission.
401.Rationale: The study provides direct evidence that bat-associated ticks sometimes feed on non-bat hosts (dogs, horses, wild boar) and carry zoonotic agents (e.g., N. mikurensis). The authors explicitly state these ticks "may play a role" in transmission and identify cave-visiting dogs and stabled horses as potential bridge hosts, and flag possible transmission to humans as an underestimated route. While causation and magnitude are not established, the inference that bat-associated ticks can act as bridge vectors in shared environments and thereby increase risk of disease emergence is consistent with the study's findings and authors' conclusions.

401.Option: The presence of Bartonella in a tick that fed on a bat and a Rhinolophus species suggests that bat-associated ticks are efficient transmitters of this pathogen across bat species and to other mammals, increasing the risk of cross-species infection in shared habitats.
Evidence: Bacterial pathogen screening detected the presence of Bartonella sp. in a single I. vespertilionis (n = 1, EW83, Hungary) in the nymph stage that had fed on R. ferrumequinum (Figs 2 and 3).
Bartonella spp. occasionally exhibit a relatively high prevalence in bat-associated ticks, such as 11% prevalence in I. ariadnae and 4% in I. vespertilionis [11].

the presence of pathogen DNA may result from the blood meal source of the previous life cycle stage (e.g. in males), however the vectorial potential of ticks (implying transstadial survival of the relevant pathogen) cannot be excluded.
The presence of pathogens in field-collected ticks does not provide evidence of vectorial capacity, as they may simply originate from the host’s blood meal.
401.Rationale: While Bartonella DNA was detected in a single nymph that had fed on R. ferrumequinum and Bartonella is known from bat ectoparasites, the authors caution that pathogen detection in ticks can reflect the host blood meal rather than active vectoring. They explicitly state that presence of pathogen DNA does not prove vectorial capacity and that evidence is limited. Thus concluding that bat-associated ticks are "efficient transmitters" across bat species and to other mammals is not supported by the data presented.

401.Option: Since bat-associated ticks have been found to feed on wild boars and horses, they are likely to be major contributors to the spread of zoonotic pathogens in agricultural and rural ecosystems, especially where livestock and wildlife interact.
Evidence: I. vespertilionis indicated the broadest spectrum of host feeding, including ... Equus caballus (n = 1), and Sus scrofa (n = 1) (Fig 1).
Furthermore, one I. vespertilionis tick was positive for horse DNA.

Overall, our results reveal a potentially important aspect of wildlife–domestic animal–human interactions, in this case with the role of cave-visiting stray dogs and stabled horses, that may serve as bridge hosts in the transmission of bat-associated pathogens.
two of the tick species studied had small sample sizes, and the methods used here cannot accurately determine the frequency of non-bat feeding.
Until the frequency of non-bat host feeding behavior of bat-associated ticks is quantified and their vectorial capacity is determined, their epidemiological significance cannot be fully assessed.
401.Rationale: The study documents occasional feeding on horses and wild boar (single detections), and the authors note these interactions could connect bats with domestic/wild animals. However, sample sizes are small, the frequency of such feeding is unknown, and vectorial capacity and epidemiological impact are not established. Therefore it is not supported to claim these ticks are "likely to be major contributors" to spread of zoonoses in agricultural/rural systems; the data suggest a potential role but are insufficient to justify that stronger inference.

402.Option: Hybridization is most likely to occur when both species are exposed to the same environmental conditions in the intermediate host
Evidence: habitat isolation is a three-level constraint that initially has to be overcome (i.e., same geographic area, same host individual, and same localisation in the host).

hosts are dynamic habitats imposing strong selective pressures (co-evolutionary arms race) requiring constant adaptation of parasites for the completion of their life cycle. The specialisation of parasite species to a particular host is thus expected to be a strong pre-zygotic isolation mechanism preventing hybridization and favouring speciation.
final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding
402.Rationale: The passage emphasizes host (especially definitive host) specificity and the three-level habitat isolation (geographic area, same host individual, and localisation within the host) as the critical barriers to hybridization. It does not present shared environmental conditions in the intermediate host as the primary determinant of whether hybridization will occur; rather, meeting in the same definitive host is highlighted as the necessary condition. Therefore the statement focusing on intermediate-host environmental conditions is not supported as the most likely determining factor.

402.Option: Hybridization is most likely when both species are exposed to the same chemotherapeutic agent, such as Praziquantel
Evidence: In particular hetero-specific male S. haematobium under-expressed a fair amount of genes involved in response to drugs compared to homo-specific ones (e.g., Multidrug and toxin extrusion, Multidrug and toxin extrusion 2, Multidrug resistance or Multidrug resistance-associated).

These observations may raise important questions regarding schistosomes’ drug response in the context of co-infection and hybridization especially since a lower sensitivity to PZQ of S. bovis x S. haematobium hybrids compared to pure S. haematobium parasites has been proposed to be at the origin of the spread of the hybrid form in Senegal [27]. However, it is important to pinpoint that any changes associated to PZQ response in hybrids is still theoretical and there is not current evidence that there is any difference in drug response in natural infections.
402.Rationale: While the passage discusses drug-response genes and raises theoretical questions about praziquantel (PZQ) sensitivity, it explicitly states that any link between PZQ exposure and hybridization/spread is speculative and that there is currently no evidence that drug exposure drives hybridization in nature. Thus exposure to the same chemotherapeutic agent is not supported as the most likely determinant of whether hybridization occurs.

402.Option: The frequency of hybridization is determined by the relative abundance of each species in the definitive host population
Evidence: Overall, the data shows that S. haematobium and S. bovis mate in a random fashion and depend only on the presence and the relative abundance of each species in the definitive host.

This lack of pre-zygotic barriers does imply that in areas where S. haematobium and S. bovis are sympatric and infect the same definitive hosts, hybrids and introgressed individuals should be more likely to be found.
402.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that, in the absence of mate recognition barriers, mating (and therefore the frequency of hybridization) depends on the presence and relative abundance of each species in the definitive host. If both species occur together in hosts, higher relative abundance of one or both will increase encounter and pairing probabilities and thus the observed frequency of hybrids.

402.Option: The occurrence of hybridization depends on whether both species infect the same definitive host
Evidence: habitat isolation is a three-level constraint that initially has to be overcome (i.e., same geographic area, same host individual, and same localisation in the host).
final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding
This isolation mechanism “by the host” may be so efficient that species may lack any post-zygotic or other pre-zygotic mechanisms ultimately allowing them to hybridize when the opportunity arises.

This lack of pre-zygotic barriers does imply that in areas where S. haematobium and S. bovis are sympatric and infect the same definitive hosts, hybrids and introgressed individuals should be more likely to be found.
402.Rationale: The passage identifies ‘by the host’ isolation and final host specificity as potentially the single most important barrier to interbreeding. If the two species do not infect the same definitive host (or do not occupy the same localisation within that host), they cannot meet to pair; conversely, if they do infect the same definitive host individual, the opportunity for hybridization exists despite lack of behavioural barriers. Thus infection of the same definitive host is the primary determinant of whether hybridization can occur.

402.Option: Hybridization is primarily driven by the presence of a shared intermediate host species that supports both parasite life cycles
Evidence: Schistosomes have a two-host life cycle, which includes a mammalian definitive host, in which sexual reproduction occurs and a mollusc intermediate host in which asexual multiplication takes place.
The specialisation of parasite species to a particular host is thus expected to be a strong pre-zygotic isolation mechanism preventing hybridization and favouring speciation.
final host specificity may be the sole barrier preventing interbreeding
402.Rationale: Although sharing an intermediate snail host is necessary for transmission, the passage emphasizes the mammalian definitive host (where sexual reproduction occurs) and final-host specificity as the key barrier to hybridization. The authors state that ‘final host specificity may be the sole barrier’ and stress the importance of being in the same definitive host individual and localisation. Therefore presence of a shared intermediate host is not presented as the primary driver of hybridization occurrence.

403.Option: The current study's smaller sample size may have reduced statistical power to detect TT, leading to a false-negative result
Evidence: Following data cleaning, 1009 children were included in the study, which is very close to the targeted sample size of 1018.
the target sample size was very nearly achieved, and our individual participation rate was over 99%, therefore the risk of attrition bias is considered to be low.
403.Rationale: The passage states the study nearly achieved its target sample size (1009 vs 1018) and had >99% participation, so lack of statistical power from a smaller sample is unlikely to explain the absence of TT. The authors instead point to other explanations for the discrepancy with the 2012 PBPS.

403.Option: Misclassification of eyelash epilation as trachomatous trichiasis due to cultural practices likely contributed to overestimation of TT prevalence in the 2012 survey
Evidence: we have observed social practices of eyelash epilation in Fiji that may have been misdiagnosed as trachomatous trichiasis.
403.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify eyelash epilation as a social practice that could be misinterpreted clinically as TT, providing a plausible and directly stated explanation for the higher TT prevalence reported in the 2012 survey. This misclassification would produce an overestimate of TT in earlier clinical-only surveys.

403.Option: Differences in clinical grading criteria between the two surveys may have led to underestimation of TF and TT in the current study
Evidence: The difference in TF prevalence between the present data and the 2012 PBPS could be due to poor consensus between graders, seasonal variation in trachoma prevalence or an artefact of the cross-sectional study designs.
403.Rationale: The passage explicitly lists poor consensus between graders (which encompasses differences in grading or grader agreement) as a possible cause of the discrepancy between surveys. Variation in grader assessment or grading criteria can lead to systematic differences in measured TF/TT prevalence between surveys.

403.Option: The use of PCR testing in the current study may have led to underestimation of Ct infection, contributing to the lower prevalence of active trachoma
Evidence: Evidence from our positive control swabs indicates that a substantial proportion of chlamydial and human DNA is lost during storage over a few months at 5°C.
The loss of sensitivity is however a limitation when considering the findings of this cross-sectional prevalence study.
Improving specimen transport and storage conditions may have resulted in a closer association between clinical signs of disease and Ct infection.
we consider their detection not to be critical.
403.Rationale: While the authors acknowledge storage-related DNA loss and reduced PCR sensitivity as limitations that could underestimate Ct infection, they also state that loss of low-load infections may not be critical and that this affects infection detection rather than clinical signs. The main discrepancy with the 2012 PBPS concerns clinical TF/TT prevalence (and especially the unexpectedly high TT previously reported), for which misclassification and grader/seasonal differences are emphasized. Thus PCR underestimation of infection is a possible but less likely primary explanation for the discrepancy in clinical prevalence.

403.Option: Seasonal variation in trachoma transmission may have led to a lower prevalence in the current study compared to the 2012 survey
Evidence: The difference in TF prevalence between the present data and the 2012 PBPS could be due to poor consensus between graders, seasonal variation in trachoma prevalence or an artefact of the cross-sectional study designs.
403.Rationale: The authors explicitly cite seasonal variation in trachoma prevalence as one possible explanation for differing results between surveys. Seasonal changes in transmission can produce different prevalence estimates when cross-sectional surveys are conducted at different times of year.

404.Option: The presence of O. moubata in both wild and domestic animal reservoirs ensures continuous spillover of pathogens into human populations
Evidence: Ornithodoros moubata is an argasid tick widely distributed throughout many countries of central, eastern and southern mainland Africa and Madagascar, where it constitutes an important medical and veterinary problem because it transmits lethal diseases such as human relapsing fever (HRF) and African swine fever (ASF) [5,6].

In these countries, O. moubata is found in nature, associated to warthogs and other wild hosts inhabiting burrows, as well as in anthropic environments, living inside human dwellings and domestic animal premises, which greatly contributes to the transmission and persistence of HRF and ASF in the affected areas.
404.Rationale: The passage explicitly states O. moubata occurs both in nature (associated with wild hosts) and in anthropic settings (human dwellings and domestic animal premises), and that this distribution contributes to transmission and persistence. That supports option 0 as an important contributing factor. However, the question asks which factor most critically enables sustained transmission in human populations; the passage emphasizes that colonization and reproduction inside human dwellings (endophily) and the tick's biology (repeated feeding/reproduction in refuges) make eradication difficult and directly sustain human transmission. Therefore, while reservoir presence (wild + domestic) contributes to spillover, the text points to the ticks' habitation and life cycle in human dwellings as the more critical enabling factor, so option 0 is not the single most critical factor described.

404.Option: The high genetic diversity of salivary proteins in O. moubata allows for immune evasion and sustained transmission across diverse host populations
Evidence: These sialomes have uncovered thousands of protein-coding sequences and large multigene protein families, many of them conserved between both tick families, which underlines that tick sialomes and saliva composition are highly complex and functionally redundant [19].

These proteins include several clades of lipocalins and a range of metalloproteases, protease inhibitors, phospholipases A2, apyrases and vitellogenins, which significantly increase the current repertory of protective candidate antigens from argasid ticks.

This functional redundancy clearly indicates which host defensive responses must be necessarily abrogated by the tick to be able to fed, namely blood clotting, platelet aggregation and inflammation. Moreover, functional redundancy underscores the notion that targeting individual tick antigens will probably not be enough to reach total protection (for example, complete blocking of tick feeding) and highlights the need of simultaneously targeting several functionally redundant tick antigens to completely abolish the involved tick anti-defensive mechanism and reach full protection; in other words, functional redundancy points to the necessity of developing multiantigenic vaccines rather than single antigen vaccines for the control tick infestations and tick-borne disease transmission.
404.Rationale: The passage documents large multigene families and functional redundancy in salivary proteins, and notes this redundancy complicates vaccine development and likely helps ticks counter host defenses. This provides a plausible biological mechanism for immune evasion. However, the text does not present this genetic/antigenic diversity as the primary ecological driver that sustains transmission within human populations. The authors instead emphasize endophily (colonization of human dwellings) and the ticks' capacity to feed/reproduce repeatedly in human-associated refuges as the key reasons transmission persists and eradication is difficult. Thus, while salivary protein diversity contributes to persistence by hampering control, it is not identified in the passage as the most critical enabling factor for sustained human transmission.

404.Option: The ability of O. moubata to survive in a wide range of environmental conditions allows it to maintain transmission across diverse geographic regions
Evidence: Ornithodoros moubata is an argasid tick widely distributed throughout many countries of central, eastern and southern mainland Africa and Madagascar [4], where it constitutes an important medical and veterinary problem because it transmits lethal diseases such as human relapsing fever (HRF) and African swine fever (ASF) [5,6].
404.Rationale: The passage notes a broad geographic distribution of O. moubata across many African regions and Madagascar, which implies a wide range. However, it does not explicitly state that survival across varied environmental conditions is the critical mechanism enabling sustained transmission in human populations. The text focuses more on behavioural/ecological traits—endophily, colonization of human dwellings, and reproductive/feeding biology—that directly maintain local transmission and persistence in anthropic settings. There is insufficient direct evidence in the passage to support the claim that environmental tolerance (survival across diverse conditions) is the most critical enabling factor, so option 2 is not supported as the primary factor.

404.Option: The rapid feeding duration of O. moubata minimizes host detection and allows for frequent transmission events in human dwellings
Evidence: Argasid tick feeding takes less than 1 hour and, after detachment they moult and reproduce inside their refuges.
Conversely, the rapidly feeding argasid ticks have all the salivary molecules they need to complete feeding already synthetised and stored in the salivary glands before they access the host.
404.Rationale: The passage clearly states that argasid ticks feed rapidly (less than 1 hour) and have preformed salivary components facilitating quick feeding. Rapid feeding likely reduces host detection and helps successful blood meals, which can facilitate transmission. However, the study emphasizes that the critical features enabling sustained transmission in human populations are the ticks' endophilic behaviour—colonizing human dwellings and domestic premises—and their ability to moult, reproduce, and feed multiple times within refuges. Rapid feeding is a facilitating trait but is not presented as the most critical factor enabling continuous transmission in human populations; therefore option 3 is not the best choice.

404.Option: The ability to repeatedly feed and reproduce within human dwellings enables continuous transmission cycles between humans, domestic animals, and the environment
Evidence: Argasid ticks are endophilic ticks that live inside the burrows of their wild hosts and colonize human dwellings and domestic animal premises.
Argasid tick feeding takes less than 1 hour and, after detachment they moult and reproduce inside their refuges. Adult argasids can feed and reproduce up to 10 times [3].

In these countries, O. moubata is found in nature, associated to warthogs and other wild hosts inhabiting burrows, as well as in anthropic environments, living inside human dwellings and domestic animal premises, which greatly contributes to the transmission and persistence of HRF and ASF in the affected areas.
Thus, the presence of this argasid in the anthropic environment makes it difficult to eradicate these diseases from endemic areas.
404.Rationale: The passage repeatedly emphasizes that O. moubata is an endophilic species that colonizes human dwellings and domestic animal premises, and that after feeding they moult and reproduce inside refuges—adult argasids can feed and reproduce multiple times. The authors explicitly link the ticks' presence in anthropic environments and their lifecycle traits to sustained transmission and persistence of human relapsing fever (HRF) and African swine fever (ASF) and to difficulties in eradication. Together, these facts most directly explain continuous transmission cycles within human settings, making option 4 the best-supported and most critical factor described in the study.

405.Option: The detection of Africa-1b lineage in the East region of Cameroon, previously reported in Central African Republic, supports the hypothesis that rabies spreads through natural animal migration across international borders
Evidence: One isolate (14V-4292) detected in 2014 in Garoua Boulai (East region) fell within the Africa-1b lineage with previously described isolates from Central African Republic (Fig 3).
This indicates the potential role of intercountry circulation in the sustainment of the enzootic cycle of rabies in Africa.
it could be hypothesized that the unique Africa-1b identified in this study was introduced from Central African Republic.

An outstanding application of that marker is provided by the recent finding by Bourhy et al., suggesting that the maintenance of the enzootic cycle of rabies at local geographic level in Bangui is more likely driven by human-mediated waves of spread rather than by continuous dispersion in a relatively large and homogenous dogs’ population [45].
405.Rationale: While the finding that an Africa-1b isolate in eastern Cameroon is closely related to isolates from the Central African Republic does support cross-border spread, the passage itself favors human-mediated movement rather than attributing spread to natural animal migration. The quoted sentences explicitly propose intercountry circulation and even cite work suggesting human-mediated waves of spread are more likely than continuous natural dispersion. Therefore the option's attribution specifically to natural animal migration is not supported by the passage.

405.Option: Genetically identical RABV isolates from geographically distant regions within Cameroon suggest movement of infected animals across regions
Evidence: Indeed, they were strikingly related (99.8 to 100% nt and 100.0% aa identity) to their counterparts originating from the Centre region (Fig 3).

12V-007 and 13V-4215, originating respectively from Bafoussam and Batcham in the West region; and 14V-4199 from Douala in the Littoral region. Indeed, they were strikingly related (99.8 to 100% nt and 100.0% aa identity) to their counterparts originating from the Centre region (Fig 3).
These results indicate that RABV circulate in close proximity between geographically close regions.

Striking phylogenetic evidence of outcasts to the apparent in-country geographic differentiation of RABV variants provided further support to the idea that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies at least in urban areas.
405.Rationale: The passage reports near-identical sequences (99.8–100% nt identity) from viruses sampled in different, geographically separated regions of Cameroon and explicitly interprets such 'outcasts' as evidence supporting movement of rabid animals contributing to spread. This direct genetic-geographic concordance is the strongest support for animal movement (most plausibly human-mediated movement of dogs) spreading rabies within the country.

405.Option: The co-circulation of genetically distinct Africa-1 and Africa-2 lineages in the same regions suggests that multiple independent introductions of rabies occurred through natural animal migration
Evidence: This study uncovered the co-circulation of both Africa-1 and Africa-2 in two southern regions of Cameroon and indicated that they circulate in close proximity between neighboring administrative regions of Cameroon as well as between Cameroon and neighboring countries.
Africa-2 isolates, detected from 2010 to 2014, were geographically restricted to the two neighboring South West and North West regions (Fig 2 and Table 1).
it could be hypothesized that the unique Africa-1b identified in this study was introduced from Central African Republic.

Striking phylogenetic evidence of outcasts to the apparent in-country geographic differentiation of RABV variants provided further support to the idea that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies at least in urban areas.
405.Rationale: Although co-circulation of distinct lineages is documented, the passage does not ascribe this pattern specifically to natural animal migration. The authors emphasize introductions and intercountry circulation and explicitly discuss human-mediated movements as a likely driver. Thus inferring that co-circulation demonstrates multiple introductions via natural migration is not supported; human-mediated transport of dogs is a more consistent explanation in the text.

405.Option: The high genetic similarity between RABV isolates from Cameroon and those from Nigeria and Gabon indicates that rabies is spreading through long-distance wildlife migration across African ecosystems
Evidence: The other 70 studied isolates of the Africa-1 subclade grouped in the Africa-1a lineage along with previously described isolates from diverse geographic origin in North Africa (Algeria, Ethiopia, Nigeria, and Morocco) and Central Africa (Cameroon, Gabon and Equatorial Guinea) (Fig 3).

Striking phylogenetic evidence of outcasts to the apparent in-country geographic differentiation of RABV variants provided further support to the idea that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies at least in urban areas.
it has been recently suggested that RABV of the Africa-1 and Africa-2 variants circulate along the trunk roads between Cameroon and the city of Bangui.
405.Rationale: Although the passage notes genetic similarity with isolates from neighboring countries, it does not attribute this to long-distance wildlife migration. On the contrary, the text highlights human-associated movement (e.g., spread along trunk roads, human-mediated waves of spread) and urban dog movements as plausible mechanisms. Therefore the option's specific claim of long-distance wildlife migration is not supported by the evidence provided.

405.Option: The presence of Africa-1 and Africa-2 lineages in neighboring regions suggests that rabies is maintained through continuous local transmission within each region, independent of external sources
Evidence: This study uncovered the co-circulation of both Africa-1 and Africa-2 in two southern regions of Cameroon and indicated that they circulate in close proximity between neighboring administrative regions of Cameroon as well as between Cameroon and neighboring countries.
it could be hypothesized that the unique Africa-1b identified in this study was introduced from Central African Republic.
it has been recently suggested that RABV of the Africa-1 and Africa-2 variants circulate along the trunk roads between Cameroon and the city of Bangui.

Striking phylogenetic evidence of outcasts to the apparent in-country geographic differentiation of RABV variants provided further support to the idea that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies at least in urban areas.
405.Rationale: The data do show lineages present in neighboring regions, but the authors interpret patterns as consistent with introductions and movement between regions and countries (including along trunk roads) rather than purely local, independent maintenance. The passage explicitly raises the possibility of introductions from neighboring countries and human-mediated spread, which contradicts the option's claim of independent local maintenance without external influence.

406.Option: Lack of trust between the Arab community and national health services prevents effective implementation of public health messaging and behavioral change campaigns
Evidence: Lack of trust that this community has in the national services provided by the government is an additional barrier to disease prevention [48,55].
These minorities generally suffer from poverty and political exclusion and the roots of this distrust go much deeper, establishing a state of distrust from both directions.

It seems that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture).
Both in 1997 and 20 years later in 2017 funding was discontinued for various reasons and as a result the vaccination, testing and culling of animals declined.
406.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies lack of trust in national services as an important barrier that hinders prevention efforts and community engagement, providing direct support for this option. However, the authors state that the main reason for persistence was inconsistent, discontinuous implementation and funding of interventions by the ministries, with funding discontinuation leading to declines in vaccination, testing and culling. Thus, while lack of trust is an important contributing factor, the passage indicates it is not the single most critical influence driving persistence compared with the systemic failure to sustain animal-control programs.

406.Option: Failure to implement consistent and sustained vaccination programs for small ruminants leads to ongoing animal reservoirs and repeated spillover to humans
Evidence: The success of the eradication of Brucella abortus was due to a successful intervention of testing for the disease and culling affected animals immediately, while simultaneously vaccinating female calves with a single dose vaccination [31,35].
By 1964 the vaccine REV1 was adopted for use [51] and since 1980 only female animals up to the age of 6 months are vaccinated.

The main reason that emerged from analysing the reports and publications, is the fact that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture).

Both in 1997 and 20 years later in 2017 funding was discontinued for various reasons and as a result the vaccination, testing and culling of animals declined. Consequently, an increase in the incidence of the disease was to be expected as suggested by Shemesh and Yagupsky [52].
406.Rationale: The passage presents vaccination of animals as a core, effective intervention (citing eradication of B. abortus when testing, culling and vaccination were done). It then identifies inconsistent, non-sustained implementation—explicitly including declines in vaccination when funding stopped—as the principal reason outbreaks recurred. Therefore failure to implement consistent, sustained vaccination of small ruminants is presented as a critical driver of persistent animal reservoirs and repeated human spillover, making this option correct.

406.Option: Geopolitical instability and lack of international collaboration prevent effective cross-border surveillance and control of brucellosis in shared animal populations
Evidence: herds belonging to nomadic herders (Bedouins) are not isolated from other herds, including transfer of animals from the Palestinian Authority in which brucellosis is also endemic.
Farmers buy and sell animals across the border bringing in the disease.

The disease can also infect a herd via wild animals from neighbouring countries and locally. Collaboration with Israel’s neighbours is not ideal to say the least, which serves as a barrier to successful control and prevention.

The main reason that emerged from analysing the reports and publications, is the fact that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture).
406.Rationale: The passage documents cross-border and transboundary transmission as an important barrier—nomadic herders, animal movements across borders, and imperfect regional collaboration are explicitly mentioned. However, the authors emphasize that the primary reason for persistence is domestic inconsistency in implementing and funding control measures (vaccination/testing/culling). Thus geopolitical factors are significant contributors but are not presented as the most critical single factor in the text.

406.Option: Culturally entrenched consumption of unpasteurized dairy products combined with cross-border transmission from neighboring regions creates a persistent reservoir that undermines sustained disease control
Evidence: Most of the human cases are due to consumption of homemade unpasteurized dairy products in the Arab community [30,47].
However, consumption of non-pasteurized dairy products persisted in the Arab community both in the Southern and Northern regions [48] leaving the community vulnerable to the disease.
Farmers buy and sell animals across the border bringing in the disease.

The main reason that emerged from analysing the reports and publications, is the fact that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture).
406.Rationale: The passage clearly states that culturally entrenched consumption of unpasteurized dairy is a major behavioural barrier that keeps humans at risk and that cross-border animal movements import infection. These factors sustain transmission. Nevertheless, the authors single out the inconsistent, discontinuous implementation and funding of veterinary and public health measures (testing, culling, vaccination) as the central reason outbreaks recur. Therefore cultural practices plus cross-border transmission are important drivers but not identified as the most critical single factor in the analysis.

406.Option: Inadequate funding for veterinary services results in inconsistent testing, culling, and vaccination of livestock, allowing the disease to persist in animal populations
Evidence: The main reason that emerged from analysing the reports and publications, is the fact that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture).

It seems that the periods of low incidence following interventions resulted in complacency, leading to the false notion that the interventions were successful and therefore decreased the motivation and willingness of professionals and decision makers to continue funding and running the interventions [43,52].
Both in 1997 and 20 years later in 2017 funding was discontinued for various reasons and as a result the vaccination, testing and culling of animals declined.

The State Comptroller and Ombudsman of Israel ... reported that the veterinary services failed to act on data showing increases in the rates of the disease. The intervention, they concluded, was not fully implemented, failed to vaccinate a high rate of herds and did not clear herds of sick animals.
406.Rationale: The passage directly links discontinuation and insufficiency of funding to declines in core veterinary control activities (testing, culling, vaccination) and identifies this lack of sustained implementation/funding as the main reason for recurring outbreaks. Thus inadequate funding of veterinary services — causing inconsistent application of testing, culling and vaccination — is presented as a critical driver of persistence and is correctly identified as such.

407.Option: Assign one CDD per village and rely on community leaders to report household members who were absent during the initial distribution to ensure all are reached
Evidence: The CDDs are also few and so they do not reach all the places. Sometimes you are given the drugs to distribute but the village is so vast and you are expected to distribute to the whole village on your own.

Another one is that the CDDs are very few. You can imagine a whole village covering like 300 households is given only a CDD to distribute the drugs. There will be problems during registration because they won’t reach all the households to register them.
When you visit a household and find out that some people are not in
Migration, some people are available during registration period but relocate without notice.
407.Rationale: The passage repeatedly identifies having too few CDDs or one CDD covering a whole village as a key implementation problem that prevents reaching all households. Relying on community leaders to report absentees does not address the fundamental issue of insufficient field capacity and the practical challenges (large areas, migration, absenteeism) described. The quoted passages show one-distributor coverage is insufficient and that absenteeism/migration is common, so this strategy would not effectively ensure all eligible individuals are reached.

407.Option: Use a centralized drug distribution model where all households in a sub-county are required to visit a central clinic on a single day to receive medication
Evidence: The distribution of drugs is done house-to-house to all persons aged two years and above.
When you visit a household and find out that some people are not in
Migration, some people are available during registration period but relocate without notice.
The best time to conduct the MDA is when people are not working on their farms that is April and September.
407.Rationale: The program uses house-to-house delivery because centralized, single-day approaches would likely miss many people given the documented barriers: absenteeism, seasonal migration, and that people are often working away from central locations. The passage emphasizes the need to reach people at home and to schedule timing appropriately. A one-day centralized clinic would exacerbate access issues rather than overcome the logistical and community-level barriers described.

407.Option: Extend the MDA campaign duration to 10 days and allow CDDs to return to the central facility to request additional drugs only after completing their initial rounds
Evidence: To tackle the issue of absenteeism due to migration, opinion leaders felt that the duration of the MDA should be extended especially in the rural areas and areas with high population density.
The period should be a little longer so that the CDDs can be able to revisit the houses where people were not present, that way everyone will be reached.
There is no system to replenish the stock of drugs when they get finished and walking to and fro the facility every time the drugs are exhausted affects our target.
There is no system to replenish the stock of drugs when they get finished and walking to and fro the facility every time the drugs are exhausted affects our target.
407.Rationale: While extending the campaign duration is supported by the passage to address absenteeism and permit revisits, the clause 'allow CDDs to return to the central facility to request additional drugs only after completing their initial rounds' would likely worsen stockout problems. The passage explicitly criticizes the lack of a replenishment system and the burden of walking back to facilities when stocks run out; it recommends plans for replenishing stock and decentralized solutions. Thus this particular operational constraint makes the option ineffective.

407.Option: Conduct a single, intensive one-day training session for all CDDs and distribute drugs in advance to households to ensure full coverage
Evidence: The training is just a briefing for a few hours
Training of the CDDs is done by the CHEWs and is planned to take one full day

The CDDs reported that sometimes the trainings are not given at all or that they received only a few hours of briefing in the morning and were expected to conduct household registration from the afternoon of the same day.
There is no system to replenish the stock of drugs when they get finished and walking to and fro the facility every time the drugs are exhausted affects our target.
407.Rationale: The passage identifies inadequate training (too brief or absent) as a problem and recommends adequate, well-planned training that builds confidence; it does not support a single intensive one-day session as necessarily sufficient. Moreover, distributing drugs in advance to households is not discussed and could create logistical, hygiene, and adherence problems; the passage stresses supervised, hygienic dispensing and the need for supervision and stock management, not pre-distribution to households. Overall, this option does not align with the recommended sustained training quality, supervision, and drug management measures.

407.Option: Increase the number of CDDs per village, extend training to include practical problem-solving, and establish decentralized drug refill points to prevent stockouts
Evidence: The CDDs are also few and so they do not reach all the places. Sometimes you are given the drugs to distribute but the village is so vast and you are expected to distribute to the whole village on your own.
They should go back to how things were done before, we were 4 CDDs at that time and now we were reduced to 2 per village.
The training is just a briefing for a few hours
Adequate and well planned training would assist in confidence building allowing the CDDs to better conduct their role for improved community drug uptake.
There is no system to replenish the stock of drugs when they get finished and walking to and fro the facility every time the drugs are exhausted affects our target.
The local health facilities together with the CHEWs need to come up with a plan of ensuring that there are no gaps in replenishing drug stock-out among the CDDs during house-to-house administration.
407.Rationale: This option directly addresses the core implementation challenges identified in the passage: insufficient numbers of CDDs covering large/vast areas, inadequate/truncated training, and drug stockouts that impede coverage. The passage recommends increasing CDD numbers, improving and lengthening training to build confidence and skills, and ensuring systems to replenish drugs during house-to-house administration. Establishing decentralized refill points and practical training/problem-solving would preserve coverage, reduce time lost fetching drugs, and improve CDD performance—making this the most effective strategy described.

408.Option: Younger individuals have higher exposure to contaminated water due to age-related roles in water-dependent activities, explaining the disparity in infection rates and highlighting the need for age-targeted interventions
Evidence: S. haematobium infection in the country is variable, location-specific and depends on many factors including geographic location, gender, age, occupation, contact with water, distance to water resources and socioeconomic status [9].

The selection of these sites was based on reports of cases of schistosomiasis-like symptoms, closeness to water bodies, increased water contacts activities (due to fishing, farming, swimming, and domestic use) and the lack of social amenities such as health, water and toilet facilities.

The transmission of urogenital schistosomiasis was associated with age, occupation, perceived mode of Schistosoma transmission, knowledge of Schistosoma prevention and awareness of schistosomiasis treatment. Other significant factors included frequency of visit to water bodies, as well as water usage patterns.
Schistosoma-positive participants were significantly younger than Schistosoma negative participants (29.68 ± 14.08 vs 36.35 ± 15.55, p = 0.009).
The majority (67.9%) visits rivers very often.

Mass drug administration coupled with nutritional intervention, good water supply, vector control and intensive public health education would be necessary in addressing the prevalence and deleterious effects of S. haematobium infections in the district.
408.Rationale: The passage explicitly links infection to age and to water contact behaviours ("frequency of visit to water bodies" and common "water contact activities" such as fishing, farming, swimming and domestic use). It also shows younger participants had higher infection. The most direct link between environment and the age disparity is differential exposure to contaminated water driven by age-related roles/activities. That relationship supports interventions targeted to those higher-exposure age groups (e.g., focused education, MDA, improved water supply and vector control) rather than interventions that ignore the age–exposure pathway.

408.Option: The age-related difference in infection rates is explained by the higher frequency of water contact among younger individuals due to recreational activities, which indicates that restricting access to water bodies would be an effective intervention
Evidence: The transmission of urogenital schistosomiasis was associated with age, occupation, perceived mode of Schistosoma transmission, knowledge of Schistosoma prevention and awareness of schistosomiasis treatment. Other significant factors included frequency of visit to water bodies, as well as water usage patterns.

The selection of these sites was based on reports of cases of schistosomiasis-like symptoms, closeness to water bodies, increased water contacts activities (due to fishing, farming, swimming, and domestic use) and the lack of social amenities such as health, water and toilet facilities.
The majority (67.9%) visits rivers very often.

Mass drug administration coupled with nutritional intervention, good water supply, vector control and intensive public health education would be necessary in addressing the prevalence and deleterious effects of S. haematobium infections in the district.
408.Rationale: While the passage supports that frequency of water contact (including recreational activities like swimming) explains part of the age-related difference, it does not recommend or provide evidence that restricting access to water bodies is an effective or feasible intervention. Instead, recommended strategies are mass drug administration, improved water supply, vector control and public health education. Thus the first half of the option (exposure via water contact) is supported, but the proposed strategy of restricting access to water bodies is not supported by the passage and may be impractical given the communities' dependence on rivers for livelihood and domestic use.

408.Option: Younger individuals are more likely to be infected because they are less aware of schistosomiasis transmission, which implies that general public education campaigns would be sufficient to reduce infection disparities
Evidence: Participants demonstrated inadequate knowledge of schistosomiasis as the majority indicated that they have not heard of schistosomiasis (60.7%), do not know the mode of transmission (49.3%) and do not know how the disease could be spread (52.9%).

The transmission of urogenital schistosomiasis was associated with age, occupation, perceived mode of Schistosoma transmission, knowledge of Schistosoma prevention and awareness of schistosomiasis treatment. Other significant factors included frequency of visit to water bodies, as well as water usage patterns.

Mass drug administration coupled with nutritional intervention, good water supply, vector control and intensive public health education would be necessary in addressing the prevalence and deleterious effects of S. haematobium infections in the district.

A schistosomiasis education campaign should focus on the causes, transmission, treatment, and prevention of the disease as well as, the impact of the infection on the health and nutrition of the inhabitants.
408.Rationale: Although the study documents poor overall knowledge about schistosomiasis, it does not show that age-specific lack of awareness is the primary driver of higher infection in younger people. The passage identifies behavioural exposure (frequency of water contact, water-usage patterns) as key factors linked with age. Moreover, the authors recommend a package of interventions (MDA, water supply improvements, vector control) in addition to public education, indicating that education alone would likely be insufficient to eliminate the disparities.

408.Option: The disparity in infection rates across age groups is primarily due to differences in access to healthcare, which suggests that expanding treatment coverage would reduce age-based disparities
Evidence: Two-third of those who did nothing when they had infection stated that they did not seek for medical attention because they lacked funds (Table 3).
Only a few (18/140) have received Schistosoma treatment before of which most (9/18) received it between 3–9 months ago.

The transmission of urogenital schistosomiasis was associated with age, occupation, perceived mode of Schistosoma transmission, knowledge of Schistosoma prevention and awareness of schistosomiasis treatment. Other significant factors included frequency of visit to water bodies, as well as water usage patterns.

Mass drug administration coupled with nutritional intervention, good water supply, vector control and intensive public health education would be necessary in addressing the prevalence and deleterious effects of S. haematobium infections in the district.
408.Rationale: While the passage documents barriers to seeking care and low prior treatment coverage, it identifies environmental exposure (frequency of visits to water bodies and water-usage patterns) as the more direct link to age-related infection differences. Expanding treatment coverage (e.g., MDA) is indeed part of the recommended response, but the primary factor explaining why younger individuals have higher infection in this study is differential exposure to contaminated water rather than differential access to healthcare alone.

408.Option: The higher prevalence among younger individuals is due to greater susceptibility to S. haematobium infection, which necessitates age-specific prophylactic treatment programs
Evidence: S. haematobium infection in the country is variable, location-specific and depends on many factors including geographic location, gender, age, occupation, contact with water, distance to water resources and socioeconomic status [9].

The transmission of urogenital schistosomiasis was associated with age, occupation, perceived mode of Schistosoma transmission, knowledge of Schistosoma prevention and awareness of schistosomiasis treatment. Other significant factors included frequency of visit to water bodies, as well as water usage patterns.
Schistosoma-positive participants were significantly younger than Schistosoma negative participants (29.68 ± 14.08 vs 36.35 ± 15.55, p = 0.009).

Mass drug administration coupled with nutritional intervention, good water supply, vector control and intensive public health education would be necessary in addressing the prevalence and deleterious effects of S. haematobium infections in the district.
408.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence that biological susceptibility (age-related host susceptibility) explains higher prevalence among younger people. Instead, it emphasizes exposure-related factors (contact with water, frequency of visits, occupation) as associated with age. Recommended strategies focus on MDA, water supply, vector control and education rather than age-specific prophylaxis based on increased biological susceptibility, so the claim of greater innate susceptibility and the prescription of age-specific prophylactic programs is not supported by the study text.

409.Option: The conditional destabilisation of USP allows researchers to observe a linear decline in nucleotide sugar levels that directly correlates with increasing cell death
Evidence: The degron system enables stabilisation of the protein of interest by addition of FK506 to the culture medium and its conditional destabilisation by removal of FK506 [5].
When using Glc-1P as substrate, lysate of the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant grown with 1 μM FK506 presented about 4.5% of the activity obtained with wild type lysate.
Only 1% activity could still be measured when the enzyme was stabilised with 0.05 μM FK506 and this dropped below the limits of detection when lower drug concentrations were used.
A strong reduction of the UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal pools was nevertheless observed upon destabilisation of USP in medium containing 0.01 μM FK506.
growth of the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant slowed down with decreasing FK506 concentrations.
with a FK506 concentration of 0.005μM or less, the percentage of dead cells exceeded 35%.
409.Rationale: The passage shows that the degron system produces dose-dependent decreases in enzyme activity and nucleotide sugar pools and that lower FK506 (more destabilisation) is associated with slowed growth and increased cell death. However, the text does not claim a strictly "linear decline" in nucleotide sugar levels nor quantify a direct linear correlation between levels and cell death. It describes stepwise/dose-dependent reductions and a threshold effect (activity dropping below detection and viability loss), not a demonstrated linear relationship. Therefore the statement asserting a linear decline that directly correlates is not supported.

409.Option: The conditional destabilisation of USP enables researchers to infer that UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal biosynthesis is redundant, as the parasite survives even when both UGP and USP are inactivated
Evidence: Targeted deletion of both UGP and USP was however unsuccessful suggesting that the metabolites UDP-Glc and/or UDP-Gal are essential for Leishmania in vitro growth.

With low FK506 concentrations, ddUSP was destabilised and the UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal biosynthesis could be reduced to minimal level leading to growth arrest and cell death, allowing us to demonstrate that UDP-Gal and/or UDP-Glc are essential metabolites for L. major promastigotes.
409.Rationale: The passage explicitly indicates that deleting both enzymes was unsuccessful and that reducing UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal to minimal levels via USP destabilisation causes growth arrest and cell death, demonstrating essentiality rather than redundancy. Thus the claim that biosynthesis is redundant and parasites survive when both are inactivated contradicts the presented evidence.

409.Option: The conditional degron system enables a dose-dependent reduction in UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal levels, revealing a threshold below which parasite viability is lost
Evidence: The degron system enables stabilisation of the protein of interest by addition of FK506 to the culture medium and its conditional destabilisation by removal of FK506 [5].
Only 1% activity could still be measured when the enzyme was stabilised with 0.05 μM FK506 and this dropped below the limits of detection when lower drug concentrations were used.
A strong reduction of the UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal pools was nevertheless observed upon destabilisation of USP in medium containing 0.01 μM FK506.
growth of the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant slowed down with decreasing FK506 concentrations. ... no proliferation was observed if FK506 was absent or present at a concentration of 0.005 μM.

With low FK506 concentrations, ddUSP was destabilised and the UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal biosynthesis could be reduced to minimal level leading to growth arrest and cell death, allowing us to demonstrate that UDP-Gal and/or UDP-Glc are essential metabolites for L. major promastigotes.
409.Rationale: The passage describes use of the degron (ddUSP) controlled by FK506 to generate graded (dose-dependent) reductions in USP activity and corresponding decreases in UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal pools. It also documents that below certain FK506 concentrations (and hence below certain nucleotide sugar levels) parasites stop proliferating and show increased cell death. This supports the interpretation that the system reveals a threshold level of nucleotide sugars required for viability.

409.Option: By destabilising USP, researchers can determine that UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal levels are not essential for parasite viability, as growth persists even when levels are undetectable
Evidence: Only 1% activity could still be measured when the enzyme was stabilised with 0.05 μM FK506 and this dropped below the limits of detection when lower drug concentrations were used.
A strong reduction of the UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal pools was nevertheless observed upon destabilisation of USP in medium containing 0.01 μM FK506.
no proliferation was observed if FK506 was absent or present at a concentration of 0.005 μM.

With low FK506 concentrations, ddUSP was destabilised and the UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal biosynthesis could be reduced to minimal level leading to growth arrest and cell death, allowing us to demonstrate that UDP-Gal and/or UDP-Glc are essential metabolites for L. major promastigotes.
409.Rationale: The passage indicates that when USP is destabilised and nucleotide sugar levels fall to minimal or undetectable levels, parasites cease to proliferate and exhibit substantial cell death. The authors explicitly conclude that UDP-Glc and/or UDP-Gal are essential. Thus the option claiming viability persists despite undetectable levels is contradicted by the data.

409.Option: The conditional degron system allows researchers to assess the impact of USP degradation on nucleotide sugar levels independently of FK506 concentration, enabling a direct comparison of viability across all conditions
Evidence: The degron system enables stabilisation of the protein of interest by addition of FK506 to the culture medium and its conditional destabilisation by removal of FK506 [5].
When using Glc-1P as substrate, lysate of the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant grown with 1 μM FK506 presented about 4.5% of the activity obtained with wild type lysate.
Only 1% activity could still be measured when the enzyme was stabilised with 0.05 μM FK506 and this dropped below the limits of detection when lower drug concentrations were used.
growth of the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant slowed down with decreasing FK506 concentrations. ... no proliferation was observed if FK506 was absent or present at a concentration of 0.005 μM.
409.Rationale: FK506 concentration is the mechanism by which ddUSP is stabilised or destabilised; the impact on nucleotide sugar levels is therefore explicitly tied to FK506 dose. The system does not assess USP degradation independently of FK506 concentration—rather it uses graded FK506 levels to produce the degradation gradient. Therefore the claim of independence from FK506 is incorrect.

410.Option: The outbreak was sustained by a local sylvatic transmission cycle involving arboreal Aedes mosquitoes and non-human primates, given the forested environment and lack of prior dengue cases in the village.
Evidence: Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region. Therefore, although the outbreak was in a forested area, it was not caused by a genotypically ‘sylvatic’ dengue virus in the conventional use of the term.
dengue is regarded as an urban disease and incidence is low during the cooler months of November and December.
there are no data on incidence of dengue in the Latsavang area before the outbreak.
410.Rationale: The genetic data directly contradict the assertion that the outbreak was sustained by a sylvatic (genotypically sylvatic) cycle: the outbreak strain belongs to the human-associated 'Asia 3' clade of Genotype I and is very similar to human infection strains. Although the village is forested and prior incidence data are lacking, the molecular evidence does not support a sylvatic reservoir as the sustaining transmission cycle.

410.Option: The outbreak likely resulted from a recombination event between a sylvatic dengue strain and a human-adapted strain, given the presence of a novel viral lineage in a remote forested area.
Evidence: recombination analysis, using only bioinformatic tools, did not identify recombinant events amongst DENV-1 Lao strains.

Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region.

We identified a closely related strain in Vientiane in late December 2008 (Figure 3) and both strains appear to be descended from viruses described circulating in Thailand, and constitute a lineage that has not been identified anywhere else.
410.Rationale: The authors explicitly report that recombination analysis did not identify recombinant events among Lao DENV-1 strains, so invoking a recombination event as the cause is not supported. Moreover, the Latsavang strain groups with human-associated Genotype I ('Asia 3') and closely related urban/Vientiane strains, consistent with human-lineage descent rather than a recombination between sylvatic and human strains.

410.Option: The outbreak was likely caused by introduction of a human-adapted dengue strain via human movement, rather than local sylvatic transmission, despite the rural forested setting
Evidence: Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region. Therefore, although the outbreak was in a forested area, it was not caused by a genotypically ‘sylvatic’ dengue virus in the conventional use of the term.

We identified a closely related strain in Vientiane in late December 2008 (Figure 3) and both strains appear to be descended from viruses described circulating in Thailand, and constitute a lineage that has not been identified anywhere else.

The virus may have been brought to Latsavang by someone from Vientiane or elsewhere, provoking an outbreak in a population with limited prior exposure against DENV-1, as suggested by the significant proportion of adults affected (there are no data on incidence of dengue in the Latsavang area before the outbreak).
This demonstrates wide geographical viral dispersal, presumably associated with infected patients travelling within Laos.
410.Rationale: The genetic data show the outbreak strain is a human-associated Genotype I lineage closely related to a Vientiane strain and regional viruses, and the authors explicitly propose introduction by an infected person as a plausible explanation. Combined genetic similarity to urban strains and the documented potential for human-mediated dispersal support introduction of a human-adapted strain rather than local sylvatic transmission.

410.Option: The outbreak was caused by a zoonotic spillover from a sylvatic reservoir, supported by the detection of dengue in a rural forested village and the absence of prior dengue cases in the population.
Evidence: Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region. Therefore, although the outbreak was in a forested area, it was not caused by a genotypically ‘sylvatic’ dengue virus in the conventional use of the term.
Alternatively, the virus could have been maintained in the Latsavang area through a forest cycle and then transmitted to humans.
there are no data on incidence of dengue in the Latsavang area before the outbreak.
410.Rationale: Although the authors note the theoretical possibility of local maintenance via a forest cycle, the molecular characterisation argues against a genotypically sylvatic origin. The lack of prior incidence data does not provide evidence for zoonotic spillover; the genetic and phylogenetic data point toward a human-associated lineage and make sylvatic spillover the less supported inference.

410.Option: The outbreak was maintained through a persistent local transmission cycle involving Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, as evidenced by the high genetic similarity between the Latsavang strain and urban strains from Vientiane.
Evidence: Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region.

We identified a closely related strain in Vientiane in late December 2008 (Figure 3) and both strains appear to be descended from viruses described circulating in Thailand, and constitute a lineage that has not been identified anywhere else.

A major limitation of this investigation was the lack of entomological investigations to determine which mosquito species (Aedes albopictus, Aedes aegypti, or arboreal Aedes species) were dengue vectors in Latsavang.
First, there is strong evidence that a given strain can be maintained and circulate during consecutive years in specific geographically limited evolutionary clusters.
410.Rationale: The genetic similarity to urban/Vientiane strains supports possible introduction and/or local circulation of a human-associated strain. However, the specific claim that transmission was maintained by Aedes aegypti is not supported by the data: the study lacked entomological investigations and explicitly lists uncertainty about which mosquito species were vectors in Latsavang. While local persistence of a strain is plausible, assigning Aedes aegypti as the responsible vector is not justified by the evidence provided.

411.Option: Cumulative exposure to infected sand flies over time increases the likelihood of seroconversion, explaining higher seroprevalence in older individuals despite lower clinical disease incidence
Evidence: Although symptomatic VL was most common among young adults (age group 20–29 years) and became increasingly rare in the older age groups in our study, the frequency of both rK39- and DAT-positivity increased with age.

The observation that nevertheless sero-prevalence and the frequency of sero-conversion increased with age could be explained by individuals experiencing repeated inoculations with infected sand flies, of which the early ones are more prone to lead to disease.
However, the most likely explanation for the age-pattern we observed is that as people get older, their chances of ever having been infected increase.

Assuming that, as also suggested by the observations of Hailu et al. [35], repeated sero-conversions are mainly due to repeated exposure, sero-conversion is probably an adequate measure of (re)infection.
411.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the observed increase in seroprevalence with age to repeated inoculations (cumulative exposure) by infected sand flies, while noting that clinical disease is concentrated in younger ages. Those quotes directly support cumulative exposure as the most likely explanation for higher seroprevalence in older persons despite declining clinical incidence.

411.Option: Older individuals have a stronger immune response to Leishmania infection, leading to higher seroprevalence but reduced progression to clinical disease
Evidence: Host factors also play a role, and individuals prone to develop disease are probably more likely to develop VL when first infected, at an early age.

Although symptomatic VL was most common among young adults (age group 20–29 years) and became increasingly rare in the older age groups in our study, the frequency of both rK39- and DAT-positivity increased with age.
411.Rationale: The passage does not claim that older people have a stronger immune response that reduces progression; rather it emphasizes that disease is more likely at first infection and at younger ages and attributes higher seroprevalence in older ages to cumulative/repeated exposure. The provided quotes counter the notion that a qualitatively stronger immune response in older individuals is the primary explanation.

411.Option: The decline in clinical VL incidence with age is due to reduced vector exposure in older individuals, which explains the lower disease burden despite higher seroprevalence
Evidence: At any given moment there is probably not much difference in the level of exposure between different age groups present in the villages.
Older people may also have experienced periods of higher transmission in the past than younger individuals.
