425.Option: Variation in specimen types, particularly stool and rectal swabs, likely contributed to heterogeneity by increasing false-positive vanB results due to cross-reactivity with anaerobic bacteria, which may reduce the assay's reliability in clinical settings where such specimens are common
Evidence: The result of meta regression (Table 2) indicated that sample types might be one possible source of heterogeneity.

That there were more false-positive results in vanB group may be attributed to the presence of genes in several species of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria that were highly similar to the vanB sequences [5,7].

Stool and rectal swabs might be the harbors where anaerobic microbes were commonly checked, which increased the risks of detecting false-positive vanB results [11]. The meta regression also confirmed the specimen types might be one of possible sources of heterogeneity.
425.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify specimen type as a likely source of heterogeneity via meta-regression and describe a biologically plausible mechanism: stool/rectal swabs harbor anaerobic species with sequences similar to vanB, increasing false positives and lowering PPV for vanB. This directly supports the option and implies that performance may be less reliable in settings that rely on those specimen types, so the option correctly captures the key contributor and its clinical implication.

426.Option: Compare the geometric mean levels of key cytokines (e.g., IL-10, MCP-1, IFN-β2) between vaccinated and placebo recipients at baseline and during acute breakthrough illness to infer changes in immune protection over time
Evidence: As a post-hoc exploratory analysis, we tested blood samples from selected participants at one month post-dose 3 for baseline cytokine assessment, and during the acute phase of febrile illness from participants who required hospitalization for virological confirmation of dengue infection in the active phase, and during the first and beginning of the second year of long-term follow up.

A limitation of these studies, including our present work, is the fact that samples are often collected at a single time point and at different times of the clinical course of infection (between 1 and 15 days after fever onset). This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.
At baseline, only 7 cytokines had median levels above the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ): IP-10, MCP-1, MDC, MIP-1β, EGF, GRO and IL-8.
Median cytokine values in children at baseline (1 month post-dose 3) (N = 40) and during the acute phase of dengue illness (N = 33) irrespective of treatment group.
426.Rationale: Simply comparing geometric mean cytokine levels at baseline (one month post-dose) and during acute breakthrough illness does not provide a valid assessment of waning protection over time. The passage highlights that baseline sampling was limited (only 40 baseline samples) and cytokine measurements are transient and typically collected at a single, heterogeneous time point during illness, which can mislead inference about temporal trends. Evaluating waning immunity requires analysis across time since vaccination (multiple post-vaccination intervals) and control for prior exposure; a two-timepoint comparison without time-since-vaccine stratification or accounting for variable sampling timing is insufficient.

426.Option: Conduct a time-stratified analysis of immune profiles in breakthrough cases, stratified by age and prior exposure indicators, to detect shifts in cytokine signatures associated with waning protection
Evidence: The comparisons between the CYD-TDV and placebo groups were stratified as follows: i) all hospitalized cases, irrespective of phase or severity; ii) severe or non-severe cases only; iii) active phase or hospital phase only; iv) cases aged <9 years or aged ≥9 years.
Of the 15 cytokines with median levels above the LLOQ, only two, IL-1Ra and MCP-1, significantly differed between the treatment groups. Both cytokines were higher in the placebo group.
Severe dengue is most often observed in previously infected subjects undergoing secondary infection with a different DENV serotype.
This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.

It can be hypothesized that, in children aged <9 years, infections occurring in the CYD-TDV group are akin to secondary infections where vaccination may be considered as the primary infection/challenge and breakthrough disease as secondary infection.
426.Rationale: A time-stratified analysis of immune profiles that also stratifies by age and markers/indicators of prior exposure is the most appropriate approach to evaluate waning in a population with heterogeneous prior exposures. The passage documents that stratified comparisons (including by age) were used and that prior infection history (primary vs secondary infection) strongly influences disease severity and cytokine expression. Time-stratification (i.e., grouping breakthrough cases by interval since vaccination) combined with stratification for age and prior-exposure indicators would allow detection of shifts in cytokine signatures over time while controlling for confounding from age and exposure history—addressing the limitations the authors noted about single timepoint sampling and heterogeneous prior immunity.

426.Option: Stratify breakthrough cases by time since vaccination and compare the proportion of severe versus non-severe cases across strata to identify temporal patterns in disease severity
Evidence: After the initial active surveillance phase (active phase) for breakthrough dengue (up to 25 months after the first injection), a four-year long-term follow-up phase was initiated to monitor breakthrough disease leading to hospitalization (hospital phase).
Severe dengue is most often observed in previously infected subjects undergoing secondary infection with a different DENV serotype.

The comparisons between the CYD-TDV and placebo groups were stratified as follows: i) all hospitalized cases, irrespective of phase or severity; ii) severe or non-severe cases only; iii) active phase or hospital phase only; iv) cases aged <9 years or aged ≥9 years.

A limitation of these studies, including our present work, is the fact that samples are often collected at a single time point and at different times of the clinical course of infection (between 1 and 15 days after fever onset). This heterogeneity in sampling time could lead to misleading results due the transient expression of circulating cytokines.
426.Rationale: Stratifying breakthrough cases by time since vaccination and comparing severity proportions across strata is a reasonable epidemiologic approach to detect waning effectiveness in clinical outcomes. However, in a population with varying prior exposure histories, this approach alone can be misleading unless prior-exposure (or strong correlates such as serostatus or age) are measured and either adjusted for or used in additional stratification. The passage emphasizes that severity is strongly related to prior infection history (secondary infections) and that the study used age stratification; therefore, failing to account for prior exposure would confound the relationship between time-since-vaccination and severity. Because Option 2 does not specify controlling for prior exposure, it is incomplete for the situation posed and thus is not the most appropriate by itself.

427.Option: The integration of multiple NTD screening into a single activity allowed for the identification of co-occurring cases, suggesting shared environmental risk factors across diseases
Evidence: In the implementation of this integrated management of NTD with cutaneous manifestations, we used the approach proposed by Mitja et al [21]. We identified co-endemic communities; we trained the team; carried out social mobilization; performed mobile medical consultations; conducted the screening and management of cases; mapping and held review meetings.

Moreover, this integrated management allowed the community-based screening team to maintain continuous monitoring; to suspect cases of yaws; to detect other NTD (lymphatic filariasis) and other dermatological conditions.
427.Rationale: While the passage clearly documents that integration enabled identification of co-endemic communities and detection of multiple NTDs (evidence above), that feature primarily increases case detection efficiency and surveillance breadth. It is supportive of hypothesizing shared risk factors but is not the most direct methodological feature for tracing transmission patterns or spatial clustering (which requires spatial analysis/mapping). Thus, although relevant, it does not most directly support tracing transmission patterns compared with geospatial mapping.

427.Option: Geospatial mapping of confirmed cases enabled visualization of clustering patterns to infer shared transmission risks and environmental exposures
Evidence: The base maps were loaded on the DIVA-GIS program (http://www.diva-gis.org/) and completed by base maps of the “Programme National de Lutte contre la Lèpre et l’Ulcère de Buruli” (PNLLUB). The map was made using the software QGIS version 1.8.0.
The mapping of confirmed BU and leprosy cases is presented in Fig 2.
The detection rate for BU cases ranged from 0 to 34 cases per 100,000 inhabitants per sub-district.
427.Rationale: Geospatial mapping is the methodological feature that most directly permits visualization of spatial clustering and patterns necessary to trace potential transmission and identify shared environmental or geographic risk factors. The passage explicitly describes creation of maps (DIVA-GIS, QGIS) and presentation of mapped confirmed cases and sub-district detection rates, which are the tools used to infer clustering and shared exposures.

427.Option: Standardized education and awareness sessions using WHO posters ensured consistent case definitions across all screening sites, enabling reliable comparison of clinical features between cases
Evidence: The activity started with an education and awareness raising session on BU, leprosy and yaws. Using the video projector, documentaries on BU and leprosy were shown to the participants. After that, the trained health workers and community health volunteers provided clear explanation on the disease by using the WHO posters on BU, leprosy and yaws.
The training of the health workers covered basic epidemiology, clinical diagnosis, differential diagnosis, complications, social consequences and treatment of these three diseases were discussed.
427.Rationale: Education and use of WHO posters and training can improve awareness and may promote more uniform recognition of conditions, as the quoted text shows. However, the passage does not state that these sessions established or enforced standardized case definitions across sites, nor that they were the primary methodological tool for tracing transmission patterns or identifying shared risk factors. Thus this feature is helpful for case finding and consistency but not the most direct method for tracing transmission or spatially linking cases.

427.Option: The use of trained health workers and community volunteers across multiple villages allowed for rapid identification and reporting of suspected cases, facilitating timely outbreak response
Evidence: This district has 115 primary schools, a CDTUB and each of its sub-districts has a health center. Thirty community health volunteers are actively involved in skin NTDs control in this district.
In accordance with this schedule, the trained health workers went to villages and primary schools.

Moreover, this integrated management allowed the community-based screening team to maintain continuous monitoring; to suspect cases of yaws; to detect other NTD (lymphatic filariasis) and other dermatological conditions.
427.Rationale: The passage documents that trained workers and volunteers conducted village- and school-based screening and maintained monitoring, which supports rapid identification and reporting (evidence above). While this is important for timely outbreak response, it is an enabling surveillance/resource feature; it does not by itself provide the analytical or spatial method needed to trace transmission patterns or identify shared exposures as directly as mapping does.

427.Option: The centralized reporting of results to the PNLLUB and district health authorities enabled real-time monitoring and immediate follow-up of suspected cases, supporting outbreak containment
Evidence: The results of this study were given to the district health authorities and the “Programme National de Lutte contre la Lèpre et l’Ulcère de Buruli” (PNLLUB) for decision-making.

The ownership and adherence of the health authorities to this approach was indicated by the active participation of the PNLLUB, the health authorities, the local elected officials and the community health volunteers.
427.Rationale: The passage indicates that results were shared with district authorities and PNLLUB for decision-making (evidence above), which supports follow-up and programmatic response. However, the text does not describe real-time reporting or immediate follow-up in a way that would directly enable tracing transmission patterns or identifying shared risk factors. Centralized reporting facilitates coordination and response but is less directly the methodological feature used to infer transmission patterns than geospatial mapping.

428.Option: Use of a pre-specified sample size of 300 patients to ensure sufficient power and allow for early stopping based on interim results
Evidence: Total sample sizes (across both treatments) of between 150 and 300 patients prove to be adequate in many cases, the precise value depending on both the magnitude of the treatment advantage and the nature of the ordinal scale.
The trial will be monitored using a series of up to 20 interim analyses, equally spaced by newly accrued patient responses.
Although a maximum of 20 analyses is allowed, it is very unlikely that more than 16 will be required.
428.Rationale: The passage describes GOST as a sequential design that does not rely on a single fixed large sample size; instead it uses interim analyses and flexible stopping rules with typical total sample sizes between 150 and 300 depending on circumstances. Pre-specifying a fixed sample size of 300 is not the feature the authors highlight as the mechanism supporting causal inference or efficiency. Rather, the sequential monitoring and the design choices (ordinal outcome, allocation ratio, interim analyses) determine efficiency and allow early stopping; a hard-coded fixed sample size of 300 alone does not capture GOST’s intended approach.

428.Option: Implementation of a fixed number of interim analyses (e.g., 3) to minimize the risk of false-positive findings and ensure trial integrity
Evidence: The trial will be monitored using a series of up to 20 interim analyses, equally spaced by newly accrued patient responses.

Setting 20 interim analyses for GOST is a subjective choice of the authors achieving a much quicker reaction to the message of the data than setting just 3 or 4 interim analyses while being more practical than updating the sequential plot every time a Day D report is received.
428.Rationale: The passage explicitly endorses a relatively large number of interim looks (up to 20) rather than only a small fixed number like 3. The triangular sequential framework used in GOST controls error rates while allowing many interim analyses; reducing the number of analyses to only 3 is contrary to the authors’ efficiency-driven recommendation and is not the principal feature that supports causal inference. Therefore the specific proposal of implementing only three interim analyses is not supported by the passage.

428.Option: Use of a binary outcome measure (e.g., alive vs. dead) to simplify data collection and reduce the number of required interim analyses
Evidence: An ordinal scale is a categorisation of outcomes for which there is an intrinsic ranking (or order) of the categories in terms of desirability, but there is no specific numerical value attached to each one.
GOST is presented for the case of an ordinal response because expected sample sizes will be reduced if more than two outcome categories can be reliably identified [3].
It will now take 520 patient responses to ensure that V eventually reaches the value where the stopping boundaries in Fig 1 meet. Thus 26 new responses will be required at each interim analysis.
The inflation in sample size due to dichotomising the ordinal scale is a factor of 1.18: an 18% increase in sample size.
428.Rationale: The passage argues the opposite of this option: using an ordinal outcome is preferred because it is more informative and reduces required sample size and hence improves statistical efficiency. Dichotomizing to a binary endpoint increases required responses (and thus reduces efficiency). While binary outcomes may simplify collection, they do not reduce the number of interim analyses or improve efficiency as claimed.

428.Option: Randomization of patients to treatment arms with a 1:1 ratio and stratification by treatment center to ensure balance in prognostic factors
Evidence: Patients will be randomised between an experimental treatment (E) and standard care (S), stratified by treatment centre and perhaps by one or two other key prognostic factors.
Usually the allocation ratio will be set to 1:1 for simplicity and because expected sample sizes are minimised if this choice is made [3].
428.Rationale: Randomization is the primary design feature that supports causal inference by ensuring comparability of treatment groups with respect to both measured and unmeasured confounders. The passage explicitly endorses randomisation and stratification by centre (and key prognostic factors), which further ensures balance on important covariates. The 1:1 allocation ratio is noted to minimise expected sample size, i.e. to improve statistical efficiency. Taken together, these elements most directly support drawing a causal inference about treatment effect while maintaining efficiency.

428.Option: Use of a pre-specified ordinal outcome scale with fixed follow-up and sequential analysis based on actual accrued data
Evidence: The primary trial endpoint in GOST is an ordinal categorisation of patient outcome as recorded a specified number of days following randomisation, and the sequential monitoring will lead to stopping as soon as it is clear that the experimental treatment has an advantage or that sufficient advantage is unlikely to be detected.
Use of a response that is available after a short and fixed duration of follow-up reduces the risk of loss to follow-up and is essential if the trial is to yield an early conclusion.
GOST is presented for the case of an ordinal response because expected sample sizes will be reduced if more than two outcome categories can be reliably identified [3].
The trial will be monitored using a series of up to 20 interim analyses, equally spaced by newly accrued patient responses.
428.Rationale: A pre-specified ordinal outcome with fixed follow-up and sequential analysis is central to GOST’s statistical efficiency (it reduces sample size and enables early stopping). However, while this feature improves efficiency and the quality of outcome measurement, it does not by itself establish causal inference—the randomisation (and stratification) is the design element that most directly enables causal attribution. Therefore although Option 4 correctly describes an efficiency-enhancing feature of GOST, it is not the single feature that most directly supports drawing causal conclusions.

429.Option: The fact that L-AmB was effective in 80% of cases due to L. infantum suggests that the drug induces a strong host immune response that is sustained over time, contributing to long-term protection
Evidence: That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
To our knowledge, no study has systematically evaluated skin penetration of L-AmB following systemic administration in human subjects.
A species-related difference in drug susceptibility is a third potentially important determinant of outcome in TL and VL.
429.Rationale: The passage documents higher cure rates with L. infantum but attributes this to species-related differences in susceptibility (and other parasite- or tissue-related factors), not to L-AmB inducing a sustained host immune response. There is no statement in the passage that the drug's benefit is mediated by inducing long-term host immunity; instead the authors emphasize parasite species, tissue penetration, and possible parasite dormancy as explanations. Therefore the option's mechanism (drug-induced sustained immune protection) is not supported by the passage.

429.Option: The high rate of relapse among patients infected with Viannia subgenus species suggests that these parasites may have a higher replication rate, leading to faster disease progression and treatment failure
Evidence: Five patients experienced a relapse after a median of 6 months [range 3–27] post treatment. Of the five patients who relapsed, four (80%) were infected by a Leishmania species belonging to the Viannia subgenus.
A species-related difference in drug susceptibility is a third potentially important determinant of outcome in TL and VL.
A dormant metabolic state of parasites in skin lesions may also contribute to the suboptimal effectiveness of L-AmB in CL, a process suspected to occur only rarely in VL.
429.Rationale: While the passage reports that most relapses occurred in patients infected with Viannia subgenus species, it does not state that this is due to a higher parasite replication rate or faster progression. The authors suggest species-related differences in susceptibility and possible parasite dormancy as potential mechanisms, but do not attribute relapses to replication rate. The specific causal claim in the option (higher replication rate causing treatment failure) is speculative and not supported by the provided text.

429.Option: The lack of association between immunosuppression and treatment outcome suggests that host immune status does not influence the effectiveness of L-AmB, indicating that the drug's mechanism is entirely independent of host immunity
Evidence: No other clinical or biological factors were associated with the outcome after L-AmB therapy.

Among immunocompromised patients (5/43; 12%), three had human immunodeficiency infection (HIV) infection and two received prolonged corticosteroid therapy and/or immunosuppressive treatment for autoimmune diseases.
Apart from the impact of L. infantum, we found no obvious epidemiological, clinical or laboratory parameter associated with outcome after treatment with L-AmB in TL.
429.Rationale: Although the study did not find an association between immunosuppression and outcome in this cohort, the sample of immunocompromised patients was small (5/43), and the authors caution about limited power and heterogeneous populations. Absence of an observed association in this dataset does not prove that host immune status has no influence or that the drug's mechanism is entirely independent of immunity. The passage emphasizes species effects and limitations of the study rather than concluding immunity is irrelevant.

429.Option: Infection with L. infantum is associated with a significantly higher likelihood of complete healing, suggesting species-specific differences in parasite susceptibility or immune evasion mechanisms
Evidence: In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).
That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
A species-related difference in drug susceptibility is a third potentially important determinant of outcome in TL and VL.
429.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies infection with L. infantum as independently associated with better outcomes and discusses species-related differences in susceptibility as an important determinant of treatment success. This supports the interpretation that host-pathogen interactions (i.e., which species infects the host and how that species interacts with the drug and host defenses) can influence outcomes beyond purely pharmacological properties of L-AmB.

429.Option: The variation in treatment outcomes across different geographic regions suggests that environmental factors such as climate or altitude may modulate the pharmacokinetics of L-AmB, leading to reduced effectiveness in certain areas
Evidence: In univariate analysis (Table 2), the area where infection was acquired was associated with L-AmB effectiveness (p≤ .05).
That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
To our knowledge, no study has systematically evaluated skin penetration of L-AmB following systemic administration in human subjects.
429.Rationale: The passage notes geographic variation in effectiveness, but attributes such differences to the infecting Leishmania species, tissue penetration, parasite biology, and host factors rather than environmental modulation of L-AmB pharmacokinetics (climate or altitude). There is no evidence in the text that climate or altitude affect drug pharmacokinetics; the authors emphasize species-related susceptibility and drug distribution to skin as more plausible explanations.

430.Option: The use of a safety vaccinator to prevent needle stick injuries among veterinary personnel would significantly improve long-term program success
Evidence: The major concern is the common occurrence of the needle stick injuries and the accidental inoculation in veterinary personnel while participating in Brucella vaccination programs [36].
The rates of accidental exposure ranging from 6·7% to 46% have been reported [37].

To avoid needle stick injuries, research should be conducted in the use of a safety vaccinator as used for other vaccines such as Gudair® vaccination in Australia [39] and animals should be properly restrained before vaccination.
430.Rationale: The passage documents that needle‑stick injuries and accidental inoculation are an important occupational safety issue and that use of a safety vaccinator is recommended to reduce those injuries. However, the authors do not present this as the single most critical determinant of long‑term program success. The modelling and sensitivity analyses repeatedly identify vaccination coverage and persistent non‑coverage of herds as the key drivers of long‑term prevalence and economic outcomes (see evidence used for Option 1). Therefore, while safety vaccinators would improve program safety and may aid implementation, they are not identified in the passage as the most critical factor determining long‑term success.

430.Option: Sustained vaccination coverage over time, particularly preventing recurrent non-coverage in previously unvaccinated herds, is critical for long-term success
Evidence: In our sensitivity analysis we considered the realistic fact that herds that were not covered once were more likely to remain uncovered due to issues associated with inability to reach them or farmers’ will to cooperate.
Reduction of the vaccination rate led to reduced NPV and BCR values. The same impact had the assumption that herds that were not covered were more likely to remain uncovered the next year.
However, even better NPV and BCR will be achieved if the vaccination coverage/efficacy is improved.
430.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies sustained vaccination coverage and the problem of herds that remain unvaccinated year‑to‑year as critical inputs in the sensitivity analysis. It states that reductions in vaccination rate and persistent non‑coverage reduce net present value (NPV) and benefit‑cost ratios (BCR), and that improving coverage/efficacy yields better economic outcomes. These statements directly support that preventing recurrent non‑coverage and maintaining high, sustained coverage over time is the most critical factor for long‑term program success.

430.Option: The availability of a DIVA-compatible vaccine that allows differentiation between infected and vaccinated animals would ensure long-term success of the control program
Evidence: Therefore, there is a need for the development of a better vaccine that can differentiate infected from vaccinated animals (DIVA).

RB51 vaccine can be used instead of S19 as it allows serological differentiation between naturally infected and vaccinated animals but it is not currently available in India and is considered to have a lower efficacy than S19.
The S19 vaccine could interfere with the recommended diagnostic tests and may cause abortion in the pregnant animals [35, 40].
430.Rationale: The passage notes the value of a DIVA vaccine and that RB51 would permit differentiation but has limitations and is unavailable. While a DIVA vaccine would improve surveillance and certain programmatic aspects, the modelling and sensitivity analyses emphasize vaccination coverage and persistent non‑coverage as the dominant determinants of long‑term prevalence reduction and economic outcomes. Thus, availability of a DIVA vaccine is beneficial but not presented as the single most critical factor for long‑term success in the passage.

430.Option: The implementation of a test and slaughter strategy after a decade of vaccination would maximize long-term eradication potential
Evidence: The third intervention considered the annual mass vaccination of replacements for a decade followed by a decade of a test and slaughter strategy.

Although the third approach–i.e. annual mass vaccination of replacements for a decade followed by a decade of a test and slaughter strategy–was also found to be cost-effective, it is less likely to be adopted in India because it is a Hindu majority country and Hindus consider cows to be sacred.
Thus it would be difficult to get community support for a strategy involving animal slaughter although it drastically reduces prevalence of the disease if implemented after a decade of vaccination.
Therefore, this may not be the preferred strategy in the Indian situation.
430.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges that the test‑and‑slaughter approach can drastically reduce prevalence and can be cost‑effective, it also emphasizes severe cultural, religious and acceptability barriers (cow slaughter bans and likely lack of community support) and additional costs. The authors therefore conclude it is less likely to be adopted in India. The key modelling sensitivity results instead point to sustained vaccination coverage as the critical factor. Thus test‑and‑slaughter may maximize eradication potential in principle but is not presented as the most critical, feasible determinant of long‑term success for India.

430.Option: The economic benefit of reducing livestock losses through vaccination is the primary driver of long-term program sustainability
Evidence: All programs had positive NPVs and >1 BCRs indicating the benefits from all programs are higher than their respective costs.

The results suggest that all of the three approaches investigated for controlling the disease would be beneficial as the prevalence of Brucella infection will drop below 2% in cattle and 3% in buffalo after 20 years of the implementation of disease control program.
Reduction of the vaccination rate led to reduced NPV and BCR values. The same impact had the assumption that herds that were not covered were more likely to remain uncovered the next year.
430.Rationale: The passage documents strong economic benefits (positive NPV, BCR >1) which support sustainability, but it also demonstrates that these economic gains are sensitive to vaccination coverage: reductions in coverage or persistent unvaccinated herds reduce NPV and BCR. Therefore, while economic benefits are important, they are not identified as the primary driver; sustained vaccination coverage (preventing recurrent non‑coverage) is portrayed as the critical factor determining whether those economic benefits are realized long‑term.

431.Option: Conduct a cross-sectional analysis of monthly rainfall and case counts to identify the month with the highest correlation between precipitation and disease incidence
Evidence: Time series analysis was used to describe the leptospirosis trend between 2007 and 2019 and to forecast the disease incidence in 2020.
In the present study, seasonality was tested to detect whether leptospirosis occurrence was concentrated in some of these months.
they suggested a time interval between the precipitation peak and the appearance of symptoms, wherein the number of cases begins to increase approximately a month after the rainy season.
431.Rationale: A single cross-sectional month-by-month correlation ignores the time-series properties of the data (seasonality, autocorrelation) and cannot properly capture delayed/lagged responses. The passage emphasizes using time-series methods to describe trends and seasonality and notes an observed lag (about one month) between precipitation peaks and case increases. Therefore a simple cross-sectional correlation by month is insufficient to identify timing of peak transmission relative to environmental triggers while accounting for lags and serial dependence.

431.Option: Use a SARIMA model with exogenous variables (SARIMAX) to identify the lagged impact of environmental triggers on disease incidence peaks
Evidence: Time series analysis was used to describe the leptospirosis trend between 2007 and 2019 and to forecast the disease incidence in 2020.
The SARIMA time series model was used to assess trend and seasonality components and their adjustment [36].
In the present study, seasonality was tested to detect whether leptospirosis occurrence was concentrated in some of these months.
they suggested a time interval between the precipitation peak and the appearance of symptoms, wherein the number of cases begins to increase approximately a month after the rainy season.
431.Rationale: SARIMAX (SARIMA with exogenous covariates) extends the SARIMA approach described in the passage and allows inclusion of environmental drivers (e.g., rainfall) as external regressors and specification of lagged effects. The passage highlights use of SARIMA to model seasonality and trend and documents a lag between precipitation and cases, supporting an approach that jointly models seasonal structure and lagged impacts—precisely what SARIMAX enables.

431.Option: Apply a generalized additive model (GAM) with a spline function for rainfall data to estimate the delayed effect of precipitation on leptospirosis incidence
Evidence: GAM represents an extension of the generalized linear model [25] and an alternative for modeling nonlinear relationships with an undefined shape.
In the present study, a smoothing function was used in the year variable to confirm the dependence of observations on time.
The knowledge of temporal dynamics and the prediction of infectious disease outbreaks by using time series, particularly GAM and SARIMA, are the goals of several researchers [12,37].
they suggested a time interval between the precipitation peak and the appearance of symptoms, wherein the number of cases begins to increase approximately a month after the rainy season.
431.Rationale: GAMs are explicitly cited in the passage as tools for modeling nonlinear temporal relationships and were used in the study to capture temporal dynamics with smoothing functions. A GAM framework can accommodate nonlinear effects of rainfall (via splines) and can be extended to include lagged terms or distributed-lag specifications to estimate delayed effects. Thus, while GAM must be implemented with lagged predictors or a distributed-lag extension to capture delay, it is a valid and flexible approach for estimating nonlinear, delayed precipitation effects on incidence.

431.Option: Use a simple linear regression model with rainfall data lagged by one month to estimate the effect of precipitation on leptospirosis incidence
Evidence: Time series can be decomposed into the following equation: where trend (Tt) refers to an increasing or decreasing pattern during the observation period, seasonality (St) shows fluctuations occurring in periods shorter than one year, and the random component or error (at) shows irregular random oscillations [36].
The SARIMA time series model was used to assess trend and seasonality components and their adjustment [36].

The adequacy of each model was confirmed using the residual autocorrelation function (ACF) and partial autocorrelation function (PACF) histogram graphs; furthermore, the Ljung–Box test was performed to investigate the randomness of the residuals.
431.Rationale: A simple linear regression with a single one-month lag does not adequately account for seasonality, autocorrelation, nonlinearity, or multi-month distributed lags described in the passage. The study used time-series approaches (SARIMA, GAM) and diagnostic checks (ACF/PACF, Ljung–Box) because these features matter for correct inference. A single linear lagged regression is too simplistic and risks biased inference in the presence of serial correlation and seasonal structure.

431.Option: Perform a time-lagged correlation analysis between rainfall and case counts at multiple lags to identify the optimal delay for peak transmission
Evidence: they suggested a time interval between the precipitation peak and the appearance of symptoms, wherein the number of cases begins to increase approximately a month after the rainy season.
The SARIMA time series model was used to assess trend and seasonality components and their adjustment [36].

The adequacy of each model was confirmed using the residual autocorrelation function (ACF) and partial autocorrelation function (PACF) histogram graphs; furthermore, the Ljung–Box test was performed to investigate the randomness of the residuals.
431.Rationale: Time-lagged correlation can be a useful exploratory tool to suggest candidate delays, but it does not formally account for seasonality, autocorrelation, or confounding and therefore may produce misleading results when used alone. The passage emphasizes formal time-series modeling (SARIMA) and diagnostic checks (ACF/PACF, Ljung–Box) to properly account for autocorrelation and seasonal structure. Consequently, while lagged correlations can help explore plausible lags, they are not the best standalone approach for identifying timing of peak transmission relative to environmental triggers when accounting for lagged effects.

432.Option: The presence of concurrent Pneumocystis jirovecii infection increases the risk of prolonged hospitalization following antiretroviral therapy initiation
Evidence: In IRIS group, 3 patients (12.5%) were co-infected with Mycobacterium tuberculosis, 3 (12.5%) with non-tuberculous mycobacteria, 5 (20.8%) with cytomegalovirus, 9 (37.5%) with Pneumocystis jirovecii, and 8 (33.3%) with candida. In non-IRIS group, 12 patients (6.0%) were co-infected with Mycobacterium tuberculosis, 9 (4.5%) with non-tuberculous mycobacteria, 52 (26.0%) with cytomegalovirus, 10 (5.0%) with Pneumocystis jirovecii, and 43 (21.5%) with candida.

No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).

Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001). In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: Although Pneumocystis jirovecii co-infection numerically appears more common in the IRIS group (9/24 vs 10/200), the authors state that there were "No significant differences... regarding ... co-infection status" at baseline. The study links prolonged hospitalization and higher readmission to the occurrence of IRIS overall, but it does not establish that concurrent P. jirovecii infection independently predicts those adverse outcomes. Therefore this option is not supported by the presented evidence.

432.Option: Elevated C-reactive protein levels at baseline are predictive of increased hospitalization duration after antiretroviral therapy initiation
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).

We analyzed serial laboratory test data in IRIS group at baseline, ART initiation, and IRIS onset. Compared with baseline, CRP levels decreased significantly at ART initiation (48.3 mg/L vs. 9.0 mg/L, P = 0.007) and then significantly increased at IRIS onset (9.0 mg/L vs. 37.9 mg/L, P = 0.027).
At IRIS onset, CRP levels were higher in IRIS group than non-IRIS group (37.9 mg/L vs. 10.6 mg/L, P = 0.003).
Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: The passage shows CRP dynamics (decrease at ART initiation, rise at IRIS onset) and that CRP at IRIS onset is higher in IRIS patients, but explicitly states there were no significant differences in baseline blood test results between groups. Baseline CRP was not reported as predictive of longer hospitalization; instead, elevated CRP at IRIS onset is associated with IRIS, which in turn had longer stays. Therefore baseline CRP is not supported as the predictor claimed.

432.Option: A more rapid increase in CD4+ count and CD4+/CD8+ ratio following ART initiation is associated with a higher risk of prolonged hospitalization and readmission
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).

When comparing the changes from ART initiation to IRIS onset, IRIS group showed greater increases in CD4+ count (40.0 cells/μL vs. 27.0 cells/μL, P = 0.031) and CD4+/CD8+ ratio (0.09 vs 0.02, P = 0.001) compared to non-IRIS group (Fig 4B, 4D and 4F).

At IRIS onset, CRP levels were higher in IRIS group than non-IRIS group (37.9 mg/L vs. 10.6 mg/L, P = 0.003). The IRIS group also exhibited higher CD4+ count (74.5 cells/μL vs. 40.0 cells/μL, P = 0.001) and CD4+/CD8+ ratio (0.14 vs. 0.07, P = 0.002) at IRIS onset, with no difference in HIV RNA load (Fig 4A, 4C and 4E).

Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001). In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: The study reports similar baseline immunosuppression (no baseline CD4+ differences) but finds that the IRIS group experienced larger increases in CD4+ count and CD4+/CD8+ ratio after ART initiation. IRIS patients had significantly longer hospital stays and higher readmission rates. These linked findings support that a rapid immune recovery (greater CD4+ and ratio increases) is associated with clinically significant adverse outcomes (prolonged hospitalization and readmission), consistent with pathophysiology of IRIS.

432.Option: A higher baseline CD4+ count is associated with a greater likelihood of requiring readmission after antiretroviral therapy initiation
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).

Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001). In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
At IRIS onset, ... The IRIS group also exhibited higher CD4+ count (74.5 cells/μL vs. 40.0 cells/μL, P = 0.001) ... at IRIS onset,
432.Rationale: The passage indicates baseline CD4+ counts did not differ significantly between groups, so higher baseline CD4+ is not associated with readmission. The higher CD4+ values were observed at IRIS onset (after ART) rather than at baseline, and it is the post-ART increase/IRIS that correlates with worse outcomes. Thus the option is not supported.

432.Option: A longer interval between antifungal therapy and antiretroviral therapy initiation increases the risk of readmission after ART initiation
Evidence: No significant differences were observed between two groups regarding demographic characteristics, co-infection status, the interval between antifungal and ART initiation, CD4+ counts, and blood test results at baseline (Tables 2 and S2).

Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001). In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
432.Rationale: The authors explicitly report no significant difference in the interval between antifungal therapy and ART initiation between IRIS and non-IRIS groups. The study does not provide evidence that a longer interval increases readmission risk. Instead, readmission is associated with IRIS occurrence, not with the antifungal-to-ART interval as presented.

433.Option: The number of AC L3 larvae migrating through the lung during the acute infection phase is the primary host factor predicting fatal outcome in nonpermissive hosts
Evidence: mice were separately infected with different doses of AC L3 (30, 60, 120, 300), and survival curves were generated during the early infection phase (1–7 dpi).
no mouse died after infection with 30, 60 and 120 AC L3, while 10% of mice infected with 300 AC L3 died at 1 dpi, 70% died at 2 dpi and all died at 3 dpi (Fig 4A).
AC L3 migration caused progressively more severe lung hemorrhage from 2 to 12 hpi (Fig 1C)
433.Rationale: The passage shows that parasite dose (number of AC L3 administered) is associated with fatal outcomes (300 L3 caused deaths), which is a parasite exposure variable rather than a host-intrinsic factor. The question asks for a host factor predicting transition to fatal outcomes; the number of larvae migrating is a property of the parasite dose/exposure, not a host biological response. Therefore this option is not the best answer.

433.Option: The duration of time between AC L3 infection and lung migration is the most critical host factor determining fatal outcome in nonpermissive hosts
Evidence: we used live imaging technology to explore the migration route of AC in nonpermissive host mice, revealing that after infection, AC L3 migrated from the intestinal wall to the liver at 2 hpi, then to the lung at 4 hpi and the brain at 8 hpi.

We found that the green fluorescence in the liver was detected at 2 hpi, peaked at 4 hpi and decreased at 8 hpi (Fig 1B upper panel), while that of the lung was detected at 4 hpi, peaked at 8 hpi and decreased at 12 hpi (Fig 1B lower panel).
AC L3 migration caused progressively more severe lung hemorrhage from 2 to 12 hpi (Fig 1C)
433.Rationale: The passage documents the timing of parasite migration (hours post-infection) but provides no evidence that variability in the host-determined duration between infection and lung migration predicts fatal versus nonfatal outcomes. Fatality is linked to high parasite dose and to host inflammatory signaling, not to differences in the migration timing. Thus duration to lung migration is not supported as the key host predictor.

433.Option: Activation of the Stat3/IL-6 signaling pathway in the lung is the key host factor determining fatal outcome in nonpermissive hosts during the early infection phase
Evidence: High-dose AC infection leads to fatal pneumonia in nonpermissive hosts during the early infection phase
the protein level of IL-6 was significantly elevated but that of IL-13 showed only a slight increase (Fig 4D).

the protein levels of total Stat3 and p65 exhibited no obvious change upon AC infection at 1 dpi, while those of activated Stat3 and p65 were significantly upregulated by 4- and 2-fold, respectively (Fig 4D).
These data indicated that activation of Stat3 played more important roles in the host lung at 1 dpi.
infection with a high dose of AC L3 could lead to fatal pneumonia in a nonpermissive host during the early infection phase before the manifestation of eosinophilic meningitis.

Moreover, inhibitors targeting Stat3 or IL-6 could significantly attenuate the pneumonia induced by AC, expanding our understanding of angiostrongyliasis cantonensis and providing effective candidate targets for intervention of parasitic pneumonia.
As expected, the upregulation of Stat3 signaling and IL-6 caused by AC infection was significantly attenuated... inflammatory cell infiltration was also diminished upon LMT-28 and C188-9 treatment
433.Rationale: The passage directly links fatal early pneumonia with strong activation of lung Stat3 and elevated IL-6 at 1 dpi, and shows that blocking Stat3/IL-6 signaling reduces lung inflammation and pathology. Those observations identify host activation of the Stat3/IL-6 pathway as the key host biological factor predicting or mediating the shift from nonfatal to fatal outcomes during the early phase.

433.Option: The level of IL-13 expression in the lung is the primary host factor that determines fatal outcome during the early infection phase
Evidence: For Th2 cytokines, we observed that levels of IL-6 strikingly increased by 7-fold and the levels of IL-13 increased by 20-fold (Fig 3A lower) at 1 dpi compared to 0 dpi and dropped to normal at 4 dpi but rose again to high levels from 5–7 dpi.
we found that the protein level of IL-6 was significantly elevated but that of IL-13 showed only a slight increase (Fig 4D).
Stat3/IL-6 signaling was dominantly enhanced at 1 dpi (Fig 4D–4G).
433.Rationale: Although IL-13 transcripts rose, the passage states that IL-13 protein showed only a slight increase at 1 dpi while IL-6 protein and Stat3 activation were markedly elevated and implicated in fatal pneumonia. The evidence therefore points to IL-6/Stat3 rather than IL-13 as the primary host determinant of early fatality.

433.Option: The presence of eosinophilic meningitis at 21 dpi is the key host factor that determines whether the infection becomes fatal during the early infection phase
Evidence: nonpermissive host mice infected with 30 AC L3 exhibited severe eosinophilic meningitis at 21 dpi and died before 28 dpi.

Altogether, the results suggested that infection with a high dose of AC L3 could lead to fatal pneumonia in a nonpermissive host during the early infection phase before the manifestation of eosinophilic meningitis.
433.Rationale: Eosinophilic meningitis occurs in the late phase (around 21 dpi) and is a cause of death later in infection, but the question asks about transition to fatal outcomes during the early infection phase. The passage explicitly notes fatal pneumonia can occur in the early phase prior to meningitis; thus meningitis at 21 dpi is not the host factor predicting early-phase fatality.

435.Option: The lower infection rate in Sungai Layau is due to a higher rate of anthelminthic drug use among its residents, which reduced parasite burden and transmission
Evidence: In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR = 1.29; 95% CI = 1.01-1.65; p = 0.038).
unscheduled deworming without proper monitoring system was common among the children of these communities.
435.Rationale: The passage explicitly attributes Sungai Layau's low prevalence to provision of concrete houses and treated water rather than to deworming. While not taking anthelminthics was associated with slightly higher odds (OR=1.29), the effect size is small compared with sanitation and income predictors, and the text notes unscheduled/unmonitored deworming is common (limiting effectiveness and comparability). Therefore, greater anthelminthic use alone is not the most supported explanation.

435.Option: The lower infection rate in Sungai Layau is primarily due to higher levels of health literacy and personal hygiene practices among its residents, which reduced the likelihood of parasite transmission
Evidence: This proved that proper provision of basic infrastructure and education are effective tools to reduce the prevalence of these infections.
behavioral (i.e., personal hygiene such as wearing shoes and food consumption)
indiscriminate defecation (OR = 3.45; 95% CI = 2.76–4.32; p < 0.001)
435.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that education and personal hygiene can reduce IPIs, but it emphasizes that Sungai Layau's markedly lower prevalence is linked to concrete houses and treated water (infrastructure) and higher household income. Multivariate analysis highlights untreated water supply and indiscriminate defecation as strong predictors; the text does not present evidence that higher health literacy/hygiene practices alone were the primary driver in Sungai Layau. Thus this option overstates the evidence for health literacy as the primary cause.

435.Option: The geographic isolation of Sungai Layau village limited human movement and reduced the introduction of new parasite strains, thereby lowering infection rates
Evidence: All villages were located at lowland altitude at the jungle fringes surrounded by rubber and palm oil estates.
In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
435.Rationale: The passage describes all surveyed villages as similarly located (lowland jungle fringes) and does not state that Sungai Layau was geographically isolated or had restricted movement. The authors attribute the low prevalence to concrete housing, treated water and higher household income rather than to isolation or limited introduction of strains. There is no textual support for reduced human movement or unique geographic isolation explaining the difference.

435.Option: The presence of treated water supply and improved sanitation infrastructure in Sungai Layau likely disrupted transmission pathways by reducing exposure to contaminated water and enabling hygienic defecation practices
Evidence: In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
This proved that proper provision of basic infrastructure and education are effective tools to reduce the prevalence of these infections.

Multivariate analysis using forward logistic regression model further confirmed that participant less than 12 years old were 2.2 times (95% CI = 1.45–3.45; p < 0.001), low household income had 4.9 times (95% CI = 3.15–7.73; p < 0.001), using untreated water supply had 2.1 times (95% CI = 1.36–3.21; p < 0.001) and indiscriminate defecation were 5 times (95% CI = 3.30–7.62; p < 0.001) more likely to suffer from an IPIs, respectively.
435.Rationale: The passage directly links Sungai Layau's low prevalence to provision of treated water and improved housing and states that basic infrastructure reduces IPIs. The multivariate results identify untreated water and indiscriminate defecation as strong, significant predictors of infection. Together these statements strongly support that treated water supply and improved sanitation disrupted transmission pathways and plausibly explain the stark contrast in infection rates.

435.Option: The absence of domestic animals in Sungai Layau village likely reduced environmental contamination and thus lowered the risk of intestinal parasitic infections compared to other villages
Evidence: Rearing of animals such as pigs, chickens, ducks, dogs, cats and monkeys are common practices.

Although being a female (73.3%; p = 0.935), jobless (74.1%; p = 0.088), have had no close contact with animals (80%; p = 0.193) and not taking any iron supplement (73.5%; p = 0.801) were factors which had higher infection rates, nonetheless these variables were not statistically significant (Table 4).
In Sungai Layau village where each family was provided with a concrete house and basic amenities like treated water supply, prevalence of IPIs was shown to be significantly lower (4.5%).
435.Rationale: The passage does not report an absence of domestic animals in Sungai Layau; on the contrary, rearing animals is described as common in the surveyed communities. Moreover, close contact with animals was not a statistically significant predictor of infection in this study. The authors attribute Sungai Layau's low prevalence to infrastructure and income rather than to lack of domestic animals, so this option is not supported by the text.

436.Option: Conduct a before-and-after comparison of case detection rates using the same screening protocols to isolate the intervention effect
Evidence: In 2015, passive surveillance was expanded by equipping health facilities with new diagnostic tools.
The single (unstaged) data point in 2015 is unfortunately insufficient to generate robust estimates here.
For vector control, the lack of a control (non-intervention) area is a limitation for the study.

However, the aim of the intervention was to protect the people of Mandoul from sleeping sickness. This is an isolated focus without a comparable area, which could have served as an appropriate control. In the absence of a control, empirical data, gathered before and after entomological intervention, has been used in conjunction with models to estimate its relative impact.
436.Rationale: A simple before-and-after comparison assumes screening practices remain constant and that any change in detected cases reflects the intervention. The passage states passive surveillance was expanded in 2015, changing detection capacity, and that the single 2015 data point was insufficient to robustly estimate effects. It also highlights absence of a control area, which limits inference from before-after comparisons. Therefore a raw before-and-after comparison using observed case detection rates would not reliably separate the intervention effect from contemporaneous changes in surveillance.

436.Option: Use a matched cohort design with individuals from the same villages and time periods to compare case incidence between those who received vector control and those who did not
Evidence: For vector control, the lack of a control (non-intervention) area is a limitation for the study.
This is an isolated focus without a comparable area, which could have served as an appropriate control.
The first deployment was done in January—February 2014, and all targets were replaced at the same period in 2015 and 2016.
436.Rationale: A matched cohort comparing exposed and unexposed individuals requires there to be populations that did not receive the vector control within comparable time periods. The passage explicitly notes there was no non-intervention control area and that targets were deployed across the focus, making it infeasible to construct contemporaneous matched cohorts differing by vector-control exposure. Thus this design is not practicable here for separating intervention effects from other changes.

436.Option: Calculate the correlation between tsetse catch rates and reported HAT cases over time to infer the intervention's impact
Evidence: Entomological monitoring was done using 44 geo-referenced sentinel traps which were selected according to both geographical coverage of the area and tsetse presence in the traps during the T0 survey.

At the first evaluation post target deployment (T1, 2 months later), only 2 tsetse were caught (0.02 tsetse/trap/day) were observed (Fig 3). The same density was again observed during the second evaluation (T2) but thereafter no tsetse was caught during the next three successive surveys (T3-T6)...
The model provides estimates for the decline in new infections, but also the predicted impact of both medical and vector controls on future HAT reporting and transmission.

Counterfactual model simulations with medical interventions but no vector control were conducted to establish the expected impact that active and passive screening would have had in the absence of vector interventions.
436.Rationale: Temporal correlation between vector abundance and reported cases could be suggestive, and the passage documents dramatic declines in tsetse catches after targets. However, correlation alone cannot disentangle the effects of changing surveillance/screening from true transmission changes. The authors therefore relied on modelling and counterfactual simulations to attribute changes to vector control versus medical/surveillance changes, indicating that simple correlation is insufficient to isolate the intervention effect.

436.Option: Compare the ratio of stage 1 to stage 2 cases before and after the intervention to assess changes in transmission dynamics
Evidence: In 2014, there was a decrease in the stage 1 to stage 2 detection ratio for both active and passive surveillance. For active screening this ratio was 1.5 stage 1 cases for every stage 2 case which decreased from 2.7 in 2012 and 2013 when a similar level of active screening was achieved. For passive surveillance there were 0.16 stage 1 cases per stage 2 case in 2014, which decreased from 0.22 and 0.24 in 2012 and 2013 respectively.

This relative decline in the stage 1 to stage 2 case ratio supports the hypothesis that vector control has reduced new transmissions, and is expected to continue to decline over the coming years whilst tsetse control remains in place, although the improved passive surveillance that began in 2015 could skew this ratio.
One particular challenge was to estimate the impact of new enhanced medical strategies, which began in the region in 2015, on case detection and reporting.
436.Rationale: Changes in the stage 1:stage 2 ratio can provide signals consistent with reduced transmission (earlier detection or fewer new infections). The passage notes such a decline and interprets it as supportive evidence. However, it also cautions that enhanced passive surveillance beginning in 2015 could skew this ratio and that estimating the impact of changed medical strategies is challenging. Thus while informative, this metric alone cannot reliably separate intervention effects from surveillance changes without additional methods (e.g., modelling).

436.Option: Use counterfactual modeling to simulate outcomes in the absence of vector control and compare with observed data to isolate the intervention's impact
Evidence: In order to determine the unobservable impact of vector control upon the incidence of new infections, a previously developed mathematical model [21, 22] for Gambian HAT was modified.

Case data from 2000–2013 was used to fit the model, while 2014 and 2015 were predicted based on known active screening levels continued passive surveillance and tsetse population reduction. These years were therefore used as validation years to test the predictive ability of the model.

Counterfactual model simulations with medical interventions but no vector control were conducted to establish the expected impact that active and passive screening would have had in the absence of vector interventions.
In the absence of a control, empirical data, gathered before and after entomological intervention, has been used in conjunction with models to estimate its relative impact.
436.Rationale: The passage describes exactly this approach: fitting a transmission model to historical data and running counterfactual simulations (medical interventions but no vector control) to estimate what would have happened without the vector intervention. This method allows separation of the effects of vector control from changes in screening/surveillance because the model can incorporate differing screening strategies and generate alternative scenarios, enabling attribution of observed declines to vector control versus changes in surveillance.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence in camels and cattle compared to sheep and goats, despite similar age distributions, indicates that species-specific differences in immune response duration are the primary driver of the observed patterns.
Evidence: This study revealed considerable prevalence differences between animal species (Table 1 and Fig 2B). Cattle samples showed a seroprevalence of 68.66% ... The prevalence of camels (81%), however, was even higher than in cattle.
Most of the camels in this study were older than 10 years.

However, age effects alone cannot explain prevalence differences between large and small ruminants in young, as well as in old age groups. Therefore, differences in husbandry systems may also play a role in terms of CCHFV seroprevalence.
437.Rationale: The option assumes similar age distributions across species, but the passage explicitly indicates camels (and some cattle) were older on average and that age and husbandry differences likely contribute to prevalence differences. The text therefore does not support the claim that species-specific immune persistence is the primary driver; instead it identifies age and husbandry as important contributors.

437.Option: The extensive grazing and higher tick exposure in camels and cattle compared to semi-intensive management of sheep and goats provides stronger evidence for exposure-driven seroprevalence than differences in antibody persistence
Evidence: It was shown for cattle [13] and small ruminants [38] that nomadic grazing will significantly increase seroprevalences due to higher tick exposition risks compared to stationary trough feeding systems.

In general, the husbandry of camels in Mauritania differs from the small ruminants. Camels are extensively kept and spend almost their entire live grazing in the bushlands. In contrast, sheep and goats are bred semi-extensively and spend less time on the pasture. They also receive more frequently veterinary treatment (antiparasitics etc.) than camels.
Therefore, differences in husbandry systems may also play a role in terms of CCHFV seroprevalence.
437.Rationale: The passage directly links husbandry (extensive grazing, nomadic grazing) to higher tick exposure and higher seroprevalence, and contrasts camel/cattle extensive grazing with sheep/goat semi‑extensive systems. These statements provide the strongest direct support for an exposure-driven explanation rather than differences in antibody persistence across species.

437.Option: The significant age-related increase in seroprevalence across all species, combined with the lack of experimental data on antibody persistence, suggests that longer immune memory in older animals is the most plausible explanation for the observed patterns.
Evidence: It is clear that the steady rise of antibody prevalences with increasing age in all species can be due to an additive effect.
However, hardly any experimental data are available on the CCHFV antibody persistence in livestock and wildlife, determining the extent of this effect.
Obviously, higher age coincides with a greater chance of exposure to CCHFV-positive ticks in endemic regions and thus becoming infected with the virus.
437.Rationale: Although age-related increases are observed, the authors explicitly caution that this can reflect cumulative exposure (an additive effect) rather than longer immune memory, and they note a lack of data on persistence. The passage therefore does not support the assertion that longer immune memory is the most plausible explanation; instead it favors repeated exposure as the likely cause.

437.Option: The fact that antibody prevalence rises steadily with age in all species, including those with shorter lifespans like sheep and goats, suggests that the immune system of these animals maintains protective antibodies for longer durations than in cattle and camels.
Evidence: It is clear that the steady rise of antibody prevalences with increasing age in all species can be due to an additive effect.
Obviously, higher age coincides with a greater chance of exposure to CCHFV-positive ticks in endemic regions and thus becoming infected with the virus.
Sheep and goats represent an important source of meat in Mauritania and are therefore slaughtered at an early age, while cattle and camels are often used as dual-purpose breeds.
437.Rationale: The passage interprets the age-related rise as likely due to increased cumulative exposure rather than longer antibody maintenance, and it highlights differences in slaughter age and husbandry that affect exposure opportunity. There is no evidence presented that small ruminants maintain antibodies longer than cattle and camels; the text instead points to exposure differences.

437.Option: The lack of significant differences in seroprevalence between goats and sheep, despite different husbandry systems, indicates that immune persistence is the dominant factor shaping seroprevalence patterns across species.
Evidence: When considering the species, there were no significant seroprevalence differences between goats and sheep nor between cattle and camels.

In general, the husbandry of camels in Mauritania differs from the small ruminants. Camels are extensively kept and spend almost their entire live grazing in the bushlands. In contrast, sheep and goats are bred semi-extensively and spend less time on the pasture.
Therefore, differences in husbandry systems may also play a role in terms of CCHFV seroprevalence.
437.Rationale: Although goats and sheep had similar seroprevalence, the passage notes that husbandry for small ruminants is broadly similar (both semi‑extensive) and emphasizes husbandry/exposure as a driver overall. The cited lack of difference between goats and sheep does not imply immune persistence dominates across all species; rather it is consistent with similar exposure patterns among these two species and with exposure-driven explanations for inter-species differences elsewhere.

438.Option: Conducting periodic population-based surveys using the Tropical Data protocol to assess trachoma prevalence across all health districts
Evidence: The aim of this survey was to determine the extent of trachoma among children aged 1 to 9 years on the one hand, and on the other hand, to estimate the prevalence of TT in people aged 15 years and over according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by WHO.

The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds (Table 3).
The 2017 survey, conducted according to the WHO recommended methodology, revealed that the prevalence of key indicators was below the WHO elimination threshold for trachoma.
438.Rationale: Population-based Tropical Data surveys are important for establishing prevalence and validating elimination but are episodic (e.g., the 2017 survey) and resource intensive. Because they are periodic rather than continuous, they are less directly suited to timely detection and rapid response to a resurgence compared with routine surveillance systems. The cited passage describes the surveys' role in assessing prevalence and validation, not as the primary ongoing mechanism for early detection.

438.Option: Use of door-to-door mass treatment campaigns to identify suspected trichiasis cases and trigger immediate surgical intervention
Evidence: Since 2015, the NPNTD has been organizing the Integrated mass treatment with Ivermectin, Albendazole and Praziquantel ... every year.
As part of the mass treatment activities conducted door-to-door in all regions of the country, Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis.

This door-to-door strategy provided a national overview of the situation, particularly in the Plateaux ... and Maritime ... districts, which had not benefited from a prevalence survey for active or sequelae trachoma.
438.Rationale: Door-to-door mass treatment campaigns with trained CHWs were used effectively for case-finding and provided broad geographic coverage, but these campaigns are periodic activities (linked to mass treatment rounds) rather than continuous surveillance. While they help identify cases and refer for surgery, the passage emphasizes integration of routine reporting into DHIS2 for ongoing detection. Thus mass-treatment case-finding is useful but does not most directly provide timely, continuous detection and response.

438.Option: Integration of routine trachoma case reporting into the national DHIS2 surveillance system
Evidence: In 2011, Togo, like other member countries of the WHO African Region, adopted the Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response technical guide which aims to strengthen the national surveillance system. Trachoma is included in the Integrated Disease Surveillance and Response technical guide and is one of the notifiable diseases and events.
NCD focal points are trained on how to enter trachoma surveillance indicators into District Health Information Software 2 (DHIS2).

The indicators on TF and TT are integrated into the DHIS2 and filled in by the NTD district focal points and the ophthalmic technicians in the districts. Routine TF and TT cases are reported by the senior ophthalmic technicians and entered into the DHIS2 by the NTD district focal points.
Post- validation monitoring and surveillance is essential to detect the resurgence of the disease because elimination as a public health problem is a reversible state.
438.Rationale: Routine integration of TF and TT reporting into DHIS2 creates a continuous, centralized, and timely reporting channel (with trained focal points and technicians entering routine cases), which most directly enables early detection of increases (resurgence) and prompt response. The passage explicitly links inclusion in surveillance guidance, DHIS2 data entry, and the importance of post-validation monitoring for detecting resurgence.

438.Option: Training Community Health Workers to conduct annual awareness campaigns on facial cleanliness and hygiene promotion
Evidence: Facial cleansing activities were highly integrated with those of the integrated onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis and geo-helminthiasis mass treatment, as well as with those of the Hygiene and Sanitation service.
Each year, during the implementation of the integrated mass treatment ... the Community Health Workers sensitize household members on hand and face washing.
438.Rationale: Training CHWs to promote facial cleanliness is an important prevention strategy that reduces transmission risk, but it is an intervention/behavior-change activity rather than a surveillance mechanism. The question asks about the feature that most directly supports timely detection and response; prevention/awareness campaigns do not themselves provide routine case reporting or monitoring needed for timely detection.

438.Option: Establishing a national registry of all individuals who received trichiasis surgery to monitor long-term outcomes and recurrence
Evidence: The project diagnosed 108 people with TT with 159 eyes to cure. 116 eyes received surgery while 43 eyes received epilation of eyelashes for those who refused surgery.

For the 3 to 6 month postoperative follow-up, most of patients did not respond to the follow-up appointment. Therefore, the Program did not have the opportunity to perform long-term follow-up for most of the patients.
438.Rationale: A national surgical registry could aid monitoring of outcomes and recurrence, but the passage does not describe such a registry and in fact highlights poor postoperative follow-up, limiting long-term monitoring. Moreover, a registry of operated individuals monitors surgical outcomes rather than providing broad, routine surveillance of incident TF/TT across the population needed to rapidly detect resurgence. Thus this option is not the feature most directly supporting timely detection and response as described in the passage.

439.Option: Enhanced IL-7 signaling capacity in CD4+ T cells despite lymphopenia, as indicated by elevated pSTAT5 levels at baseline and after stimulation
Evidence: Upon rhIL-7 stimulation pSTAT5 was more noticeable in VL patient as compared to EC CD4+ T cells, seen both as frequency of cells positive for pSTAT5 (Fig 5B and 5C) and MFI of pSTAT5 (Fig 5D). Baseline levels (unstimulated cells) of pSTAT5 were higher in VL patient CD4+ T cell compared to ECs (Fig 5D), in line with reported higher STAT5 mRNA levels in VL compared to EC CD4+ cells in our previous Nano-String mRNA expression analysis [18].
In contrast, pSTAT5 levels were higher in VL patient CD4+ T cells at baseline and after stimulation with rhIL-7, compared to EC CD4+ T cells.
The increased IL-7 signaling may be a response to the lymphopenia to support the survival of existing T cells in VL.
439.Rationale: The passage reports higher baseline and rhIL-7–stimulated pSTAT5 in VL patient CD4+ T cells compared with endemic controls, and explicitly interprets increased IL-7 signaling as a possible response to lymphopenia to support T cell survival. Elevated pSTAT5 is direct functional evidence that IL-7 signaling is increased, which most directly supports the idea of a compensatory immune response despite persistent dysfunction.

439.Option: Elevated levels of IL-7 in plasma of VL patients, which serve as a biomarker of lymphopenia and indicate systemic immune activation
Evidence: analysis of soluble IL-7 in plasma demonstrated that the IL-7 levels were significantly higher in active, VL compared to EC (p<0.001) (Fig 2B).
The reduction in sIL-7Rα was accompanied by increased levels of IL-7 in plasma from VL patients (D0) compared to EC and VL post treatment (D30).
Increased plasma level of IL-7 is a sign of lymphopenia [30,31], something frequently observed in VL patients [32].
439.Rationale: The passage documents significantly higher plasma IL-7 in active VL and explicitly states that increased plasma IL-7 is a sign of lymphopenia. Elevated IL-7 therefore directly supports the hypothesis that the immune system is producing more IL-7 (a compensatory response) in the setting of lymphopenia, consistent with attempts to restore T cell numbers/function.

439.Option: Increased surface expression of IL-7Rα on CD4+ T cells in VL patients compared to endemic controls, indicating upregulation of the receptor in response to lymphopenia
Evidence: We found a divergence between the IL7RA mRNA and protein expression, with an increase in IL-7Rα surface protein on VL patient CD4+ T cells compared to ECs.
