454.Option: Using linear distance to the nearest water source may lead to differential misclassification because households with higher seroprevalence are more likely to use distant sources due to poor hygiene practices
Evidence: Second, we did not ask household which water source they were using. Households may use water sources that are further away via linear distance because of taste preference, ease of access, water source type or other reasons, namely terrain [51].
All of the above scenarios may have introduced non-differential misclassification of the exposure, which could bias associations towards the null.
454.Rationale: The passage notes households might use more distant sources for reasons like taste, access, or terrain, not because of household infection status; and it states these scenarios likely introduced non-differential misclassification. The option asserts differential misclassification tied to seroprevalence and hygiene practices, a claim not supported by the authors' described mechanisms, so the option is incorrect.

454.Option: The use of distance to the nearest water source as an exposure measure may overestimate the effect of water access on seroprevalence because it does not account for seasonal variation in water availability
Evidence: Third, the study site region is arid and there is variation in water availability by season. We simply measured the distance to the nearest water source at the time of the census and this may have not reflected a water source that was flowing and available at different times of the year.
All of the above scenarios may have introduced non-differential misclassification of the exposure, which could bias associations towards the null.
454.Rationale: The passage acknowledges that measuring distance at a single timepoint ignores seasonal variation, but frames this as a source of misclassification that would likely be non-differential and bias estimates toward the null, not systematically overestimate effects. Thus the option's assertion of overestimation is not supported by the text.

454.Option: The use of Euclidean distance may underestimate the true burden of water collection, leading to non-differential misclassification that biases associations toward the null
Evidence: First, we measured absolute Euclidean distance rather than walking distance or time it takes to collect water.

The study site region has tremendous gradation in altitude, with many high plateaus and steep valleys. In some cases, the distance to the nearest water source may not reflect the time it would take to ascend, descend or otherwise traverse the terrain.
All of the above scenarios may have introduced non-differential misclassification of the exposure, which could bias associations towards the null.
454.Rationale: The authors explicitly state they used Euclidean distance rather than walking time and note the rugged terrain can make linear distance a poor proxy for collection burden. They further state these measurement issues likely caused non-differential misclassification that would bias associations toward the null. Thus the option correctly describes the influence of the exposure definition on interpretation.

454.Option: Measuring distance to the nearest water source may result in overestimation of the association between water access and seroprevalence because it fails to distinguish between improved and unimproved water sources
Evidence: and thus used distance to the nearest water source (improved or unimproved) for the analysis.

Finally, we opted to measure distance to the nearest protected or unprotected water source to evaluate the effect of water quantity on enteropathogen and C. trachomatis transmission rather than water quality.

An alternative approach would be to evaluate the effect of water quality on enteropathogen transmission would be to assess the type of water source that was used by each household, measure the distance to that source and then evaluate associations between distance, water source type and seroprevalence, ideally tracking microbiological water quality \ longitudinally.
454.Rationale: The passage indicates the investigators deliberately included both improved and unimproved sources and framed the measure as assessing water quantity rather than water quality. They suggest an alternative analysis to assess water quality effects, but do not claim failure to distinguish source type would necessarily overestimate associations; direction of bias is not specified. Therefore the specific claim of overestimation is unsupported by the text.

455.Option: Prolonged prothrombin time (>13 seconds) combined with mechanical ventilation during hospitalization
Evidence: The non-recovery group was associated with a disproportionately higher incidence of the need for mechanical ventilation (25%), bacterial pneumonia (40%), and fungal pneumonia (35%) during the hospitalization course (P < 0.05).

These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).

Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery
455.Rationale: While prolonged PT (>13s) is an independent risk factor for delayed platelet recovery, mechanical ventilation was described as more common in the non-recovery group but was not identified as an independent baseline predictor of PLT recovery in the multivariate Cox model. Mechanical ventilation reflects severity during hospitalization (a later-occurring variable) rather than a baseline feature to guide early intervention. The strongest baseline combination should rely on independent baseline predictors (days from onset to admission and PT), so PT + mechanical ventilation is not the optimal baseline indicator.

455.Option: High baseline platelet count (>90 × 10⁹/L) combined with delayed hospital admission (>6 days)
Evidence: Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery, and baseline PLT greater than 90 × 109/L (HR: 7.929, 95%CI: 1.066–58.990, P = 0.043) contributed to PLT recovery (Table 2).

In this study, we constructed a Cox proportional hazard model associated with PLT recovery of SFTS patients to explore independent risk factors, which identified early treatment as a decisive factor in platelet recovery, providing clinicians with a practical approach to improve decision-making and avoid adverse clinical events.
Our study ... proposed that patients could improve more quickly if they sought timely medical attention within six days of onset.
455.Rationale: This option mixes one risk factor (delayed admission >6 days) with one strongly protective baseline feature (PLT >90 ×10⁹/L). The passage shows baseline PLT >90 ×10⁹/L is associated with markedly increased likelihood of recovery (HR ~7.9), so its presence undermines the argument that the combination 'most strongly indicates a need for early intervention' — delayed admission alone is a concern, but a high baseline PLT indicates favorable prognosis, making this pair not the strongest signal for urgent early intervention.

455.Option: Delayed hospital admission (>6 days) combined with prolonged prothrombin time (>13 seconds)
Evidence: These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).

Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery
Our study ... proposed that patients could improve more quickly if they sought timely medical attention within six days of onset.
455.Rationale: Both delayed admission (>6 days) and prolonged PT (>13s) are independent baseline predictors associated with reduced probability of platelet recovery within 14 days (significant HRs in multivariate Cox regression). The authors emphasize that seeking care within six days improves outcomes; therefore the combination of delayed presentation and coagulation abnormality most strongly signals the need for early intervention to prevent prolonged PLT recovery.

455.Option: Prolonged prothrombin time (>13 seconds) combined with diarrhea at admission
Evidence: The non-recovery group had a higher incidence of diarrhea (P = 0.007), while no significant differences were noted in symptoms of fever, phlegm, and neurological signs (P > 0.05).

Univariable Cox regression analysis of the PLT recovery within 14 days identified several factors: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline stratification of PLT, lymphocytes, PT, age, and Ca2+, as well as phlegm, Neurological signs, and diarrhea (P < 0.1). These variables were subsequently included in a multivariate analysis, which determined three independent predictors of PLT recovery: stratification of days from onset to admission, baseline PT, and baseline PLT (P < 0.05).

Days from onset to admission greater than 6 days (HR: 0.444, 95%CI: 0.259–0.763, P = 0.003) and PT greater than 13s (HR: 0.547, 95%CI: 0.373–0.800, P = 0.002) were identified as risk factors for PLT recovery
455.Rationale: Although diarrhea was more common in the non-recovery group and appeared in univariable analysis (P < 0.1), it was not retained as an independent predictor in the multivariate Cox model. Prolonged PT is an independent risk factor, but pairing it with diarrhea (a non-independent factor) is a weaker signal for prioritizing early intervention than pairing PT with another independent baseline risk (e.g., delayed admission).

456.Option: Use the presence of R. norvegicus fecal droppings as the sole criterion for identifying high-risk households due to its strong association with infection
Evidence: R. norvegicus fecal droppings had the strongest association with case households in the final model followed by rodent burrows, rodent access to water and un-plastered exterior wall surface.
To build a risk score, we assigned numerical scores to each of the four independent variables from the final model proportionate to the regression coefficient for each variable (Table 2).

These findings are encouraging and suggest that this tool could help inform more aggressive rat control to household locations with similar risk profiles without increasing the current workload of zoonotic control assessment teams.
456.Rationale: Although R. norvegicus fecal droppings had the strongest association with case households, the investigators used a multivariable model and combined four independent variables into a weighted risk score. The passage argues for using the composite score to classify and prioritize households rather than a single marker. Relying solely on fecal droppings would ignore other independent predictors (burrows, water access, un-plastered walls) that the study found important and used to improve classification accuracy.

456.Option: Conduct repeated environmental surveys at 3-month intervals to capture real-time changes in rodent infestation and household conditions
Evidence: Our study was limited by the time lag between occurrence of Leptospira infection and assessment of housing rodent survey.

Because of the time lag between occurrence of Leptospira infection and the household rodent survey, we asked the head-of-household if domicile or peridomicile structure changes had occurred (i.e. rebuilding or expansion), if nearby open sewers were closed/created and if refuse deposits were removed/created since the year of Leptospira infection.

Environmental surveys of case and control households were conducted by a team of rodent control specialists ... The survey team used a modified exterior inspection form, adapted from the CDC manual ... Team performing the surveys was blinded regarding household case status.
456.Rationale: The authors explicitly identify the time lag between infection and the single-time environmental assessment as a study limitation and took steps (questionnaire about structural changes) to try to account for temporal change. Repeated environmental surveys (e.g., quarterly) would directly address this limitation by capturing dynamic, real-time changes in rodent infestation and household conditions, preserving the ability to identify households whose risk status changes over time. The passage supports the need to account for temporal modification and shows the investigators considered temporal changes in their design and questions.

456.Option: Use a validated risk score based on persistent environmental and rodent infestation markers to prioritize households for targeted interventions
Evidence: Herein, by using a community-based cohort study aimed to evaluate Leptospira infection, we describe an environmental and rodent survey instrument, adapted from CDC guidelines, for use in a tropical slum area. In addition, we developed and evaluated the accuracy of a scoring system to predict Leptospira transmission using data easily produced by this instrument.

The final model retained four variables: two household environmental variables and two rodent infestation factors (Table 2). R. norvegicus fecal droppings had the strongest association with case households in the final model followed by rodent burrows, rodent access to water and un-plastered exterior wall surface.

Our summary score value for high risk environments is easy to use and increases accuracy in identifying high risk households. The score performed well in discriminating case from control households with an accuracy of 0.78 and a sensitivity and specificity at a point value of 3 of 80% and 60%, respectively.

We propose that targeting households at higher predicted risk for leptospirosis for augmented chemical, environmental and educational interventions would result in the greatest reduction of Leptospira transmission.
456.Rationale: The passage describes development, validation (AUC 0.78) and operational use of a weighted risk score based on objective environmental and infestation markers. Using this validated score to prioritize households is directly supported by the study findings and recommendations. While the score uses markers that may be relatively persistent and objectively measured, the authors also acknowledge temporal issues; nevertheless, the score is presented as a practical, accurate tool to identify and target high-risk households for interventions.

456.Option: Match case and control households on the number of inhabitants and household size to eliminate confounding by population density
Evidence: We observed that case households had a higher number of inhabitants and number of subjects enrolled in the cohort study than control subjects (4 [IQR: 4–6] vs. 4 [3]–[5], respectively, Table 1; and 4 [IQR: 2–5] vs. 3 [2]–[4], respectively, P<0.05).
Environmental variables with p<0.1 in the bivariate analysis were included in a multivariate logistic regression analysis to identify independent predictors of risk for Leptospira transmission.
We built three multivariate logistic regression models using backward elimination.
456.Rationale: Although household size and number of inhabitants differed between case and control households and could confound associations, the study addressed confounding using multivariable logistic regression rather than matching. Matching on household size would not by itself address the question of identifying high-risk households over time or capture dynamic environmental changes. Thus this approach is not the most effective for the study's stated operational goal of identifying high-risk households while accounting for changing environmental conditions.

456.Option: Calculate incidence rates of leptospirosis in households with and without rodent infestation signs to assess temporal trends in risk
Evidence: As previously described, we enrolled 2,003 participants from 684 households in a community cohort study designed to measure risk factors and infection rates for leptospirosis ... We identified 104 Leptospira infections in 97 participants residing in 80 households during the three year study period.
information regarding the incidence of leptospiral infection as determined by annual serosurvey is costly and labor-intensive, consequently such studies are a rarity.

These findings are encouraging and suggest that this tool could help inform more aggressive rat control to household locations with similar risk profiles without increasing the current workload of zoonotic control assessment teams.
456.Rationale: Calculating incidence rates can assess temporal trends but requires longitudinal cohort follow-up and repeated, costly serosurveys; the authors note that incidence measurement via annual serosurveys is labor-intensive and rare. For practical, scalable identification and targeting of high-risk households while accounting for dynamic environmental conditions, the study favors operational tools (the survey instrument and risk score) and/or repeated environmental monitoring rather than routine incidence-rate estimation, which is less feasible for programmatic use.

457.Option: The fetal environment imposes strong selective pressures that favor specific viral variants, leading to a reduction in diversity and the emergence of shared haplotypes across fetuses
Evidence: There is a sharp reduction in ZIKV diversity in most fetal bodies at this later stage of ZIKV infection.

The homogenous ZIKV populations in the fetal bodies and brains may arise due to genetic drift, purifying selection, or positive selection pressures on variants with beneficial transmission or replication phenotypes.

The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift.

The increase in frequency from the placenta to the fetal body to the fetal brain suggests that these variants may have been selected for in the fetal tissues, however we cannot definitively make this conclusion.
457.Rationale: The passage describes a clear pattern: early transmission from placenta to fetus preserves diversity, but at later stages fetal bodies and brains become homogenous with dominant variants and shared haplotypes across multiple fetuses/dams. The authors explicitly state that the presence of the same haplotypes across multiple fetuses suggests positive selection, and that the increase in frequency from placenta → fetal body → brain is "suggestive of selection." Thus the observed pattern is best interpreted as the fetal environment imposing selective pressures favoring particular variants, producing reduced diversity and repeated haplotypes across fetuses (while the authors note this is suggestive rather than definitive).

457.Option: The observed reduction in viral diversity in fetal tissues is due to random genetic drift, as the small number of founding viral particles in the fetus leads to stochastic loss of variants over time
Evidence: The homogenous ZIKV populations in the fetal bodies and brains may arise due to genetic drift, purifying selection, or positive selection pressures on variants with beneficial transmission or replication phenotypes.

The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift.

Of the four fetal brains/bodies containing the 3895 single variant haplotype (D-1, D-2, E-1, E-2), this haplotype was present in the majority of sequences from all four of their matched placentas (variant frequency 57–82%; Fig 4B and Tables 4 and 5). Therefore, this haplotype likely was present in the fetus at the time of transmission to the brain, and may have become the dominant variant in the brain simply due to chance.
457.Rationale: The authors acknowledge that genetic drift (founder effects) could explain homogenous populations in some cases and explicitly mention drift as one possible mechanism. However, they also emphasize that the repeated observation of identical haplotypes dominating across multiple fetuses and dams argues for positive selection rather than mere chance. Option 1 asserts drift as the (sole) cause; the passage presents drift as a possible contributor but privileges selection as the more likely explanation for the shared haplotypes, so the statement as stated is not the best interpretation of the observed pattern.

457.Option: The reduction in viral diversity is primarily driven by the maternal immune response, which selectively clears certain viral variants from the fetal circulation
Evidence: We used the previously established anti-IFNAR1 treated Rag1-/- (AIR) mouse model of ZIKV vertical transmission.
Although these mice also lack B and T cells, we selected this model because it was associated with higher fetal viability than the Ifnar-/- x WT model.
However, as adaptive immune responses do not develop until after birth in the mouse, there would be no effect of lymphocyte deficiency on selection in the fetus.
It is therefore possible that the lack of B and T cells in the dam could influence the viral variants present in the placenta and their ability to vertically transmit to the fetus.
457.Rationale: The suggestion that the maternal immune response is the primary driver is inconsistent with the experimental model and the authors' interpretation. The study used immunocompromised (anti-IFNAR1 treated Rag1-/-) dams that lack B and T cells, and the authors state adaptive immune responses in mice do not develop until after birth, so maternal adaptive immunity is unlikely to be the selective force shaping fetal viral populations. The authors instead point to selection within the fetal environment (or drift/bottlenecks) as explanations.

457.Option: The fetal brain acts as a reservoir that maintains high viral diversity, which is then transmitted back to the fetal body, explaining the homogenous populations observed in both tissues
Evidence: ZIKV populations in fetal brains are homogenous and similar to the fetal bodies.
All 10 fetal brains contained dominant variants, compared to 8 of 10 fetal bodies and 0 of 10 placentas (Tables 4–6).
the decrease in diversity in ZIKV populations in the fetal bodies may be due to the dominant fetal brain variants disseminating out of the brain and replacing the population in the periphery.
457.Rationale: The premise of this option is that the fetal brain maintains high diversity and seeds the periphery; the passage actually reports that fetal brains are largely homogenous (contain dominant variants), not high-diversity reservoirs. The authors do raise the possibility that dominant variants in the brain might disseminate outwards and contribute to peripheral homogeneity, but that is the brain seeding the body with dominant (low-diversity) variants, not the brain maintaining high diversity. Therefore the option's key claim (brain maintains high diversity) is contradicted by the text.

457.Option: The similarity in viral populations between placentas and fetal bodies at early stages indicates that the fetal environment does not exert any selective pressure, and the reduction in diversity later is due to replication errors and increased mutation rates
Evidence: These results indicate that while specific frequencies of variants may differ between placentas and fetuses, the overall makeup of variants and ZIKV population structures were very similar between the placenta and fetal virus populations at the early stages of placenta-to-fetus transmission. This suggests that there is little or no selection of specific ZIKV variants during vertical transmission.

Overall, there was a small reduction in ZIKV diversity in the placentas and a sharp reduction of ZIKV diversity in most fetal bodies at this later stage of ZIKV infection. This observation suggests that after viral entry into the fetus from the placenta, selection pressures within the fetus result in the homogenous population.

The homogenous ZIKV populations in the fetal bodies and brains may arise due to genetic drift, purifying selection, or positive selection pressures on variants with beneficial transmission or replication phenotypes.
457.Rationale: While early similarity between placenta and fetus shows limited selection at the moment of transmission, the authors attribute the later reduction in diversity to selection pressures within the fetus (or alternatively drift/bottlenecks), not to increased mutation rates. Replication errors and increased mutation rates would generally tend to increase diversity rather than cause the sharp reduction observed. The passage repeatedly frames the later reduction as the result of selection/drift/founder effects, not increased mutation, so option 4 is not supported by the text.

458.Option: Use multivariable logistic regression adjusting for parasitaemia to assess the independent association between neutrophil activation and acute lung injury
Evidence: Logistic regression models were fitted to evaluate increasing plasma NE or neutrophil counts as predictors of ALI at the time of patient enrolment. To predict ALI outcome, logistic regression models were fitted, including controlling for age (≥45 years), neutrophilia (>7,700 neutrophils/μL using hospital automated counts) and log-transformed parasitaemia with backwards stepwise selection of variables method. Final model selection was evaluated using the area under the receiver-operating-characteristic curve (AUC).

Although univariable logistic regression showed that NE was a predictor variable for ALI (aOR 2.00, p<0.001), it was not an independent predictor when controlling for parasitaemia, age and neutrophil counts on multivariable logistic regression (Model 1, S2 Table).

In a second multivariate logistic regression model for clinical predictors of ALI at hospital presentation with routinely collected demographic and laboratory data, neutrophilia (aOR 4.30, 95%CI:1.42–13.0, p = 0.01) and age>45 years (aOR 3.41, 95%CI:1.34–8.68, p = 0.010) remained independent clinical predictors of ALI when controlling for the other known major association with severe disease (parasite count>15,000/μL).
458.Rationale: Multivariable logistic regression that includes parasitaemia as a covariate is the standard analytic approach to assess whether the association between neutrophil activation (e.g., plasma NE or neutrophil counts) and a binary outcome (ALI) is independent of parasite burden. The study itself used exactly this approach, fitting logistic regression models that controlled for parasitaemia (and other covariates) and reporting that NE was not an independent predictor after adjustment while neutrophilia was. Thus this strategy is both appropriate and demonstrably effective in this dataset.

458.Option: Stratify the analysis by parasite count categories and compare the odds of acute lung injury within each stratum to assess independence from parasite burden
Evidence: In a second multivariate logistic regression model for clinical predictors of ALI at hospital presentation with routinely collected demographic and laboratory data, neutrophilia (aOR 4.30, 95%CI:1.42–13.0, p = 0.01) and age>45 years (aOR 3.41, 95%CI:1.34–8.68, p = 0.010) remained independent clinical predictors of ALI when controlling for the other known major association with severe disease (parasite count>15,000/μL).

Our study had several limitations. ... despite the relatively large sample size for severe knowlesi malaria series, we did not have the power to examine the independence of relationships for most individual severe malaria syndromes.
458.Rationale: Stratification by parasite-count categories can in principle assess whether the neutrophil activation–ALI association persists within levels of parasitaemia (i.e., examine effect within strata). However, compared with multivariable regression it is less efficient (requires larger samples per stratum) and can suffer from sparse data and loss of power. In this study the authors used multivariable models controlling for parasite burden, and they explicitly note power limitations that would make stratified analyses less reliable; therefore stratification is a valid method but not the best approach here.

458.Option: Use a mediation analysis to determine whether the effect of parasitaemia on acute lung injury is transmitted through neutrophil activation
Evidence: Our study design did not allow for delineation of a causative relationship.

Logistic regression models were fitted to evaluate increasing plasma NE or neutrophil counts as predictors of ALI at the time of patient enrolment. To predict ALI outcome, logistic regression models were fitted, including controlling for age (≥45 years), neutrophilia (>7,700 neutrophils/μL using hospital automated counts) and log-transformed parasitaemia with backwards stepwise selection of variables method.
458.Rationale: Mediation analysis addresses a different question—whether parasitaemia leads to ALI via neutrophil activation—requiring stronger causal assumptions, temporal ordering, and typically larger samples and longitudinal data. The authors explicitly state their study design did not allow delineation of causation, and they instead used (multivariable) logistic regression to assess independence. Thus mediation analysis is not the appropriate or feasible method in this context to simply establish independence of the neutrophil–ALI association from parasite burden.

458.Option: Calculate the partial correlation between neutrophil activation and acute lung injury after controlling for parasitaemia to assess independence
Evidence: Associations between log-transformed NE, parasitaemia, and markers of disease severity were assessed using Pearson correlation.
Logistic regression models were fitted to evaluate increasing plasma NE or neutrophil counts as predictors of ALI at the time of patient enrolment.
458.Rationale: Partial correlation can measure the association between two continuous variables controlling for a third, and the study used Pearson correlations for continuous-variable relationships (e.g., NE and parasitaemia). However, ALI is a binary outcome, so correlation-based methods are not appropriate to estimate adjusted associations with a dichotomous outcome. Logistic regression (used by the authors) is the correct approach for assessing independence of predictors for a binary endpoint; therefore partial correlation is not the best strategy here.

458.Option: Compare the mean plasma NE levels between ALI and non-ALI groups after excluding patients with high parasitaemia to assess independence
Evidence: There were 33 P. knowlesi cases with ALI at presentation.

Our study had several limitations. ... despite the relatively large sample size for severe knowlesi malaria series, we did not have the power to examine the independence of relationships for most individual severe malaria syndromes.
Logistic regression models were fitted to evaluate increasing plasma NE or neutrophil counts as predictors of ALI at the time of patient enrolment.
458.Rationale: Excluding patients with high parasitaemia to compare means is problematic: it reduces sample size, risks selection bias, and does not appropriately adjust for the continuous confounding effect of parasitaemia. The study instead used multivariable logistic regression to adjust for parasitaemia while retaining all observations. Given the limited number of ALI cases (n=33) and the authors' note about power limitations, exclusion-based comparisons would further undermine power and are not an appropriate means to establish independence.

459.Option: The increased susceptibility to complement lysis in the NTPD1 null mutant is due to a loss of secreted NTPD2 activity, which normally protects the parasite from host immune factors
Evidence: In contrast, NTPD2 was secreted, and was not required for lesion development, suggesting that its primary role is in the sandfly vector.

In contrast to the NTPD1 null mutant, the NTPD2 null mutant exhibited a virulence phenotype in BALB/c mice that was indistinguishable from wild type parasites, regardless of whether promastigotes or amastigotes were used to initiate infection (Fig. 3D and 3E).
NTPD1 null mutant promastigotes were significantly more sensitive to serum lysis than wild type parasites (Fig. 5B–D).
459.Rationale: The passage states that NTPD2 is secreted but explicitly reports that deletion of ntpd2 did not alter virulence (NTPD2 “was not required for lesion development” and its null mutant was “indistinguishable from wild type parasites”), so loss of secreted NTPD2 cannot explain the increased serum sensitivity and delayed lesions observed in the NTPD1 null mutant. The observed complement sensitivity is linked in the text specifically to loss of NTPD1 and associated LPG defects, not to loss of NTPD2.

459.Option: The delay in lesion development results from a systemic host immune response triggered by the release of parasite nucleotides due to NTPD1 deficiency
Evidence: We propose that NTPD1 has an important role in regulating glycosylation pathways in the Golgi apparatus as loss of NTPD1 resulted in a defect in LPG elongation in stationary phase promastigotes.

Collectively, these results provide strong evidence that loss of Golgi NTPDase results in less efficient elongation of LPG in virulent stationary phase promastigotes, leading to increased susceptibility to complement lysis and a marked delay in lesion development.

NTPD1 is primarily located to the Golgi apparatus, and plays an important role in regulating both the maturation of surface LPG and the capacity of L. major promastigotes to initially establish lesions.
459.Rationale: While the passage mentions broader ideas about nucleotide hydrolysis and purinergic signaling earlier, the study's data and interpretation link the delay in lesion development to a Golgi-localized defect in LPG elongation caused by loss of NTPD1. The authors conclude the pathway leading to delayed lesions is increased susceptibility to complement lysis secondary to shorter LPG chains, not a systemic host response provoked by released parasite nucleotides. Moreover, NTPD1 is Golgi-localized (not a secreted ecto-enzyme), making the 'release of parasite nucleotides' mechanism unlikely based on the presented evidence.

459.Option: Shorter LPG chains on the parasite surface reduce resistance to complement-mediated lysis, leading to delayed disease progression in the host
Evidence: However, the LPG isolated from null mutant promastigotes reproducibly exhibited a lower average molecular weight on the SDS-PAGE gels (Fig. 4A) and eluted later from the octyl-Sepharose column (Fig. 4B), indicating shorter average chain length and/or reduced side chain branching.
NTPD1 null mutant promastigotes were significantly more sensitive to serum lysis than wild type parasites (Fig. 5B–D).

Collectively, these results provide strong evidence that loss of Golgi NTPDase results in less efficient elongation of LPG in virulent stationary phase promastigotes, leading to increased susceptibility to complement lysis and a marked delay in lesion development.
These studies demonstrate that NTPD1 is required during the early stages of promastigote infectivity, but has limited function in production of lesions following amastigote infection.
459.Rationale: The passage directly documents that the NTPD1 null mutant produces shorter LPG chains, that these mutants are more susceptible to complement (serum) lysis, and that this increased complement sensitivity is associated with a marked delay in lesion development when infections are initiated with promastigotes. The authors explicitly link loss of Golgi NTPD1 → decreased LPG elongation → increased complement lysis → delayed lesion development, which matches this option.

459.Option: The absence of NTPD1 leads to increased surface expression of GPI-anchored glycoproteins, which enhances complement activation and delays lesion development
Evidence: A number of surface molecules, including an abundant lipophosphoglycan (LPG) and several GPI-anchored glycoproteins, have been shown to be important for promastigote survival during these initial stages of infection [3].
expression of LPG is down-regulated in amastigote stages and neither LPG nor GPI-anchored proteins are required for the long term growth and survival of this stage in macrophages.
We propose that NTPD1 has an important role in regulating glycosylation pathways in the Golgi apparatus as loss of NTPD1 resulted in a defect in LPG elongation in stationary phase promastigotes.
459.Rationale: The passage presents evidence that loss of NTPD1 affects LPG elongation, not that it increases GPI-anchored glycoprotein expression. There is no data showing increased surface GPI-anchored proteins in the NTPD1 mutant; instead the mechanistic link drawn by the authors is defective LPG chain elongation leading to complement sensitivity and delayed lesions. The statement that GPI-anchored proteins are not required for long-term amastigote survival further undermines the specific claim that increased GPI-anchored expression explains the observed phenotype.

459.Option: The delay in lesion development is due to impaired intracellular replication of the NTPD1 null mutant within macrophages, independent of surface characteristics
Evidence: Interestingly, no delay in lesion development was observed when amastigote stages of the NTPD1 null mutant were used to initiate the infection (Fig. 3A–C).
Together, these studies demonstrate that NTPD1 is required during the early stages of promastigote infectivity, but has limited function in production of lesions following amastigote infection.

Collectively, these results provide strong evidence that loss of Golgi NTPDase results in less efficient elongation of LPG in virulent stationary phase promastigotes, leading to increased susceptibility to complement lysis and a marked delay in lesion development.
459.Rationale: If intracellular replication of amastigotes were impaired independently of surface characteristics, one would expect delayed lesion development when infections are initiated with amastigotes. However, the passage reports no delay when amastigotes initiate infection, and emphasizes the promastigote-stage LPG defect and complement sensitivity as the cause of delayed lesions. Thus the evidence points to a surface-related promastigote infectivity defect rather than impaired intracellular replication as the primary explanation.

460.Option: The presence of community-led health education campaigns in peri-urban areas reduces transmission risk more effectively than in rural fishing villages
Evidence: The paucity of peer-reviewed literature on community engagement in cholera control in Zambia, as shown in our review (Fig 2), points to a critical gap in evidence.

They concluded that challenges in the partnership collaboration included inadequate resources, poor communication, poor coordination, lack of clear shared vision, reactive response, poor involvement of the community, hegemonic powers and mistrust of each other [65]: “Despite the attempts at co-ordination, ministries have a tendency of operating in isolation, this has resulted in lack of a clear shared vision. This also contributes to duplications of tasks in trying to prevent an outbreak of cholera” [65].
Efforts to combat vaccine hesitancy must be sustained and embedded within long-term public health strategies, rather than implemented reactively during outbreaks [65].
460.Rationale: The passage does not describe peri-urban areas as having more or more-effective community-led education campaigns; instead it highlights a general paucity of peer-reviewed literature on community engagement, poor involvement of community, and challenges in coordination and trust. Therefore there is no textual support that community-led health education is the primary explanation for disparity in risk between peri-urban and rural fishing villages.

460.Option: The presence of partially accessible safe water infrastructure in peri-urban areas reduces exposure risk compared to rural fishing villages, which lack such alternatives
Evidence: It was found that in areas underserved by the municipal utility companies, private borehole companies known as ‘Water Trusts’ would operate small shops known as ‘kiosks’ where community members could go and draw small quantities of water in buckets at a minimal cost to cover the fees only, and not for profit [28].

Despite this limitation, they were noted to present a safer alternative than privately owned consumer boreholes and shallow wells in terms of faecal contamination with Escherichia coli and nitrite content of the water [28].
Those unable to afford the kiosk water tended to use unsafe surface water sources such as shallow wells in their locality [24,25].
The major risk factors for recurrent outbreaks in the country were poor access to water and sanitation services in urban unplanned settlements and the rural fishing villages [19,24,25].
460.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes peri-urban areas having partial, though limited, access to treated kiosk water operated by ‘Water Trusts’ that are safer than shallow wells or private boreholes. This contrasts with people who rely on unsafe surface water or shallow wells. That difference in availability of a partially accessible safer water source directly explains a disparity in exposure risk between peri-urban settlements (some access to safer kiosk water) and rural fishing villages (greater reliance on unsafe sources).

460.Option: Rural fishing villages have greater exposure to contaminated water due to reliance on open water bodies, which are more prone to environmental contamination than urban shallow wells
Evidence: They identified 16 other cholera hotspots and noted that outside of Lusaka, cases were mostly identified in districts bordering Tanzania, Mozambique, Malawi or the DRC – suggesting a linkage to the movement of people to and from neighbouring countries [19].

These presented the highest risk of contamination, particularly due to topographical features such as the high-water table in Lusaka Province, leading to a high risk of contamination of these shallow wells from nearby pit latrines [27,28].

The smallest surveillance unit of population reported was the ward level. It was found that the greatest risk for cholera was in the wards with the densest populations, unimproved sanitation and evidence of E. coli contamination of piped sources [14].
They identified ... the fishing villages in many of the areas bordering lakes and in high-density, peri-urban communities of Lusaka and the Copperbelt [19,22,26–28].
460.Rationale: The passage identifies rural fishing villages as areas bordering lakes and highlights environmental contamination of water sources (groundwater, shallow wells, and piped sources with E. coli). While it also discusses contaminated shallow wells in peri-urban settings, the specific mention of fishing villages bordering open water bodies and the discussion of environmental vibrio and groundwater contamination support the idea that reliance on open surface waters increases exposure to contaminated water in rural fishing communities. Thus the option is supported by the text as a direct explanatory factor.

460.Option: The higher population density in peri-urban areas leads to more efficient transmission, thereby increasing risk compared to rural fishing villages
Evidence: The smallest surveillance unit of population reported was the ward level. It was found that the greatest risk for cholera was in the wards with the densest populations, unimproved sanitation and evidence of E. coli contamination of piped sources [14].
The major risk factors for recurrent outbreaks in the country were poor access to water and sanitation services in urban unplanned settlements and the rural fishing villages [19,24,25].
Those presented the highest risk of contamination ... in high-density, peri-urban communities of Lusaka and the Copperbelt [19,22,26–28].
460.Rationale: The passage explicitly links the greatest cholera risk to wards with the densest populations combined with unimproved sanitation and contaminated water. This supports the interpretation that higher population density in peri-urban areas can produce more efficient person-to-person and environment-mediated transmission and thus explains why peri-urban settlements may have a higher or distinct risk profile compared with less densely populated rural fishing villages.

460.Option: Rural fishing villages experience higher risk due to greater exposure to raw fish consumption, which is a known transmission route for *Vibrio cholerae*
Evidence: Whilst poor hygiene practices (mostly superimposed on people due to lack of facilities) were a notable risk factor, consumption of food products, particularly fresh fish, was not associated with an increased risk [16].
460.Rationale: The passage clearly states that consumption of fresh fish was not associated with increased cholera risk, so attributing higher risk in fishing villages to raw fish consumption is contradicted by the evidence presented.

461.Option: Spotted doves may facilitate the long-distance spread of the Japanese lineage (clade E) of SFTSV due to their migratory behavior and high susceptibility to this viral strain.
Evidence: some species of migratory birds, such as swan geese (Anser cygnoides) and spotted doves, could be both parasitized by H. longicornis and infected by SFTSV. These two characteristics demonstrate the potential for these species to contribute to the long-distance spread of SFTSV via migratory flyways [7].

Of the two viral strains tested, JS2014 led to one death and higher viremia titers among infected birds, while JS2010 caused no fatalities and had relatively lower virus titers in the blood of inoculated birds (Fig 1).
Thus, as a potential amplifying host, the spotted doves may be more efficient in transmitting the Japanese lineage SFTSV.
At present, the SFTSV strains of clade E are the most widely disseminated in East Asia.
461.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that spotted doves can be parasitized by the vector tick and can be infected with SFTSV, enabling long-distance spread via migratory flyways. Experimental results show higher viremia and one mortality with JS2014 (the Japanese lineage, clade E) compared with JS2010, and the authors state spotted doves "may be more efficient in transmitting the Japanese lineage SFTSV." Combined with the note that clade E is widely disseminated, the evidence supports the inference that spotted doves could facilitate long-distance spread of the Japanese lineage.

461.Option: Spotted doves may be responsible for the introduction of the Chinese lineage (clade A) of SFTSV into Japan and South Korea due to their migratory routes overlapping with these regions.
Evidence: clades A, B, C and D are classified as the Chinese lineage, while clade E is classified as the Japanese lineage.
and all strains from Japan are from only clade E [4,25,26].

some species of migratory birds, such as swan geese (Anser cygnoides) and spotted doves, could be both parasitized by H. longicornis and infected by SFTSV. These two characteristics demonstrate the potential for these species to contribute to the long-distance spread of SFTSV via migratory flyways [7].
461.Rationale: The passage indicates that strains from Japan are exclusively clade E (the Japanese lineage), not clade A. While spotted doves' migratory behavior could move virus among regions, the experimental findings and phylogenetic data emphasize clade E dissemination. There is no passage evidence that spotted doves introduced the Chinese lineage (clade A) into Japan or South Korea; in fact the passage states Japan's strains are clade E, contradicting the option's claim.

461.Option: Spotted doves may contribute to the spread of SFTSV across East Asia by serving as reservoir hosts that maintain the virus year-round in both northern and southern regions.
Evidence: SFTSV has a broad spectrum of animal hosts but none of the animals thus far have been confirmed as reservoir hosts.
Spotted doves are common birds in China. This species is found in most parts of China in summer months, but in winter, most migrate to warmer areas of southern China [17].
This suggests that the infection of spotted doves with SFTSV was primarily self-limiting and infected birds mostly recovered after a period of viremia.
461.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that no animal has been confirmed as a reservoir host and describes spotted dove infections as self-limiting with recovery after a period of viremia. Additionally, spotted doves are migratory (present in northern areas in summer and moving to southern areas in winter), which argues against year-round local maintenance in both regions. Therefore, there is no support for the claim that spotted doves serve as year-round reservoirs maintaining the virus.

461.Option: Spotted doves may have played a key role in the initial emergence of SFTSV in central China due to their high susceptibility and widespread presence in the region.
Evidence: SFTS is an emerging zoonotic disease which is traced back to reports of an unknown infectious disease in rural areas of Hubei and Henan provinces in central China in 2009 [1].

To date, only a few mammals have been used as models for the study of SFTSV infection, including mice, goats, hamsters and macaques [10,14,15,16]. To our knowledge, this is the first study to examine spotted doves as a host of SFTSV.
This suggests that the infection of spotted doves with SFTSV was primarily self-limiting and infected birds mostly recovered after a period of viremia.
461.Rationale: Although spotted doves are susceptible and widespread, the passage provides no evidence that they were responsible for the initial emergence in central China. The authors note this is the first study examining spotted doves as a host and do not attribute the origin event to them. Moreover, infections were self-limiting rather than indicative of a long-term source driving initial emergence. Thus the claim is not supported by the passage.

461.Option: Spotted doves may act as a bridge vector between ticks and humans by transmitting SFTSV directly through contact, thereby increasing the risk of human infection in southern China.
Evidence: some species of migratory birds, such as swan geese (Anser cygnoides) and spotted doves, could be both parasitized by H. longicornis and infected by SFTSV.
Wild birds often travel long distances carrying parasites, including ticks, which may be infected with viruses and bacteria.
SFTSV has a broad spectrum of animal hosts but none of the animals thus far have been confirmed as reservoir hosts.
461.Rationale: The passage supports the role of birds as carriers of infected ticks and as potential amplifying hosts (i.e., facilitating tick transmission), but it does not provide evidence that spotted doves directly transmit SFTSV to humans via contact. The text emphasizes birds carrying parasitic ticks and possible transmission to ticks, not direct bird-to-human transmission. Additionally, no animals have been confirmed as reservoirs. Therefore the specific claim of direct transmission to humans and acting as a bridge vector by direct contact is not supported.

462.Option: Deployment was prioritized in areas with the highest human population density, as these are most likely to experience high disease incidence
Evidence: The human population of Yasa Bonga is estimated to be ~180,000 people [14].
remotely-sensed data to produce a map of potential tsetse habitat for this important vector, which guided the deployment of targets and allowed us to assess their impact.

This information and the resulting suitability map can be used by tsetse control programmes to validate their initial choices of where to deploy targets and identify additional areas in which to either expand the intervention or to establish additional monitoring sites.
462.Rationale: The passage does report the human population size, but it explicitly states that deployment was guided by remotely sensed habitat maps rather than human density. The quoted sentences show that habitat suitability mapping—rather than prioritization by human population density—guided where targets were deployed.

462.Option: Prioritization of deployment was based on predicted habitat suitability derived from environmental variables, despite potential underestimation of tsetse abundance due to species-specific trap inefficiency
Evidence: remotely-sensed data to produce a map of potential tsetse habitat for this important vector, which guided the deployment of targets and allowed us to assess their impact.
A total of 15 environmental covariates derived from the remotely sensed data presented in Table 1 were considered.

percentage of forest cover within a 350m radius, the range of EVI within a 350m radius, the mean topographical water index (TWI) within a 350m radius and land surface temperature are important contributors to the model.

This information and the resulting suitability map can be used by tsetse control programmes to validate their initial choices of where to deploy targets and identify additional areas in which to either expand the intervention or to establish additional monitoring sites.

The low catches of G. f. quanzensis in the current study may reflect differences in the sampling efficiency of pyramidal traps for G. f. quanzensis and G. f. fuscipes. There are no data to allow us to estimate any such difference but studies of other species of tsetse show that there can be large inter-specific differences in sampling efficiency [34].
462.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that remotely sensed environmental data were used to produce habitat suitability maps which "guided the deployment of targets." It also details the environmental covariates (e.g., forest cover, EVI, TWI, LST) used in the models. The authors note that observed catches may underestimate true abundance because of species-specific sampling efficiency, supporting the caveat in the option. Thus this option accurately summarizes the primary decision factor and its ecological caveat.

462.Option: Deployment was prioritized in areas where tsetse were first detected during exploratory surveys, as these locations represent active transmission foci
Evidence: Prior to the deployment of Tiny Targets, pyramidal traps [15] were deployed at 1,234 individual locations (Fig 1) and revisited at 1–2 day intervals to collect captured tsetse.

Data were collected over multiple field surveys conducted in 2015–2017, with the locations of these traps being driven by factors specific to each field visit i.e. exploratory surveys to determine whether flies could be found in specific environments (along stretches of river, around fishponds, in villages).
Based on results from the pre-intervention surveys of tsetse, we selected 23 locations which produced the largest tsetse catches to monitor the impact of targets.
remotely-sensed data to produce a map of potential tsetse habitat for this important vector, which guided the deployment of targets and allowed us to assess their impact.
462.Rationale: While exploratory surveys and trap detections informed understanding and were used to select sentinel monitoring sites, the passage states that deployment of targets was guided by habitat suitability maps derived from remotely sensed environmental data. Thus prioritization was driven principally by predicted habitat suitability rather than simply where tsetse were first detected.

462.Option: Deployment was prioritized in areas with the highest forest cover and vegetation index, as these are the most favorable conditions for tsetse survival and transmission
Evidence: percentage of forest cover within a 350m radius, the range of EVI within a 350m radius, the mean topographical water index (TWI) within a 350m radius and land surface temperature are important contributors to the model.
A total of 15 environmental covariates derived from the remotely sensed data presented in Table 1 were considered.
remotely-sensed data to produce a map of potential tsetse habitat for this important vector, which guided the deployment of targets and allowed us to assess their impact.
462.Rationale: Although forest cover and EVI were important contributors to the habitat suitability model, deployment was guided by the overall predicted habitat suitability derived from multiple environmental covariates rather than prioritization based solely on highest forest cover or EVI. Therefore the option overstates a single-factor rule when the study used a multivariable suitability model.

462.Option: Deployment was prioritized in areas with the highest observed tsetse catch rates, as these locations are most likely to have high transmission risk
Evidence: Based on results from the pre-intervention surveys of tsetse, we selected 23 locations which produced the largest tsetse catches to monitor the impact of targets.
we selected 23 locations which produced the largest tsetse catches to monitor the impact of targets.
remotely-sensed data to produce a map of potential tsetse habitat for this important vector, which guided the deployment of targets and allowed us to assess their impact.
462.Rationale: High observed catch rates were used to choose sentinel monitoring locations, but the text explicitly says that remotely sensed habitat maps guided the deployment of Tiny Targets. Thus while observed catch rates informed monitoring site selection, prioritization of deployment locations relied primarily on predicted habitat suitability rather than simply highest observed catches.

463.Option: The high rate of post-interventional complications in patients with concomitant chronic hepatitis B infection indicates that viral coinfection is the most critical determinant of poor prognosis in hepatosplenic schistosomiasis
Evidence: Following TIPS insertion, a major deterioration of liver function with an increase of MELD score up to 28 was observed in one case, presumably aggravated by concomitant chronic hepatitis B virus infection. The patient later underwent liver transplantation at another center.
In another patient with suspected liver cirrhosis due to diabetes mellitus type 2 and inactive hepatitis B virus infection, a significant increase in liver enzymes was observed.

The study has some limitations, especially due to the small number of cases as a consequence of the rarity of HSS in Europe, and the limited follow-up period due to challenging long-term evaluation in this particular patient collective (e.g. patients with refugee status were re-allocated several times and thus lost to follow-up).

The data presented have been collected retrospectively and no control cohort is available. The study therefore does not allow differentiation between shunt surgery and TIPS in terms of complication rates, long-term survival, and DALYs.
463.Rationale: While the passage documents worse post-interventional outcomes in individual patients with chronic hepatitis B (e.g., major MELD increase and later transplantation), the case series is small and retrospective and includes multiple comorbidities (e.g., diabetes plus inactive HBV). The authors explicitly note limitations (small numbers, no control cohort), so the data do not support the stronger claim that viral coinfection is the single most critical determinant of poor prognosis. The evidence supports that HBV coinfection can worsen outcomes in some cases, but not that it is the dominant or sole determinant.

463.Option: The recurrence of hepatic encephalopathy after discontinuation of lactulose therapy suggests that the intervention itself is insufficient to prevent long-term neurological complications in patients with hepatosplenic schistosomiasis
Evidence: In addition to the three previously mentioned patients with hepatic encephalopathy immediately after TIPS insertion, two more patients experienced de-novo episodes of overt hepatic encephalopathy during later follow-up. One of these episodes was associated with a gastrointestinal infection, the other with discontinuation of prophylactic lactulose treatment. Both subsided upon increasing or restart of the lactulose therapy and addition of rifaximin.
After the administration of lactulose and rifaximin or management of the underlying infection, no episodes of encephalopathy reoccurred.
463.Rationale: The passage shows encephalopathy recurred after stopping lactulose but resolved when lactulose/rifaximin were restarted, indicating that encephalopathy was manageable with medical therapy rather than representing inevitable long-term neurologic damage. Thus the observation supports the importance of ongoing host-specific management (medication adherence, treating precipitating infections) rather than proving that the intervention (TIPS/shunt) is inherently insufficient to prevent long-term neurological complications.

463.Option: The presence of comorbid conditions such as chronic hepatitis B infection or type 2 diabetes mellitus may significantly increase the risk of severe post-interventional complications despite stable underlying liver function in hepatosplenic schistosomiasis
Evidence: Liver function in patients with HSS is generally unimpaired, with isolated portal hypertension due to periportal fibrosis.

Following TIPS insertion, a major deterioration of liver function with an increase of MELD score up to 28 was observed in one case, presumably aggravated by concomitant chronic hepatitis B virus infection. The patient later underwent liver transplantation at another center.

In another patient with suspected liver cirrhosis due to diabetes mellitus type 2 and inactive hepatitis B virus infection, a significant increase in liver enzymes was observed. Both patients showed de-novo episodes of hepatic encephalopathy which subsided under intensified treatment with oral lactulose and/or rifaximin.
In two patients, the episode of encephalopathy was observed during an acute infection.
463.Rationale: The passage contrasts generally preserved liver function in HSS with specific cases where patients with comorbidities (chronic hepatitis B, type 2 diabetes) experienced marked post-interventional deterioration (increased MELD, elevated enzymes, encephalopathy, and one eventual transplant). These examples indicate that host-specific conditions beyond the baseline periportal fibrosis can materially affect post-procedure risk, supporting this option as the best-supported interpretation.

463.Option: The development of elevated bilirubin levels post-TIPS in patients with hypersplenism indicates that mechanical hemolysis from splenic hyperflow is a primary driver of liver function deterioration
Evidence: The noticeable elevation of Child-Pugh and especially MELD scores following TIPS was mostly attributable to elevated bilirubin levels, with predominantly unconjugated bilirubin indicating hemolysis.

However, hemolysis is less likely in newer covered TIPS, so the bilirubinemia may most likely be attributable to a combination of reduced liver perfusion via shunting and hemolysis owing to hypersplenism.
Portal hyperflow in our HSS patients with massively enlarged spleens may contribute to the higher degree of hemolysis.
463.Rationale: While unconjugated bilirubin suggesting hemolysis and the role of hypersplenism/portal hyperflow are noted, the authors explicitly attribute the bilirubinemia to a combination of reduced liver perfusion from shunting and hemolysis rather than naming mechanical hemolysis as the primary driver. Therefore the option's claim that hemolysis from splenic hyperflow is the primary driver overstates what the data support.

464.Option: The inconsistent public health messaging about physical contact led to widespread confusion, causing individuals to disregard all guidance and engage in uncontrolled physical contact with the sick.
Evidence: one message said, “Don’t touch,” while another said, “Touch, but use plastic gloves.” The lack of consistency may have resulted in experimentation and innovation, but it also elevated local perceptions that the message “Don’t Touch” was impractical and unhelpful in the context of immediate need.
It will be impossible that my child or husband is sick and I refuse to touch them. I do not have the courage or heart to do that.
women—reported that they would care for their sick family members on their own, and that they preferred to do so inside the home.
464.Rationale: The passage documents inconsistent messaging, but it attributes continued physical contact primarily to caregiving imperatives and the impracticality of the advice in real-life caregiving situations, not to a simple pattern of widespread disregard leading to 'uncontrolled' contact. Thus the causal claim that inconsistency alone caused widespread disregard and uncontrolled contact overstates what the passage supports.

464.Option: The cultural imperative to provide family caregiving, especially among women, outweighed the public health message to avoid physical contact, leading to unavoidable exposure despite inconsistent guidance
Evidence: women—reported that they would care for their sick family members on their own, and that they preferred to do so inside the home.
It will be impossible that my child or husband is sick and I refuse to touch them. I do not have the courage or heart to do that.
Women showed an intense conviction that they should care for their families, and showed a desire to do so, despite risks to their own health.
464.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes a strong cultural/ethical imperative to provide caregiving—especially among women—which led people to provide hands-on care despite public health messages advising avoidance of contact. This directly supports the option's assertion that caregiving norms outweighed avoidance messages and produced unavoidable exposures.

464.Option: The failure of health facilities to respond to community calls created a perception that hospitals were unsafe, leading individuals to avoid seeking care and instead rely on home-based isolation, increasing household transmission.
Evidence: When community leaders called for the creation of a communications infrastructure, it was because their calls to hotlines had gone unanswered.
The anonymity involved in the process of removing the sick and the dead terrified people, and played a role in their decisions to avoid ETUs and hospitals and to conduct secret burials.
By the time of this study, many of those who had gone to hospitals and ETUs had never returned, or had been turned away from multiple facilities due to lack of beds.
Instead, community-based quarantine was identified as the best available strategy or approach.
464.Rationale: The passage links failures in response (unanswered hotlines, turnaways, fear of ETUs) to avoidance of formal care and to reliance on community/home-based quarantine and care. That shift to home-based care plausibly increased household exposure and sustained transmission, which the passage supports.

464.Option: The gendered division of labor in caregiving placed women at higher risk, leading to a disproportionate number of infections within households and sustained transmission through female caregivers.
Evidence: The domestication of surveillance among women caring for the bodies of others within households might have put women at a greater risk of infection within the household, especially under quarantine and isolation conditions, while men were more likely to be infected outside of the household (e.g. through transportation and porterage activities).

This may have also had unrecognized repercussions on infant and child mortality, and on the now-confirmed explosive chains of Ebola transmission within kinship networks and families during the Ebola crisis [46].
This may have also had unrecognized repercussions ... although this has not been borne out by the official data [47–48, 22].
464.Rationale: While the passage describes a gendered division of labor that likely increased women's exposure, it explicitly states that a disproportionate burden in official infection data has not been borne out. The option's stronger claim—that this led to a disproportionate number of infections and sustained transmission via female caregivers—is not supported by the passage.

464.Option: The widespread use of anti-malarials in home care settings led to delayed diagnosis and treatment of Ebola, increasing the duration of infectious contact and contributing to ongoing transmission.
Evidence: When someone within a household fell ill, community leaders reported that they were first cared for within the home with palliative care. The person was administered “first-aid treatment,” including locally available pharmaceuticals, herbal remedies, locally accessible oral rehydration solutions or therapies, the provision of fluids, and the early administration of anti-malarials.
464.Rationale: The passage documents that home caregivers often used anti-malarials as part of early home care, but it does not provide evidence that this practice delayed diagnosis/treatment of Ebola or directly increased duration of infectious contact. The causal link asserted in the option is not stated or supported in the text.

465.Option: ZIKV prevents stress granule formation by directly cleaving eIF2α and blocking its phosphorylation at Ser51
Evidence: No alteration was observed in the levels of G3BP-1, TIAR and PABP between mock and infected cells (Fig 1C).
The amount of total eIF2α was similar under all conditions tested (Fig 4A), indicating that ZIKV replication does not alter its expression.
These results indicate that ZIKV infection impairs eIF2α phosphorylation triggered by oxidative stress.
eIF2α dephosphorylation is modulated by ZIKV
Our results show that are GADD34 levels are significantly higher in ZIKV-infected cells as compared to uninfected cells (Fig 6A and 6B).
465.Rationale: The passage explicitly states total eIF2α levels are unchanged, arguing against direct proteolytic cleavage of eIF2α by the virus. Instead, the data point to reduced phospho-eIF2α levels through altered dephosphorylation (increased GADD34). Thus there is no support for the claim that ZIKV prevents SG formation by cleaving eIF2α; the mechanism is modulation of phosphorylation status rather than proteolytic loss of the protein.

465.Option: ZIKV selectively disrupts eIF2α-dependent stress granule assembly by enhancing dephosphorylation of eIF2α via upregulation of GADD34
Evidence: These findings indicate that ZIKV infection blocks the assembly of type I SGs.

Ars is an oxidative agent that rapidly induces SG assembly through phosphorylation of eIF2α [31]. ... In contrast, ZIKV infected cells presented SG at a rate of only 21.6% (Fig 2A, bottom panel and Fig 2B).
ZIKV infection did not impair PatA-induced SG assembly, as 97.5% of the infected cells presented TIAR and eIF4G puncta (Fig 3A, top panels, and 3B).
Similarly to PatA-induced SG, no significant difference was observed in the assembly of SG between mock and infected cells treated with Se (Fig 3A, bottom panels, and 3B).
These results indicate that ZIKV infection impairs eIF2α phosphorylation triggered by oxidative stress.
Our results show that are GADD34 levels are significantly higher in ZIKV-infected cells as compared to uninfected cells (Fig 6A and 6B).
inhibiting eIF2α dephosphorylation reduces the ability of ZIKV infection to block Ars-induced SG assembly.
Treatment of cells with salubrinal causes an accumulation of phospho-eIF2α through an inhibition of PP1/GADD34-mediated dephosphorylation of eIF2α
465.Rationale: The passage shows ZIKV blocks SG assembly induced by eIF2α-dependent stressors (arsenite, DTT) but does not block SG assembly induced by eIF2α-independent stressors (PatA, Se), indicating selectivity for the eIF2α pathway. It also documents increased GADD34 in infected cells and shows that inhibiting eIF2α dephosphorylation (salubrinal/sal003) restores phospho-eIF2α and SG assembly and reduces viral replication. Together these findings support that ZIKV disrupts eIF2α-dependent SGs by enhancing dephosphorylation via upregulation of GADD34.

465.Option: ZIKV induces a general suppression of cellular translation that prevents stress granule formation regardless of the stressor type
Evidence: ZIKV infection triggers a potent repression of host cell translation initiation, while viral protein synthesis remains unaffected [19].
ZIKV infection did not impair PatA-induced SG assembly, as 97.5% of the infected cells presented TIAR and eIF4G puncta (Fig 3A, top panels, and 3B).
Similarly to PatA-induced SG, no significant difference was observed in the assembly of SG between mock and infected cells treated with Se (Fig 3A, bottom panels, and 3B).
These findings indicate that ZIKV infection blocks the assembly of type I SGs.
465.Rationale: Although ZIKV represses host translation initiation, the experimental data show that SG assembly still occurs in response to eIF2α-independent stressors (PatA and Se). Thus suppression of translation is not sufficient to explain loss of SGs across all stress types, and the effect is selective for eIF2α-dependent pathways rather than a general block of SG formation regardless of stressor.

465.Option: ZIKV broadly inhibits all forms of stress granule assembly by sequestering key nucleating proteins like G3BP1 and TIAR to sites of viral replication
Evidence: TIAR was still found in both the cytoplasm and nucleus but also concentrated in foci in the perinuclear region (Fig 1A and 1B), colocalizing with the ZIKV RNA (Fig 1A) and viral nonstructural protein, NS1 (Fig 1B).
No alteration was observed in the levels of G3BP-1, TIAR and PABP between mock and infected cells (Fig 1C).
ZIKV infection did not impair PatA-induced SG assembly, as 97.5% of the infected cells presented TIAR and eIF4G puncta (Fig 3A, top panels, and 3B).
Similarly to PatA-induced SG, no significant difference was observed in the assembly of SG between mock and infected cells treated with Se (Fig 3A, bottom panels, and 3B).
465.Rationale: While TIAR redistribution to perinuclear foci colocalizing with viral RNA is described, the data do not show wholesale sequestration that abolishes all SG assembly. Critically, ZIKV fails to block SG assembly induced by eIF2α-independent agents (PatA, Se), so the inhibition is not broad across all SG types. Also total levels of nucleating proteins are unchanged, arguing against widespread sequestration as the sole mechanism of inhibition.

465.Option: ZIKV blocks stress granule assembly by suppressing the expression of eIF2α kinases such as HRI and PERK during infection
Evidence: The activation of PERK in DTT-treated cells, demonstrated by an increased PERK mobility, was similar in mock and ZIKV-infected cells (Fig 5C, compare lanes 2 and 4, position 1: activate PERK; position 2: inactive PERK), suggesting that the reduced levels of p-eIF2α in infected cells are a result of an interference downstream the activation of the eIF2α kinases.

As expected, high levels of eIF2α phosphorylation (40-fold increase) were observed in extracts of mock-infected cells treated with Ars. However, in ZIKV-infected and Ars treated cells, levels of eIF2α phosphorylation were consistently lower (10.5-fold increase).
Our results show that are GADD34 levels are significantly higher in ZIKV-infected cells as compared to uninfected cells (Fig 6A and 6B).
inhibiting eIF2α dephosphorylation reduces the ability of ZIKV infection to block Ars-induced SG assembly.
465.Rationale: The passage shows eIF2α kinases are activated (PERK activation is similar in infected and mock cells), implying ZIKV does not suppress kinase activation/expression. Instead, evidence points to reduced phospho-eIF2α due to enhanced dephosphorylation (increased GADD34), i.e., action downstream of kinase activation. Therefore suppression of HRI/PERK expression is not supported.

466.Option: Calculate the cure rate using only individuals with egg counts above the detection threshold of the Kato-Katz technique to reduce the impact of false negatives
Evidence: Due to low diagnostic sensitivity of the Kato-Katz technique, it is more likely to miss infections of individuals with low intensities, and hence, overestimate CRs [29].
Our analysis addresses this issue, and hence, our ‘true’ CR estimates are lower than the observed CRs (Fig 2).
We also estimated the diagnostic sensitivity of the Kato-Katz thick smear technique.
466.Rationale: Excluding individuals with egg counts below the Kato-Katz detection threshold would systematically remove low-intensity infections and bias the sample toward higher-intensity infections; that would not solve the problem of overestimated cure rates and would likely distort generalizability. The passage emphasizes addressing the diagnostic error (missed low-intensity infections) through modelling (producing lower 'true' CRs), rather than by excluding low-count individuals.

466.Option: Use a fixed-effects meta-analysis with inverse-variance weighting to pool cure rates, adjusting for the number of Kato-Katz slides used in each trial
Evidence: This is the first model-based meta-analysis of the effects of different drugs and treatment regimen against hookworm infection, which takes into account the diagnostic error of the widely used Kato-Katz thick smear technique.
The model formulation enabled estimation of the egg intensity-dependent sensitivity of the Kato-Katz method for different number of slides.
466.Rationale: A fixed-effects pooling of observed cure rates, even with adjustment for slide number, would not directly model the measurement error arising from sensitivity that varies by infection intensity and day-to-day egg output. The authors present a model-based (Bayesian, individual-level egg count) approach that explicitly estimates intensity-dependent sensitivity and day-to-day variation; that direct modelling of diagnostic error is what addresses overestimation, whereas fixed-effects pooling of observed (biased) CRs would not.

466.Option: Use a Bayesian model that estimates diagnostic sensitivity as a function of infection intensity and incorporates day-to-day variation in egg counts
Evidence: In contrast, Bärenbold et al. (2017) developed an individual-level egg count Bayesian model, which takes into account the dependence of sensitivity on the infection intensity and includes the day-to-day variation in helminth egg output [16].

In this study, we pursued a Bayesian meta-analysis to compare the ‘true’ cure rate (CR) and egg reduction rate (ERR) of different treatments against hookworm infection considering the diagnostic error of the Kato-Katz thick smear technique.

We fitted a model to the observed egg count data that took into account the distribution of worms and estimated diagnostic sensitivity as a function of the infection intensity. We considered day-to-day variation in the egg counts and correlation of slides from the same stool sample.
The model formulation enabled estimation of the egg intensity-dependent sensitivity of the Kato-Katz method for different number of slides.
