Evidence: PCR is often positive in symptomatic Q fever patients before seroconversion [15] and with this suite of tests, it is unlikely any acute Q fever cases were missed.

Concurrently, the data received on Q fever tests already performed by NSW Health Pathology showed that Phase 1 and Phase 2 IgG and IgM assays (and occasionally IgA) had been done routinely when requested, but not PCR.

Fourthly, the Q fever diagnostic tests done for this study were slightly different to that done routinely by the NSW Health pathology. For example, C. burnetii PCR was not done for any of the samples tested in NSW Health pathology and paired serum testing done by NSW Health Pathology could not be done for any of the samples of this study. This effects direct head-to-head comparison of positive results.
477.Rationale: Although PCR can detect infection earlier than serology (and the study's use of PCR likely improved case detection), the passage does not present availability of routine PCR as the principal factor driving whether patients are diagnosed in practice. Instead, the strongest repeatedly emphasized determinant is clinician suspicion/familiarity (clinician-requested tests had much higher positivity). The passage notes PCR was not routinely performed by the clinical lab, and that this difference affects comparisons, but it does not claim PCR availability is the main reason patients are diagnosed versus missed.

478.Option: Inadequate patient isolation due to facility and resource limitations is the primary barrier to preventing secondary transmission after initial exposure
Evidence: The most common problems were the isolation of patients (47.8%), diagnosis-related problems (31.1%), management of individuals who were in contact with patients with infection (17.8%), problems related to staff caring for patients with infection (16.7%), and treatment problems (11.1%).

Regarding patient isolation, the following difficulties were encountered: insufficient facilities for long-term isolation, insufficient supplies such as linens and personal protective equipment, and difficulty in identifying and transferring patients with infection to other hospitals with facilities for long-term isolation [8, 16].
We found that confirmation of scabies diagnosis took over a week in approximately half of the cases (Table 3), resulting in the spread of secondary infections, making treatment difficult.
478.Rationale: The passage explicitly identifies patient isolation as the most common infection-control problem (47.8%) and details concrete facility- and resource-related obstacles (insufficient isolation facilities, linen/PPE shortages, difficulty transferring patients). Those limitations directly impede the ability to separate infectious individuals promptly and therefore most strongly promote secondary spread after an initial exposure. The citation linking delayed confirmation and secondary spread further supports that failure to isolate promptly (often due to resource/facility limits) leads to onward transmission.

478.Option: The lack of dedicated infection control personnel in long-term care hospitals directly correlates with higher rates of secondary transmission
Evidence: There was a difference in the incidence of scabies according to the number of hospital beds (p = 0.005) and the presence of an infection control department (p = 0.012).
Approximately 84% of the hospitals without an infection control department had cases of scabies.
The rate was significantly higher in hospitals without an infection control department than in hospitals with such a department.
478.Rationale: The passage provides statistical evidence that absence of an infection control department correlates with more scabies cases (84% of hospitals without such a department had cases, and incidence differed by presence of an infection control department). Although the text frames this as incidence rather than explicit secondary transmission rates, higher institutional burden of cases implies greater secondary spread within facilities lacking dedicated infection control personnel. Thus lack of infection control staff is a strong institutional factor associated with increased transmission.

478.Option: The high proportion of staff with limited knowledge about scabies leads to delayed diagnosis and increased secondary spread
Evidence: Providing regular education to the hospital staff and patients about scabies is considered beneficial in the prevention and control of scabies [28]. In this study, although most hospitals reported that they provided education on scabies to the staff (Table 1), they also suggested that regular education was necessary to control scabies outbreaks (Table 8).
One-time training and educating the staff only are not enough for preventing the spread of scabies. Increased efforts are needed to increase the effectiveness of scabies-related education.
With the absence of regular education on scabies, immediate management of patients with infection is difficult.
We found that confirmation of scabies diagnosis took over a week in approximately half of the cases (Table 3), resulting in the spread of secondary infections, making treatment difficult.
478.Rationale: The passage links insufficient or inadequate staff education to difficulties in immediate management and implies that this contributes to delayed diagnosis and subsequent spread. Although many hospitals reported some training, the authors question its sufficiency and state that one-time training is inadequate. The documented diagnostic delays ('confirmation... took over a week') are tied to spread of secondary infections, supporting the inference that limited staff knowledge (and insufficient ongoing education) is an important driver of secondary transmission.

478.Option: The use of shared medical equipment and environmental surfaces is the primary mechanism driving secondary transmission in long-term care hospitals
Evidence: Regarding infection control, 80.0% of the patients used individual medical equipment or devices.

Regarding linen collection, 54.4% of the hospitals used a separate covered trolley. A total of 22.2% of the hospitals used dedicated laundry equipment, 15.6% used dedicated tableware, and 10.0% used dedicated washing machines for the tableware.

Environmental disinfection of the rooms of patients with infection was performed in manner similar to that for other patient rooms in 33.3% of the hospitals, and 66.7% of the nursing hospitals used separate disinfection methods.

The most common problems were the isolation of patients (47.8%), diagnosis-related problems (31.1%), management of individuals who were in contact with patients with infection (17.8%), problems related to staff caring for patients with infection (16.7%), and treatment problems (11.1%).
478.Rationale: The passage does not identify shared equipment or surfaces as the primary mechanism of secondary spread. On the contrary, it reports high use of individual medical equipment (80%) and describes other infection-control measures (linen handling, disinfection). The authors emphasize isolation failures and diagnostic/management problems as the most common issues, not shared equipment. Therefore there is insufficient evidence to support that shared equipment is the primary driver.

478.Option: The high rate of asymptomatic transmission among first exposed individuals significantly increases the risk of secondary spread
Evidence: Further, 83% of patients were suspected of having scabies at the time of admission. Further, 83% of patients had symptoms related to scabies at the time of admission, and 17% of patients did not have scabies-related symptoms (Table 4). That means we cannot rely on the occurrence of symptom.
Latent symptoms of itching occur due to suppression of innate immunity.

The number of individuals with first exposure was 786 in total, ranging from 1 to 67 individuals per LTCH (mean, 14.3 ± 13.4). Of these, 157 individuals were diagnosed with scabies, ranging from 1 to 23 individuals per LTCH (mean, 5.4 ± 4.9).
478.Rationale: While the passage notes that a minority (17%) of patients lacked symptoms at admission and that latent/asymptomatic presentation can occur, it does not document a 'high rate' of asymptomatic transmission among first exposed individuals or quantify asymptomatic onward transmission as the main driver. The descriptive exposure data show many contacts but do not attribute secondary spread primarily to asymptomatic transmission; instead, the text emphasizes isolation challenges and diagnostic delays as key contributors. Therefore the specific claim that asymptomatic transmission is the principal driver is not supported by the provided evidence.

479.Option: The lack of a significant association between water source and HEV seroprevalence suggests that waterborne transmission is not a major route of HEV infection in Nepal
Evidence: HEV ... spreading from person-to-person via contaminated water [1].

In endemic areas, both large outbreaks and sporadic cases of HEV infections occur frequently usually due to genotype 1 or 2 HEV via fecal to oral transmission through contamination of drinking water or contaminated food [27].
We didn’t find any statistically significant association between wealth quintile, water source, and toilet facility with HEV IgG seroprevalence (Table 1).

The first major outbreak outside of the Kathmandu valley was documented in 2014 and was estimated to affect approximately 7,000 people in Biratnagar city following consumption of contaminated municipal water [8].
479.Rationale: The absence of an association in this cross-sectional seroprevalence analysis does not prove that waterborne transmission is unimportant. The passage explicitly states that HEV spreads via contaminated water and documents outbreaks linked to contaminated municipal water. The quoted finding of no significant association is an observation from this survey but cannot overturn the established transmission biology and outbreak evidence; it may reflect limitations of the survey (e.g., exposure misclassification, timing, sample size) rather than absence of waterborne transmission. Therefore the option's causal inference is not supported by the passage.

479.Option: The geographic variation in seroprevalence across ecological regions reflects differences in access to healthcare rather than differences in infection risk
Evidence: The three distinct ecological regions of Nepal exhibited variation in seroprevalence.

Seroprevalence was significantly lower (P<0.001) in clusters sampled in the flat Terai region in the south of the country at 14.2% (95% CI: 12.7–15.8), compared with the Mountain region in the north of the country at 24.6% (95% CI: 18.8–30.5), and Hilly region in between at 28.9% (95% CI: 26.6–31.2).

Lack of access to clean water may contribute to a higher HEV seroprevalence in these ecological regions as compared to terai region, as Terai is known to have higher coverage of improved water sources than mountain and hilly regions [19].

Our study is subject to several limitations. ... household-based sampling may systematically exclude migrant populations and those living in informal settlements who may be at higher risk for HEV infection due to poor access to water and sanitation facilities.
479.Rationale: The passage attributes geographic variation to differences in environmental risk (e.g., access to clean water) and documents higher seroprevalence in certain ecological regions; it does not claim that differences are driven by healthcare access. The provided quotes point to water and sanitation as plausible drivers and note sampling limitations, but do not support the specific claim that variation reflects healthcare access rather than infection risk. Thus the option is not supported by the passage.

479.Option: The low seroprevalence in young children may reflect lower exposure to HEV in early life, suggesting that infection risk is primarily acquired in adulthood
Evidence: Only 5.8% (95% CI: 1.88–9.63) of males and 4.0% (95% CI: 0.16–7.76) of females were seropositive in the age group 2–5 years, which contrasts with a much higher 51.6% (95% CI: 44.4–58.9) and 42.9% (95% CI: 35.6–50.2) in males and females respectively in the age group 60+ years.
we demonstrated the steep rise in seroprevalence after age of 20 years in Nepal, suggesting infection risk may be higher in adults.

The seroprevalence of HEV IgG as a surrogate marker for past infection may underestimate the true infection rate, as antibodies against HEV are known to wane over time. This decline in antibody levels, known as seroreversion, could lead to false negatives, particularly among those infected in the distant past.
Another study in Bangladesh reported antibody loss, i.e., seroreversion, of 20% 10 years post HEV infection, and the seroreversion was higher among those who were infected at younger age [25].

A recent study ... reported the annual rate to be 15% (95% CI: 10–21%). This same study found that children are more likely to serorevert which could contribute to the lower HEV IgG seroprevalence among young children in our data.
479.Rationale: Although the raw age-specific seroprevalence is lower in young children and higher in adults, the passage explicitly warns that antibody waning (seroreversion), particularly higher in those infected at younger ages, may cause seroprevalence to underestimate past infection in children. Therefore concluding that low child seroprevalence solely reflects lower exposure (infection primarily acquired in adulthood) ignores the important factor of seroreversion, and is not the best interpretation supported by the passage.

479.Option: The higher seroprevalence in older adults indicates that HEV infection is primarily a disease of aging, with little risk in early life
Evidence: Overall, 20.8% (95% CI: 19.54–22.16) of the individuals tested positive for anti-HEV IgG.

Only 5.8% (95% CI: 1.88–9.63) of males and 4.0% (95% CI: 0.16–7.76) of females were seropositive in the age group 2–5 years, which contrasts with a much higher 51.6% (95% CI: 44.4–58.9) and 42.9% (95% CI: 35.6–50.2) in males and females respectively in the age group 60+ years.
we demonstrated the steep rise in seroprevalence after age of 20 years in Nepal, suggesting infection risk may be higher in adults.

The seroprevalence of HEV IgG as a surrogate marker for past infection may underestimate the true infection rate, as antibodies against HEV are known to wane over time. ... seroreversion ... could lead to false negatives, particularly among those infected in the distant past.
This same study found that children are more likely to serorevert which could contribute to the lower HEV IgG seroprevalence among young children in our data.
479.Rationale: While older adults have higher measured seroprevalence, the passage does not support the conclusion that HEV is primarily a disease of aging with little risk in early life. It explicitly notes antibody waning and higher seroreversion in those infected at younger ages, which can make past infections in children less detectable by serology. Therefore the observed age pattern may reflect cumulative exposure and antibody waning dynamics rather than an inherent lack of early-life risk.

479.Option: The observed low seroprevalence in young children likely underestimates their true infection burden due to higher rates of seroreversion over time
Evidence: The seroprevalence of HEV IgG as a surrogate marker for past infection may underestimate the true infection rate, as antibodies against HEV are known to wane over time.
This decline in antibody levels, known as seroreversion, could lead to false negatives, particularly among those infected in the distant past.
Another study in Bangladesh reported antibody loss, i.e., seroreversion, of 20% 10 years post HEV infection, and the seroreversion was higher among those who were infected at younger age [25].

A recent study that aimed to measure the rate of HEV seroreversion in Bangladesh reported the annual rate to be 15% (95% CI: 10–21%). This same study found that children are more likely to serorevert which could contribute to the lower HEV IgG seroprevalence among young children in our data.
Only 5.8% (95% CI: 1.88–9.63) of males and 4.0% (95% CI: 0.16–7.76) of females were seropositive in the age group 2–5 years...
479.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that antibody waning (seroreversion) can cause seroprevalence to underestimate past infection and cites studies showing seroreversion is higher among those infected at younger ages and that children are more likely to serorevert. Therefore, when interpreting low seroprevalence in young children, investigators must consider that seroreversion may lead to underestimation of their true past infection burden.

480.Option: The significant increase in IgA memory B cell responses to both Ogawa and Inaba serotypes at day 21 and day 44, as IgA responses are associated with mucosal immunity and protection at the site of infection
Evidence: We also observed an increase in the number of circulating V. cholerae OSP specific IgA MBCs to both the Inaba and Ogawa serotypes, though these responses were detected in the circulation for a shorter duration of time after vaccination.
Again, most vaccinees had detectable Ogawa IgA MBC responses following vaccination with a 51% responder frequency at day 21 and 60% at day 44.
This is an important finding since OSP specific MBC responses appear to be an immunological indicator of long-term protective immunity against cholera.
480.Rationale: Although IgA memory B cells increased at days 21 and 44 (evidence: responder frequencies and observed increase), the passage explicitly notes these IgA responses were detected for a shorter duration than the IgG OSP MBC responses. The authors emphasize OSP-specific MBCs—particularly IgG OSP MBCs—as indicators of long-term protection. Therefore, the transient IgA MBC increase at early time points does not most strongly support durable protection.

480.Option: The detection of IgM memory B cell responses to both Ogawa and Inaba serotypes in culture supernatants, as IgM is a marker of early immune activation and may indicate robust immune priming
Evidence: V. cholerae Ogawa and Inaba-OSP IgM-MBC responses (Fig 5), as measured by ELISA in cell culture supernatants, (see methods for more experimental rationale), were detected after vaccination.
They were no longer elevated after day 90 for Ogawa and day 44 for Inaba.
480.Rationale: While IgM MBC responses were detected after vaccination, the passage shows these IgM responses were short-lived (no longer elevated after day 90 for Ogawa and day 44 for Inaba). IgM generally reflects early immune activation rather than durable memory. Because durable protection requires longer-lived memory (the authors highlight OSP-specific IgG MBCs as indicators of long-term immunity), the transient IgM finding does not most strongly support the hypothesis of durable protection.

480.Option: The sustained elevation of vibriocidal antibody titers to both Ogawa and Inaba serotypes for at least one year after vaccination, as these titers are directly correlated with protection against cholera
Evidence: Vibriocidal antibody response is a measurement of the total amount of antibodies targeting the V. cholerae bacteria and is commonly used to determine the level of prior exposure within an individual.

The vibriocidal antibody response peaked on day 21 after 2 doses of vaccine and decreased in the subsequent time-points (day 44, 90, 180 and 360). However, in aggregate, the vibriocidal antibody titers remained significantly elevated over baseline titers even up to 1 year after vaccination for both the Inaba and Ogawa serotypes.
This is an important finding since OSP specific MBC responses appear to be an immunological indicator of long-term protective immunity against cholera.

the presence of detectable circulating IgA or IgG OSP MBCs are predictive of protection against V. cholerae following infection[5] or vaccination[22], even in exposed individuals without persistently elevated levels of circulating vibriocidal antibodies[5].
480.Rationale: Although vibriocidal titers remained elevated up to one year, the passage presents vibriocidal antibodies primarily as markers of prior exposure and notes that OSP-specific MBCs (especially IgG) are the immunologic indicator most associated with long-term protection. The authors even state MBC presence predicts protection 'even in... individuals without persistently elevated levels of circulating vibriocidal antibodies.' Thus, vibriocidal rise alone is not characterized in the paper as the strongest support for durable protection.

480.Option: The higher fold-rise in Inaba vibriocidal antibody responses compared to Ogawa on day 44, suggesting a stronger immune response to the Inaba serotype that may contribute to longer-lasting protection
Evidence: There is a significantly greater fold rise over baseline of Inaba vibriocidal antibody responses versus Ogawa on day 44, as measured by a paired t-test (p = 0.02).
We also observed longer lasting IgG antibody and memory B cell responses to the Ogawa-OSP antigen compared to Inaba-OSP after vaccination.
480.Rationale: While Inaba vibriocidal titers showed a greater fold-rise at day 44, the passage reports that longer-lasting antibody and MBC responses were observed to Ogawa rather than Inaba. The greater short-term fold-rise for Inaba on day 44 does not translate in the study to evidence of longer durability; in fact, Inaba MBCs were significant up to 6 months (less durable than Ogawa IgG MBCs which persisted to 12 months). Therefore this option does not best support durable protection.

480.Option: The persistence of O-specific polysaccharide-specific IgG memory B cell responses up to 6 months post-vaccination, given their established role as a correlate of long-term protection
Evidence: V. cholerae Ogawa-OSP antigen-specific MBCs demonstrated the most robust increase over baseline. The majority of vaccinees had a V. cholerae Ogawa-OSP IgG MBC response, with 67% demonstrating a response at day 21 and 64% demonstrating a response at day 44. In aggregate, these responses remained significantly elevated over baseline at 12 months post vaccination, the latest time point assessed.
V. cholerae Inaba-OSP specific IgG-MBCs were also significantly elevated up to 6 months after vaccination.
This is an important finding since OSP specific MBC responses appear to be an immunological indicator of long-term protective immunity against cholera.

the presence of detectable circulating IgA or IgG OSP MBCs are predictive of protection against V. cholerae following infection[5] or vaccination[22], even in exposed individuals without persistently elevated levels of circulating vibriocidal antibodies[5].
480.Rationale: The authors emphasize that O-specific polysaccharide (OSP) IgG memory B cell responses are an immunologic indicator correlated with long-term protection. The study found robust OSP-specific IgG MBC responses (Ogawa persisting up to 12 months and Inaba up to 6 months). Option text cites persistence up to 6 months, which is true for Inaba and conservative relative to the Ogawa finding; these sustained IgG MBC responses—known correlates of protection—provide the strongest immunologic support in the paper for durable vaccine-induced protection.

481.Option: The relationship between admission FPG levels and in-hospital death in SFTS patients without preexisting diabetes is fully explained by the severity of disease at presentation
Evidence: After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A).
Multivariate Cox regression analysis indicated that elevated FPG concentrations were significantly correlated with an increased risk of death among patients with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.
481.Rationale: The passage reports that elevated FPG remained an independent predictor of in-hospital death after adjustment for other clinical variables (i.e., not fully explained by severity at presentation). Therefore the relationship is not fully explained by severity alone; FPG has an independent effect.

481.Option: Admission FPG levels are significantly associated with in-hospital mortality and disease severity in SFTS patients without preexisting diabetes, but this association is primarily driven by age and inflammatory markers
Evidence: After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A).

multivariate logistic analysis (Fig 4B) revealed that high FPG levels (7.0–11.1 mmol/L) at admission was an independent risk factor of the severe type of SFTS patients (odds ratio [OR] = 2.964, 95%CI 1.235–7.116, P = 0.015).
481.Rationale: While age and inflammatory markers (e.g., PCT) are independent predictors, the passage shows FPG itself is independently associated with both mortality and severity after adjustment. That means the association is not primarily driven away by age/inflammation; FPG retains independent predictive value.

481.Option: Admission FPG levels are not independently associated with clinical outcomes in SFTS patients without preexisting diabetes, as the effect is confounded by the use of glucocorticoids and other treatments
Evidence: The exclusion criteria were patients with SFTSV (3) who received glucocorticoid therapy before transfer to our hospital;
After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A).

multivariate logistic analysis (Fig 4B) revealed that high FPG levels (7.0–11.1 mmol/L) at admission was an independent risk factor of the severe type of SFTS patients (odds ratio [OR] = 2.964, 95%CI 1.235–7.116, P = 0.015).
481.Rationale: Glucocorticoid-treated patients were explicitly excluded, so glucocorticoids do not confound the association. Moreover, multivariate analyses demonstrated that FPG was independently associated with outcomes after adjustment for other factors. Thus the option is incorrect.

481.Option: Elevated admission FPG levels independently predict in-hospital mortality and severe disease in SFTS patients without preexisting diabetes, even after accounting for age and systemic inflammation
Evidence: After adjusting for some clinical variables, age (≥60 years), tremor, and high PCT and FPG levels (≥11.1 mmol/L) were independently associated with in-hospital death among these patients (Fig 4A).

multivariate logistic analysis (Fig 4B) revealed that high FPG levels (7.0–11.1 mmol/L) at admission was an independent risk factor of the severe type of SFTS patients (odds ratio [OR] = 2.964, 95%CI 1.235–7.116, P = 0.015).
Multivariate Cox regression analysis indicated that elevated FPG concentrations were significantly correlated with an increased risk of death among patients with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.
481.Rationale: The study reports that elevated FPG remained independently associated with in-hospital death (multivariate Cox) and with severe disease (multivariate logistic regression) after adjustment for age and inflammatory markers (e.g., PCT), supporting the conclusion that admission FPG independently predicts worse outcomes.

481.Option: Elevated admission FPG levels are a reliable predictor of secondary infection in SFTS patients without preexisting diabetes, particularly when combined with high PCT and age
Evidence: Logistic regression analysis did not identify high FPG level as a risk factor for the occurrence of secondary infection among these patients (OR1 = 2.286, 95%CI:0.529–9.874, P = 0.268; OR2 = 3.066, 95%CI:0.692–13.575, P = 0.14) (Table 4).
One possible explanation for this finding might be the routine clinician of antibiotics to these patients to prevent sepsis upon admission from our medical center.
481.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that high FPG was not identified as a risk factor for secondary infection (non-significant ORs), and the authors suggest routine antibiotic use as a possible reason. Therefore elevated admission FPG is not shown to be a reliable predictor of secondary infection in this cohort.

482.Option: The presence of zoonotic Bartonella lineages in common, synanthropic rodent hosts and their associated mites suggests a heightened risk of spillover to humans in this mixed-use landscape
Evidence: The phylogenetic relatedness of five Bartonella lineages from this study (RS1, MM1, MB1, FT1, and SM1) to these zoonotic species indicates that these lineages could also potentially be zoonotic.
Bartonella haplotypes identified from mites were not novel and were identical to their respective hosts (M. musculus and M. booduga).

The detection of mites carrying these haplotypes substantiates the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to other incidental hosts including humans, heightening the risk of bartonellosis in the landscape.
The presence of diverse Bartonella lineages in common rodent species, such as R. satarae and M. musculus highlights suspected reservoirs that future research must monitor.
482.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents phylogenetic similarity of several lineages to known zoonotic Bartonella species, reports that mites carried identical haplotypes to their hosts, and states that these findings "substantiates the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to other incidental hosts including humans, heightening the risk of bartonellosis in the landscape." Together these statements support the inference that the observed host associations and genetic relationships point to a heightened spillover risk in this mixed-use landscape.

482.Option: The high haplotype diversity observed in R. satarae and M. musculus, combined with their synanthropic nature, implies that these species are the only potential reservoirs for human Bartonella infections in India
Evidence: We report Bartonella from five species of small mammals and small mammal-associated mites.
This is the first time that Bartonella was documented in three of these species—Rattus satarae, Mus booduga, and Funambulus tristriatus.
Targeted sampling of rare species could address this bias [50].
uncaptured Bartonella diversity exists in small mammals in the mixed-use landscapes of the Western Ghats.
482.Rationale: The passage indicates Bartonella was found in multiple species (five species) and emphasizes that sampling limitations likely underestimate diversity. It does not claim R. satarae and M. musculus are the only potential reservoirs; rather it identifies them as important or suspected reservoirs and calls for broader sampling. Therefore the absolute statement that they are the only potential reservoirs is not supported.

482.Option: The presence of Bartonella in mites from both R. satarae and M. musculus, along with their phylogenetic similarity to human-pathogenic species, suggests that these mites are capable of direct transmission to humans without requiring an intermediate host
Evidence: Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive. Other mite pools from R. satarae, G. ellioti, and P. lasiurus were negative.
Bartonella haplotypes identified from mites were not novel and were identical to their respective hosts (M. musculus and M. booduga).
However, the vector competence of these arthropods from this study is unknown.
These arthropods may be involved in the transmission.
482.Rationale: The passage does report Bartonella-positive mite pools (from M. musculus and M. booduga) and genetic identity to hosts, but explicitly states that 'the vector competence of these arthropods from this study is unknown.' It also notes mites from R. satarae were negative. There is no direct evidence in the study demonstrating mites can transmit to humans without an intermediate host; therefore the claim is unsupported.

482.Option: The phylogenetic similarity of Bartonella lineages to known zoonotic species, along with their presence in mites, suggests that these mites are the primary source of human bartonellosis in the region
Evidence: The phylogenetic relatedness of five Bartonella lineages from this study (RS1, MM1, MB1, FT1, and SM1) to these zoonotic species indicates that these lineages could also potentially be zoonotic.

The detection of mites carrying these haplotypes substantiates the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to other incidental hosts including humans, heightening the risk of bartonellosis in the landscape.
However, the vector competence of these arthropods from this study is unknown.
These arthropods may be involved in the transmission.
482.Rationale: While the study suggests mites may play a role and documents phylogenetic links to zoonotic species, it does not provide evidence that mites are the primary source of human infections. The authors explicitly note uncertainty about vector competence and call for further study. Thus declaring mites the primary source is beyond the data.

482.Option: The high prevalence of Bartonella in R. satarae and M. musculus, combined with the detection of mites carrying the same haplotypes, indicates that these species are definitive hosts for human Bartonella infections
Evidence: Bartonella was present in 52.48% of the small mammals tested (Table 1).
Among the four common species found in the landscape, the highest prevalence was observed in R. satarae followed by M. musculus.
The presence of diverse Bartonella lineages in common rodent species, such as R. satarae and M. musculus highlights suspected reservoirs that future research must monitor.

The detection of mites carrying these haplotypes substantiates the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to other incidental hosts including humans, heightening the risk of bartonellosis in the landscape.
482.Rationale: The passage describes high prevalence in these species and suggests they are 'suspected reservoirs' and that mites carry identical haplotypes, raising concern. However, it does not establish that these species are definitive hosts for human infections; the language is cautious ('suspected', 'could also potentially be zoonotic') and the study lacks direct evidence of transmission to humans. Therefore calling them definitive hosts is not supported.

483.Option: Compare mean levels of inflammatory proteins between patients with and without fluid accumulation using an unpaired t-test
Evidence: The presence of ascites, pleural fluid and gall-bladder wall thickening was assessed daily using handheld ultrasonography (Phillips Lumify, Amsterdam, The Netherlands) with 6 hours fasting prior to the procedure.
Associations between variables were analyzed using Spearman’s correlation analysis.

Limitations of our study are the fact that the number of participants was limited, and the participants were relatively homogenous in terms of severity of illness. This precludes the evaluation of the performance of the proteins identified here in the prediction of severe illness.
483.Rationale: A simple unpaired t-test comparing mean protein levels between patients with and without fluid accumulation does not account for confounding by overall disease severity or other covariates and assumes normality and adequate sample size. The study used Spearman correlations for associations and noted limited sample size and relative homogeneity of severity, so a t-test would be insufficient to establish an association independent of disease severity and may be invalid given distributional and sample-size constraints.

483.Option: Conduct a principal component analysis (PCA) to identify clusters of inflammatory proteins associated with fluid accumulation
Evidence: Principal component analysis (PCA) of the inflammatory proteins (n = 337) revealed a clear separation between the acute dengue samples with convalescent dengue and control samples (all p < 0.0001) (Fig 1A).
PCA analysis did not reveal a separation across samples of the different hospitals (S1 Fig).
483.Rationale: PCA is a useful exploratory tool to identify patterns or clusters in high-dimensional protein data (and the study did perform PCA to separate acute vs convalescent samples), but PCA alone does not test whether specific proteins are associated with plasma leakage independent of overall disease severity. It can generate hypotheses (clusters) but cannot provide adjusted, inferential estimates of association controlling for severity or other confounders; multivariable modeling would be needed for that.

483.Option: Use Spearman’s correlation analysis to assess the relationship between inflammatory proteins and dengue severity classification
Evidence: Associations between variables were analyzed using Spearman’s correlation analysis.
Overall, there were no differences in levels of inflammation-related proteins between patients classified as DF and those with DHF (S4A and S4B Fig).
483.Rationale: Spearman correlation was used in the study to assess associations with continuous clinical measures, but dengue severity classification (DF vs DHF) is categorical and Spearman correlation is not the ideal method to assess independent associations with a binary outcome or to adjust for confounding. Moreover, the question asks for establishing links to plasma leakage independent of overall disease severity; that requires adjustment (e.g., multivariable modeling) rather than unadjusted correlation.

483.Option: Use multivariable regression adjusting for dengue severity classification to assess associations between inflammatory proteins and fluid accumulation
Evidence: Differential expression analysis was performed using a moderated paired t-test (acute versus convalescent samples (n = 35)) or a linear model (acute (n = 43) versus control (n = 10) samples) of NPX values using Limma R package [12], which employs an empirical Bayes method to moderate the standard errors of the fold change.
Age, sex and BMI were statistically insignificant between patients and controls and were therefore not included in the linear model as covariates.

Overall, there were only few significant associations between DEPs and the following markers of dengue severity: platelet number, hematocrit, plasma albumin, gallbladder wall thickness and the presence of ascites or pleural fluid.
Associations between variables were analyzed using Spearman’s correlation analysis.
483.Rationale: To determine whether specific proteins are linked to plasma leakage (e.g., ascites/pleural fluid) independent of overall disease severity, a multivariable regression approach that models fluid accumulation as the outcome and includes dengue severity classification and other potential confounders as covariates is appropriate. The study already used linear modeling (Limma) and adjusted for covariates where relevant, demonstrating feasibility of regression approaches. Multivariable regression provides adjusted effect estimates, allowing assessment of associations between proteins and plasma leakage while controlling for severity and other confounders, which directly answers the question.

484.Option: The combination of anatomical concealment of early-stage blisters, limited field supervision timing, and paper-based data entry increases the risk of systematic misclassification of cases and containment status
Evidence: The standard for containment requires people to tether their dogs at the blister stage, before worm emergence, but supervisors usually cannot arrive to the field location in time to verify that the dog was indeed tethered at the blister stage.
Timing is critical for containment because there are often only a few hours between development of a blister and worm emergence, when the dog will contaminate water sources.
Guinea worms in dogs are difficult to identify, especially before the worms have emerged, as blisters often occur near the paws or in between toes and are obscured by fur.

Paper-based survey forms are prone to physical damage or loss and errors in handwritten information is a reality. Data could be inadvertently or maliciously changed, and auditing is more difficult because of the lack of version control.
484.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents three linked problems that would systematically bias case identification: (1) early-stage blisters are anatomically concealed and thus hard to detect ('blisters often occur near the paws ... and are obscured by fur'), (2) supervisors cannot reliably verify containment at the critical blister stage because they 'usually cannot arrive ... in time' and there are only 'a few hours' before emergence, and (3) reliance on paper-based forms creates data loss, entry errors, and poor auditability. Together these factors can produce systematic misclassification of case status and containment (not random error), supporting the option.

484.Option: The inability to verify containment compliance due to delayed supervisor arrival creates a risk of false-negative case reporting, particularly in remote areas with limited connectivity
Evidence: The standard for containment requires people to tether their dogs at the blister stage, before worm emergence, but supervisors usually cannot arrive to the field location in time to verify that the dog was indeed tethered at the blister stage.
Timing is critical for containment because there are often only a few hours between development of a blister and worm emergence, when the dog will contaminate water sources.
community members (and potentially even field staff) may be inaccurately reporting data, especially canine containment data.
staff reported having received adequate training in surveillance and program activities, but they faced challenges in technology adoption due to limited connectivity in the region.
484.Rationale: The passage documents that supervisors often cannot verify tethering at the blister stage and that only a brief window exists before worm emergence, which undermines verification of containment. It also states that community members and field staff may 'be inaccurately reporting data, especially canine containment data' and notes 'limited connectivity in the region.' These facts together indicate a plausible and documented pathway to systematic under-ascertainment or misreporting of cases (i.e., false negatives or misclassified containment), particularly in remote, poorly connected areas, so the option describes a real critical challenge.

484.Option: The use of paper-based forms with no digital version increases the likelihood of data duplication and inconsistent data entry across multiple databases
Evidence: Paper-based survey forms are prone to physical damage or loss and errors in handwritten information is a reality. Data could be inadvertently or maliciously changed, and auditing is more difficult because of the lack of version control.

Paper-based rumor investigation forms for humans (and animals) are completed in the field when staff learn of a rumor. The forms are then sent to the regional level for electronic data entry and cleaning, after which the data are shared and compiled at the headquarters-level.

Thus, the mapping exercise revealed a total of three forms (using three unique Microsoft Excel databases) that could potentially derive from rumor investigations. Despite having a common origin, none of these databases are linked, and each is updated manually, thereby introducing the possibility of data entry error.
484.Rationale: The passage describes a paper-to-electronic workflow that results in multiple unlinked electronic databases derived from the same paper forms and indicates manual updates and entry errors. It also highlights inherent problems with paper forms (damage, handwriting errors, lack of version control). These statements directly support the claim that paper-based collection increases duplication and inconsistent entry across databases, making this option a correct description of a critical challenge.

485.Option: Sustained high titers of neutralizing antibodies over 12 weeks post-vaccination
Evidence: To evaluate the longevity of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP induced antibody responses, a group of C57BL/6 mice inoculated with a single-dose of 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP were not challenged and maintained for 12 weeks, and the titers of CHIKV-specific and neutralizing antibodies were continuingly analyzed at two-week intervals post-immunization.

As shown in Fig 6A and 6B, the CHIKV-specific IgG and the neutralizing antibodies increased to their peak titers of 1:51,200 at week 6 and 1: 1,280 at week 8, respectively, and both of them remained at a high level thereafter.
These results suggest that a single-dose immunization of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP vaccine can induce robust and long-term antibody responses against CHIKV infection.
485.Rationale: Durable protection is best supported by evidence of sustained neutralizing antibody levels over an extended period after a single immunization. The passage explicitly reports a single-dose group followed for 12 weeks, with neutralizing antibodies peaking and then 'remaining at a high level thereafter,' and states that a single dose 'can induce robust and long-term antibody responses.' These statements directly support that sustained neutralizing titers over 12 weeks most strongly underpin the claim of durable protection.

485.Option: Dose-dependent increase in neutralizing antibody titers following a single immunization
Evidence: The antibody responses induced by rAAV1-CHIKV-SP was dose-dependent, with mice receiving 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP showing the highest levels of CHIKV-specific and neutralizing antibodies.

Consistent with the prime-boost strategy, the titers of the CHIKV-specific and neutralizing antibodies were at low levels on day 14 and significantly increased by day 28 after immunization (Fig 4B and 4C). The antibody responses induced by rAAV1-CHIKV-SP was dose-dependent...
485.Rationale: While dose-dependency demonstrates that higher single-dose amounts produce greater neutralizing responses (supporting potency), it does not by itself demonstrate durability of protection over time. The passage shows dose-dependent titers but does not indicate that dose-dependence alone is the primary evidence for long-term protection; sustained high titers over 12 weeks are the direct durability evidence.

485.Option: Complete absence of viremia and minimal footpad swelling in mice challenged 30 days after a single dose
Evidence: The C57BL/6 mice receiving a single dose immunization of 5×108, 5×109, and 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP were challenged with 2.5×105 PFU CHIKV-ECSA viruses on day 30 post-vaccination, and the viremia and foot swelling conditions were monitored after challenge (Fig 4A).

As shown in Fig 5, mice receiving the dosages of 5×109 and 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP completely controlled the virus challenge with no detectable viremia and significant foot swelling after the virus attack.
485.Rationale: The absence of viremia and minimal footpad swelling at 30 days post-vaccination demonstrates short-term protective efficacy, but 'durable' implies long-term protection. The cited results are for a 30-day challenge (short-term), so although they confirm that a single dose can protect at one month, they do not by themselves provide the strongest evidence for durability over multiple weeks/months—unlike the sustained neutralizing titers observed over 12 weeks.

485.Option: Robust induction of both IgG1 and IgG2c antibodies with a high IgG2c/IgG1 ratio after a single immunization
Evidence: similar with the prime-boost immunization, both IgG1 and IgG2c were elicited, but the IgG2c levels were higher than IgG1 (Fig 4D–4F), further confirming that the rAAV-CHIKV-SP vaccine elicits Th1-polarized immune responses.
We found that rAAV-CHIKV-SP immunization in C57BL/6 mice generated a Th1-skewed immune response with the IgG2c/IgG1 ratio > 1.
485.Rationale: A high IgG2c/IgG1 ratio indicates a Th1-biased immune response, which speaks to the quality and type of immunity elicited (potentially favorable), but it does not directly demonstrate long-term durability. The passage links IgG subtype profiles to immune bias rather than persistence over time; thus this feature is supportive of immune quality but is not the strongest direct evidence that a single dose provides durable protection.

485.Option: High peak titers of CHIKV-specific IgG antibodies at week 6 post-vaccination
Evidence: As shown in Fig 6A and 6B, the CHIKV-specific IgG and the neutralizing antibodies increased to their peak titers of 1:51,200 at week 6 and 1: 1,280 at week 8, respectively, and both of them remained at a high level thereafter.

A single-dose injection of 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP elicited comparable antibody responses with the two-dose vaccination of 5×1010 GC rAAV1-CHIKV-SP and 5×1010 GC rAAV9-CHIKV-SP, and sustained long-lasting neutralization titers.
485.Rationale: A high peak at week 6 indicates strong immunogenicity, but a peak alone does not equal durability. The crucial point for durable protection is that titers 'remained at a high level thereafter' over 12 weeks. Thus the peak at week 6 is part of the durability story but is not the single feature that most strongly supports the conclusion—sustained neutralizing titers over the 12-week follow-up are the stronger evidence.

487.Option: The representativeness of the sample population used in validation may overestimate the test's real-world performance due to selection bias from controlled laboratory conditions
Evidence: It is noteworthy that these excellent sensitivity results were obtained using a majority (88.5%) of frozen samples given that the freezing-thawing step could have contributed to the degradation of the virus inducing a decrease of sensitivity.

RT-hn-PCR could only be carried out on D1 for logistic reasons (access to laboratory equipment and sample processing time (including drying time for FP)), and this delay could also have negatively impacted sensitivity.

The successful use of molecular diagnostic methods using brain tissue directly or brain tissue dried on filter paper or FTA cards ... to detect different species of lyssavirus (including rabies virus) has been demonstrated by several studies [14–17]. Yet, to our knowledge, the sensitivity and specificity of these methods using field collected samples had not yet been thoroughly evaluated.

We also tested the use of FP for the first 10 samples collected in the field ... These field results should be strengthened by collecting a greater number of samples and by asking different teams in multiple locations to test the process.

Compared to the sample size initially calculated, the number of true positive samples was sufficient to evaluate sensitivity with 80% power margin of error no greater than 0.05. Conversely, to reach the same power and to keep the same margin of error for specificity, 164 true negative samples should have been included.

These results were obtained in artificial conditions of important laboratory work load involving multiple series of PCRs carried out on an important number of samples, which can increase the risk of human error.
487.Rationale: The passage explicitly notes that validation used predominantly frozen samples, laboratory processing constraints, and only a small number of field-collected samples. It also states that field-collected-sample performance had not been thoroughly evaluated and that more field data are needed. Those statements support the concern that the validation sample set may not be representative of real-world conditions and therefore could overestimate performance (selection/setting bias).

487.Option: The test's performance is most critically influenced by the cost of filter paper and PCR reagents, which limits widespread use in low-income settings
Evidence: Another argument against this technique might be that if NRL only receive brain tissue impregnated FP instead of brain tissue samples, rabies viral strains will no longer be available to sustain virus repositories.
The implementation of RT-hn-PCR-FP for surveillance activities will increase the lab costs (because RT-hn-PCR are more expensive than FAT tests).

A compromise might be that i) in areas of low-access, from which there are currently no samples being shipped, the use of FP as sample support is implemented and ii) when samples are collected from more accessible areas, brain biopsies are collected ideally alongside to (or if not possible, instead of) impregnated FP.

A similar study to evaluate the sensitivity and specificity of molecular diagnosis using brain impregnated FTA cards instead of Whatman filter papers, as done with RT-qPCR [17], could be carried out using a statistically supportive sample size, although costs would increase slightly.
487.Rationale: The passage acknowledges increased costs and slightly higher costs for FTA cards and PCR relative to FAT, but it frames cost as an implementation barrier rather than the critical factor that affects interpretation of the test's diagnostic performance in real-world conditions. The text emphasizes storage/transport time, storage temperature, limited field validation, and personnel/laboratory capacity as primary influences on observed sensitivity/specificity, not cost per se.

487.Option: The sensitivity and specificity of the test may be underestimated in real-world settings due to higher viral loads in field-collected samples compared to laboratory samples
Evidence: It is noteworthy that these excellent sensitivity results were obtained using a majority (88.5%) of frozen samples given that the freezing-thawing step could have contributed to the degradation of the virus inducing a decrease of sensitivity.
RT-qPCR TaqMan L results showed that the viral load was low (ct = 32 when using brain tissue and ct = 37.6 when using FP).

Sample 3656–15 ... was positive by RT-hn-PCR-FP (D1, duplicates R1 and R2) and RT-qPCR TaqMan L FP when tested at NRL laboratory (ct value of 35). However, all other tests performed at the NRC-R were negative.
487.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that field-collected samples systematically have higher viral loads leading to underestimation of test performance. In fact, it highlights that frozen/thawed samples in the lab could have reduced viral load and that some field samples had low viral loads (high Ct values). Thus the evidence does not substantiate the specific mechanism proposed in this option and in some cases suggests the opposite (that laboratory handling may reduce viral load).

487.Option: The test's performance is most critically influenced by the time elapsed between sample collection and PCR testing, which varies widely in real-world settings
Evidence: The impact of the duration of storage was also evaluated.
On days 7 and 15 elution, RNA extraction and RT-hn-PCR were repeated for positive samples of brain tissue dried on FP to test the impact of storage duration.

RT-hn-PCR-FP enabled to detect rabies virus on the 58 true-positive filter papers stored at room temperature with the same level of 100% of sensitivity at day 7. At day 15, two samples out of 58 tested negative leading to 96.6% of sensitivity. At 24 months, all 67 samples tested were positive by RT-hn-PCR-FP.

Thus, from a public health point of view, we can conservatively affirm that sending impregnated filter papers to the NRL within a one-week delay would not impact the sensitivity of the RT-hn-PCR-FP. Longer storage periods are probably possible but more thorough investigations should be carried out. In particular, before field implementation of FP as a support for molecular testing (in the lab) of rabies virus, we recommend to further assess the combined effect of time and temperatures (i.e. testing storage at higher temperatures).

Rapid transport of samples, especially under a temperature-controlled supply chain to the laboratory can be particularly difficult in Africa because of the very low accessibility to remote rural areas and the limited access to appropriate transport containers.
487.Rationale: The study directly evaluated storage duration and showed sensitivity changes by day 15 and discussed transport delays, ambient temperature and recommended sending FP within one week. Those statements indicate that variable time between collection and testing (and associated temperature exposure) is a critical factor that will influence observed performance in real-world settings.

487.Option: The test's performance is most critically influenced by the availability of trained personnel to perform the RT-hn-PCR assay in remote field locations
Evidence: Given that molecular diagnostic capacities in Madagascar are extremely limited (the NRL is the only laboratory performing rabies molecular diagnosis in the country), and given the short (one-week) [4] and free-of-charge PEP protocol in place in Madagascar, the main objective of using impregnated FP in Madagascar is not to guide PEP but rather to facilitate sample collection from low-access areas which have never or seldom submitted samples of rabies suspected animals.
Vaccination and training of the public health officers and field staff on these methods would also need to be implemented in Madagascar.

These results were obtained in artificial conditions of important laboratory work load involving multiple series of PCRs carried out on an important number of samples, which can increase the risk of human error.

Confusion between samples rarely occurs when good laboratory practices are respected. As for any other technique, the application of the good laboratory practices and training on biosafety would be imperative if this technique were to be implemented for routine surveillance.
The main practical drawback from this field experience was that the quantity of brain obtained using the straw method was small, limiting further testing of the samples.
487.Rationale: The passage emphasizes limited molecular diagnostic capacity centralized in a single lab, the need for training of field and lab personnel, the risk of human error and contamination under heavy workload, and logistical constraints in sample collection. These operational and human-resource limitations can substantially affect how the assay performs and how results are interpreted in routine, decentralized settings, making personnel availability and training a critical real-world influence.

488.Option: A persistent antibody response in the absence of active infection is the most likely explanation for continued seropositivity
Evidence: it is possible that a persistent antibody response has developed in these individuals in the absence of ongoing infection, in a situation analogous to the “serofast” response sometimes seen in treponemal infection [27].
Faecal PCR studies were conducted in 6 cases and were negative, suggesting an absence of infection, or at least an absence of larval shedding at the time of testing.
On balance, the further tests conducted on the 9 non-responder cases in this study do not support the presence of on-going infection with S. stercoralis.
488.Rationale: The passage explicitly proposes a persistent antibody (serologic) response as a plausible explanation for continued ELISA positivity despite multiple treatments, comparing it to a known ‘serofast’ phenomenon. This interpretation is strengthened by objective test results: faecal PCR in six non-responders was negative (suggesting no larval shedding), and the authors conclude that additional testing "do not support the presence of on-going infection." Together these statements most strongly support that persistent seropositivity may reflect antibodies rather than active infection.

488.Option: Resistance to ivermectin has developed in the parasite population, as evidenced by the failure of multiple treatments to clear seropositivity
Evidence: Resistance of the worm to treatment is certainly a possibility, although this is yet to be reported in a human population [4].
On balance, the further tests conducted on the 9 non-responder cases in this study do not support the presence of on-going infection with S. stercoralis.
488.Rationale: While the authors acknowledge that resistance is a theoretical possibility, they explicitly state that resistance has not been reported in human populations. Additionally, further testing of non-responders (e.g., negative faecal PCR) led the authors to conclude these tests do not support ongoing infection. Thus there is not persuasive evidence in the passage that ivermectin resistance explains the persistent seropositivity.

488.Option: The ELISA test is producing false positive results due to cross-reactivity with other helminth infections in the population
Evidence: Concerns over cross reactivity with other helminth infections have less relevance in the Australian setting where these infections are not found.
ELISA testing is now generally accepted as being sensitive and specific enough for use in both clinical and research settings [1, 10].
488.Rationale: The passage states that cross-reactivity concerns are less relevant in the Australian setting because other helminth infections that could cause cross-reaction "are not found." ELISA is presented as sufficiently sensitive and specific for this context. Therefore the passage does not support the idea that cross-reactive false positives are the main reason for persistent seropositivity in this community.

488.Option: Reinfection from environmental sources is likely, given the high prevalence of infection in the community and the persistence of seropositivity after treatment
Evidence: Repeated re-infection of these subjects would seem unlikely as an explanation given the low overall incident rate of infection in the population.
Only three new cases were diagnosed in the first 6 months of follow up, with a further three in the period up to 3 years.
488.Rationale: Although the community has a history of high prevalence, the study observed a low incident rate of new infections during follow-up (only a few new cases over three years). The authors explicitly state that repeated re-infection "would seem unlikely" to explain persistent positivity. Thus reinfection is not the most strongly supported explanation in this passage.

488.Option: The non-responders are experiencing chronic auto-infection, which explains the continued seropositivity despite treatment
Evidence: Because of the chronic nature of this infection and its capacity for sustained auto-infection within individuals, it is possible to have a high prevalence of infection both in communities where conditions allow for high transmission rates of the infection, and equally in communities where transmission is lower, but there are a high number of chronic, untreated infections.

It is not possible to ascertain whether these three cases represent relapse of existing infection, presumably through the process of “auto-infection “which is known to occur in chronic strongyloidiasis, or re-infection from environmental sources.
Faecal PCR studies were conducted in 6 cases and were negative, suggesting an absence of infection, or at least an absence of larval shedding at the time of testing.
On balance, the further tests conducted on the 9 non-responder cases in this study do not support the presence of on-going infection with S. stercoralis.
488.Rationale: Auto-infection is a known biological property of Strongyloides and could in principle cause ongoing infection. However, for these non-responders the passage reports negative faecal PCR in tested cases and concludes that further testing does not support ongoing infection. While auto-infection is a theoretical possibility, the reported test results and the authors' interpretation make it a less supported explanation for the persistent seropositivity than a persistent antibody response.

489.Option: Differences in parasite load and genetic diversity of T. cruzi strains across regions likely influenced the detectability of DNA, contributing to lower test sensitivity in the Argentinean cohort
Evidence: We observed a lower sensitivity of the OligoC-TesTs in the patients from Argentina compared to the patients from Chile and Spain.
Compared to the other cohorts, the Argentinean patients showed a lower percentage of patients with Chagas symptoms which may be related with parasite load.
Particularly during the chronic phase, T. cruzi parasites circulate in very low and variable numbers in the bloodstream [19], [20].
Genetic differences of parasite strains and/or DTUs might influence parasitaemia in man and partially explain the discrepancies of PCR sensitivity between cohorts from different endemic areas.
In addition, T. cruzi DTUs vary in DNA gene copy number, thus harbouring a variable number of repeats of the PCR targets.
Elias et al. described that the satDNA sequences can be 4 to 6 times more abundant in one DTU compared to another [16].
489.Rationale: The passage explicitly links lower sensitivity in the Argentinean cohort to lower percentage with symptoms 'which may be related with parasite load' and emphasizes that in the chronic phase parasitaemia is 'very low and variable'. It also states that 'genetic differences of parasite strains and/or DTUs might influence parasitaemia' and that DTUs vary in target gene copy number (e.g., satDNA abundance differs 4–6×), directly affecting PCR detectability. Together these statements identify differing parasite load and parasite genetic diversity across regions as the most likely explanation for intercohort sensitivity variation.

489.Option: Differences in the DNA extraction efficiency between laboratories may have contributed to the variation in test sensitivity across geographic cohorts
Evidence: DNA extractions were done according to the manufacturer's instructions but with some modifications for the High Pure PCR Template Preparation Kit.
Purified DNA ... was obtained from the DNA reference bank ...
All blood samples in the study were collected, stored and shipped following a standardized procedure.

We checked for degradation of the DNA in the Argentinean blood samples using primers targeting the human cytochrome b gene [21]. All samples were positive in this control PCR indicating no DNA degradation (data not shown).
489.Rationale: While different extraction kits were used for the Chile cohort versus the others (a factual procedural difference), the investigators stressed that samples were collected, stored and shipped using a standardized procedure and they checked for DNA degradation with a human cytochrome b control PCR (all samples were positive), arguing against gross DNA loss or extraction failure. The passage attributes the sensitivity differences primarily to lower parasite load/DTU differences rather than extraction inefficiency, so extraction differences are less likely to be the main explanation.

489.Option: The use of different reference tests in different countries may have introduced bias in the classification of Chagas patients, leading to misclassification and lower sensitivity estimates in the Argentinean cohort
Evidence: Study participants were classified as Chagas disease patients if positive test results were obtained in ELISA and IHA (Argentina) or ELISA and IIF (Spain and Chile).
All blood samples in the study were collected, stored and shipped following a standardized procedure.
Compared to the other cohorts, the Argentinean patients showed a lower percentage of patients with Chagas symptoms which may be related with parasite load.
489.Rationale: It is true the reference serological algorithms differed (ELISA+IHA in Argentina versus ELISA+IIF elsewhere), which could introduce classification differences. However, the authors do not present misclassification from reference tests as the primary cause; instead they discuss lower symptom prevalence and lower parasitaemia and DTU differences as probable reasons for reduced PCR sensitivity in Argentina. Thus although differing reference tests could hypothetically bias sensitivity estimates, the passage provides stronger, direct evidence for parasite load/strain effects as the most likely driver.

489.Option: Differences in the prevalence of co-infections with Leishmania spp. or T. rangeli among the cohorts may have affected PCR specificity and thus influenced sensitivity estimates
Evidence: false-positive results occur due to cross-reactions with antibodies induced by other microorganisms such as Leishmania spp. or T. rangeli [8].
Both OligoC-TesTs amplified purified T. rangeli DNA, but required between 10^2 and 10^7 times more template DNA than for T. cruzi.
In the current study, we analyzed 5 ng L. chagasi DNA with the kDNA OligoC-TesT and the test remained negative.
Both OligoC-TesTs showed high specificity on the diseased non-endemic and healthy endemic controls, ranging from 94.3% to 100%.
The overall specificities in these participant groups were 99.1% (95% CI 95.2%–99.8%) for the satDNA OligoC-TesT and 97.4% (95% CI 92.6%–99.1%) for the kDNA OligoC-TesT.
489.Rationale: Co-infections causing cross-reactivity would affect specificity (false positives) more than sensitivity (false negatives). The study found high specificities across controls and experimentally showed that detection of T. rangeli or Leishmania required orders of magnitude more template or remained negative, arguing against common cross-reactivity driving the observed lower sensitivity in a cohort. The passage points to low parasitaemia and DTU differences as the likelier reasons for sensitivity variation.

489.Option: Variation in the timing of sample collection relative to symptom onset may have led to differences in parasite load and thus test sensitivity across cohorts
Evidence: Particularly during the chronic phase, T. cruzi parasites circulate in very low and variable numbers in the bloodstream [19], [20].
Compared to the other cohorts, the Argentinean patients showed a lower percentage of patients with Chagas symptoms which may be related with parasite load.
Time between sample collection and conducting the index tests was maximum six months.
489.Rationale: While timing of sample collection relative to disease course can influence parasitaemia and thus PCR sensitivity, the passage does not report differences between cohorts in timing relative to symptom onset. The investigators indicate standardized collection/storage/shipping and a maximum six-month delay to testing, and they specifically discuss lower symptom prevalence and probable lower parasite loads and DTU-related copy-number differences as explanations. Therefore there is insufficient direct evidence in the passage to support variation in sampling timing as the most likely contributor to the intercohort sensitivity differences.

490.Option: Persistent parasite presence in cardiomyocytes is associated with increased susceptibility to spontaneous action potentials, a known substrate for lethal arrhythmias in chronic Chagas disease
Evidence: Finally, using RT-PCR analysis and endogenous 18S mice for internal normalization, the detection of T. cruzi in isolated LVC from infected mice was demonstrated.

In the present study, we demonstrate that in the chronic phase of experimental infection with Trypanosoma cruzi (TcI, Colombian strain), LVCs have increased AP duration and they are more susceptible to display early and delayed after-depolarizations, leading to increased frequency of cells showing spontaneous AP.

Second, we speculate that off-target effects of Ni2+ other than a block of INCX may also contribute to the antiarrhythmic effect. Also, we did not access the fraction of cardiomyocytes infected or not with T. cruzi, since cellular electrical remodeling was investigated as a result of the net heart impairment after chronic infection with T. cruzi.
490.Rationale: While the study documents (1) detection of T. cruzi nucleic acid in isolated left ventricular cardiomyocytes and (2) increased susceptibility of chagasic LVCs to spontaneous APs, it does not provide direct evidence linking parasite presence in individual cardiomyocytes to the electrical instability. The authors explicitly state they "did not access the fraction of cardiomyocytes infected or not with T. cruzi" and only "speculate" parasite persistence may contribute. Therefore the claim that persistent parasite presence is associated (and thus the strongest support) overstates the data and is not directly supported by the passage.

490.Option: The significant reduction in sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+ content observed in chagasic cardiomyocytes directly contributes to the development of spontaneous action potentials and poor prognosis
Evidence: In control LVC, the application of caffeine induced a large inward current, as depicted in Fig 9A (Top trace). However, in chagasic LVC, caffeine-induced inward current was significantly smaller (Fig 9A, Bottom trace). Fig 9B shows composite data.
Our results also point towards a reduction of SR Ca2+ load, as indicated by the reduction of caffeine-induced Ca2+ release from SR.

In the present study, we demonstrate that in the chronic phase of experimental infection with Trypanosoma cruzi (TcI, Colombian strain), LVCs have increased AP duration and they are more susceptible to display early and delayed after-depolarizations, leading to increased frequency of cells showing spontaneous AP.

ITi is dependent on intracellular Ca2+ concentration [23,24], a condition that we have previously shown to be increased in cardiomyocytes isolated from chagasic mice in the acute phase (30–45 days post-infection [9]).
The tachycardia-like protocol favors Ca2+ release from SR and, along with reduced SERCA2A function, would contribute to Ca2+ accumulation in the sarcoplasm.
490.Rationale: The study demonstrates a reduced SR Ca2+ load in chagasic cardiomyocytes (smaller caffeine-induced current) and links altered Ca2+ handling to arrhythmogenic mechanisms: ITi depends on intracellular Ca2+ and the authors describe how impaired SR handling and reduced SERCA2A can lead to diastolic Ca2+ accumulation that promotes ITi and after-depolarizations. Those after-depolarizations and spontaneous APs are the cellular substrate for lethal arrhythmias, which underlies poor prognosis. Although causality is complex, the observed SR Ca2+ handling abnormalities are strongly supported in the passage as a mechanistic contributor to cellular electrical instability and thus to adverse outcomes.

490.Option: The use of Ni2+ and SEA0400 to reduce action potential duration and transient inward current demonstrates that inhibiting Na+/Ca2+ exchange is sufficient to prevent all forms of electrical instability in chronic Chagas disease
Evidence: As depicted in Fig 3A, AP paced at 0.2 Hz from healthy LVCs are sensitive to 5 mM Ni2+exposure. Importantly, when LVC from chagasic mice were exposed to 5 mM Ni2+ we observed a reduction of APR time taken at 90% repolarization (T90), as shown in Fig 3B.

Since Ni2+ is a non-selective inhibitor of INCX we performed the same experiment, however, challenging cells with SEA0400, which is considered a selective inhibitor of INCX [18]. ... After exposure of diseased LVC to SEA0400, AP duration was similar to that observed in the control group, which supports the hypothesis that INCX plays an important role in AP remodeling in CCM.
Combined these results indicate that only rescuing AP waveform may be not enough to prevent arrhythmogenesis in CD.

It is relevant mentioning that Ni2+ can have off-target effects on ionic conductances other than the blockage of Na+/Ca2+ exchanger with variable selectivity... Hence, the off-target effects of Ni2+ could be overestimating the contribution of Na+/Ca2+ exchanger on remodeled AP from chagasic mice.
490.Rationale: Although Ni2+ and SEA0400 reduced AP duration and ITi, the authors caution that Ni2+ has off-target effects and explicitly state that "only rescuing AP waveform may be not enough to prevent arrhythmogenesis in CD." The passage therefore does not support the sweeping claim that inhibiting NCX is sufficient to prevent all forms of electrical instability; rather, NCX inhibition is implicated as one contributing mechanism but not a complete cure.

490.Option: The presence of T. cruzi DNA in isolated cardiomyocytes confirms that the parasite is the sole driver of electrical remodeling and arrhythmogenic substrate in chronic Chagas disease
Evidence: Finally, using RT-PCR analysis and endogenous 18S mice for internal normalization, the detection of T. cruzi in isolated LVC from infected mice was demonstrated.

Lastly, since the molecular identification of T. cruzi in isolated LVC from chagasic mice was found, we may speculate that parasite persistence in the heart is an important component to the development of CCM, which would favor a chronic inflammation in the tissue, and then contribute to the observed electrical remodeling observed [35–37].

Also, we did not access the fraction of cardiomyocytes infected or not with T. cruzi, since cellular electrical remodeling was investigated as a result of the net heart impairment after chronic infection with T. cruzi.
Future experiments are necessary to access the putative association between parasite persistence and electrical remodeling in experimental CCM.
490.Rationale: The study shows detection of T. cruzi nucleic acid in isolated cardiomyocytes and the authors speculate parasite persistence may contribute to remodeling. However, they explicitly note they did not measure the fraction of infected cells and call for future experiments to assess the association. The passage does not support the stronger claim that the parasite is the sole driver of electrical remodeling; other mechanisms (inflammation, altered ion handling) are discussed.

490.Option: The frequency-dependent prolongation of action potential duration in chagasic cardiomyocytes is primarily due to reduced L-type Ca2+ current density, which directly increases the risk of sudden cardiac death
Evidence: Previous studies on experimental CD have found prolongation of cardiomyocyte AP repolarization [6,7,9,10]. However, in most of these studies sarcoplasmic Ca2+ was strongly chelated which probably had attenuated the participation of membrane Ca2+-dependent conductance.
Major changes were observed in action potential (AP) repolarization and a consistent reduction of L-type Ca2+ current density [6,7,9,10].

In previous studies using a murine model of CD, it was found profound alteration in cellular excitability of LVC during the time-course of the disease [6], and this was associated with the production of inflammatory cytokines [6,12]. Importantly it was described an AP prolongation that was linked to the reduction of voltage-dependent K+ currents [6,7].
Fig 1B represents time to 50 and 90% of AP repolarization (APR). At 0.2 and 1 Hz a substantial increase in 90% of APR was observed when comparing healthy and diseased LVC...
490.Rationale: While reduced L-type Ca2+ current density has been reported in prior studies, this investigation emphasizes AP prolongation linked to reduced voltage-dependent K+ currents and altered Ca2+ handling (including SR content and INCX-related effects). The passage does not provide evidence that frequency-dependent AP prolongation is primarily due to reduced L-type Ca2+ current density, nor does it claim a direct, sole pathway to sudden cardiac death. Thus the option overstates and misattributes the primary mechanism.
